SectionScore /5Last Attempt
Hormone Classification
Peptide Synthesis
Steroid Synthesis & Enzymes
CAH Types
Thyroid Hormone Synthesis
Amine Hormones
Transport & Free Hormone
Clearance & Half-lives
  1. Which hormone class is synthesised as a preprohormone in the rough ER?

A. Peptide/protein hormones (e.g., insulin, GH, ACTH)

B. Steroid hormones

C. Thyroid hormones

D. Catecholamines

  1. What is the rate-limiting step in steroid hormone synthesis?

A. Cholesterol → Pregnenolone (STAR + CYP11A1)

B. Pregnenolone → 17-OH Pregnenolone

C. 11-Deoxycortisol → Cortisol

D. DOC → Aldosterone

  1. Which enzyme deficiency causes 21-hydroxylase deficiency (95% of CAH)?

A. CYP21A2

B. CYP17A1

C. CYP11B1

D. CYP11B2

  1. What is the half-life of T4 and its main binding protein?

A. 5-7 days; TBG

B. 1 day; TBG

C. 60-90 min; CBG

D. Minutes; Albumin

  1. Which transport protein is increased in pregnancy and OCP use?

A. TBG, CBG, SHBG (all increased)

B. Only TBG

C. Only CBG

D. Albumin

  1. C-peptide is a marker of what?

A. Endogenous insulin secretion

B. Insulin resistance

C. Beta-cell mass

D. Glucagon secretion

  1. Which deiodinase activates T4 to T3?

A. DIO1 and DIO2

B. DIO3

C. DIO1 only

D. DIO2 only

  1. 17,20-lyase activity is part of which enzyme?

A. CYP17A1

B. CYP21A2

C. CYP11B1

D. CYP11B2

  1. PNMT is induced by what?

A. Cortisol

B. ACTH

C. Angiotensin II

D. Potassium

  1. Free hormone hypothesis states:

A. Only unbound hormone is biologically active

B. Total hormone correlates with activity

C. Bound hormone is active

D. Albumin-bound hormone is active

  1. A 30-year-old woman on combined OCP has total T4 180 nmol/L (ref 60-160), TSH 1.5 mIU/L. Explanation?

A. Increased TBG → elevated total T4, free T4 normal

B. Primary hyperthyroidism

C. TSH-secreting adenoma

D. Thyroid hormone resistance

E. Exogenous thyroxine intake

  1. Patient with suspected insulinoma has low glucose. Best test for endogenous hyperinsulinaemia?

A. C-peptide level

B. Proinsulin level

C. Insulin antibodies

D. HbA1c

E. Fasting insulin

  1. In 21-hydroxylase deficiency, which steroid causes virilisation?

A. 17-OH Progesterone → Androstenedione/Testosterone

B. DOC → Aldosterone

C. Cortisol

D. Corticosterone

E. 11-Deoxycortisol

  1. Patient on high-dose prednisolone for 6 months stops abruptly. Main risk?

A. Adrenal crisis (HPA suppression)

B. Hyperglycaemia

C. Hypertension

D. Osteoporosis

E. Electrolyte imbalance

  1. Which hormone has the longest half-life?

A. T4 (5-7 days)

B. T3 (1 day)

C. Cortisol (60-90 min)

D. Insulin (5 min)

E. ACTH (10 min)

  1. Anti-TPO antibodies are most associated with?

A. Hashimoto's thyroiditis

B. Graves disease

C. Thyroid cancer

D. Subacute thyroiditis

E. Toxic nodular goitre

  1. 25yo male with hypertension, hypokalaemia, elevated 17-OH progesterone. Diagnosis?

A. 17α-hydroxylase deficiency

B. 11β-hydroxylase deficiency

C. 21-hydroxylase deficiency

D. Aldosterone synthase deficiency

E. 17β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase deficiency

  1. Correct statement about catecholamine synthesis?

A. PNMT converts noradrenaline to adrenaline in adrenal medulla

B. Tyrosine hydroxylase is not rate-limiting

C. Dopamine β-hydroxylase is in cytoplasm

D. MAO is the main clearance pathway

E. COMT converts dopamine to adrenaline

  1. Selenium deficiency impairs?

A. Peripheral T4 to T3 conversion (DIOs)

B. Iodide uptake (NIS)

C. Iodination (TPO)

D. TSH receptor signalling

E. Thyroglobulin synthesis

  1. Nephrotic syndrome: low total T4, normal TSH. Why?

A. Low albumin → low total T4, free T4 normal

B. Primary hypothyroidism

C. Central hypothyroidism

D. Non-thyroidal illness

E. TSH-secreting adenoma

15. Flashcards

  • Q: Peptide hormone synthesis

A: Preprohormone → Prohormone (ER) → Active hormone + C-peptide (Golgi) → Granules → Regulated secretion (Ca2+)

  • Q: Steroid synthesis rate-limiting

A: Cholesterol → Pregnenolone via STAR + CYP11A1 (P450scc) in inner mitochondrial membrane

  • Q: 21-hydroxylase deficiency (95% CAH)

A: ↓ Cortisol, ↓ Aldosterone → ↑ 17-OH Progesterone → ↑ Androgens → Salt-wasting + Virilisation

  • Q: 11β-hydroxylase deficiency

A: ↓ Cortisol → ↑ 11-Deoxycortisol + ↑ DOC (mineralocorticoid) → HTN + Virilisation

  • Q: 17α-hydroxylase deficiency

A: ↓ Sex steroids → Sexual infantilism; ↑ Mineralocorticoids (DOC) → HTN

  • Q: T4 synthesis steps

A: Iodide → NIS → TPO (iodination + coupling on Thyroglobulin) → Colloid storage → TSH → Endocytosis → T4/T3 release

  • Q: Deiodinases

A: DIO1/DIO2 (Se-dependent): T4→T3 activation; DIO3: Inactivation (rT3, T2)

  • Q: Catecholamine pathway

A: Tyrosine → DOPA → Dopamine → Noradrenaline → (PNMT, cortisol-induced) → Adrenaline

  • Q: Transport proteins

A: CBG: Cortisol (~90%); SHBG: Testosterone/Oestradiol; TBG: T4>T3 (99.97%); Albumin: Low affinity carrier

  • Q: Free hormone hypothesis

A: Only free (unbound) hormone is biologically active; Total hormone misleading if binding proteins altered

  • Q: Half-lives

A: Peptides: 3-30 min; Steroids: 60-90 min; T4: 5-7 d; T3: 1 d; Catecholamines: min

  • Q: STAR protein

A: Cholesterol transfer to inner mitochondrial membrane; Mutations → Lipoid CAH (lethal salt-wasting)

  • Q: Anti-TPO antibodies

A: Hashimoto's (90-95%), Graves (80%), Postpartum thyroiditis; PTU inhibits TPO

  • Q: C-peptide vs Insulin

A: C-peptide: Endogenous secretion marker, t½ 30 min, no hepatic extraction; Insulin: t½ 5 min

16. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. A. Peptide/protein hormones (e.g., insulin, GH, ACTH) — Peptide hormones are synthesised as preprohormones in the rough ER; steroids are synthesised in smooth ER/mitochondria from cholesterol; thyroid hormones from tyrosine/iodide in follicular cells; catecholamines from tyrosine in adrenal medulla.
  1. A. Cholesterol → Pregnenolone (STAR + CYP11A1) — The conversion of cholesterol to pregnenolone by STAR and CYP11A1 (P450scc) is the rate-limiting step in all steroid hormone synthesis.
  1. A. CYP21A2 — 21-hydroxylase deficiency accounts for ~95% of congenital adrenal hyperplasia cases.
  1. A. 5-7 days; TBG — T4 is bound to thyroxine-binding globulin (TBG), giving it a half-life of 5-7 days. T3 has a half-life of ~1 day.
  1. A. TBG, CBG, SHBG (all increased) — Oestrogen (pregnancy, OCP) increases hepatic synthesis of TBG, CBG, and SHBG, raising total hormone levels while free levels remain normal.
  1. A. Endogenous insulin secretion — C-peptide is co-secreted with insulin in equimolar amounts but is not present in exogenous insulin preparations, making it a marker of endogenous beta-cell function.
  1. A. DIO1 and DIO2 — Both DIO1 (liver, kidney) and DIO2 (brain, pituitary, brown fat) convert T4 to T3 (activation). DIO3 inactivates T4/T3.
  1. A. CYP17A1 — CYP17A1 has both 17α-hydroxylase and 17,20-lyase activities; the latter is required for sex steroid synthesis.
  1. A. Cortisol — PNMT (phenylethanolamine N-methyltransferase) is cortisol-induced and converts noradrenaline to adrenaline specifically in the adrenal medulla.
  1. A. Only unbound hormone is biologically active — The free hormone hypothesis states that only the unbound fraction of hormone can cross cell membranes and exert biological effects.

SBAs

  1. A. Increased TBG → elevated total T4, free T4 normal — OCP increases TBG, raising total T4; free T4 and TSH remain normal. This is a classic "euthyroid hyperthyroxinaemia" pattern.
  1. A. C-peptide level — C-peptide is not present in exogenous insulin and has a longer half-life; elevated C-peptide with hypoglycaemia confirms endogenous hyperinsulinaemia.
  1. A. 17-OH Progesterone → Androstenedione/Testosterone — In 21-hydroxylase deficiency, 17-OH progesterone accumulates and is shunted to androgen synthesis, causing virilisation.
  1. A. Adrenal crisis (HPA suppression) — >3 weeks of exogenous steroids causes HPA axis suppression; abrupt withdrawal leads to adrenal crisis due to inability to mount stress cortisol response.
  1. A. T4 (5-7 days) — T4 bound to TBG has the longest half-life (5-7 days) compared to T3 (1 day), cortisol (60-90 min), insulin (5 min), ACTH (10 min).
  1. A. Hashimoto's thyroiditis — Anti-TPO antibodies are present in 90-95% of Hashimoto's, ~80% of Graves, and postpartum thyroiditis.
  1. A. 17α-hydroxylase deficiency — Impaired sex steroid synthesis (sexual infantilism) with mineralocorticoid excess (DOC) causing hypertension and hypokalaemia.
  1. A. PNMT converts noradrenaline to adrenaline in adrenal medulla — PNMT is adrenal medulla-specific (cortisol-induced), distinguishing adrenal from extra-adrenal phaeochromocytoma.
  1. A. Peripheral T4 to T3 conversion (DIOs) — Deiodinases (DIO1, DIO2) are selenoenzymes; selenium deficiency impairs peripheral T4→T3 conversion.
  1. A. Low albumin → low total T4, free T4 normal — Nephrotic syndrome reduces albumin (low-affinity thyroid hormone carrier), lowering total T4 but free T4 and TSH remain normal.

B. Steroid hormones

C. Thyroid hormones

D. Catecholamines

  1. What is the rate-limiting step in steroid hormone synthesis?

B. Pregnenolone → 17-OH Pregnenolone

C. 11-Deoxycortisol → Cortisol

D. DOC → Aldosterone

B. CYP17A1

C. CYP11B1

B. 1 day; TBG

19. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 30-year-old woman on combined OCP has total T4 180 nmol/L (ref 60-160), TSH 1.5 mIU/L. Explanation?

A. Increased TBG → elevated total T4, free T4 normal

B. Primary hyperthyroidism

C. TSH-secreting adenoma

D. Thyroid hormone resistance

E. Exogenous thyroxine intake

  1. Patient with suspected insulinoma has low glucose. Best test for endogenous hyperinsulinaemia?

A. C-peptide level

B. Proinsulin level

C. Insulin antibodies

D. HbA1c

E. Fasting insulin

  1. In 21-hydroxylase deficiency, which steroid causes virilisation?

A. 17-OH Progesterone → Androstenedione/Testosterone

B. DOC → Aldosterone

C. Cortisol

D. Corticosterone

E. 11-Deoxycortisol

  1. Patient on high-dose prednisolone for 6 months stops abruptly. Main risk?

A. Adrenal crisis (HPA suppression)

B. Hyperglycaemia

C. Hypertension

D. Osteoporosis

E. Electrolyte imbalance

  1. Which hormone has the longest half-life?

A. T4 (5-7 days)

B. T3 (1 day)

C. Cortisol (60-90 min)

D. Insulin (5 min)

E. ACTH (10 min)

  1. Anti-TPO antibodies are most associated with?

A. Hashimoto's thyroiditis

B. Graves disease

C. Thyroid cancer

D. Subacute thyroiditis

E. Toxic nodular goitre

  1. 25yo male with hypertension, hypokalaemia, elevated 17-OH progesterone. Diagnosis?

A. 17α-hydroxylase deficiency

B. 11β-hydroxylase deficiency

C. 21-hydroxylase deficiency

D. Aldosterone synthase deficiency

E. 17β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase deficiency

  1. Correct statement about catecholamine synthesis?

A. PNMT converts noradrenaline to adrenaline in adrenal medulla

B. Tyrosine hydroxylase is not rate-limiting

C. Dopamine β-hydroxylase is in cytoplasm

D. MAO is the main clearance pathway

E. COMT converts dopamine to adrenaline

  1. Selenium deficiency impairs?

A. Peripheral T4 to T3 conversion (DIOs)

B. Iodide uptake (NIS)

C. Iodination (TPO)

D. TSH receptor signalling

E. Thyroglobulin synthesis

  1. Nephrotic syndrome: low total T4, normal TSH. Why?

A. Low albumin → low total T4, free T4 normal

B. Primary hypothyroidism

C. Central hypothyroidism

D. Non-thyroidal illness

E. TSH-secreting adenoma

20. Flashcards

  • Q: Peptide hormone synthesis

A: Preprohormone → Prohormone (ER) → Active hormone + C-peptide (Golgi) → Granules → Regulated secretion (Ca2+)

  • Q: Steroid synthesis rate-limiting

A: Cholesterol → Pregnenolone via STAR + CYP11A1 (P450scc) in inner mitochondrial membrane

  • Q: 21-hydroxylase deficiency (95% CAH)

A: ↓ Cortisol, ↓ Aldosterone → ↑ 17-OH Progesterone → ↑ Androgens → Salt-wasting + Virilisation

  • Q: 11β-hydroxylase deficiency

A: ↓ Cortisol → ↑ 11-Deoxycortisol + ↑ DOC (mineralocorticoid) → HTN + Virilisation

  • Q: 17α-hydroxylase deficiency

A: ↓ Sex steroids → Sexual infantilism; ↑ Mineralocorticoids (DOC) → HTN

  • Q: T4 synthesis steps

A: Iodide → NIS → TPO (iodination + coupling on Thyroglobulin) → Colloid storage → TSH → Endocytosis → T4/T3 release

  • Q: Deiodinases

A: DIO1/DIO2 (Se-dependent): T4→T3 activation; DIO3: Inactivation (rT3, T2)

  • Q: Catecholamine pathway

A: Tyrosine → DOPA → Dopamine → Noradrenaline → (PNMT, cortisol-induced) → Adrenaline

  • Q: Transport proteins

A: CBG: Cortisol (~90%); SHBG: Testosterone/Oestradiol; TBG: T4>T3 (99.97%); Albumin: Low affinity carrier

  • Q: Free hormone hypothesis

A: Only free (unbound) hormone is biologically active; Total hormone misleading if binding proteins altered

  • Q: Half-lives

A: Peptides: 3-30 min; Steroids: 60-90 min; T4: 5-7 d; T3: 1 d; Catecholamines: min

  • Q: STAR protein

A: Cholesterol transfer to inner mitochondrial membrane; Mutations → Lipoid CAH (lethal salt-wasting)

  • Q: Anti-TPO antibodies

A: Hashimoto's (90-95%), Graves (80%), Postpartum thyroiditis; PTU inhibits TPO

  • Q: C-peptide vs Insulin

A: C-peptide: Endogenous secretion marker, t½ 30 min, no hepatic extraction; Insulin: t½ 5 min

21. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Peptide/protein hormones (e.g., insulin, GH, ACTH) — Which hormone class is synthesised as a preprohormone in the rough ER?
  1. Cholesterol → Pregnenolone (STAR + CYP11A1) — What is the rate-limiting step in steroid hormone synthesis?
  1. CYP21A2 — Which enzyme deficiency causes 21-hydroxylase deficiency (95% of CAH)?
  1. 5-7 days; TBG — What is the half-life of T4 and its main binding protein?
  1. TBG, CBG, SHBG (all increased) — Which transport protein is increased in pregnancy and OCP use?
  1. Endogenous insulin secretion — C-peptide is a marker of what?
  1. DIO1 and DIO2 — Which deiodinase activates T4 to T3?
  1. CYP17A1 — 17,20-lyase activity is part of which enzyme?
  1. Cortisol — PNMT is induced by what?
  1. Only unbound hormone is biologically active — Free hormone hypothesis states:

SBAs

  1. Increased TBG → elevated total T4, free T4 normal — A 30-year-old woman on combined OCP has total T4 180 nmol/L (ref 60-160), TSH 1.5 mIU/L. Explanation?
  1. C-peptide level — Patient with suspected insulinoma has low glucose. Best test for endogenous hyperinsulinaemia?
  1. 17-OH Progesterone → Androstenedione/Testosterone — In 21-hydroxylase deficiency, which steroid causes virilisation?
  1. Adrenal crisis (HPA suppression) — Patient on high-dose prednisolone for 6 months stops abruptly. Main risk?
  1. T4 (5-7 days) — Which hormone has the longest half-life?
  1. Hashimoto's thyroiditis — Anti-TPO antibodies are most associated with?
  1. 17α-hydroxylase deficiency — 25yo male with hypertension, hypokalaemia, elevated 17-OH progesterone. Diagnosis?
  1. PNMT converts noradrenaline to adrenaline in adrenal medulla — Correct statement about catecholamine synthesis?
  1. Peripheral T4 to T3 conversion (DIOs) — Selenium deficiency impairs?
  1. Low albumin → low total T4, free T4 normal — Nephrotic syndrome: low total T4, normal TSH. Why?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.11 20.11 7
▸ 20.11.01 Endocrine Complications Of Immunotherapy
[!info]
Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor (ICI) Endocrine Toxicities (irAEs) = Autoimmune Endocrinopathies Triggered by ICIs. Anti-CTLA-4 (Ipilimumab) > Anti-PD-1/PD-L1 for Hypophysitis; Anti-PD-1/PD-L1 > Anti-CTLA-4 for Thyroiditis, Diabetes, Adrenal Insufficiency. Hormone Replacement = Mainstay (Not Immunosuppression for Hormone Deficiency).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise ICI-related endocrine irAEs (Hypophysitis, Thyroiditis, Adrenal Insufficiency, Type 1 Diabetes)
  • [ ] Apply grading (CTCAE) and management algorithms
  • [ ] Differentiate primary vs secondary adrenal insufficiency in ICI context
  • [ ] Apply monitoring schedules for patients on ICIs
  • [ ] Manage ICI continuation vs hold vs discontinuation

2. ICI Endocrine Toxicities — Overview

Agent ClassCommonest Endocrine irAEIncidenceOnset
Anti-CTLA-4 (Ipilimumab)Hypophysitis5-15% (High)6-12 Weeks
Anti-PD-1/PD-L1 (Nivolumab, Pembrolizumab, Atezolizumab)Thyroiditis (Hyper → Hypo)5-10%6-18 Weeks
Anti-PD-1/PD-L1Primary Adrenal Insufficiency1-5%8-20 Weeks
Anti-PD-1/PD-L1Type 1 Diabetes (Autoimmune)<1%Variable
Combination (CTLA-4 + PD-1)Higher Rates (All Endocrine)20-30% (Any)Earlier

3. Hypophysitis (Anti-CTLA-4 Predominant)

Clinical Features

FeatureDetails
Onset6-12 Weeks After ICI Initiation
SymptomsHeadache (Bifrontal), Fatigue, Visual Disturbance, Nausea, Polydipsia/Polyuria (DI)
SignsVisual Field Defects (Bitemporal), Cranial Nerve Palsies

Biochemical Pattern

HormonePattern
ACTH/CortisolFirst Affected (Central Adrenal Insufficiency) → Low/Inappropriately Normal ACTH, Low Cortisol
TSH/fT4Central Hypothyroidism → Low/Normal TSH, Low fT4
LH/FSHCentral Hypogonadism → Low/Normal LH/FSH, Low Sex Steroids
ProlactinVariable (May Be High/Normal/Low)

MRI Findings

FindingDescription
Pituitary EnlargementHomogeneous Enhancement (Inflammatory)
Stalk Thickening>2-3mm
Mass EffectOptic Chiasm Compression (Visual Fields)

Grading & Management (CTCAE v5.0)

GradeClinicalManagement
G1Asymptomatic; Biochemical OnlyContinue ICI; Hormone Replacement; Monitor q2-4wk
G2Symptomatic (Mild); No End-Organ DysfunctionHold ICI; Hormone Replacement; Prednisolone 0.5-1mg/kg if Symptomatic
G3Severe; End-Organ Dysfunction (Adrenal Crisis Risk)Discontinue ICI; IV Hydrocortisone 100mg q6h → Oral Taper; High-Dose Steroids (Pred 1-2mg/kg)
G4Life-Threatening (Adrenal Crisis, Coma)Discontinue ICI; ICU; IV Hydrocortisone 100mg q6h; High-Dose Steroids

Hormone Replacement Protocol

AxisReplacementNotes
Adrenal (AI)Hydrocortisone 15-20mg AM + 5-10mg PMSTRESS DOSES for Illness/Surgery
ThyroidLevothyroxine 1.6µg/kg/dayStart after Cortisol Replacement (Else Crisis)
GonadalTestosterone / Oestrogen + ProgestogenIf Symptomatic / Fertility Desired

ICI Management

SeverityICI ActionSteroid
G1ContinueNone
G2HoldPrednisolone 0.5-1mg/kg (If Symptomatic)
G3/G4DiscontinueIV Hydrocortisone 100mg q6h → Oral Taper (4-12 Weeks)

4. Thyroiditis (Anti-PD-1/PD-L1 Predominant)

Natural History

PhaseTimingTSHfT4/fT3TRAb
Thyrotoxic6-12 WeeksSuppressed (<0.01)HighNegative
Hypothyroid12-24 WeeksHighLowNegative
Permanent Hypo20-40%HighLowNegative
Key: TRAb Negative (vs Graves); Painless (vs Subacute Thyroiditis).

Clinical Features

PhaseSymptoms
ThyrotoxicPalpitations, Tremor, Weight Loss, Anxiety, Heat Intolerance
HypothyroidFatigue, Weight Gain, Cold Intolerance, Constipation, Depression

Management

PhaseTreatment
ThyrotoxicBeta-Blocker (Propranolol 20-40mg TDS) for Symptom Control; No Antithyroid Drugs (Destructive)
HypothyroidLevothyroxine 1.6µg/kg/day (Start Low 25-50µg in Elderly)
SubclinicalMonitor q6-8wk; Treat if TSH >10 or Symptomatic

Grading & ICI Management (CTCAE)

GradeThyroid FunctionICI Action
G1Asymptomatic; fT4 NormalContinue ICI
G2Symptomatic Thyrotoxic / HypoContinue ICI (May Hold if Severe)
G3Severe Thyrotoxic (AF, HF) / Severe Hypo (Myxoedema)Hold ICI → Consider Steroids (Pred 0.5-1mg/kg)
G4Life-Threatening (Thyroid Storm / Myxoedema Coma)Discontinue ICI → ICU + Steroids

5. Primary Adrenal Insufficiency (Anti-PD-1/PD-L1)

FeatureDetails
Incidence1-5% (Higher with Combo)
Onset8-20 Weeks
MechanismAutoimmune Adrenalitis (Anti-Adrenal Antibodies)
BiochemistryLow Cortisol, High ACTH, Hyperkalaemia, Hyponatraemia
ImagingAdrenal Enlargement (Early) → Atrophy (Late)

Management

SeverityManagement
G1Monitor; Continue ICI
G2Hydrocortisone 15-20mg AM + 5-10mg PM; Hold ICI
G3IV Hydrocortisone 100mg q6h; Discontinue ICI
G4Adrenal Crisis Protocol (IV Hydrocortisone 100mg q6h + Fluids); Discontinue ICI

6. Type 1 Diabetes (ICI-Induced)

FeatureDetails
Incidence<1% (Rare but Critical)
MechanismAutoimmune β-Cell Destruction (Anti-GAD, IA-2, ZnT8+)
PresentationAcute Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) Often First Presentation
OnsetVariable (Weeks-Months)
BiochemistryAutoantibodies Positive (GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8); C-Peptide Low

Management

ScenarioManagement
DKA at PresentationStandard DKA Protocol (Fluids, Insulin, K⁺); ICI Hold/Discontinue
New-Onset DiabetesInsulin Initiation (Basal-Bolus); Discontinue ICI (Usually)
AutoantibodiesGAD65, IA-2, ZnT8 Positive (Confirm Autoimmune)

7. ICI Monitoring Schedule

TimepointTests
Baseline (Pre-ICI)TSH, fT4, fT3, Cortisol (9am), ACTH, Glucose, HbA1c, Electrolytes
Every 3-4 Weeks (During ICI)TSH, fT4, Cortisol (9am), Glucose, Electrolytes
At Symptom OnsetFull Endocrine Panel (TSH, fT4, fT3, Cortisol, ACTH, Prolactin, LH/FSH, Testosterone/Oestradiol, Glucose, HbA1c, Electrolytes)
Post-ICI Completionq3mo × 1yr, then q6mo

8. Management Algorithm — ICI Endocrine irAEs

ICI Patient + New Endocrine Symptoms / Abnormal Labs
         │
         ▼
IDENTIFY AXIS: HPA / HPT / HPG / ADRENAL / PANCREAS
         │
         ▼
GRADE (CTCAE v5.0)
         │
         ├── G1: Asymptomatic / Biochemical Only
         │     → CONTINUE ICI; Hormone Replacement; Monitor q2-4wk
         │
         ├── G2: Symptomatic / Mild End-Organ Dysfunction
         │     → HOLD ICI; Hormone Replacement; Prednisolone 0.5-1mg/kg (If Symptomatic)
         │
         ├── G3: Severe / End-Organ Dysfunction
         │     → DISCONTINUE ICI; IV Hydrocortisone 100mg q6h; High-Dose Steroids
         │
         └── G4: Life-Threatening
               → DISCONTINUE ICI; ICU; IV Hydrocortisone 100mg q6h; High-Dose Steroids

9. Steroid Tapering Protocol (Post-irAE)

PhaseDoseDuration
InductionPrednisolone 1-2mg/kg/dayUntil Symptoms Resolve + Hormones Stabilised
TaperReduce 10mg q1-2wk to 20mg4-8 Weeks
Slow Taper2.5-5mg q2-4wk to 5mg8-12 Weeks
PhysiologicalHydrocortisone 15-20mg AM + 5-10mg PMLifelong if Permanent AI
Key: Taper Slowly to Avoid Adrenal Crisis; Faster Taper if ICI Rechallenge Planned.

10. ICI Rechallenge After irAE

ScenarioDecision
Endocrine G1/G2Consider Rechallenge (If Hormones Stable on Replacement)
Endocrine G3/G4Generally Avoid Rechallenge (High Recurrence Risk)
Non-Endocrine irAE G3/G4Generally Avoid Rechallenge
Pre-RechallengeHormones Stable on Replacement; No Acute Symptoms; MDT Discussion

11. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Anti-CTLA-4Hypophysitis (Commonest Endocrine irAE)
Anti-PD-1/PD-L1Thyroiditis (Commonest), Primary Adrenal Insufficiency, Type 1 DM
HypophysitisHeadache + Central AI (Low Cortisol/Normal ACTH); MRI: Pituitary Enlargement
Thyroiditis (ICI)Painless; TRAb Negative; Thyrotoxic → Hypothyroid Sequence
Adrenal Insufficiency (ICI)Primary (High ACTH, Low Cortisol); Primary (Autoimmune)
Type 1 DM (ICI)DKA Often First Presentation; Autoantibodies +; C-Peptide Low
Hypophysitis GradingG1: Continue ICI; G2: Hold; G3/G4: Discontinue
Thyroiditis GradingG1/G2: Continue; G3: Hold; G4: Discontinue
Adrenal InsufficiencyPrimary (High ACTH); Hydrocortisone 15-20mg AM + 5-10mg PM
Thyroiditis PatternTRAb Negative; Thyrotoxic → Hypothyroid
ICI Hold/DiscontinueG2: Hold; G3/G4: Discontinue
Steroid TaperSlow (4-12+ Weeks); Physiological Replacement if Permanent
MonitoringBaseline + q3-4wk (TSH, fT4, Cortisol, ACTH, Glucose)
Key DifferenceHypophysitis = CTLA-4; Thyroiditis/Adrenal AI/DM = PD-1/PD-L1
RechallengeG1/G2 Endocrine: Consider; G3/G4: Generally Avoid
Steroid TaperSlow (4-12+ Weeks); Hydrocortisone 15-20mg AM + 5-10mg PM

12. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Hypophysitis vs Primary Adrenal AIHypophysitis = Central (Low/Normal ACTH); Primary AI = High ACTH
ICI Thyroiditis vs GravesICI Thyroiditis = TRAb Negative, Painless, Thyrotoxic→Hypo Sequence
ICI Thyroiditis vs SubacuteICI = Painless, TRAb(-); Subacute = Painful, TRAb(-)
Hypophysitis ACTH PatternCentral AI: Low/Inappropriately Normal ACTH, Low Cortisol
Primary AI ACTH PatternPrimary: High ACTH, Low Cortisol, Hyperkalaemia, Hyponatraemia
Steroid for HypophysitisHigh-Dose for Acute (Pred 1-2mg/kg); Then Taper
ICI ManagementG1/G2: Hold/Continue; G3/G4: Discontinue
Thyroiditis SequenceThyrotoxic (Weeks 6-12) → Hypothyroid (Weeks 12-24) → Permanent (20-40%)
Thyroiditis G3Severe Thyrotoxic (AF, HF) / Severe Hypo (Myxoedema)
Type 1 DM ICIDKA Often First Presentation; Autoantibodies +; C-Peptide Low
Steroid TaperSlow (4-12+ Weeks); Physiological Replacement if Permanent
RechallengeG1/G2 Endocrine: Consider; G3/G4: Generally Avoid

13. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((ICI Endocrine irAEs))
    Anti-CTLA-4 (Ipilimumab)
      Hypophysitis (5-15%)
        Headache, Central AI (Low Cortisol/Normal ACTH)
        MRI: Pituitary Enlargement/Stalk Thickening
        Grading: G1-Continue; G2-Hold; G3/4-Discontinue
      Thyroiditis: Less Common
    Anti-PD-1/PD-L1
      Thyroiditis (5-10%)
        Painless, TRAb(-); Thyrotoxic → Hypo
        Beta-blocker (Thyrotoxic); LT4 (Hypo)
      Primary Adrenal AI (1-5%)
        High ACTH, Low Cortisol
        HC 15-20mg AM + 5-10mg PM
      Type 1 DM (<1%)
        DKA Presentation; AutoAb+; C-pep Low
        Insulin + Discontinue ICI
    Combination (CTLA-4 + PD-1)
      Higher Rates (20-30% Endocrine)
      Earlier Onset
    Grading & Management
      G1: Continue ICI
      G2: Hold ICI + Hormone Replacement ± Pred 0.5-1mg/kg
      G3/4: Discontinue + IV HC 100mg q6h + High-Dose Steroids
    Steroid Taper
      Pred 1-2mg/kg → Taper 4-12wk → Physiological HC
    Monitoring
      Baseline + q3-4wk (TSH, fT4, Cortisol, ACTH, Glucose)
    Rechallenge
      G1/2 Endocrine: Consider if Stable on Replacement
      G3/4: Avoid

14. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Anti-CTLA-4 Commonest irAEHypophysitis (5-15%)
Anti-PD-1/PD-L1 Commonest irAEThyroiditis (5-10%)
Hypophysitis PresentationHeadache + Central AI (Low Cortisol, Normal ACTH)
Hypophysitis MRIPituitary Enlargement; Stalk Thickening >2-3mm
ICI ThyroiditisPainless, TRAb Negative; Thyrotoxic → Hypothyroid Sequence
ICI Adrenal AIPrimary (High ACTH, Low Cortisol, Hyperkalaemia)
ICI Type 1 DMDKA Often First Presentation; Autoantibodies +; C-Peptide Low
Hypophysitis GradingG1: Continue ICI; G2: Hold; G3/G4: Discontinue
Thyroiditis GradingG1/G2: Continue; G3: Hold; G4: Discontinue
Thyroiditis PatternTRAb Negative; Thyrotoxic (6-12wk) → Hypo (12-24wk)
Steroid TaperSlow 4-12 Weeks; Physiological Replacement if Permanent
Adrenal AI ReplacementHydrocortisone 15-20mg AM + 5-10mg PM
ICI Type 1 DMDKA Often First Presentation; AutoAb +; C-Peptide Low
Steroid for HypophysitisHigh-Dose (Pred 1-2mg/kg) for G3/G4
Thyroiditis DKANot Typical; DKA = ICI Type 1 DM
MonitoringBaseline + q3-4wk (TSH, fT4, Cortisol, ACTH, Glucose)

15. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Special Topics|Special Topics in Endocrinology]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Hypophysitis]], [[Thyroiditis]], [[Adrenal Crisis]], [[Type 1 Diabetes]], [[Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors]]

16. MCQs (10)

  1. Checkpoint inhibitor-induced hypophysitis most commonly affects which cell type?

A. Corticotrophs (ACTH deficiency)

B. Thyrotrophs (TSH deficiency)

C. Somatotrophs (GH deficiency)

D. Gonadotrophs (LH/FSH deficiency)

E. Lactotrophs (prolactin)

  1. Ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4) hypophysitis typically presents at what median time?

A. 6-12 weeks

B. 1-2 weeks

C. 3-4 weeks

D. 6 months

E. 1 year

  1. Anti-PD-1/PD-L1 (nivolumab, pembrolizumab) thyroid dysfunction is most commonly:

A. Thyrotoxicosis (destructive thyroiditis) → hypothyroidism

B. Primary hypothyroidism only

C. Graves disease

D. Central hypothyroidism

E. Euthyroid sick syndrome

  1. Checkpoint inhibitor-induced adrenal insufficiency: which axis is primarily affected?

A. Primary adrenal (cortisol low, ACTH high)

B. Secondary (ACTH low)

C. Tertiary

D. Isolated mineralocorticoid deficiency

E. No adrenal involvement

  1. Anti-PD-1 thyroiditis: typical time to onset after treatment initiation:

A. 6-12 weeks

B. 1-2 weeks

C. 3-4 months

D. 6 months

E. >1 year

  1. Management of Grade 2 (moderate) checkpoint inhibitor hypophysitis:

A. Hold ICI + high-dose glucocorticoids (prednisolone 1-2 mg/kg)

B. Continue ICI + symptomatic Rx

C. Permanent discontinuation only

D. Thyroxine replacement only

E. Octreotide

  1. ICI-induced type 1 diabetes mellitus: characteristic presentation:

A. Acute onset DKA, low C-peptide, positive autoantibodies (GAD65, IA-2)

B. Gradual onset, insulin resistance

C. Ketosis-prone type 2

D. Drug-induced (steroid)

E. MODY

  1. Anti-CTLA-4 (ipilimumab) vs anti-PD-1: which has higher hypophysitis risk?

A. Ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4) > anti-PD-1

B. Anti-PD-1 > anti-CTLA-4

C. Equal risk

D. No difference

E. Only combination therapy

  1. ICI-induced primary hypothyroidism: management:

A. Levothyroxine replacement; continue ICI if asymptomatic

B. Permanent ICI discontinuation

C. High-dose steroids

D. Radioactive iodine

E. Thionamides

  1. ICI endocrinopathy monitoring baseline and on-treatment:

A. TSH, fT4, cortisol (8am), ACTH, glucose, HbA1c q4-6wk

B. Only TSH

C. Only cortisol

D. Only glucose

E. No monitoring needed

17. SBA Questions (5)

  1. 60yo man on pembrolizumab for melanoma: week 8, fatigue, nausea, hypotension, hyponatraemia. AM cortisol 80 nmol/L, ACTH undetectable. TSH 0.1, fT4 8. Management?

A. Hydrocortisone 100mg IV q6h → prednisolone 1-2mg/kg; hold pembrolizumab; thyroxine after cortisol replacement

B. Levothyroxine only

C. Fludrocortisone only

D. Continue pembrolizumab

E. Dexamethasone

  1. 55yo woman on ipilimumab+nivolumab: week 10, neck pain, thyrotoxic symptoms. TSH <0.01, fT4 45, fT3 12, ESR 60. Thyroid uptake low. Next?

A. Beta-blocker + prednisolone 0.5-1mg/kg (destructive thyroiditis); hold ICI

B. Carbimazole

C. Radioactive iodine

D. Thyroidectomy

E. Observation only

  1. 45yo man on nivolumab: new-onset polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, glucose 28, ketones 4+, pH 7.15, C-peptide 0.1, GAD65+. Diagnosis?

A. ICI-induced type 1 DM with DKA

B. Type 2 DM

C. Steroid-induced DM

D. Pancreatic cancer DM

E. MODY

  1. 70yo woman on atezolizumab: routine bloods week 12: TSH 18, fT4 8, asymptomatic. Antibodies negative. Management?

A. Levothyroxine 50-100mcg OD; continue atezolizumab

B. Hold atezolizumab + prednisolone

C. Radioactive iodine

D. Repeat in 4 weeks

E. Refer for thyroidectomy

  1. Which ICI combination has HIGHEST risk of endocrinopathies?

A. Ipilimumab + nivolumab (anti-CTLA-4 + anti-PD-1)

B. Nivolumab monotherapy

C. Pembrolizumab monotherapy

D. Atezolizumab monotherapy

E. Durvalumab monotherapy

18. Flashcards

  • Q: ICI hypophysitis (anti-CTLA-4)

A: Ipilimumab: 5-10%; diffuse pituitary enlargement → ACTH/TSH/LH/FSH deficiency; 6-12 wks

  • Q: ICI thyroiditis (anti-PD-1)

A: Nivolumab/pembrolizumab: 5-10%; destructive thyroiditis → thyrotoxicosis → hypothyroidism; 6-12 wks

  • Q: ICI adrenal insufficiency

A: Primary adrenalitis (cortisol↓, ACTH↑) or secondary (hypophysitis); check 8am cortisol, ACTH

  • Q: ICI type 1 DM

A: Acute onset, DKA common, low C-peptide, positive GAD65/IA-2/ZnT8; anti-PD-1 > anti-CTLA-4

  • Q: ICI hypophysitis Rx

A: Hold ICI; hydrocortisone 100mg IV q6h → pred 1-2mg/kg taper; thyroxine AFTER cortisol

  • Q: ICI thyroiditis Rx

A: Thyrotoxic phase: beta-blocker ± steroids; hypothyroid phase: levothyroxine; often continue ICI

  • Q: ICI adrenal Rx

A: Hydrocortisone 15-25mg/day (10/5/5 or 10/5); fludrocortisone if primary; hold ICI if Grade ≥3

  • Q: ICI DM Rx

A: Insulin mandatory; hold ICI if DKA; consider restarting after stabilisation with specialist input

  • Q: Monitoring

A: Baseline: TSH, fT4, 8am cortisol, ACTH, glucose, HbA1c, electrolytes; q4-6wk on treatment

  • Q: Grade toxicity

A: G1: asymptomatic; G2: symptomatic, hold ICI; G3: severe, hold + high-dose steroids; G4: life-threatening, permanent stop

  • Q: Anti-CTLA-4 vs PD-1

A: CTLA-4: higher hypophysitis, colitis; PD-1: higher thyroiditis, pneumonitis, diabetes

  • Q: Combination ICI

A: Ipi+Nivo: highest endocrine toxicity (hypophysitis 10-15%, thyroiditis 15-20%, adrenal 5%)

  • Q: Rechallenge

A: After Grade 2: consider restart if controlled on hormone replacement + steroids tapered

  • Q: Primary vs secondary AI

A: Primary: cortisol↓ ACTH↑ hyperkalaemia hyponatraemia; Secondary: cortisol↓ ACTH↓/normal euK

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Corticotrophs (ACTH deficiency) — Checkpoint inhibitor-induced hypophysitis most commonly affects which cell type?
  1. 6-12 weeks — Ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4) hypophysitis typically presents at what median time?
  1. Thyrotoxicosis (destructive thyroiditis) → hypothyroidism — Anti-PD-1/PD-L1 (nivolumab, pembrolizumab) thyroid dysfunction is most commonly:
  1. Primary adrenal (cortisol low, ACTH high) — Checkpoint inhibitor-induced adrenal insufficiency: which axis is primarily affected?
  1. 6-12 weeks — Anti-PD-1 thyroiditis: typical time to onset after treatment initiation:
  1. Hold ICI + high-dose glucocorticoids (prednisolone 1-2 mg/kg) — Management of Grade 2 (moderate) checkpoint inhibitor hypophysitis:
  1. Acute onset DKA, low C-peptide, positive autoantibodies (GAD65, IA-2) — ICI-induced type 1 diabetes mellitus: characteristic presentation:
  1. Ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4) > anti-PD-1 — Anti-CTLA-4 (ipilimumab) vs anti-PD-1: which has higher hypophysitis risk?
  1. Levothyroxine replacement; continue ICI if asymptomatic — ICI-induced primary hypothyroidism: management:
  1. TSH, fT4, cortisol (8am), ACTH, glucose, HbA1c q4-6wk — ICI endocrinopathy monitoring baseline and on-treatment:

SBAs

  1. Hydrocortisone 100mg IV q6h → prednisolone 1-2mg/kg; hold pembrolizumab; thyroxine after cortisol replacement — 60yo man on pembrolizumab for melanoma: week 8, fatigue, nausea, hypotension, hyponatraemia. AM cortisol 80 nmol/L, ACTH undetectable. TSH 0.1, fT4 8. Management?
  1. Beta-blocker + prednisolone 0.5-1mg/kg (destructive thyroiditis); hold ICI — 55yo woman on ipilimumab+nivolumab: week 10, neck pain, thyrotoxic symptoms. TSH <0.01, fT4 45, fT3 12, ESR 60. Thyroid uptake low. Next?
  1. ICI-induced type 1 DM with DKA — 45yo man on nivolumab: new-onset polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, glucose 28, ketones 4+, pH 7.15, C-peptide 0.1, GAD65+. Diagnosis?
  1. Levothyroxine 50-100mcg OD; continue atezolizumab — 70yo woman on atezolizumab: routine bloods week 12: TSH 18, fT4 8, asymptomatic. Antibodies negative. Management?
  1. Ipilimumab + nivolumab (anti-CTLA-4 + anti-PD-1) — Which ICI combination has HIGHEST risk of endocrinopathies?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.11.02 Endocrine In Ckd And Dialysis
[!info]
CKD = Systemic Endocrine Disorder. CKD-MBD (Mineral and Bone Disorder) is universal in G4-5. Glucose, Adrenal, Thyroid, Gonadal Axes All Affected. Dialysis Adds Unique Challenges (Clearance, Fluid Shifts, Catabolism).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply CKD-MBD management algorithm (KDIGO 2024)
  • [ ] Select and sequence phosphate binders, vitamin D analogues, calcimimetics
  • [ ] Manage glucose, thyroid, adrenal, gonadal axes in CKD 4-5D
  • [ ] Apply dialysis-specific endocrine adjustments
  • [ ] Recognise and manage calciphylaxis and adynamic bone disease

2. CKD-Mineral and Bone Disorder (CKD-MBD) — KDIGO 2024

Pathophysiology — The Vicious Cycle

CKD ↓ GFR
    │
    → Phosphate Retention → FGF23 ↑ → 1α-Hydroxylase Inhibition → 1,25-(OH)₂D ↓
    │
    → Hypocalcaemia → PTH ↑↑ (Secondary HPT)
    │
    → Bone Resorption ↑ → High Turnover Bone Disease
    │
    → Vascular Calcification (Ca × P > 4.5)
    │
    → Adynamic Bone (Over-Suppression) ↔ High Turnover (High PTH)

KDIGO 2024 Targets (CKD G3-5D)

ParameterTarget (CKD 3-5D)Notes
PTH2-9x ULN (Avoid <2x = Adynamic)Dialysis: 2-9x ULN
Serum Calcium2.1-2.5 mmol/L (Corrected)Avoid >2.6; Avoid <2.1
Phosphate0.8-1.5 mmol/L (G3-4)Dialysis: 1.1-1.5 mmol/L
Ca × P Product<4.5 mmol²/L²<4.5 = Lower CV Risk
25-OH Vitamin D≥30 ng/mL (75 nmol/L)Supplement if Low
Alkaline PhosphataseMonitor TrendMarker of Bone Turnover

3. CKD-MBD Management Algorithm (KDIGO 2024)

CKD G3-5 / Dialysis + Abnormal Ca/Phos/PTH
         │
         ▼
STEP 1: DIETARY PHOSPHATE RESTRICTION (800-1000 mg/day)
         │
         ▼
STEP 2: PHOSPHATE BINDERS (With Meals)
         ├── CALCIUM-BASED (Carbonate/Acetate)
         │       1st Line if Ca²⁺ Normal/Low; Cheap
         │       Max 1.5-2g Elemental Ca/day
         │
         ├── NON-CALCIUM (Sevelamer, Lanthanum, Sucroferric Oxyhydroxide, Ferric Citrate)
         │       1st Line if Ca²⁺ High / Vascular Calcification / Adynamic Bone
         │       Sevelamer 800-2400mg TDS; Lanthanum 750-2250mg/day
         │
         └── FERRIC CITRATE (Newer; Also Treats Anaemia)
                 Dose: 1g TDS with Meals
         │
         ▼
STEP 3: VITAMIN D ANALOGUES (If PTH >9x ULN Despite Phosphate Control)
         ├── ALFACALCIDOL (1α-OH D3) 0.25-1µg/day
         ├── CALCITRIOL 0.25-1µg/day
         └── PARICALCITOL (Selective VDR) 1-2µg 3x/week (Post-HD)
         │   Advantage: Less Hypercalcaemia/Hyperphosphataemia
         │
         ▼
STEP 4: CALCIMIMETICS (If PTH >9x ULN Despite Above)
         │
         ├── CINACALCET 30-180mg/day (BD) — CaSR Agonist
         │       Monitor: Ca²⁺, PTH q1-2mo; Hypocalcaemia Risk
         └── ETELCALCETIDE (IV, Post-HD) 5-15mg 3x/week (HD Only)
                 Monitor Ca²⁺, PTH Monthly

4. Phosphate Binder Selection Algorithm

Patient Profile1st Line BinderAlternative
Normal Calcium, No Vascular CalcificationCalcium Carbonate/Acetate (Cheap)—
Hypercalcaemia / Vascular Calcification / Adynamic BoneSevelamer / Lanthanum / Ferric CitrateSucroferric Oxyhydroxide
Dialysis + HyperphosphataemiaSevelamer / Lanthanum / Ferric CitrateFerric Citrate (Also Treats Anaemia)
Low CalciumCalcium-Based (Calcium Carbonate/Acetate)—
Key Principle: Avoid Calcium-Based Binders if Ca²⁺ High or Vascular Calcification Present.

5. Vitamin D Analogues in CKD

AgentIndicationDoseAdvantage
Alfacalcidol (1α-OH D3)CKD G3-50.25-1µg/dayRapid 1α-Hydroxylation; Reliable
Calcitriol (1,25-(OH)₂D₃)CKD G4-5, Dialysis0.25-1µg/dayActive Form; Short t½
ParicalcitolCKD G4-5D / Dialysis1-2µg 3x/week (Post-HD)Selective VDR → Less Hypercalcaemia/Phosphataemia
DoxercalciferolCKD G4-510-30µg TIW1α-OH D2 Analogue
Key: Nutritional D2/D3 (Cholecalciferol/Ergocalciferol) INSUFFICIENT in CKD G4-5/Dialysis — Requires Renal 1α-Hydroxylation.

6. Monitoring Targets (KDIGO)

ParameterCKD G3-4CKD G5 / Dialysis
PTH2-9x ULN (q3-6mo)2-9x ULN (Monthly)
Corrected Calcium2.1-2.5 mmol/L (q3-6mo)2.1-2.5 mmol/L (Monthly)
Phosphate0.8-1.5 mmol/L (q3-6mo)1.1-1.5 mmol/L (Monthly)
Ca × P Product<4.5 mmol²/L²<4.5 mmol²/L²
25-OH Vit D≥30 ng/mL (q6-12mo)≥30 ng/mL (q6mo)
Alkaline PhosphataseAnnual (Bone Turnover)q3-6mo

7. Adynamic Bone Disease — Over-Suppression

FeatureRecognitionManagement
CluePTH <2x ULN (or <100 pg/mL) + Low ALP + Low Bone TurnoverStop/Reduce Vitamin D Analogues & Calcimimetics
RiskFracture ↑ (Low Bone Turnover), ↑ Vascular CalcificationReduce/Stop Vit D Analogues & Calcimimetics
DiagnosisBone Biopsy (Gold Standard); Low PTH + Low ALPPTH Target 2-9x ULN (Not Lower)

8. Calciphylaxis (Calcific Uraemic Arteriolopathy)

FeatureManagement
DiagnosisPainful Violaceous Skin Lesions → Necrosis/Ulceration; Biopsy (Medial Calcification)
Risk FactorsCa×P >4.5, Obesity, Diabetes, Warfarin, High PTH, High Calcium/Phosphate
Emergency ManagementUrgent Parathyroidectomy (If Severe SHPT) + Intensive Wound Care
MedicalSodium Thiosulfate 25g IV 3x/week (Post-HD); Cinacalcet (↓ PTH, Ca, Phos); Bisphosphonates (IV Zoledronic)
Wound CareMultidisciplinary (Nephrology, Dermatology, Surgery, Plastics)
Mortality50-80% (Sepsis, Sepsis)

9. Glucose Management in CKD/Dialysis

HbA1c Targets

PopulationTarget HbA1c
Non-Dialysis CKD<53 mmol/mol (7.0%) (Individualised)
Dialysis<58 mmol/mol (7.5%) (Higher Due to ↓ RBC Survival)

Drug Selection & Dosing (CKD/Dialysis)

Drug ClassCKD G3 (30-59)CKD G4 (15-29)CKD G5 / Dialysis
MetforminFull Dose (eGFR ≥30)Max 1g/day (eGFR 15-29)Contraindicated (eGFR <15)
SGLT2i (Dapagliflozin/Empagliflozin)✅ Full Dose (eGFR ≥20)✅ (eGFR ≥20)✅ HD/PD: YES (Empagliflozin/Dapagliflozin)
GLP-1 RA (Semaglutide/Dulaglutide)✅ No Adjustment✅ No Adjustment✅ Safe (No Renal Clearance)
DPP-4iLinagliptin: No AdjustLinagliptin: No Adjust✅ Linagliptin Safe
SulfonylureasGliclazide PreferredAvoid (Hypoglycaemia)Avoid
InsulinStandard↓ Dose (25-50%)↓ 30-50%; Basal Preferred
DPP-4i (Sitagliptin, Vildagliptin, Saxagliptin, Alogliptin)Dose ReduceDose ReduceDose Reduce (Except Linagliptin)
SGLT2iDAPA/EMAPA ≥20DAPA/EMAPA ≥20HD/PD: Yes (DAPA/EMPA)
SGLT2i Renal ProtectionDAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY (CKD ± DM)

10. Thyroid in CKD

ParameterCKD G3-4Dialysis
TSHNormal (May Be Slightly ↑)Normal (Can Be Low Due to Illness)
fT4NormalLow/Normal (Non-Thyroidal Illness)
fT3Low (Low T3 Syndrome)Low
LevothyroxineStandard DoseNo Dose Reduction (Not Dialysable)
MonitoringTSH/fT4 q6moTSH/fT4 Monthly

11. Adrenal Axis in CKD

AspectCKD G4-5 / Dialysis
CortisolNormal Metabolism (Hepatic)
Adrenal InsufficiencyStandard Dosing (Hydrocortisone 15-20mg AM + 5-10mg PM)
Adrenal CrisisStandard Stress Doses (100mg IV Hydrocortisone)
FludrocortisoneNot Needed (Aldosterone Preserved in Most CKD)

12. Gonadal Axis in CKD/Dialysis

AxisChangesManagement
Male↓ Testosterone (↑ SHBG, ↓ Leydig Function)TRT If Symptomatic + Low T (<12 nmol/L)
FemaleMenstrual Irregularity, AnovulationHRT if Indicated; Fertility Counselling
Erectile DysfunctionCommonPDE5 Inhibitors (Sildenafil) — Caution with Nitrates
Fertility↓ in Both SexesPre-Dialysis Counselling; Oocyte/Sperm Cryopreservation

13. Thyroid in Dialysis

AspectDialysis Patient
TSHNormal / Slightly ↑
fT4Normal / Low (Illness)
fT3Low (Low T3 Syndrome)
LevothyroxineNo Dose Reduction (Protein-Bound, Not Dialysable)
MonitoringTSH/fT4 Monthly

14. Drug Dosing Adjustments in Dialysis (Endocrinology)

Drug ClassDrugDosing in HD
Insulin↓ 30-50% (Reduced Clearance)Post-HD Dosing Preferred
SGLT2i✅ Dapagliflozin/Empagliflozin (HD/PD)
GLP-1 RA✅ No Adjustment (Semaglutide, Dulaglutide)
DPP-4iLinagliptin (No Adjust); Others ↓ Dose
SGLT2iDAPA/EMAPA (HD/PD Approved)
BisphosphonatesAvoid (Adynamic Bone Risk)
CinacalcetStandard Dose (Metabolised Hepatically)
Denosumab✅ Safe (No Renal Clearance); ↑ Hypocalcaemia Risk

15. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
CKD-MBD TriadPhosphate ↑ → FGF23 ↑ → 1,25-(OH)₂D ↓ → Ca²⁺ ↓ → PTH ↑↑
KDIGO PTH Target2-9x ULN (Avoid <2x = Adynamic Bone)
Phosphate Binder 1st LineCa²⁺ Normal: Calcium Carbonate; Ca²⁺ High → Sevelamer/Lanthanum
Non-Ca BindersSevelamer, Lanthanum, Ferric Citrate, Sucroferric Oxyhydroxide
Vit D in CKDG1-3: Nutritional D3 OK; G4-5/Dialysis: Active Analogues (Alfacalcidol/Calcitriol/Paricalcitol)
CinacalcetCaSR Agonist; ↓ PTH, ↓ Ca, ↓ Phos; Hypocalcaemia Risk
Adynamic BonePTH <2x ULN (Over-Suppressed); Stop Vit D Analogues/Calcimimetics
CalciphylaxisUrgent PTX + Sodium Thiosulfate + Cinacalcet; Mortality 50-80%
Glucose in HDHbA1c <58 mmol/mol (7.5%); SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA Safe; Metformin <15 Contraindicated
SGLT2i in HDDapagliflozin/Empagliflozin Approved; No Dose Adjustment
Insulin in HD↓ 30-50% Dose; Post-HD Dosing Preferred
Levothyroxine in HDNo Dose Reduction (Protein Bound, Not Dialysable)
Cinacalcet MonitoringCa²⁺ q1-2wk Initially; Hypocalcaemia Common
PTH Target HD2-9x ULN; Avoid <2x ULN (Adynamic Bone)
CalciphylaxisUrgent PTX + Sodium Thiosulfate + Cinacalcet
Phosphate Binder ChoiceCa Normal → Calcium Carbonate; Ca High/Vascular Calc → Sevelamer/Lanthanum
25-OH Vit D Target≥30 ng/mL (75 nmol/L)
Calciphylaxis Mortality50-80%
SGLT2i in CKDDAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY Proven Benefit (eGFR ≥20)

16. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Vit D in DialysisNutritional D2/D3 Ineffective (No Renal 1α-Hydroxylase) → Use Active Analogues
PTH Target2-9x ULN (Wide Range); <2x = Adynamic Bone
CalciphylaxisUrgent PTX + Thiosulfate + Cinacalcet
SGLT2i in HDDapagliflozin/Empagliflozin Approved (No Dose Adjust)
Insulin in HDReduce 30-50%; Dose Post-HD
Levothyroxine in HDNo Dose Reduction (Protein Bound, Not Dialysable)
Non-Ca BindersSevelamer, Lanthanum, Ferric Citrate, Sucroferric Oxyhydroxide
Adynamic BonePTH <2x ULN; Stop Vit D Analogues / Calcimimetics
CalciphylaxisMedical Emergency; PTX + Sodium Thiosulfate + Cinacalcet
Cinacalcet SEHypocalcaemia (Monitor Ca q1-2wk Initially)

17. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Endocrine in CKD/Dialysis))
    CKD-MBD
      Pathophysiology: Phos ↑ → FGF23↑ → VitD↓ → Ca↓ → PTH↑
      Targets: PTH 2-9xULN, Ca 2.1-2.5, Phos 0.8-1.5, Ca×P<4.5
      Binders: Ca-based (Ca normal) vs Non-Ca (Ca high/Calcification)
      Vit D: G1-3=Cholecalciferol; G4-5/HD=Active Analogues (Alfacalcidol/Calcitriol/Paricalcitol)
      Cinacalcet: CaSR Agonist → ↓PTH,↓Ca,↓Phos; Watch Hypocalcaemia
      Adynamic Bone: PTH<2xULN → Stop VitD/Calcimimetic
      Calciphylaxis: Ca×P>4.5 → Urgent PTX + Thiosulfate + Cinacalcet
    Glucose in CKD/Dialysis
      Metformin: G3 OK; G4 max 1g; G5/HD CONTRAINDICATED
      SGLT2i: Dapa/Empa ≥20 eGFR; GLP-1: No adjust
      Insulin: ↓30-50% dose; Post-HD dosing
      HbA1c Target: DM 7.0% (CKD); 7.5% (HD)
    Thyroid
      Levothyroxine: NO dose reduction in HD
      TSH/fT4 Monthly in HD
    Adrenal
      AI: Standard doses; Crisis: 100mg IV HC
      Fludro NOT needed (Aldosterone preserved)
    Gonadal
      Male: Low Testosterone common; TRT if symptomatic
      Female: Menstrual irregularities; Fertility counseling
    Drug Dosing HD
      Metformin: Stop if eGFR<30
      SGLT2i: Dapa/Empa OK in HD
      GLP-1: No adjust
      DPP4: Linagliptin no adjust
      Insulin: ↓30-50%; Post-HD dosing
      Cinacalcet: No adjust

20. MCQs (10)

  1. Secondary hyperparathyroidism in CKD: primary driver is:

A. Phosphate retention → ↓ ionised Ca²⁺ → PTH ↑ + ↓ Vit D synthesis

B. Direct phosphate stimulation of parathyroid

C. Calcium deficiency alone

D. Vitamin D deficiency alone

E. Aluminium toxicity

  1. CKD-MBD triad includes:

A. Abnormal Ca/P/PTH/Vit D, Bone abnormalities, Vascular calcification

B. Only high PTH

C. Only low calcium

D. Only high phosphate

E. Only bone disease

  1. KDIGO target for PTH in CKD Stage 3-5 (not on dialysis):

A. 2-9 times upper limit of normal

B. Normal range

C. <50 pg/mL

D. >500 pg/mL

E. No target

  1. Calcitriol (active Vit D) deficiency in CKD is due to:

A. Loss of renal 1α-hydroxylase + FGF23 inhibition

B. Dietary deficiency only

C. Malabsorption

D. Liver disease

E. PTH suppression

  1. Cinacalcet (calcimimetic) mechanism:

A. Allosteric CaSR agonist → ↓ PTH secretion

B. Vitamin D analogue

C. Phosphate binder

D. PTH receptor antagonist

E. Calcium supplement

  1. Dialysis patients: target serum phosphate (KDIGO):

A. 1.13-1.78 mmol/L (3.5-5.5 mg/dL)

B. <1.0 mmol/L

C. 2.0-2.5 mmol/L

D. No target

E. Any level acceptable

  1. Adynamic bone disease in CKD: characteristic:

A. Low PTH (<2x ULN), low bone turnover, fracture risk ↑

B. High PTH

C. Normal PTH

D. Osteitis fibrosa

E. Renal osteodystrophy type 2

  1. FGF23 in CKD:

A. Rises early (CKD 2-3); inhibits 1α-hydroxylase; predicts progression

B. Decreases

C. Unchanged

D. Only rises in dialysis

E. No clinical significance

  1. Tertiary hyperparathyroidism definition:

A. Autonomous PTH hypersecretion after prolonged secondary HPT; persistent hypercalcaemia

B. Primary HPT in CKD

C. Vitamin D toxicity

D. Cinacalcet failure

E. Post-transplant only

  1. Renal transplant: persistent hyperparathyroidism management:

A. Time (6-12mo); cinacalcet; parathyroidectomy if severe hypercalcaemia

B. Immediate parathyroidectomy

C. High-dose vitamin D

D. Calcium supplements only

E. Observation only

21. SBA Questions (5)

  1. CKD Stage 4 (eGFR 25): phosphate 1.9, calcium 2.2, PTH 350 (ULN 65), 25-OH vit D 30. Management?

A. Cholecalciferol 1000-2000 IU/day + dietary phosphate restriction + phosphate binder if needed

B. Calcitriol immediately

C. Cinacalcet

D. Parathyroidectomy

E. Calcium supplements only

  1. Dialysis patient: PTH 800, phosphate 2.2, calcium 2.5 on calcium acetate. Next?

A. Switch to non-calcium binder (sevelamer) + consider cinacalcet

B. Increase calcium acetate

C. Calcitriol

D. Parathyroidectomy

E. Stop binder

  1. CKD Stage 5D: adynamic bone disease suspected (PTH 80, low turnover on biopsy). Fracture risk?

A. Avoid oversuppression of PTH; target PTH 2-9x ULN; minimise calcium load

B. Aggressive PTH suppression

C. High-dose vitamin D

D. Teriparatide

E. Bisphosphonates

  1. Post-renal transplant (3 months): calcium 2.7, PTH 180, eGFR 45. Management?

A. Observe 6-12 months; Usually resolves; if persistent hypercalcaemia → cinacalcet/parathyroidectomy

B. Immediate parathyroidectomy

C. Cinacalcet now

D. High-dose vitamin D

E. Stop immunosuppression

  1. CKD patient on cinacalcet: symptomatic hypocalcaemia. Action?

A. Reduce/hold cinacalcet; calcium/vitamin D supplementation

B. Increase cinacalcet

C. Parathyroidectomy

D. Calcitriol bolus

E. Dialysis

22. Flashcards

  • Q: CKD-MBD definition

A: Systemic disorder of mineral/bone metabolism: Ca/P/PTH/Vit D abnormalities + bone disease + vascular calcification

  • Q: Secondary HPT pathophysiology

A: Phosphate retention → ↓ ionised Ca²⁺ → PTH↑ + ↓ renal 1α-hydroxylase → ↓ calcitriol → ↓ Ca²⁺ absorption → PTH↑↑

  • Q: FGF23

A: Osteocyte hormone; ↑ early in CKD; inhibits 1α-hydroxylase; promotes phosphaturia; predicts CVD mortality

  • Q: KDIGO PTH targets

A: CKD 3-5 non-dialysis: 2-9x ULN; Dialysis: 2-9x ULN (individualised)

  • Q: Phosphate binders

A: Calcium-based (acetate/carbonate) vs non-calcium (sevelamer, lanthanum, sucroferric oxyhydroxide)

  • Q: Calcitriol/Alfacalcidol

A: Active Vit D analogues; suppress PTH; risk hypercalcaemia/hyperphosphataemia; use if PTH rising despite binders

  • Q: Cinacalcet

A: Calcimimetic (CaSR agonist); ↓ PTH, ↓ Ca, ↓ P; EVOLVE trial: no CV mortality benefit but ↓ parathyroidectomy

  • Q: Adynamic bone

A: Low PTH (<100-150), low turnover; ↑ fracture risk; avoid oversuppression, minimise calcium load

  • Q: Tertiary HPT

A: Autonomous PTH secretion after prolonged secondary HPT; hypercalcaemia; post-transplant may persist

  • Q: Renal osteodystrophy types

A: High turnover (osteitis fibrosa), Low turnover (adynamic, osteomalacia), Mixed

  • Q: Transplant HPT

A: Usually resolves 6-12mo; persistent → cinacalcet or parathyroidectomy if severe hypercalcaemia

  • Q: Vitamin D in CKD

A: Cholecalciferol (nutritional) for 25-OH D <30; Calcitriol/alfacalcidol for secondary HPT with ↑ PTH

  • Q: Aluminium bone disease

A: Historical: aluminium-containing binders → osteomalacia, adynamic bone, dementia

23. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Phosphate retention → ↓ ionised Ca²⁺ → PTH ↑ + ↓ Vit D synthesis — Secondary hyperparathyroidism in CKD: primary driver is:
  1. Abnormal Ca/P/PTH/Vit D, Bone abnormalities, Vascular calcification — CKD-MBD triad includes:
  1. 2-9 times upper limit of normal — KDIGO target for PTH in CKD Stage 3-5 (not on dialysis):
  1. Loss of renal 1α-hydroxylase + FGF23 inhibition — Calcitriol (active Vit D) deficiency in CKD is due to:
  1. Allosteric CaSR agonist → ↓ PTH secretion — Cinacalcet (calcimimetic) mechanism:
  1. 1.13-1.78 mmol/L (3.5-5.5 mg/dL) — Dialysis patients: target serum phosphate (KDIGO):
  1. Low PTH (<2x ULN), low bone turnover, fracture risk ↑ — Adynamic bone disease in CKD: characteristic:
  1. Rises early (CKD 2-3); inhibits 1α-hydroxylase; predicts progression — FGF23 in CKD:
  1. Autonomous PTH hypersecretion after prolonged secondary HPT; persistent hypercalcaemia — Tertiary hyperparathyroidism definition:
  1. Time (6-12mo); cinacalcet; parathyroidectomy if severe hypercalcaemia — Renal transplant: persistent hyperparathyroidism management:

SBAs

  1. Cholecalciferol 1000-2000 IU/day + dietary phosphate restriction + phosphate binder if needed — CKD Stage 4 (eGFR 25): phosphate 1.9, calcium 2.2, PTH 350 (ULN 65), 25-OH vit D 30. Management?
  1. Switch to non-calcium binder (sevelamer) + consider cinacalcet — Dialysis patient: PTH 800, phosphate 2.2, calcium 2.5 on calcium acetate. Next?
  1. Avoid oversuppression of PTH; target PTH 2-9x ULN; minimise calcium load — CKD Stage 5D: adynamic bone disease suspected (PTH 80, low turnover on biopsy). Fracture risk?
  1. Observe 6-12 months; Usually resolves; if persistent hypercalcaemia → cinacalcet/parathyroidectomy — Post-renal transplant (3 months): calcium 2.7, PTH 180, eGFR 45. Management?
  1. Reduce/hold cinacalcet; calcium/vitamin D supplementation — CKD patient on cinacalcet: symptomatic hypocalcaemia. Action?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.11.03 Endocrine In Elderly
[!info]
Ageing = Physiological Endocrine Remodelling, Not Just Pathology. Key Principle: Distinguish Age-Related Changes from True Pathology. Start Low, Go Slow — Prescribing Principle.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Differentiate physiological ageing changes from endocrine pathology
  • [ ] Apply age-appropriate diagnostic thresholds and treatment targets
  • [ ] Prescribe endocrine therapies safely in frail/multimorbid elderly
  • [ ] Manage common geriatric endocrine syndromes (frailty, falls, cognitive decline)

2. Physiological Endocrine Changes with Ageing

AxisAge-Related ChangeClinical Significance
Thyroid↑ TSH (Mild, 4-6 mIU/L), ↓ fT3, Stable fT4Mild TSH Elevation Normal; Do Not Treat if fT4 Normal
Adrenal↓ DHEA/DHEAS (Adrenopause), Preserved CortisolDHEAS ↓ 80% by Age 70; No Routine Replacement
Gonadal (Male)↓ Testosterone (1-2%/yr after 30), ↑ SHBGLate-Onset Hypogonadism (LOH) vs Pathology
Gonadal (Female)Menopause (Mean 51); FSH ↑↑, Oestradiol ↓HRT Until 50-51 If Premature/Oearly
GH/IGF-1↓↓ GH/IGF-1 (Somatopause); ↓ Muscle, ↑ Visceral FatNot Treated Unless Severe GHD
Insulin/Glucose↑ Insulin Resistance, ↓ β-Cell Compensation↑ T2DM Risk; ↓ β-Cell Function
Bone/Calcium↓ BMD (Osteoporosis), ↑ PTH, ↓ Vit D SynthesisFracture Risk ↑; Vit D + Ca Supplement
Thyroid↑ TSH (Subclinical), Stable fT4Do Not Treat Subclinical Unless TSH >10/Ab+
ACTH/CortisolPreserved Diurnal Rhythm; ↑ Basal CortisolNo Adrenal Insufficiency Unless Pathology

3. Thyroid in the Elderly

Physiological vs Pathological

ParameterAge-Related ChangePathological Threshold
TSHMildly ↑ (4-6 mIU/L)>10 mIU/L ± Symptoms = Treat
fT4Stable / Slight ↓Low = Overt Hypothyroidism
fT3↓ (Low T3 Syndrome)Not Routinely Measured
AntibodiesTPOAb ↑ with AgeTPOAb+ = ↑ Risk Progression

Subclinical Hypothyroidism

TSH RangeAction
4.5-10 mIU/LObserve (q6-12mo), Treat if TPOAb+, Symptoms, Lipids, Cardiac Risk
>10 mIU/LTreat (Levothyroxine 25-50µg → Titrate)

Hyperthyroidism in Elderly

FeatureAtypical Presentation ("Apathetic Thyrotoxicosis")
Typical SymptomsOften Absent (No Tremor, Weight Gain Not Loss)
PresentationConfusion, Falls, AF, Weight Loss, Depression, Heart Failure
DiagnosisTSH Suppressed + fT4/fT3 ↑ (Same as Young)
TreatmentCarbimazole (Start Low 5-10mg); Avoid RAI if Frail; Surgery High Risk

4. Diabetes in the Elderly

FeatureYoung AdultElderly (>75)
PresentationClassic (Polyuria, Polydipsia)Atypical (Confusion, Falls, Incontinence, Weight Loss)
HbA1c Target<48 mmol/mol (6.5%)Individualised: 53-58 (7.0-7.5%) if Frail/Comorbidities
Hypoglycaemia RiskLowerHigh (Cognitive Decline, Falls, CV Events, Mortality)
HbA1c Target<48 mmol/mol (6.5%)Individualised: 53-58 (Frail) / 58-64 (Very Frail)
Hypoglycaemia UnawarenessRareCommon (Autonomic Neuropathy, Polypharmacy)
First-Line TherapyMetforminMetformin (If eGFR >30); Avoid Sulfonylureas (Hypo Risk)
SGLT2i / GLP-1 RA2nd/3rd Line1st Line if CVD/CKD/HF (Cardio-Renal Benefit)
InsulinBasal-BolusBasal Insulin Preferred (Simpler, ↓ Hypo Risk)

Hypoglycaemia in Elderly

FeatureManagement
Risk FactorsRenal Impairment, Polypharmacy, Cognitive Impairment, Missed Meals
PresentationConfusion, Falls, Agitation, Silent (No Adrenergic Symptoms)
ManagementRelaxed Targets, Simplify Regimen, Avoid Sulfonylureas

5. Thyroid Disorders — Special Considerations

ConditionElderly Nuance
Subclinical Hypo (TSH 4.5-10)Observe (Ab-/Asymptomatic); Treat if TPOAb+, Goitre, CVD Risk
Overt HypothyroidismStart Low (25-50µg Levothyroxine) → Titrate Slowly (25µg q6-8wk)
HyperthyroidismApathetic Thyrotoxicosis (No Classic Symptoms); Carbimazole Start Low
Subclinical HyperTSH <0.1; Observe if Asymptomatic; Treat if TSH <0.1 + Risk Factors
AmiodaroneType 1 (Jod-Basedow) / Type 2 (Destructive); Monitor TSH q3mo

6. Diabetes — Special Considerations

AspectElderly Specifics
HbA1c TargetIndividualised: Healthy 53-58 (7-7.5%); Frail 58-64 (7.5-8.0%)
HypoglycaemiaMajor Harm (Falls, Fractures, CV Events, Dementia Progression)
SulfonylureasAvoid (High Hypo Risk); Prefer DPP-4i, SGLT2i, GLP-1 RA
SGLT2i / GLP-1 RA1st Line if CVD/CKD/HF/Obesity (Cardio-Renal Protection)
InsulinBasal Preferred (Simpler, Less Hypo); Pen Devices; Carer Support
FrailtyRockwood/CFS Scale; Deprescribe Complex Regimens

7. Adrenal & Gonadal Axes

Adrenal Insufficiency

AspectElderly Specifics
DiagnosisSame Biochemistry; Higher Clinical Suspicion (Atypical Presentation)
ReplacementHydrocortisone 10-15mg AM + 5mg PM (Lower Doses); No Fludrocortisone if BP Normal
Stress DosesEssential (Frailty ↑ Crisis Risk)

Male Hypogonadism (Late-Onset)

AspectApproach
DiagnosisSymptomatic + Testosterone <12 nmol/L (2 Occasions)
Testosterone ReplacementGel/Gel Preferred (Steady Levels); Monitor Hct, PSA, Lipids
ContraindicationsHct >54%, Active Prostate Ca, Severe HF, Uncontrolled HTN

Female Hypogonadism / Menopause

AspectElderly (>70)
HRTNot Initiated >60-65 (WHI Harm); If Already On → Continue if Benefit > Risk
Vaginal OestrogenFirst-Line for GSM (No Systemic Absorption)
BoneDEXA q2-3yr; Bisphosphonate/Denosumab if High Risk

8. Bone & Calcium Metabolism

AspectElderly Management
OsteoporosisDEXA q2-3yr (Women >65, Men >70); FRAX for 10yr Risk
Vitamin D25-OH D ≥50 nmol/L (20 ng/mL); Supplement 800-2000 IU/day
Calcium1200mg/day (Diet + Supplement); Citrate if Achlorhydria
Fracture PreventionFRAX >10% (Major) / 3% (Hip) → Bisphosphonate/Denosumab
Falls PreventionExercise (Balance/Strength), Home Hazards, Vit D, Medication Review

9. Vitamin D in Elderly

ParameterRecommendation
25-OH D Target>50 nmol/L (20 ng/mL)
Supplementation800-2000 IU/day (Cholecalciferol)
Malabsorption/ObeseHigher Dose; IM 300,000 IU q6mo Option
Monitoring25-OH D q6-12mo Until Target Achieved

10. Special Considerations

Polypharmacy & Drug Interactions

Drug ClassEndocrine Interaction
PPIs↓ B12, ↓ Mg, ↑ Fracture Risk (Long-Term)
Loop Diuretics↑ Ca²⁺ Excretion; Hypomagnesaemia
Glucocorticoids↑ Fracture Risk (Dose/Duration Dependent)
AEDs (Enzyme Inducers)↑ Vit D Catabolism → Osteomalacia Risk
Antipsychotics↑ Prolactin, Weight Gain, Metabolic Syndrome

Renal Impairment (eGFR <30)

DrugAdjustment
MetforminContraindicated (eGFR <30)
SGLT2iContraindicated (eGFR <30)
GLP-1 RASafe (Dulaglutide, Semaglutide — No Dose Adjustment)
DPP-4iLinagliptin (No Adjustment); Others Reduce
Insulin↓ Dose (25-50%); ↑ Hypoglycaemia Risk

11. Geriatric Syndromes & Endocrine Links

SyndromeEndocrine LinkManagement
FrailtyLow Testosterone, Low IGF-1, High Cortisol, Low Vit DExercise, Nutrition, Vit D, Testosterone (If Symptomatic Low T)
FallsHypoglycaemia, Orthostatic Hypotension (Autonomic Neuropathy), Cataracts, PolypharmacyMed Review, Exercise, Vit D, Cataract Surgery
Cognitive DeclineT2DM, Hypoglycaemia, Hypothyroidism, Low TestosteroneGlycaemic Control, Thyroid Optimisation, B12/Folate
SarcopeniaLow Testosterone, Low Vit D, Low GH/IGF-1, InactivityResistance Exercise, Protein, Vit D, Testosterone (If Symptomatic)

12. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
TSH in ElderlyMild ↑ Normal (4-6 mIU/L); Treat Only if >10 or Symptoms
Subclinical HypothyroidismTSH 4.5-10: Observe; >10 → Treat
Apathetic ThyrotoxicosisNo Classic Symptoms; Confusion, Falls, AF, Weight Loss
HypoglycaemiaMajor Harm in Elderly (Falls, CV Events, Dementia); Avoid Sulfonylureas
HbA1c TargetHealthy: 53-58; Frail: 58-64 (7.5-8.0%)
SGLT2i/GLP-11st Line if CVD/CKD/HF/Obesity; Renal Safe (Dapagliflozin ≥25)
MetforminContinue if eGFR >30; Reduce if 20-30; Stop <15
Subclinical Hypo in ElderlyObserve if TSH 4.5-10; Treat if >10 or Ab+
Bone HealthDEXA >65 (F) / >70 (M); Vit D 800-2000 IU + Ca 1200mg
Vit D Target>50 nmol/L (20 ng/mL)
Sick EuthyroidLow T3, Normal TSH/fT4 (Illness); Do Not Treat
Thyroid Storm in ElderlyAtypical (No Fever); High Mortality
Adrenal InsufficiencyHydrocortisone 10-15mg AM + 5mg PM; Stress Doses for Illness

13. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
TSH in ElderlyMild ↑ (4-6) is Normal Ageing; Treat if >10 or Symptomatic
Apathetic ThyrotoxicosisNo Tremor/Weight Loss; Confusion, Falls, AF, Apathy
Subclinical HypothyroidismTSH 4.5-10 → Observe; >10 → Treat
HbA1c TargetFrail: 58-64 (7.5-8.0%); Avoid <53 (Hypo Risk)
SGLT2i in CKDDapagliflozin ≥25 eGFR; Empagliflozin ≥20
SulfonylureasAvoid (High Hypo Risk); Use DPP-4i / SGLT2i / GLP-1
Bisphosphonate HolidayAfter 3-5yr Oral / 3yr IV Zoledronic; Reassess DEXA
Denosumab HolidayContraindicated — Rapid Bone Loss on Stopping
Testosterone in Elderly MenOnly If Symptomatic + Low T (<12 nmol/L)
Vit D Target>50 nmol/L (20 ng/mL); Dose 800-2000 IU/day

14. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Endocrine in Elderly))
    Thyroid
      TSH ↑ Mildly Normal (4-6)
      Subclinical Hypo: TSH 4.5-10 Observe; >10 Treat
      Hyperthyroid: Apathetic (Confusion, Falls, AF)
    Diabetes
      HbA1c Target: Healthy 53-58; Frail 58-64
      Avoid Sulfonylureas (Hypo)
      SGLT2i/GLP-1 1st Line if CVD/CKD/HF
      Insulin: Basal Preferred; Pen Devices
    Adrenal
      AI: Same Dx; Lower Dose Hydrocortisone
      Stress Doses: Essential (Frailty ↑ Crisis Risk)
    Bone
      DEXA >65F/>70M; FRAX >10% → Bisphosphonate
      Vit D >50 nmol/L; Ca 1200mg
    Male Hypogonadism
      Testosterone <12 + Symptoms → Gel/IM
      Klinefelter: 47,XXY; High FSH/LH
    Female
      HRT Not Initiated >60-65
      Vaginal Oestrogen (GSM) 1st Line
    Drug Interactions
      PPI → B12/Mg↓; Loop Diuretic → Ca/Mg Loss; Steroid → Fracture
      AEDs → Vit D Catabolism
    Geriatric Syndromes
      Frailty: Low T, Low IGF-1, High Cortisol
      Falls: Hypoglycaemia, Orthostatic BP, Polypharmacy
      Cognitive: T2DM, Hypothyroid, Low T
      Sarcopenia: Resistance Exercise + Protein + Vit D + T
    Monitoring
      HbA1c 6mo; TSH Annual; DEXA q2-3yr; Vit D Annual

15. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Elderly TSHMild ↑ (4-6) Normal; Treat if >10 or Symptomatic
Subclinical HypoTSH 4.5-10 Observe; >10 Treat
Apathetic ThyrotoxicosisConfusion, Falls, AF, Weight Loss (No Classic Symptoms)
HbA1c Target Frail58-64 (7.5-8.0%)
SulfonylureasAvoid (Hypoglycaemia, Falls, CV Events)
SGLT2i/GLP-11st Line if CVD/CKD/HF/Obesity
Metformin eGFR≥30 OK; 20-30 Reduce; <15 Stop
Subclinical Hypo ElderlyTSH 4.5-10 Observe; >10 Treat
Frail TargetsHbA1c 58-64; BP <150/90; Accept Higher Glucose
Bone HealthDEXA >65F/70M; Vit D 800-2000 IU + Ca 1200mg
Vit D Target>50 nmol/L (20 ng/mL)
Frail HypoglycaemiaMajor Harm (Falls, CV Events, Dementia)
Testosterone ElderlyOnly If Symptomatic + Low (<12)
HRT >65Not Initiated; If Continuing → Annual Review
Geriatric FallsReview Meds (Sulfonylureas, Diuretics, Anticholinergics)

16. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Endocrinology in Elderly|Endocrine in Elderly]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Hypothyroidism Overview]], [[Diabetes in the Elderly]], [[Osteoporosis & Metabolic Bone Disease]], [[Male Hypogonadism]], [[Female Hypogonadism]], [[Adrenal Insufficiency- Primary (Addison)]]

17. MCQs (10)

  1. Thyroid function in healthy ageing: typical pattern:

A. TSH ↑ (upper reference limit increases), fT4 normal

B. TSH ↓, fT4 ↑

C. TSH normal, fT4 ↓

D. TSH ↓, fT4 ↓

E. No change

  1. Subclinical hypothyroidism in elderly (>80): TSH 6-10, asymptomatic. Management?

A. Observe + repeat in 3-6 months (no routine treatment if TSH <10)

B. Immediate levothyroxine

C. Liothyronine

D. Combo T4/T3

E. Refer for thyroidectomy

  1. Levothyroxine dosing in elderly: start low go slow. Typical starting dose:

A. 25-50 µg/day (12.5-25 µg if cardiac disease)

B. 100 µg/day

C. 1.6 µg/kg

D. Full replacement immediately

E. 500 µg/week

  1. Adrenal insufficiency in elderly: atypical presentation:

A. Fatigue, anorexia, weight loss, postural hypotension, hyponatraemia (often no hyperpigmentation)

B. Classic Addisonian crisis

C. Hyperpigmentation always

D. Hypertension

E. Hyperglycaemia

  1. SIADH in elderly: most common drug causes:

A. SSRIs, SNRIs, carbamazepine, PPIs, opioids

B. Diuretics only

C. Beta-blockers only

D. ACE inhibitors only

E. Statins only

  1. Hyponatraemia correction in elderly: max safe rate:

A. 4-6 mmol/L/24h (max 8 in first 24h if severe symptoms)

B. 10-12 mmol/L/24h

C. 15 mmol/L/24h

D. No limit

E. 0.5 mmol/L/h

  1. Diabetes in elderly: HbA1c target (frail, multi-morbidity):

A. 7.5-8.5% (58-69 mmol/mol)

B. <6.5%

C. 6.5-7.0%

D. <7.0% for all

E. No target

  1. Metformin in elderly CKD: eGFR threshold for dose reduction:

A. eGFR <45: reduce dose; <30: contraindicated

B. eGFR <60

C. eGFR <30 only

D. No adjustment

E. eGFR <15

  1. Osteoporosis treatment in elderly: first-line:

A. Oral bisphosphonate (alendronate) + Ca/Vit D; denosumab if contraindicated

B. Teriparatide first

C. Romosozumab first

D. HRT

E. Calcitonin

  1. Denosumab in elderly: key monitoring:

A. Hypocalcaemia (ensure Vit D replete); stop → rapid bone loss (rebound vertebral #)

B. Hypercalcaemia

C. Renal function only

D. LFTs only

E. No monitoring

18. SBA Questions (5)

  1. 85yo woman: falls, fatigue, TSH 8.5, fT4 12, no goitre, no cardiac history. Best approach?

A. Repeat TSH/fT4 in 3 months; treat if TSH >10 or symptomatic

B. Start levothyroxine 50µg

C. Start levothyroxine 100µg

D. Refer endocrinology

E. Radioactive iodine

  1. 80yo man on sertraline: confused, Na+ 118, euvolaemic, urine osm 400, urine Na+ 60. Management?

A. Fluid restriction <1L + stop sertraline → consider tolvaptan if severe

B. 3% saline rapid correction

C. Demeclocycline

D. Hypertonic saline + furosemide

E. Fludrocortisone

  1. 78yo woman: T2DM, eGFR 38, HbA1c 8.2% on metformin 1g BD. Best adjustment?

A. Reduce metformin to 500mg BD (eGFR 30-45); review HbA1c target 7.5-8.0%

B. Stop metformin

C. Add sulfonylurea

D. Start insulin

E. Increase metformin

  1. 82yo man: hip fracture, DEXA T-score -3.0, eGFR 45. First-line?

A. Alendronate 70mg weekly + Ca/Vit D; dental review

B. Denosumab 60mg 6-monthly

C. Teriparatide

D. Zoledronic acid

E. Raloxifene

  1. 90yo woman: community-acquired pneumonia, on prednisolone 5mg for PMR. Acute illness: stress-dose steroids?

A. Hydrocortisone 50mg IV q6h (if AI suspected) or double oral dose; taper after recovery

B. Continue 5mg

C. Stop prednisolone

D. Increase to 20mg

E. Switch to dexamethasone

19. Flashcards

  • Q: TSH in ageing

A: Upper reference limit increases with age (e.g., >80y: up to 7-8 mU/L); fT4 stable

  • Q: Subclinical hypo elderly

A: TSH 4.5-10, asymptomatic: observe, repeat 3-6mo; treat if TSH>10, symptomatic, goitre, antibodies

  • Q: Levothyroxine elderly

A: Start 12.5-25µg (cardiac disease) or 25-50µg; titrate q6-8wk; target TSH upper half ref range

  • Q: AI in elderly

A: Atypical: fatigue, anorexia, weight loss, postural hypotension, hyponatraemia; often NO hyperpigmentation

  • Q: SIADH elderly drugs

A: SSRIs/SNRIs (most common), carbamazepine, PPIs, opioids, cyclophosphamide

  • Q: Hyponatraemia correction

A: Max 4-6 mmol/L/24h (8 if seizures/coma); avoid osmotic demyelination

  • Q: DM elderly targets

A: Fit: 7-7.5%; Frail/multimorbidity: 7.5-8.5%; End-of-life: avoid symptomatic hyperglycaemia

  • Q: Metformin CKD

A: eGFR 45-59: full dose; 30-44: max 1g/day; <30: contraindicated (lactic acidosis risk)

  • Q: SGLT2i elderly

A: eGFR ≥20: cardiovascular/renal benefits; watch volume depletion, genital infections, euglycaemic DKA

  • Q: Osteoporosis elderly

A: Alendronate 1st line; denosumab if contraindicated/intolerant; zoledronic acid if adherence issue

  • Q: Denosumab caveat

A: STOP → rapid BMD loss + rebound vertebral fractures (MUST transition to bisphosphonate)

  • Q: Falls risk

A: Review medications (sedatives, antihypertensives); vit D 800-1000 IU; exercise; home safety

  • Q: Sick day rules

A: AI: double/triple glucocorticoid; DM: SGLT2i hold, metformin hold if dehydrated, insulin adjust

20. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. TSH ↑ (upper reference limit increases), fT4 normal — Thyroid function in healthy ageing: typical pattern:
  1. Observe + repeat in 3-6 months (no routine treatment if TSH <10) — Subclinical hypothyroidism in elderly (>80): TSH 6-10, asymptomatic. Management?
  1. 25-50 µg/day (12.5-25 µg if cardiac disease) — Levothyroxine dosing in elderly: start low go slow. Typical starting dose:
  1. Fatigue, anorexia, weight loss, postural hypotension, hyponatraemia (often no hyperpigmentation) — Adrenal insufficiency in elderly: atypical presentation:
  1. SSRIs, SNRIs, carbamazepine, PPIs, opioids — SIADH in elderly: most common drug causes:
  1. 4-6 mmol/L/24h (max 8 in first 24h if severe symptoms) — Hyponatraemia correction in elderly: max safe rate:
  1. 7.5-8.5% (58-69 mmol/mol) — Diabetes in elderly: HbA1c target (frail, multi-morbidity):
  1. eGFR <45: reduce dose; <30: contraindicated — Metformin in elderly CKD: eGFR threshold for dose reduction:
  1. Oral bisphosphonate (alendronate) + Ca/Vit D; denosumab if contraindicated — Osteoporosis treatment in elderly: first-line:
  1. Hypocalcaemia (ensure Vit D replete); stop → rapid bone loss (rebound vertebral #) — Denosumab in elderly: key monitoring:

SBAs

  1. Repeat TSH/fT4 in 3 months; treat if TSH >10 or symptomatic — 85yo woman: falls, fatigue, TSH 8.5, fT4 12, no goitre, no cardiac history. Best approach?
  1. Fluid restriction <1L + stop sertraline → consider tolvaptan if severe — 80yo man on sertraline: confused, Na+ 118, euvolaemic, urine osm 400, urine Na+ 60. Management?
  1. Reduce metformin to 500mg BD (eGFR 30-45); review HbA1c target 7.5-8.0% — 78yo woman: T2DM, eGFR 38, HbA1c 8.2% on metformin 1g BD. Best adjustment?
  1. Alendronate 70mg weekly + Ca/Vit D; dental review — 82yo man: hip fracture, DEXA T-score -3.0, eGFR 45. First-line?
  1. Hydrocortisone 50mg IV q6h (if AI suspected) or double oral dose; taper after recovery — 90yo woman: community-acquired pneumonia, on prednisolone 5mg for PMR. Acute illness: stress-dose steroids?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.11.04 Endocrine In Pregnancy
[!info]
Pregnancy = Unique Endocrine State with Profound Physiological Adaptations. Thyroid, Adrenal, Pancreatic, Calcium, Pituitary Axes All Altered. Multidisciplinary Care Essential for Optimal Maternal-Fetal Outcomes.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Describe physiological endocrine adaptations in pregnancy
  • [ ] Apply trimester-specific targets for thyroid, diabetes, adrenal, and calcium disorders
  • [ ] Manage pre-existing and new-onset endocrine disorders in pregnancy
  • [ ] Counsel on breastfeeding, contraception, and postpartum follow-up

2. General Endocrine Adaptations in Pregnancy

Thyroid Axis

ChangeMechanismClinical Significance
↑ TBG (2-3x)Oestrogen ↑ TBG Synthesis↑ Total T4/T3 (2-3x); fT4/fT3 Reliable
hCG Cross-ReactivityhCG → Weak TSH AgonistTSH ↓ 1st Tri (Often <0.1-0.4 mIU/L); Normalises 2nd Trimester
Iodine Requirement↑ Renal Clearance + Fetal UptakeRDA 250 µg/day (vs 150 µg Non-Pregnant)
TSH Targets1st Tri: 0.1-2.5; 2nd: 0.2-3.0; 3rd: 0.3-3.0 mIU/LTrimester-Specific Reference Ranges

Adrenal Axis

ChangeMechanismClinical Impact
↑ CBG (2-3x)Oestrogen ↑ CBG Synthesis↑ Total Cortisol; Free Cortisol ↑ 2-3x
HPA Axis HyperplasiaPlacental CRH + Maternal HPA StimulationPhysiological Hypercortisolism
Physiological Hypercortisolism↑ Free Cortisol 2-3xSuppresses ACTH (Mild)
Adrenal InsufficiencyHydrocortisone ↑ 20-40% in 2nd/3rd TriStress Doses for Delivery (100mg IV q6h)

Glucose/Insulin

ChangeMechanism
Insulin ResistancePlacental Hormones (hPL, Cortisol, Progesterone) → ↑ Insulin Resistance
β-Cell AdaptationHyperplasia/Hypertrophy → ↑ Insulin Secretion 2-3x
GDM Risk↑↑ (Screening 24-28w; High-Risk Earlier)

Calcium & Bone

ChangeMechanism
Calcium Transfer↑ Fetal Demand (250-300mg/day 3rd Tri)
Vitamin D↑ 1,25-(OH)₂D (↑ 1α-Hydroxylase); ↑ Intestinal Ca²⁺ Absorption
BoneMaternal Bone Resorption (↑ PTHrP, PTH) → Transient BMD Loss (Recovers Postpartum)
PTHrPPlacental/Breast Production → ↑ Bone Resorption

3. Thyroid Disease in Pregnancy

Hypothyroidism

AspectManagement
Pre-conceptionTSH <2.5 mIU/L; Optimise Levothyroxine
1st TrimesterTSH 0.1-2.5 mIU/L; ↑ Dose ~30% at +ve Test
2nd/3rd TrimesterTSH 0.2-3.0 / 0.3-3.0 mIU/L; Monitor q4-6wk
PostpartumReduce to Pre-Pregnancy Dose; Check TSH 6wk
Subclinical HypoTreat if TPOAb+ or TSH > Trimester Target

Hyperthyroidism

TrimesterPreferred ATD
1st TrimesterPTU 50-150mg/day (Lower Teratogenicity)
2nd/3rd TrimesterSwitch to Carbimazole (PTU Hepatotoxicity Risk ↑)
TargetsfT4 Upper Normal Range; TSH Low but Detectable
TRAb MonitoringEach Trimester; TRAb >5 IU/L → Fetal US (Goitre/Tachycardia/Hydrops)

Postpartum

ActionTiming
Reduce HRTPre-Pregnancy Dose Immediately Postpartum
TSH Check6 Weeks Postpartum
BreastfeedingLevothyroxine, Carbimazole, PTU Compatible (Low Milk Transfer)

4. Diabetes in Pregnancy

GDM Screening

StrategyDetails
Universal (IADPSG/ADA)75g OGTT 24-28w (All)
Selective (NICE)Risk Factors → OGTT 24-28w
Diagnostic (IADPSG)FBG ≥5.1, 1h ≥10.0, 2h ≥8.5 mmol/L (1 Abnormal = GDM)

GDM Management

StepIntervention
1. MNTDiet (Low GI, 175g Carb/day), Exercise 150min/wk
2. Metformin500mg BD → 1g BD (If Fasting/1h/2h Targets Not Met)
3. InsulinBasal-Bolus / Basal Only; Targets: Fasting <5.3, 1h <7.8, 2h <6.4 mmol/L

Pre-Existing Diabetes

AspectManagement
Pre-ConceptionHbA1c <48 mmol/mol (6.5%); Folic Acid 5mg; Stop Teratogens (ACEi, ARB, Statin, SGLT2i, GLP-1)
PregnancyIntensive Insulin (Basal-Bolus / Pump); Targets: FBG <5.3, 1h <7.8, 2h <6.4 mmol/L
Delivery37-38+6w (Insulin); 38-40w (Diet/Metformin)
PostpartumInsulin ↓ Rapidly; Breastfeeding Encouraged; Contraception

5. Adrenal Insufficiency in Pregnancy

AspectManagement
Hydrocortisone↑ 20-40% in 2nd/3rd Tri (Physiological Hypercortisolism)
FludrocortisoneContinue (Safe); Monitor BP, K⁺
DeliveryStress Dose: Hydrocortisone 100mg IV q6h During Labour
PostpartumReduce to Pre-Pregnancy Dose; Crisis Prevention

6. Calcium & Vitamin D

RequirementDose
Iodine250 µg/day (vs 150 µg Non-Pregnant)
Calcium1000-1200 mg/day (Diet + Supplement)
Vitamin D600-800 IU/day (Higher if Deficient)
Folic Acid5mg Daily (Pre-Conception + 1st Tri)

7. Endocrine Emergencies in Pregnancy

EmergencyManagement
Thyroid StormPTU + Propranolol + Iodine + Hydrocortisone; Multidisciplinary ICU
Adrenal CrisisHydrocortisone 100mg IV STAT + Fluids; Stress Doses for Delivery
DKAInsulin Infusion + Fluids + K⁺ Replacement; Fetal Monitoring
HHSHydration + Insulin; Avoid Rapid Correction

8. Postpartum Endocrine Management

AspectAction
ThyroidReduce Levothyroxine to Pre-Preg Dose; Check TSH 6wk
AdrenalReduce Hydrocortisone to Pre-Preg Dose
DiabetesInsulin ↓ Rapidly; GDM → 75g OGTT 6-13wk Postpartum
BreastfeedingLevothyroxine, Carbimazole, PTU, Metformin Compatible
ContraceptionLARC (IUD/Implant) Preferred; COCP If No Contraindications

9. Autoimmune in Pregnancy

ConditionMonitoring
Thyroid (TPOAb+)TSH q4-6wk; ↑ Dose if TSH ↑
Antiphospholipid (APS)Aspirin 75-150mg OD from 12w; LWMH if Prior Thrombosis
SLE/APSHCQ Continued; Prednisolone if Flare
T1DMIntensive Insulin; CGM Preferred; Pre-Conception HbA1c <48

10. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
TSH in 1st TrimesterCan be <0.1 (hCG Effect); Normal if fT4 Normal
TSH Targets1st: 0.1-2.5; 2nd: 0.2-3.0; 3rd: 0.3-3.0 mIU/L
Levothyroxine Dose↑ 30% at +ve Test; Monitor q4wk (1st Tri)
PTU vs CarbimazolePTU 1st Tri (Less Teratogenic); Switch Carbimazole 2nd/3rd Tri
TRAb in Pregnancy>5 IU/L → Fetal US (Goitre, Tachycardia, Hydrops)
GDM ScreeningIADPSG: 75g OGTT 24-28w (1 Abnormal = GDM); NICE: Risk-Based
GDM TargetsFBG <5.3, 1h <7.8, 2h <6.4 mmol/L
Insulin in GDMBasal-Bolus / Basal Only; Metformin if Needed
Adrenal Insufficiency↑ Hydrocortisone 20-40% in 2nd/3rd Tri; Stress Doses Delivery
Postpartum Thyroiditis5-10% Pregnancies; TPOAb+ → 30-50% Risk; 20-30% Permanent Hypo
BreastfeedingLevothyroxine, Carbimazole, PTU, Metformin Compatible
Iodine250 µg/day (vs 150 µg Non-Pregnant)
Contraception PostpartumLARC (IUD/Implant) Preferred; COCP if No Contraindications

11. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Low TSH in 1st TrimesterNormal (hCG Cross-Reactivity); Treat as Thyrotoxicosis Only if fT4 ↑
TSH Targets1st: 0.1-2.5; 2nd: 0.2-3.0; 3rd: 0.3-3.0 mIU/L
Levothyroxine Increase~30% ↑ at Positive Test; Monitor q4wk
PTU vs CarbimazolePTU 1st Trimester (Less Teratogenic); Switch Carbimazole 2nd/3rd
RAI in PregnancyContraindicated (Fetal Thyroid Ablation)
BreastfeedingLevothyroxine, Carbimazole, PTU, Metformin Safe
TSH in PostpartumReduce to Pre-Pregnancy Dose; Check TSH 6wk
GDM ScreeningIADPSG: Universal 75g OGTT 24-28w; NICE: Risk-Based
Metformin in PregnancySafe (Category B); Continue if Pre-Existing or GDM
Subclinical Hypo in PregnancyTreat if TPOAb+ or TSH > Trimester Limit

12. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Endocrine in Pregnancy))
    Thyroid
      Physiology: ↑TBG, hCG↓TSH, ↑Iodine Need
      TSH Targets: 1st 0.1-2.5, 2nd 0.2-3.0, 3rd 0.3-3.0
      Hypothyroidism: ↑ LT4 30% at +ve β-hCG; Target TSH Trimester-Specific
      Hyperthyroidism: PTU 1st Tri → Carbimazole 2nd/3rd
      TRAb >5 IU/L → Fetal US
    Adrenal
      ↑ CBG 2-3x → Total Cortisol ↑
      AI: ↑ HC 20-40% 2nd/3rd Tri; Stress Dose Delivery
    Glucose
      GDM Screening: IADPSG (Universal 75g OGTT) vs NICE (Risk-Based)
      GDM: Diet → Metformin → Insulin
      Targets: FBG<5.3, 1h<7.8, 2h<6.4 mmol/L
    Calcium/Bone
      Iodine 250µg/d; Ca 1000-1200mg; Vit D 600-800 IU
      Folic Acid 5mg Pre-Conception
    Emergencies
      Thyroid Storm: PTU + Propranolol + Iodine + HC
      Adrenal Crisis: HC 100mg IV + Fluids
    Postpartum
      LT4: Reduce to Pre-Preg Dose
      HC: Stress Doses Delivery; Taper
      Breastfeeding: LT4, Carbimazole, PTU, Metformin Safe
      OGTT 6-13wk Postpartum (GDM)
    Contraception
      LARC 1st Line; COCP if No Contraindication

13. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
TSH in 1st TrimesterCan be <0.1 (hCG Cross-Reactivity) — Normal if fT4 Normal
TSH Targets1st: 0.1-2.5; 2nd: 0.2-3.0; 3rd: 0.3-3.0 mIU/L
Levothyroxine ↑~30% Increase at +ve Test; Monitor q4wk
PTU vs CarbimazolePTU 1st Tri (Less Teratogenic); Switch Carbimazole 2nd/3rd
RAI in PregnancyContraindicated (Fetal Thyroid Ablation)
TRAb >5 IU/L↑ Neonatal Thyrotoxicosis Risk; Fetal US Indicated
Postpartum Thyroiditis5-10% Pregnancies; TPOAb+ → 30-50% Risk; 20-30% Permanent Hypo
BreastfeedingLevothyroxine, Carbimazole, PTU, Metformin Safe
Iodine Requirement250 µg/day (vs 150 µg Non-Pregnant)
Postpartum LevothyroxineReduce to Pre-Pregnancy Dose; Check TSH 6wk
Subclinical Hypo in PregnancyTreat if TPOAb+ / TSH >2.5 (1st) / >3.0 (2nd/3rd)
GDM ScreeningIADPSG: Universal 75g OGTT 24-28w; NICE: Risk-Based
GDM TargetsFBG <5.3, 1h <7.8, 2h <6.4 mmol/L
Metformin in GDM500mg BD → 1g BD; Safe in Pregnancy
Insulin in GDMBasal-Bolus; Targets: FBG<5.3, 1h<7.8, 2h<6.4
Adrenal CrisisHydrocortisone 100mg IV + Fluids; Stress Doses for Delivery
Thyroid StormPTU + Propranolol + Iodine + Hydrocortisone

14. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)
