Parent: [[../Endocrine in Pregnancy|Endocrine in Pregnancy]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Thyroid in Pregnancy]], [[Diabetes in Pregnancy]], [[Adrenal Insufficiency in Pregnancy]], [[Postpartum Thyroiditis]], [[Reproductive Endocrinology]]

15. MCQs (10)

  1. hCG cross-reactivity with TSH receptor causes:

A. Transient gestational thyrotoxicosis (1st trimester), TSH suppressed, fT4 ↑

B. Hypothyroidism

C. Thyroid storm

D. Thyroid nodules

E. No effect

  1. Iodine requirement in pregnancy (WHO):

A. 250 µg/day (vs 150 µg non-pregnant)

B. 150 µg/day

C. 500 µg/day

D. 100 µg/day

E. No increase needed

  1. TSH trimester-specific reference ranges (ATA):

A. 1st: 0.1-2.5; 2nd: 0.2-3.0; 3rd: 0.3-3.0 mU/L

B. Same as non-pregnant

C. 0.4-4.0 all trimesters

D. 0.1-4.0

E. Lower in 3rd trimester

  1. Levothyroxine dose adjustment in pregnant hypothyroid woman:

A. Increase by 25-50% (2 extra doses/week) at confirmation; target TSH trimester-specific

B. No change

C. Decrease dose

D. Stop

E. Switch to liothyronine

  1. Graves disease in pregnancy: antithyroid drug of choice:

A. 1st trimester: Propylthiouracil (PTU) → 2nd/3rd: Carbimazole/Methimazole (switch at 16wks)

B. Carbimazole throughout

C. PTU throughout

D. Radioactive iodine

E. Surgery only

  1. PTU vs Carbimazole in pregnancy: PTU preferred 1st trimester because:

A. Lower teratogenicity risk (aplasia cutis, choanal atresia with carbimazole)

B. More potent

C. Longer half-life

D. Cheaper

E. Better breastfeeding safety

  1. Postpartum thyroiditis: typical course:

A. Thyrotoxic phase (1-4mo) → hypothyroid (4-8mo) → recovery (12mo); 20-40% permanent hypothyroidism

B. Only thyrotoxic

C. Only hypothyroid

D. Permanent hyperthyroidism

E. No thyroid dysfunction

  1. Gestational diabetes (GDM) diagnosis (IADPSG/WHO 2013):

A. 75g OGTT: fasting ≥5.1, 1h ≥10.0, 2h ≥8.5 mmol/L (one abnormal = GDM)

B. Fasting ≥7.0

C. 2h ≥11.1

D. HbA1c ≥6.5%

E. Random ≥11.1

  1. GDM management first-line:

A. Medical nutrition therapy + glucose monitoring; insulin if targets not met

B. Metformin first

C. Glibenclamide first

D. Diet only

E. Immediate insulin

  1. Postpartum follow-up after GDM:

A. 75g OGTT at 6-12 weeks; then q1-3 years (lifelong T2DM risk 50% at 10y)

B. No follow-up

C. HbA1c only

D. Fasting glucose only

E. Only if symptomatic

16. SBA Questions (5)

  1. 32yo woman, 8 weeks pregnant, known hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 100µg. TSH 4.5 (ref 0.1-2.5). Action?

A. Increase levothyroxine by ~25-30% (e.g., +25µg daily or 2 extra doses/week); recheck TSH in 4 weeks

B. No change

C. Decrease dose

D. Switch to PTU

E. Stop levothyroxine

  1. 28yo woman, 10 weeks: Graves disease, on carbimazole 15mg. TSH <0.01, fT4 35. Best switch?

A. Switch to PTU 100-200mg/day (1st trimester); plan to switch back to carbimazole at 16 weeks

B. Continue carbimazole

C. Stop antithyroid drug

D. Radioactive iodine

E. Thyroidectomy

  1. 26yo woman, 26 weeks: GDM diagnosed (fasting 5.8, 1h 11.2, 2h 9.8). Diet-controlled. Targets?

A. Fasting <5.3, 1h postprandial <7.8, 2h <6.4 mmol/L

B. Fasting <4.0

C. No targets

D. Pre-meal <7.0

E. HbA1c <6.0%

  1. Postpartum day 3: woman with T1DM on insulin pump. Breastfeeding. Insulin requirement:

A. Decreases significantly (↓30-50%); risk hypoglycaemia; reduce basal/bolus

B. Increases

C. No change

D. Stop insulin

E. Switch to metformin

  1. Postpartum thyroiditis at 3 months: symptomatic thyrotoxicosis (palpitations, tremor). Management?

A. Propranolol 20-40mg TDS for symptoms; observe (self-limiting); recheck TSH in 4-6 weeks

B. Carbimazole

C. PTU

D. Radioactive iodine

E. Prednisolone

17. Flashcards

  • Q: hCG & thyroid

A: hCG cross-reacts with TSH-R → transient gestational thyrotoxicosis (week 8-14); TSH↓, fT4↑

  • Q: Iodine in pregnancy

A: 250 µg/day (WHO); 150 µg non-pregnant; deficiency → cretinism, goitre, neurocognitive deficit

  • Q: TSH ref ranges pregnancy

A: 1st tri: 0.1-2.5; 2nd: 0.2-3.0; 3rd: 0.3-3.0 mU/L (ATA)

  • Q: Levothyroxine in pregnancy

A: ↑ dose 25-50% at +ve pregnancy test; target trimester-specific TSH; monitor q4wk

  • Q: Graves pregnancy drugs

A: PTU 1st tri (less teratogenic); switch to carbimazole/methimazole at 16wks (PTU hepatotoxicity)

  • Q: PTU vs MMI teratogenicity

A: MMI: aplasia cutis, choanal atresia, oesophageal atresia; PTU: hepatotoxicity (rare)

  • Q: Postpartum thyroiditis

A: 5-10% women; thyrotoxic 1-4mo → hypothyroid 4-8mo → recovery 12mo; 20-40% permanent hypothyroid

  • Q: GDM diagnosis

A: 75g OGTT: fasting ≥5.1, 1h ≥10.0, 2h ≥8.5 mmol/L (IADPSG); one abnormal = GDM

  • Q: GDM management

A: MNT + glucose monitoring → insulin if fasting ≥5.3 or 1h ≥7.8 or 2h ≥6.4; metformin/glibenclamide 2nd line

  • Q: Postpartum GDM follow-up

A: 75g OGTT 6-12 weeks postpartum; then q1-3y lifelong (50% T2DM at 10y)

  • Q: T1DM pregnancy

A: Preconception HbA1c <6.5% (48 mmol/mol); pump/CGM; targets: fasting <5.3, 1h <7.8, 2h <6.4

  • Q: Postpartum insulin

A: ↓ requirements 30-50% (esp. breastfeeding); risk nocturnal hypoglycaemia

  • Q: Breastfeeding & thyroid drugs

A: PTU/MMI/levothyroxine safe; carbimazole <30mg/day safe; RAI contraindicated

18. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Transient gestational thyrotoxicosis (1st trimester), TSH suppressed, fT4 ↑ — hCG cross-reactivity with TSH receptor causes:
  1. 250 µg/day (vs 150 µg non-pregnant) — Iodine requirement in pregnancy (WHO):
  1. 1st: 0.1-2.5; 2nd: 0.2-3.0; 3rd: 0.3-3.0 mU/L — TSH trimester-specific reference ranges (ATA):
  1. Increase by 25-50% (2 extra doses/week) at confirmation; target TSH trimester-specific — Levothyroxine dose adjustment in pregnant hypothyroid woman:
  1. 1st trimester: Propylthiouracil (PTU) → 2nd/3rd: Carbimazole/Methimazole (switch at 16wks) — Graves disease in pregnancy: antithyroid drug of choice:
  1. Lower teratogenicity risk (aplasia cutis, choanal atresia with carbimazole) — PTU vs Carbimazole in pregnancy: PTU preferred 1st trimester because:
  1. Thyrotoxic phase (1-4mo) → hypothyroid (4-8mo) → recovery (12mo); 20-40% permanent hypothyroidism — Postpartum thyroiditis: typical course:
  1. 75g OGTT: fasting ≥5.1, 1h ≥10.0, 2h ≥8.5 mmol/L (one abnormal = GDM) — Gestational diabetes (GDM) diagnosis (IADPSG/WHO 2013):
  1. Medical nutrition therapy + glucose monitoring; insulin if targets not met — GDM management first-line:
  1. 75g OGTT at 6-12 weeks; then q1-3 years (lifelong T2DM risk 50% at 10y) — Postpartum follow-up after GDM:

SBAs

  1. Increase levothyroxine by ~25-30% (e.g., +25µg daily or 2 extra doses/week); recheck TSH in 4 weeks — 32yo woman, 8 weeks pregnant, known hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 100µg. TSH 4.5 (ref 0.1-2.5). Action?
  1. Switch to PTU 100-200mg/day (1st trimester); plan to switch back to carbimazole at 16 weeks — 28yo woman, 10 weeks: Graves disease, on carbimazole 15mg. TSH <0.01, fT4 35. Best switch?
  1. Fasting <5.3, 1h postprandial <7.8, 2h <6.4 mmol/L — 26yo woman, 26 weeks: GDM diagnosed (fasting 5.8, 1h 11.2, 2h 9.8). Diet-controlled. Targets?
  1. Decreases significantly (↓30-50%); risk hypoglycaemia; reduce basal/bolus — Postpartum day 3: woman with T1DM on insulin pump. Breastfeeding. Insulin requirement:
  1. Propranolol 20-40mg TDS for symptoms; observe (self-limiting); recheck TSH in 4-6 weeks — Postpartum thyroiditis at 3 months: symptomatic thyrotoxicosis (palpitations, tremor). Management?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.11.05 Genetic Endocrinology
[!info]
Genetic Endocrinology = Monogenic & Syndromic Endocrine Disorders. High-Yield for FCPS/MRCP: Inheritance Patterns, Gene-Disease Associations, Genetic Testing Criteria, and Counselling.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify genetic endocrine disorders by inheritance pattern
  • [ ] Recognise key gene-disease associations
  • [ ] Apply genetic testing criteria (ACGS/UKGTN)
  • [ ] Counsel on recurrence risk and cascade testing
  • [ ] Recognise when to refer for clinical genetics

2. Inheritance Patterns & Recurrence Risks

PatternRecurrence RiskKey Features
Autosomal Dominant (AD)50% (Each Pregnancy)Variable Penetrance, Variable Expressivity
Autosomal Recessive (AR)25% (Each Pregnancy)Both Parents Carriers; Consanguinity ↑ Risk
X-Linked Recessive (XLR)50% Sons Affected, 50% Daughters CarriersMales Affected; Females Carriers (May Be Symptomatic)
X-Linked Dominant (XLD)50% Each OffspringMales Often More Severe; Lethal in Males (e.g. Incontinentia Pigmenti)
MitochondrialMaternal Only (100% Offspring)Heteroplasmy (Variable Threshold)
ImprintingParent-of-Origin DependentMaternal (Prader-Willi) vs Paternal (Angelman)

3. Monogenic Diabetes

TypeGeneInheritanceKey FeaturesTreatment
HNF1A-MODY (MODY3)HNF1AADMost Common MODY; Progressive β-Cell Dysfunction; Sulfonylurea SensitiveLow-Dose Sulfonylurea (1st Line)
HNF4A-MODY (MODY1)HNF4AADMacrosomia, Neonatal Hypoglycaemia; Progressive; Sulfonylurea SensitiveSulfonylurea
GCK-MODY (MODY2)GCKADMild Fasting Hyperglycaemia (5.5-8 mmol/L); Non-Progressive; No Treatment NeededDiet Only
HNF1B-MODY (MODY5)HNF1BADRenal Cysts, Genital Tract Malformations, Pancreatic AgenesisInsulin Often Needed
Neonatal Diabetes (PNDM/TNDM)KCNJ11, ABCC8, INS, GCKAD/AR<6 Months; KCNJ11/ABCC8 → Sulfonylurea ResponsiveHigh-Dose Sulfonylurea (If K-ATP Channel)
Mitochondrial DiabetesmtDNA (3243 A>G)MaternalMELAS; Sensorineural Deafness, Short Stature; Insulin Often NeededInsulin (Sulfonylurea Contraindicated)

4. Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia (MEN)

SyndromeGeneInheritanceKey Tumours
MEN1MEN1 (Menin)ADParathyroid, Pituitary, Pancreas (3 Ps)
MEN2ARET (Codon 634)ADMTC, Phaeochromocytoma, PHPT
MEN2BRET (M918T)ADMTC, Phaeochromocytoma, Marfanoid, Neuromas
MEN4CDKN1B (p27)ADMEN1-Like; MEN1-Negative

5. Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH)

TypeGeneInheritanceEnzyme DeficiencyKey Features
21-Hydroxylase DeficiencyCYP21A2AR21-Hydroxylase (95%)Salt-Wasting, Simple Virilising, Non-Classic
11β-Hydroxylase DeficiencyCYP11B1AR11β-HydroxylaseHypertension, Virilisation
17α-Hydroxylase DeficiencyCYP17A1AR17α-HydroxylaseHypertension, Sexual Infantilism
3β-HSD DeficiencyHSD3B2AR3β-HSDSalt-Wasting, Ambiguous Genitalia

6. Calcium & Bone Disorders

DisorderGeneInheritanceKey Features
Familial Hypocalciuric Hypercalcaemia (FHH)CaSRADInactivating; Hypocalciuria (Ca/Cr <0.01); Benign
Neonatal Severe HPT (NSHPT)CaSRAR (Homozygous)Severe Neonatal Hypercalcaemia; Lethal if Untreated
Autosomal Dominant Hypocalcaemia (ADH)CaSRADActivating; Hypocalcaemia, Hypercalciuria
Hypophosphataemic Rickets (XLH)PHEXXLDPhosphate Wasting; FGF23 ↑; Rickets
Osteogenesis ImperfectaCOL1A1/COL1A2AD/ARBrittle Bones, Blue Sclerae, Deafness
Pseudohypoparathyroidism (PHP1a)GNASMaternal ImprintingAHO Phenotype + PTH Resistance

7. Pituitary & Growth Disorders

DisorderGeneInheritanceKey Features
Kallmann SyndromeANOS1 (KAL1), FGFR1, PROKR2, PROK2XLR/AD/ARAnosmia + Hypogonadotropic Hypogonadism
Combined Pituitary Hormone Deficiency (CPHD)PROP1, POU1F1, HESX1, LHX3, LHX4AR/ADMultiple Pituitary Hormone Deficiencies
Isolated GH DeficiencyGH1, GHRHRAR/ADShort Stature, Low GH/IGF-1
Laron Syndrome (GH Resistance)GHRARHigh GH, Low IGF-1, Short Stature
Septo-Optic DysplasiaHESX1AD/AROptic Nerve Hypoplasia, Pituitary Hypoplasia, Absent Septum Pellucidum

8. Thyroid Disorders

DisorderGeneInheritanceKey Features
Dyshormonogenic Congenital HypothyroidismDUOX2, DUOXA2, TG, TPO, SLC5A5 (NIS)ARGoitre + Hypothyroidism (Organification Defects)
Thyroid Hormone Resistance (RTH)THRBADHigh fT4/fT3, Non-Suppressed TSH; Goitre, Tachycardia
TSH ResistanceTSHRARElevated TSH, Normal/High Thyroid Hormones
Pendred SyndromeSLC26A4ARDeafness + Goitre (Thyroid Dyshormonogenesis)

9. Reproductive Disorders

DisorderGeneInheritanceKey Features
Turner Syndrome45,X / MosaicChromosomalShort Stature, Webbed Neck, Coarctation, Streak Ovaries
Klinefelter Syndrome47,XXYChromosomalTall, Small Testes, Gynaecomastia, Azoospermia
Androgen Insensitivity SyndromeARXLR46,XY; Female External Genitalia; Testes; Müllerian Absent
5α-Reductase DeficiencySRD5A2AR46,XY; Ambiguous Genitalia; Virilisation at Puberty
Mayer-Rokitansky-Küster-Hauser (MRKH)Unknown (WNT4, LHX1)AD/Complex46,XX; Müllerian Agenesis; Normal Ovarian Function
Premature Ovarian Insufficiency (POI)FMR1 (Premutation), BMP15, FOXL2X-Linked/AD<40 Years; FSH >40; Autoimmune/Gentic

10. Metabolic & Obesity Syndromes

SyndromeGeneInheritanceKey Features
Prader-Willi Syndrome15q11-q13 (Paternal Deletion/UPD Maternal)ImprintingNeonatal Hypotonia, Hyperphagia → Obesity, Hypogonadism, ID
Bardet-Biedl SyndromeMultiple BBS GenesARRetinal Dystrophy, Polydactyly, Obesity, Renal Anomalies, ID
Alström SyndromeALMS1ARObesity, Hear Loss, Cardiomyopathy, T2DM, Renal/Hepatic
Leptin DeficiencyLEPARSevere Early-Onset Obesity; Leptin Replacement Curative
Leptin Receptor DeficiencyLEPRARSimilar to Leptin Deficiency; No Leptin Therapy
MC4R DeficiencyMC4RAD/ARMost Common Monogenic Obesity; Hyperphagia

11. Genetic Testing — Indications & Approach

ACGS/UKGTN Testing Criteria

IndicationAction
Clinical Diagnosis SuspectedTargeted Gene Panel / Single Gene Test
Family HistoryCascade Testing (Index Case First → Relatives)
Atypical PresentationWhole Exome/Genome Sequencing
Neonatal/PaediatricRapid Exome/Genome (Trio)
Reproductive PlanningPrenatal (CVS/Amnio) or PGD (Preimplantation)

Counselling Essentials

ElementDetails
Pre-TestExplain Test Scope, Possible Results (VUS, Incidental), Implications
Post-TestResult Interpretation, Recurrence Risk, Cascade Testing Offer
VUS (Variant of Uncertain Significance)Do Not Use for Clinical Management; Segregation Studies; Functional Studies

12. Genetic Testing Strategies

ScenarioTest
MODY SuspectedHNF1A, HNF4A, GCK, HNF1B, PDX1, KLF11, CEL, BLK, KCNJ11, ABCC8 (Panel)
Neonatal DiabetesKCNJ11, ABCC8, INS, GCK, PDX1, PTF1A (Rapid Panel)
CAHCYP21A2 (21-OH Deficiency); CYP11B1, CYP17A1, HSD3B2 (Rare)
MEN1MEN1 Sequencing + MLPA (Deletion/Duplication)
MEN2RET (Codon-Specific: 634, 918, 883, etc.)
FHH/NSHPTCaSR
PHP1aGNAS (Methylation Analysis + Sequencing)
KallmannANOS1, FGFR1, PROKR2, PROK2, CHD7, SOX10
POIFMR1 (Premutation), BMP15, FOXL2
Monogenic ObesityLEP, LEPR, MC4R, PCSK1, POMC, SIM1, BDNF

13. Recurrence Risks & Counselling

ScenarioRecurrence RiskCounselling Point
AD (Parent Affected)50%Variable Penetrance/Expressivity
AR (Both Parents Carriers)25%Consanguinity ↑ Risk; Carrier Testing
XLR (Mother Carrier)50% Sons Affected; 50% Daughters CarriersPrenatal Sex Determination
Mitochondrial100% Maternal TransmissionHeteroplasmy Level Predicts Severity
Imprinting (PWS/AS)<1% (De Novo); 50% if Parent Carries Imprinting DefectImprinting Centre Analysis

14. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
MODY TypesHNF1A (MODY3) = Sulfonylurea Sensitive; GCK (MODY2) = No Treatment; HNF1B = Renal Cysts
Neonatal DiabetesKCNJ11/ABCC8 → Sulfonylurea Responsive (High Dose)
CAH21-OH Deficiency (CYP21A2) = 95%; Salt-Wasting vs Simple Virilising vs Non-Classic
MEN1MEN1 Gene (Menin); AD; 3 Ps (Parathyroid, Pituitary, Pancreas)
MEN2ARET Codon 634; MTC + Phaeo + HPT
MEN2BRET M918T; MTC + Phaeo + Marfanoid + Neuromas
FHHCaSR Inactivating; Ca/Cr <0.01; Benign
NSHPTCaSR Homozygous; Severe Neonatal Hypercalcaemia
PHP1aGNAS (Maternal Imprinting); AHO + PTH Resistance
KallmannAnosmia + HH; ANOS1 (XLR), FGFR1 (AD), PROKR2 (AR)
Turner45,X; Short Stature, Webbed Neck, Coarctation, Streak Ovaries
Klinefelter47,XXY; Small Testes, Gynaecomastia, Azoospermia
AISAR Mutation; 46,XY Female Phenotype; Testes + No Müllerian
Prader-Willi15q11-q13 Paternal Del/UPD Mat; Hypotonia → Hyperphagia → Obesity
Bardet-BiedlRetinal Dystrophy, Polydactyly, Obesity, Renal, ID
Leptin DeficiencyLEP Gene; Severe Early Obesity; Leptin Replacement Curative
MC4RMost Common Monogenic Obesity (AD); Hyperphagia
RTHTHRB Mutation (AD); High fT4/fT3 + Non-Suppressed TSH
Genetic TestingTargeted Panel → Exome/Genome; Cascade Testing Essential

15. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
MODY vs T1DM/T2DMMODY = Young (<25), AD Family Hx, AutoAb Negative, C-Peptide Preserved
HNF1A vs HNF4ABoth Sulfonylurea Sensitive; HNF4A = Macrosomia + Neonatal Hypoglycaemia
GCK-MODYMild Fasting Hyperglycaemia (5.5-8); No Treatment
Neonatal Diabetes<6 Months; KCNJ11/ABCC8 = Sulfonylurea Responsive
MEN1 vs MEN2MEN1 = 3Ps; MEN2 = MTC + Phaeo ± HPT
MEN2A vs MEN2BMEN2A = HPT; MEN2B = Marfanoid/Neuromas (No HPT)
FHH vs PHPFHH = CaSR Inactivating (Hypocalciuria); PHP = GNAS (PTH Resistance)
NSHPTCaSR Homozygous/Compound Het; Severe Neonatal Hypercalcaemia
Kallmann vs Isolated HHKallmann = Anosmia; Isolated HH = No Anosmia
Turner vs NoonanTurner = 45,X; Webbed Neck, Coarctation; Noonan = Normal Karyotype, PS
AIS vs 5α-ReductaseAIS = 46,XY Female; Testes; No Müllerian; 5α-Red = Ambiguous → Virilises at Puberty
PHP1aGNAS Maternal Imprinting; AHO + PTH Resistance
ImprintingPWS = Paternal 15q Del / Maternal UPD; AS = Maternal 15q Del / Paternal UPD
Mitochondrial Diabetes3243 A>G; Maternal Inheritance; Deafness + Diabetes

16. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Genetic Endocrinology))
    Inheritance
      AD: 50% (MEN1, MEN2, MODY, FHH, PHP1a)
      AR: 25% (CAH, PNDM, XLH, PWS/AS if paternal del)
      XLR: 50% Sons (Kallmann ANOS1, AIS, XLH)
      XLD: 50% (XLH, PWS/AS if maternal del)
      Mitochondrial: Maternal (3243 A>G)
      Imprinting: PWS/AS, GNAS (PHP1a)
    Monogenic Diabetes
      HNF1A (MODY3): SU Sensitive
      HNF4A (MODY1): SU Sensitive + Macrosomia
      GCK (MODY2): No Rx
      HNF1B (MODY5): Renal Cysts
      Neonatal DM: KCNJ11/ABCC8 → SU
      Mitochondrial: 3243 A>G + Deafness
    MEN
      MEN1: MEN1 (Menin) - 3 Ps
      MEN2A: RET 634 - MTC, Phaeo, HPT
      MEN2B: RET M918T - MTC, Phaeo, Marfanoid
      MEN4: CDKN1B - MEN1-like
    CAH
      21-OH (CYP21A2) - 95%
      11β-OH (CYP11B1) - HTN + Virilisation
      17α-OH (CYP17A1) - HTN + Infantilism
    Calcium/Bone
      FHH: CaSR Inactivating - Ca/Cr <0.01
      NSHPT: CaSR Homozygous
      ADH: CaSR Activating
      XLH: PHEX - Phosphate Wasting
      PHP1a: GNAS Maternal - AHO + PTH Resistance
    Pituitary/Growth
      Kallmann: Anosmia + HH (ANOS1 XLR)
      CPHD: PROP1, POU1F1, HESX1
      Laron: GHR - High GH, Low IGF-1
    Thyroid
      RTH: THRB - High fT4/fT3 + Non-Supp TSH
      Dyshormonogenic CH: DUOX2, TG, TPO, NIS
    Reproductive
      Turner: 45,X - Short, Webbed Neck, Coarctation
      Klinefelter: 47,XXY - Small Testes, Gynaecomastia
      AIS: AR - 46,XY Female + Testes
      MRKH: Müllerian Agenesis
    Obesity
      Prader-Willi: 15q11-q13 Paternal Del
      Bardet-Biedl: Retinal, Polydactyly, Obesity
      Leptin/LEPR/MC4R: Monogenic Obesity
    Testing
      Targeted Panels (MODY, CAH, MEN, Kallmann)
      Exome/Genome (Atypical/Neonatal)
      Cascade Testing (Family)
      Prenatal/PGD

17. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
MODY3 (HNF1A)Most Common; Sulfonylurea Sensitive
MODY2 (GCK)Mild Fasting Hyperglycaemia; No Treatment
Neonatal Diabetes<6 Months; KCNJ11/ABCC8 → Sulfonylurea Responsive
CAH21-OH Deficiency = 95% (CYP21A2)
MEN1 GeneMEN1 (Menin); AD; 3 Ps
MEN2A MutationRET Codon 634 (85%)
MEN2B MutationRET M918T (95%)
FHHCaSR Inactivating; Ca/Cr <0.01; Benign
NSHPTCaSR Homozygous; Severe Neonatal Hypercalcaemia
PHP1aGNAS (Maternal Imprinting); AHO + PTH Resistance
KallmannAnosmia + HH; ANOS1 (XLR), FGFR1 (AD)
Turner Syndrome45,X; Short Stature, Webbed Neck, Coarctation, Streak Ovaries
Klinefelter47,XXY; Small Testes, Gynaecomastia, Azoospermia
AISAR Mutation; 46,XY Female + Testes; No Müllerian
Prader-Willi15q11-q13 Paternal Del/UPD Mat; Hypotonia → Hyperphagia → Obesity
MC4RMost Common Monogenic Obesity (AD); Hyperphagia
RTHTHRB Mutation (AD); High fT4/fT3 + Non-Suppressed TSH
Genetic TestingPanel → Exome/Genome; Cascade Testing Essential

18. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Special Topics|Special Topics in Endocrinology]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[MEN1]], [[MEN2A]], [[MEN2B]], [[MEN4]], [[Monogenic Diabetes]], [[Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia]], [[FHH]], [[Kallmann Syndrome]], [[Turner Syndrome]], [[Klinefelter Syndrome]], [[Prader-Willi Syndrome]]

19. MCQs (10)

  1. MEN1 gene location and protein:

A. 11q13; Menin (tumour suppressor, transcriptional regulation)

B. 17p13; p53

C. 10q23; PTEN

D. 3p25; VHL

E. 11p15; CDKN1C

  1. MEN2A/2B gene and mutation pattern:

A. RET proto-oncogene (10q11); gain-of-function mutations (Cys 634 for 2A, M918T for 2B)

B. MEN1

C. CDKN1B

D. VHL

E. SDHx

  1. MEN4 (CDKN1B) phenotype:

A. MEN1-like (parathyroid, pituitary, pancreas) but MEN1 negative; p27 loss

B. MEN2-like

C. Isolated hyperparathyroidism

D. Only pituitary

E. Only pancreatic NETs

  1. Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia inheritance:

A. All autosomal dominant

B. Autosomal recessive

C. X-linked

D. Mitochondrial

E. Sporadic only

  1. Familial isolated hyperparathyroidism (FIHP) genes:

A. MEN1, CDC73 (HRPT2), CASR, GCM2

B. RET

C. VHL

D. SDHx

E. CDKN1B

  1. Familial hypocalciuric hypercalcaemia (FHH) type 1:

A. CASR inactivating mutation (loss of function) → ↑ Ca²⁺ set-point

B. CASR activating

C. PTH mutation

D. VDR mutation

E. CYP27B1 mutation

  1. Neonatal diabetes mellitus (transient) most common gene:

A. 6q24 methylation defect (TNDM); KCNJ11/ABCC8 (permanent)

B. INS

C. GCK

D. HNF1A

E. HNF4A

  1. MODY 1 (HNF4A) and MODY 3 (HNF1A) features:

A. Progressive beta-cell dysfunction; sensitive to sulfonylureas; no microvascular complications early

B. Insulin resistance

C. Autoimmune

D. Ketosis-prone

E. Neonatal onset

  1. Thyroid dysgenesis genes:

A. TTF1 (NKX2-1), TTF2 (FOXE1), PAX8, TSHR, DUOX2

B. RET

C. MEN1

D. CASR

E. GNAS

  1. Resistance syndromes: Pseudohypoparathyroidism type 1a:

A. GNAS mutation (maternal imprinting) → Albright hereditary osteodystrophy + multi-hormone resistance

B. PTH mutation

C. VDR mutation

D. CASR mutation

E. CYP27B1 mutation

20. SBA Questions (5)

  1. 30yo woman: hypercalcaemia, PTH 12, urinary calcium/creatinine clearance ratio 0.01. Mother similar. Gene?

A. CASR (FHH type 1) - inactivating mutation

B. MEN1

C. RET

D. CDC73

E. CDKN1B

  1. 25yo man: medullary thyroid cancer, phaeochromocytoma, marfanoid habitus, mucosal neuromas. RET mutation?

A. M918T (MEN2B, exon 16)

B. Cys 634 (MEN2A, exon 11)

C. VHL

D. SDHB

E. MEN1

  1. 10yo girl: diabetes diagnosed 6 months, non-obese, negative antibodies, strong family history (3 generations). Sulfonylurea responsive. Gene?

A. HNF1A (MODY 3) or HNF4A (MODY 1)

B. GCK (MODY 2)

C. INS

D. KCNJ11

E. MT-TL1

  1. Neonate: diabetes at 2 weeks, remission by 6 months, macrosomia. Genetic test?

A. 6q24 methylation analysis (TNDM)

B. KCNJ11

C. GCK

D. HNF1A

E. INS

  1. Family: hyperparathyroidism, pituitary adenoma, gastrinoma. MEN1 negative. Next gene?

A. CDKN1B (MEN4)

B. RET

C. VHL

D. SDHx

E. CDC73

21. Flashcards

  • Q: MEN1

A: 11q13; Menin; 3Ps: Parathyroid (95%), Pituitary (30-40%), Pancreas (duodenal gastrinoma, insulinoma)

  • Q: MEN2A

A: RET (10q11) Cys 634; MTC 95%, Phaeo 50%, pHPT 20-30%

  • Q: MEN2B

A: RET M918T (95%); MTC 100%, Phaeo 50%, Marfanoid, Mucosal neuromas, NO pHPT

  • Q: MEN4

A: CDKN1B (12p13); p27Kip1 loss; MEN1-like phenotype but MEN1 negative

  • Q: FHH

A: CASR inactivating → ↑ Ca set-point; high Ca, normal/mildly ↑ PTH, low urine Ca (CCCR <0.01)

  • Q: Neonatal DM transient

A: 6q24 methylation (TNDM); macrosomia; remission ~6mo; relapse possible

  • Q: Neonatal DM permanent

A: KCNJ11 (Kir6.2), ABCC8 (SUR1); sulfonylurea responsive; developmental delay possible

  • Q: MODY 1 (HNF4A)

A: Progressive beta-cell failure; macrosomia at birth; sulfonylurea sensitive

  • Q: MODY 2 (GCK)

A: Glucokinase; mild fasting hyperglycaemia (5.5-8); non-progressive; NO treatment needed

  • Q: MODY 3 (HNF1A)

A: Most common MODY; progressive; sulfonylurea sensitive; renal cysts possible

  • Q: MODY 5 (HNF1B)

A: Renal cysts/dysplasia + diabetes; genital tract malformations

  • Q: Thyroid dysgenesis

A: TTF1/NKX2-1 (lung+thyroid), FOXE1/TTF2, PAX8, TSHR, DUOX2

  • Q: Pseudohypoparathyroidism 1a

A: GNAS (maternal imprinting); Albright hereditary osteodystrophy (short stature, brachydactyly, subcutaneous ossification) + PTH/TSH/GHRH resistance

  • Q: Albright hereditary osteodystrophy

A: GNAS mutation (paternal = PHP1b - no AHO; maternal = PHP1a + AHO)

22. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. 11q13; Menin (tumour suppressor, transcriptional regulation) — MEN1 gene location and protein:
  1. RET proto-oncogene (10q11); gain-of-function mutations (Cys 634 for 2A, M918T for 2B) — MEN2A/2B gene and mutation pattern:
  1. MEN1-like (parathyroid, pituitary, pancreas) but MEN1 negative; p27 loss — MEN4 (CDKN1B) phenotype:
  1. All autosomal dominant — Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia inheritance:
  1. MEN1, CDC73 (HRPT2), CASR, GCM2 — Familial isolated hyperparathyroidism (FIHP) genes:
  1. CASR inactivating mutation (loss of function) → ↑ Ca²⁺ set-point — Familial hypocalciuric hypercalcaemia (FHH) type 1:
  1. 6q24 methylation defect (TNDM); KCNJ11/ABCC8 (permanent) — Neonatal diabetes mellitus (transient) most common gene:
  1. Progressive beta-cell dysfunction; sensitive to sulfonylureas; no microvascular complications early — MODY 1 (HNF4A) and MODY 3 (HNF1A) features:
  1. TTF1 (NKX2-1), TTF2 (FOXE1), PAX8, TSHR, DUOX2 — Thyroid dysgenesis genes:
  1. GNAS mutation (maternal imprinting) → Albright hereditary osteodystrophy + multi-hormone resistance — Resistance syndromes: Pseudohypoparathyroidism type 1a:

SBAs

  1. CASR (FHH type 1) - inactivating mutation — 30yo woman: hypercalcaemia, PTH 12, urinary calcium/creatinine clearance ratio 0.01. Mother similar. Gene?
  1. M918T (MEN2B, exon 16) — 25yo man: medullary thyroid cancer, phaeochromocytoma, marfanoid habitus, mucosal neuromas. RET mutation?
  1. HNF1A (MODY 3) or HNF4A (MODY 1) — 10yo girl: diabetes diagnosed 6 months, non-obese, negative antibodies, strong family history (3 generations). Sulfonylurea responsive. Gene?
  1. 6q24 methylation analysis (TNDM) — Neonate: diabetes at 2 weeks, remission by 6 months, macrosomia. Genetic test?
  1. CDKN1B (MEN4) — Family: hyperparathyroidism, pituitary adenoma, gastrinoma. MEN1 negative. Next gene?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.11.06 Lipodystrophy Syndromes
[!info]
Lipodystrophy = Heterogeneous Group of Disorders Characterised by Selective Loss of Adipose Tissue. Congenital (Genetic) vs Acquired; Generalised vs Partial. Severe Metabolic Complications: Insulin Resistance, Diabetes, Hypertriglyceridaemia, Hepatic Steatosis, Pancreatitis, Cardiomyopathy. Leptin Replacement (Metreleptin) = Disease-Modifying Therapy for Generalised Forms.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify lipodystrophy (Generalised vs Partial, Congenital vs Acquired)
  • [ ] Recognise clinical features and metabolic complications
  • [ ] Apply metabolic monitoring and management strategies
  • [ ] Prescribe metreleptin (leptin replacement) for eligible patients
  • [ ] Counsel on genetic testing and family screening

2. Classification

CategorySubtypes
Congenital Generalised Lipodystrophy (CGL)CGL1 (AGPAT2), CGL2 (BSCL2), CGL3 (CAV1), CGL4 (PTRF/CAVIN1)
Acquired Generalised Lipodystrophy (AGL)Lawrence Syndrome (Autoimmune, Post-Infectious, Idiopathic)
Familial Partial Lipodystrophy (FPLD)FPLD1 (Unknown), FPLD2 (LMNA), FPLD3 (PPARG), FPLD4 (PLIN1), FPLD5 (CIDEC), FPLD6 (LIPE)
Acquired Partial Lipodystrophy (APL)Barraquer-Simons Syndrome (Complement 3 Nephritic Factor)
HIV-Associated LipodystrophyART-Related (NRTI, PI) — Lipoatrophy (Face, Limbs) ± Lipohypertrophy (Visceral)

3. Major Subtypes — Clinical Features

Congenital Generalised Lipodystrophy (CGL) — Berardinelli-Seip Syndrome

FeatureCGL1 (AGPAT2)CGL2 (BSCL2)CGL3 (CAV1)CGL4 (PTRF)
Adipose LossNear-Total (Birth/Infancy)Near-TotalNear-TotalNear-Total
Metabolic SeveritySevereMost SevereSevereSevere
Additional FeaturesHepatomegaly, AcromegaloidMental Retardation, CardiomyopathyCardiac, RhabdomyolysisMyopathy, Pyloric Stenosis
LeptinVery Low/UndetectableVery LowLowLow

Familial Partial Lipodystrophy Type 2 (FPLD2, Dunnigan Variety)

FeatureDetails
GeneLMNA (Lamin A/C) — AD
Fat LossLimbs, Gluteal, Truncal (Puberty Onset)
Fat AccumulationFace, Neck, Intra-Abdominal (Cushingoid)
MetabolicSevere Insulin Resistance, Diabetes, HyperTG, PCOS (Women)
CardiacConduction Defects, Cardiomyopathy (LMNA Cardiac Phenotype)
MuscularMuscular Dystrophy (Some)

Familial Partial Lipodystrophy Type 3 (FPLD3)

FeatureDetails
GenePPARG (Peroxisome Proliferator-Activated Receptor Gamma) — AD
Fat LossLimbs, Gluteal
MetabolicEarly-Onset Diabetes, Severe IR, Hypertension
OtherAcanthosis Nigricans, PCOS

Acquired Generalised Lipodystrophy (AGL) — Lawrence Syndrome

FeatureDetails
OnsetChildhood/Adolescence (After Normal Infancy)
TriggerAutoimmune (Panniculitis), Infections (Measles, Varicella), Idiopathic
Fat LossProgressive, Generalised (Including Face, Palms, Soles)
AutoimmuneHigh Association (SLE, RA, Autoimmune Hepatitis, Low C3/C4)
MetabolicSevere IR, Diabetes, HyperTG, Hepatic Steatosis

Acquired Partial Lipodystrophy (APL) — Barraquer-Simons Syndrome

FeatureDetails
OnsetChildhood/Adolescence
Fat LossCephalocaudal (Face, Neck, Arms, Trunk) → Lower Body Spared
GenderFemale > Male (4:1)
ComplementC3 Nephritic Factor (C3NeF) → Low C3, Normal C4
RenalMembranoproliferative GN (MPGN) Type 1 (20-25%)
AutoimmuneAssociated Autoimmunity (Precedes MPGN)

HIV-Associated Lipodystrophy

FeatureDetails
LipoatrophyFace, Limbs, Buttocks (NRTI: Stavudine, Zidovudine, Didanosine)
LipohypertrophyVisceral Abdomen, Dorsocervical Fat Pad (Buffalo Hump), Breasts (PI: Lopinavir, Indinavir)
MetabolicInsulin Resistance, Diabetes, HyperTG, Low HDL
ManagementSwitch ART (Avoid Stavudine/Zidovudine); Tesamorelin (GH-RH Analogue) for Visceral Fat

4. Metabolic Complications (Universal)

ComplicationMechanismManagement
Severe Insulin Resistance↓ Adipose Tissue → ↓ Leptin → ↓ Insulin SensitivityMetreleptin (If Generalised); High-Dose Insulin; Metformin; TZDs (Cautious)
Diabetes MellitusInsulin Resistance + β-Cell FailureHigh-Dose Insulin (Often >2 U/kg/day); Metreleptin Improves Glycaemia
HypertriglyceridaemiaImpaired Clearance + Hepatic OverproductionFibrates (Fenofibrate), Omega-3, Metreleptin ↓ TG; Avoid Alcohol/Oestrogen
Acute PancreatitisSevere HyperTG (>10-20 mmol/L)Aggressive TG Lowering; Insulin Infusion; Plasmapheresis (Emergency)
Hepatic Steatosis / NASHEctopic Fat DepositionWeight Loss (If Partial); Metreleptin ↓ Liver Fat; Pioglitazone (Cautious)
CardiomyopathyLipotoxicity, Metabolic StressEcho Surveillance; Metreleptin May Improve; Standard HF Therapy
PCOS / Hyperandrogenism (Women)Insulin Resistance → Ovarian Theca Cell StimulationCOCP, Metformin, Weight Loss (If Partial)
Acanthosis NigricansInsulin → IGF-1 Receptor Cross-ActivationMarker of Severe IR

5. Leptin Replacement — Metreleptin

AspectDetails
IndicationGeneralised Lipodystrophy (CGL, AGL) with Metabolic Complications
MechanismRecombinant Human Leptin → Restores Leptin Signalling → ↑ Insulin Sensitivity, ↓ Hepatic Gluconeogenesis, ↓ Lipogenesis
Dose0.06-0.12 mg/kg/day SC (Start Low, Titrate by 0.02 mg/kg q2-4wk)
Max Dose10mg/day (Women), 5mg/day (Men)
EfficacyHbA1c ↓ 1.5-2.5%; TG ↓ 30-60%; Liver Fat ↓ 30-50%
MonitoringHbA1c, TG, Liver Fat (MRI/MRE), Weight, Anti-Leptin Antibodies
Adverse EffectsAnti-Leptin Antibodies (Rare Neutralising), Injection Site Reactions, Hypoglycaemia (With Insulin), T-Cell Lymphoma Risk (Theoretical)
ContraindicationsGeneralised Obesity (Not Indicated); Pregnancy (Category C)

6. Metabolic Management Algorithm

Lipodystrophy Diagnosed
         │
         ▼
ASSESS: Type (Generalised vs Partial), Metabolic Severity
         │
         ├── GENERALISED (CGL, AGL)
         │       ├── METRELEPTIN INDICATED
         │       │       0.06 mg/kg/day SC → Titrate
         │       │       Target: HbA1c <53, TG <2.3, Liver Fat ↓
         │       │
         │       ├── DIABETES
         │       │       High-Dose Insulin (Often 1-2+ U/kg/day)
         │       │       Metformin (Adjunct)
         │       │       TZDs (Cautious — Weight Gain, HF Risk)
         │       │
         │       ├── HYPERTRIGLYCERIDAEMIA
         │       │       Fenofibrate 145-200mg/day
         │       │       Omega-3 (2-4g/day)
         │       │       Metreleptin (Primary)
         │       │       Emergency: TG >20 mmol/L → Insulin Infusion ± Plasmapheresis
         │       │
         │       └── HEPATIC STEATOSIS
         │               Ultrasound/MRI q1-2yr; Metreleptin; Pioglitazone (If Indicated)
         │
         └── PARTIAL (FPLD, APL)
                 ├── LIFESTYLE (Weight Maintenance/Reduction)
                 ├── METFORMIN (1st Line for IR)
                 ├── TZDs (Pioglitazone) — **If No HF** (↑ Adipogenesis in Remaining Depots)
                 ├── SGLT2i / GLP-1 RA (Cardio-Renal Benefit)
                 ├── INSULIN (If Uncontrolled)
                 └── METRELEPTIN — **Not Routine** (Consider if Severe Metabolic Despite Above)

7. Monitoring & Surveillance

ParameterFrequencyTarget
HbA1cq3 Months<48-53 mmol/mol (6.5-7.0%)
Fasting Lipids (TG, HDL, LDL)q3-6 MonthsTG <2.3 mmol/L; HDL >1.0
Liver Function (ALT, AST, GGT)q3-6 MonthsNormal
Liver Fat (MRI-PDFF / FibroScan)Annual↓ Trend
Cardiac EchoAnnual (CGL/FPLD2)LV Function; Conduction (FPLD2)
Renal Functionq6 MonthsNormal (APL: Monitor for MPGN)
Complement (C3, C4, C3NeF)q6-12 Months (APL)C3 Normalisation
Anti-Leptin Antibodiesq6-12 Months (On Metreleptin)Non-Neutralising
Bone Density (DEXA)q2-3 YearsT-Score >-2.5

8. Special Considerations

Pregnancy in Lipodystrophy

AspectManagement
MetreleptinContraindicated (Category C); Stop Pre-Conception
DiabetesInsulin Intensification (Basal-Bolus); Targets: FBG <5.3, 1h <7.8, 2h <6.4
HyperTGFenofibrate (Stop if Pregnant); Omega-3; Insulin Infusion if TG >10 mmol/L
DeliveryMultidisciplinary (Endo, OB, Cardiology if Cardiac Involvement)

Paediatric Considerations

AspectManagement
GrowthMonitor Height Velocity; Metreleptin May ↑ Growth Velocity
PubertyDelayed in CGL; Monitor LH/FSH/Sex Steroids
Metreleptin DosingWeight-Based (0.06-0.12 mg/kg); Adjust q3mo for Growth
CardiacEcho q6-12mo (CGL2/BSCL2 High Cardiac Risk)

9. Genetic Testing & Counselling

SyndromeGeneTestCounselling
CGL1AGPAT2Sequencing + MLPAAR; 25% Recurrence; Prenatal/PGD
CGL2BSCL2Sequencing + MLPAAR; 25%; Cardiac + Neuro Risk
CGL3CAV1SequencingAR; 25%
CGL4PTRF (CAVIN1)SequencingAR; 25%; Myopathy, Pyloric Stenosis
FPLD2LMNASequencingAD; 50%; Cardiac Conduction Risk
FPLD3PPARGSequencingAD; 50%
FPLD4PLIN1SequencingAD
APLC3NeF (Acquired)Complement StudiesNot Genetic (Somatic)

10. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Lipodystrophy ClassificationGeneralised (CGL, AGL) vs Partial (FPLD, APL, HIV)
CGL (Berardinelli-Seip)Near-Total Fat Loss from Birth; Severe IR; Metreleptin Indicated
FPLD2 (Dunnigan)LMNA (AD); Limb/Gluteal Fat Loss; Face/Neck/Visceral Fat Gain; Cardiac Risk
FPLD3PPARG; Early Diabetes, HTN
AGL (Lawrence)Acquired Generalised; Autoimmune/Panniculitis Trigger; Severe Metabolic
APL (Barraquer-Simons)Cephalocaudal Fat Loss; C3NeF + Low C3; MPGN Risk
HIV LipodystrophyNRTI → Lipoatrophy; PI → Lipohypertrophy; Switch ART
MetreleptinIndicated for Generalised (CGL, AGL) with Metabolic Complications
Metreleptin Dose0.06-0.12 mg/kg/day SC; Max 10mg (F) / 5mg (M)
Metreleptin EffectsHbA1c ↓ 1.5-2.5%; TG ↓ 30-60%; Liver Fat ↓
FPLD2 CardiacLMNA → Conduction Defects, Cardiomyopathy; Echo + ECG Surveillance
APL RenalC3NeF + Low C3 → MPGN (20-25%); Complement Monitoring
FPLD2 vs FPLD3FPLD2 = LMNA (Cardiac Risk); FPLD3 = PPARG (Early DM, HTN)
Leptin in ObesityNot Indicated (Leptin Resistance, Not Deficiency)
PancreatitisTG >10-20 mmol/L → Insulin Infusion ± Plasmapheresis
Cardiac EchoAnnual in CGL/FPLD2 (Conduction, Cardiomyopathy)
CGL1-CGL4CGL1=AGPAT2; CGL2=BSCL2 (Neuro/Cardiac); CGL3=CAV1; CGL4=PTRF (Myopathy)

11. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Generalised vs PartialGeneralised = Near-Total Fat Loss (CGL, AGL); Partial = Regional (FPLD, APL)
CGL vs AGLCGL = Congenital (Birth); AGL = Acquired (Childhood/Adolescence)
FPLD2 vs FPLD3FPLD2 = LMNA (Cardiac Risk); FPLD3 = PPARG (Early DM/HTN)
APL vs FPLDAPL = Acquired, Cephalocaudal, Female Predominant, C3NeF; FPLD = Genetic, AD
Metreleptin IndicationGeneralised Lipodystrophy ONLY (Not Partial, Not Obesity)
Metreleptin in ObesityContraindicated (Leptin Resistance, Not Deficiency)
FPLD2 CardiacLMNA → Conduction Defects, Cardiomyopathy (Not in Other FPLD)
APL RenalC3NeF + Low C3 → MPGN (20-25%)
HIV LipodystrophyNRTI (Stavudine) → Lipoatrophy; PI → Lipohypertrophy
Leptin Deficiency vs LipodystrophyLeptin Deficiency = Monogenic (LEP); Lipodystrophy = Fat Loss → Low Leptin
CGL2 (BSCL2)Neurodevelopmental Delay + Cardiomyopathy (Unique)
ACL vs APLAGL = Generalised; APL = Partial (Cephalocaudal)
Metreleptin Max Dose10mg/day (Women), 5mg/day (Men)

12. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Lipodystrophy))
    Classification
      Generalised
        CGL (Congenital): AGPAT2, BSCL2, CAV1, PTRF
        AGL (Acquired): Lawrence (Autoimmune/Panniculitis)
      Partial
        FPLD (Familial): LMNA (FPLD2), PPARG (3), PLIN1 (4), CIDEC (5), LIPE (6)
        APL (Acquired): Barraquer-Simons (C3NeF)
        HIV: ART-Related (NRTI Lipoatrophy, PI Lipohypertrophy)
    Metabolic Complications
      Severe IR, Diabetes, HyperTG, Pancreatitis, Hepatic Steatosis, Cardiomyopathy, PCOS
    Metreleptin
      Indication: Generalised (CGL, AGL) + Metabolic Complications
      Dose: 0.06-0.12 mg/kg SC; Titrate; Max 10mg (F)/5mg (M)
      Effects: HbA1c↓1.5-2.5%, TG↓30-60%, Liver Fat↓30-50%
      AE: Anti-Leptin Abs, Injection Site, Hypoglycaemia (with Insulin)
    FPLD2 (Dunnigan)
      LMNA (AD)
      Limb/Gluteal Loss + Face/Neck/Visceral Gain
      Cardiac: Conduction Defects, Cardiomyopathy
    APL (Barraquer-Simons)
      Cephalocaudal Fat Loss (Face→Trunk)
      C3NeF → Low C3 → MPGN (20-25%)
      Female > Male (4:1)
    CGL Types
      CGL1: AGPAT2
      CGL2: BSCL2 (Neuro/Cardiac)
      CGL3: CAV1
      CGL4: PTRF (Myopathy, Pyloric Stenosis)
    Monitoring
      HbA1c q3mo; TG q3-6mo; Liver Fat MRI Annual; Echo Annual (FPLD2/CGL)
      Complement q6-12mo (APL); Anti-Leptin Abs q6-12mo (Metreleptin)

13. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Lipodystrophy TypesGeneralised (CGL, AGL) vs Partial (FPLD, APL, HIV)
CGLNear-Total Fat Loss from Birth; Metreleptin Indicated
FPLD2 (Dunnigan)LMNA (AD); Limb/Gluteal Loss + Face/Neck/Visceral Gain; Cardiac Risk
FPLD3PPARG; Early Diabetes, Hypertension
AGL (Lawrence)Acquired Generalised; Autoimmune Trigger; Severe Metabolic
APL (Barraquer-Simons)Cephalocaudal Fat Loss; C3NeF + Low C3; MPGN (20-25%)
HIV LipodystrophyNRTI → Lipoatrophy; PI → Lipohypertrophy
MetreleptinGeneralised Only; 0.06-0.12 mg/kg SC; HbA1c↓, TG↓, Liver Fat↓
FPLD2 CardiacLMNA → Conduction Defects, Cardiomyopathy
APL RenalC3NeF + Low C3 → MPGN (20-25%)
Metreleptin Dose0.06-0.12 mg/kg SC; Max 10mg (F)/5mg (M)
PancreatitisTG >10-20 mmol/L → Insulin Infusion ± Plasmapheresis
CGL2 (BSCL2)Neurodevelopmental Delay + Cardiomyopathy
CGL4 (PTRF)Myopathy + Pyloric Stenosis
Leptin in ObesityContraindicated (Resistance, Not Deficiency)
CGL vs AGLCGL = Birth; AGL = Acquired Childhood
FPLD vs APLFPLD = Genetic (AD); APL = Acquired (C3NeF)

14. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Special Topics|Special Topics in Endocrinology]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Insulin Resistance]], [[Metabolic Syndrome]], [[Leptin Deficiency]], [[Genetic Endocrinology]], [[HIV Lipodystrophy]], [[Familial Partial Lipodystrophy]]

15. MCQs (10)

  1. Congenital generalized lipodystrophy (CGL) type 1 (Berardinelli-Seip):

A. AGPAT2 mutation (1-acylglycerol-3-phosphate O-acyltransferase 2)

B. BSCL2

C. CAV1

D. PTRF

E. LMNA

  1. CGL type 2 (BSCL2/seipin):

A. Severe, early onset; associated with intellectual disability, cardiomyopathy

B. Mild, late onset

C. Only metabolic

D. Autoimmune

E. Infectious

  1. Familial partial lipodystrophy type 1 (FPLD1, Kobberling):

A. Unknown gene; femora-gluteal fat loss; insulin resistance

B. LMNA

C. PPARG

D. PLIN1

E. CIDEC

  1. FPLD2 (Dunnigan):

A. LMNA mutation (lamin A/C); fat loss limbs/trunk, excess face/neck (cushingoid)

B. PPARG

C. PLIN1

D. CIDEC

E. AKT2

  1. FPLD3 (PPARG):

A. PPARG mutation; partial lipodystrophy + severe insulin resistance + hypertension

B. LMNA

C. PLIN1

D. CIDEC

E. AKT2

  1. Acquired generalized lipodystrophy (AGL, Lawrence):

A. Autoimmune (panniculitis, complement); onset childhood/adolescence; ↓ C3, C3NeF

B. Genetic

C. Drug-induced

D. Infectious

E. HIV-related only

  1. HIV-associated lipodystrophy:

A. NRTI (stavudine, zidovudine) → lipoatrophy limbs/face; PI → central adiposity

B. Only lipoatrophy

C. Only central obesity

D. Neither

E. Only with older ART

  1. Metabolic complications of lipodystrophy:

A. Severe insulin resistance → diabetes, hypertriglyceridaemia, NAFLD, PCOS

B. Only diabetes

C. Only hypertriglyceridaemia

D. Only NAFLD

E. None

  1. Leptin replacement (metreleptin) indicated for:

A. Generalized lipodystrophy (CGL, AGL) with metabolic complications

B. Partial lipodystrophy only

C. HIV lipodystrophy only

D. Obesity

E. Type 2 diabetes without lipodystrophy

  1. Metreleptin monitoring:

A. HbA1c, triglycerides, liver enzymes, anti-leptin antibodies, TSH

B. Only glucose

C. Only lipids

D. Only weight

E. No monitoring

16. SBA Questions (5)

  1. 6yo girl: near-total fat loss since infancy, acanthosis nigricans, hepatomegaly, diabetes, hypertriglyceridaemia. Genetic testing: AGPAT2 mutation. Diagnosis?

A. CGL type 1 (Berardinelli-Seip)

B. CGL type 2

C. FPLD2

D. AGL

E. HIV lipodystrophy

  1. 35yo woman: progressive fat loss from limbs/trunk since puberty, cushingoid facies, diabetes, severe hypertriglyceridaemia. Mother affected. Gene?

A. LMNA (FPLD2, Dunnigan)

B. PPARG

C. PLIN1

D. AGPAT2

E. BSCL2

  1. 20yo man: HIV on stavudine+zidovudine+efavirenz. Fat loss face/limbs/buttocks, central adiposity. Management?

A. Switch ART (avoid NRTI thymidine analogues); metreleptin NOT indicated

B. Metreleptin

C. Tesamorelin (for visceral fat only)

D. Pioglitazone

E. Growth hormone

  1. Generalized lipodystrophy with uncontrolled diabetes (HbA1c 10%), triglycerides 25mmol/L. Add to insulin?

A. Metreleptin (leptin analogue) — FDA approved for generalized lipodystrophy

B. Metformin only

C. Fibrates only

D. GLP-1 agonist only

E. SGLT2 inhibitor only

  1. Acquired generalized lipodystrophy: complement findings?

A. Low C3, C3NeF positive (autoimmune panniculitis)

B. Normal complement

C. Low C4 only

D. High C3

E. C1 esterase inhibitor low

17. Flashcards

  • Q: CGL1 (Berardinelli-Seip)

A: AGPAT2; near-total fat loss from birth; severe metabolic syndrome

  • Q: CGL2 (BSCL2/Seipin)

A: Most severe; intellectual disability, cardiomyopathy, early death

  • Q: FPLD1 (Kobberling)

A: Unknown gene; femoro-gluteal fat loss; female predominance

  • Q: FPLD2 (Dunnigan)

A: LMNA; limbs/trunk loss, face/neck excess; metabolic complications

  • Q: FPLD3

A: PPARG; severe insulin resistance, hypertension

  • Q: FPLD4

A: PLIN1; perilipin 1 mutation

  • Q: FPLD5

A: CIDEC; cell death-inducing DFFA-like effector C

  • Q: FPLD6

A: AKT2; insulin signalling defect

  • Q: AGL (Lawrence)

A: Autoimmune panniculitis; childhood onset; ↓ C3, C3NeF+

  • Q: HIV lipodystrophy

A: NRTI (d4T, AZT) → lipoatrophy; PI → visceral adiposity; switch ART

  • Q: Metabolic complications

A: Severe IR → diabetes, hyperTG, NAFLD/NASH, PCOS, cardiovascular disease

  • Q: Metreleptin

A: Leptin analogue; FDA approved for generalized lipodystrophy; improves IR, TG, hepatic steatosis

  • Q: Metreleptin monitoring

A: HbA1c, TG, LFTs, anti-leptin antibodies, TSH, weight

  • Q: Tesamorelin

A: GHRH analogue; reduces visceral adipose tissue in HIV lipodystrophy only

18. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. AGPAT2 mutation (1-acylglycerol-3-phosphate O-acyltransferase 2) — Congenital generalized lipodystrophy (CGL) type 1 (Berardinelli-Seip):
  1. Severe, early onset; associated with intellectual disability, cardiomyopathy — CGL type 2 (BSCL2/seipin):
  1. Unknown gene; femora-gluteal fat loss; insulin resistance — Familial partial lipodystrophy type 1 (FPLD1, Kobberling):
  1. LMNA mutation (lamin A/C); fat loss limbs/trunk, excess face/neck (cushingoid) — FPLD2 (Dunnigan):
  1. PPARG mutation; partial lipodystrophy + severe insulin resistance + hypertension — FPLD3 (PPARG):
  1. Autoimmune (panniculitis, complement); onset childhood/adolescence; ↓ C3, C3NeF — Acquired generalized lipodystrophy (AGL, Lawrence):
  1. NRTI (stavudine, zidovudine) → lipoatrophy limbs/face; PI → central adiposity — HIV-associated lipodystrophy:
  1. Severe insulin resistance → diabetes, hypertriglyceridaemia, NAFLD, PCOS — Metabolic complications of lipodystrophy:
  1. Generalized lipodystrophy (CGL, AGL) with metabolic complications — Leptin replacement (metreleptin) indicated for:
  1. HbA1c, triglycerides, liver enzymes, anti-leptin antibodies, TSH — Metreleptin monitoring:

SBAs

  1. CGL type 1 (Berardinelli-Seip) — 6yo girl: near-total fat loss since infancy, acanthosis nigricans, hepatomegaly, diabetes, hypertriglyceridaemia. Genetic testing: AGPAT2 mutation. Diagnosis?
  1. LMNA (FPLD2, Dunnigan) — 35yo woman: progressive fat loss from limbs/trunk since puberty, cushingoid facies, diabetes, severe hypertriglyceridaemia. Mother affected. Gene?
  1. Switch ART (avoid NRTI thymidine analogues); metreleptin NOT indicated — 20yo man: HIV on stavudine+zidovudine+efavirenz. Fat loss face/limbs/buttocks, central adiposity. Management?
  1. Metreleptin (leptin analogue) — FDA approved for generalized lipodystrophy — Generalized lipodystrophy with uncontrolled diabetes (HbA1c 10%), triglycerides 25mmol/L. Add to insulin?
  1. Low C3, C3NeF positive (autoimmune panniculitis) — Acquired generalized lipodystrophy: complement findings?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.11.07 Transgender Hormone Therapy
[!info]
Gender-Affirming Hormone Therapy (GAHT) = Feminising (Transfeminine) or Masculinising (Transmasculine) Hormone Regimens. Goal: Align Secondary Sex Characteristics with Gender Identity. Multidisciplinary Care (Endocrinology, Mental Health, Surgery, Primary Care). Evidence-Based, Patient-Centred, Harm-Reduction Approach.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Prescribe evidence-based feminising and masculinising hormone regimens
  • [ ] Apply monitoring protocols (hormones, metabolic, cardiovascular, bone, cancer screening)
  • [ ] Counsel on fertility preservation options
  • [ ] Manage peri-operative hormone considerations for gender-affirming surgery
  • [ ] Apply long-term health surveillance

2. General Principles

PrincipleDetails
Informed Consent ModelCapacity-Based (Not Mandatory Mental Health Assessment in Many Guidelines)
AssessmentWPATH SOC8 / Endocrine Society Guidelines — Persistent Gender Incongruence, Capacity, Medical Optimisation
Mental HealthSupportive, Not Gatekeeping — Co-Morbid Mental Health Assessment/Treatment
Harm ReductionLower Doses Acceptable If High Doses Contraindicated; Goal = Affirmation, Not Perfection
Multidisciplinary TeamEndocrinology, Mental Health, Primary Care, Surgery, Fertility, Social Work

3. Feminising Hormone Therapy (Transfeminine / MTF)

Goals

GoalTarget
Breast DevelopmentOestrogen-Driven (2-3 Years for Maximal)
Fat RedistributionGynoid Pattern (Hips, Thighs)
Skin ChangesSofter, Thinner
Testicular Atrophy↓ Testosterone Production
↓ Body HairMild Reduction (Laser/Electrolysis Often Needed)
VoiceNo Pitch Change (Voice Therapy/Surgery Needed)

Hormone Regimens

AgentDoseRouteNotes
Oestradiol (17β-Oestradiol)2-6mg/day (Start 2mg, Titrate)Transdermal Preferred (Patch/Gel/Spray)1st Line; ↓ VTE Risk
Oestradiol Valerate5-20mg IM q2wkIMAlternative
Anti-Androgen (GnRH Agonist)Goserelin 3.6mg SC q4wk / Leuprorelin 3.75mg IM q4wkSC/IMBest (Suppressed T to Castrate)
Cyproterone Acetate10-50mg/dayPOAlternative (↓ T + Progestogenic); ↓ Bone Density Risk
Spironolactone50-200mg/dayPOCommon (USA); K⁺-Sparing Diuretic; ↑ VTE Risk with Oral Oestrogen
Finasteride / Dutasteride1-5mg/dayPO↓ DHT (Hair/Prostate); No T Suppression

Preferred Regimens

RegimenComponents
Gold StandardTransdermal Oestradiol + GnRH Agonist
Alternative (Cost)Transdermal Oestradiol + Cyproterone 10-25mg
USA CommonOral/Transdermal Oestradiol + Spironolactone 100-200mg

4. Masculinising Hormone Therapy (Transmasculine / FTM)

Goals

GoalTarget
Voice DeepeningTestosterone-Driven (Irreversible)
Facial/Body Hair↑ Growth (Terminal Hair)
Fat RedistributionAndroid Pattern (Abdomen)
Muscle Mass/Strength↑
Cessation of Menses↑ Testosterone → Endometrial Atrophy
Clitoral EnlargementIrreversible

Hormone Regimens

AgentDoseRouteNotes
Testosterone Undecanoate1000mg (4mL) IM q10-14 weeksIM (Nebido)1st Line (Steady Levels, Long Duration)
Testosterone Enantate/Cypionate250mg IM q2-3 weeks / 100mg IM weeklyIMAlternative; Peaks/Troughs
Testosterone Gel50-100mg/day (Pump/ Sachet)TransdermalPhysiological; Daily Application
Testosterone Pellets600-1200mg SC q4-6moSC ImplantLong-Lasting; Surgical
Dutasteride (Adjunct)0.5mg ODPOIf Prostate Concerns / Hairline

5. Monitoring Protocols

Feminising Therapy

ParameterBaseline3 Months6 Months12 MonthsAnnually
Oestradiol✅✅✅✅✅
Testosterone✅✅✅✅✅
Prolactin✅—✅—✅
LFTs✅✅✅✅✅
Lipids✅——✅✅
Glucose/HbA1c✅——✅✅
BP / Weight✅✅✅✅✅
DEXAIf >50 / Risk Factors———q2-3yr
Breast Exam———✅q1-2yr
ProstateIf >50 / Symptoms———As Per Guidelines

Masculinising Therapy

ParameterBaseline3 Months6 Months12 MonthsAnnually
Testosterone✅✅✅✅✅
Hb / Hct✅✅✅✅✅
Lipids✅——✅✅
LFTs✅——✅✅
Glucose/HbA1c✅——✅✅
BP / Weight✅✅✅✅✅
DEXAIf >50 / Risk Factors———q2-3yr
Cervical ScreeningIf Cervix Present———Per Guidelines

6. Target Hormone Ranges

TherapyTarget RangeNotes
Feminising — Oestradiol200-400 pg/mL (730-1500 pmol/L)Physiological Female Range
Feminising — Testosterone<50 ng/dL (<1.7 nmol/L)Castrate Level
Masculinising — Testosterone400-700 ng/dL (14-24 nmol/L)Physiological Male Range
Masculinising — Oestradiol<50 pg/mL (<180 pmol/L)Suppressed

7. Contraindications / Cautions

Absolute Contraindications

ConditionFeminisingMasculinising
Active VTE / ThrombophiliaAbsolute (Oestrogen)Relative (Testosterone ↑ Hct)
Active Breast CancerAbsolute—
Active Prostate Cancer—Absolute
Severe Liver DiseaseAbsoluteAbsolute
Uncontrolled CVDAbsoluteRelative

Relative Contraindications (Individualise)

ConditionFeminisingMasculinising
Age >50 / CVD RiskTransdermal OestrogenMonitor Hct, Lipids
Migraine with AuraTransdermal OestrogenMonitor Hct
Obesity (BMI >30)Transdermal + GnRH AgonistMonitor Hct, Sleep Apnoea
SmokingStrongly Counsel CessationStrongly Counsel Cessation
ProlactinomaCaution (Oestrogen ↑)—
PorphyriaCautionCaution

8. Fertility Preservation (Pre-GAHT)

PopulationOptionsTiming
Transfeminine (AMAB)Sperm Cryopreservation (Before/Soon After GAHT)Ideally Before GAHT
Transmasculine (AFAB)Oocyte/Embryo Cryopreservation (Before Testosterone)Before Testosterone
Post-GAHTMay Require Temporary Hormone Cessation (3-6mo)Variable Recovery
Key: Counsel BEFORE Starting GAHT — Testosterone Suppresses Ovulation/Spermatogenesis Rapidly.

9. Surgical Considerations

SurgeryPeri-Operative Hormone Management
VaginoplastyStop Oestrogen 2-4 Weeks Pre-Op (VTE Risk); Restart Post-Op When Mobile
Phalloplasty / MetoidioplastyContinue Testosterone (Wound Healing); Monitor Hct
Mastectomy (Top Surgery)Continue Testosterone; No VTE Risk Change
Breast AugmentationStop Oestrogen 2-4 Weeks Pre-Op; Restart Post-Op
Facial FeminisationContinue / Adjust Per Surgeon
Tracheal ShaveContinue / Adjust

10. Long-Term Health Surveillance

Health DomainFeminisingMasculinising
CardiovascularVTE Risk (Oestrogen); Lipids q1-2yrHct ↑ (Monitor), Lipids, BP
BoneDEXA if >50 / Risk FactorsDEXA if >50 / Risk Factors
CancerBreast (If >5yr Oestrogen)Cervical (If Cervix Present)
ProstateIf >50 / SymptomsIf >50 / Symptoms (Not Reduced)
BreastIf >50 / >5yr Oestrogen—
Cervical—If Cervix Present (Per Guidelines)
LiverLFTs Annual (Oral > Transdermal)LFTs Annual
RenalSpironolactone → K⁺, Creatinine—

11. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Feminising RegimenTransdermal Oestradiol + GnRH Agonist (Gold Standard)
Masculinising RegimenTestosterone Undecanoate 1000mg IM q10-14wk (1st Line)
Anti-AndrogenGnRH Agonist (Best); Cyproterone/Spironolactone Alternatives
Testosterone Target (MTF)<50 ng/dL (<1.7 nmol/L)
Oestradiol Target (MTF)200-400 pg/mL (730-1500 pmol/L)
Testosterone Target (FTM)400-700 ng/dL (14-24 nmol/L)
VTE RiskOestrogen ↑ VTE; Transdermal ↓ Risk vs Oral
Prolactinoma RiskCyproterone ↑ Prolactin; Monitor Prolactin
Bone HealthDEXA if >50 / Risk Factors
Fertility PreservationSperm (AMAB) / Oocyte (AFAB) BEFORE GAHT
Surgical VTE ProphylaxisStop Oestrogen 2-4wk Pre-Op (Vaginoplasty/Breast Aug)
MonitoringHormones, Prolactin, LFTs, Lipids, Glucose, Hb/Hct, BP
Cyproterone SE↑ Prolactin, ↓ Bone Density, Hepatotoxicity
Spironolactone SEHyperkalaemia, Gynaecomastia (FTM), Diuretic Effect
GnRH AgonistBest T Suppression; Costly; Requires Injection

12. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Oral vs Transdermal OestrogenTransdermal = ↓ VTE, ↓ Liver First-Pass, ↓ Triglycerides
Cyproterone vs SpironolactoneCyproterone = Progestogenic + Anti-Androgen; Spironolactone = K⁺-Sparing Diuretic + Anti-Androgen
GnRH Agonist vs AntagonistAgonist (Downregulation) = Standard; Antagonist = For Fertility/Precocious Puberty
Testosterone Undecanoate vs EnantateUndecanoate = q10-14wk (Steady); Enantate = q2-3wk (Peaks/Troughs)
VTE RiskOestrogen ↑ Risk; Transdermal ↓ VTE vs Oral
HCT Rise on TestosteroneCastrate → Replace → Hct ↑↑ (Polycythaemia Risk); Monitor q3-6mo
FertilityPreserve BEFORE GAHT — Testosterone/Oestrogen Rapidly Suppress Gonadal Function
Breast Cancer ScreeningIf >5yr Oestrogen + >50 → Mammogram per Guidelines
Prostate Cancer ScreeningNot Reduced by Testosterone (If >50 / Symptoms)
Cervical ScreeningIf Cervix Present (FTM) — Per Guidelines
VoiceTestosterone Deepens (Irreversible); Oestrogen Does Not Raise Pitch
Menses CessationTestosterone → Endometrial Atrophy (Usually 2-6mo)
Bone DensityDEXA if >50 / Risk Factors (Both Directions)

13. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Transgender Hormone Therapy))
    Principles
      Informed Consent Model
      WPATH SOC8 / Endocrine Society Guidelines
      Harm Reduction / Patient-Centred
      Multidisciplinary Team
    Feminising (MTF)
      Oestradiol: Transdermal 2-6mg (1st Line)
      Anti-Androgen: GnRH Agonist (Best) / Cyproterone / Spiro
      Targets: E2 200-400 pg/mL; T <50 ng/dL
      VTE Risk: Transdermal ↓ Risk
      Monitoring: E2, T, PRL, LFT, Lipids, Glucose
    Masculinising (FTM)
      Testosterone: Undecanoate 1000mg IM q10-14wk (1st Line)
      Alternatives: Enantate IM, Gel, Pellets
      Target: T 400-700 ng/dL; E2 <50 pg/mL
      Hct Rise: Monitor q3-6mo
      Monitoring: T, Hct, Lipids, LFTs, Glucose
    Fertility
      AMAB: Sperm Cryo (Pre-GAHT)
      AFAB: Oocyte/Embryo Cryo (Pre-T)
      Post-GAHT: Possible Pause (3-6mo)
    Surgery
      Vaginoplasty: Stop E2 2-4wk Pre-Op
      Phalloplasty/Metoidioplasty: Continue T
      Mastectomy: Continue T
      Breast Aug: Stop E2 2-4wk Pre-Op
    Monitoring
      Baseline, 3mo, 6mo, 12mo, Annual
      Hormones, Prolactin, LFT, Lipids, Glucose, Hct
      DEXA, Breast/Cervical/Prostate Screening
    Contraindications
      Absolute: Active VTE, Breast/Prostate Ca, Severe Liver
      Relative: CVD, Migraine w/ Aura, Obesity, Smoking

14. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Feminising Gold StandardTransdermal Oestradiol + GnRH Agonist
Masculinising Gold StandardTestosterone Undecanoate 1000mg IM q10-14wk
Anti-AndrogenGnRH Agonist (Best T Suppression); Cyproterone/Spironolactone Alternatives
Oestradiol Target (MTF)200-400 pg/mL (730-1500 pmol/L)
Testosterone Target (FTM)400-700 ng/dL (14-24 nmol/L)
Testosterone Target (MTF)<50 ng/dL (<1.7 nmol/L) (Castrate)
VTE RiskOestrogen ↑ VTE; Transdermal ↓ Risk vs Oral
Fertility PreservationSperm (AMAB) / Oocyte (AFAB) BEFORE GAHT
Surgical VTE ProphylaxisStop Oestrogen 2-4wk Pre-Op (Vaginoplasty/Breast Aug)
Cyproterone SE↑ Prolactin, ↓ Bone Density, Hepatotoxicity
Spironolactone SEHyperkalaemia, Diuretic Effect
Hct on TestosteronePolycythaemia Risk; Monitor q3-6mo; Venesection if >54%
Bone HealthDEXA if >50 / Risk Factors (Both Directions)
Cyproterone vs SpiroCypro = Progestogenic + Anti-Androgen; Spiro = K+-Sparing Diuretic
GnRH AgonistBest T Suppression; Costly; Requires Injection
Transdermal Oestrogen↓ VTE, ↓ Liver First-Pass, ↓ Triglycerides
Testosterone Undecanoate1000mg IM q10-14wk (Steady, Long-Acting)

15. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Reproductive Endocrinology|Reproductive Endocrinology]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Male Hypogonadism]], [[Female Hypogonadism]], [[Reproductive Endocrinology]], [[Infertility]], [[Contraception]]

16. MCQs (10)

  1. Transfeminine hormone therapy (MtF):

A. Oestradiol + anti-androgen (spironolactone/cyproterone/GnRH agonist)

B. Testosterone + oestradiol

C. Oestradiol only

D. Anti-androgen only

E. Progesterone only

  1. Oestradiol formulations for transfeminine:

A. Transdermal (gel/patch) preferred (↓ VTE risk); oral; IM

B. Oral only

C. IM only

D. Subcutaneous pellets only

E. Intranasal only

  1. Anti-androgens for transfeminine:

A. Spironolactone (100-300mg/day); Cyproterone acetate (10-50mg/day); GnRH agonist (gold standard)

B. Finasteride only

C. Dutasteride only

D. Flutamide only

E. Bicalutamide only

  1. Transmasculine hormone therapy (FtM):

A. Testosterone (gel/patch/IM/SC); target male physiological range

B. Oestradiol + anti-androgen

C. Testosterone + oestradiol

D. Anti-androgen only

E. GnRH agonist only

  1. Testosterone formulations for transmasculine:

A. IM testosterone undecanoate (1000mg/12wk) or enanthate/cypionate (250mg/2-3wk) or gel/patch daily

B. Oral only

C. Transdermal only

D. Subcutaneous pellets only

E. Intranasal only

  1. Monitoring transfeminine HRT (3mo, 6mo, then annually):

A. Oestradiol, testosterone, LFTs, lipids, glucose, prolactin, BP, weight

B. Only oestradiol

C. Only testosterone

D. Only LFTs

E. No monitoring

  1. Monitoring transmasculine HRT:

A. Testosterone, Hb/Hct (polycythaemia), LFTs, lipids, glucose, BP, weight

B. Only testosterone

C. Only Hb

D. Only LFTs

E. No monitoring

  1. VTE risk in transfeminine HRT:

A. Oral oestradiol > transdermal; spironolactone may ↑ K+; screen thrombophilia if history

B. No VTE risk

C. Only with cyproterone

D. Only with oral

E. Only with IM

  1. Polycythaemia risk in transmasculine HRT:

A. Testosterone → ↑ erythropoietin → Hb/Hct ↑; monitor q3mo ×1yr then annually; venesection if Hct >54%

B. No risk

C. Only with IM

D. Only with gel

E. Only with patches

  1. Fertility preservation counselling:

A. BEFORE starting HRT (sperm/embryo/oocyte cryopreservation); HRT may impair fertility

B. After HRT

C. Not needed

D. Only for transmasculine

E. Only for transfeminine

17. SBA Questions (5)

  1. 25yo transfeminine patient: requesting HRT. Medical history: DVT 2 years ago (on apixaban). Best oestradiol route?

A. Transdermal (gel/patch) — lower VTE risk vs oral

B. Oral oestradiol valerate

C. IM oestradiol

D. Subcutaneous pellets

E. Intranasal

  1. Same patient: anti-androgen choice with DVT history?

A. GnRH agonist (triptorelin) — no VTE risk; or bicalutamide (monitor LFTs)

B. Cyproterone acetate (↑ VTE)

C. Spironolactone (↑ K+, monitor)

D. Finasteride (weak)

E. No anti-androgen needed

  1. 30yo transmasculine patient: on testosterone gel 50mg/day for 6 months. Hb 185, Hct 56%. Management?

A. Reduce dose/switch formulation; therapeutic venesection if Hct >54%; assess sleep apnoea

B. Stop testosterone

C. Increase dose

D. Aspirin only

E. Erythropoietin

  1. 22yo transmasculine: requests hysterectomy/oophorectomy. Testosterone stopped 4 weeks pre-op?

A. NO — continue testosterone peri-op (no VTE risk from testosterone alone); standard VTE prophylaxis

B. Stop 4 weeks pre-op

C. Stop 2 weeks pre-op

D. Switch to gel

E. Bridge with oestradiol

  1. Non-binary patient: low-dose testosterone (microdosing) for partial masculinisation. Monitoring?

A. Same parameters (testosterone, Hb/Hct, LFTs, lipids) but targets individualised

B. No monitoring needed

C. Only testosterone

D. Only Hb

E. Only LFTs

18. Flashcards

  • Q: Transfeminine HRT

A: Oestradiol (transdermal preferred) + anti-androgen (spiro/cyproterone/GnRHa)

  • Q: Transmasculine HRT

A: Testosterone (gel/patch/IM/SC); target male physiological range

  • Q: Oestradiol routes

A: Transdermal > IM > oral (VTE risk); avoid oral if thrombophilia/DVT history

  • Q: Anti-androgens

A: Spironolactone (K+ sparing diuretic); Cyproterone (progestin, VTE risk); GnRHa (gold standard)

  • Q: Testosterone routes

A: IM undecanoate 1000mg/12wk; enanthate/cypionate 250mg/2-3wk; gel/patch daily

  • Q: Monitoring transfeminine

A: E2, T, LFTs, lipids, glucose, prolactin, BP, weight at 3,6,12mo then annually

  • Q: Monitoring transmasculine

A: T, Hb/Hct (polycythaemia), LFTs, lipids, glucose, BP, weight

  • Q: VTE risk

A: Oral E2 > transdermal; screen thrombophilia; avoid cyproterone if high risk

  • Q: Polycythaemia

A: T → ↑ EPO → ↑ Hb/Hct; monitor q3mo×1yr then annually; venesection if Hct>54%

  • Q: Liver

A: Oral E2: 1st pass hepatotoxicity; monitor ALT/AST; transdermal/IM safer

  • Q: Lipids

A: Transfeminine: ↑ TG, ↓ LDL; Transmasculine: ↑ LDL, ↓ HDL, ↑ TG

  • Q: Bone health

A: Both: DEXA if risk factors; ensure Vit D/calcium; weight-bearing exercise

  • Q: Fertility

A: COUNSEL BEFORE HRT: sperm/embryo/oocyte cryopreservation; HRT may impair permanently

  • Q: Mental health

A: Improved with gender-affirming care; screen depression/anxiety; support access

  • Q: Surgical readiness

A: WPATH: 12mo HRT (for genital surgery); 12mo living in role; mental health support

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Oestradiol + anti-androgen (spironolactone/cyproterone/GnRH agonist) — Transfeminine hormone therapy (MtF):
  1. Transdermal (gel/patch) preferred (↓ VTE risk); oral; IM — Oestradiol formulations for transfeminine:
  1. Spironolactone (100-300mg/day); Cyproterone acetate (10-50mg/day); GnRH agonist (gold standard) — Anti-androgens for transfeminine:
  1. Testosterone (gel/patch/IM/SC); target male physiological range — Transmasculine hormone therapy (FtM):
  1. IM testosterone undecanoate (1000mg/12wk) or enanthate/cypionate (250mg/2-3wk) or gel/patch daily — Testosterone formulations for transmasculine:
  1. Oestradiol, testosterone, LFTs, lipids, glucose, prolactin, BP, weight — Monitoring transfeminine HRT (3mo, 6mo, then annually):
  1. Testosterone, Hb/Hct (polycythaemia), LFTs, lipids, glucose, BP, weight — Monitoring transmasculine HRT:
  1. Oral oestradiol > transdermal; spironolactone may ↑ K+; screen thrombophilia if history — VTE risk in transfeminine HRT:
  1. Testosterone → ↑ erythropoietin → Hb/Hct ↑; monitor q3mo ×1yr then annually; venesection if Hct >54% — Polycythaemia risk in transmasculine HRT:
  1. BEFORE starting HRT (sperm/embryo/oocyte cryopreservation); HRT may impair fertility — Fertility preservation counselling:

SBAs

  1. Transdermal (gel/patch) — lower VTE risk vs oral — 25yo transfeminine patient: requesting HRT. Medical history: DVT 2 years ago (on apixaban). Best oestradiol route?
  1. GnRH agonist (triptorelin) — no VTE risk; or bicalutamide (monitor LFTs) — Same patient: anti-androgen choice with DVT history?
  1. Reduce dose/switch formulation; therapeutic venesection if Hct >54%; assess sleep apnoea — 30yo transmasculine patient: on testosterone gel 50mg/day for 6 months. Hb 185, Hct 56%. Management?
  1. NO — continue testosterone peri-op (no VTE risk from testosterone alone); standard VTE prophylaxis — 22yo transmasculine: requests hysterectomy/oophorectomy. Testosterone stopped 4 weeks pre-op?
  1. Same parameters (testosterone, Hb/Hct, LFTs, lipids) but targets individualised — Non-binary patient: low-dose testosterone (microdosing) for partial masculinisation. Monitoring?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.2 20.2 14
▸ 20.2.01 Acth Cortisol Axis
[!info]
HPA axis = CRH → ACTH → Cortisol. Central to stress response, glucose homeostasis, immune regulation, and circadian rhythm. Dysregulation → Cushing syndrome, adrenal insufficiency, steroid-induced suppression.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Describe the HPA axis anatomy, hormone synthesis, and regulation
  • [ ] Apply feedback loops to distinguish primary, secondary, and tertiary adrenal insufficiency
  • [ ] Interpret dynamic tests (Synacthen, LDDST, HDDST, ITT)
  • [ ] Distinguish Cushing disease, ectopic ACTH, and adrenal Cushing
  • [ ] Apply stress-dose steroid protocols in adrenal insufficiency

2. HPA Axis — Anatomy & Physiology

Hormone Synthesis & Secretion

StepHormoneSourceRegulation
1. CRHCorticotropin-Releasing HormoneHypothalamus (PVN)Stress ↑; Circadian (peak 06-08h); Negative feedback by Cortisol
2. ACTHAdrenocorticotropic HormoneAnterior Pituitary (Corticotrophs)CRH (stimulatory); Cortisol (negative feedback); AVP (synergistic)
3. CortisolGlucocorticoidAdrenal Cortex (Zona fasciculata/reticularis)ACTH (stimulatory); Negative feedback at pituitary + hypothalamus

Precursor Pathway (Cortisol Synthesis)

Cholesterol → (STAR) → Pregnenolone → (CYP17A1) → 17-OH Pregnenolone
         ↓
         (CYP21A2) → 17-OH Progesterone → (CYP11B1) → 11-Deoxycortisol → **Cortisol** (CYP11B1)
EnzymeGeneProductDeficiency Syndrome
STARSTARCholesterol transferLipoid CAH
CYP11A1CYP11A1PregnenoloneLipoid CAH
CYP17A1 (17α-hydroxylase/17,20-lyase)CYP17A117-OH steroids, DHEA17α-OH deficiency
CYP21A2 (21-hydroxylase)CYP21A211-Deoxycortisol, DOC21-OH deficiency (95% CAH)
CYP11B1 (11β-hydroxylase)CYP11B1Cortisol11β-OH deficiency
CYP11B2 (Aldosterone synthase)CYP11B2Aldosterone—

3. Regulation & Secretion Patterns

AspectDetails
CRHHypothalamus (PVN); Stimulated by: Stress, Hypoglycaemia, Exercise; Inhibited by: Cortisol (negative feedback)
ACTHAnterior pituitary (Corticotrophs); Stimulated by: CRH, AVP; Inhibited by: Cortisol (negative feedback)
CortisolAdrenal cortex (Zona fasciculata); Stimulated by: ACTH; Negative feedback on CRH + ACTH

Secretion Patterns

PatternDetails
Circadian RhythmPeak 06:00-08:00 (cortisol ~400-700 nmol/L); Nadir 23:00-24:00 (<50 nmol/L)
PulsatileACTH/Cortisol pulses ~hourly; ~15-20 pulses/24h
Stress ResponseAcute stress → ↑ CRH + AVP → ↑ ACTH → ↑ Cortisol (within minutes)
FeedbackLong-loop: Cortisol → ↓ CRH + ACTH; Short-loop: ACTH → ↓ CRH

4. HPA Axis Feedback Patterns — Diagnostic Key

DisorderCortisolACTHCRHInterpretation
NormalNormalNormalNormal—
Primary AI (Addison)↓↓↑↑↑↑Adrenal failure
Secondary AI (Pituitary)↓↓/N↑Pituitary failure
Tertiary AI (Hypothalamic/Steroid)↓↓/N↓Steroid withdrawal, hypothalamic lesion
Cushing Disease↑↑N/↑↓Pituitary ACTH adenoma
Adrenal Cushing↑↑↓↓↓↓Adrenal autonomy
Ectopic ACTH↑↑↑↑↓Ectopic ACTH source

Key Diagnostic Rule: Primary = Target bad + Tropic opposite; Secondary = Both low; Tertiary = Same as secondary but CRH low

5. Cushing Syndrome — Differential Diagnosis

Diagnostic Approach

Cushing Suspected → Confirm (24h UFC×2 + Late-night Salivary Cortisol×2 + 1mg DST)
         │
         ├── NEGATIVE → Excluded
         │
         └── POSITIVE → Measure ACTH (09:00, EDTA, Ice)
                 │
                 ├── ACTH LOW (<10 pg/mL) → ACTH-INDEPENDENT
                 │       Unilateral adrenal mass → Adrenal Adenoma/Carcinoma
                 │       Bilateral hyperplasia → PPNAD / AIMAH
                 │
                 └── ACTH NORMAL/HIGH (>10-20) → ACTH-DEPENDENT
                         │
                         ├── HDDST (2mg q6h × 48h)
                         │       Suppresses >50% → **Cushing Disease** (Pituitary)
                         │       No suppression → Ectopic ACTH / Adrenal Cushing
                         │
                         └── If Equivocal → CRH Test / IPSS / ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET

Cushing Subtypes — Comparison

FeatureCushing DiseaseAdrenal CushingEctopic ACTH
ACTHNormal / HighLow / SuppressedHigh
HDDSTSuppresses >50%No suppressionNo suppression
ImagingPituitary MRI (microadenoma)Adrenal CT (unilateral/bilateral)CT Chest/Abdomen; DOTATATE PET
IPSSCentral:Peripheral ACTH gradient >2N/AN/A
TreatmentTranssphenoidal surgeryAdrenalectomyResection of ectopic source

6. Adrenal Insufficiency — Classification & Diagnosis

Classification

TypeACTHCortisolKey Features
Primary (Addison)↑↑↓↓Hyperpigmentation, ↑K⁺, ↓Na⁺, Hypotension
Secondary (Pituitary)↓/N↓No hyperpigmentation; Other pituitary deficits; Aldosterone preserved
Tertiary (Hypothalamic/Steroid)↓/N↓Steroid withdrawal; HPA suppression; Aldosterone preserved

Diagnostic Thresholds

TestNormalAdrenal Insufficiency
09:00 Cortisol200-700 nmol/L<100 nmol/L (diagnostic); 100-500 = equivocal
Synacthen 250µg (30min)≥500-550 nmol/L<400-500 nmol/L
Synacthen 1µg (30min)≥500-550 nmol/L<400-500 nmol/L
ITT (Peak Cortisol)>500-550 nmol/L<500-550 nmol/L

Synacthen Test Protocol

ParameterStandard (250µg)Low-Dose (1µg)
Dose250µg IM/IV1µg IV
Sampling0, 30, 60min0, 20, 30, 60min
Normal ResponseCortisol ≥500-550 at 30/60minSame
AI DiagnosisCortisol <400-500 at 30/60minSame
IndicationGeneral screeningSecondary AI (more physiological)

7. Cushing Syndrome — Investigation Algorithm

Cushing Suspected → 24h UFC ×2 + Late-night Salivary Cortisol ×2 + 1mg DST
         │
         ├── NEGATIVE → Excluded
         │
         └── POSITIVE → ACTH (09:00, EDTA, Ice)
                 │
                 ├── ACTH <10 pg/mL → ACTH-INDEPENDENT
                 │       Adrenal CT → Unilateral → Adrenal Adenoma/Carcinoma
                 │       Bilateral → PPNAD / AIMAH
                 │
                 └── ACTH >10-20 pg/mL → ACTH-DEPENDENT
                         │
                         ├── HDDST (2mg q6h × 48h)
                         │       Suppresses >50% → **Cushing Disease**
                         │       MRI Pituitary → IPSS if equivocal
                         │
                         └── No Suppression → Ectopic ACTH / Adrenal Cushing
                                 CT Chest/Abd/Pelvis → ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET

8. Dynamic Testing — HPA Axis

Synacthen Test (Standard 250µg)

TimeCortisol (nmol/L)Interpretation
0 min200-700Baseline
30 min≥500-550Normal
60 min≥500-550Normal
<400-500 at 30/60minAdrenal Insufficiency

Dexamethasone Suppression Tests

Low-Dose DST (LDDST) — Screening

ProtocolOvernight 1mg at 23:00 → Cortisol 09:00 next day
Cut-offCortisol <50 nmol/L = Normal suppression
Non-suppressedCortisol ≥50 nmol/L → Proceed to HDDST

High-Dose DST (HDDST) — Differential

Protocol2mg q6h × 8 doses (48h) OR Overnight 8mg
Cushing DiseaseCortisol suppresses >50% (or <50% of baseline)
Ectopic ACTH / AdrenalNo suppression (<50% suppression)

DST Pitfalls: Enzyme inducers (Phenytoin, Rifampicin) → False non-suppression; Obesity/Depression/Alcohol → False positive; Pregnancy/OCP → Use salivary cortisol/24h UFC.

OGTT for Acromegaly (GH Suppression)

Protocol75g Glucose PO → GH at 0, 30, 60, 90, 120min
NormalGH suppressed <1 µg/L at 60-120min
AcromegalyGH not suppressed ≥1 µg/L

Insulin Tolerance Test (ITT) — Gold Standard

ProtocolInsulin 0.1 U/kg IV → Glucose <2.2 mmol/L
Cortisol Peak>500-550 nmol/L = Normal HPA axis
GH Peak>3 µg/L (adults) = Normal GH reserve
ContraindicationsIHD, CVD, Epilepsy, Severe hypopituitarism, Age >60, Pregnancy

9. Stress-Dose Steroid Protocol

Stress LevelHydrocortisone IVDuration
Minor (Dental, Minor surgery)25mg pre-opSingle dose
Moderate (Major surgery, Sepsis, Trauma)50-100mg pre-op → 50mg q6-8h24-48h
Major (Cardiac surgery, Septic shock, Major trauma)100mg IV bolus → 50-100mg q6h48-72h
Labour/Delivery100mg IV at onset → 50mg q6hUntil 24h post-delivery

Oral Stress Dosing: Triple usual oral dose for duration of illness.

10. Steroid Withdrawal & HPA Recovery

Steroid DurationHPA SuppressionRecovery Time
<3 weeksMinimalDays
>3 weeksSignificant (adrenal atrophy)Weeks-months
High dose (>40mg pred equivalent)SevereMonths
Inhaled/Topical (high dose)ModerateWeeks

Stress Dose Protocol (Oral → IV)

Stress LevelHydrocortisone IVOral Equivalent
Minor (Dental, Minor surgery)25mg stat2-3x usual dose
Moderate50-100mg → 50mg q6-8h × 24-48h3x usual dose
Major100mg IV bolus → 50-100mg q6h × 48-72hN/A (IV required)

11. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Circadian CortisolPeak 06-08h (400-700 nmol/L); Nadir 23-24h; Loss of rhythm in Cushing
Synacthen 250µgCortisol ≥500-550 at 30/60min = Normal; <400-500 = AI
Synacthen 1µgMore sensitive for secondary AI; Technically demanding
LDDST1mg overnight; Cortisol <50 = Normal suppression; ≥50 = Cushing possible
HDDST2mg q6h × 48h; >50% suppression = Cushing Disease; No suppression = Ectopic/Adrenal
ACTH in CushingDisease: N/↑; Adrenal: ↓↓; Ectopic: ↑↑
ITTGold Standard GH+Cortisol; Insulin 0.1U/kg → Glucose <2.2; Contra: IHD, CVD, Epilepsy, Age>60
Cortisol Cut-offsDST: <50; Synacthen: ≥500-550; ITT: >500-550; Morning: 200-700 nmol/L
ITT ContraindicationsIHD, CVD, Epilepsy, Severe hypopit, Age>60, Pregnancy
Steroid Withdrawal>3 weeks = HPA suppression; Taper over weeks-months
Stress DosingMinor 25mg; Moderate 50-100mg; Major 100mg + 50mg q6h
Adrenal Crisis100mg Hydrocortisone IV STAT + 1L 0.9% NaCl; Never delay for tests
Primary vs Secondary AIPrimary: Hyperkalaemia + Hyperpigmentation; Secondary: Neither

12. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
LDDST vs HDDSTLDDST = Screening (is it Cushing?); HDDST = Differential (Pituitary vs Ectopic/Adrenal)
Synacthen 250µg vs 1µg250µg = Standard screening; 1µg = More sensitive for secondary/early AI
GH StimulationITT = Gold standard but dangerous; Glucagon = Safer alternative; GHRH+Arg = Most sensitive for adults
DST FailureMost common cause = Drugs (enzyme inducers); Obesity/Depression/Alcohol also
Cortisol Cut-offsDST: <50; Synacthen: ≥500-550; ITT: >500-550; Morning: 200-700 nmol/L
ITT vs GlucagonITT tests cortisol + GH simultaneously; Glucagon safer but only GH well-validated
OGTT for AcromegalyOnly validated suppression test for acromegaly; GH <1 µg/L = Normal

Mnemonic: HPA AXIS FEEDBACK PATTERNS

  • Primary AI: Cortisol↓ + ACTH↑↑ (Opposite)
  • Secondary AI: Cortisol↓ + ACTH↓ (Same)
  • Cushing Disease: ACTH N/↑ (Pituitary makes ACTH)
  • Adrenal Cushing: ACTH↓↓ (Adrenal autonomous)
  • Ectopic ACTH: ACTH↑↑ (Tumour makes ACTH)

13. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((ACTH/Cortisol Axis))
    HPA Axis
      CRH (Hypothalamus) → ACTH (Pituitary) → Cortisol (Adrenal)
      Feedback: Cortisol → CRH + ACTH
    Secretion
      Circadian: Peak 6-8am, Nadir 11pm
      Pulsatile: Hourly pulses
      Stress: CRH + AVP → ACTH → Cortisol
    Feedback
      Long-loop: Cortisol → CRH + ACTH
      Short-loop: ACTH → CRH
      Ultra-short: CRH → CRH
    Cushing Patterns
      Disease: ACTH N/↑ (Pituitary)
      Adrenal: ACTH ↓↓ (Autonomous)
      Ectopic: ACTH ↑↑ (Tumour)
    Testing
      Synacthen 250µg: Cortisol ≥550 normal
      LDDST: <50 normal
      HDDST: >50% suppression = Pituitary
      ITT: Glucose <2.2 → Cortisol >550
    Stress Dosing
      Minor: 25mg; Moderate: 50-100mg; Major: 100mg + 50mg q6h
    Steroid Withdrawal
      >3 weeks = HPA suppression
      Taper: weeks-months
    Adrenal Crisis
      100mg Hydrocortisone IV + 1L Saline IMMEDIATE

TopicKey Point
Circadian CortisolPeak 06-08h (400-700 nmol/L); Nadir 23-24h; Loss of rhythm in Cushing
Synacthen 250µgCortisol ≥500-550 at 30/60min = Normal; <400-500 = AI
Synacthen 1µgMore sensitive for secondary AI; Technically demanding
LDDST1mg overnight; Cortisol <50 = Normal suppression; ≥50 = Cushing possible
HDDST2mg q6h × 48h; >50% suppression = Cushing Disease; No suppression = Ectopic/Adrenal
ACTH in CushingDisease: N/↑; Adrenal: ↓↓; Ectopic: ↑↑
ITTGold Standard GH+Cortisol; Insulin 0.1U/kg → Glucose <2.2; Contra: IHD, CVD, Epilepsy, Age>60
Cortisol Cut-offsDST: <50; Synacthen: ≥500-550; ITT: >500-550; Morning: 200-700 nmol/L
ITT ContraindicationsIHD, CVD, Epilepsy, Severe hypopit, Age>60, Pregnancy
Steroid Withdrawal>3 weeks = HPA suppression; Taper over weeks-months
Stress DosingMinor 25mg; Moderate 50-100mg; Major 100mg + 50mg q6h
Adrenal Crisis100mg Hydrocortisone IV STAT + 1L 0.9% NaCl; Never delay for tests
Primary vs Secondary AIPrimary: Hyperkalaemia + Hyperpigmentation; Secondary: Neither
