17. MCQs (10)

  1. HPA axis sequence:

A. CRH → ACTH → Cortisol

B. ACTH → CRH → Cortisol

C. Cortisol → ACTH → CRH

D. CRH → Cortisol → ACTH

E. ACTH → Cortisol → CRH

  1. Rate-limiting step in cortisol synthesis:

A. Cholesterol → Pregnenolone (STAR + CYP11A1)

B. Pregnenolone → 17-OH Pregnenolone

C. 11-Deoxycortisol → Cortisol

D. DOC → Aldosterone

E. 17-OH Progesterone → 11-Deoxycortisol

  1. Normal 09:00 cortisol range:

A. 400-700 nmol/L

B. 100-200 nmol/L

C. 200-300 nmol/L

D. 700-1000 nmol/L

E. 50-100 nmol/L

  1. Cortisol nadir occurs at:

A. 23:00-24:00 (<50 nmol/L)

B. 06:00-08:00

C. 12:00-14:00

D. 18:00-20:00

E. Random

  1. Primary adrenal insufficiency biochemistry:

A. Cortisol ↓, ACTH ↑↑

B. Cortisol ↓, ACTH ↓

C. Cortisol ↑, ACTH ↓

D. Cortisol ↑, ACTH ↑

E. Cortisol normal, ACTH ↑

  1. Secondary adrenal insufficiency biochemistry:

A. Cortisol ↓, ACTH low/normal

B. Cortisol ↓, ACTH ↑↑

C. Cortisol ↑, ACTH ↑

D. Cortisol normal, ACTH ↓

E. Cortisol ↑, ACTH normal

  1. Synacthen test (250mcg) normal cortisol:

A. >=500-550 nmol/L at 30/60 min

B. >=400 nmol/L

C. >=300 nmol/L

D. >=600 nmol/L

E. >=200 nmol/L

  1. Dexamethasone suppression test fail to suppress →

A. Cushing syndrome

B. Adrenal insufficiency

C. Normal HPA

D. Addison's

E. CAH

  1. In Cushing disease, high-dose DST:

A. Suppresses cortisol

B. Does not suppress

C. Elevates ACTH

D. Suppresses ACTH only

E. No change

  1. Cortisol circadian rhythm peak:

A. 06:00-08:00

B. 12:00-14:00

C. 18:00-20:00

D. 24:00

E. Random

18. SBA Questions (10)

  1. Low cortisol, high ACTH, hyperpigmentation. Diagnosis?

A. Primary AI (Addison's)

B. Secondary AI

C. Cushing disease

D. Ectopic ACTH

E. CAH

  1. Low cortisol, low ACTH, on prednisolone 6 months. Diagnosis?

A. Tertiary AI (steroid withdrawal)

B. Primary AI

C. Secondary AI

D. Cushing syndrome

E. CAH

  1. Cushing syndrome: high cortisol, low ACTH. Next step?

A. Adrenal CT

B. Pituitary MRI

C. IPSS

D. CRH test

E. 24h UFC

  1. Suspected secondary AI. Best dynamic test?

A. Low-dose (1mcg) Synacthen

B. Standard 250mcg Synacthen

C. ITT

D. Overnight DST

E. CRH test

  1. Addisonian crisis: hypotension, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia. Immediate?

A. Hydrocortisone 100mg IV + 1L 0.9% NaCl

B. Fludrocortisone only

C. Oral hydrocortisone

D. Dexamethasone

E. NSAIDs

  1. Patient on long-term steroids needs surgery. Stress dose?

A. Major: 100mg IV bolus → 50-100mg q6h

B. Minor: 100mg IV

C. Moderate: 25mg IV

D. No extra needed

E. Double oral dose only

  1. Cortisol 09:00 = 120 nmol/L. Interpretation?

A. Possible AI → do Synacthen test

B. Normal

C. Cushing

D. Adrenal adenoma

E. Factitious

  1. Cushing disease vs ectopic ACTH differentiation:

A. High-dose DST (suppresses pituitary) or CRH/IPSS

B. MRI only

C. ACTH level only

D. Adrenal CT

E. 24h UFC only

  1. CAH 21-hydroxylase deficiency: accumulation?

A. 17-OH Progesterone → androgens

B. DOC → cortisol

C. 11-Deoxycortisol → aldosterone

D. Cortisol → androstenedione

E. Pregnenolone → progesterone

  1. Stress dose hydrocortisone for minor procedure:

A. 25mg IV/IM pre-op + usual oral

B. 100mg IV

C. 50mg IV q6h

D. 200mg IV

E. No extra needed

19. Flashcards

  • Q: HPA axis

A: CRH (hypothalamus) → ACTH (pituitary) → Cortisol (adrenal cortex)

  • Q: Cortisol rhythm

A: Peak 6-8am (~400-700 nmol/L); Nadir midnight (<50 nmol/L)

  • Q: Primary AI

A: Cortisol ↓, ACTH ↑↑ (>100 pg/mL); hyperpigmentation, hyperkalaemia

  • Q: Secondary AI

A: Cortisol ↓, ACTH low/normal; no hyperpigmentation, no hyperkalaemia

  • Q: Tertiary AI

A: Steroid withdrawal → low cortisol, low ACTH; HPA suppression

  • Q: Synacthen 250mcg

A: Normal: Cortisol ≥500-550 at 30/60min; AI: <400-500

  • Q: Synacthen 1mcg

A: More sensitive for secondary/tertiary AI; same cut-offs

  • Q: Overnight 1mg DST

A: Normal: Cortisol <50 nmol/L; Cushing: not suppressed

  • Q: High-dose 8mg DST

A: Cushing disease: suppresses >50%; Ectopic/adrenal: no suppression

  • Q: Addison crisis

A: Hypotension + hyponatraemia + hyperkalaemia → HC 100mg IV + 1L saline STAT

  • Q: Stress dosing

A: Minor 25mg; Moderate 50-100mg; Major 100mg+50mg q6h

  • Q: Cortisol feedback

A: Long-loop: Cortisol → ↓CRH/↓ACTH; Short-loop: ACTH → ↓CRH

  • Q: CAH 21-OH

A: ↓Cortisol, ↓Aldo → ↑17-OHP → ↑Androgens → salt-wasting + virilisation

  • Q: CAH 11β-OH

A: ↓Cortisol → ↑DOC (HTN) + ↑Androgens (virilisation)

  • Q: CAH 17α-OH

A: ↓Sex steroids (infantilism) + ↑Mineralocorticoids (HTN)

20. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. CRH → ACTH → Cortisol — HPA axis sequence:
  1. Cholesterol → Pregnenolone (STAR + CYP11A1) — Rate-limiting step in cortisol synthesis:
  1. 400-700 nmol/L — Normal 09:00 cortisol range:
  1. 23:00-24:00 (<50 nmol/L) — Cortisol nadir occurs at:
  1. Cortisol ↓, ACTH ↑↑ — Primary adrenal insufficiency biochemistry:
  1. Cortisol ↓, ACTH low/normal — Secondary adrenal insufficiency biochemistry:
  1. >=500-550 nmol/L at 30/60 min — Synacthen test (250mcg) normal cortisol:
  1. Cushing syndrome — Dexamethasone suppression test fail to suppress →
  1. Suppresses cortisol — In Cushing disease, high-dose DST:
  1. 06:00-08:00 — Cortisol circadian rhythm peak:

SBAs

  1. Primary AI (Addison's) — Low cortisol, high ACTH, hyperpigmentation. Diagnosis?
  1. Tertiary AI (steroid withdrawal) — Low cortisol, low ACTH, on prednisolone 6 months. Diagnosis?
  1. Adrenal CT — Cushing syndrome: high cortisol, low ACTH. Next step?
  1. Low-dose (1mcg) Synacthen — Suspected secondary AI. Best dynamic test?
  1. Hydrocortisone 100mg IV + 1L 0.9% NaCl — Addisonian crisis: hypotension, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia. Immediate?
  1. Major: 100mg IV bolus → 50-100mg q6h — Patient on long-term steroids needs surgery. Stress dose?
  1. Possible AI → do Synacthen test — Cortisol 09:00 = 120 nmol/L. Interpretation?
  1. High-dose DST (suppresses pituitary) or CRH/IPSS — Cushing disease vs ectopic ACTH differentiation:
  1. 17-OH Progesterone → androgens — CAH 21-hydroxylase deficiency: accumulation?
  1. 25mg IV/IM pre-op + usual oral — Stress dose hydrocortisone for minor procedure:
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.2.02 Anterior Pituitary Hormones Overview
[!info]
Anterior pituitary (adenohypophysis) produces 6 key hormones from 5 distinct cell types. Understanding each hormone's regulation, target, and clinical effects is fundamental to all pituitary pathology.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Identify the 5 anterior pituitary cell types and their hormones
  • [ ] Describe the regulation of each hormone (releasing/inhibiting factors)
  • [ ] State the physiological actions of each hormone
  • [ ] Apply feedback loop logic to anterior pituitary axes
  • [ ] Recognise clinical features of excess and deficiency for each hormone

2. Anterior Pituitary Cell Types & Hormones

Cell Type% of CellsHormone(s)AbbreviationTarget Organ
Corticotroph15-20%Adrenocorticotropic HormoneACTHAdrenal Cortex (Cortisol)
Thyrotroph5-10%Thyroid-Stimulating HormoneTSHThyroid (T4/T3)
Somatotroph40-50%Growth HormoneGHLiver (IGF-1), Tissues
Gonadotroph10-15%Follicle-Stimulating HormoneFSHGonads (Folliculogenesis/Spermatogenesis)
Luteinizing HormoneLHGonads (Ovulation/Leydig)
Lactotroph15-20%ProlactinPRLBreast (Lactation)

Note: Gonadotrophs produce both FSH and LH. Total = 6 distinct hormones.

3. Hypothalamic Regulation of Anterior Pituitary

AxisReleasing HormoneInhibiting HormonePituitary HormoneTarget Hormone
HPACRH (Corticotropin-Releasing Hormone)—ACTHCortisol
HPTTRH (Thyrotropin-Releasing Hormone)Somatostatin, DopamineTSHT4/T3
GH/IGF-1GHRH (Growth Hormone-Releasing Hormone)Somatostatin (GHIH)GHIGF-1 (Liver)
HPGGnRH (Gonadotropin-Releasing Hormone)—FSH + LHOestradiol/Testosterone
ProlactinTRH (stimulatory)Dopamine (PIH, Prolactin-Inhibiting Hormone)Prolactin— (Breast)

Portal Circulation: Hypothalamic hormones travel via hypophyseal portal vessels to anterior pituitary.

4. Individual Hormone Profiles

ACTH (Corticotropin)

FeatureDetails
PrecursorPOMC (Pro-opiomelanocortin) → ACTH + β-LPH → β-Endorphin + γ-LPH
RegulationCRH (stimulatory); Cortisol (negative feedback at pituitary + hypothalamus)
SecretionPulsatile, Diurnal peak 06:00-08:00, nadir 23:00-24:00; Stress → ↑
TargetAdrenal Cortex (Zona fasciculata/reticularis) → Cortisol
Clinical ExcessCushing Syndrome (ACTH-dependent: Cushing disease, ectopic ACTH)
Clinical DeficiencySecondary Adrenal Insufficiency (pituitary failure)

TSH (Thyroid-Stimulating Hormone)

FeatureDetails
Structureα-subunit (shared with LH, FSH, hCG) + β-subunit (TSH-specific)
RegulationTRH (stimulatory); T3/T4 (negative feedback, mainly T3 at pituitary); Somatostatin, Dopamine (inhibitory)
SecretionPulsatile; Diurnal peak 02:00-04:00; Cold exposure → ↑
TargetThyroid (TSH-R) → T4/T3 synthesis & release
Clinical ExcessSecondary Hyperthyroidism (TSH-oma); TSH-secreting adenoma
Clinical DeficiencyCentral Hypothyroidism (pituitary/hypothalamic failure)

GH (Growth Hormone / Somatotropin)

FeatureDetails
Structure191 amino acid polypeptide; 22kDa (major) + 20kDa variant
RegulationGHRH (stimulatory); Somatostatin (GHIH) (inhibitory); Ghrelin (stimulatory); IGF-1 (negative feedback); Glucose/Insulin (inhibitory)
SecretionPulsatile; Major peak during slow-wave sleep; Stress, Exercise, Hypoglycaemia, Fasting → ↑; Glucose, Obesity, Ageing → ↓
TargetLiver (IGF-1 production); Direct on tissues (lipolysis, anti-insulin)
Clinical ExcessAcromegaly (adult); Gigantism (child, pre-epiphyseal fusion)
Clinical DeficiencyGHD: Child = Growth failure; Adult = Metabolic syndrome, ↓ QoL

FSH (Follicle-Stimulating Hormone)

FeatureDetails
Structureα-subunit (shared) + β-subunit (FSH-specific)
RegulationGnRH (stimulatory); Inhibin B (ovary/testis) → Negative feedback on FSH selectively; Oestradiol/Testosterone (general)
SecretionPulsatile; Follicular phase: FSH > LH; Mid-cycle: LH > FSH
TargetOvary: Granulosa cells → Folliculogenesis, Oestradiol; Testis: Sertoli cells → Spermatogenesis, Inhibin B
Clinical ExcessRare; Ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome
Clinical DeficiencyHypogonadotropic hypogonadism; Infertility, Absent spermatogenesis

LH (Luteinizing Hormone)

FeatureDetails
Structureα-subunit (shared) + β-subunit (LH-specific)
RegulationGnRH (stimulatory); Oestradiol/Testosterone (negative feedback); Positive feedback (Oestradiol mid-cycle → LH surge)
SecretionPulsatile; Mid-cycle surge (Ovulation); LH > FSH in PCOS (LH:FSH >2:1)
TargetOvary: Theca cells → Androgens → Oestradiol; Ovulation; Corpus luteum → Progesterone; Testis: Leydig cells → Testosterone
Clinical ExcessPCOS (LH:FSH >2:1); LH-secreting adenoma (rare)
Clinical DeficiencyHypogonadotropic hypogonadism; Anovulation, Low testosterone

Prolactin (PRL)

FeatureDetails
Structure199 amino acid polypeptide; Macroprolactin (PRL-IgG complex)
RegulationDopamine (PIH) = Tonically inhibitory (main regulator); TRH (stimulatory); Oestrogen (stimulates lactotroph hyperplasia); Suckling, Stress, Sleep → ↑
SecretionDiurnal: Peak during sleep; Pregnancy: ↑ 10-20x (oestrogen-driven); Postpartum: Suckling → surges
TargetBreast: Mammogenesis, Lactogenesis, Galactopoiesis; Gonads: High PRL → ↓ GnRH → Hypogonadism
Clinical ExcessProlactinoma (most common functioning pituitary adenoma); Drug-induced (antipsychotics, metoclopramide); Hypothyroidism (TRH-driven); Renal failure
Clinical DeficiencyRare; Postpartum failure of lactation (Sheehan syndrome)

5. Anterior Pituitary Axes — Quick Reference

AxisReleasing HormonePituitary HormoneTarget HormoneKey Feedback
HPACRHACTHCortisolCortisol → ↓ CRH + ACTH
HPTTRHTSHT4/T3T3/T4 → ↓ TRH + TSH
GH/IGF-1GHRH / SomatostatinGHIGF-1 (Liver)IGF-1 → ↓ GH + GHRH
HPGGnRHFSH + LHOestradiol/TestosteroneSteroids + Inhibin → ↓ GnRH + LH/FSH
ProlactinTRH (stim) / Dopamine (inhib)Prolactin—Dopamine → ↓ PRL

6. Clinical Patterns of Anterior Pituitary Dysfunction

PatternHormone ProfileCommon Causes
Pan-hypopituitarismAll axes deficientPituitary surgery, Radiotherapy, Apoplexy, Infiltration (sarcoid, haemochromatosis)
Selective deficiencySingle axisMicroadenoma, Autoimmune (lymphocytic hypophysitis), Genetic (PROP1, POU1F1)
Functioning adenomaSingle hormone excessProlactinoma, GH-oma (acromegaly), ACTH-oma (Cushing), TSH-oma
Non-functioning adenomaMass effect + hypopituitarismGonadotroph/Null cell adenoma (large, compress normal pituitary)
Stalk effect↑ Prolactin (disinhibition) + other axes lowLarge non-functioning adenoma compressing stalk → ↓ Dopamine → ↑ PRL

7. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Anterior pituitary hormonesACTH, TSH, GH, FSH, LH, Prolactin (6 hormones)
POMC derivativesACTH, β-LPH, β-Endorphin, γ-LPH
TSH structureα-subunit (shared) + β-subunit (unique)
GhrelinStimulates GH (via GHS-R1a receptor on somatotrophs)
SomatostatinInhibits GH, TSH, Insulin, Glucagon, Gastrin
DopaminePIH = Prolactin-Inhibiting Hormone (tonic inhibition)
Prolactin regulationDopamine (PIH) = Main inhibitor; TRH = stimulatory; Oestrogen = stimulatory
ProlactinomaMost common functioning pituitary adenoma; PRL >1000 → macroprolactinoma
MacroprolactinaemiaPRL-IgG complex; PEG precipitation to confirm; clinically insignificant
Sheehan syndromePostpartum pituitary necrosis → Panhypopituitarism + failure of lactation
Growth hormone pulsatilityMajor peak in slow-wave sleep; suppressed by glucose, insulin, obesity
IGF-1Liver-derived; mediates most GH effects; better screening than random GH
GonadotrophsProduce both FSH and LH; α-subunit shared, β-subunits distinct
Inhibin BSelective FSH negative feedback (from Sertoli/granulosa cells)

8. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Anterior Pituitary))
    Cell Types (5)
      Corticotroph (15-20%)
        ACTH → Cortisol
        POMC → ACTH + β-Endorphin + γ-LPH
      Thyrotroph (5-10%)
        TSH (α+β subunit) → T4/T3
      Somatotroph (40-50%)
        GH → IGF-1 (Liver)
      Gonadotroph (10-15%)
        FSH (α+β); LH (α+β)
      Lactotroph (15-20%)
        Prolactin
    Releasing Hormones
      CRH → ACTH
      TRH → TSH + PRL
      GHRH → GH
      GnRH → FSH + LH
      Somatostatin → inhibits GH, TSH
      Dopamine (PIH) → inhibits PRL
    Axes
      HPA: CRH→ACTH→Cortisol
      HPT: TRH→TSH→T4/T3
      GH/IGF-1: GHRH/SST→GH→IGF-1
      HPG: GnRH→FSH+LH→Sex Steroids
      PRL: Dopamine (inhib), TRH (stim)
    Pathology
      Functioning Adenomas
        Prolactinoma (most common)
        Acromegaly (GH)
        Cushing Disease (ACTH)
        TSH-oma
      Non-functioning
        Mass effect + hypopituitarism
        Stalk effect → ↑ PRL
      Hypopituitarism
        Order of loss: GH → Gonadotrophs → TSH → ACTH

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Anterior pituitary hormonesACTH, TSH, GH, FSH, LH, Prolactin (6 hormones from 5 cell types)
POMC derivativesACTH, β-LPH, β-Endorphin, γ-LPH
TSH structureα-subunit (shared with LH, FSH, hCG) + β-subunit (TSH-specific)
GH pulsatilityMajor peak in slow-wave sleep; suppressed by glucose, insulin, obesity
IGF-1Liver-derived; mediates most GH effects; better screening than random GH
GhrelinStimulates GH via GHS-R1a on somatotrophs
SomatostatinInhibits GH, TSH, Insulin, Glucagon, Gastrin
Dopamine (PIH)Prolactin-Inhibiting Hormone — tonic inhibition of Prolactin
Prolactin regulationDopamine (PIH) = main inhibitor; TRH = stimulatory; Oestrogen stimulates lactotrophs
ProlactinomaMost common functioning pituitary adenoma; PRL >1000 = macroprolactinoma
MacroprolactinaemiaPRL-IgG complex; PEG precipitation to confirm; clinically insignificant
Sheehan syndromePostpartum pituitary necrosis → Panhypopituitarism + failure of lactation
Hypopituitarism order of lossGH → Gonadotrophs → TSH → ACTH (last)
Stalk effectCompression → ↓ Dopamine → ↑ Prolactin (mild elevation)

10. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Anterior Pituitary))
    Cell Types (5)
      Corticotroph (15-20%)
        ACTH → Cortisol
        POMC → ACTH + β-Endorphin + γ-LPH
      Thyrotroph (5-10%)
        TSH (α+β subunit) → T4/T3
      Somatotroph (40-50%)
        GH → IGF-1 (Liver)
        GHRH → GH; Somatostatin inhibits
      Gonadotroph (10-15%)
        FSH (α+β); LH (α+β)
        GnRH → FSH+LH
      Lactotroph (15-20%)
        Prolactin
    Releasing Hormones
      CRH → ACTH
      TRH → TSH + PRL
      GHRH → GH
      GnRH → FSH + LH
      Somatostatin → inhibits GH, TSH
      Dopamine (PIH) → inhibits PRL
    Axes
      HPA: CRH→ACTH→Cortisol
      HPT: TRH→TSH→T4/T3
      GH/IGF-1: GHRH/SST→GH→IGF-1
      HPG: GnRH→FSH+LH→Sex Steroids
      PRL: Dopamine (inhib), TRH (stim)
    Pathology
      Functioning Adenomas
        Prolactinoma (most common)
        Acromegaly (GH)
        Cushing Disease (ACTH)
        TSH-oma
      Non-functioning
        Mass effect + hypopituitarism
        Stalk effect → ↑ PRL
      Hypopituitarism

11. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Anterior pituitary hormonesACTH, TSH, GH, FSH, LH, Prolactin (6 hormones from 5 cell types)
POMC derivativesACTH, β-LPH, β-Endorphin, γ-LPH
TSH structureα-subunit (shared with LH, FSH, hCG) + β-subunit (TSH-specific)
GH pulsatilityMajor peak in slow-wave sleep; suppressed by glucose, insulin, obesity
IGF-1Liver-derived; mediates most GH effects; better screening than random GH
GhrelinStimulates GH via GHS-R1a on somatotrophs
SomatostatinInhibits GH, TSH, Insulin, Glucagon, Gastrin
Dopamine (PIH)Prolactin-Inhibiting Hormone — tonic inhibition of Prolactin
Prolactin regulationDopamine (PIH) = main inhibitor; TRH = stimulatory; Oestrogen stimulates lactotrophs
ProlactinomaMost common functioning pituitary adenoma; PRL >1000 = macroprolactinoma
MacroprolactinaemiaPRL-IgG complex; PEG precipitation to confirm; clinically insignificant
Sheehan syndromePostpartum pituitary necrosis → Panhypopituitarism + failure of lactation
Growth hormone pulsatilityMajor peak in slow-wave sleep; suppressed by glucose, insulin, obesity
IGF-1Liver-derived; mediates most GH effects; better screening than random GH
GonadotrophsProduce both FSH and LH; α-subunit shared, β-subunits distinct
Inhibin BSelective FSH negative feedback (from Sertoli/granulosa cells)

12. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Hypothalamic-Pituitary Axis|Hypothalamic-Pituitary Axis]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]

13. MCQs (10)

  1. Anterior pituitary hormones:

A. ACTH, TSH, GH, LH, FSH, Prolactin

B. ADH, Oxytocin

C. Cortisol, Aldosterone

D. Insulin, Glucagon

E. Thyroxine, Triiodothyronine

  1. Posterior pituitary hormones:

A. ADH, Oxytocin

B. ACTH, TSH

C. GH, Prolactin

D. LH, FSH

E. Cortisol

  1. GH regulation:

A. Stimulated by GHRH, ghrelin; Inhibited by somatostatin, IGF-1

B. Stimulated by TRH

C. Inhibited by cortisol

D. Stimulated by ADH

E. Inhibited by oxytocin

  1. Prolactin regulation:

A. Inhibited by dopamine (PIH)

B. Stimulated by TRH

C. Inhibited by somatostatin

D. Stimulated by GnRH

E. Inhibited by GH

  1. TSH regulation:

A. Stimulated by TRH; Inhibited by T3/T4

B. Stimulated by GHRH

C. Inhibited by cortisol

D. Stimulated by GnRH

E. Inhibited by dopamine

  1. ACTH regulation:

A. Stimulated by CRH, AVP; Inhibited by cortisol

B. Stimulated by TRH

C. Inhibited by somatostatin

D. Stimulated by GnRH

E. Inhibited by dopamine

  1. Gonadotrophin regulation:

A. LH/FSH stimulated by GnRH; Inhibited by sex steroids/inhibin

B. Stimulated by TRH

C. Inhibited by cortisol

D. Stimulated by GHRH

E. Inhibited by dopamine

  1. Pituitary apoplexy:

A. Acute haemorrhage/infarction of pituitary adenoma; sudden headache, visual loss, ophthalmoplegia

B. Gradual onset

C. Only in microadenomas

D. No visual symptoms

E. Asymptomatic

  1. Empty sella syndrome:

A. CSF herniation into sella turcica; may cause hypopituitarism

B. Pituitary adenoma

C. Craniopharyngioma

D. Meningioma

E. Normal variant only

  1. Craniopharyngioma:

A. Rathke's pouch remnants; bimodal age peaks (5-14, 50-75); suprasellar calcified mass

B. Pituitary adenoma

C. Meningioma

D. Germ cell tumour

E. Metastasis

14. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 50yo woman: sudden severe headache, visual loss, hypotension. MRI: pituitary haemorrhage. Immediate?

A. High-dose dexamethasone 8mg IV q6h + fluids + urgent MRI

B. Surgery immediately

C. Observation

D. Dopamine agonist

E. Radiotherapy

  1. Same patient day 1: cortisol 80. Management?

A. Hydrocortisone 100mg IV q8h; taper over days

B. Stop steroids

C. Increase dexamethasone

D. Fludrocortisone

E. TSH

  1. Craniopharyngioma 10yo: bitemporal hemianopia, obesity, polyuria. Suprasellar calcified mass. Treatment?

A. Surgery + adjuvant RT if residual; hormone replacement

B. RT only

C. Observation

D. Chemo

E. Dopamine agonist

  1. Empty sella syndrome: incidentally found, asymptomatic. Management?

A. Screen for hypopituitarism; reassure if normal

B. Surgery

C. RT

D. Dopamine agonist

E. No follow-up needed

  1. Pituitary apoplexy with improving vision post-steroids. Next?

A. Continue steroids; monitor vision; elective decompression if stable

B. Urgent surgery

C. Stop steroids

D. Radiotherapy

E. Dopamine agonist

15. Flashcards

  • Q: Anterior pituitary hormones

A: ACTH, TSH, GH, LH, FSH, Prolactin

  • Q: Posterior pituitary hormones

A: ADH, Oxytocin

  • Q: GH regulation

A: GHRH/ghrelin stimulate; somatostatin/IGF-1 inhibit

  • Q: Prolactin regulation

A: Dopamine inhibits; TRH stimulates

  • Q: TSH regulation

A: TRH stimulates; T3/T4 inhibit

  • Q: ACTH regulation

A: CRH/AVP stimulate; cortisol inhibits

  • Q: Gonadotrophins

A: GnRH stimulates; sex steroids/inhibin inhibit

  • Q: Pituitary apoplexy

A: Acute pituitary haemorrhage; dexamethasone + urgent MRI -> surgery if deteriorating

  • Q: Empty sella

A: CSF in sella; screen for hypopituitarism; usually asymptomatic

  • Q: Craniopharyngioma

A: Rathke's pouch remnants; bimodal peaks; calcification 90%; surgery + RT

16. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. ACTH, TSH, GH, LH, FSH, Prolactin — Anterior pituitary hormones:
  1. ADH, Oxytocin — Posterior pituitary hormones:
  1. Stimulated by GHRH, ghrelin; Inhibited by somatostatin, IGF-1 — GH regulation:
  1. Inhibited by dopamine (PIH) — Prolactin regulation:
  1. Stimulated by TRH; Inhibited by T3/T4 — TSH regulation:
  1. Stimulated by CRH, AVP; Inhibited by cortisol — ACTH regulation:
  1. LH/FSH stimulated by GnRH; Inhibited by sex steroids/inhibin — Gonadotrophin regulation:
  1. Acute haemorrhage/infarction of pituitary adenoma; sudden headache, visual loss, ophthalmoplegia — Pituitary apoplexy:
  1. CSF herniation into sella turcica; may cause hypopituitarism — Empty sella syndrome:
  1. Rathke's pouch remnants; bimodal age peaks (5-14, 50-75); suprasellar calcified mass — Craniopharyngioma:

SBAs

  1. High-dose dexamethasone 8mg IV q6h + fluids + urgent MRI — 50yo woman: sudden severe headache, visual loss, hypotension. MRI: pituitary haemorrhage. Immediate?
  1. Hydrocortisone 100mg IV q8h; taper over days — Same patient day 1: cortisol 80. Management?
  1. Surgery + adjuvant RT if residual; hormone replacement — Craniopharyngioma 10yo: bitemporal hemianopia, obesity, polyuria. Suprasellar calcified mass. Treatment?
  1. Screen for hypopituitarism; reassure if normal — Empty sella syndrome: incidentally found, asymptomatic. Management?
  1. Continue steroids; monitor vision; elective decompression if stable — Pituitary apoplexy with improving vision post-steroids. Next?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.2.03 Craniopharyngioma
[!info]
Craniopharyngioma = Benign Suprasellar Tumour arising from Rathke's Pouch Remnants. Bimodal Age Distribution (Children 5-14, Adults 50-70). Most Common Paediatric Suprasellar Tumour. Calcifications + Cystic/Solid on Imaging. High Recurrence Rate.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Describe embryology, pathology, and bimodal age distribution
  • [ ] Recognise clinical presentation (visual, endocrine, hypothalamic)
  • [ ] Interpret imaging findings (CT calcifications, MRI cystic/solid)
  • [ ] Apply management algorithms (surgery ± radiotherapy)
  • [ ] Understand long-term complications (hypothalamic obesity, DI, panhypopituitarism)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetails
OriginRathke's Pouch Remnants (Embryological Remnant of Anterior Pituitary Development)
WHO GradeGrade 1 (Benign) — But Locally Aggressive
Age DistributionBimodal: Peak 1: 5-14 years (Paediatric); Peak 2: 50-74 years (Adult)
Incidence0.5-2.5 per million/year; Most Common Paediatric Suprasellar Tumour
SexEqual
LocationSuprasellar (95%); Intrasellar (5%); Often Extends to Third Ventricle, Hypothalamus, Optic Chiasm
WHO GradeGrade 1 (Benign Histology) — But Locally Invasive, High Recurrence

3. Histopathology

SubtypeHistologyFrequencyCalcifications
AdamantinomatousWet Keratin (Squamous Epithelium), Cholesterol Clefts, Calcifications, Cystic Fluid ("Motor Oil"), Palisading Nuclei90-95% (Children)90% (Coarse/Chunky)
PapillaryPapillary Architecture, Squamous Epithelium, No Wet Keratin5-10% (Adults)Rare

4. Clinical Presentation

By Tumour Location / Compression

Compressed StructureClinical Features
Optic ChiasmBi-temporal Hemianopia (Classic), Visual Acuity Loss, Colour Vision Defect
Optic NervesUnilateral Visual Loss, Visual Field Defects
Pituitary Stalk/PituitaryHypopituitarism (GH > Gonad > TSH > ACTH), Growth Failure (Children), Delayed Puberty
HypothalamusHypothalamic Obesity (Rapid Weight Gain), Diabetes Insipidus, Temperature Dysregulation, Sleep Disturbance, Behavioural Changes
Third VentricleObstructive Hydrocephalus → Headache, Nausea, Vomiting, Papilloedema
Optic NervesUnilateral Visual Loss, Optic Atrophy

Age-Based Presentation

FeatureChildren (5-14y)Adults (50-74y)
Commonest PresentationVisual Loss (Bi-temporal Hemianopia)Visual Loss / Headache
EndocrineGrowth Failure (GH Deficiency), Delayed PubertyHypogonadism, Hypopituitarism
HypothalamicObesity, DI, Temperature DysregulationObesity, DI, Temperature Dysregulation
HydrocephalusCommon (Third Ventricle Obstruction)Less Common
Calcifications on CT>90% (Adamantinous)Variable (Papillary Rare)

5. Imaging — Key Diagnostic Features

CT Findings

FeatureAdamantinousPapillary
Calcifications>90% (Coarse, Chunky, "Starry Sky")Rare
Cystic ComponentProminent ("Motor Oil" Fluid, High Protein)Less Prominent
Solid ComponentEnhancing NodulesSolid Enhancing Mass
Suprasellar ExtensionYes (Almost Always)Yes

MRI Findings

SequenceFindings
T1Cyst Fluid: Variable (High Protein → High T1; "Motor Oil" Appearance), Solid: Isointense
T2Cyst Fluid: Hyperintense; Solid: Heterogeneous
Post-GadoliniumRim Enhancement (Cyst Wall), Solid Nodule Enhancement
DWIRestricted Diffusion in Cyst (High Protein)
Suprasellar ExtensionUniversal (Optic Chiasm, Third Ventricle, Hypothalamus)
CalcificationsBlooming on SWI/T2* (Adamantinous)

Differential Diagnosis on Imaging

FeatureCraniopharyngiomaPituitary AdenomaArachnoid CystRathke's Cleft CystHypothalamic Glioma
AgeBimodal (Child/Adult)Adult PredominantAnyAnyChild/Young Adult
Calcifications>90% (Adamantinous)RareNoneRareNone
Cystic/SolidCystic + Solid (Enhancing Nodules)Mostly Solid (Enhancing)Pure CystPure CystMostly Solid
LocationSuprasellar (95%)Intrasellar +/- SuprasellarSuprasellar/PontineIntersellar (Ant/Post Pituitary)Hypothalamic/Suprasellar
CalcificationsChunky/CoarseNoneNoneNoneNone
EnhancementRim + NodularHomogeneousNoneNoneHeterogeneous
Calcifications on CT>90%<5%0%<5%0%

6. Clinical Syndromes

Visual Pathway Compression

FeatureDetails
ClassicBi-temporal Hemianopia (Chiasmal Compression)
EarlySuperior Quadrantanopia (Superior Chiasm Compression)
AdvancedCentral Visual Acuity Loss, Optic Atrophy, Colour Vision Defect
UnilateralOptic Nerve Compression (Rare)

Hypothalamic Syndrome

FeatureDetails
ObesityRapid, Severe, Refractory (Ventromedial Nucleus Damage); Insatiable Appetite
Diabetes InsipidusCentral DI (Supraoptic Nucleus/Stalk Damage); 30-50% at Presentation, ↑ Post-Surgery
Temperature DysregulationPoikilothermia (Anterior Hypothalamus), Hyperthermia (Posterior)
Sleep DisturbanceNarcolepsy-like, Circadian Rhythm Disruption (SCN Damage)
Behavioural/PsychiatricApathy, Aggression, Cognitive Impairment, Memory Deficits (Mammillary Bodies)

Endocrine Deficiencies (Order of Loss)

OrderAxisFrequency
1stGH>90% (Children) — Growth Failure
2ndGonadotrophs80-90% — Delayed Puberty / Hypogonadism
3rdTSH50-70% — Central Hypothyroidism
4thACTH30-50% — Secondary Adrenal Insufficiency
CommonDI30-50% (Hypothalamic/Stalk Damage)

7. Management Algorithm

Craniopharyngioma Suspected (Imaging + Clinical)
         │
         ▼
MULTIDISCIPLINARY TEAM (Neurosurgery, Endocrinology, Ophthalmology, Radiation Oncology, Paediatrics)
         │
         ▼
STAGING & ASSESSMENT
         │
         ├── MRI Brain (3mm, Gadolinium) — Extent, Hypothalamic Involvement, Vessels
         ├── CT Head (Calcifications) — Adamantinous vs Papillary
         ├── Visual Fields (Goldmann/Humphrey) — Baseline
         ├── Full Hormone Panel (GH/IGF-1, LH/FSH, TSH/fT4, Cortisol/ACTH, PRL, Electrolytes)
         ├── Visual Acuity + Fundoscopy
         └── Genetic Counselling (If Familial/Syndromic)
         │
         ▼
MANAGEMENT DECISION
         │
         ├── **GROSS TOTAL RESECTION (GTR) IF FEASIBLE**
         │       (No Hypothalamic Involvement; Tumour Accessible)
         │       → Transsphenoidal (If Intrasellar) / Transcranial (Suprasellar)
         │       → **Adjuvant RT if GTR** (To Reduce Recurrence)
         │
         ├── **SUBTOTAL RESECTION (STR) + ADJUVANT RADIOTHERAPY**
         │       (Hypothalamic Involvement / Vascular Encasing / High Risk GTR)
         │       → **Stereotactic Radiosurgery (SRS)** or **Fractionated RT** (54 Gy/30 fractions)
         │
         ├── **CYSTIC DISEASE DOMINANT**
         │       → **Cyst Aspiration / Cystoperitoneal Shunt / Intracystic Bleomycin/Interferon/¹⁸⁹Re**
         │       → RT if Residual Solid Component
         │
         └── **PALLIATIVE / BEST SUPPORTIVE CARE**
                 (Elderly / Comorbid / Advanced Disease / Patient Choice)
                 → Symptom Control (DI, Hormone Replacement, Obesity Management)
         │
         ▼
POST-OP MANAGEMENT
         │
         ├── IMMEDIATE: DI (Desmopressin), Hormone Replacement, Visual Fields, Electrolytes
         ├── ENDOCRINE REPLACEMENT (GH, Thyroxine, Sex Steroids, Hydrocortisone)
         ├── DI MANAGEMENT (Desmopressin; Monitor Sodium)
         ├── HYPOTHALAMIC OBESITY (GLP-1 Agonists, Lifestyle, Bariatric Surgery Consideration)
         └── SURVEILLANCE (MRI 3mo, 6mo, 1yr then Yearly; Endocrine q6mo; Visual Fields q6-12mo)

8. Surgical Approaches

ApproachIndicationAdvantages
Endoscopic Endonasal (Transsphenoidal)Intrasellar / Small SuprasellarMinimally Invasive; No Brain Retraction; Good for Cystic Component
Transcranial (Pterional / Subfrontal / Interhemispheric)Large Suprasellar / Third Ventricle / Hypothalamic InvolvementBetter Access to Suprasellar / Third Ventricle / Vessels
Combined (Endonasal + Transcranial)Giant / Complex / Extensive Vascular InvolvementMaximum Exposure

Extent of Resection

GoalCriteriaRecurrence Risk
Gross Total Resection (GTR)No Residual Tumour on Post-op MRILowest (10-20% at 10yr)
Near-Total (>95%)Minimal ResidualLow (20-30% at 10yr)
Subtotal (STR)Significant ResidualHigh (50-70% at 10yr) → Requires Adjuvant RT

9. Adjuvant Radiotherapy

IndicationModalityDose / Schedule
Post-STRFractionated RT (Conformal/IMRT)54 Gy / 30 Fractions (1.8 Gy/Fx)
Small Residual <3cmStereotactic Radiosurgery (SRS)12-14 Gy (Single Fraction)
Recurrence / InoperableSRS or Fractionated RTPer Protocol
ComplicationIncidenceMitigation
Hypopituitarism30-50% at 10-15yrConformal Techniques; Dose Constraints to Pituitary/Chiasm
Optic Neuropathy<1% (Fractionated); 1-3% (SRS)Dose Constraint to Optic Chiasm/Nerves
Cognitive DeclineVariableModern Conformal Techniques
Secondary Malignancy<1% (Long-term)Modern Techniques
Cerebral VasculopathyMoyamoya-like (Rare)Vascular Dose Constraints

10. Complications & Long-term Management

Diabetes Insipidus (DI)

TimingFrequencyManagement
Pre-op30-50%Baseline Assessment
Post-op (Acute)60-80% (Transient)Desmopressin (Intranasal/Oral/SC) + Fluid Balance
Permanent10-20%Lifelong Desmopressin + Fluid Management

Hypothalamic Obesity

FeatureManagement
MechanismVentromedial Hypothalamus Damage → Leptin Resistance + Hyperphagia + ↓ Energy Expenditure
MedicalGLP-1 RA (Liraglutide/Semaglutide); Metformin; Topiramate (Adjunct)
LifestyleStructured Diet, Exercise, Behavioural Therapy
SurgicalBariatric Surgery (RYGB/Sleeve) — Consider in Severe Refractory Cases

Endocrine Deficiencies — Replacement

DeficiencyReplacement
GHChildren: RH-GH 0.025-0.035 mg/kg/day SC; Adults: 0.1-0.3 mg/day SC (if Symptomatic)
TSH/fT4Levothyroxine (Start BEFORE Glucocorticoid)
ACTH/CortisolHydrocortisone 15-20mg AM + 5-10mg PM
GonadotrophsChildren: Puberty Induction (Low Dose → Adult Dose); Adults: Testosterone / HRT
DIDesmopressin (Intranasal/Oral/SC) + Fluid Balance

11. Recurrence & Surveillance

Extent of Resection5-Year Recurrence10-Year RecurrenceAdjuvant RT
GTR10-20%20-30%Optional (Low Risk)
Near-Total (>95%)20-30%30-40%Often Recommended
STR50-60%60-70%Mandatory

Surveillance Schedule

TimepointInvestigations
3 MonthsMRI Brain (Contrast), Visual Fields, Full Hormones
6 MonthsMRI Brain, Visual Fields, Hormones (GH/IGF-1, TSH/fT4, Cortisol, LH/FSH)
1 YearMRI Brain (Contrast), Visual Fields, Full Hormones
Annually (Years 2-5)MRI Brain (Contrast), Visual Fields, Hormones
Years 5-10MRI + Hormones + Visual Fields (Every 1-2 Years)
>10 Years2-3 Yearly MRI + Hormones + Visual Fields

12. Special Complications

Hypothalamic Obesity

FeatureDetails
PathophysiologyVentromedial Hypothalamus Damage → Leptin Resistance + Hyperphagia + ↓ Energy Expenditure
OnsetRapid (Months Post-Surgery/RT)
ManagementGLP-1 RA (Liraglutide/Semaglutide), Metformin, Topiramate, Bariatric Surgery (Refractory)

Vasculopathy

TypeFeatures
Moyamoya-like VasculopathyPost-RT (5-10yr); Stenosis of Circle of Willis; Ischaemic Strokes
Cavernous Sinus / Vascular InjuryPost-Surgical (Rare)

Secondary Malignancy

RiskTimeline
Secondary Glioma / Sarcoma10-20 Years Post-RT; <1% Cumulative Incidence

13. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Craniopharyngioma OriginRathke's Pouch Remnants (Embryological)
Bimodal AgeChildren 5-14 (Adamantinous) + Adults 50-74 (Papillary)
Most Common Paediatric Suprasellar TumourCraniopharyngioma
Adamantinous (Child)Calcifications >90%; Cystic ("Motor Oil"); Wet Keratin
Papillary (Adult)No Calcifications; Papillary Architecture
Calcifications on CT>90% (Adamantinous); "Starry Sky" / Chunky
MRI Cyst FluidHigh T1 (Protein-Rich "Motor Oil"); T2 Hyperintense
Visual FieldsBi-temporal Hemianopia (Chiasmal Compression)
Hypothalamic SyndromeObesity + DI + Temperature Dysregulation + Sleep Disturbance
GH Deficiency>90% Children → Growth Failure
DI30-50% at Presentation; ↑ Post-Surgery (60-80% Transient, 10-20% Permanent)
Surgical GoalGTR if Hypothalamus Spared → STR + RT if Hypothalamic Involvement
RecurrenceGTR: 10-20%; STR: 50-70% → Adjuvant RT Mandatory for STR
DI Post-Surgery60-80% Transient; 10-20% Permanent → Desmopressin
Hypothalamic ObesityVentromedial Damage → Leptin Resistance + Hyperphagia; GLP-1 RA Beneficial
RecurrenceGTR: 20% at 10yr; STR + RT: 30-40% at 10yr
SurveillanceMRI + Visual Fields + Hormones; 3mo → 6mo → 1yr → Yearly ×5 → 2-3yrly
RT ComplicationsHypopituitarism (30-50% at 10yr), Optic Neuropathy (<1%), Moyamoya-like Vasculopathy

14. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Craniopharyngioma vs Pituitary AdenomaCraniopharyngioma = Suprasellar, Calcified, Cystic; Adenoma = Intrasellar, Enhancing, No Calcification
Craniopharyngioma vs Rathke's CystCranio = Solid+Cystic + Calcifications; Rathke's = Pure Cystic, Midline, No Calcification
Adamantinous vs PapillaryAdamantinous = Child + Calcifications + Wet Keratin; Papillary = Adult + No Calcification + Papillary Architecture
Craniopharyngioma vs Hypothalamic GliomaCranio = Cystic + Calcifications + Enhancing Nodules; Glioma = Solid + Infiltrative + No Calcification
GTR vs STRGTR = Curative Intent (10-20% Recurrence); STR = Requires RT (50-70% Recurrence without RT)
Post-op DITransient in 60-80%; Permanent 10-20% → Lifelong Desmopressin

Mnemonic: CRANIO

  • Calcifications (>90% Adamantinous)
  • Rathke's Pouch Origin
  • Adamantinous (Child) vs Papillary (Adult)
  • Near Optic Chiasm → Bi-temporal Hemianopia
  • Intracystic "Motor Oil" Fluid (High Protein)
  • Obesity (Hypothalamic) + Diabetes Insipidus + Growth Failure

15. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Craniopharyngioma))
    Origin
      Rathke's Pouch Remnants
      Adamantinous (Child) vs Papillary (Adult)
    Pathology
      Adamantinous: Wet Keratin, Cholesterol Clefts, Calcifications
      Papillary: Papillary Architecture, No Calcification
    Presentation
      Visual: Bi-temporal Hemianopia
      Endocrine: Growth Failure, Delayed Puberty, Hypopituitarism
      Hypothalamic: Obesity, DI, Temperature/Sleep Dysregulation
      Hydrocephalus (3rd Ventricle)
    Imaging
      CT: Calcifications >90% (Adamantinous)
      MRI: Cystic (High T1 "Motor Oil") + Solid Enhancing Nodules
      Suprasellar + 3rd Ventricle + Hypothalamus
    Management
      GTR if Hypothalamus Spared
      STR + RT if Hypothalamic Involvement
      RT: 54Gy/30Fx or SRS
    Complications
      DI (60-80% Transient; 10-20% Permanent)
      Hypothalamic Obesity (Leptin Resistance)
      Panhypopituitarism
      Moyamoya-like Vasculopathy (Post-RT)
    Surveillance
      MRI + Visual Fields + Hormones
      3mo → 6mo → 1yr → Yearly ×5 → 2-3yrly
    Recurrence
      GTR: 10-20% at 10yr
      STR+RT: 30-40% at 10yr

16. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
OriginRathke's Pouch Remnants (Embryological)
Bimodal AgeChildren 5-14 (Adamantinous) + Adults 50-74 (Papillary)
Most Common Paediatric Suprasellar TumourCraniopharyngioma
Adamantinous vs PapillaryAdamantinous = Child, Calcifications, Wet Keratin; Papillary = Adult, No Calcification
CT Calcifications>90% (Adamantinous); "Starry Sky" / Chunky
MRI Cyst FluidHigh T1 ("Motor Oil" - Protein Rich); T2 Hyperintense
Visual FieldsBi-temporal Hemianopia (Chiasmal Compression)
Hypothalamic SyndromeObesity + DI + Temperature/Sleep Dysregulation
GH Deficiency>90% Children → Growth Failure
DI30-50% at Presentation; 60-80% Post-Op (Transient); 10-20% Permanent
SurgeryGTR if Hypothalamus Spared; STR + RT if Involved
RecurrenceGTR 10-20%; STR 50-70% (Needs Adjuvant RT)
DI Post-Op60-80% Transient (6-12mo); 10-20% Permanent
Hypothalamic ObesityVentromedial Damage → Leptin Resistance; GLP-1 RA Beneficial
RT ComplicationsHypopituitarism (30-50% at 10yr); Optic Neuropathy; Moyamoya Vasculopathy
SurveillanceMRI + Visual Fields + Hormones; 3mo → 6mo → 1yr → Yearly ×5 → 2-3yrly

18. MCQs (10)

  1. Craniopharyngioma origin:

A. Rathke's pouch remnants (ectoderm)

B. Pituitary adenoma

C. Meningioma

D. Germ cell tumour

E. Metastasis

  1. Craniopharyngioma peaks:

A. Bimodal: children 5-14yr, adults 50-75yr

B. Children only

C. Adults only

D. Infants

E. Elderly only

  1. Craniopharyngioma presentation:

A. Visual field defects (bitemporal), headache, hypothalamic/pituitary dysfunction

B. Only headache

C. Only visual loss

D. Only diabetes insipidus

E. Asymptomatic

  1. Craniopharyngioma imaging:

A. Suprasellar cystic mass with calcification (90%)

B. Solid enhancing

C. No calcification

D. Intracranial only

E. Intrasellar only

  1. Craniopharyngioma histology:

A. Adamantinomatous (children): wet keratin, calcification; Papillary (adults): solid

B. Pure solid

C. Pure cystic

D. Malignant

E. No specific features

  1. Craniopharyngioma treatment:

A. Surgery (transsphenoidal/craniotomy) + adjuvant RT if residual; hormone replacement

B. RT only

C. Surgery only

D. Observation

E. Chemo

  1. Craniopharyngioma complications:

A. Hypothalamic obesity, diabetes insipidus, panhypopituitarism, visual loss, seizures

B. Only DI

C. Only obesity

D. Only visual loss

E. No complications

  1. Craniopharyngioma recurrence:

A. High (50-90% at 10yr) if gross residual; RT reduces risk

B. Never recurs

C. Only if malignant

D. Only in children

E. Never

  1. Craniopharyngioma hypothalamus:

A. Hypothalamic injury → obesity, temperature dysregulation, sleep disorders, DI

B. No effect

C. Only DI

D. Only obesity

E. Only sleep

  1. Craniopharyngioma in adults:

A. More commonly papillary type; less calcification; better visual outcome

B. Same as children

C. Worse prognosis

D. No surgery needed

E. Always malignant

19. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 10yo boy: headache, bitemporal hemianopia, obesity, polyuria. Suprasellar cystic calcified mass. Diagnosis?

A. Craniopharyngioma

B. Pituitary adenoma

C. Meningioma

D. Germinoma

E. Rathke's cleft cyst

  1. Same patient: optimal treatment?

A. Surgery (transsphenoidal/craniotomy) + adjuvant RT if residual; hormone replacement

B. RT only

C. Observation

D. Chemo

E. Dopamine agonist

  1. Post-surgery: severe obesity, hyperphagia, temperature dysregulation. Mechanism?

A. Hypothalamic injury (ventromedial nucleus)

B. Pituitary GH deficiency

C. Thyroid dysfunction

D. Cortisol excess

E. Medication side effect

  1. Adult 55yo: suprasellar mass, calcified, bitemporal hemianopia. Type?

A. Papillary craniopharyngioma (adults)

B. Adamantinomatous (children)

C. Pituitary adenoma

D. Meningioma

E. Germinoma

  1. Craniopharyngioma post-op: polyuria, hypernatraemia. Management?

A. DDAVP + fluid management (DI); monitor hopelessness

B. Fluid restriction

C. Demeclocycline

D. Tolvaptan

E. Observation

20. Flashcards

  • Q: Craniopharyngioma origin

A: Rathke's pouch remnants (ectoderm)

  • Q: Bimodal peaks

A: Children 5-14yr; Adults 50-75yr

  • Q: Adamantinomatous (children)

A: Cystic, calcification, wet keratin, BRAF V600E

  • Q: Papillary (adults)

A: Solid, less calcification, CTNNB1 mutation

  • Q: Imaging

A: Suprasellar cystic + solid + CALCIFICATION (90%)

  • Q: Treatment

A: Surgery (transsphenoidal/craniotomy) + adjuvant RT if residual; hormone replacement

  • Q: Complications

A: Hypothalamic obesity, DI, panhypopituitarism, visual loss, seizures

  • Q: Recurrence

A: High (50-90% at 10yr) if gross residual; RT reduces risk

  • Q: Hypothalamic injury

A: Obesity, temperature dysregulation, sleep disorders, DI

  • Q: Adult type

A: Papillary; less calcification; CTNNB1 mutation

21. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Rathke's pouch remnants (ectoderm) — Craniopharyngioma origin:
  1. Bimodal: children 5-14yr, adults 50-75yr — Craniopharyngioma peaks:
  1. Visual field defects (bitemporal), headache, hypothalamic/pituitary dysfunction — Craniopharyngioma presentation:
  1. Suprasellar cystic mass with calcification (90%) — Craniopharyngioma imaging:
  1. Adamantinomatous (children): wet keratin, calcification; Papillary (adults): solid — Craniopharyngioma histology:
  1. Surgery (transsphenoidal/craniotomy) + adjuvant RT if residual; hormone replacement — Craniopharyngioma treatment:
  1. Hypothalamic obesity, diabetes insipidus, panhypopituitarism, visual loss, seizures — Craniopharyngioma complications:
  1. High (50-90% at 10yr) if gross residual; RT reduces risk — Craniopharyngioma recurrence:
  1. Hypothalamic injury → obesity, temperature dysregulation, sleep disorders, DI — Craniopharyngioma hypothalamus:
  1. More commonly papillary type; less calcification; better visual outcome — Craniopharyngioma in adults:

SBAs

  1. Craniopharyngioma — 10yo boy: headache, bitemporal hemianopia, obesity, polyuria. Suprasellar cystic calcified mass. Diagnosis?
  1. Surgery (transsphenoidal/craniotomy) + adjuvant RT if residual; hormone replacement — Same patient: optimal treatment?
  1. Hypothalamic injury (ventromedial nucleus) — Post-surgery: severe obesity, hyperphagia, temperature dysregulation. Mechanism?
  1. Papillary craniopharyngioma (adults) — Adult 55yo: suprasellar mass, calcified, bitemporal hemianopia. Type?
  1. DDAVP + fluid management (DI); monitor hopelessness — Craniopharyngioma post-op: polyuria, hypernatraemia. Management?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.2.04 Empty Sella Syndrome
[!info]
Empty Sella Syndrome (ESS) = Enlarged Sella Turcica with Flattened/Attenuated Pituitary Gland. Primary (Idiopathic) vs Secondary (Post-Surgery/RT/Sheehan). Most are Asymptomatic Incidental Findings; Hypopituitarism in 10-15%. Not a tumour.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Define Empty Sella Syndrome and classify Primary vs Secondary
  • [ ] Recognise radiological criteria on MRI/CT
  • [ ] Differentiate from Pituitary Adenoma, Arachnoid Cyst, Craniopharyngioma
  • [ ] Assess pituitary function and manage hypopituitarism if present
  • [ ] Counsel patients on prognosis and surveillance

2. Definition & Classification

TypeDefinitionMechanism
Primary (Idiopathic) ESSEnlarged Sella + Atresia of Diaphragma Sellae → CSF Pulsations → Pituitary FlatteningCongenital Diaphragma Sellae Defect → CSF Pulsations → Pituitary Atrophy
Secondary ESSPost-Destructive (Surgery, Radiotherapy, Haemorrhage, Infection, Sheehan Syndrome)Pituitary Destruction → CSF Fills Sella
FeaturePrimary ESSSecondary ESS
AetiologyCongenital Diaphragma Sellae DefectPituitary Destruction (Surgery, RT, Apoplexy, Sheehan, Infection)
DemographicsWomen > Men (4:1); 30-60 yearsAny Age; Post-Surgery/RT/Sheehan
Pituitary Height<2mm (Flattened)Variable
Pituitary FunctionMostly Normal (85-90%)Hypopituitarism Common (50-70%)
Associated ConditionsObesity, Hypertension, Idiopathic Intracranial HypertensionSheehan, Post-Surgery, RT, Apoplexy

3. Radiological Diagnosis (MRI)

Diagnostic Criteria (MRI)

CriterionFinding
Sella VolumeEnlarged (Volume >1000 mm³ or Area >500 mm²)
Pituitary Height<2mm (Flattened against Sella Floor)
Pituitary Volume<20% of Sella Volume
CSF SignalCSF Signal Intensity Filling Sella (T1 Hypointense, T2 Hyperintense)
Pituitary StalkCentral / Slightly Deviated; Intact
Diaphragma SellaeIncompetent/Absent (CSF Pulsations Visible)

Imaging Modalities

ModalityRole
MRI (T1/T2, Sagittal/Coronal, 3mm)Gold Standard; Pituitary Gland, Stalk, Optic Chiasm, Diaphragma
CT (Thin Slices)Bony Sella Enlargement; Calcifications; If MRI Contraindicated

Differential Diagnosis on Imaging

FindingEmpty SellaPituitary AdenomaArachnoid CystCraniopharyngioma
Pituitary GlandFlattened (<2mm)Mass Expanding SellaAbsent/DisplacedDisplaced/Invaded
CSF in SellaYes (Fills Sella)No (Solid/Cystic)Yes (CSF Signal)Cystic + Solid (Calcifications)
Pituitary StalkCentralDisplaced/EncasedDisplacedDisplaced/Invaded
Diaphragma SellaeIncompetentIntactIntactIntact/Disrupted
EnhancementNone (Pituitary Thin)Avid EnhancementNo EnhancementRim + Solid Enhancement + Calcifications

4. Clinical Presentation

ScenarioFrequencyDetails
Asymptomatic Incidentaloma85-90% (Primary ESS)Found on MRI for Headache/Dizziness/Trauma
Headache20-30%Non-specific; May Relate to Intracranial Hypertension
Visual Disturbances<10%Chiasmal Compression (Rare; ESS Usually No Chiasmal Compression)
Hypopituitarism10-15% (Primary ESS); 50-70% (Secondary ESS)GH > Gonadotrophs > TSH > ACTH
Cerebrospinal Fluid LeakRareCSF Rhinorrhoea (Defective Diaphragma Sellae)
Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension (IIH)AssociationObese Women; Papilloedema; ESS Common in IIH

Hypopituitarism in ESS

AxisFrequency (Primary ESS)Frequency (Secondary ESS)
GH10-15%40-50%
Gonadotrophs5-10%30-40%
TSH5-10%20-30%
ACTH<5%10-20%

Order of Loss: GH → Gonadotrophs → TSH → ACTH (Same as General Hypopituitarism)

5. Aetiology & Pathophysiology

Primary (Idiopathic) ESS

MechanismDetails
Diaphragma Sellae DefectCongenital Incompetence/Absence of Diaphragma Sellae
CSF PulsationsPulsatile CSF Pressure → Pituitary Flattening Against Sella Floor
Risk FactorsFemale Gender, Obesity, Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension (IIH), Hypertension
Pituitary FunctionMostly Preserved (Pituitary Tissue Compressed but Viable)

Secondary ESS

CauseMechanism
Pituitary SurgeryTranssphenoidal Resection → Pituitary Volume Loss
RadiotherapyRadiation-Induced Atrophy + Fibrosis
Pituitary ApoplexyHaemorrhagic Infarction → Necrosis + CSF Replacement
Sheehan SyndromePostpartum Pituitary Necrosis → Empty Sella
Radiation NecrosisDelayed (Years Post-RT)
Infection/InflammationMeningitis, Sarcoidosis, Lymphocytic Hypophysitis

6. Clinical Presentation

SymptomFrequencySignificance
Asymptomatic80-90% (Primary)Incidental MRI Finding
Headache20-30%Non-specific; May Relate to IIH
Visual Disturbances<5%ESS Usually Does Not Compress Chiasm (Unlike Adenoma)
Hypopituitarism10-15% (Primary)Screen if Symptomatic
CSF RhinorrhoeaRareDefective Diaphragma Sellae
Secondary ESS50-70% HypopituitarismSheehan, Post-Surgery, RT, Apoplexy

7. Diagnosis

MRI Diagnostic Criteria (Primary ESS)

CriterionThreshold
Sella EnlargementAnteroposterior Diameter >12mm (or Volume >1000 mm³)
Pituitary Height<2mm (or <3mm in some definitions)
Pituitary Volume<20% of Sella Volume
CSF in SellaCSF Signal Intensity (T2 Hyperintense) Filling Sella
Pituitary StalkCentral / Slightly Deviated, Intact
Diaphragma SellaeIncompetent/Absent (Visible CSF Pulsations)

Assessment of Pituitary Function (All ESS)

AxisTestsInterpretation
GH/IGF-1IGF-1 (Age-Adjusted)Low = GHD
GonadalLH/FSH + Testosterone/OestradiolLow = Central Hypogonadism
ThyroidfT4 + TSHLow fT4 + Low/Normal TSH = Central Hypo
Adrenal09:00 Cortisol + ACTHLow Cortisol + Low/Normal ACTH = Secondary AI
ProlactinProlactinNormal or Mildly ↑ (Stalk Effect)

8. Management

Asymptomatic Primary ESS (Normal Pituitary Function)

ApproachDetails
ReassuranceNot a Tumour; Benign Radiological Finding
SurveillanceAnnual Clinical Review for 2-3 Years; Repeat MRI if New Symptoms
Hormonal ScreeningBaseline Hormones; Repeat if Symptoms Develop

Symptomatic Primary ESS (Hypopituitarism)

DeficiencyReplacement
GH DeficiencyRH-GH 0.1-0.3 mg/day SC (Adults); 0.025-0.035 mg/kg/day (Children)
Gonadotroph DeficiencyTestosterone (Male) / HRT (Female)
TSH DeficiencyLevothyroxine (Start BEFORE Glucocorticoid)
ACTH DeficiencyHydrocortisone 15-20mg AM + 5-10mg PM
MonitoringAnnual Hormones for 2-3 Years; Then if Stable, Less Frequent

Secondary ESS (Post-Surgery/RT/Sheehan)

ApproachDetails
Full Hormonal AssessmentAll Anterior Pituitary Axes + Prolactin
Aggressive ReplacementHigher Likelihood of Panhypopituitarism
Sheehan SyndromeAgalactorrhoea + Amenorrhoea + Hair Loss; Empty Sella on MRI; Lifelong Replacement
Post-RTSurveillance for 10+ Years (Delayed Hypopituitarism)
Post-SurgeryImmediate Post-op Hormonal Assessment; Replace if Deficient

9. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionKey Differentiators
Pituitary AdenomaSolid Mass Expanding Sella; Enhancement; Stalk Displacement
Arachnoid CystCystic, CSF Signal, Thin Wall, No Pituitary Tissue
CraniopharyngiomaSuprasellar; Calcifications; Rim Enhancement; Stalk Displacement
Rathke's Cleft CystMidline Cystic; No Enhancement; Between Ant/Post Pituitary
MeningiomaDural Tail; Homogeneous Enhancement; Suprasellar
Hypothalamic GliomaSuprasellar; Infiltrative; Optic Pathway Involvement

10. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
ESS DefinitionSella Enlarged + Pituitary Flattened (<2mm) + CSF-filled Sella
Primary vs SecondaryPrimary = Idiopathic (Diaphragma Defect); Secondary = Post-Surgery/RT/Sheehan
Primary ESS DemographicsWomen 4:1; Age 30-60; Obesity, Hypertension, IIH Association
Symptoms85-90% Asymptomatic (Incidental); Headache 20-30%; Visual Loss Rare
Hypopituitarism in Primary ESS10-15% (Mostly GH/Gonadotrophs); ACTH Rare
Hypopituitarism in Secondary ESS50-70% (Sheehan, Post-Surgery, RT)
ESS vs AdenomaESS = Flattened Pituitary + CSF in Sella; Adenoma = Mass + Enhancement
ESS vs Arachnoid CystArachnoid Cyst = Cystic + Thin Wall; ESS = Pituitary Tissue Present (Flattened)
Pituitary Stalk in ESSCentral / Intact (vs Displaced in Adenoma)
Visual Fields in ESSUsually Normal (No Chiasmal Compression)
CSF RhinorrhoeaRare; Defective Diaphragma Sellae
IIH AssociationESS Common in IIH (Obese Women, Papilloedema)
Management Primary ESSAsymptomatic = Reassure + Annual Review; Symptomatic = Replace Deficiencies

11. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
ESS vs Pituitary AdenomaESS = Flattened Pituitary + CSF in Sella; Adenoma = Mass + Enhancement
ESS vs Arachnoid CystArachnoid Cyst = Pure Cystic CSF; ESS = Flattened Pituitary Tissue Present
Primary vs Secondary ESSPrimary = Idiopathic (Women, Obesity, IIH); Secondary = Post-Surgery/RT/Sheehan
ESS vs SheehanSheehan = Postpartum Necrosis → Secondary ESS; Primary ESS = Idiopathic
ESS vs Rathke's CystRathke's = Midline Cyst Between Ant/Post Pituitary; ESS = Flattened Pituitary in Sella
Visual Fields in ESSUsually Normal (No Chiasmal Compression)
Pituitary Stalk in ESSCentral/Intact (vs Displaced in Adenoma)

Mnemonic: EMPTY SELLA

  • Empty = Pituitary Flattened <2mm
  • Mostly Women (4:1)
  • Primary (Idiopathic) vs Secondary (Surgery/RT/Sheehan)
  • Thyroid/Adrenal/Gonadal Usually Normal (Primary)
  • Young Women: Obesity + IIH Association
  • Sella Enlarged + CSF Filled
  • ESS = Incidentaloma 85-90%
  • Leads to Hypopituitarism Rarely (10-15% Primary)
  • Leads to HYPOPIT in Secondary (50-70%)
  • As Symptomatic = Replace Deficiencies

12. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Empty Sella Syndrome))
    Types
      Primary (Idiopathic)
        Diaphragma Sellae Defect
        Women 4:1, Obesity, IIH
        Mostly Asymptomatic
        Hypopituitarism 10-15%
      Secondary
        Post-Surgery, RT, Apoplexy, Sheehan
        Hypopituitarism 50-70%
    MRI Criteria
      Sella Enlarged (AP >12mm)
      Pituitary Height <2mm
      Pituitary Volume <20% Sella
      CSF Signal in Sella
      Stalk Central/Intact
    Differential
      Pituitary Adenoma (Mass + Enhancement)
      Arachnoid Cyst (Cystic, Thin Wall)
      Craniopharyngioma (Suprasellar + Calcification)
      Rathke's Cyst (Midline Cyst Ant/Post Pituitary)
    Hypopituitarism
      Primary ESS: 10-15% (GH > Gonad > TSH > ACTH)
      Secondary ESS: 50-70%
    Management
      Primary Asymptomatic: Reassure + Annual ×2-3yr
      Primary Symptomatic: Replace Deficiencies
      Secondary: Full Replacement + Surveillance
    Associations
      Women 4:1
      Obesity
      Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension (IIH)
      CSF Rhinorrhoea (Rare)

13. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
ESS DefinitionEnlarged Sella + Pituitary Height <2mm + CSF in Sella
Primary ESSIdiopathic; Women 4:1; Obesity/IIH; 10-15% Hypopituitarism
Secondary ESSPost-Surgery/RT/Sheehan/Apoplexy; 50-70% Hypopituitarism
ESS vs AdenomaESS = Flattened Pituitary + CSF; Adenoma = Mass + Enhancement
ESS vs Arachnoid CystArachnoid = Pure Cyst; ESS = Flattened Pituitary Tissue
Primary ESS Hypopituitarism10-15% (GH > Gonad > TSH > ACTH)
Secondary ESS Hypopituitarism50-70%
Pituitary Stalk in ESSCentral / Intact (vs Displaced in Adenoma)
Visual Fields in ESSUsually Normal (No Chiasmal Compression)
Primary ESS ManagementAsymptomatic = Reassure + Annual Review ×2-3yr
Secondary ESS ManagementFull Hormonal Replacement + Lifelong Surveillance
Sheehan SyndromePostpartum Necrosis → Secondary ESS + Panhypopituitarism
IIH AssociationESS Common in IIH (Obese Women, Papilloedema)

14. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Empty Sella Syndrome))
    Types
      Primary (Idiopathic)
        Diaphragma Sellae Defect
        Women 4:1, Obesity, IIH
        Asymptomatic 85-90%
        Hypopituitarism 10-15%
      Secondary
        Post-Surgery/RT/Apoplexy/Sheehan
        Hypopituitarism 50-70%
    MRI
      Sella Enlarged
      Pituitary Height <2mm
      CSF in Sella
      Stalk Central
    Differential
      Pituitary Adenoma (Mass)
      Arachnoid Cyst (Pure Cyst)
      Craniopharyngioma (Suprasellar + Calcification)
      Rathke's Cyst (Midline Cyst)
    Associations
      Women 4:1
      Obesity
      IIH
      Hypertension
    Management
      Primary Asymptomatic: Reassure
      Primary Symptomatic: Replace
      Secondary: Full Replacement
    Associations
      Women 4:1
      Obesity
      IIH

15. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Hypothalamic-Pituitary Axis|Hypothalamic-Pituitary Axis]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Hypopituitarism]], [[Sheehan Syndrome]], [[Pituitary Adenomas: Non-Functioning]], [[Pituitary Apoplexy]], [[Craniopharyngioma]]

16. MCQs (10)

  1. Empty sella syndrome =

A. CSF herniation into sella turcica (primary or secondary); may cause hypopituitarism

B. Pituitary adenoma

C. Craniopharyngioma

D. Meningioma

E. Normal variant only

  1. Primary empty sella:

A. Idiopathic CSF herniation; often asymptomatic; common in obese women with hypertension

B. Post-surgery

C. Post-RT

D. Post-haemorrhage

E. Genetic

  1. Secondary empty sella:

A. Post-pituitary surgery/RT/haemorrhage; pituitary atrophy

B. Idiopathic

C. Genetic

D. Infectious

E. Autoimmune

  1. Empty sella presentation:

A. Often asymptomatic; headache; visual field defects if chiasm compression; hypopituitarism

B. Acute headache only

C. Sudden visual loss only

D. Acute hormonal crisis

E. Never asymptomatic

  1. Empty sella diagnosis:

A. MRI: CSF in sella, flattened pituitary; CSF signal on T1/T2

B. CT only

C. X-ray

D. Clinical only

E. Hormones only

  1. Empty sella management:

A. Screen for hypopituitarism; reassure if normal; treat hormone deficiencies

B. Surgery always

C. RT always

D. Dopamine agonists

E. No treatment

  1. Empty sella hypopituitarism:

A. GH deficiency most common; then gonadotrophins, TSH, ACTH

B. ACTH most common

C. TSH most common

D. Prolactin most common

E. ADH most common

  1. Primary empty sella demographics:

A. Obese women 30-50yr with hypertension

B. Men only

C. Children only

D. Elderly men

E. Thin women

  1. Empty sella vs pituitary adenoma:

A. MRI: CSF signal in sella (empty sella) vs enhancing mass (adenoma)

B. Same appearance

C. CT better

D. X-ray better

E. Clinical only

  1. Empty sella and pregnancy:

A. Physiological pituitary enlargement may fill sella; monitor for hypopituitarism

B. No effect

C. Contraindicated

D. Always causes DI

E. Always causes SIADH

17. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 40yo woman: incidental empty sella on MRI, normal hormones. Management?

A. Reassure; screen for hypopituitarism annually; no surgery

B. Surgery

C. RT

D. Dopamine agonist

E. No follow-up needed

  1. Same patient: 2yr later develops fatigue, amenorrhoea. Hormones: low LH/FSH, TSH, cortisol. Management?

A. Hormone replacement (levothyroxine, sex hormones, hydrocortisone); monitor

B. Surgery

C. RT

D. Dopamine agonist

E. Observation

  1. Post-pituitary surgery: empty sella on MRI, panhypopituitarism. Cause?

A. Secondary empty sella (post-surgical atrophy)

B. Primary empty sella

C. Pituitary apoplexy

D. Craniopharyngioma

E. Meningioma

  1. Empty sella with visual field defects. Management?

A. Ophthalmology referral; consider surgery if chiasm compression

B. Observation

C. Dopamine agonist

D. RT

E. No treatment

  1. Empty sella in pregnancy. Monitoring?

A. Screen for hypopituitarism; physiological pituitary enlargement may fill sella

B. Terminate pregnancy

C. Surgery

D. Dopamine agonist

E. No monitoring needed

18. Flashcards

  • Q: Empty sella

A: CSF herniation into sella; primary (idiopathic) or secondary (post-surgical/RT/haemorrhage)

  • Q: Primary empty sella

A: Obese hypertensive women 30-50yr; often asymptomatic

  • Q: Secondary empty sella

A: Post-pituitary surgery/RT/haemorrhage; pituitary atrophy

  • Q: Diagnosis

A: MRI: CSF signal in sella, flattened pituitary

  • Q: Hypopituitarism

A: GH deficiency most common; then gonadotrophins, TSH, ACTH

  • Q: Management

A: Screen for hypopituitarism; treat deficiencies; reassure if normal

  • Q: Primary demographics

A: Obese women 30-50yr with hypertension

  • Q: Secondary cause

A: Post-pituitary surgery/RT/haemorrhage

  • Q: Pregnancy

A: Physiological pituitary enlargement may fill sella; monitor for hypopituitarism

  • Q: Surgery

A: Only if chiasm compression with visual loss

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. CSF herniation into sella turcica (primary or secondary); may cause hypopituitarism — Empty sella syndrome =
  1. Idiopathic CSF herniation; often asymptomatic; common in obese women with hypertension — Primary empty sella:
  1. Post-pituitary surgery/RT/haemorrhage; pituitary atrophy — Secondary empty sella:
  1. Often asymptomatic; headache; visual field defects if chiasm compression; hypopituitarism — Empty sella presentation:
  1. MRI: CSF in sella, flattened pituitary; CSF signal on T1/T2 — Empty sella diagnosis:
  1. Screen for hypopituitarism; reassure if normal; treat hormone deficiencies — Empty sella management:
  1. GH deficiency most common; then gonadotrophins, TSH, ACTH — Empty sella hypopituitarism:
  1. Obese women 30-50yr with hypertension — Primary empty sella demographics:
  1. MRI: CSF signal in sella (empty sella) vs enhancing mass (adenoma) — Empty sella vs pituitary adenoma:
  1. Physiological pituitary enlargement may fill sella; monitor for hypopituitarism — Empty sella and pregnancy:

SBAs

  1. Reassure; screen for hypopituitarism annually; no surgery — 40yo woman: incidental empty sella on MRI, normal hormones. Management?
  1. Hormone replacement (levothyroxine, sex hormones, hydrocortisone); monitor — Same patient: 2yr later develops fatigue, amenorrhoea. Hormones: low LH/FSH, TSH, cortisol. Management?
  1. Secondary empty sella (post-surgical atrophy) — Post-pituitary surgery: empty sella on MRI, panhypopituitarism. Cause?
  1. Ophthalmology referral; consider surgery if chiasm compression — Empty sella with visual field defects. Management?
  1. Screen for hypopituitarism; physiological pituitary enlargement may fill sella — Empty sella in pregnancy. Monitoring?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.2.05 Fsh Lh Gonadal Axis
[!info]
HPG axis = GnRH → FSH/LH → Oestradiol/Testosterone. Central to puberty, reproduction, gonadal function, and sex steroid feedback. Dysregulation → Hypogonadism, PCOS, precocious/delayed puberty, infertility.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Describe the HPG axis anatomy, hormone synthesis, and regulation
  • [ ] Interpret FSH/LH patterns for primary vs secondary hypogonadism
  • [ ] Apply GnRH stimulation and sex steroid feedback tests
  • [ ] Diagnose and manage hypogonadism, PCOS, and puberty disorders
  • [ ] Interpret inhibin B, anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) in fertility assessment

2. HPG Axis — Anatomy & Physiology

Hormone Synthesis & Secretion

StepHormoneSourceRegulation
1. GnRHGonadotropin-Releasing HormoneHypothalamus (Preoptic Area)Pulsatile; Kisspeptin (key upstream); Sex steroids (negative/positive feedback)
2. FSHFollicle-Stimulating HormoneAnterior Pituitary (Gonadotrophs)GnRH (stimulatory); Inhibin B (selective negative feedback); Oestradiol/Testosterone (general)
3. LHLuteinizing HormoneAnterior Pituitary (Gonadotrophs)GnRH (stimulatory); Oestradiol/Testosterone (negative/positive feedback)
4. Sex SteroidsOestradiol / TestosteroneGonads (Ovary: Theca+Granulosa; Testis: Leydig)LH/FSH (stimulatory); Negative feedback on GnRH/LH/FSH; Inhibin B (FSH specific)

Hormone Structure

HormoneStructureShared Subunit
FSHα-subunit (shared) + β-subunit (FSH-specific)α-subunit (shared with LH, TSH, hCG)
LHα-subunit (shared) + β-subunit (LH-specific)α-subunit (shared with FSH, TSH, hCG)
hCGα-subunit + β-subunitSimilar to LH (cross-reacts with LH receptor)

3. HPG Axis Regulation

Feedback Loops

GnRH (Hypothalamus) → FSH + LH (Pituitary) → Oestradiol/Testosterone (Gonads)
    ↑_______________|________________|
          Negative Feedback (Oestradiol/Testosterone + Inhibin)
          Positive Feedback (Oestradiol → LH Surge)

Feedback Patterns

Gonadal StatusLH/FSHOestradiol/TestosteroneInhibin BKey Features
Primary Hypogonadism↑↑↓↓Gonadal failure
Secondary Hypogonadism↓/N↓N/↓Pituitary/Hypothalamic failure
PCOSLH ↑↑, FSH N/↓ (LH:FSH >2:1)Testosterone ↑N/↓Ovarian resistance + Insulin resistance
Menopause↑↑↓↓ (Oestradiol)↓Ovarian failure
PregnancySuppressedOestradiol/Progesterone ↑↑↓High oestrogen/progesterone negative feedback
Ageing MaleSlight ↑Gradual ↓Slight ↓Late-onset hypogonadism

4. Gonadotropin Physiology

GnRH

FeatureDetails
SourceHypothalamus (Medial preoptic area)
SecretionPulsatile (every 60-90 min); Frequency encodes signal
RegulationKisspeptin (Key upstream stimulator); Sex steroids (Negative/Positive feedback); Stress, Energy status, Leptin (modulatory)
Pulse FrequencyLow (1-2/h) → FSH dominance; High (1/30-60min) → LH dominance

FSH

FeatureDetails
Structureα-subunit (shared) + β-subunit (FSH-specific)
Primary RegulatorGnRH (frequency-dependent); Inhibin B (selective negative feedback)
Actions — FemaleFolliculogenesis (Granulosa cells); Oestradiol synthesis (with LH); Inhibin B production
Actions — MaleSpermatogenesis (Sertoli cells); Inhibin B production
RegulationSelective negative feedback by Inhibin B (Granulosa/Sertoli cells)

LH

FeatureDetails
Structureα-subunit (shared) + β-subunit (LH-specific)
Primary RegulatorGnRH (frequency-dependent); Oestradiol/Testosterone (negative/positive feedback)
Actions — FemaleTheca cells → Androgen synthesis → Granulosa → Oestradiol; Ovulation (LH surge); Corpus luteum → Progesterone
Actions — MaleLeydig cells → Testosterone production
Positive FeedbackSustained Oestradiol >200 pg/mL × 50h → LH Surge (Ovulation)

5. Gonadal Steroid Physiology

Testosterone

FeatureMaleFemale
SourceLeydig cells (95%), Adrenal (5%)Ovary (Theca), Adrenal
BindingSHBG ~60%, Albumin ~38%, Free ~2%SHBG ↑ in women
Metabolism5α-Reductase → DHT (potent); Aromatase → OestradiolAromatisation
RegulationLH → Leydig cells → Testosterone; Negative feedback on LH/FSHPart of ovarian steroidogenesis

Oestradiol

FeatureFemaleMale
SourceOvary (Granulosa: Aromatisation of Androstenedione)Testis (Sertoli), Adipose (Aromatase)
BindingSHBG ~60%, Albumin ~38%, Free ~2%SHBG ~60%
RegulationFSH + LH → Theca Androgens → Granulosa Aromatase → OestradiolAromatase in Adipose, Brain, Bone

6. HPG Axis Patterns — Diagnostic Key

Hypogonadism Classification

TypeLH/FSHTestosterone/OestradiolInhibin B/AMHCommon Causes
Primary (Hypergonadotropic)↑↑↓↓Klinefelter, Turner, Mumps, Chemo, Autoimmune, Genetic
Secondary (Hypogonadotropic)↓/N↓N/↓Pituitary adenoma, Hyperprolactinaemia, Kallmann, Functional
Functional (Stress/Weight/Exercise)↓/N↓/N↓Anorexia, Athletics, Stress, Obesity

Male Hypogonadism — Key Patterns

TypeTestosteroneLH/FSHClinical Features
Primary<12 nmol/L↑↑Klinefelter, Mumps, Chemo, Autoimmune, Ageing
Secondary<12 nmol/L↓/NPituitary adenoma, Hyperprolactinaemia, Kallmann, Obesity
Late-Onset (LOH)8-12 nmol/LN/↑Ageing, Obesity, Comorbidities

Female Hypogonadism — Key Patterns

TypeOestradiolLH/FSHClinical Features
POI (<40y)↓↑↑Turner, FMR1, Autoimmune, Chemo, Idiopathic
Secondary Amenorrhoea↓↓/NPCOS, Hyperprolactinaemia, Functional, Thyroid
Menopause↓↓↑↑Natural ovarian failure

7. PCOS — Rotterdam Criteria (2 of 3)

CriterionDefinition
1. Oligo/anovulation<8 cycles/year or Cycle >35 days
2. HyperandrogenismClinical (Hirsutism, Acne, Alopecia) OR Biochemical (Testosterone >2.5-3 nmol/L, FAI >5)
3. Polycystic Ovaries (US)≥20 follicles (2-9mm) per ovary OR Ovarian volume >10 mL

Must Exclude: CAH (17-OHP), Androgen-secreting tumours, Cushing, Thyroid disease, Hyperprolactinaemia.

8. PCOS — Management

TargetIntervention
Menstrual RegulationCOCP (Oestradiol + Cyproterone/Drospirenone) OR Cyclical Progesterone (Medroxyprogesterone 10mg 10d/month)
Hirsutism/AcneCOCP → Add Anti-androgen (Spironolactone 50-100mg, Cyproterone, Flutamide) + Topical Eflornithine
Metabolic/IRMetformin 500mg BD → 1g BD (if BMI ≥25 or IGT)
Weight Loss5-10% wt loss → Restores ovulation in 50%
Ovulation InductionLetrozole 2.5-5mg Days 2-6 (1st line) → Clomifene → Gonadotrophins → IVF
Long-termScreen T2DM (OGTT q1-3y), Lipids, BP, Endometrial cancer risk

9. Puberty & Growth

Normal Puberty Onset

SexThelarche/Genital GrowthPeak Height VelocityMenarche/Spermarche
Female8-13 years (Avg 10-11)11-12 years12-13 years (Avg 12.5)
Male9-14 years (Avg 11-12)13-14 years13-14 years (Spermarche)

Delayed Puberty

DefinitionFemale: No thelarche by 13; Male: No testicular enlargement by 14
Constitutional DelayMost common; Family history; Normal growth velocity; Bone age delayed
Hypogonadotropic HypogonadismKallmann syndrome (Anosmia + HH); Isolated HH; Chronic disease
Hypergonadotropic HypogonadismTurner (45,X), Klinefelter (47,XXY), Autoimmune, Chemotherapy

Precocious Puberty

DefinitionFemale: <8 years; Male: <9 years
Central (GnRH-dependent)True puberty early; CPP; Idiopathic, CNS tumours, Hamartoma
Peripheral (GnRH-independent)McCune-Albright (GNAS), Adrenal androgen excess, Ovarian/Testicular tumours

10. Infertility — Endocrine Workup

PartnerTests
FemaleTSH, Prolactin, FSH/LH (Day 2-4), Oestradiol (Day 2-4), AMH, Progesterone (Day 21), US Pelvis (AFC, Fibroids), HSG
MaleSemen Analysis (WHO 2021), Testosterone, LH, FSH, Prolactin, US Scrotum, Genetic (if severe)
TimingInvestigate after 12 months (or 6 months if female >35)

11. Contraception — Endocrine Methods

MethodEfficacy (Typical)Key Contraindications
COCP91%VTE, Breast cancer, Migraine with aura, Smoker >35, BP >160/100
POP (Desogestrel)91%Active breast cancer, Unexplained bleeding
Injectable (DMPA)94%Osteoporosis risk (long-term), Weight gain, Delayed fertility return
Implant (ENG)99.9%Active VTE, Breast cancer
IUS (LNG 52mg)99.8%Uterine anomaly, Active pelvic infection
Copper IUD99.2%Heavy periods, Copper allergy, Wilson's disease

12. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
HPG AxisGnRH → FSH/LH → Oestradiol/Testosterone; Negative feedback + Positive feedback (LH surge)
GnRH PulsatilityLow freq (1-2/h) → FSH dominant; High freq (1/30-60min) → LH dominant
Primary HypogonadismLH/FSH ↑↑, Testosterone/Oestradiol ↓ — Gonadal failure
Secondary HypogonadismLH/FSH ↓/N, Testosterone/Oestradiol ↓ — Pituitary/Hypothalamic failure
PCOS2/3 Rotterdam: Oligo-ovulation + Hyperandrogenism + PCO morphology
PCOS 1st Line OvulationLetrozole 2.5-5mg Days 2-6 (Superior to Clomifene)
Male HypogonadismTestosterone <12 nmol/L; LH/FSH ↑ = Primary; LH/FSH ↓ = Secondary
Kallmann SyndromeHypogonadotropic hypogonadism + Anosmia (KAL1/ANOS1 gene)
POIFSH >40 IU/L on 2 occasions 4-6wk apart; Age <40; HRT until 50
Menopause HRTOestrogen only (Hysterectomy); Combined (Intact uterus); Lowest effective dose
InfertilityInvestigate after 12mo (6mo if female >35); AMH = Ovarian reserve marker
AMHGranulosa cell product; Reflects antral follicle count; ↓ with age

13. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
FSH vs LH in PCOSLH > FSH (LH:FSH >2:1); FSH relatively lower
Primary vs Secondary HypogonadismPrimary = Gonad failure → LH/FSH ↑; Secondary = Pituitary failure → LH/FSH ↓
GnRH Pulse FrequencyLow freq (1-2/h) → FSH dominant; High freq (1/30-60min) → LH dominant
AMH vs FSHAMH = Ovarian reserve (stable through cycle); FSH = Cycle-dependent (Day 2-4)
Letrozole vs ClomifeneLetrozole superior for ovulation in PCOS (higher live birth, lower multiples)
Kallmann SyndromeHypogonadotropic hypogonadism + Anosmia (KAL1/ANOS1 gene)
FAIFree Androgen Index = (Testosterone/SHBG) × 100; >5 = Hyperandrogenism

14. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((FSH/LH/Gonadal Axis))
    HPG Axis
      GnRH (Pulsatile) → FSH + LH → Sex Steroids
      Feedback: Negative (Steroids + Inhibin) + Positive (Oestradiol→LH Surge)
    Hormones
      GnRH: Pulsatile, Kisspeptin regulator
      FSH: Folliculogenesis, Spermatogenesis, Inhibin B
      LH: Ovulation, Leydig cells, Theca cells
      Inhibin B: Selective FSH feedback
      AMH: Ovarian reserve (Granulosa cells)
    Patterns
      Primary Hypo: LH/FSH ↑↑, Steroid ↓
      Secondary Hypo: LH/FSH ↓/N, Steroid ↓
      PCOS: LH ↑↑, LH:FSH >2:1, Androgens ↑
    PCOS
      Rotterdam: 2/3 (Oligo + Hyperandrogen + PCO)
      1st Line Ovulation: Letrozole
      Metabolic: Metformin if BMI≥25/IGT
    Puberty
      Delayed: Constitutional vs Hypogonadotropic
      Precocious: Central (CPP) vs Peripheral (PPP)
    Infertility
      Female: AMH, AFC, TSH, PRL, FSH/LH, HSG
      Male: Semen Analysis, Testosterone, LH/FSH
    Contraception
      COCP, POP, Implant, IUS, DMPA, Cu-IUD

15. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
HPG AxisGnRH → FSH/LH → Oestradiol/Testosterone; Negative feedback + Positive feedback (LH surge)
GnRH PulsatilityLow freq (1-2/h) → FSH dominant; High freq (1/30-60min) → LH dominant
Primary HypogonadismLH/FSH ↑↑, Testosterone/Oestradiol ↓ — Gonadal failure
Secondary HypogonadismLH/FSH ↓/N, Testosterone/Oestradiol ↓ — Pituitary/Hypothalamic failure
PCOS2/3 Rotterdam: Oligo-ovulation + Hyperandrogenism + PCO morphology
PCOS 1st Line OvulationLetrozole 2.5-5mg Days 2-6 (Superior to Clomifene)
Male HypogonadismTestosterone <12 nmol/L; LH/FSH ↑ = Primary; LH/FSH ↓/N = Secondary
Kallmann SyndromeHypogonadotropic hypogonadism + Anosmia (KAL1/ANOS1 gene)
POIFSH >40 IU/L on 2 occasions 4-6wk apart; Age <40; HRT until 50
Menopause HRTOestrogen only (Hysterectomy); Combined (Intact uterus); Lowest effective dose
InfertilityInvestigate after 12mo (6mo if female >35); AMH = Ovarian reserve marker
AMHGranulosa cell product; Reflects antral follicle count; ↓ with age

16. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
FSH vs LH in PCOSLH > FSH (LH:FSH >2:1); FSH relatively lower
Primary vs Secondary HypogonadismPrimary = Gonad failure → LH/FSH ↑; Secondary = Pituitary failure → LH/FSH ↓
GnRH Pulse FrequencyLow freq (1-2/h) → FSH dominant; High freq (1/30-60min) → LH dominant
AMH vs FSHAMH = Ovarian reserve (stable through cycle); FSH = Cycle-dependent (Day 2-4)
Letrozole vs ClomifeneLetrozole superior for ovulation in PCOS (higher live birth, lower multiples)
Kallmann SyndromeHypogonadotropic hypogonadism + Anosmia (KAL1/ANOS1 gene)
FAIFree Androgen Index = (Testosterone/SHBG) × 100; >5 = Hyperandrogenism

17. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((FSH/LH/Gonadal Axis))
    HPG Axis
      GnRH (Pulsatile) → FSH + LH → Sex Steroids
      Feedback: Negative (Steroids + Inhibin) + Positive (Oestradiol→LH Surge)
    Hormones
      GnRH: Pulsatile, Kisspeptin regulator
      FSH: Folliculogenesis, Spermatogenesis, Inhibin B
      LH: Ovulation, Leydig cells, Theca cells
      Inhibin B: Selective FSH feedback
      AMH: Ovarian reserve (Granulosa cells)

      Secondary Hypo: LH/FSH ↓/N, Steroid ↓
      PCOS: LH ↑↑, LH:FSH >2:1, Androgens ↑
    PCOS
      Rotterdam: 2/3 (Oligo + Hyperandrogen + PCO)
      1st Line Ovulation: Letrozole
      Metabolic: Metformin if BMI≥25/IGT
