Cluster HeadacheUnilateral Peri-orbital Pain; Autonomic Features; No Visual Field Defect
Metastatic Pituitary DepositOlder Age, Known Primary, Lytic Bone Lesions, Diplopia

6. Diagnostic Workup

Immediate Investigations (Emergency)

InvestigationPurpose
MRI Pituitary (Urgent)Gold Standard; Haemorrhage (T1/T2*), Infarction, Mass Effect, Chiasmal Compression
CT Head (If MRI Unavailable)Hyperdense Sella (Acute Blood); Bony Sella Enlargement
Visual Fields (Goldmann/Humphrey)Bitemporal Hemianopia (Chiasmal Compression)
Hormonal PanelCortisol, ACTH, TSH, fT4, Prolactin, LH/FSH, Testosterone/Oestradiol, GH/IGF-1
Electrolytes / GlucoseNa+, K+, Urea, Creatinine, Glucose (Adrenal Crisis Check)
Coagulation ScreenINR, APTT, Fibrinogen, D-dimer (Anticoagulation Status)

MRI Findings in Apoplexy

SequenceFinding
T1Hyperintense (Methaemoglobin) in Acute Phase; Heterogeneous
T2Heterogeneous (Haemorrhage + Infarction + Oedema)
T2* / SWIBlooming (Susceptibility) = Haemorrhage
Post-GadoliniumPeripheral Enhancement (Viable Tumour Rim); Central Non-enhancing (Infarct)
Mass EffectChiasmal Compression, Cavernous Sinus Invasion, Third Ventricle Obstruction

7. Emergency Management Protocol

SUSPECTED PITUITARY APOPLEXY
         │
         ▼
1. ABCDE + IMMEDIATE STABILISATION
         │
         ├── AIRWAY / BREATHING: High-Flow O₂, Intubate if GCS <8
         ├── CIRCULATION: **HYDROCORTISONE 100mg IV STAT** + 1L 0.9% NaCl OVER 1 HOUR
         │       (DO NOT DELAY FOR TESTS — LIFE-SAVING)
         ├── DISABILITY: GCS, Pupillary Response, Visual Acuity
         └── EXPOSURE: Temperature, Full Examination
         │
         ▼
2. URGENT IMAGING (Within 1 Hour)
         │
         ├── MRI PITUITARY (Gold Standard) — Haemorrhage, Infarction, Mass Effect
         └── IF MRI UNAVAILABLE → CT HEAD (Hyperdense Sella)
         │
         ▼
3. HORMONAL RESUSCITATION
         │
         ├── HYDROCORTISONE 100mg IV STAT → 50-100mg IV Q6H (Taper Over Days)
         ├── FLUids: 2-3L 0.9% NaCl / 24H (Monitor U&Es, Glucose, JVP, UOP)
         ├── CORRECT HYPONATRAEMIA (3% Saline if Severe/Neuro Symptoms)
         ├── CORRECT HYPOGLYCAEMIA (10% Dextrose)
         ▼
         ▼
4. URGENT NEUROSURGICAL REFERRAL
         │
         ├── INDICATIONS FOR SURGERY (Within 24-48h):
         │       • Deteriorating Visual Fields / Acuity
         │       • Deteriorating Consciousness / Ophthalmoplegia
         │       • Persistent Hypotension Despite Steroids/Fluids
         │       • Hydrocephalus (Third Ventricle Obstruction)
         ├── CONSERVATIVE MANAGEMENT IF:
         │       • Stable/Improving Neurological Status
         │       • No Visual Deterioration
         │       • No Hydrocephalus
         │       • Small Haemorrhage / Minimal Mass Effect
         │
         ▼
5. DEFINITIVE MANAGEMENT
         │
         ├── URGENT TSS (Within 24-48h) — If Surgical Indications Met
         ├── CONSERVATIVE (High-dose Dexamethasone + Fluids + Monitoring) — If Stable
         │
         ▼
6. POST-ACUTE MANAGEMENT
         │
         ├── HIGH-DOSE DEXAMETHASONE (4-8mg IV q6h) — Reduces Oedema/Inflammation
         ├── HYDROCORTISONE TAPER (50-100mg q6h → 25mg q8h → Oral Taper)
         ├── VISUAL FIELDS (Daily Inpatient; Then Outpatient)
         ├── HORMONAL ASSESSMENT (Day 1, Week 1, Month 1, 3mo, 6mo, 1yr)
         └── LONG-TERM SURVEILLANCE (MRI 3mo, 6mo, 1yr, then Yearly)

8. Medical Management — Conservative Approach

Indications for Conservative Management

  • Stable/Improving Neurological Status
  • No Visual Deterioration (Stable Visual Fields)
  • Small Haemorrhage / Minimal Mass Effect
  • No Hydrocephalus
  • Patient Unfit for Surgery

Conservative Protocol

InterventionDose / Details
Dexamethasone4-8mg IV q6h (High-dose; Reduces Oedema/Inflammation)
Hydrocortisone100mg IV STAT → 50-100mg q6h → Taper to Oral over 5-7 Days
Fluids2-3L 0.9% NaCl / 24h (Target UOP >0.5mL/kg/h)
ElectrolytesMonitor Na+, K+, Glucose q1-2h Initially
Head Elevation30° (Reduce Intracranial Pressure)
MonitoringGCS q1h, Visual Fields q4-6h, Pupils q1h, Vitals q15min

9. Surgical Indications & Outcomes

Surgical Indications (Within 24-48 Hours)

IndicationUrgency
Deteriorating Visual Fields / AcuityUrgent (Within 24h)
Deteriorating Consciousness / New CN PalsiesUrgent (Within 24h)
Hydrocephalus (Third Ventricle Obstruction)Emergent
Persistent Hypotension Despite Full Medical RxUrgent
Progressive OphthalmoplegiaUrgent

Surgical Approach

ApproachDetails
Transsphenoidal (Endoscopic)Preferred; Minimally Invasive; Direct Decompression
CraniotomyRare; Giant Adenomas / Extensive Suprasellar / Failed TSS

Surgical Outcomes

OutcomeRate
Visual Improvement70-85% (If Operated <72h); <50% if >2 Weeks
Ophthalmoplegia Recovery50-70% (CN III Best; CN IV/VI Worse)
New Hypopituitarism20-30% (New Deficits Post-Surgery)
Recurrence10-15% at 5 Years (Residual Tumour)
Mortality<1% (Elective TSS); 5-10% (Emergency Apoplexy Surgery)

10. Post-Apoplexy Management

Hormonal Assessment Schedule

TimepointTests
Day 1Cortisol, ACTH, TSH, fT4, Na+, K+, Glucose, Prolactin
Day 3-7Full Hormone Panel (Cortisol, ACTH, TSH, fT4, PRL, LH/FSH, GH/IGF-1)
Week 1-2Full Panel + Visual Fields
Month 1, 3, 6, 12Full Hormone Panel + MRI (1mo, 3mo, 6mo, 1yr)
AnnualHormones + MRI (Yearly ×3, then 2-3yrly)

Hormone Replacement Post-Apoplexy

DeficiencyReplacement
ACTH/CortisolHydrocortisone 15-20mg AM + 5-10mg PM (Immediate IV if Crisis)
TSH/fT4Levothyroxine (Initiate AFTER Glucocorticoid)
GH/IGF-1RH-GH if Confirmed GHD (ITT Peak <3 µg/L)
GonadotrophsTestosterone / HRT (If Hypogonadal)
ProlactinNot Replaced (Except Lactation)

11. Complications & Prognosis

ComplicationFrequencyManagement
Visual Loss (Permanent)10-15% (If Surgery <72h)Early Surgery Critical
New Hypopituitarism20-30% (Post-Surgery)Hormone Replacement
Diabetes Insipidus10-20% (Transient); 1-2% PermanentDesmopressin
CSF Leak2-5%Conservative / Lumbar Drain / Surgical Repair
Meningitis<1%IV Antibiotics
Carotid Artery Injury<1%Emergency Vascular Repair
Recurrence10-15% at 5 YearsSurveillance MRI

12. Differential Diagnosis Table

ConditionHeadacheVisual LossCN PalsiesSigns of MeningismImaging
Pituitary ApoplexyThunderclapBitemporal HemianopiaCN III/IV/VIMild (Chemical)MRI: Haemorrhage in Sella
SAHThunderclapRareCN III (Posterior Communicating)SevereCT: Hyperdense Basal Cisterns
MeningitisSubacuteRareCN Palsies (Late)SevereCSF: ↑WBC, ↓Glucose, ↑Protein
Cavernous Sinus ThrombosisSubacuteProptosis + Visual LossMultiple CN (III, IV, VI, V1/V2)CommonMRI/MRV: Thrombosed Cavernous Sinus
Cluster HeadacheSevere UnilateralNonePtosis/Miosis (Horner)NoneNormal Imaging

13. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Classic TriadSudden Headache + Visual Loss + Ophthalmoplegia (Present in ~50%)
Most Common SymptomSudden Severe Headache (>90%)
Most Common Visual DefectBi-temporal Hemianopia (Chiasmal Compression)
Most Common CN PalsyCN III (Ptosis, Mydriasis, "Down & Out")
Emergency TreatmentHydrocortisone 100mg IV STAT + 1L 0.9% NaCl (DO NOT DELAY)
Dexamethasone in Apoplexy4-8mg IV q6h (Reduces Oedema; Not Replacement for Hydrocortisone)
Surgery TimingVisual Deterioration → Urgent (Within 24-48h)
Visual Recovery70-85% if Surgery <72h; <50% if >2 Weeks
New Hypopituitarism Post-Surgery20-30% (New Deficits)
Apoplexy vs SAHSAH = Thunderclap + Severe Meningism + CT Basal Cistern Hyperdensity
Apoplexy vs MeningitisMeningitis = Fever + Meningism + CSF Pleocytosis
Post-Apoplexy Hypopituitarism20-30% New Deficits; GH → Gonad → TSH → ACTH
Dexamethasone vs HydrocortisoneDex = Anti-oedema (High Dose); Hydrocortisone = Replacement (Physiological)

14. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Apoplexy vs SAHApoplexy = Haemorrhage Within Sella (Pituitary); SAH = Haemorrhage Basal Cisterns
Apoplexy vs MeningitisMeningitis = Fever + Meningism + CSF Pleocytosis; Apoplexy = Thunderclap + Visual Loss + CN Palsies
Dexamethasone vs HydrocortisoneDex = Anti-oedema (High Dose); Hydrocortisone = Glucocorticoid Replacement (Physiological)
Surgery TimingVisual Deterioration = Urgent (24h); Stable = Conservative + Serial Monitoring
Visual RecoveryTime is Vision — Surgery <72h = 70-85% Recovery; >2 Weeks = <50%
Apoplexy in MicroadenomaRare (<10%); Usually Macroadenoma (>10mm)

Mnemonic: APOPLEXY MANAGEMENT

  • Adrenal Crisis → Hydrocortisone 100mg IV STAT
  • Pituitary MRI → Urgent (Haemorrhage + Mass Effect)
  • Ophthalmoplegia → CN III/IV/VI (Visual Fields Daily)
  • Pressure (ICP) → Dexamethasone 4-8mg IV q6h (Anti-oedema)
  • Loss of Vision → Urgent TSS (Within 24h)
  • Electrolytes → Na+, K+, Glucose, U&Es q1-2h
  • X-ray (CT) if MRI Unavailable → Hyperdense Sella
  • Yield to Neurosurgery → TSS Within 24-48h if Surgical Indications

15. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Pituitary Apoplexy))
    Definition
      Acute Haemorrhage/Infarction in Pituitary Adenoma
      Macroadenoma >10mm >90%
    Presentation
      Sudden Headache (90%)
      Visual Loss (Bi-temporal Hemianopia) 70-80%
      Ophthalmoplegia (CN III/IV/VI) 50-70%
      Adrenal Crisis (Hypotension, Hyponatraemia, Hypoglycaemia)
    Differential
      SAH (CT Hyperdense Cisterns)
      Meningitis (Fever, CSF Pleocytosis)
      Cavernous Sinus Thrombosis (Proptosis, Multi-CN)
      Metastatic Deposit
    Emergency Management
      Hydrocortisone 100mg IV STAT + 1L Saline
      Dexamethasone 4-8mg q6h (Anti-oedema)
      Urgent MRI Pituitary
      Fluids + Electrolytes + Glucose Monitoring
    Surgery Indications
      Visual Deterioration → Urgent TSS (<24h)
      Deteriorating Consciousness
      Hydrocephalus
      Refractory Hypotension
    Surgical Outcomes
      Visual Recovery: 70-85% (<72h); <50% (>2wks)
      New Hypopituitarism 20-30%
      DI: 10-20% Transient
    Post-Op Surveillance
      MRI 1, 3, 6, 12mo then Yearly
      Hormones: 1mo, 3mo, 6mo, 1yr then Annually
      Visual Fields: Daily Inpt → Outpt q3-6mo

16. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Classic TriadHeadache + Visual Loss + Ophthalmoplegia (~50% have all 3)
Most Common SymptomSudden Severe Headache (>90%)
Apoplexy vs SAHApoplexy = Intrasellar Haemorrhage; SAH = Subarachnoid (Basal Cisterns)
Emergency SteroidHydrocortisone 100mg IV STAT (Glucocorticoid Replacement)
Dexamethasone RoleAnti-oedema (4-8mg IV q6h); NOT Replacement
Surgery IndicationVisual Deterioration / CN Palsy / Hydrocephalus / Refractory Hypotension
Surgery TimingUrgent TSS Within 24-48h (Visual Recovery Best <72h)
Visual Recovery70-85% if Surgery <72h; <50% if >2 Weeks
New Hypopituitarism20-30% Post-Surgery; GH → Gonad → TSH → ACTH
Dexamethasone vs HydrocortisoneDex = Anti-oedema; Hydrocortisone = Replacement
Diabetes Insipidus Post-Op10-20% Transient; 1-2% Permanent
Apoplexy TriggersHTN, Anticoagulation, Pregnancy, Dynamic Testing, Trauma

17. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Hypothalamic-Pituitary Axis|Hypothalamic-Pituitary Axis]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Pituitary Adenomas: Functioning]], [[Pituitary Adenomas: Non-Functioning]], [[Hypopituitarism]], [[Adrenal Crisis]], [[Endocrine Emergencies]]

18. MCQs (10)

  1. Pituitary apoplexy =

A. Acute haemorrhage/infarction of pituitary adenoma

B. Pituitary tumour rupture

C. Pituitaryitis

D. Empty sella haemorrhage

E. Craniopharyngioma haemorrhage

  1. Pituitary apoplexy presentation:

A. Sudden severe headache, visual loss, ophthalmoplegia, hypotension, decreased consciousness

B. Gradual headache

C. Slow visual loss

D. Only hypotension

E. Only nausea

  1. Pituitary apoplexy immediate management:

A. High-dose dexamethasone 4-8mg IV q6h + fluids + urgent MRI -> surgery if deteriorating

B. Surgery immediately

C. Observation only

D. Dopamine agonists

E. Radiotherapy

  1. Pituitary apoplexy visual loss:

A. Urgent transsphenoidal decompression if deteriorating; high-dose steroids

B. Observation

C. Radiotherapy

D. Dopamine agonists

E. Orbital decompression

  1. Pituitary apoplexy commonest hormonal deficit:

A. ACTH deficiency (adrenal insufficiency)

B. TSH deficiency

C. GH deficiency

D. Gonadotrophin deficiency

E. ADH deficiency

  1. Pituitary apoplexy risk factors:

A. Large macroadenoma, hypertension, anticoagulation, pregnancy, dynamic testing

B. Small microadenoma

C. No risk factors

D. Only trauma

E. Only surgery

  1. Pituitary apoplexy diagnosis:

A. MRI pituitary (T1 hyperintensity = blood); visual fields; hormonal panel

B. CT only

C. X-ray

D. Clinical only

E. Blood tests only

  1. Pituitary apoplexy post-decompression:

A. High-dose steroids; monitor vision, electrolytes, cortisol; hormone replacement

B. Stop steroids

C. Dopamine agonists

D. Radiotherapy

E. Observation

  1. Pituitary apoplexy in pregnancy:

A. High risk; Sheehan syndrome differential; urgent MRI if visual loss

B. No risk

C. Only postpartum

D. No management needed

E. Termination required

  1. Pituitary apoplexy vs meningioma:

A. Apoplexy: sudden onset, pituitary adenoma; Meningioma: gradual, dural tail

B. Same

C. Apoplexy: gradual

D. Meningioma: sudden

E. No difference

19. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 50yo woman: known pituitary macroadenoma, sudden severe headache, bitemporal hemianopia, hypotension (90/50). Immediate?

A. High-dose dexamethasone 8mg IV + fluids + urgent MRI -> surgery if deteriorating

B. Surgery immediately

C. Observation

D. Dopamine agonist

E. Radiotherapy

  1. Same patient: day 1 post-decompression, cortisol 80 nmol/L. Action?

A. Continue hydrocortisone 100mg IV q8h; taper over days

B. Stop steroids

C. Increase dexamethasone

D. Fludrocortisone

E. TSH

  1. Pituitary apoplexy with improving vision post-steroids. Next?

A. Continue steroids; monitor visual fields; elective decompression if stable

B. Urgent surgery

C. Stop steroids

D. Radiotherapy

E. Dopamine agonist

  1. Pituitary apoplexy: panhypopituitarism day 1 post-surgery. Hormone replacement?

A. Hydrocortisone 15-20mg AM + 5-10mg PM + levothyroxine + sex hormones

B. Hydrocortisone only

C. Levothyroxine only

D. Sex hormones only

E. No replacement needed

  1. Pituitary apoplexy: which hormone to replace FIRST?

A. Cortisol (hydrocortisone) - adrenal crisis risk

B. Levothyroxine

C. Sex hormones

D. GH

E. ADH

20. Flashcards

  • Q: Pituitary apoplexy

A: Acute haemorrhage/infarction of pituitary adenoma; sudden headache, visual loss, ophthalmoplegia, hypotension

  • Q: Immediate Rx

A: High-dose dexamethasone 4-8mg IV q6h + fluids + urgent MRI -> transsphenoidal surgery if deteriorating

  • Q: Visual loss

A: Urgent decompression if deteriorating; high-dose steroids

  • Q: Commonest deficit

A: ACTH deficiency (adrenal insufficiency) - replace FIRST

  • Q: Risk factors

A: Large macroadenoma, hypertension, anticoagulation, pregnancy, dynamic testing

  • Q: Diagnosis

A: MRI pituitary (T1 hyperintensity = blood); visual fields; hormonal panel

  • Q: Post-op

A: High-dose steroids; monitor vision, electrolytes, cortisol; panhypopituitarism replacement

  • Q: Pregnancy

A: High risk; differential with Sheehan syndrome; urgent MRI if visual loss

  • Q: Causes

A: Haemorrhage > infarction; macroadenoma prone

  • Q: Key principle

A: CORTISOL FIRST (adrenal crisis risk); then thyroid, gonadal, GH, ADH

21. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Acute haemorrhage/infarction of pituitary adenoma — Pituitary apoplexy =
  1. Sudden severe headache, visual loss, ophthalmoplegia, hypotension, decreased consciousness — Pituitary apoplexy presentation:
  1. High-dose dexamethasone 4-8mg IV q6h + fluids + urgent MRI -> surgery if deteriorating — Pituitary apoplexy immediate management:
  1. Urgent transsphenoidal decompression if deteriorating; high-dose steroids — Pituitary apoplexy visual loss:
  1. ACTH deficiency (adrenal insufficiency) — Pituitary apoplexy commonest hormonal deficit:
  1. Large macroadenoma, hypertension, anticoagulation, pregnancy, dynamic testing — Pituitary apoplexy risk factors:
  1. MRI pituitary (T1 hyperintensity = blood); visual fields; hormonal panel — Pituitary apoplexy diagnosis:
  1. High-dose steroids; monitor vision, electrolytes, cortisol; hormone replacement — Pituitary apoplexy post-decompression:
  1. High risk; Sheehan syndrome differential; urgent MRI if visual loss — Pituitary apoplexy in pregnancy:
  1. Apoplexy: sudden onset, pituitary adenoma; Meningioma: gradual, dural tail — Pituitary apoplexy vs meningioma:

SBAs

  1. High-dose dexamethasone 8mg IV + fluids + urgent MRI -> surgery if deteriorating — 50yo woman: known pituitary macroadenoma, sudden severe headache, bitemporal hemianopia, hypotension (90/50). Immediate?
  1. Continue hydrocortisone 100mg IV q8h; taper over days — Same patient: day 1 post-decompression, cortisol 80 nmol/L. Action?
  1. Continue steroids; monitor visual fields; elective decompression if stable — Pituitary apoplexy with improving vision post-steroids. Next?
  1. Hydrocortisone 15-20mg AM + 5-10mg PM + levothyroxine + sex hormones — Pituitary apoplexy: panhypopituitarism day 1 post-surgery. Hormone replacement?
  1. Cortisol (hydrocortisone) - adrenal crisis risk — Pituitary apoplexy: which hormone to replace FIRST?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.2.11 Posterior Pituitary Adh Vasopressin
[!info]
ADH (Vasopressin, Arginine Vasopressin) = Posterior pituitary hormone. Synthesised in hypothalamus, released from posterior pituitary. Central to water homeostasis and blood pressure regulation. Key pathologies: Diabetes Insipidus (Central/Nephrogenic) and SIADH.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Describe ADH synthesis, secretion, and receptor physiology
  • [ ] Distinguish Central DI, Nephrogenic DI, and Primary Polydipsia
  • [ ] Interpret Water Deprivation Test + Desmopressin Challenge
  • [ ] Diagnose and manage SIADH
  • [ ] Apply desmopressin and vaptan therapy

2. ADH (Vasopressin) Physiology

Synthesis & Secretion

FeatureDetails
SynthesisHypothalamic nuclei: Supraoptic (main) + Paraventricular
TransportAxoplasmic transport → Posterior pituitary (neurohypophysis)
Structure9 amino acid peptide; Arginine Vasopressin (AVP) in humans
Half-life10-20 minutes (rapid)
Main RegulatorPlasma Osmolality (Osmoreceptors in OVLT → Supraoptic/PVN)
Secondary RegulatorsVolume/BP (Baroreceptors: Low pressure → ↑ ADH); Nausea, Pain, Stress, Hypoglycaemia, Angiotensin II

ADH Receptors & Actions

ReceptorLocationPrimary ActionClinical Use
V2Renal Collecting Duct (Principal cells)Water Reabsorption (↑ Aquaporin-2 insertion)Desmopressin (DDAVP) = V2 agonist
V1aVascular Smooth MuscleVasoconstriction (Pressor effect)Vasopressin (V1a/V2) for septic shock
V1bAnterior Pituitary (Corticotrophs)ACTH Release (Synergistic with CRH)Not targeted therapeutically
V2 → cAMP → AQP2Collecting duct cells↑ AQP2 insertion → Water permeability → Concentrated Urine

3. ADH Regulation

Primary Stimulus: Plasma Osmolality

OsmolalityADH Response
<280 mOsm/kgMaximal Suppression (ADH undetectable)
280-295Gradual Increase
>295 mOsm/kgSteep Rise (1% Osm rise → 1-2 pg/mL ADH rise)

Threshold: ~280-285 mOsm/kg (Adiposity shifts threshold)

Volume / Baroreceptor Regulation

Volume StatusBaroreceptor InputADH Response
Normal VolumeNormal firingBaseline ADH
Hypovolaemia (↓10%)↓ Baroreceptor firing↑ ADH (Non-osmotic release)
Severe Hypovolaemia (↓20-30%)Marked ↓ firingMassive ADH (Overrides osmostat)

Key: Volume depletion → ADH release even if osmolality low (Non-osmotic release)

Other Stimuli

StimulusADH Effect
Nausea / VomitingPotent stimulus (via chemoreceptor trigger zone)
Pain / Stress↑ ADH
Hypoglycaemia↑ ADH
Angiotensin II↑ ADH
AlcoholInhibits ADH (Diuresis)
Atrial Natriuretic Peptide (ANP)Inhibits ADH

4. Diabetes Insipidus (DI) — Classification & Diagnosis

TypePathologyADH LevelUrine ConcentrationKey Differentiator
Central DIPituitary stalk/hypothalamus lesionLow/AbsentDiluteResponds to Desmopressin
Nephrogenic DIRenal resistance to ADHHighDiluteNo response to Desmopressin
Primary PolydipsiaExcessive water intakeSuppressed (Physiological)Dilute/NormalConcentrates during deprivation
Gestational DIPlacental VasopressinaseRelative DeficiencyDilutePregnancy; Responds to Desmopressin

Aetiology

Central DI

CauseFrequency
Idiopathic~30%
Pituitary Surgery / Trauma~20%
Infiltration (Sarcoidosis, LCH, Metastases, Haemochromatosis)~15%
Genetic (AVP gene mutations)Rare
Autoimmune (Lymphocytic Infundibuloneurohypophysitis)~10%

Nephrogenic DI

CauseMechanism
Lithium↓ AQP2 expression, ↓ cAMP (Most common acquired)
DrugsDemeclocycline, Amphotericin B, Foscarnet, Ofloxacin
ElectrolyteHypercalcaemia, Hypokalaemia
Obstructive UropathyTubulointerstitial damage
GeneticAQP2 (Autosomal Recessive), AVPR2 (X-linked Recessive)

5. Water Deprivation Test (WDT) — Gold Standard for DI

Protocol

PhaseProcedureTarget
1. Fluid DeprivationNPO + Fluid restriction; Weight, Serum/Urine Osmolality q1-2hUntil ↑ >5% weight loss OR Urine Osm > Plasma Osm
2. Desmopressin ChallengeDDAVP 2µg SC/IV (or 10-40µg Intranasal)Urine Osmolality at 60min

Interpretation Matrix

ResultCentral DINephrogenic DIPrimary Polydipsia
Baseline Urine OsmLow (<300)Low (<300)Low/Variable
After DeprivationLow (<300)Low (<300)Concentrates (>600)
Post-DDAVP (60min)↑ >50%No Response (<10% rise)N/A (Already concentrated)
Plasma ADHLowHighLow

Stop Criteria (Safety)

Stop IfUrine Osm > Plasma Osm
Weight Loss>5% body weight
HaemodynamicsSBP <90, Tachycardia, Syncope
Time>8 hours (usually 4-6h)

6. SIADH (Syndrome of Inappropriate ADH Secretion)

Diagnostic Criteria (Schwartz-Bartter)

CriterionRequired
1. HyponatraemiaSerum Na+ <135 mmol/L
2. Hypo-osmolalitySerum Osm <275 mOsm/kg
3. Inappropriately Concentrated UrineUrine Osm >100 mOsm/kg (usually >Serum)
4. Elevated Urine SodiumUrine Na+ >20 mmol/L (Euvolaemic)
5. Normal Thyroid/Adrenal/RenalTSH, Cortisol, Creatinine Normal
6. No DiureticsExclude Recent Diuretic Use

Aetiology

CategoryCauses
DrugsSSRIs, SNRIs, Carbamazepine, Cyclophosphamide, Vincristine, Chlorpropamide, PPIs
MalignancySmall Cell Lung Cancer (Most common), Pancreatic, Lymphoma, Thymoma
CNSStroke, Haemorrhage, Meningitis, Encephalitis, Guillain-Barré, Pituitary Surgery
PulmonaryPneumonia, TB, Lung Abscess, Positive Pressure Ventilation
EndocrineHypothyroidism, Adrenal Insufficiency
Post-op / Pain / NauseaTransient

7. SIADH — Management

Stepwise Approach

StepInterventionIndication
1. Treat CauseDiscontinue offending drug; Treat infection/malignancyAlways 1st
2. Fluid Restriction<1 L/day (1000-1500 mL)1st line (Mild-Moderate)
3. PharmacologicalDemeclocycline 600-900mg/day (Inhibits ADH action on V2)Refractory / Cannot restrict fluids
4. Vaptans (V2 Antagonists)Tolvaptan 15-60mg OD (Start 15mg, titrate)Severe symptomatic (Na+ <120) / Refractory
5. Hypertonic Saline3% NaCl 100-150mL over 1-2h (Target Na+ rise 4-6 mmol/L/24h)Severe Neuro Symptoms (Seizures, Coma)

Demeclocycline

AspectDetails
MechanismInhibits ADH-induced cAMP in collecting duct → Nephrogenic DI-like state
Dose150mg QDS → Titrate to 300mg QDS (Max 600-1200mg/day)
Onset3-5 days
ToxicityPhotosensitivity, Nephrotoxicity, Hepatotoxicity, Azotemia

Tolvaptan (Vaptan)

AspectDetails
MechanismSelective V2 Receptor Antagonist → Aquaresis (Water excretion without Na+ loss)
Dose15mg OD PO → Titrate to 30, 45, 60mg OD
MonitoringSerum Na+ q4-6h initially; Liver function (Hepatotoxicity risk)
ContraindicationsAnuria, Hypovolaemia, Pregnancy, Concomitant strong CYP3A4 inhibitors
DurationMax 30 days (US); Longer in some regions

8. Desmopressin (DDAVP) — Central DI & Others

FeatureDetails
MechanismSynthetic V2 Agonist (No V1 pressor effect)
RouteIntranasal (10-40µg), Oral (0.1-0.4mg), SC/IV (2-4µg)
Half-life2-4 hours (vs ADH 10-20min)
Central DI1st Line: DDAVP 10-40µg Intranasal q12-24h / 0.1-0.4mg Oral q12-24h / 2µg SC/IV q24h
Nocturnal EnuresisDDAVP 0.2-0.6mg Oral at bedtime
Bleeding Disorders (vWD, Haemophilia A)DDAVP 0.3µg/kg IV → ↑ vWF + Factor VIII
CautionsHyponatraemia (Water retention); Fluid restriction mandatory

9. Cerebral Salt Wasting (CSW) vs SIADH

FeatureSIADHCerebral Salt Wasting (CSW)
Volume StatusEuvolaemicHypovolaemic
Urine Na+>20 mmol/L>20 mmol/L (often higher)
Serum Na+LowLow
Urine Osm>100 (Inappropriate)High
Volume CluesNormal JVP, No oedemaLow JVP, Dry mucosa, Postural hypotension
Urine OutputLow/NormalHigh (Polyuria)
ManagementFluid restriction, VaptansIV Saline + Fludrocortisone
ContextEuvolaemic hyponatraemiaPost-neurosurgery, TBI, SAH

10. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
ADH ReceptorsV2 = Renal Water Reabsorption (Desmopressin); V1a = Vasoconstriction
DI TypesCentral (Low ADH) vs Nephrogenic (High ADH, Renal Resistance)
WDT Stop CriteriaWeight loss >5% OR Urine Osm > Plasma Osm
Central DIDDAVP → Urine Osm ↑ >50%
Nephrogenic DIDDAVP → No Response
Primary PolydipsiaUrine Concentrates During Deprivation (No DDAVP needed)
SIADH CriteriaHyponatraemia + Hypo-osmolality + Urine Osm >100 + Urine Na+ >20 + Euvolaemic
SIADH TreatmentFluid Restriction <1L → Demeclocycline → Tolvaptan (Severe)
TolvaptanV2 Antagonist; Aquaresis; Hepatotoxicity Risk; Max 30 days
DDAVPV2 Agonist; Central DI 1st Line; Nocturnal Enuresis; vWD/Haemophilia A
CSW vs SIADHCSW = Hypovolaemic + Polyuria; SIADH = Euvolaemic + Low Urine Output
LithiumMost Common Cause of Nephrogenic DI (↓ AQP2)
Gestational DIPlacental Vasopressinase → Responds to DDAVP

11. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
DI vs SIADHDI = Polyuria + Dilute Urine; SIADH = Oliguria + Concentrated Urine
Central vs Nephrogenic DIDDAVP Response: Central = Works; Nephrogenic = Fails
Primary Polydipsia vs DIWDT: Polydipsia concentrates urine during deprivation; DI does not
SIADH vs CSWSIADH = Euvolaemic; CSW = Hypovolaemic (JVP, Postural drop)
DDAVP vs VasopressinDDAVP = Pure V2 (No pressor effect); Vasopressin = V1+V2
DDAVP in EnuresisIntranasal/Oral at bedtime; No fluid restriction needed
DDAVP in vWD↑ vWF + Factor VIII; Use before surgery/dental

12. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((ADH/Vasopressin))
    Physiology
      Supraoptic + Paraventricular Nuclei
      V2: Collecting Duct (AQP2) → Water Retention
      V1a: Vascular Smooth Muscle → Vasoconstriction
      T½: 10-20 min; DDAVP t½ 2-4h
    Regulation
      Osmoreceptors: Osm ↑ → ADH ↑ (Threshold ~285)
      Baroreceptors: Vol ↓ → ADH ↑ (Non-osmotic)
      Inhibitors: Alcohol, ANP
      Stimuli: Nausea, Pain, Hypoglycaemia, Angiotensin II
    DI
      Central: ADH Low → DDAVP Works
      Nephrogenic: ADH High → DDAVP Fails
      WDT: Deprivation → DDAVP Challenge
    SIADH
      Euvolaemic Hyponatraemia + Urine Conc + Urine Na+ >20
      Dx: Schwartz-Bartter Criteria
      Rx: Fluid Restrict → Demeclocycline → Tolvaptan
    DDAVP
      V2 Agonist; Central DI 1st Line
      Nocturnal Enuresis; vWD/Haemophilia A
      Hyponatraemia Risk (Fluid Restrict!)

13. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
ADH ReceptorsV2 = Renal Water Reabsorption (Desmopressin); V1a = Vasoconstriction
DI TypesCentral (Low ADH) vs Nephrogenic (High ADH, Renal Resistance)
WDT Stop CriteriaWeight loss >5% OR Urine Osm > Plasma Osm
Central DIDDAVP → Urine Osm ↑ >50%
Nephrogenic DIDDAVP → No Response
Primary PolydipsiaUrine Concentrates During Deprivation (No DDAVP needed)
SIADH CriteriaHyponatraemia + Hypo-osmolality + Urine Osm >100 + Urine Na+ >20 + Euvolaemic
SIADH TreatmentFluid Restriction <1L → Demeclocycline → Tolvaptan (Severe)
TolvaptanV2 Antagonist; Aquaresis; Hepatotoxicity Risk; Max 30 days
DDAVPV2 Agonist; Central DI 1st Line; Nocturnal Enuresis; vWD/Haemophilia A
CSW vs SIADHCSW = Hypovolaemic + Polyuria; SIADH = Euvolaemic + Low Urine Output
LithiumMost Common Cause of Nephrogenic DI (↓ AQP2)
Gestational DIPlacental Vasopressinase → Responds to DDAVP

14. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Hypothalamic-Pituitary Axis|Hypothalamic-Pituitary Axis]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Prolactin Axis]], [[Anterior Pituitary Hormones Overview]], [[Diabetes Insipidus vs SIADH]], [[Endocrine Emergencies]]

15. MCQs (10)

  1. ADH (vasopressin) is synthesised primarily in which hypothalamic nucleus?

A. Supraoptic nucleus

B. Paraventricular nucleus

C. Arcuate nucleus

D. Ventromedial nucleus

E. Anterior nucleus

  1. Which receptor mediates ADH-induced water reabsorption in the collecting duct?

A. V1a

B. V1b

C. V2

D. V3

E. Oxytocin receptor

  1. Plasma osmolality threshold for ADH release is approximately:

A. 270 mOsm/kg

B. 280-285 mOsm/kg

C. 300 mOsm/kg

D. 310 mOsm/kg

E. 295 mOsm/kg

  1. In SIADH, which finding is INCONSISTENT with the diagnosis?

A. Urine osmolality >100 mOsm/kg

B. Urine sodium >20 mmol/L

C. Hypovolaemia

D. Normal thyroid/adrenal function

E. Serum osmolality <275 mOsm/kg

  1. Water deprivation test: Central DI is confirmed by:

A. No urine concentration after dehydration, >50% rise after desmopressin

B. Urine concentration after dehydration

C. No response to desmopressin

D. High baseline plasma ADH

E. Urine osmolality > plasma osmolality at baseline

  1. Nephrogenic DI is caused by renal resistance to ADH. Most common acquired cause:

A. Lithium

B. Demeclocycline

C. Hypercalcaemia

D. Hypokalaemia

E. Sarcoidosis

  1. Desmopressin (DDAVP) differs from natural ADH by:

A. V1a agonist activity

B. Longer half-life (2-4h vs 10-20min), selective V2 agonist

C. Shorter half-life

D. V1b agonist activity

E. Oral bioavailability only

  1. Cerebral salt wasting (CSW) differs from SIADH by:

A. Euvolaemia in CSW

B. Hypovolaemia in CSW with polyuria

C. Low urine sodium in CSW

D. Fluid restriction treats CSW

E. CSW occurs without CNS pathology

  1. Which drug is a V2 receptor antagonist (vaptan) used in SIADH?

A. Tolvaptan

B. Demeclocycline

C. Desmopressin

D. Conivaptan

E. Furosemide

  1. Gestational diabetes insipidus is caused by:

A. Placental vasopressinase degrading ADH

B. Pituitary infarction

C. Autoimmune hypophysitis

D. Renal resistance to ADH

E. Excess oral intake

16. SBA Questions (5)

  1. 25yo woman: polyuria 5L/day, polydipsia, serum Na+ 148, serum osm 305, urine osm 150. After water deprivation: urine osm 180. Post-desmopressin (2µg IV): urine osm 550. Diagnosis?

A. Central diabetes insipidus

B. Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus

C. Primary polydipsia

D. Partial central DI

E. SIADH

  1. 45yo man on lithium 10 years: polyuria, serum Na+ 149, urine osm 120. Water deprivation test: no concentration. Desmopressin: no response. Next step?

A. Hydrochlorothiazide + amiloride

B. Desmopressin high dose

C. Stop lithium immediately

D. Fluid restriction

E. Tolvaptan

  1. 30yo woman post-pituitary surgery: serum Na+ 125, serum osm 260, urine osm 400, urine Na+ 50, euvolaemic, normal thyroid/adrenal. Best initial management?

A. Fluid restriction <1L/day

B. 3% saline infusion

C. Tolvaptan 15mg OD

D. Demeclocycline

E. Desmopressin

  1. 60yo man with small cell lung cancer: serum Na+ 118, confusion, seizures. Urine osm 500, urine Na+ 60, euvolaemic. Emergency management?

A. 3% NaCl 100-150mL over 1-2h (target Na+ rise 4-6 mmol/L/24h)

B. Fluid restriction only

C. Tolvaptan immediate

D. Demeclocycline loading

E. Desmopressin

  1. 28yo pregnant woman at 32 weeks: new-onset polyuria/polydipsia, serum Na+ 145. Responds to desmopressin. Cause?

A. Placental vasopressinase

B. Pituitary adenoma

C. Nephrogenic DI

D. Primary polydipsia

E. SIADH

17. Flashcards

  • Q: ADH synthesis site

A: Supraoptic + Paraventricular nuclei → axonal transport → posterior pituitary

  • Q: ADH main regulator

A: Plasma osmolality (threshold ~285 mOsm/kg) via OVLT osmoreceptors

  • Q: V2 receptor

A: Collecting duct principal cells → cAMP → AQP2 insertion → water reabsorption

  • Q: V1a receptor

A: Vascular smooth muscle → vasoconstriction (pressor effect)

  • Q: Central DI

A: Low/absent ADH → dilute urine → responds to desmopressin (>50% urine osm rise)

  • Q: Nephrogenic DI

A: High ADH + renal resistance → no response to desmopressin; Li+ common cause

  • Q: Primary polydipsia

A: Psychogenic polydipsia → urine concentrates during water deprivation

  • Q: SIADH criteria

A: Hyponatraemia + hypo-osmolality + urine osm >100 + urine Na+ >20 + euvolaemic

  • Q: SIADH Rx ladder

A: Fluid restrict <1L → demeclocycline → tolvaptan (severe) → 3% saline (emergency)

  • Q: CSW vs SIADH

A: CSW = hypovolaemic + polyuria; SIADH = euvolaemic + oliguria

  • Q: DDAVP uses

A: Central DI, nocturnal enuresis, vWD/haemophilia A (↑ vWF + FVIII)

  • Q: Tolvaptan

A: V2 antagonist → aquaresis; hepatotoxicity risk; max 30 days US

  • Q: Demeclocycline

A: Tetracycline derivative → nephrogenic DI-like state; onset 3-5 days; photosensitivity

  • Q: WDT stop criteria

A: Weight loss >5% OR urine osm > plasma osm OR haemodynamic instability

  • Q: Lithium DI

A: Downregulates AQP2 expression; most common acquired nephrogenic DI

18. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Supraoptic nucleus — ADH (vasopressin) is synthesised primarily in which hypothalamic nucleus?
  1. V2 — Which receptor mediates ADH-induced water reabsorption in the collecting duct?
  1. 280-285 mOsm/kg — Plasma osmolality threshold for ADH release is approximately:
  1. Hypovolaemia — In SIADH, which finding is INCONSISTENT with the diagnosis?
  1. No urine concentration after dehydration, >50% rise after desmopressin — Water deprivation test: Central DI is confirmed by:
  1. Lithium — Nephrogenic DI is caused by renal resistance to ADH. Most common acquired cause:
  1. Longer half-life (2-4h vs 10-20min), selective V2 agonist — Desmopressin (DDAVP) differs from natural ADH by:
  1. Hypovolaemia in CSW with polyuria — Cerebral salt wasting (CSW) differs from SIADH by:
  1. Tolvaptan — Which drug is a V2 receptor antagonist (vaptan) used in SIADH?
  1. Placental vasopressinase degrading ADH — Gestational diabetes insipidus is caused by:

SBAs

  1. Central diabetes insipidus — 25yo woman: polyuria 5L/day, polydipsia, serum Na+ 148, serum osm 305, urine osm 150. After water deprivation: urine osm 180. Post-desmopressin (2µg IV): urine osm 550. Diagnosis?
  1. Hydrochlorothiazide + amiloride — 45yo man on lithium 10 years: polyuria, serum Na+ 149, urine osm 120. Water deprivation test: no concentration. Desmopressin: no response. Next step?
  1. Fluid restriction <1L/day — 30yo woman post-pituitary surgery: serum Na+ 125, serum osm 260, urine osm 400, urine Na+ 50, euvolaemic, normal thyroid/adrenal. Best initial management?
  1. 3% NaCl 100-150mL over 1-2h (target Na+ rise 4-6 mmol/L/24h) — 60yo man with small cell lung cancer: serum Na+ 118, confusion, seizures. Urine osm 500, urine Na+ 60, euvolaemic. Emergency management?
  1. Placental vasopressinase — 28yo pregnant woman at 32 weeks: new-onset polyuria/polydipsia, serum Na+ 145. Responds to desmopressin. Cause?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.2.12 Posterior Pituitary Oxytocin
[!info]
Oxytocin = Hormone of parturition and lactation. Synthesised in hypothalamic paraventricular nucleus, released from posterior pituitary. Unique for its positive feedback loop during labour. Synthetic oxytocin (Syntocinon) widely used in obstetrics.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Describe oxytocin synthesis, secretion, and receptor physiology
  • [ ] Explain the positive feedback loop in labour
  • [ ] Apply synthetic oxytocin (Syntocinon) protocols for induction/augmentation/PPH
  • [ ] Understand atosiban mechanism as oxytocin receptor antagonist (tocolytic)
  • [ ] Recognise oxytocin's role in lactation and social bonding

2. Oxytocin Physiology

Synthesis & Secretion

FeatureDetails
SynthesisHypothalamic Paraventricular Nucleus (PVN) (also some in Supraoptic)
TransportAxoplasmic transport → Posterior Pituitary (Neurohypophysis)
Structure9 amino acid cyclic peptide (Cys-Tyr-Ile-Gln-Asn-Cys-Pro-Leu-Gly) with disulphide bond
Half-life3-5 minutes (very short)
Main StimuliCervical stretch (Ferguson reflex), Suckling (Milk ejection reflex), Sexual activity, Stress

Oxytocin Receptor (OTR)

FeatureDetails
TypeGq/11-coupled receptor (Oxytocin Receptor, OTR)
SignallingGq → PLC → IP3/DAG → ↑ Intracellular Ca²⁺ → Contraction
Main TargetsUterus (Myometrial contraction), Breast (Myoepithelial cells → Milk ejection), Brain (Social bonding, Trust, Anxiety modulation)
UpregulationOestrogen ↑ OTR expression (Late pregnancy); Progesterone ↓ OTR

3. Oxytocin in Labour — Positive Feedback Loop

The Ferguson Reflex (Positive Feedback)

Cervical Stretch (Fetal Descent)
         ↓
Afferent Signals → Spinal Cord → Brainstem → Hypothalamus (PVN)
         ↓
**Oxytocin Release** (Posterior Pituitary)
         ↓
Uterine Contraction (via OTR → ↑ Intracellular Ca²⁺)
         ↓
**Further Cervical Stretch** → Positive Feedback Loop
         ↓
**Exponential Rise** in Oxytocin & Contraction Frequency/Intensity
         ↓
**Delivery** → Stretch Ceases → Feedback Loop Breaks

Key Features of Parturition Oxytocin

FeatureDetails
TriggerCervical stretch (Fetal head descent)
Release PatternPulsatile, increasing frequency & amplitude
OTR UpregulationOestrogen ↑ OTR expression (Late pregnancy); Progesterone withdrawal removes inhibition
Positive FeedbackRare example of physiological positive feedback; Drives labour to completion
TerminationDelivery → Stretch removed → Afferent input stops → Oxytocin falls

4. Oxytocin in Lactation — Milk Ejection Reflex

Milk Ejection Reflex (Let-down)

Suckling (Nipple Stimulation)
         ↓
Afferent → Spinal Cord → Brainstem → Hypothalamus (PVN)
         ↓
**Oxytocin Release** (Posterior Pituitary)
         ↓
**Myoepithelial Cell Contraction** (Breast Alveoli/Ducts)
         ↓
**Milk Ejection** (Milk flows from alveoli → Ducts → Nipple)
         ↓
**Sustained Suckling** → Pulsatile Oxytocin → Multiple Let-downs per Feed

Key Features of Lactation Oxytocin

FeatureDetails
TriggerSuckling (Nipple stimulation) → Afferent via sympathoadrenal pathway
Release PatternPulsatile (Multiple let-downs per feed)
ActionMyoepithelial Cell Contraction (Alveoli/Ducts) → Milk ejection
Prolactin SynergyProlactin = Milk Synthesis (Lactogenesis); Oxytocin = Milk Ejection (Galactopoiesis)
Conditioned ReflexMother's thought/baby's cry → Oxytocin release (Learned)

5. Synthetic Oxytocin (Syntocinon) — Clinical Use

Indications

IndicationRegimenMonitoring
Induction of LabourStart 0.5-2 mU/min IV → Increase by 1-2 mU/min q30-60minCTG Continuous; Uterine Activity (IUPC); Max 20 mU/min
Augmentation of LabourStart 1-2 mU/min → Titrate to 3-4 contractions/10minSame as Induction
Postpartum Haemorrhage (PPH)5-10 IU IV Bolus → 10-40 IU in 500mL NS at 125 mL/hUterine Tone; Vaginal Bleeding; Vitals
Retained Placenta10-20 IU IV/IMUterine Contraction
Incomplete Abortion5-10 IU IVUterine Evacuation

Syntocinon Dosing Protocol (IV Infusion)

StepRateTitration
Start0.5-2 mU/min-
Increment1-2 mU/min every 30-60 minUntil 3-4 contractions/10min
Maintenance8-16 mU/min (Typical)Max 20 mU/min
Max Rate20 mU/minHigher → Hyperstimulation Risk

Oxytocin Dilution (Standard)

PreparationConcentration
10 IU in 500mL Normal Saline20 mU/mL (1 mL/min = 20 mU/min)
5 IU in 500mL Normal Saline10 mU/mL

6. Oxytocin — Adverse Effects & Contraindications

Adverse EffectMechanismManagement
Uterine HyperstimulationExcessive contractions (>5/10min) + ↓ Resting ToneStop/Reduce Oxytocin; Tocolytic (Terbutaline/Atosiban)
Fetal DistressUteroplacental insufficiencyStop Oxytocin; Left lateral; O₂; Consider C-section
Water Intoxication / HyponatraemiaOxytocin V2 effect (ADH-like) + Large fluid volumesLimit fluids; Monitor Na+; Stop Oxytocin if severe
Postpartum HypertensionVasoconstriction (V1-like at high doses)Monitor BP; Antihypertensives if needed
Amniotic Fluid EmbolismRare, catastrophicEmergency C-section, Supportive care

Contraindications to Oxytocin

AbsoluteRelative
Cephalopelvic DisproportionGrand Multiparity (>5)
Malpresentation (Transverse, Brow)Previous Uterine Surgery (Previous C-section — relative)
Placenta PraeviaMultiple Pregnancy
Active Genital HerpesPrevious Uterine Rupture
Fetal Distress (Pre-existing)Grand Multiparity

7. Atosiban — Oxytocin Receptor Antagonist (Tocolytic)

FeatureDetails
MechanismCompetitive Oxytocin Receptor Antagonist (OTR)
IndicationAcute Tocolysis for Preterm Labour (24-33 weeks)
DosingLoading: 6.75mg IV over 15 min → Maintenance: 18mg/h IV for 3h → 6mg/h IV up to 45h (Max 72h total)
AdvantagesNo β-agonist side effects (Tachycardia, Hypokalaemia, Hyperglycaemia); No cardiac contraindications
Advantages vs β-agonistsSafe in cardiac disease, Diabetes, Hyperthyroidism
Contraindications>33 weeks, Placental Abruption, Chorioamnionitis, Severe Pre-eclampsia, Fetal Distress

Atosiban vs β-agonists (Salbutamol/Terbutaline)

FeatureAtosibanSalbutamol/Terbutaline
MechanismOTR Antagonistβ₂-Agonist
TachycardiaNoYes (Dose-limiting)
HypokalaemiaNoYes
HyperglycaemiaNoYes
Maternal HRNo change↑↑
Cardiac ContraindicationsNoneIHD, Arrhythmias, HTN

8. Oxytocin in Postpartum Haemorrhage (PPH)

WHO/FIGO Recommendations

ScenarioRegimen
Prophylaxis (Active Management 3rd Stage)10 IU IM (or 5 IU IV) immediately after delivery
Treatment (Uterine Atony PPH)10 IU IV Bolus → 20-40 IU in 500-1000mL NS at 125-250 mL/h
Refractory PPHAdd Ergometrine (0.2-0.5mg IM/IV) / Carboprost (250µg IM) / Misoprostol (800µg PR/SL) / Tranexamic Acid 1g IV

WHO PPH Bundle (2018)

  1. Oxytocin 10 IU IM/IV immediately after birth
  2. Controlled Cord Traction
  3. Uterine Massage
  4. Monitor Uterine Tone & Bleeding for 24h

9. Oxytocin in Social Behaviour & Brain

FunctionEvidence
Social BondingPair bonding, Maternal-infant attachment, Trust
Anxiety ReductionAnxiolytic effects via amygdala OTR
Autism SpectrumLow CSF Oxytocin; Intranasal trials (Mixed results)
Sexual FunctionOrgasm, Erection, Ejaculation facilitation
Stress BufferingHPA axis modulation (↓ Cortisol)

10. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Oxytocin SynthesisHypothalamic Paraventricular Nucleus (PVN); Posterior Pituitary release
Positive FeedbackOnly hormonal positive feedback in humans = Oxytocin in Labour (Ferguson Reflex)
Labour TriggerCervical Stretch → Ferguson Reflex → Oxytocin Pulses → Contractions
LactationSuckling → Oxytocin → Myoepithelial Contraction → Milk Ejection (Prolactin = Milk Synthesis)
Syntocinon InductionStart 0.5-2 mU/min → Max 20 mU/min; CTG Continuous
Oxytocin in PPH10 IU IV Bolus → 20-40 IU in 500-1000mL NS @ 125-250 mL/h
Hyperstimulation>5 Contractions/10min OR Resting Tone >20mmHg → Stop Oxytocin
Water IntoxicationOxytocin V2 activity + Fluids → Hyponatraemia (Limit fluids, Monitor Na+)
AtosibanOTR Antagonist; Tocolytic 24-33+6 wks; No β-side effects; Loading 6.75mg/15min → 18mg/h → 6mg/h
CarbetocinLong-acting Oxytocin Analog (t½ 40-80min); Single 100µg IM/IV for PPH Prophylaxis
Ergometrine0.2-0.5mg IM/IV; Second-line PPH; Contraindicated in HTN/Pre-eclampsia/Heart Disease

11. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Oxytocin vs ADHADH = V2 Water Retention; Oxytocin = Uterine Contraction + Milk Ejection
Oxytocin vs ProstaglandinsOxytocin = Induction/Augmentation; PGE2/Misoprostol = Cervical Ripening
Oxytocin in PPH1st Line (10 IU IV); Ergometrine/Carboprost = 2nd/3rd Line
Syntocinon Dilution10 IU in 500mL = 20 mU/mL; 1 mL/min = 20 mU/min
Atosiban vs β-agonistsAtosiban = No tachycardia/hypokalaemia; Safe in Heart Disease/Diabetes
Oxytocin V2 EffectCross-reactivity with V2 → Water Retention → Hyponatraemia (Limit Fluids)
Ergometrine ContraindicationsHTN, Pre-eclampsia, IHD, Sepsis, Sepsis

Mnemonic: OXYTOCIN IN LABOUR

  • Oxytocin = Only positive feedback hormone
  • X = X-tra contractions (Ferguson Reflex)
  • Y = Yields uterine contraction
  • T = Trigger = Cervical Stretch
  • O = Oxycontin... no, Oxytocin release from PVN
  • C = Contraction frequency ↑
  • I = Induction/Augmentation/PPH
  • N = No V1 pressor at low dose (V2 water retention only)

12. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Oxytocin))
    Physiology
      PVN (Hypothalamus) → Posterior Pituitary
      9aa Peptide; T½ 3-5 min
      OTR: Gq → Ca2+ → Contraction
      Upregulated by Oestrogen
    Labour (Positive Feedback)
      Cervical Stretch → Ferguson Reflex → Oxytocin → Contraction → More Stretch
      Pulsatile Release; Oestrogen Upregulates OTR
    Lactation
      Suckling → PVN → Oxytocin → Myoepithelial Contraction → Milk Ejection
      Prolactin = Lactogenesis; Oxytocin = Galactopoiesis
    Synthetic Oxytocin (Syntocinon)
      Induction: 0.5-2 mU/min → Max 20
      Augmentation: 1-2 mU/min → 3-4 ctn/10min
      PPH: 10 IU IV Bolus → 20-40 IU Infusion
    Complications
      Hyperstimulation (>5/10min); Hyponatraemia (V2); Fetal Distress
    Atosiban (OTR Antagonist)
      Tocolytic 24-33+6 wks
      Loading 6.75mg/15min → 18mg/h → 6mg/h
      No β-side effects
    PPH Protocol
      10 IU IM/IV → Carbetocin 100µg → Ergometrine → Carboprost → Misoprostol → TXA

13. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Oxytocin SynthesisHypothalamic Paraventricular Nucleus (PVN); Posterior Pituitary release
Positive FeedbackOnly hormonal positive feedback in humans = Oxytocin in Labour (Ferguson Reflex)
Labour TriggerCervical Stretch → Ferguson Reflex → Oxytocin Pulses → Contractions
LactationSuckling → Oxytocin → Myoepithelial Contraction → Milk Ejection (Prolactin = Milk Synthesis)
Syntocinon InductionStart 0.5-2 mU/min → Max 20 mU/min; CTG Continuous
Oxytocin in PPH10 IU IV Bolus → 20-40 IU in 500-1000mL NS @ 125-250 mL/h
Hyperstimulation>5 Contractions/10min OR Resting Tone >20mmHg → Stop Oxytocin
Water IntoxicationOxytocin V2 activity + Fluids → Hyponatraemia (Limit fluids, Monitor Na+)
AtosibanOTR Antagonist; Tocolytic 24-33+6 wks; No β-side effects
CarbetocinLong-acting Oxytocin Analog (t½ 40-80min); Single 100µg IM/IV for PPH Prophylaxis
Ergometrine0.2-0.5mg IM/IV; Second-line PPH; Contraindicated in HTN/Pre-eclampsia/Heart Disease
CarbetocinLong-acting Oxytocin analog; 100µg IM/IV single dose for PPH prophylaxis

14. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Hypothalamic-Pituitary Axis|Hypothalamic-Pituitary Axis]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Posterior Pituitary- ADH-Vasopressin]], [[Anterior Pituitary Hormones Overview]], [[Reproductive Endocrinology]], [[Pregnancy & Endocrine]]

15. MCQs (10)

  1. Oxytocin is synthesised in which hypothalamic nuclei?

A. Paraventricular + Supraoptic

B. Paraventricular only

C. Supraoptic only

D. Arcuate + Ventromedial

E. Preoptic area

  1. Primary physiological role of oxytocin in females:

A. Milk ejection reflex + uterine contraction

B. Water reabsorption

C. Cortisol release

D. GH release

E. TSH release

  1. Oxytocin receptor is a:

A. Gq-coupled GPCR

B. Gs-coupled GPCR

C. Gi-coupled GPCR

D. Ion channel

E. Nuclear receptor

  1. During labour, oxytocin release is stimulated by:

A. Ferguson reflex (cervical stretch)

B. Plasma osmolality rise

C. Hypovolaemia

D. Suckling

E. Progesterone withdrawal

  1. Milk ejection reflex pathway:

A. Suckling → spinal cord → hypothalamus → posterior pituitary → oxytocin → myoepithelial contraction

  1. Synthetic oxytocin (Syntocinon) clinical use:

A. Induction/augmentation of labour, postpartum haemorrhage prophylaxis

B. Diabetes insipidus

C. Hyponatraemia

D. Hypertension

E. Heart failure

  1. Oxytocin half-life:

A. 3-5 minutes (IV)

B. 20-30 minutes

C. 1-2 hours

D. 6-8 hours

E. 10-20 minutes like ADH

  1. Oxytocin in males:

A. Sperm transport, ejaculation, social bonding

B. No physiological role

C. Testosterone synthesis

D. Spermatogenesis only

E. Prostate secretion

  1. Carbetocin vs oxytocin:

A. Long-acting oxytocin analogue (t½ ~40 min) for PPH prophylaxis

B. Shorter acting

C. V2 agonist

D. V1a antagonist

E. Oral formulation

  1. Oxytocin challenge test (contraction stress test):

A. Assesses fetal reserve by inducing contractions with oxytocin

B. Tests maternal ADH reserve

C. Diagnoses diabetes insipidus

D. Measures uterine sensitivity

E. Screens for preterm labour

16. SBA Questions (5)

  1. Primigravida at 40 weeks: failed induction. Oxytocin infusion started. Fetal heart rate shows late decelerations. Immediate action?

A. Stop oxytocin, O2, left lateral tilt, tocolysis if needed

B. Increase oxytocin rate

C. Continue at same rate

D. Switch to prostaglandins

E. Emergency C-section immediately

  1. Postpartum woman: atonic uterus, bleeding 1000mL. Oxytocin 10IU IV given. Bleeding continues. Next?

A. Ergometrine 0.5mg IM/IV (if no HTN) → carboprost → surgical

B. More oxytocin

C. Tranexamic acid only

D. Hysterectomy

E. Desmopressin

  1. Breastfeeding mother: poor milk ejection. Oxytocin nasal spray prescribed. Mechanism?

A. V1a-like receptor on myoepithelial cells → contraction → milk ejection

B. V2 receptor → water retention

C. Prolactin release

D. Alveolar synthesis

E. Ductal dilation

  1. Oxytocin infusion for labour induction: max recommended dose?

A. 20 mU/min (protocol-dependent); titrate to 3-4 contractions/10min

B. 5 mU/min

C. 50 mU/min

D. 100 mU/min

E. No maximum

  1. Oxytocin + ergometrine combination for PPH: contraindication for ergometrine?

A. Hypertension / pre-eclampsia (ergometrine causes vasoconstriction)

B. Asthma

C. Diabetes

D. Anaemia

E. Previous C-section

17. Flashcards

  • Q: Oxytocin synthesis

A: Paraventricular + Supraoptic nuclei

  • Q: Oxytocin main actions

A: Uterine contraction + milk ejection (myoepithelial cells)

  • Q: Oxytocin receptor

A: Gq-coupled GPCR → IP3/DAG → Ca2+ release

  • Q: Ferguson reflex

A: Cervical stretch → afferent → hypothalamus → oxytocin surge → stronger contractions

  • Q: Milk ejection reflex

A: Suckling → afferent → hypothalamus → oxytocin → myoepithelial contraction

  • Q: Syntocinon (synthetic)

A: Labour induction/augmentation; PPH prophylaxis (10IU IM/IV)

  • Q: Oxytocin half-life

A: 3-5 min IV; nasal spray available historically

  • Q: Carbetocin

A: Long-acting analogue (t½ ~40 min); single dose PPH prophylaxis

  • Q: Oxytocin in males

A: Ejaculation, sperm transport, social bonding, trust

  • Q: Contraindications

A: Malpresentation, CPD, previous classical C-section, hyperstimulation

  • Q: Oxytocin + ergometrine

A: Ergometrine contraindicated in hypertension/pre-eclampsia

  • Q: Oxytocin monitoring

A: Continuous CTG; uterine activity ≤5 contractions/10min; watch for water intoxication

  • Q: Water intoxication risk

A: Oxytocin has weak V2 effect → high-dose prolonged infusion → hyponatraemia

18. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Paraventricular + Supraoptic — Oxytocin is synthesised in which hypothalamic nuclei?
  1. Milk ejection reflex + uterine contraction — Primary physiological role of oxytocin in females:
  1. Gq-coupled GPCR — Oxytocin receptor is a:
  1. Ferguson reflex (cervical stretch) — During labour, oxytocin release is stimulated by:
  1. Suckling → spinal cord → hypothalamus → posterior pituitary → oxytocin → myoepithelial contraction — Milk ejection reflex pathway:
  1. Induction/augmentation of labour, postpartum haemorrhage prophylaxis — Synthetic oxytocin (Syntocinon) clinical use:
  1. 3-5 minutes (IV) — Oxytocin half-life:
  1. Sperm transport, ejaculation, social bonding — Oxytocin in males:
  1. Long-acting oxytocin analogue (t½ ~40 min) for PPH prophylaxis — Carbetocin vs oxytocin:
  1. Assesses fetal reserve by inducing contractions with oxytocin — Oxytocin challenge test (contraction stress test):

SBAs

  1. Stop oxytocin, O2, left lateral tilt, tocolysis if needed — Primigravida at 40 weeks: failed induction. Oxytocin infusion started. Fetal heart rate shows late decelerations. Immediate action?
  1. Ergometrine 0.5mg IM/IV (if no HTN) → carboprost → surgical — Postpartum woman: atonic uterus, bleeding 1000mL. Oxytocin 10IU IV given. Bleeding continues. Next?
  1. V1a-like receptor on myoepithelial cells → contraction → milk ejection — Breastfeeding mother: poor milk ejection. Oxytocin nasal spray prescribed. Mechanism?
  1. 20 mU/min (protocol-dependent); titrate to 3-4 contractions/10min — Oxytocin infusion for labour induction: max recommended dose?
  1. Hypertension / pre-eclampsia (ergometrine causes vasoconstriction) — Oxytocin + ergometrine combination for PPH: contraindication for ergometrine?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.2.13 Prolactin Axis
[!info]
Prolactin = Lactotroph product. Unique anterior pituitary hormone under tonic dopaminergic inhibition. Most common functioning pituitary adenoma (prolactinoma). Key concepts: dopamine inhibition, macroprolactinaemia, cabergoline treatment.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Describe prolactin synthesis, secretion, and dopamine regulation
  • [ ] Interpret hyperprolactinaemia causes and diagnostic algorithm
  • [ ] Differentiate true prolactinoma from macroprolactinaemia and stalk effect
  • [ ] Apply cabergoline dosing and withdrawal protocols
  • [ ] Manage prolactinoma in pregnancy

2. Prolactin Physiology

Synthesis & Secretion

FeatureDetails
SourceAnterior Pituitary Lactotrophs (15-20% of cells)
Structure199 amino acid polypeptide; Macroprolactin = PRL-IgG complex (large, inactive)
RegulationDopamine (PIH) = Tonic inhibition (Main regulator); TRH (stimulatory); Oestrogen (stimulates lactotroph hyperplasia); Suckling, Stress, Sleep → ↑
Circadian RhythmPeak during sleep; Nadir mid-morning
Pregnancy↑ 10-20x (Oestrogen stimulates lactotroph hyperplasia); Increases from 6 weeks, plateaus at term
PostpartumSuckling → Prolactin surges → Lactation; Lactational amenorrhoea (↓ GnRH)

Receptor & Actions

ReceptorPRL-R (Cytokine receptor family, JAK2/STAT5 pathway)
Main ActionsMammogenesis (Breast development), Lactogenesis (Milk synthesis), Galactopoiesis (Milk maintenance)
Gonadal EffectsHigh PRL → ↓ GnRH pulsatility → ↓ LH/FSH → Hypogonadism, Infertility, Galactorrhoea

3. Hyperprolactinaemia — Diagnostic Algorithm

Prolactin >500 mIU/L (or > upper reference limit)
         │
         ▼
EXCLUDE PHYSIOLOGICAL / PHARMACOLOGICAL CAUSES:
         │
         ├── DRUGS: Antipsychotics (Risperidone > Olanzapine > Quetiapine), Metoclopramide, Antidepressants (SSRIs, TCAs), OCP, Verapamil, Opioids
         ├── HYPOTHYROIDISM (TRH-driven) → Check TSH
         ├── RENAL FAILURE (Reduced clearance)
         ├── PREGNANCY
         ├── NIPPLE STIMULATION / STRESS (Recent)
         └── MACROPROLACTINAEMIA
         │
         ▼
REPEAT PROLACTIN (Fasting, Non-stressed, Avoid Nipple Stimulation)
         │
         ├── NORMAL → Transient/Physiological
         │
         └── PERSISTENTLY ELEVATED
                 │
                 ▼
PEG PRECIPITATION TEST → MACROPROLACTINAEMIC?
                 │
                 ├── YES → MACROPROLACTINAEMIA (Benign, No treatment)
                 │
                 └── NO → TRUE HYPERPROLACTINAEMIA
                         │
                         ▼
                 MRI PITUITARY (3mm slices, Gadolinium)
                         │
                         ├── NO TUMOUR + Prolactin <1000 → IDIOPATHIC / STALK EFFECT
                         ├── MICROADENOMA (<10mm) → CABERGOLINE
                         ├── MACROADENOMA (≥10mm) → CABERGOLINE / SURGERY
                         └── NO TUMOUR + Prolactin >1000 → STALK EFFECT / ECTOPIC (Rare)

4. Macroprolactinaemia

FeatureDetails
DefinitionProlactin complexed with IgG autoantibody (PRL-IgG complex)
CharacteristicsHigh molecular weight (>150 kDa); Biologically inactive (cannot cross capillary endothelium)
Prevalence10-25% of hyperprolactinaemia
DiagnosisPEG (Polyethylene Glycol) Precipitation → Precipitates macroprolactin; Recovery <60% = Macroprolactinaemia
Clinical SignificanceBenign; No pituitary tumour; No galactorrhoea/hypogonademia; No treatment needed
PitfallCan coexist with true hyperprolactinaemia → Measure monomeric PRL after PEG

5. Prolactinoma — Classification & Management

Classification

TypeSizeProlactin LevelPrevalence
Microprolactinoma<10 mmUsually 500-5000 mIU/L~60%
Macroprolactinoma≥10 mm>5000 mIU/L (often >10,000)~40%
Giant>40 mmVery highRare

Clinical Presentation

FeatureMicroprolactinomaMacroprolactinoma
GalactorrhoeaCommonCommon
HypogonadismAmenorrhoea, Infertility, Low libidoSevere (Mass effect + PRL suppression of GnRH)
Visual FieldsNormalBi-temporal Hemianopia (Chiasmal compression)
HeadacheRareCommon
Other Pituitary DeficitsRareCommon (Hypopituitarism)

6. Prolactinoma — Management

Microprolactinoma (<10mm)

AspectProtocol
1st LineCabergoline 0.25mg BD → Titrate weekly by 0.25mg BD
Target Dose0.5-1mg BD (Max 2mg BD)
GoalProlactin normalisation + Tumour shrinkage (>50% volume reduction)
MonitoringProlactin q4-8w during titration; MRI at 6-12m if macro features
WithdrawalAfter 2-3 years normal PRL + No tumour on MRI → Trial off cabergoline

Macroprolactinoma (≥10mm)

AspectProtocol
1st LineCabergoline 0.5mg BD → Titrate to 1-2mg BD (Max 3mg BD)
TargetProlactin normalisation + >50% volume reduction
Surgery IndicationsCabergoline resistant/intolerant; Apoplexy; Chiasmal compression with visual loss despite medical Rx
Visual FieldsMandatory (Bi-temporal hemianopia = Urgent decompression)
Visual ImprovementUsually within days-weeks of cabergoline initiation

Cabergoline Dosing Schedule

WeekMicroprolactinomaMacroprolactinoma
1-20.25mg BD0.5mg BD
3-40.5mg BD1mg BD
5-81mg BD1.5-2mg BD
Max2mg BD3mg BD (rarely >2mg)

7. Cabergoline — Key Points

AspectDetails
MechanismD2 Dopamine Receptor Agonist → Inhibits Prolactin secretion + Shrinks tumour
Dose Range0.25-3mg/week (divided BD); Titrate slowly
Half-life63-69 hours (long; allows BD/Weekly dosing)
OnsetProlactin falls within hours; Tumour shrinkage over weeks-months
Side EffectsNausea (40%), Postural hypotension, Nasal congestion, Impulse control disorders (Rare), Valvulopathy (High dose, chronic)
MonitoringEchocardiogram if dose >2mg/day or duration >5 years; Prolactin q3-6m

8. Withdrawal / Discontinuation Criteria

CriteriaRequired
Duration≥2-3 years on stable dose
BiochemicalProlactin normalised on stable dose for ≥2 years
RadiologicalNo tumour visible on MRI (or microadenoma resolved)
ProcessTaper cabergoline over 3-6 months; Recheck Prolactin q3mo; Restart if recurrence

9. Pregnancy & Prolactinoma

ScenarioMicroprolactinomaMacroprolactinoma
Pre-conceptionStop cabergoline if Prolactin normal + MRI stableContinue cabergoline (risk of growth)
During PregnancyStop cabergoline; Monitor PRL q trimester; Visual fields if macroContinue cabergoline (Risk of growth >10%)
DeliveryVaginal preferred; Monitor BPC-section if chiasmal compression risk
PostpartumBreastfeeding → Prolactin ↑; Cabergoline suppresses lactationBreastfeeding → Cabergoline suppresses lactation
MonitoringVisual fields q trimester if macroMRI if new neuro symptoms

Key Point: Microprolactinoma → Stop cabergoline pre-conception; Macroprolactinoma → Continue cabergoline throughout pregnancy.

10. Stalk Effect / Disconnection Hyperprolactinaemia

FeatureDetails
MechanismPituitary stalk compression → ↓ Dopamine delivery to lactotrophs → Disinhibition
Prolactin LevelUsually <2000 mIU/L (rarely >3000)
MRINon-functioning macroadenoma compressing stalk / Suprasellar mass
ManagementTreat underlying mass (Surgery if macroadenoma); Prolactin normalises post-decompression
Key DifferentiatorVisual field defects common; Other pituitary deficits often coexist

11. Drug-Induced Hyperprolactinaemia

Drug ClassMechanismTypical Prolactin Level
Antipsychotics (Typical > Atypical)D2 Antagonism → DisinhibitionRisperidone > Olanzapine > Quetiapine > Clozapine > Aripiprazole (Low)
Metoclopramide / DomperidoneD2 Antagonism (GI motility)Moderate-High
Antidepressants (SSRIs, TCAs)Serotonergic modulationMild-Moderate
Opioidsμ-receptor → ↓ DopamineVariable
Verapamil / Alpha-methyldopaCentral D2 effectMild
OCPOestrogen → Lactotroph hyperplasiaMild

Management: Switch to lower-risk agent if possible; If essential → Monitor; Cabergoline if symptomatic and drug cannot be stopped.

12. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Prolactin RegulationDopamine (PIH) = Tonic inhibition; TRH = Stimulatory; Oestrogen = Stimulatory
Micro vs MacroMicro <10mm; Macro ≥10mm (Chiasmal compression risk)
Prolactin in MicroUsually 500-5000 mIU/L
Prolactin in Macro>5000 mIU/L (often >10,000)
MacroprolactinaemiaPEG precipitation; Recovery <60% = Macroprolactin; Benign, No treatment
Cabergoline 1st LineD2 Agonist; Start 0.25mg BD (Micro) / 0.5mg BD (Macro); Titrate
Cabergoline MaxMicro: 2mg BD; Macro: 3mg BD (Rarely >2mg)
WithdrawalAfter 2-3yr normal PRL + No tumour on MRI
Pregnancy + MicroStop cabergoline (Low growth risk)
Pregnancy + MacroContinue cabergoline (Growth risk >10%)
Stalk EffectNon-functioning macroadenoma → ↓ Dopamine delivery → Prolactin <2000
MacroprolactinaemiaPEG Precipitation; Recovery <60% = Macroprolactin; No treatment

13. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Prolactinoma vs Stalk EffectStalk effect: Non-functioning macroadenoma + prolactin <2000 + Visual fields + Other deficits
Macroprolactinaemia vs True HyperPRLPEG precipitation → Recovery <60% = Macro; True hyper = Monomeric PRL > monomeric upper limit
Cabergoline vs BromocriptineCabergoline preferred: Longer half-life, Better tolerability, Higher efficacy, Once/twice weekly
Prolactin in PregnancyPhysiological ↑ 10-20x; Not pathological; No treatment
Micro vs Macro ProlactinMicro: 500-5000; Macro: >5000 (often >10,000)
Cabergoline StopAfter 2-3yr normal PRL + MRI resolution
Pregnancy + MacroContinue cabergoline (Growth risk)
Pregnancy + MicroStop cabergoline (Low risk)

14. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Prolactin Axis))
    Physiology
      Lactotroph (15-20%)
      Dopamine (PIH) = Tonic inhibition
      TRH = Stimulatory
      Oestrogen = Stimulatory
      Suckling → ↑ PRL
    Hyperprolactinaemia
      Causes: Drugs, Hypothyroid, Renal, Pregnancy, Macroprolactin, Stalk Effect
      Drug causes: Antipsychotics > Metoclopramide > Antidepressants
    Macroprolactinaemia
      PRL-IgG complex; PEG precip; Recovery <60% = Macro
      Benign; No treatment
    Prolactinoma
      Micro <10mm (PRL 500-5000) → Cabergoline
      Macro ≥10mm (PRL >5000) → Cabergoline/Surgery
      Giant >40mm
    Cabergoline
      D2 Agonist; 0.25mg BD (Micro) / 0.5mg BD (Macro)
      Max: 2mg BD (Micro), 3mg BD (Macro)
      Withdrawal: 2-3yr normal PRL + No tumour
    Pregnancy
      Micro: Stop Cabergoline
      Macro: Continue Cabergoline (Growth risk)
    Stalk Effect
      Non-functioning macro → ↓ Dopamine delivery → PRL <2000
    Macroprolactinaemia
      PEG Precipitation; Recovery <60%
      Benign; No treatment

15. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Prolactin RegulationDopamine (PIH) = Tonic inhibition; TRH = Stimulatory; Oestrogen = Stimulatory
Micro vs MacroMicro <10mm; Macro ≥10mm (Chiasmal compression risk)
Prolactin in MicroUsually 500-5000 mIU/L
Prolactin in Macro>5000 mIU/L (often >10,000)
MacroprolactinaemiaPEG precipitation; Recovery <60% = Macroprolactin; Benign, No treatment
Cabergoline 1st LineD2 Agonist; Start 0.25mg BD (Micro) / 0.5mg BD (Macro); Titrate
Cabergoline MaxMicro: 2mg BD; Macro: 3mg BD
WithdrawalAfter 2-3yr normal PRL + No tumour on MRI
Pregnancy + MicroStop cabergoline (Low growth risk)
Pregnancy + MacroContinue cabergoline (Growth risk >10%)
Stalk EffectNon-functioning macroadenoma → ↓ Dopamine delivery → Prolactin <2000
MacroprolactinaemiaPEG precipitation; Recovery <60% = Macroprolactin; Benign, No treatment

16. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Prolactin Axis))
    Physiology
      Lactotroph (15-20%)
      Dopamine (PIH) = Tonic inhibition
      TRH = Stimulatory
      Oestrogen = Stimulatory
      Suckling → ↑ PRL
    Hyperprolactinaemia
      Causes: Drugs, Hypothyroid, Renal, Pregnancy, Macroprolactin, Stalk Effect
      Drug causes: Antipsychotics > Metoclopramide > Antidepressants
    Macroprolactinaemia
      PRL-IgG complex; PEG precip; Recovery <60% = Macro
      Benign; No treatment
    Prolactinoma
      Micro <10mm (PRL 500-5000) → Cabergoline
      Macro ≥10mm (PRL >5000) → Cabergoline/Surgery
      Giant >40mm
    Cabergoline
      D2 Agonist; 0.25mg BD (Micro) / 0.5mg BD (Macro)
      Max: 2mg BD (Micro), 3mg BD (Macro)
      Withdrawal: 2-3yr normal PRL + No tumour
    Pregnancy
      Micro: Stop Cabergoline
      Macro: Continue Cabergoline (Growth risk)
    Stalk Effect
      Non-functioning macro → ↓ Dopamine delivery → PRL <2000
    Macroprolactinaemia
      PEG Precipitation; Recovery <60%
      Benign; No treatment

17. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Hypothalamic-Pituitary Axis|Hypothalamic-Pituitary Axis]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Anterior Pituitary Hormones Overview]], [[Pituitary Adenomas: Functioning]], [[Pituitary Apoplexy]], [[Pregnancy & Endocrine]]

18. MCQs (10)

  1. Prolactin axis:

A. Hypothalamus (dopamine inhibits) -> Pituitary (lactotrophs secrete PRL) -> Target organs (breast)

B. Hypothalamus stimulates via TRH -> Prolactin

C. Prolactin inhibits own secretion

D. Prolactin stimulates ovulation

E. Prolactin inhibits GH

  1. Prolactin regulation:

A. Inhibited by dopamine (PIH); stimulated by TRH, VIP, oestrogen

B. Stimulated by dopamine

C. Inhibited by TRH

D. No feedback

E. Stimulated by GH

  1. Hyperprolactinaemia causes:

A. Prolactinoma, stalk effect, drugs (antipsychotics, metoclopramide, antiemetics), hypothyroidism, pregnancy, renal failure, chest wall trauma

B. Only prolactinoma

C. Only pregnancy

D. Only drugs

E. Only hypothyroidism

  1. Prolactinoma prolactin levels:

A. Microadenoma: 100-2000 mIU/L; Macroadenoma: >2000 mIU/L (often >5000)

B. Always <100

C. Always >5000

D. Inversely related to size

E. Normal in macroadenoma

  1. Prolactinoma diagnosis:

A. Prolactin >2000 + pituitary adenoma on MRI

B. Prolactin >100

C. Prolactin <100

D. Prolactin >5000 required

E. MRI only

  1. Prolactinoma treatment - cabergoline:

A. 0.25mg BD titrate to max 1-2mg/week; normalises prolactin; shrinks tumour 80-90%

B. Bromocriptine only

C. Surgery first line

D. Radiotherapy

E. Observation only

  1. Prolactinoma treatment - surgery:

A. Transsphenoidal if macroadenoma with visual loss / resistant to cabergoline / pregnant & intolerant

B. First line for all

C. Never indicated

D. Only for microadenomas

E. Radiotherapy preferred

  1. Prolactinoma in pregnancy:

A. Cabergoline stop at confirmation; monitor prolactin/visual fields; restart postpartum if needed

B. Continue cabergoline throughout

C. RAI

D. Surgery in 1st tri

E. Stop all meds

  1. Hyperprolactinaemia drug-induced:

A. Antipsychotics (risperidone), metoclopramide, domperidone, verapamil, oestrogens; stop/review drug

B. Never drug-induced

C. Only antipsychotics

D. Only metoclopramide

E. Only oestrogens

  1. Prolactin in stalk effect:

A. Mildly elevated (<100 mIU/L); due to dopamine disinhibition; distinguish from prolactinoma

B. Very high (>5000)

C. Normal

D. Only in pregnancy

E. Diagnostic of prolactinoma

19. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 35yo woman: amenorrhoea, galactorrhoea, prolactin 1500, MRI 6mm microadenoma. Treatment?

A. Cabergoline 0.25mg BD titrate

B. Surgery

C. RAI

D. Observation

E. Bromocriptine

  1. Same patient: macroadenoma 15mm, visual field defect. Urgent?

A. Surgery (decompression) -> then cabergoline

B. Cabergoline only

C. RAI

D. Observation

E. Radiotherapy

  1. 45yo man on risperidone: prolactin 800, no adenoma on MRI. Management?

A. Stop/switch antipsychotic; monitor; aripiprazole if needed

B. Cabergoline

C. Surgery

D. RAI

E. Observation

  1. Prolactinoma patient pregnant at 8wks on cabergoline. Management?

A. Stop cabergoline; monitor prolactin/visual fields; restart postpartum if needed

B. Continue cabergoline

C. RAI

D. Surgery

E. Stop all meds

  1. Prolactinoma post-partum: prolactin rising, visual fields normal. Management?

A. Restart cabergoline if macroadenoma; monitor

B. Surgery

C. RAI

D. Observation

E. Bromocriptine

20. Flashcards

  • Q: Prolactin regulation

A: Dopamine (PIH) inhibits; TRH/VIP/oestrogen stimulate

  • Q: Prolactinoma

A: Prolactin-secreting adenoma (~40% of functioning adenomas)

  • Q: Prolactin levels

A: Micro: 100-2000; Macro: >2000 (often >5000)

  • Q: Diagnosis

A: Prolactin >2000 + pituitary adenoma on MRI

  • Q: Cabergoline

A: 0.25mg BD titrate; normalise PRL; shrink 80-90%

  • Q: Surgery indication

A: Macroadenoma + visual loss / resistant / pregnant intolerant

  • Q: Pregnancy

A: Stop cabergoline; monitor; restart postpartum if macroadenoma

  • Q: Drug-induced

A: Antipsychotics, metoclopramide, oestrogens; stop drug

  • Q: Stalk effect

A: Prolactin <100; dopamine disinhibition; NOT prolactinoma

  • Q: Stalk compression

A: Prolactin usually <100-200; due to dopamine deficit

  • Q: Macroprolactin

A: High molecular weight; biologically inactive; PEG precipitation to exclude

  • Q: Bromocriptine

A: Ergot derivative; more side effects; 2nd line if cabergoline fails

21. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Hypothalamus (dopamine inhibits) -> Pituitary (lactotrophs secrete PRL) -> Target organs (breast) — Prolactin axis:
  1. Inhibited by dopamine (PIH); stimulated by TRH, VIP, oestrogen — Prolactin regulation:
  1. Prolactinoma, stalk effect, drugs (antipsychotics, metoclopramide, antiemetics), hypothyroidism, pregnancy, renal failure, chest wall trauma — Hyperprolactinaemia causes:
  1. Microadenoma: 100-2000 mIU/L; Macroadenoma: >2000 mIU/L (often >5000) — Prolactinoma prolactin levels:
  1. Prolactin >2000 + pituitary adenoma on MRI — Prolactinoma diagnosis:
  1. 0.25mg BD titrate to max 1-2mg/week; normalises prolactin; shrinks tumour 80-90% — Prolactinoma treatment - cabergoline:
  1. Transsphenoidal if macroadenoma with visual loss / resistant to cabergoline / pregnant & intolerant — Prolactinoma treatment - surgery:
  1. Cabergoline stop at confirmation; monitor prolactin/visual fields; restart postpartum if needed — Prolactinoma in pregnancy:
  1. Antipsychotics (risperidone), metoclopramide, domperidone, verapamil, oestrogens; stop/review drug — Hyperprolactinaemia drug-induced:
  1. Mildly elevated (<100 mIU/L); due to dopamine disinhibition; distinguish from prolactinoma — Prolactin in stalk effect:

SBAs

  1. Cabergoline 0.25mg BD titrate — 35yo woman: amenorrhoea, galactorrhoea, prolactin 1500, MRI 6mm microadenoma. Treatment?
  1. Surgery (decompression) -> then cabergoline — Same patient: macroadenoma 15mm, visual field defect. Urgent?
  1. Stop/switch antipsychotic; monitor; aripiprazole if needed — 45yo man on risperidone: prolactin 800, no adenoma on MRI. Management?
  1. Stop cabergoline; monitor prolactin/visual fields; restart postpartum if needed — Prolactinoma patient pregnant at 8wks on cabergoline. Management?
  1. Restart cabergoline if macroadenoma; monitor — Prolactinoma post-partum: prolactin rising, visual fields normal. Management?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.2.14 Tsh Thyroid Axis
[!info]
HPT axis = TRH → TSH → T4/T3. The most commonly tested endocrine axis in clinical practice. Mastering TSH/fT4/fT3 patterns, TRH physiology, and thyroid dysfunction patterns is essential for FCPS/MRCP.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Describe the HPT axis anatomy, hormone synthesis, and regulation
  • [ ] Interpret TSH/fT4/fT3 patterns for primary, central, and subclinical thyroid disease
  • [ ] Apply TRH stimulation and suppression tests
  • [ ] Distinguish hyperthyroidism, hypothyroidism, and subclinical variants
  • [ ] Manage thyroid disease in pregnancy

2. HPT Axis — Anatomy & Physiology

Hormone Synthesis & Secretion

StepHormoneSourceRegulation
1. TRHThyrotropin-Releasing HormoneHypothalamus (PVN)Cold, Stress ↑; T3/T4 (negative feedback); Somatostatin, Dopamine (inhibitory)
2. TSHThyroid-Stimulating HormoneAnterior Pituitary (Thyrotrophs)TRH (stimulatory); T3/T4 (negative feedback, mainly T3 at pituitary); Somatostatin, Dopamine (inhibitory)
3. T4/T3Thyroxine (T4) / Triiodothyronine (T3)Thyroid Follicular CellsTSH (stimulatory); Iodine availability; Negative feedback on TRH + TSH

Thyroid Hormone Synthesis (Follicular Cell)

Iodide Uptake (NIS) → Thyroglobulin Synthesis (RER) → Iodination (TPO + H₂O₂) → MIT + DIT
         ↓
Coupling (TPO) → T4 (2 DIT) / T3 (MIT+DIT) → Stored in Colloid
         ↓
TSH Stimulation → Endocytosis of Colloid → Lysosomal Proteolysis → T4/T3 Released
StepEnzyme/ProcessClinical Relevance
Iodide UptakeNIS (Sodium-Iodide Symporter)Perchlorate discharge test; NIS mutations → dyshormonogenesis
IodinationTPO (Thyroid Peroxidase)Anti-TPO antibodies (Hashimoto); PTU inhibits TPO
CouplingTPOTPO mutations → dyshormonogenesis
ProteolysisLysosomal enzymesIodine excess inhibits (Wolff-Chaikoff effect)
DeiodinationDIO1, DIO2 (activate T4→T3); DIO3 (inactivate)Selenium deficiency impairs DIOs; Amiodarone inhibits DIO1

Key Ratios: T4:T3 secretion ≈ 10:1; but T3 is 4x more potent. Peripheral conversion (DIO1/DIO2) provides ~80% of circulating T3.

3. HPT Axis Regulation & Secretion

Regulation

HormonePrimary StimulusPrimary InhibitorFeedback Level
TRHCold, StressT3/T4, Somatostatin, DopaminePituitary + Hypothalamus
TSHTRHT3/T4, Somatostatin, DopaminePituitary + Hypothalamus
T4/T3TSHIodine deficiency, Wolff-ChaikoffPituitary + Hypothalamus

Secretion Patterns

HormonePatternKey Features
TRHPulsatileCold exposure ↑; T3/T4 negative feedback
TSHPulsatile, Diurnal peak 02:00-04:00, nadir 17:00-18:00Sleep, Cold ↑; T3/T4 negative feedback
T4Stable, long half-life (5-7 days)Bound to TBG (99.97%), Albumin, Transthyretin
T3Shorter half-life (1 day)99.7% bound; 80% from peripheral T4→T3 conversion

4. Thyroid Function Test Interpretation — Pattern Recognition

Standard Patterns

PatternTSHfT4fT3Interpretation
Primary Hyperthyroidism↓↓ (suppressed)↑↑↑Graves, Toxic nodule, Thyroiditis
Primary Hypothyroidism↑↑↓↓/NHashimoto, Post-ablation, Iodine deficiency
Central Hypothyroidism↓/N (inappropriately low)↓↓↓Pituitary/Hypothalamic disease
Subclinical Hyperthyroidism↓↓NormalNormalEarly Graves, Toxic nodule, Excess thyroxine
Subclinical Hypothyroidism↑NormalNormalEarly Hashimoto
T3 Thyrotoxicosis↓↓Normal↑Early Graves, T3-secreting nodule
Sick Euthyroid SyndromeN/↓/↑N/↓↓Non-thyroidal illness

5. Diagnostic Patterns — Quick Reference

Primary Hyperthyroidism

FeatureDetails
TSHSuppressed (<0.01 mIU/L)
fT4/fT3Elevated
AetiologyGraves (TRAb+), Toxic Nodular Goitre, Thyroiditis, Drug-induced (Amiodarone)
TRAbPositive in Graves (>90%)

Primary Hypothyroidism

FeatureDetails
TSHElevated (>10 mIU/L = overt)
fT4Low
AetiologyHashimoto (Anti-TPO/TgAb+), Post-ablation, Iodine deficiency, Drug-induced
Anti-TPO/TgAbPositive in Hashimoto (>90%)

Central (Secondary/Tertiary) Hypothyroidism

FeatureDetails
TSHLow or Inappropriately Normal (not elevated despite low fT4)
fT4Low
AetiologyPituitary adenoma, Surgery, Radiotherapy, Infiltration, Sheehan syndrome

Subclinical Thyroid Disease

ConditionTSHfT4/fT3Management
Subclinical Hyperthyroidism<0.1 mIU/LNormalTreat if TSH <0.1 + Age>65/Cardiac risk/Osteoporosis
Subclinical Hypothyroidism4.5-10 mIU/LNormalTreat if TSH >10 or 5-10 + Ab+/Pregnant/Lipids/Symptoms

6. TRH Stimulation Test

ProtocolTRH 200-500µg IV → TSH at 0, 20, 40, 60, 90, 120min
HyperthyroidismBlunted TSH (suppressed)
Primary HypothyroidismExaggerated/Prolonged TSH rise
Central HypothyroidismBlunted/Delayed TSH
TSH-omaParadoxical Rise in TSH

7. Thyroxine (T4) vs Triiodothyronine (T3) — Key Differences

PropertyT4 (Thyroxine)T3 (Triiodothyronine)
Secretion Ratio10:1 (T4:T3)Minor direct secretion
Potency1x4x more potent
Half-life5-7 days1 day
Protein BindingTBG 99.97%, Albumin, TransthyretinTBG 99.7%, Albumin
Source90% direct thyroid secretion80% peripheral conversion (DIO1/DIO2)
ActivityProhormoneActive hormone

Clinical Pearl: T3 is the active hormone; T4 is a prohormone. In illness (NTIS), T4→T3 conversion impaired → Low T3, Normal/High T4, Normal/Low TSH.

8. Thyroid Function in Pregnancy

Physiological Changes

ChangeMechanismClinical Impact
↑ TBG (2-3x)Oestrogen ↑ TBG synthesis↑ Total T4/T3; fT4/fT3 reliable
hCG Cross-reactivityhCG = weak TSH agonistTSH ↓ 1st trimester (often <0.1)
Iodine Clearance↑ Renal iodine excretion↑ Iodine requirement (250 µg/day)
Placental TransferT4 crosses (limited); T3 crosses poorlyFetal brain depends on maternal T4 in 1st trimester

TSH Targets in Pregnancy

TrimesterTSH Target (mIU/L)Action
Pre-conception<2.5Optimise Levothyroxine
1st (0-12w)0.1-2.5↑ Dose ~30% at +ve test; Monitor q4w
2nd (13-26w)0.2-3.0Monitor q4-6w
3rd (27w+)0.3-3.0Monitor q4-6w; Plan postpartum reduction
PostpartumPre-pregnancy doseReduce to pre-pregnancy; Check TSH 6wk

9. Anti-Thyroid Antibodies

AntibodyTargetConditionsClinical Utility
Anti-TPOThyroid PeroxidaseHashimoto (>90%), Graves (80%), Postpartum thyroiditisDiagnosis of autoimmune thyroid disease
Anti-TgThyroglobulinHashimoto, Post-thyroidectomy monitoringTg monitoring after thyroid cancer
TRAb (TSHR-Ab)TSH Receptor (Stimulating)Graves Disease (>95%)Diagnosis of Graves; Predict neonatal thyrotoxicosis
TBIITSH Receptor (Binding/Blocking)Graves, Atrophic thyroiditisAlternative to TRAb

10. Thyroid Nodule — TI-RADS & Bethesda

TI-RADS (ACR) — Ultrasound Risk Stratification

TR LevelFeaturesMalignancy RiskAction
TR1Benign (cystic, spongiform)~0%No FNA
TR2Not suspicious<2%No FNA
TR3Mildly suspicious2-5%FNA if ≥2.5 cm
TR4Moderately suspicious5-20%FNA if ≥1.5 cm
TR5Highly suspicious>20%FNA if ≥1 cm

Bethesda System — FNA Cytology

CategoryDiagnosisMalignancy RiskManagement
INon-diagnostic—Repeat FNA
IIBenign0-3%Surveillance
IIIAUS/FLUS10-30%Repeat FNA / Molecular
IVFN/SFN15-30%Diagnostic Lobectomy / Molecular
VSuspicious60-75%Near-Total Thyroidectomy
VIMalignant97-99%Total Thyroidectomy ± RAI

11. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
HPT AxisTRH → TSH → T4/T3; Negative feedback mainly T3 on pituitary
Primary HypothyroidismTSH ↑↑, fT4 ↓ — Most common thyroid pattern
Central HypothyroidismTSH Low/Normal + fT4 Low — Think pituitary tumour
Primary HyperthyroidismTSH Suppressed + fT4/fT3 ↑
Subclinical HypothyroidismTSH 4.5-10, fT4 Normal; Treat if TSH >10 / Ab+ / Pregnant / Lipids
Subclinical HyperthyroidismTSH <0.1, fT4/fT3 Normal; Treat if TSH <0.1 + Age>65/Cardiac/Osteoporosis
TSH in Central HypoLow/Normal (inappropriately) — Think pituitary tumour
Pregnancy TSH Targets1st tri: 0.1-2.5; 2nd: 0.2-3.0; 3rd: 0.3-3.0 mIU/L
TSH in 1st TrimesterCan be <0.1 (hCG cross-reactivity) — Normal if fT4 normal
TRAbGraves = Positive (>95%); Predicts neonatal thyrotoxicosis
Anti-TPOHashimoto = Positive (>90%); Not diagnostic alone
Central HypothyroidismTSH Low/Normal + fT4 Low — Think pituitary tumour
Sick EuthyroidLow T3, Normal/High T4, Normal/Low TSH — Do not treat
T3 ThyrotoxicosisTSH Suppressed, fT4 Normal, fT3 ↑
TSH Suppression Test (Historical)Obsolete — Replaced by TRH/TRAb/RAI scan
TSH InhibitorsSomatostatin, Dopamine, Glucocorticoids, High-dose Biotin (assay interference)
TSH StimulatorsTRH, Cold, Primary Hypothyroidism, Leptin, Metoclopramide

12. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
TSH ↑ = Hypothyroidism?YES if fT4 low (Primary). NO if fT4 also low → Central (TSH inappropriately normal)
TSH Suppressed = Hyperthyroidism?YES if fT4/fT3 high. NO if fT4/fT3 normal → Subclinical
Central vs Primary HypoCentral: TSH Low/Normal + fT4 Low; Primary: TSH High + fT4 Low
