15. MCQs (10)

  1. Thyroid nodule evaluation first step:

A. TSH + thyroid ultrasound

B. FNA immediately

C. RAI scan

D. CT neck

E. MRI neck

  1. TI-RADS TR5 features:

A. Highly suspicious: solid, hypoechoic, irregular margins, microcalcifications, taller-than-wide

B. Purely cystic

C. Spongiform

D. Isoechoic

E. Completely isoechoic

  1. Bethesda category III (AUS/FLUS):

A. Repeat FNA or molecular testing; malignancy risk 10-30%

B. Benign

C. Malignant

D. Non-diagnostic

E. Surgery indicated

  1. Bethesda category V (suspicious):

A. Malignancy risk 60-75%; surgery (near-total thyroidectomy)

B. Repeat FNA

C. Observe

D. RAI ablation

E. Molecular testing only

  1. Bethesda category VI (malignant):

A. Malignancy risk 97-99%; surgery (total thyroidectomy ± RAI)

B. Repeat FNA

C. Observe

D. RAI only

E. Molecular testing only

  1. TI-RADS TR3 nodule size for FNA:

A. >=2.5 cm

B. >=1.5 cm

C. >=1 cm

D. Any size

E. Never FNA

  1. TI-RADS TR4 nodule size for FNA:

A. >=1.5 cm

B. >=2.5 cm

C. >=1 cm

D. Any size

E. Never FNA

  1. TI-RADS TR5 nodule size for FNA:

A. >=1 cm

B. >=1.5 cm

C. >=2.5 cm

D. Any size

E. Never FNA

  1. Molecular testing for Bethesda III/IV:

A. Afirma GSC, Thyroseq v3; rule in/out malignancy

B. Not available

C. Only for Bethesda I

D. Replaces surgery

E. Not recommended

  1. Thyroid nodule with TSH suppressed:

A. Toxic nodule likely; RAI scan -> focal hot nodule = toxic adenoma

B. Graves disease

C. Thyroiditis

D. Factitious

E. Non-functioning

16. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 45yo woman: 2cm thyroid nodule, TSH normal, TI-RADS 4. Management?

A. FNA (TR4 >=1.5cm)

B. Observation

C. RAI scan

D. Surgery

E. Repeat US in 1yr

  1. Same patient: FNA Bethesda IV (FN/SFN). Next?

A. Diagnostic lobectomy or molecular testing

B. Total thyroidectomy

C. Observation

D. RAI

E. Repeat FNA

  1. 50yo man: 1.2cm nodule, TI-RADS 5. Management?

A. FNA (TR5 >=1cm)

B. Observation

C. Surgery without FNA

D. RAI

E. Repeat US

  1. 30yo woman: 3cm nodule, Bethesda VI. Management?

A. Total thyroidectomy ± RAI

B. Lobectomy

C. Observation

D. RAI

E. Repeat FNA

  1. Indeterminate Bethesda III: molecular testing Afirma GSC negative. Next?

A. Observation (NPV ~95%)

B. Surgery

C. Repeat FNA

D. RAI

E. Repeat molecular

17. Flashcards

  • Q: Thyroid nodule workup

A: TSH -> US (TI-RADS) -> FNA (Bethesda)

  • Q: TI-RADS TR1

A: Benign (cystic, spongiform); ~0% risk; no FNA

  • Q: TI-RADS TR2

A: Not suspicious; <2% risk; no FNA

  • Q: TI-RADS TR3

A: Mildly suspicious; 2-5% risk; FNA if >=2.5cm

  • Q: TI-RADS TR4

A: Moderately suspicious; 5-20% risk; FNA if >=1.5cm

  • Q: TI-RADS TR5

A: Highly suspicious; >20% risk; FNA if >=1cm

  • Q: Bethesda I

A: Non-diagnostic; repeat FNA

  • Q: Bethesda II

A: Benign; 0-3% risk; surveillance

  • Q: Bethesda III

A: AUS/FLUS; 10-30% risk; repeat FNA/molecular

  • Q: Bethesda IV

A: FN/SFN; 15-30% risk; lobectomy/molecular

  • Q: Bethesda V

A: Suspicious; 60-75% risk; near-total thyroidectomy

  • Q: Bethesda VI

A: Malignant; 97-99% risk; total thyroidectomy ± RAI

  • Q: Molecular testing

A: Afirma GSC (rule out), Thyroseq v3 (rule in)

  • Q: Toxic nodule

A: TSH suppressed + focal hot nodule on RAI scan

18. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. TSH + thyroid ultrasound — Thyroid nodule evaluation first step:
  1. Highly suspicious: solid, hypoechoic, irregular margins, microcalcifications, taller-than-wide — TI-RADS TR5 features:
  1. Repeat FNA or molecular testing; malignancy risk 10-30% — Bethesda category III (AUS/FLUS):
  1. Malignancy risk 60-75%; surgery (near-total thyroidectomy) — Bethesda category V (suspicious):
  1. Malignancy risk 97-99%; surgery (total thyroidectomy ± RAI) — Bethesda category VI (malignant):
  1. >=2.5 cm — TI-RADS TR3 nodule size for FNA:
  1. >=1.5 cm — TI-RADS TR4 nodule size for FNA:
  1. >=1 cm — TI-RADS TR5 nodule size for FNA:
  1. Afirma GSC, Thyroseq v3; rule in/out malignancy — Molecular testing for Bethesda III/IV:
  1. Toxic nodule likely; RAI scan -> focal hot nodule = toxic adenoma — Thyroid nodule with TSH suppressed:

SBAs

  1. FNA (TR4 >=1.5cm) — 45yo woman: 2cm thyroid nodule, TSH normal, TI-RADS 4. Management?
  1. Diagnostic lobectomy or molecular testing — Same patient: FNA Bethesda IV (FN/SFN). Next?
  1. FNA (TR5 >=1cm) — 50yo man: 1.2cm nodule, TI-RADS 5. Management?
  1. Total thyroidectomy ± RAI — 30yo woman: 3cm nodule, Bethesda VI. Management?
  1. Observation (NPV ~95%) — Indeterminate Bethesda III: molecular testing Afirma GSC negative. Next?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.3.16 Thyroid Storm
[!info]
Thyroid Storm = Life-Threatening Exacerbation of Thyrotoxicosis. Medical Emergency with Mortality 10-30%. Burch-Wartofsky Score ≥45 = Diagnostic. Immediate Multimodal Therapy: PTU + Propranolol + Iodine + Steroids + Supportive Care.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Diagnose thyroid storm using Burch-Wartofsky scoring system
  • [ ] Execute immediate multimodal management protocol
  • [ ] Identify precipitating factors and prevent recurrence
  • [ ] Differentiate from severe thyrotoxicosis and other causes of hyperthermia/tachycardia

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetails
DefinitionAcute, Life-Threatening Exacerbation of Thyrotoxicosis with Multiorgan Dysfunction
Incidence0.5-1 per 100,000/year (Rare)
Mortality10-30% (Despite Modern Therapy)
Precipitating FactorsInfection (40%), Surgery, Trauma, Iodine Load, DKA, Stroke, Parturition, Drug Withdrawal, Thyroid Storm in Pregnancy

3. Burch-Wartofsky Score (Diagnostic Standard)

ParameterPoints
Temperature (°C)37.2-37.7: 5; 37.8-38.3: 10; 38.4-38.8: 15; 38.9-39.4: 20; 39.5-39.9: 25; ≥40: 30
Central Nervous SystemAbsent: 0; Mild (Agitation): 10; Moderate (Delirium): 20; Severe (Seizure/Coma): 30
GastrointestinalAbsent: 0; Moderate (Nausea/Vomiting): 10; Severe (Diarrhoea/Abdo Pain): 20
CardiacHR 90-109: 5; 110-119: 10; 120-129: 15; ≥130: 20; AF: 10; HF: 10
PrecipitantAbsent: 0; Present: 10
ScoreInterpretation
<25Unlikely
25-44Impending / Probable
≥45Diagnostic

4. Clinical Presentation

Cardinal Features

SystemFindings
ThermoregulationHyperpyrexia (39-41°C), Profuse Sweating
CardiovascularTachycardia (≥130 bpm), Atrial Fibrillation (15-20%), High-Output Heart Failure, Shock
Central Nervous SystemAgitation → Delirium → Coma, Seizures, Tremor
GastrointestinalNausea, Vomiting, Diarrhoea, Abdominal Pain, Jaundice (Cholestasis)
HepaticJaundice (Cholestatic), ↑ Transaminases
HaematologicalLeukocytosis, Thrombocytopenia, DIC

Precipitating Factors (The "I PREPARE" Mnemonic)

LetterFactor
IInfection (Pneumonia, UTI, Sepsis) — Commonest
PPregnancy / Postpartum / Parturition
RRadiation (Iodine-131), Radiocontrast, Refractory thyrotoxicosis
EExternal Trauma / Endocrine Surgery
PPostpartum, Pulmonary Embolism
AAcute Illness (MI, Stroke, DKA)
RRefractory (Non-Adherence to ATDs)
EEmotional Stress / Exercise

5. Diagnostic Criteria — Burch-Wartofsky Score ≥45

Score RangeInterpretation
<25Unlikely
25-44Impending / Probable
≥45Diagnostic of Thyroid Storm

Differential Diagnosis

ConditionKey Differentiators
Severe ThyrotoxicosisBWS <45; No Multiorgan Failure
SepsisFever, Tachycardia; Normal TSH/fT4; Positive Cultures
Malignant HyperthermiaAnaesthesia Trigger; Rigid Muscles, ↑ CK
Neuroleptic Malignant SyndromeDopamine Antagonist Exposure; Lead-Pipe Rigidity, ↑ CK
Serotonin SyndromeSerotonergic Drugs; Clonus, Hyperreflexia, Diarrhoea
Heat StrokeEnvironmental Heat Exposure; Anhidrosis
Adrenal CrisisHypotension, Hyponatraemia, Hyperkalaemia; Normal TSH/fT4

6. Emergency Management Protocol (First 60 Minutes)

THYROID STORM SUSPECTED (BWS ≥45)
         │
         ▼
1. ABCDE + ICU ADMISSION
         │
         ├── AIRWAY / BREATHING: Intubate if GCS <8 / Respiratory Failure
         ├── CIRCULATION: **Hydrocortisone 100mg IV STAT** + **Fluid Resuscitation**
         └── DISABILITY: GCS, Pupils, Neurological Monitoring
         │
         ▼
2. ANTITHYROID THERAPY (IMMEDIATE)
         │
         ├── **PTU 600mg LOADING DOSE** (Then 200mg Q6H) — **Preferred** (Blocks Peripheral T4→T3)
         │   └── If PTU Unavailable → Carbimazole 40-60mg Load → 20mg Q6H
         │
         ├── **BETA-BLOCKER** — **Propranolol 40-80mg Q6H** (IV if Needed)
         │       Contraindicated: Asthma, Severe COPD, Bradycardia, Shock
         │       Alternative: Esmolol IV Infusion (Titratable)
         │
         ├── **IODINE** — **1 Hour AFTER PTU** (Lugol's Iodine 8 Drops Q6H / SSKI 5 Drops Q6H)
         │       **Purpose**: Blocks Thyroid Hormone Release
         │       **CaUTION**: Give **AFTER** PTU (Else ↑ Hormone Synthesis)
         │
         └── **GLUCOCORTICOIDS** — **Hydrocortisone 100mg IV Q8H** (or Dexamethasone 4mg Q6H)
                 **Purpose**: Blocks Peripheral T4→T3 Conversion; Adrenal Support; ↓ Cytokines
         │
         ▼
3. SUPPORTIVE CARE
         │
         ├── **FLUIDS**: 1-2L 0.9% NaCl 1st Hour → 200-300mL/hr (Target UOP >0.5 mL/kg/h)
         ├── **COOLING**: External (Ice Packs, Cooling Blanket); **NO ACTIVE REWARMING** in Coma
         ├── **GLUCOSE**: 10% Dextrose if Hypoglycaemic; Monitor CBG q1-2h
         ├── **ELECTROLYTES**: K⁺, Mg²⁺, Phosphate q4-6h; Correct Hypokalaemia
         ├── **CARDIAC**: Propranolol (Rate Control); Digoxin if AF + HF; Amiodarone if Refractory AF
         ├── **RESPIRATORY**: O₂; Intubate if GCS<8 / pCO₂>6.5 kPa
         ├── **ANTICOAGULATION**: Heparin (If AF + Risk Factors / Immobilised)
         └── **TREAT PRECIPITANT**: **Antibiotics** (Sepsis), **Surgery** (If Indicated), **Insulin/Dextrose** (DKA), **Stop ATDs** (If Non-Adherence)

7. Drug Details & Dosing

DrugDoseRouteKey Points
PTU600mg Load → 200mg Q6HPO/NGTBlocks T4→T3; Preferred in Storm
Carbimazole40-60mg Load → 20mg Q6HPO/NGTIf PTU Unavailable; Less T4→T3 Block
Propranolol40-80mg Q6HPO/IVNon-Selective β-Blocker; Avoid if Asthma/Shock
Esmolol50-200µg/kg/min IV InfusionIVTitratable; Short t½; Safe in Asthma
Lugol's Iodine8 Drops (0.1mL) Q6HPOGive 1h AFTER PTU; Blocks Hormone Release
SSKI5 Drops Q6HPOAlternative to Lugol's
Hydrocortisone100mg IV Q8HIVBlocks T4→T3; Adrenal Support
Dexamethasone4mg IV Q6HIVAlternative to Hydrocortisone
Propranolol40-80mg Q6HPO/IVNon-Selective β-Blocker; Avoid Asthma
Esmolol50-200µg/kg/min IVIVTitratable; Safe in Asthma

8. Supportive Care & Monitoring

SystemInterventionTarget
Fluids1-2L 0.9% NaCl 1st Hour → 200-300mL/hrUOP >0.5 mL/kg/h; CVP 8-12 cmH₂O
CoolingPassive Only (Ice Packs, Cooling Blanket, Fan)Target ↓ 0.5-1°C/h; NO Active External Rewarming
Glucose10% Dextrose if HypoglycaemicCBG >4 mmol/L
ElectrolytesK⁺, Mg²⁺, Phosphate q4-6h; ReplaceK⁺ 4.0-5.0; Mg²⁺ >0.7
AnticoagulationLMWH (If AF + CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2 / Immobilised)
NutritionEnteral Preferred; High Calorie, High ProteinNGT if GCS<12
Bowel RegimenLaxatives if Constipated (Constipation Common)

9. Precipitant Management

PrecipitantSpecific Management
Infection/SepsisEmpiric Antibiotics (Blood/Urine/Sputum Cx → Targeted)
Surgery/TraumaStabilise → Surgery After Stabilisation
DKAInsulin Infusion + Fluids + K⁺ Replacement
StrokeNeurology Review; Thrombolysis/Thrombectomy if Indicated
Iodine LoadStop Iodine Source; Perchlorate (If Recent)
Drug WithdrawalRestart ATDs; Bridge with IV PTU
Pregnancy/PostpartumPTU Preferred; Multidisciplinary (OB/Gyn + Endo + ICU)

10. Monitoring & Endpoints

ParameterFrequencyTarget
Core TemperatureContinuous↓ 0.5-1°C/hr; Target <38°C
Heart RateContinuous<100 bpm (Sinus Rhythm)
BP / CVPq15min (1st hr) → q1hMAP >65 mmHg; CVP 8-12
TemperatureContinuous / q15min↓ 0.5-1°C/hr; Target <38°C
GCS / Neuroq15-30minImprovement
Urine OutputHourly>0.5 mL/kg/h
Labsq4-6hNa⁺, K⁺, Glucose, U&E, LFTs, Coag, ABG, CBC
Thyroid FunctionDaily TSH, fT4, fT3fT4/fT3 Normalisation

11. Resolution & Transition to Maintenance

MilestoneAction
Afebrile 48hTaper Cooling
Hemodynamic StableWean Vasopressors; Switch Oral β-Blocker
Mental Status NormalStop Sedation; Assess Neuro
Thyroid Function ImprovingTaper PTU → Switch to Carbimazole (Oral)
StabilisedTransition to Oral Maintenance; Plan Definitive Therapy (RAI/Surgery)

12. Complications & Prognosis

ComplicationIncidenceManagement
Cardiac Arrhythmias20-30%β-Blocker, Digoxin, Amiodarone, Cardioversion
Heart Failure20%Diuretics, ACEi, Inotropes (Dobutamine)
Respiratory Failure15%Mechanical Ventilation
DIC5-10%FFP, Platelets, Cryoprecipitate, Heparin
Hepatic Failure10-15%Supportive; NAC
Renal Failure10%CRRT if Severe

Mortality: 10-30% Despite Modern Therapy (Higher if Delayed Treatment, Age >60, Comorbidities).

13. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
BWS Score ≥45Diagnostic for Thyroid Storm
BWS <25Unlikely
Immediate DrugsPTU 600mg + Propranolol 80mg + Iodine + Hydrocortisone 100mg IV
PTU vs CarbimazolePTU Preferred (Blocks T4→T3 Conversion)
Iodine TimingGive 1 Hour AFTER PTU (Else ↑ Hormone Synthesis)
β-Blocker ChoicePropranolol (Non-Selective); Esmolol if Asthma/Shock
SteroidHydrocortisone 100mg IV (Blocks T4→T3 + Adrenal Support)
CoolingPassive Only (Active Rewarming → Vasodilatation → Shock)
BWS ≥45Diagnostic; 25-44 = Probable; <25 = Unlikely
PrecipitantInfection (30-40%) Most Common
AF in StormPropranolol/Esmolol; Digoxin if HF; Cardioversion if Unstable
AnticoagulationHeparin if AF + Risk Factors
Thyroid Storm vs SepsisStorm: ↑ T3/T4, Suppressed TSH; Sepsis: Normal TSH, ↑ Lactate, Cultures +
Thyroid Storm in PregnancyPTU Preferred; Multidisciplinary (OB + Endo + ICU)
Mortality10-30% (Despite Optimal Care)
PTU HepatotoxicityMonitor LFTs q2-3d; Switch to Carbimazole if ALT >3x ULN

14. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Thyroid Storm vs Severe ThyrotoxicosisStorm = BWS ≥45 + Multiorgan Dysfunction; Thyrotoxicosis = BWS <45
PTU vs CarbimazolePTU = Blocks T4→T3 (Preferred); Carbimazole = Only Synthesis Inhibition
Iodine TimingGive AFTER PTU (1 Hour); Before PTU → ↑ Hormone Synthesis (Jod-Basedow)
β-Blocker ChoicePropranolol (Non-Selective); Esmolol if Asthma/Shock (Titratable)
Hydrocortisone vs DexamethasoneHydrocortisone = Physiological Replacement + T4→T3 Block; Dex = Anti-inflammatory
Cooling MethodPassive Only (Ice Packs, Cooling Blanket); NO Active Rewarming
Anticoagulation in AFHeparin/LMWH if CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2 / Immobilised
PTU HepatotoxicityMonitor LFTs q2-3 Days; Switch to Carbimazole if ALT >3x ULN
BWS ScoringTemp (°C) + CNS + GI + Cardiac + Precipitant = Score
BWS ≥45Diagnostic (Specificity >90%)

15. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Thyroid Storm))
    Definition
      Life-Threatening Thyrotoxicosis
      Multiorgan Failure
      Mortality 10-30%
    BWS Score
      Temp + CNS + GI + Cardiac + Precipitant
      ≥45 = Diagnostic
      25-44 = Probable
      <25 = Unlikely
    Precipitants (I PREPARE)
      Infection (Commonest)
      Pregnancy/Postpartum
      Radiation/Iodine/Refractory
      External Trauma/Surgery
      Postpartum/PE/Postpartum
      Acute Illness (MI/Stroke/DKA)
      Refractory/Non-Adherence
      Emotional Stress/Exercise
    Emergency Rx
      PTU 600mg Load → 200mg q6h
      Propranolol 80mg q6h (+Esmolol)
      Iodine (After PTU) 8 Drops q6h
      Hydrocortisone 100mg IV q8h
      Fluids + Cooling + β-Blocker
    Precipitants
      Infection (Commonest)
      Surgery/Trauma
      Parturition
      Iodine Load
      Non-Adherence
      DKA/MI/Stroke
    Supportive
      Fluids 1-2L NaCl 1hr
      Passive Cooling
      Glucose Monitor
      Anticoagulation (AF)
      Treat Precipitant
    Complications
      Arrhythmias, HF, DIC, Hepatic/Renal Failure
      Mortality 10-30%

16. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
BWS ≥45Diagnostic for Thyroid Storm
BWS 25-44Probable / Impending
PTU vs CarbimazolePTU 1st Line (Blocks T4→T3)
Iodine TimingAfter PTU (1 Hour Later)
β-BlockerPropranolol 1st Line; Esmolol if Asthma/Shock
SteroidsHydrocortisone 100mg IV q8h (Blocks T4→T3 + Adrenal Support)
CoolingPassive Only (No Active Rewarming)
PrecipitantsInfection (Commonest), Surgery, Iodine, DKA, Trauma, Non-Compliance
AnticoagulationHeparin if AF + Risk Factors
Mortality10-30% (Despite Treatment)
PTU Dose600mg Load → 200mg Q6H
Propranolol80mg Q6H; Esmolol if Asthma/Shock
IodineLugol's 8 Drops Q6H (1h After PTU)
Hydrocortisone100mg IV q8h (Blocks T4→T3 + Adrenal Support)
CoolingPassive Only (Active Rewarming = Harm)
BWS <25Unlikely Thyroid Storm

18. MCQs (10)

  1. Thyroid storm diagnosis:

A. Burch-Wartofsky Score >=45

B. TSH <0.01 only

C. fT4 >50 only

D. Tachycardia only

E. Fever only

  1. BWS temperature scoring:

A. 37.2-37.7: 5; 37.8-38.3: 10; 38.4-38.8: 15; 38.9-39.4: 20; 39.5-39.9: 25; >=40: 30

B. Only fever >38

C. Only fever >39

D. Only fever >40

E. No scoring

  1. First-line antithyroid drug in thyroid storm:

A. PTU (blocks T4->T3 conversion)

B. Carbimazole

C. RAI

D. Propranolol only

E. Iodine only

  1. Iodine timing in thyroid storm:

A. Give 1 hour AFTER PTU (blocks release)

B. Before PTU

C. Simultaneously with PTU

D. Never give iodine

E. Only if PTU unavailable

  1. Beta-blocker of choice in thyroid storm:

A. Propranolol (non-selective)

B. Atenolol

C. Bisoprolol

D. Metoprolol

E. No beta-blocker

  1. Hydrocortisone in thyroid storm:

A. 100mg IV q8h (blocks T4->T3 + adrenal support)

B. Not needed

C. Dexamethasone preferred

D. Oral only

E. After thyroid storm resolves

  1. BWS <25:

A. Unlikely thyroid storm

B. Diagnostic

C. Impending

D. Probable

E. Severe

  1. Commonest precipitant:

A. Infection (30-40%)

B. Surgery

C. Trauma

D. Iodine load

E. Non-compliance

  1. Thyroid storm in pregnancy:

A. PTU preferred; multidisciplinary (OB+Endo+ICU)

B. Carbimazole

C. RAI

D. Surgery

E. Propranolol only

  1. Thyroid storm mortality:

A. 10-30% despite treatment

B. <1%

C. 50%

D. >50%

E. 0%

19. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 35yo woman: fever 40°C, HR 140, delirium, TSH <0.01, fT4 80. BWS?

A. >=45 (diagnostic thyroid storm)

B. <25

C. 25-44

D. Exactly 45

E. Cannot calculate

  1. Same patient: immediate drugs?

A. PTU 600mg + Propranolol 80mg + Iodine (after PTU) + Hydrocortisone 100mg IV

B. Carbimazole only

C. RAI

D. Surgery

E. Propranolol only

  1. Thyroid storm with asthma: beta-blocker?

A. Esmolol IV (cardioselective, titratable)

B. Propranolol

C. Atenolol

D. No beta-blocker

E. Labetalol

  1. Thyroid storm: Iodine given before PTU. Risk?

A. Increased hormone synthesis (Jod-Basedow)

B. No risk

C. Better efficacy

D. No effect

E. Allergy only

  1. Post-thyroid storm: cooling method?

A. Passive only (ice packs, blanket); NO active rewarming

B. Active rewarming

C. Ice water immersion

D. Heating blanket

E. Antipyretics only

20. Flashcards

  • Q: Thyroid storm

A: BWS >=45 = diagnostic; PTU + Propranolol + Iodine (after PTU) + Hydrocortisone

  • Q: BWS scoring

A: Temp + CNS + GI + Cardiac + Precipitant

  • Q: PTU preferred

A: Blocks T4->T3 conversion; 600mg load -> 200mg q6h

  • Q: Iodine timing

A: 1 hour AFTER PTU (else Jod-Basedow)

  • Q: Beta-blocker

A: Propranolol (non-selective); Esmolol if asthma/shock

  • Q: Steroids

A: Hydrocortisone 100mg IV q8h (blocks T4->T3 + adrenal support)

  • Q: Cooling

A: Passive ONLY (active rewarming = harm)

  • Q: Precipitants

A: Infection (commonest), surgery, iodine, DKA, trauma, non-compliance

  • Q: Anticoagulation

A: Heparin if AF + risk factors

  • Q: Mortality

A: 10-30% despite treatment

21. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Burch-Wartofsky Score >=45 — Thyroid storm diagnosis:
  1. 37.2-37.7: 5; 37.8-38.3: 10; 38.4-38.8: 15; 38.9-39.4: 20; 39.5-39.9: 25; >=40: 30 — BWS temperature scoring:
  1. PTU (blocks T4->T3 conversion) — First-line antithyroid drug in thyroid storm:
  1. Give 1 hour AFTER PTU (blocks release) — Iodine timing in thyroid storm:
  1. Propranolol (non-selective) — Beta-blocker of choice in thyroid storm:
  1. 100mg IV q8h (blocks T4->T3 + adrenal support) — Hydrocortisone in thyroid storm:
  1. Unlikely thyroid storm — BWS <25:
  1. Infection (30-40%) — Commonest precipitant:
  1. PTU preferred; multidisciplinary (OB+Endo+ICU) — Thyroid storm in pregnancy:
  1. 10-30% despite treatment — Thyroid storm mortality:

SBAs

  1. >=45 (diagnostic thyroid storm) — 35yo woman: fever 40°C, HR 140, delirium, TSH <0.01, fT4 80. BWS?
  1. PTU 600mg + Propranolol 80mg + Iodine (after PTU) + Hydrocortisone 100mg IV — Same patient: immediate drugs?
  1. Esmolol IV (cardioselective, titratable) — Thyroid storm with asthma: beta-blocker?
  1. Increased hormone synthesis (Jod-Basedow) — Thyroid storm: Iodine given before PTU. Risk?
  1. Passive only (ice packs, blanket); NO active rewarming — Post-thyroid storm: cooling method?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.3.17 Thyroid In Pregnancy
[!info]
Thyroid Disease in Pregnancy = Unique Physiological Changes + Specific Management. Trimester-Specific TSH Targets (1st: 0.1-2.5, 2nd: 0.2-3.0, 3rd: 0.3-3.0). Levothyroxine Dose ↑ 30% at Positive Pregnancy Test. Autoimmune diseases (Graves, Hashimoto) require special management.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] State trimester-specific TSH targets and adjust levothyroxine accordingly
  • [ ] Manage hypothyroidism, hyperthyroidism, and thyroid nodules in pregnancy
  • [ ] Monitor and manage Graves disease, postpartum thyroiditis, and thyroid nodules
  • [ ] Advise on breastfeeding, neonatal monitoring, and contraception

2. Physiological Changes in Pregnancy

ChangeMechanismClinical Impact
↑ TBG (2-3x)Oestrogen ↑ TBG Synthesis↑ Total T4/T3 (2-3x); Free T4/T3 Reliable
hCG Cross-ReactivityhCG → Weak TSH AgonistTSH ↓ 1st Trimester (Often <0.1-0.4 mIU/L)
Iodine Clearance↑ Renal Iodine Excretion↑ Iodine Requirement (250 µg/day)
Placental TransferT4 Crosses (Limited); T3 Crosses PoorlyFetal Brain Depends on Maternal T4 (1st Tri)
HCG Peak8-12 WeeksTSH Nadir (Often <0.1 in 1st Tri)

3. Trimester-Specific TSH Targets

TrimesterTSH Reference Range (mIU/L)Management Target
Pre-conception<2.5Optimise Levothyroxine Dose Pre-Conception
1st Trimester (0-12w)0.1 - 2.5↑ Dose ~30% at +ve Test; Monitor q4wk
2nd Trimester (13-26w)0.2 - 3.0Monitor q4-6wk
3rd Trimester (27w+)0.3 - 3.0Monitor q4-6wk; Plan Postpartum Dose Reduction
PostpartumPre-pregnancy DoseReduce to Pre-Pregnancy Dose; Check TSH 6wk

Key: Increase Levothyroxine ~30% at Positive Pregnancy Test (Immediate); Monitor q4wk in 1st Tri, q4-6wk thereafter.

4. Hypothyroidism in Pregnancy

Overt Hypothyroidism (Pre-existing)

AspectManagement
Pre-conceptionTSH <2.5; Optimise Levothyroxine; Screen TPOAb
DiagnosisTSH > Trimester-Specific Upper Limit + Low fT4
Levothyroxine Adjustment↑ Dose ~30% at +ve Test (Immediate); Titrate to Trimester Targets
MonitoringTSH + fT4 q4wk (1st Tri), q4-6wk (2nd/3rd Tri)
PostpartumReduce to Pre-Pregnancy Dose; Check TSH at 6wk

Subclinical Hypothyroidism (SCH) in Pregnancy

ScenarioAction
TPOAb+ with TSH >2.5 (1st Tri) / >3.0 (2nd/3rd)Treat (Levothyroxine 50-75µg)
TPOAb- with TSH >4.0 (1st) / >3.0 (2nd/3rd)Consider Treatment
TPOAb+ EuthyroidMonitor TSH q4-6wk; ↑ Dose if TSH >2.5 (1st) / >3.0 (2nd/3rd)

5. Hyperthyroidism in Pregnancy

Graves Disease

TrimesterPreferred ATDDoseKey Points
1st TrimesterPTU 50-150mg/dayLowest Effective DoseAvoid Carbimazole (Teratogenicity: Aplasia Cutis)
2nd/3rd TrimesterSwitch to Carbimazole (or Continue PTU)15-40mg/dayPTU Hepatotoxicity Risk ↑ in 2nd/3rd Tri
TargetLowest Effective Dose; fT4 Upper Normal Range

Monitoring

ParameterFrequency
TSH + fT4q4wk (1st Tri), q4-6wk (2nd/3rd)
TRAbEach Trimester; TRAb >5 IU/L → ↑ Neonatal Risk
Fetal USIf TRAb >5 IU/L: Fetal Goitre, Tachycardia, Hydrops

Neonatal Thyrotoxicosis

Risk FactorAction
Maternal TRAb >5 IU/LFetal US (Goitre, Tachycardia, Hydrops)
DeliveryVaginal Preferred; Neonate Monitored for Thyrotoxicosis (Tachycardia, Irritability, Poor Feeding)
BreastfeedingCarbimazole/PTU Compatible (Low Milk Transfer)

6. Hypothyroidism in Pregnancy — Risks

MaternalFetal
Miscarriage, Pre-eclampsia, Placental Abruption, PPH, AnaemiaPreterm Birth, Low Birth Weight, Impaired Neurodevelopment (IQ ↓7-10 pts), Stillbirth

Key: Early Treatment Prevents Neurodevelopmental Deficits.

7. Thyroxine Dose Adjustment in Pregnancy

StepAction
Pre-conceptionOptimise Dose (TSH <2.5); Stop if TSH <2.5
+ve Pregnancy Test↑ Dose ~30% Immediately (e.g., 100µg → 130µg)
1st TrimesterMonitor TSH q4wk; Target 0.1-2.5
2nd TrimesterTarget 0.2-3.0; Monitor q4-6wk
3rd TrimesterTarget 0.3-3.0; Monitor q4-6wk
PostpartumReduce to Pre-Pregnancy Dose; Check TSH at 6wk

8. Hyperthyroidism in Pregnancy

Treatment Algorithm

TrimesterDrugDoseKey Points
1st TrimesterPTU (Preferred)50-150mg/dayAvoid Carbimazole (Teratogenicity: Aplasia Cutis, Choanal Atresia)
2nd/3rd TrimesterSwitch to Carbimazole15-40mg/dayPTU Hepatotoxicity Risk ↑ in 2nd/3rd Tri
AlternativeRAI ContraindicatedSurgery if RefractoryRadioiodine Contraindicated in Pregnancy

Targets

ParameterTarget
fT4Upper Normal Range (Avoid Fetal Hypothyroidism)
TSHLow but Detectable (Not Fully Suppressed)

Fetal Monitoring

IndicationTest
Maternal TRAb >5 IU/LFetal US (Goitre, Tachycardia, Hydrops, Growth)
High-RiskFetal TSH/fT4 (Cord Blood if Available)

9. Postpartum Thyroiditis (PPT)

FeatureDetails
Incidence5-10% of Pregnancies; TPOAb+ in 1st Tri → 30-50% Risk
TimingThyrotoxic Phase: 1-4mo Postpartum; Hypothyroid Phase: 2-6mo
Risk FactorsTPOAb+ (1st Tri), Previous PPT, T1DM, Other Autoimmune
PhasesThyrotoxic (1-3mo) → Hypothyroid (2-6mo) → Recovery (or Permanent)
ManagementThyrotoxic Phase: β-Blocker; Hypothyroid Phase: Levothyroxine if TSH >10/Symptomatic
Long-term20-30% Permanent Hypothyroidism; Recurrence in Subsequent Pregnancies

10. Thyroid Nodules in Pregnancy

ScenarioManagement
New NoduleTSH + fT4 → US → FNA if TI-RADS ≥3 (Safe in Pregnancy)
Known Nodule, StableMonitor Postpartum
FNA Result Bethesda V/VISurgery Postpartum (2nd Tri Preferred)
RAI Uptake/ScanContraindicated in Pregnancy

11. Breastfeeding & Contraception

AspectRecommendation
LevothyroxineSafe (Minimal Milk Transfer)
Carbimazole/PTUCompatible (Low Milk Transfer; Infant Dose <1% Maternal)
RAIContraindicated (Radioactive Milk)
ContraceptionLARC (IUD/Implant) Preferred; Avoid COCP if VTE Risk

12. Postpartum Management

AspectAction
Levothyroxine DoseReduce to Pre-Pregnancy Dose Immediately Postpartum
TSH Check6 Weeks Postpartum
Postpartum ThyroiditisScreen TPOAb+ Women: TSH/fT4 at 3mo & 6mo
Neonatal MonitoringIf Maternal TRAb >5 IU/L → Screen Neonate for Thyrotoxicosis

13. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
TBG in Pregnancy↑ 2-3x (Oestrogen); Total T4/T3 ↑; fT4/fT3 Reliable
TSH in 1st TrimesterCan Be <0.1 (hCG Cross-Reactivity) — Normal if fT4 Normal
TSH Targets1st: 0.1-2.5; 2nd: 0.2-3.0; 3rd: 0.3-3.0 mIU/L
Levothyroxine in Pregnancy↑ Dose ~30% at +ve Test; Monitor q4wk (1st Tri)
PTU vs CarbimazolePTU 1st Tri (Less Teratogenic); Switch to Carbimazole 2nd Tri
RAI in PregnancyContraindicated (Teratogenic)
TRAb in Pregnancy>5 IU/L → ↑ Neonatal Thyrotoxicosis Risk; Fetal US Indicated
Postpartum Thyroiditis5-10% Pregnancies; TPOAb+ → 30-50% Risk; 20-30% Permanent Hypo
BreastfeedingLevothyroxine, Carbimazole, PTU Safe (Low Milk Transfer)
ContraceptionLARC Preferred; Avoid COCP if VTE Risk
Iodine Requirement250 µg/day (vs 150 µg Non-Pregnant)
Postpartum LevothyroxineReduce to Pre-Pregnancy Dose; Check TSH at 6wk

14. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Low TSH in 1st TrimesterNormal if fT4 Normal (hCG Cross-Reactivity); Only Treat if fT4 ↑
Carbimazole vs PTUPTU 1st Tri (Less Teratogenic); Switch to Carbimazole 2nd Tri
TSH Targets1st: 0.1-2.5; 2nd: 0.2-3.0; 3rd: 0.3-3.0 mIU/L
RAI in PregnancyContraindicated (Fetal Thyroid Ablation)
TRAb >5 IU/L↑ Neonatal Thyrotoxicosis Risk; Fetal US Indicated
Postpartum Thyroiditis20-30% Permanent Hypothyroidism; Recurrence Next Pregnancy
Levothyroxine Dose Increase~30% Increase at Positive Test; Monitor q4wk
Subclinical HypothyroidismTreat if TPOAb+ / TSH >2.5 (1st Tri) / >3.0 (2nd/3rd)
BreastfeedingCarbimazole/PTU/Levothyroxine Safe

15. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Thyroid in Pregnancy))
    Physiology
      ↑ TBG 2-3x → Total T4/T3 ↑
      hCG ↓ TSH (1st Tri)
      fT4/fT3 Reliable
      Iodine Need 250µg/day
    TSH Targets
      Pre-conception: <2.5
      1st Tri: 0.1-2.5
      2nd Tri: 0.2-3.0
      3rd Tri: 0.3-3.0
    Hypothyroidism
      ↑ Dose 30% at +ve Test
      Targets: 1st 0.1-2.5, 2nd 0.2-3.0, 3rd 0.3-3.0
      Subclinical: Treat if TPOAb+ or TSH > Target
    Hyperthyroidism
      PTU 1st Tri; Carbimazole 2nd/3rd
      RAI Contraindicated
      TRAb >5 → Fetal US
    Postpartum Thyroiditis
      5-10% Pregnancies
      TPOAb+ → 30-50% Risk
      20-30% Permanent Hypo
    Monitoring
      TSH q4wk (1st Tri), q4-6wk (2nd/3rd)
      Postpartum: Reduce to Pre-preg Dose

16. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
TSH in 1st TrimesterCan be <0.1 (hCG Cross-Reactivity) — Normal if fT4 Normal
TSH Targets1st: 0.1-2.5; 2nd: 0.2-3.0; 3rd: 0.3-3.0 mIU/L
Levothyroxine ↑~30% Increase at +ve Test; Monitor q4wk
PTU vs CarbimazolePTU 1st Tri (Less Teratogenic); Switch to Carbimazole 2nd Tri
RAI in PregnancyContraindicated (Fetal Thyroid Ablation)
TRAb >5 IU/L↑ Neonatal Thyrotoxicosis Risk; Fetal US Indicated
Postpartum Thyroiditis5-10% Pregnancies; 20-30% Permanent Hypothyroidism
Levothyroxine Dose ↑~30% Increase at +ve Test
BreastfeedingCarbimazole/PTU/Levothyroxine Safe (Low Milk Transfer)
Subclinical HypothyroidismTreat if TPOAb+ / TSH >2.5 (1st Tri) / >3.0 (2nd/3rd)
TSH Targets1st: 0.1-2.5; 2nd: 0.2-3.0; 3rd: 0.3-3.0 mIU/L
TRAb >5 IU/L↑ Neonatal Risk; Fetal US
PostpartumReduce to Pre-preg Dose; Check TSH 6wk

17. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Thyroid Disorders|Thyroid Disorders]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Hypothyroidism Overview]], [[Hyperthyroidism Overview]], [[Graves Disease]], [[Postpartum Thyroiditis]], [[Subclinical Thyroid Disease]], [[Reproductive Endocrinology/Pregnancy]]

18. MCQs (10)

  1. Thyroid in pregnancy - TSH targets:

A. 1st tri: 0.1-2.5; 2nd tri: 0.2-3.0; 3rd tri: 0.3-3.0 mIU/L

B. All trimesters: 0.4-4.0

C. 1st: 0.4-4.0

D. Lower in 1st

E. Higher in 3rd

  1. Levothyroxine dose increase in pregnancy:

A. ~30% increase at positive test

B. No change

C. 50% increase

D. Double dose

E. Stop

  1. TSH monitoring in pregnancy on LT4:

A. Every 4 weeks until delivery

B. Once per trimester

C. At delivery only

D. Not needed

E. Monthly in 1st tri only

  1. Graves disease in pregnancy - ATD:

A. PTU 1st trimester; switch to carbimazole 2nd/3rd

B. Carbimazole throughout

C. RAI

D. Surgery

E. No ATD needed

  1. TRAb in pregnancy:

A. Crosses placenta; monitor fetal thyroid if high

B. Does not cross

C. Only in 1st tri

D. Causes fetal hyperthyroidism only

E. Protective

  1. Postpartum thyroiditis:

A. Thyrotoxic (1-4mo) -> Hypothyroid (2-6mo) -> Recovery; 20-30% permanent hypo

B. Permanent hyperthyroidism

C. Only thyrotoxic

D. Only hypothyroid

E. No postpartum effect

  1. Iodine requirement in pregnancy:

A. 250 mcg/day

B. 150 mcg/day

C. 100 mcg/day

D. 500 mcg/day

E. No change

  1. Thyroid cancer in pregnancy:

A. Defer RAI until postpartum; surgery in 2nd tri if needed

B. RAI immediately

C. Terminate pregnancy

D. Chemotherapy

E. No treatment needed

  1. Subclinical hypothyroidism in pregnancy:

A. Treat if TSH >trimester-specific upper limit

B. Never treat

C. Treat only if TSH>10

D. Treat only if symptomatic

E. Levothyroxine contraindicated

  1. Hyperthyroidism in pregnancy:

A. PTU 1st tri -> switch carbimazole; target fT4 upper normal; monitor fetal growth

B. Carbimazole throughout

C. RAI

D. Surgery 1st tri

E. Observation only

19. SBA Questions (10)

  1. Pregnant 10wks on LT4 100mcg: TSH 4.2 (ref 0.1-2.5). Action?

A. Increase dose ~30% (to 125-150mcg); monitor q4wk

B. Stop LT4

C. Reduce dose

D. Continue same

E. Switch to T3

  1. 30yo pregnant 8wks: Graves, TSH <0.01, fT4 45. ATD?

A. PTU 100-200mg TDS

  1. Postpartum woman: TSH <0.01, fT4 40, painless, 3mo pp. Phase?

A. Postpartum thyroiditis thyrotoxic phase (1-4mo)

B. Graves

C. Silent thyroiditis

D. Subacute

E. Factitious

  1. Same patient at 5mo pp: TSH 15, fT4 5. Management?

A. Levothyroxine (hypothyroid phase); monitor for recovery

B. Carbimazole

C. RAI

D. Surgery

E. Observation

  1. Pregnant: subclinical hypo TSH 3.5 (ref 0.1-2.5), TPOAb+. Treat?

A. Yes (Ab+); levothyroxine

B. No

C. Only if TSH>10

D. Only if symptomatic

E. RAI

20. Flashcards

  • Q: Pregnancy TSH targets

A: 1st: 0.1-2.5; 2nd: 0.2-3.0; 3rd: 0.3-3.0 mIU/L

  • Q: LT4 dose increase

A: ~30% at positive test; monitor q4wk

  • Q: Graves in pregnancy

A: PTU 1st tri -> switch carbimazole 2nd/3rd

  • Q: TRAb in pregnancy

A: Crosses placenta; monitor fetal thyroid if high

  • Q: Postpartum thyroiditis

A: 1-4mo thyrotoxic -> 2-6mo hypo -> recovery; 20-30% permanent hypo

  • Q: Iodine in pregnancy

A: 250 mcg/day; deficiency -> fetal brain damage

  • Q: Subclinical hypo pregnancy

A: Treat if TSH >trimester upper limit

  • Q: Thyroid cancer pregnancy

A: Defer RAI; surgery 2nd tri if needed

  • Q: RAI in pregnancy

A: Absolutely contraindicated

  • Q: Methimazole/Carbimazole

A: Teratogenic (aplasia cutis); avoid 1st tri

21. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. 1st tri: 0.1-2.5; 2nd tri: 0.2-3.0; 3rd tri: 0.3-3.0 mIU/L — Thyroid in pregnancy - TSH targets:
  1. ~30% increase at positive test — Levothyroxine dose increase in pregnancy:
  1. Every 4 weeks until delivery — TSH monitoring in pregnancy on LT4:
  1. PTU 1st trimester; switch to carbimazole 2nd/3rd — Graves disease in pregnancy - ATD:
  1. Crosses placenta; monitor fetal thyroid if high — TRAb in pregnancy:
  1. Thyrotoxic (1-4mo) -> Hypothyroid (2-6mo) -> Recovery; 20-30% permanent hypo — Postpartum thyroiditis:
  1. 250 mcg/day — Iodine requirement in pregnancy:
  1. Defer RAI until postpartum; surgery in 2nd tri if needed — Thyroid cancer in pregnancy:
  1. Treat if TSH >trimester-specific upper limit — Subclinical hypothyroidism in pregnancy:
  1. PTU 1st tri -> switch carbimazole; target fT4 upper normal; monitor fetal growth — Hyperthyroidism in pregnancy:

SBAs

  1. Increase dose ~30% (to 125-150mcg); monitor q4wk — Pregnant 10wks on LT4 100mcg: TSH 4.2 (ref 0.1-2.5). Action?
  1. PTU 100-200mg TDS — 30yo pregnant 8wks: Graves, TSH <0.01, fT4 45. ATD?
  1. Postpartum thyroiditis thyrotoxic phase (1-4mo) — Postpartum woman: TSH <0.01, fT4 40, painless, 3mo pp. Phase?
  1. Levothyroxine (hypothyroid phase); monitor for recovery — Same patient at 5mo pp: TSH 15, fT4 5. Management?
  1. Yes (Ab+); levothyroxine — Pregnant: subclinical hypo TSH 3.5 (ref 0.1-2.5), TPOAb+. Treat?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.3.18 Thyroiditis Subacute Silent Postpartum
[!info]
Thyroiditis = Inflammatory Thyroid Disease causing release of preformed hormone → Transient Thyrotoxicosis → Hypothyroid Phase → Recovery (usually). Key differential: RAI Uptake LOW (vs Graves/Toxic Nodule where uptake is HIGH). Three main types: Subacute (De Quervain), Silent/Painless, Postpartum.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify thyroiditis types and their pathophysiological mechanisms
  • [ ] Differentiate subacute, silent, postpartum, and drug-induced thyroiditis
  • [ ] Apply investigation algorithm (TSH, fT4, TRAb, RAI uptake, ESR/CRP)
  • [ ] Predict natural history and manage each phase appropriately
  • [ ] Identify when to treat vs observe

2. Classification & Pathophysiology

TypeMechanismKey Features
Subacute (De Quervain)Viral/Post-viral → Granulomatous Inflammation → Follicular Destruction → Hormone LeakPainful, Tender Thyroid; ↑ ESR/CRP; Low RAI Uptake
Silent / PainlessAutoimmune Lymphocytic Infiltration → Follicular RupturePainless, Often Postpartum; Low RAI Uptake
Postpartum ThyroiditisAutoimmune (TPOAb+) Flare PostpartumOnset 1-4mo Postpartum; Low RAI Uptake
Drug-InducedAmiodarone (Type 1/2), Lithium, Checkpoint InhibitorsVariable (Type 1 = Iodine Load; Type 2 = Destructive)
Acute SuppurativeBacterial InfectionAcute Pain, Fever, Abscess; Rare
Riedel'sFibrotic Invasive ProcessHard Fixed "Woody" Thyroid; Compressive

3. Subacute (De Quervain) Thyroiditis

FeatureDetails
AetiologyPost-Viral (Coxsackie, Mumps, Adenovirus, EBV, COVID-19) → Granulomatous Inflammation
DemographicsWomen 30-50y (F:M 4:1); Seasonal (Summer/Autumn)
ClinicalNeck Pain (Radiates to Jaw/Ears), Tender Thyroid, Fever, Malaise, Thyrotoxic Symptoms
LabsTSH Suppressed, fT4/fT3 ↑, ESR/CRP ↑↑, TRAb Negative, TPOAb Often Positive
RAI UptakeVery Low (<2%) (Follicular Destruction → No Organification)
Natural History3 Phases: Thyrotoxic (3-6wk) → Hypothyroid (2-3mo) → Recovery (90-95%)
TreatmentNSAIDs (Ibuprofen 400-600mg TDS); Steroids (Pred 15-30mg) if Severe; β-Blocker for Symptoms

Natural History Phases

PhaseDurationTSHfT4/fT3Management
1. Thyrotoxic3-6 weeksSuppressed↑↑NSAIDs, β-Blocker, Steroids if Severe
2. Hypothyroid2-3 months↑↑↓↓Levothyroxine if Symptomatic/Severe
3. Recovery6-12 monthsNormalisesNormalisesMonitor; Stop Rx if Recovered

Outcome: 90-95% Full Recovery; 5-10% Permanent Hypothyroidism.

4. Silent / Painless Thyroiditis

FeatureDetails
AetiologyAutoimmune Lymphocytic Infiltration (TPOAb+) → Follicular Rupture
DemographicsWomen 30-50y; Often Postpartum (Overlaps with Postpartum Thyroiditis)
ClinicalPainless Goitre; Thyrotoxic Symptoms; No Neck Pain
LabsTSH Suppressed, fT4/fT3 ↑, TRAb Negative, TPOAb Positive, Normal ESR/CRP
RAI UptakeLow (<2%)
Natural HistoryThyrotoxic (2-4mo) → Hypothyroid (2-6mo) → Recovery (80%)
Treatmentβ-Blocker for Symptoms; Levothyroxine if Hypothyroid Phase Symptomatic

5. Postpartum Thyroiditis

FeatureDetails
DefinitionAutoimmune Thyroiditis Triggered by Postpartum Immune Rebound (TPOAb+)
Incidence5-10% of Pregnancies; TPOAb+ in 1st Trimester → 30-50% Risk
TimingOnset 1-4 Months Postpartum (Peak 3mo)
ClinicalPainless Goitre; Thyrotoxic Symptoms (Often Subtle) → Hypothyroid Symptoms
LabsTSH Suppressed → ↑; TPOAb Strongly Positive; TRAb Negative; fT4/fT3 ↑ then ↓
RAI UptakeLow
PhasesThyrotoxic (1-3mo) → Hypothyroid (2-6mo post-delivery) → Recovery (or Permanent Hypothyroid 20-30%)
ManagementThyrotoxic Phase: β-Blocker; Hypothyroid Phase: Levothyroxine if TSH >10 or Symptomatic
Long-term20-30% Permanent Hypothyroidism; Recurrence in Subsequent Pregnancies

6. Drug-Induced Thyroid Dysfunction

Amiodarone-Induced Thyroid Dysfunction (AIT)

TypeMechanismThyroiditis Type
Type 1 (Iodine Load)Excess Iodine → Jod-Basedow (Hyperthyroidism in Susceptible)Not True Thyroiditis; High RAI Uptake
Type 2 (Destructive)Direct Cytotoxicity → Destructive ThyroiditisLow RAI Uptake; Resembles Silent Thyroiditis
FeatureAIT Type 1AIT Type 2
MechanismIodine Load → ↑ SubstrateDirect Cytotoxicity
RAI UptakeHigh / NormalLow
Thyroid USVascularity ↑Hypoechoic, Heterogeneous
TreatmentThionamides (CBZ/PTU) ± PerchlorateSteroids (Pred 30-40mg/day); Thionamides Ineffective
DurationProlonged (Months)3-6 Months

Other Drugs

DrugEffectMechanism
LithiumHypothyroidism (Goitre) ± HyperthyroidismInhibits Hormone Release; Goitrogen
Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitors (Sunitinib, Sorafenib)Hypothyroidism (Vascular)Vascular Injury
Checkpoint Inhibitors (Anti-PD1/PDL1, CTLA-4)Immune Thyroiditis (Destructive) → Thyrotoxic → HypothyroidAutoimmune
Interferon-αAutoimmune Thyroiditis (TPOAb+)Autoimmune
GlucocorticoidsTransient TSH SuppressionCentral Suppression

7. Riedel's Thyroiditis (Rare)

FeatureDetails
PathologyFibrotic Invasive Process (IgG4-Related?) → Dense Fibrosis
Clinical"Woody" Hard Fixed Thyroid, Tracheal/Oesophageal Compression, Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve Palsy
Thyroid FunctionUsually Euthyroid or Hypothyroid; Rarely Thyrotoxic
ImagingCT/MRI: Infiltrative Mass, Invades Strap Muscles, Trachea
BiopsyDense Fibrosis, Lymphoplasmacytic Infiltrate, IgG4+ Plasma Cells
TreatmentSurgery (Decompression + Resection); Tamoxifen (Anti-fibrotic); Steroids (Limited); Rituximab (IgG4-Related)

8. Acute Suppurative Thyroiditis (Rare)

FeatureDetails
AetiologyBacterial (Staph, Strep, Anaerobes); Immunocompromised, Developmental Anomalies (Pyriform Sinus Fistula)
ClinicalAcute Neck Pain, Fever, Tender Swelling, Dysphagia, Sepsis
ImagingUS/CT: Abscess, Heterogeneous Thyroid
TreatmentIV Antibiotics (Cover Staph, Strep, Anaerobes); Incision & Drainage if Abscess; Thyroidectomy Rare

9. Investigation Algorithm

Neck Pain / Thyrotoxicosis / Goitre
         │
         ▼
TSH + fT4/fT3 + TRAb + TPOAb + ESR/CRP
         │
         ├── TSH Suppressed + fT4/fT3 ↑ + TRAb Positive → GRAVES
         │
         ├── TSH Suppressed + fT4/fT3 ↑ + TRAb Negative
         │       │
         │       ├── Neck Pain + Tenderness + ↑ESR/CRP → SUBACUTE (DE QUERVAIN)
         │       ├── Painless + Postpartum + TPOAb+ → POSTPARTUM THYROIDITIS
         │       ├── Painless + TPOAb+ (Non-Postpartum) → SILENT THYROIDITIS
         │       ├── Amiodarone → AMIODARONE-INDUCED (Type 1 vs 2)
         │       └── Other Drugs / Checkpoint Inhibitors → DRUG-INDUCED
         │
         ├── TSH Normal/High + fT4 Low → HYPOTHYROIDISM
         │       ├── TSH High → PRIMARY (HASHIMOTO)
         │       └── TSH Low/Normal → CENTRAL (PITUITARY)
         │
         └── Normal TSH + fT4/fT3 Normal → EUTHYROID (Monitor)

10. Management Summary by Type

TypeThyrotoxic Phase RxHypothyroid Phase RxKey Point
SubacuteNSAIDs, Steroids (Pred 20-40mg), β-BlockerLevothyroxine if Symptomatic/TSH >10Steroids Short Course (2-4wk)
Silentβ-Blocker (Symptomatic)Levothyroxine if TSH >10 / SymptomaticNo Steroids Needed
Postpartumβ-Blocker (Thyrotoxic)Levothyroxine if TSH >10 / Symptomatic / Breastfeeding20-30% Permanent Hypo
Amiodarone Type 1Thionamides (CBZ/PTU) ± PerchlorateUsually ResolvesStop Amiodarone if Possible
Amiodarone Type 2Steroids (Pred 30-40mg/day)May Need LevothyroxineSteroids Mainstay
Checkpoint Inhibitorsβ-Blocker → Steroids (Pred 1-2mg/kg)Levothyroxine if PersistentHold ICI if Grade ≥3

11. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Thyroiditis vs GravesThyroiditis = Low RAI Uptake; Graves = High RAI Uptake
Subacute (De Quervain)Neck Pain + Tenderness + ↑ESR/CRP; Viral Aetiology; Self-Limiting
Silent ThyroiditisPainless; TPOAb+; Postpartum Common; Low RAI
Postpartum Thyroiditis5-10% Pregnancies; TPOAb+ → 30-50% Risk; Thyrotoxic 1-3mo → Hypo 2-6mo; 20-30% Permanent Hypo
AmiodaroneType 1 = Iodine Load (Jod-Basedow) → High RAI Uptake → Thionamides; Type 2 = Destructive → Low RAI → Steroids
Checkpoint InhibitorsImmune Thyroiditis → Thyrotoxic → Hypothyroid; Steroids if Grade ≥2
RAI Uptake in ThyroiditisLOW (Follicular Destruction → No Organification)
Subacute vs SilentSubacute = Pain + ↑ESR/CRP; Silent = Painless + Normal ESR
Postpartum ThyroiditisTPOAb+ in 1st Trimester → 30-50% Risk; 20-30% Permanent Hypothyroidism
Amiodarone Type 1 vs 2Type 1 = Jod-Basedow (High RAI) → Thionamides; Type 2 = Destructive (Low RAI) → Steroids
Riedel'sFibrotic "Woody" Thyroid; IgG4-Related; Surgery + Tamoxifen
LithiumInhibits Hormone Release → Hypothyroidism + Goitre

12. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Thyroiditis))
    Subacute (De Quervain)
      Viral → Granulomatous Inflammation
      Neck Pain + Tenderness + ↑ESR/CRP
      RAI Uptake LOW
      Phases: Thyrotoxic → Hypothyroid → Recovery
      Rx: NSAIDs, Steroids, β-Blocker
    Silent / Painless
      Autoimmune (TPOAb+)
      Painless, Postpartum Common
      Low RAI Uptake
      Rx: β-Blocker, Monitor
    Postpartum Thyroiditis
      5-10% Pregnancies; TPOAb+ → 30-50% Risk
      Thyrotoxic 1-3mo → Hypo 2-6mo → Recovery
      20-30% Permanent Hypothyroidism
    Drug-Induced
      Amiodarone Type 1: Iodine Load → High RAI → Thionamides
      Amiodarone Type 2: Destructive → Low RAI → Steroids
      Checkpoint Inhibitors: Immune Thyroiditis → Steroids
      Lithium: Hypothyroidism + Goitre
    Riedel's
      Fibrotic "Woody" Thyroid
      IgG4-Related
      Surgery + Tamoxifen

13. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Thyroiditis vs GravesLow RAI Uptake (Thyroiditis) vs High RAI Uptake (Graves)
Subacute ThyroiditisNeck Pain + Tenderness + ↑ESR/CRP; Self-Limiting
Silent ThyroiditisPainless; TPOAb+; Low RAI; Postpartum Common
Postpartum Thyroiditis5-10% Pregnancies; TPOAb+ → 30-50% Risk; 20-30% Permanent Hypo
Amiodarone Type 1Jod-Basedow (Iodine Load) → High RAI Uptake → Thionamides
Amiodarone Type 2Destructive Thyroiditis → Low RAI → Steroids Mainstay
Checkpoint InhibitorsImmune Thyroiditis → Thyrotoxic → Hypothyroid; Steroids if Grade ≥2
RAI Uptake in ThyroiditisLOW (Follicular Destruction → No Organification)
Subacute vs SilentSubacute = Pain + ↑ESR/CRP; Silent = Painless + Normal ESR
Postpartum ThyroiditisTPOAb+ in 1st Trimester → 30-50% Risk; 20-30% Permanent Hypothyroidism
Amiodarone Type 1 vs 2Type 1 = Jod-Basedow (High RAI); Type 2 = Destructive (Low RAI → Steroids)

14. MCQs (10)

  1. Subacute thyroiditis (De Quervain) key feature:

A. Painful thyroid (neck pain)

B. Painless

C. Diffuse RAI uptake

D. TRAb positive

E. Always leads to permanent hypothyroidism

  1. RAI uptake in thyroiditis (thyrotoxic phase):

A. Low/absent

B. Diffuse increased

C. Focal

D. Multifocal

E. Normal

  1. Postpartum thyroiditis typical course:

A. Thyrotoxic (1-4mo) → Hypothyroid (2-6mo) → Recovery

B. Permanent hyperthyroidism

C. Permanent hypothyroidism

D. Only thyrotoxic phase

E. Only hypothyroid phase

  1. Silent thyroiditis vs subacute:

A. Painless vs painful; both low RAI

B. Diffuse RAI vs low

C. TRAb + vs -

D. Permanent vs transient

E. Different antibodies

  1. Thyrotoxic phase of thyroiditis treated with:

A. β-blocker + NSAIDs/steroids (if pain); NO ATDs

B. Carbimazole

C. RAI

D. Surgery

E. PTU

  1. Anti-TPO antibodies in thyroiditis:

A. Positive in silent/postpartum; Subacute often negative

B. Negative in all

C. Only subacute positive

D. Only silent positive

E. Diagnostic of Graves

  1. Subacute thyroiditis triggered by:

A. Viral/post-viral

B. Autoimmune

C. Iodine excess

D. Drug-induced

E. Postpartum

  1. Postpartum thyroiditis timing:

A. Thyrotoxic 1-4mo pp; Hypothyroid 2-6mo pp

B. Thyrotoxic 6-12mo pp

C. Immediate postpartum

D. Only during pregnancy

E. Random

  1. Permanent hypothyroidism after postpartum thyroiditis:

A. 20-30%

B. <5%

C. 50-60%

D. 80-90%

E. 100%

  1. Drug-induced thyroiditis (amiodarone type 2):

A. Destructive thyroiditis → steroids; stop amiodarone

B. Iodine load → carbimazole

C. RAI

D. Surgery

E. PTU only

15. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 35yo woman: 3 weeks neck pain, fever, TSH <0.01, fT4 35, low RAI uptake, ESR 60. Diagnosis?

A. Subacute (De Quervain) thyroiditis

B. Acute suppurative thyroiditis

C. Graves disease

D. Subacute thyroiditis silent

E. Postpartum thyroiditis

  1. Same patient: management?

A. NSAIDs (ibuprofen) + propranolol; prednisolone if severe pain

B. Carbimazole

C. RAI

D. Antibiotics

E. Surgery

  1. 4mo postpartum woman: TSH <0.01, fT4 30, painless, low RAI uptake. Phase?

A. Postpartum thyroiditis thyrotoxic phase (1-4mo)

B. Graves disease

C. Silent thyroiditis

D. Subacute thyroiditis

E. Factitious

  1. Same patient at 4mo pp: expect?

A. Hypothyroid phase (2-6mo) → monitor TSH; levothyroxine if symptomatic

B. Permanent hyperthyroidism

C. Recovery only

D. Thyroid storm

E. RAI needed

  1. Silent thyroiditis: TSH <0.01, fT4 28, painless, low RAI, TPOAb +. Course?

A. Thyrotoxic → Hypothyroid → Recovery (3-6mo); β-blocker for symptoms

B. Carbimazole needed

C. RAI needed

D. Permanent hypothyroidism

E. Progress to Graves

  1. Amiodarone on therapy 2yrs: thyrotoxic, low RAI, no pain. Type?

A. Type 2 (destructive thyroiditis) → stop amiodarone + steroids

B. Type 1 (iodine load) → carbimazole

C. Graves disease

D. Subacute thyroiditis

E. Toxic nodule

16. Flashcards

  • Q: Subacute thyroiditis

A: Viral/post-viral; PAINFUL thyroid; low RAI; self-limiting (3-6mo); NSAIDs/steroids

  • Q: Silent thyroiditis

A: Autoimmune; PAINLESS; low RAI; TPOAb+; thyrotoxic→hypo→recovery (3-6mo)

  • Q: Postpartum thyroiditis

A: 1-4mo pp thyrotoxic → 2-6mo pp hypo → recovery; 20-30% permanent hypo; TPOAb+ high risk

  • Q: Thyroiditis RAI uptake

A: LOW/absent (vs Graves diffuse ↑)

  • Q: Thyrotoxic phase Rx

A: β-blocker + NSAIDs/steroids (pain); NO ATDs (not synthesis excess)

  • Q: Subacute ESR

A: Markedly elevated

  • Q: Postpartum TPOAb

A: High risk if TPOAb+ in 1st trimester

  • Q: Drug-induced (amiodarone)

A: Type 1 = iodine load (ATDs); Type 2 = destructive (steroids)

17. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Painful thyroid (neck pain) — Subacute thyroiditis (De Quervain) key feature:
  1. Low/absent — RAI uptake in thyroiditis (thyrotoxic phase):
  1. Thyrotoxic (1-4mo) → Hypothyroid (2-6mo) → Recovery — Postpartum thyroiditis typical course:
  1. Painless vs painful; both low RAI — Silent thyroiditis vs subacute:
  1. β-blocker + NSAIDs/steroids (if pain); NO ATDs — Thyrotoxic phase of thyroiditis treated with:
  1. Positive in silent/postpartum; Subacute often negative — Anti-TPO antibodies in thyroiditis:
  1. Viral/post-viral — Subacute thyroiditis triggered by:
  1. Thyrotoxic 1-4mo pp; Hypothyroid 2-6mo pp — Postpartum thyroiditis timing:
  1. 20-30% — Permanent hypothyroidism after postpartum thyroiditis:
  1. Destructive thyroiditis → steroids; stop amiodarone — Drug-induced thyroiditis (amiodarone type 2):

SBAs

  1. Subacute (De Quervain) thyroiditis — 35yo woman: 3 weeks neck pain, fever, TSH <0.01, fT4 35, low RAI uptake, ESR 60. Diagnosis?
  1. NSAIDs (ibuprofen) + propranolol; prednisolone if severe pain — Same patient: management?
  1. Postpartum thyroiditis thyrotoxic phase (1-4mo) — 4mo postpartum woman: TSH <0.01, fT4 30, painless, low RAI uptake. Phase?
  1. Hypothyroid phase (2-6mo) → monitor TSH; levothyroxine if symptomatic — Same patient at 4mo pp: expect?
  1. Thyrotoxic → Hypothyroid → Recovery (3-6mo); β-blocker for symptoms — Silent thyroiditis: TSH <0.01, fT4 28, painless, low RAI, TPOAb +. Course?
  1. Type 2 (destructive thyroiditis) → stop amiodarone + steroids — Amiodarone on therapy 2yrs: thyrotoxic, low RAI, no pain. Type?

▸ 20.3.19 Toxic Nodular Goitre And Toxic Adenoma
[!info]
Toxic Multinodular Goitre (TMNG) = Multiple Autonomous Nodules causing Hyperthyroidism. 2nd Commonest Cause (10-15%). Toxic Adenoma = Single Autonomous "Hot" Nodule. 3rd Commonest (3-5%). Both: Autonomous Function → TSH Suppressed, fT4/fT3 ↑, TSH Suppressed, RAI Uptake Focal/Multifocal.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Differentiate TMNG, Toxic Adenoma, and Graves on clinical, biochemical, and imaging grounds
  • [ ] Apply investigation algorithm (TSH, fT4, RAI Uptake, US)
  • [ ] Compare management strategies (RAI vs Surgery vs ATDs)
  • [ ] Recognise specific complications (Atrial Fibrillation in Elderly, Malignancy Risk)
  • [ ] Manage in Special Populations (Elderly, Pregnancy, Cardiac Disease)

2. Toxic Multinodular Goitre (TMNG)

Epidemiology & Risk Factors

FeatureDetails
Prevalence2nd Commonest Hyperthyroidism (10-15%)
Age>50 Years (Peak 60-70y)
SexFemale > Male (2:1)
Risk FactorsLong-standing Multinodular Goitre, Iodine Deficiency → Sufficiency, Ageing, Smoking
PathogenesisAutonomous Nodules (TSH-R Mutations, Gsα Mutations) → TSH-Independent Growth + Hormone Secretion

Clinical Presentation

FeatureTMNG vs Graves
Age>50 Years (vs 30-50 in Graves)
GoitreLong-standing, Multinodular, LargeDiffuse, Smaller
SymptomsOften Subclinical/Mild; Atrial Fibrillation Common (15-20%)Classic Thyrotoxicosis
Eye SignsAbsent (No Ophthalmopathy)Present (25-50%)
Dermopathy/AcropachyAbsentPresent (Graves Only)
Tracheal CompressionCommon (Large Goitre)Rare

3. Investigation — TMNG vs Graves

InvestigationTMNGGravesToxic Adenoma
TSHSuppressedSuppressedSuppressed
fT4 / fT3↑ (Often Mild)↑↑↑ (Often Mild)
TRAbNegativePositive (>95%)Negative
RAI UptakeMultifocal / Patchy ("Salt & Pepper")Diffuse Homogeneous ↑Focal "Hot" Nodule
Thyroid USMultiple Nodules (Variable Echogenicity)Diffuse Enlargement, HypervascularSingle "Hot" Nodule
TRAbNegativePositive (>95%)Negative
TgAb / TPOAbUsually NegativePositive (80-90%)Negative
RAI Scan Appearance"Salt & Pepper" / MultifocalDiffuse HomogeneousSingle "Hot" Spot

4. Toxic Adenoma (Plummer's Disease)

Key Features

FeatureDetails
DefinitionSingle Autonomous "Hot" Nodule (TSH-Independent)
Prevalence3-5% of Hyperthyroidism
AgeYounger (30-50y) vs TMNG
SexFemale > Male (3:1)
PathogenesisSomatic TSH-R Mutation (Constitutive Activation) / Gsα Mutation
PresentationSolitary Nodule; Often Asymptomatic/Mild Thyrotoxicosis
Malignancy RiskLow (<1%) (vs 5-10% in Cold Nodules)

5. Investigation — Differential Diagnosis

RAI Uptake Scan — Key Differentiator

PatternDiagnosis
Diffuse Homogeneous ↑Graves Disease
Multifocal / "Salt & Pepper"Toxic Multinodular Goitre
Focal "Hot" Nodule (Rest of Thyroid Suppressed)Toxic Adenoma
Low / AbsentThyroiditis / Factitious / Iatrogenic

Thyroid Ultrasound

FeatureTMNGToxic AdenomaGraves
NodulesMultiple (Variable Echo)Single Dominant "Hot" NoduleDiffuse Enlargement
VascularityVariable (Nodule-Dependent)Marked IntranodularDiffuse Hypervascular
BackgroundMultinodularNormal/Suppressed ParenchymaDiffuse Heterogeneous

6. Management — TMNG

Principles

PrincipleDetail
ATDs Not CurativeNodules Autonomous → Relapse Universal on Stopping
Definitive Therapy RequiredRAI (1st Line) OR Surgery
ATDs RoleBridge to Definitive Rx / Elderly Frail / Pre-op Preparation

Management Algorithm

TMNG Diagnosed (TSH Suppressed, fT4/fT3 ↑, TRAb Negative, Multifocal RAI Uptake)
         │
         ├── ELDERLY / FRAIL / COMORBIDITIES
         │       → **RAI (¹³¹I)** — **1st Line Definitive**
         │       Dose: 400-800 MBq (Higher than Graves due to Lower Uptake)
         │
         ├── LARGE GOITRE (>80g) / COMPRESSIVE SYMPTOMS
         │       → **SURGERY (Total Thyroidectomy)** — Preferred
         │       Pre-op: ATDs → Euthyroid → Lugol's Iodine 5-7 Days
         │
         ├── YOUNGER PATIENT / PATIENT PREFERENCE
         │       → **RAI** (Definitive, Non-Invasive) OR **Surgery**
         │
         └── ATRIAL FIBRILLATION / CARDIAC DISEASE
                 → **RAI Preferred** (Lower Perioperative Risk)
                 Rate Control (β-Blockers / Digoxin) + Anticoagulation (CHA₂DS₂-VASc)

RAI in TMNG

ParameterDetails
Dose400-800 MBq (Higher than Graves due to Lower Uptake Fraction)
EfficacyCure Rate 80-90% (May Need Repeat Dose)
Hypothyroidism>90% at 2-3 Years (Higher than Graves)
Pre-treatmentStop ATDs 3-5 Days; Low Iodine Diet 2 Weeks
Post-RAIMonitor TSH/fT4 q4-6wk; Levothyroxine when Hypothyroid

7. Management — Toxic Adenoma

Management Algorithm

Toxic Adenoma (Single Hot Nodule, TSH Suppressed, fT4/fT3 ↑, TRAb Negative)
         │
         ├── **SURGERY (HEMITHYROIDECTOMY)** — **CURATIVE, 1st Line**
         │       Pre-op: ATDs → Euthyroid → Lugol's Iodine 5-7 Days
         │       Complications: RLN Injury (<1%), Hypocalcaemia, Hypothyroidism (Rare)
         │
         ├── **RAI (¹³¹I)** — Alternative if Surgery Contraindicated
         │       Dose: 400-600 MBq (Targeted to Nodule)
         │       Efficacy: 80-90% Cure; Hypothyroidism >90% at 2-3 Years
         │
         └── ATDS (Carbimazole) — Bridge Only / Frail Elderly
                 Not Curative; Relapse Universal on Stopping

Comparison: TMNG vs Toxic Adenoma Management

AspectTMNGToxic Adenoma
1st Line DefinitiveRAI (Elderly) / Surgery (Large/Compressive)Surgery (Hemithyroidectomy)
ATDs RoleBridge / Pre-opBridge Only
Surgery TypeTotal ThyroidectomyHemithyroidectomy
RAI DoseHigher (400-800 MBq)Standard (400-600 MBq)
Malignancy RiskLow (Dominant Nodule <1%)Very Low (<0.5%)

8. Special Considerations in TMNG

Atrial Fibrillation (Common in Elderly TMNG)

AspectManagement
Prevalence15-25% of TMNG (vs 10-15% Graves)
MechanismThyroid Hormone → ↑ β-Receptor Sensitivity + Electrical Remodelling
ManagementRate Control (β-Blocker 1st Line) + Anticoagulation (CHA₂DS₂-VASc)
Thyroid ControlRAI Definitive → Restores Sinus Rhythm in 60-70%

Malignancy Risk in TMNG

AspectDetails
Dominant Nodule<1% Malignancy (If Functional/Hot)
Cold Nodules in MNG5-10% Malignancy — Require FNA (Bethesda)
RAI Uptake Cold NoduleFNA Mandatory

Pregnancy in TMNG

AspectManagement
Rare (Age >50)If Occurs: PTU 1st Trimester → Switch Carbimazole 2nd/3rd
RAI ContraindicatedSurgery if Definitive Rx Needed

9. Follow-up & Surveillance (Post-RAI / Surgery)

TimelineInvestigations
4-6 WeeksTSH, fT4, fT3
3 MonthsTSH, fT4, fT3
6 MonthsTSH, fT4, fT3
12 MonthsTSH, fT4, fT3
AnnuallyTSH (Lifelong if Post-RAI/Total Thyroidectomy)
ComplicationPost-RAIPost-Surgery
Hypothyroidism>90% at 2-3 Years (TMNG)10-20% (Hemithyroidectomy)
Recurrence<5% (May Need Repeat RAI)<2% (Hemithyroidectomy)
MalignancyNo IncreaseNo Increase

10. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
TMNG Prevalence2nd Commonest Hyperthyroidism (10-15%)
Toxic Adenoma3-5% of Hyperthyroidism; Single Hot Nodule
AgeTMNG >50y; Toxic Adenoma 30-50y; Graves 30-50y
RAI UptakeTMNG = Multifocal ("Salt & Pepper"); Toxic Adenoma = Focal Hot Spot; Graves = Diffuse ↑
TRAbNegative in TMNG & Toxic Adenoma; Positive in Graves
ATDs in TMNGNot Curative (Relapse Universal)
1st Line TMNGRAI (Elderly) / Surgery (Large/Compressive)
Toxic Adenoma 1st LineSurgery (Hemithyroidectomy) — Curative
Atrial Fibrillation15-25% TMNG (Elderly); Rate Control + Anticoagulation
Malignancy RiskHot Nodule <1%; Cold Nodules in MNG 5-10%
RAI Dose TMNG400-800 MBq (Higher than Graves)
Post-RAI Hypothyroidism>90% at 2-3 Years (TMNG)
Surgery TMNGTotal Thyroidectomy (Large/Compressive)
Surgery Toxic AdenomaHemithyroidectomy (Curative)
RAI vs SurgeryRAI = Elderly/Frail; Surgery = Large/Compressive/Young

11. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
TMNG vs GravesTMNG: Multifocal RAI, No TRAb, No Eye Signs, Elderly, AF Common
Toxic Adenoma vs GravesToxic Adenoma = Single Hot Nodule; Graves = Diffuse Uptake
TMNG vs Simple MNGTMNG = Biochemical Hyperthyroidism; Simple MNG = Euthyroid
RAI in TMNGHigher Dose (400-800 MBq) vs Graves (400-600 MBq)
Toxic Adenoma SurgeryHemithyroidectomy (Curative) vs Total Thyroidectomy (TMNG)
MalignancyHot Nodule <1%; Cold Nodule in MNG = 5-10% Risk
RAI DoseTMNG: 400-800 MBq; Toxic Adenoma: 400-600 MBq

Mnemonic: TMNG vs TOXIC ADENOMA

  • TMN = Too Many Nodules (Multifocal)
  • Toxic Adenoma = Alone Nodule (Single)
  • RAI: TMN = Multifocal "Salt & Pepper"; Adenoma = Focal Hot Spot
  • Surgery: TMN = Total Thyroidectomy; Adenoma = Hemi = Half
  • Recurrence: TMN after RAI <5%; Adenoma after Hemi <2%

12. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((TMNG & Toxic Adenoma))
    TMNG (10-15%)
      >50y, Female > Male
      Multifocal RAI (Salt & Pepper)
      TRAb Negative
      No Eye Signs
      AF Common (15-25%)
      Mgmt: RAI 1st (Elderly) / Surgery (Large)
    Toxic Adenoma (3-5%)
      Single Hot Nodule
      30-50y, Female > Male
      Focal RAI (Hot Spot)
      Surgery (Hemithyroidectomy) = Curative
    Differentiation
      RAI: Diffuse=Graves, Multifocal=TMNG, Focal=Toxic Adenoma
      TRAb: Graves=Pos, TMNG/Toxic Adenoma=Neg
      US: Diffuse vs Multinodular vs Single Nodule
    Management
      TMNG: RAI (Elderly) / Surgery (Large)
      Toxic Adenoma: Surgery (Hemithyroidectomy)
      ATDs Not Curative
    Special
      AF in TMNG (15-25%)
      Malignancy: Hot Nodule <1%; Cold in MNG 5-10%

13. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
TMNG2nd Commonest Hyperthyroidism (10-15%)
Toxic Adenoma3-5% Hyperthyroidism; Single Hot Nodule
RAI UptakeTMNG = Multifocal; Toxic Adenoma = Focal; Graves = Diffuse
TRAbNegative in TMNG & Toxic Adenoma
ATDs in TMNGNot Curative (Relapse Universal)
TMNG 1st LineRAI (Elderly/Frail) / Surgery (Large/Compressive)
Toxic Adenoma 1st LineSurgery (Hemithyroidectomy)
Atrial Fibrillation15-25% TMNG (Elderly)
MalignancyHot Nodule <1%; Cold Nodule in MNG 5-10%
RAI Dose TMNG400-800 MBq (Higher than Graves)
Post-RAI Hypothyroidism>90% at 2-3 Years
Surgery TMNGTotal Thyroidectomy
Surgery Toxic AdenomaHemithyroidectomy
RAI vs SurgeryRAI = Elderly/Frail; Surgery = Large/Compressive/Young

14. MCQs (10)

  1. Toxic multinodular goitre typical age:

A. >50 years

B. 20-30 years

C. 30-40 years

D. <20 years

E. Any age

  1. RAI uptake in TMNG:

A. Multifocal/patchy

B. Diffuse increased

C. Focal hot nodule

D. Low/absent

E. Normal

  1. Best definitive treatment for TMNG:

A. RAI

B. Carbimazole长期

C. Surgery

D. PTU

E. Observation

  1. Toxic adenoma RAI uptake:

A. Focal hot nodule

B. Diffuse increased

C. Multifocal/patchy

D. Low/absent

E. Normal

  1. Best treatment for toxic adenoma:

A. Surgery (hemithyroidectomy)

B. Carbimazole

C. RAI

D. PTU

E. Observation

  1. ATDs in TMNG:

A. Not curative (autonomous nodules)

B. Curative in 50%

C. Curative in 80%

D. Preferred over RAI

E. Only option

  1. Common presentation of TMNG in elderly:

A. Atrial fibrillation

B. Thyroid storm

C. Myxoedema coma

D. Opthalmopathy

E. Pretibial myxoedema

  1. Toxic adenoma age group:

A. Young adults

B. Elderly

C. Children

D. Middle age

E. Any age

  1. RAI in TMNG:

A. Definitive; low dose often sufficient

B. Contraindicated

C. Only for toxic adenoma

D. High dose required

E. Not effective

  1. Subclinical hyperthyroidism in TMNG:

A. Common (TSH suppressed, fT4 normal)

B. Rare

C. Only in toxic adenoma

D. Never

E. Indicates thyroiditis

15. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 65yo man: long-standing MNG, new AF, TSH <0.01, fT4 22. Multifocal uptake. Best Rx?

A. RAI (definitive; lower cardiac risk than ATDs)

B. Carbimazole long-term

C. Surgery

D. PTU

E. Propranolol only

  1. 30yo woman: single 2cm hot nodule, TSH suppressed. Curative treatment?

A. Surgery (hemithyroidectomy)

B. Carbimazole

C. RAI

D. PTU

E. Observation

  1. 70yo woman: TMNG, TSH 0.05, fT4 normal (subclinical). Osteoporosis. Manage?

A. RAI (subclinical + risk factors → treat)

B. Observation

C. Carbimazole

D. Surgery

E. Propranolol

  1. TMNG patient declines RAI/surgery. Long-term ATD?

A. Not curative; nodules remain autonomous

B. Curative in 12-18mo

C. Preferred

D. Only option

E. Blocks autonomy

  1. Toxic adenoma vs Graves differentiation:

A. Focal uptake + single nodule + negative TRAb

B. Diffuse uptake + TRAb +

C. Multifocal uptake

D. Low uptake

E. Painful thyroid

16. Flashcards

  • Q: TMNG age

A: >50 years

  • Q: TMNG RAI uptake

A: Multifocal/patchy

  • Q: TMNG definitive Rx

A: RAI (or surgery if large/compressive); ATDs NOT curative

  • Q: Toxic adenoma RAI

A: Focal hot nodule

  • Q: Toxic adenoma Rx

A: Surgery (hemithyroidectomy) curative; RAI alternative

  • Q: TMNG presentation

A: Often subclinical; AF common in elderly

  • Q: ATDs in TMNG

A: Not curative - nodules autonomous

  • Q: Subclinical hyper in TMNG

A: Common; treat if TSH<0.1 + age>65/cardiac/osteoporosis

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.4 20.4 12
▸ 20.4.01 Adrenal Crisis
[!info]
Adrenal crisis = acute life-threatening adrenal insufficiency. Most common in known primary AI (Addison's) but can be first presentation. Mortality 5-10% if untreated. Immediate hydrocortisone 100mg IV + fluids is life-saving.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise clinical features of adrenal crisis
  • [ ] Execute emergency management protocol (hydrocortisone + fluids)
  • [ ] Identify precipitating factors
  • [ ] Differentiate from septic shock and other causes of hypotension
  • [ ] Manage steroid withdrawal and stress dosing

2. Definition & Epidemiology

AspectDetails
DefinitionAcute deterioration in known or undiagnosed adrenal insufficiency with hypotension unresponsive to fluids, electrolyte disturbance, and/or hypoglycaemia
Incidence5-10 per 100 patient-years in known AI; 6% present with crisis as first manifestation
Mortality5-10% (higher if delayed treatment); higher in elderly, sepsis, delayed treatment
Peak AgeAny age; more common in known AI patients during stress

3. Pathophysiology

MechanismConsequence
Glucocorticoid deficiency↓ Vascular tone (↓ vascular sensitivity to catecholamines) → Hypotension; ↓ Gluconeogenesis → Hypoglycaemia; ↓ Free water clearance → Hyponatraemia
Mineralocorticoid deficiency (Primary AI)↓ Aldosterone → Renal Na⁺ loss, K⁺ retention → Hyponatraemia, Hyperkalaemia, Volume depletion
Cortisol deficiency↓ Cortisol → Loss of negative feedback → ↑ CRH, ACTH → Hyperpigmentation (Primary AI)
HPA axis suppressionExogenous steroids → Adrenal atrophy → Inability to mount stress response

4. Clinical Presentation

SystemFeatures
CardiovascularHypotension (SBP <100 mmHg, postural drop >20 mmHg), Tachycardia, Shock refractory to fluids
MetabolicHypoglycaemia (often profound), Hyponatraemia (<135 mmol/L), Hyperkalaemia (>5.5 mmol/L, primary only)
GastrointestinalNausea, Vomiting, Abdominal pain, Anorexia, Weight loss
NeurologicalConfusion, Lethargy, Fatigue, Syncope, Seizures (hypoglycaemia)
Dermatological (Primary AI)Hyperpigmentation (palmar creases, buccal mucosa, scars, knuckles)
Precipitating HistoryInfection (sepsis, GI, respiratory), Trauma, Surgery, Missed steroid doses, Steroid withdrawal

Red Flags: Any patient on steroids presenting with hypotension + hyponatraemia + hypoglycaemia

Differential: Septic shock, Cardiogenic shock, Haemorrhage, Anaphylaxis, Neurogenic shock

5. Diagnostic Criteria

CriterionRequirement
1. Acute deteriorationAcute illness in known AI or undiagnosed
2. HypotensionSBP <100 mmHg (or drop >20 mmHg from baseline), refractory to 1L fluid
3. ElectrolytesHyponatraemia + Hyperkalaemia (Primary AI) or Hyponatraemia alone (Secondary)
4. HypoglycaemiaOften present (<3.0 mmol/L)
4. Response to HydrocortisoneRapid BP improvement within 1-2h confirms

Diagnosis is CLINICAL — do not delay treatment for tests

6. Emergency Management Protocol

IMMEDIATE (First 30 Minutes)

StepActionDetails
1. ABCDEAirway, Breathing, Circulation, Disability, ExposureHigh-flow O₂, Cardiac monitoring, IV access ×2
2. Hydrocortisone100mg IV STAT (bolus)DO NOT DELAY FOR TESTS
3. Fluids1L 0.9% NaCl over 1 hourThen 2-3L over 24h (monitor JVP, UOP, U&Es)
4. Glucose5-10% Dextrose if hypoglycaemic50-100ml 20% dextrose if severe; then 5-10% infusion
5. MonitoringU&Es, Glucose q1-2h; Cardiac monitor; Strict fluid balanceTarget UOP >0.5ml/kg/h

ONGOING (First 24 Hours)

TimeHydrocortisoneFluidsMonitoring
0-6h50-100mg IV q6h1L 1st hour, then 150-250ml/hU&Es, Glucose q1-2h; BP, HR q15min
6-24h50mg IV q6h150-250ml/hU&Es 4-6h; Glucose 4-6h; UOP
24-48h50mg IV q8hAs clinicalU&Es 6-8h; Glucose 6-8h

TAPER (Once Stable)

DayRegimen
Day 3-550mg q8h → 25mg q8h
Day 5-720mg AM, 10mg PM
Week 215mg AM, 10mg PM
Week 3-415mg AM, 5mg PM
Maintenance15-20mg AM + 5-10mg PM (or 20-30mg OD)

7. Precipitating Factors

CategoryExamples
InfectionSepsis (pneumonia, UTI, GI), TB, HIV, COVID-19
Trauma/SurgeryMajor surgery, Fractures, Burns
MedicationMissed steroid doses, Abrupt steroid withdrawal, Drug interactions (enzyme inducers), Anticoagulation
EndocrineThyrotoxicosis, Diabetic ketoacidosis, Hypothyroidism
GastrointestinalVomiting, Diarrhoea (impaired absorption), GI bleed
ObstetricLabour, Delivery, Postpartum (Sheehan's)

8. Steroid Withdrawal & Stress Dosing

HPA Axis Suppression

Steroid ExposureSuppression RiskRecovery
<3 weeksMinimalDays
>3 weeks (any dose)Significant (adrenal atrophy)Weeks to months
High dose (>40mg prednisolone equivalent)SevereMonths
Inhaled/Topical (high dose)ModerateWeeks

Stress Dose Hydrocortisone Protocol

Stress LevelHydrocortisone Dose
Minor (Dental, Minor surgery)25mg IV/IM pre-op + usual oral dose
Moderate (Major surgery, Sepsis, Trauma)50-100mg IV pre-op then 50mg q6-8h × 24-48h → Taper
Major (Cardiac surgery, Major trauma, Septic shock)100mg IV bolus then 50-100mg q6-8h × 48-72h → Taper
Labour/Delivery100mg IV at onset of labour → 50mg q6h during labour → 100mg IV at delivery

Oral Stress Dosing (if IV not available): Triple usual oral dose for duration of illness

9. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionSimilaritiesKey Differentiators
Septic ShockHypotension, Fever, ConfusionHyperkalaemia rare, Lactate ↑↑, Procalcitonin ↑, Blood cultures +ve
Cardiogenic ShockHypotension, ConfusionJVP ↑, Pulmonary oedema, ECG changes, BNP ↑, Echo: ↓ EF
Hypovolaemic ShockHypotension, TachycardiaHistory of loss, JVP low, Urea:Cr ratio >20, No hyperkalaemia
AnaphylaxisHypotension, Wheeze, UrticariaAcute onset, Triggers, Tryptase ↑, Responds to adrenaline
Neurogenic ShockHypotension, BradycardiaSpinal injury, Warm peripheries

10. Investigation (After Stabilisation)

TestPurpose
0900 Cortisol + ACTHConfirm AI; Differentiate Primary vs Secondary
ElectrolytesNa⁺, K⁺, Urea, Creatinine, Glucose
FBC, CRP, Blood CulturesIdentify precipitant
TSH, fT4Rule out concurrent thyroid disease
Adrenal AntibodiesAnti-21-hydroxylase (Autoimmune)
Adrenal CTAdrenal haemorrhage, TB, Metastases
Pituitary MRIIf Secondary AI suspected

11. Long-term Management

AspectDetails
Glucocorticoid ReplacementHydrocortisone 15-20mg AM + 5-10mg PM (or 20-30mg OD); Prednisolone 5mg OD
Mineralocorticoid (Primary AI)Fludrocortisone 50-100µg OD (adjust for BP, K⁺, Renin)
Monitoring6-monthly: BP, Weight, Electrolytes, Renin, HbA1c, Bone density (DEXA q2-3yr)
Patient EducationMedical alert bracelet, Steroid card, Triple dose for illness, IM hydrocortisone 100mg pen for emergency
VaccinationsInfluenza annually, Pneumococcal, COVID-19
Bone ProtectionCalcium + Vitamin D; DEXA q2-3yr; Bisphosphonate if T-score <-2.5
PregnancyIncrease hydrocortisone 20-40% in 2nd/3rd trimester; Stress doses for delivery

12. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Adrenal Crisis Mortality5-10% if untreated
First-Line TreatmentHydrocortisone 100mg IV STAT + 1L 0.9% NaCl
Never DelayHydrocortisone before tests
Hyponatraemia + Hyperkalaemia = Primary AI (Mineralocorticoid deficiency)
Hyponatraemia alone = Secondary AI (Glucocorticoid deficiency only)
Hyperpigmentation = Primary AI (High ACTH → MSH cross-reactivity)
PrecipitantsInfection, Trauma, Surgery, Missed doses, Steroid withdrawal
Steroid Withdrawal>3 weeks = HPA suppression; Taper over weeks-months
Stress DosingMinor: 25mg IV; Moderate: 50-100mg IV; Major: 100mg IV + 50mg q6h
FludrocortisoneOnly in Primary AI; Dose 50-100µg OD; Monitor BP, K⁺, Renin
Medical AlertBracelet + Steroid card + IM Hydrocortisone 100mg pen
Adrenal Crisis vs Septic ShockCrisis: Hyperkalaemia, Hyponatraemia, Hypoglycaemia, Responds to Hydrocortisone
PregnancyIncrease dose 20-40% in 2nd/3rd tri; Stress doses for delivery

13. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
AI vs CrisisAI = Chronic insufficiency; Crisis = Acute decompensation requiring emergency treatment
Primary vs Secondary CrisisPrimary: Hyperkalaemia + Hyperpigmentation; Secondary: No hyperkalaemia, No pigmentation
Steroid TaperNo fixed formula; Individualised by duration/dose; Generally 2.5-5mg prednisolone reduction every 1-2 weeks
Stress Dosing"Triple the dose" for oral; IV hydrocortisone for major stress
Crisis vs ShockCrisis = AI decompensation; Septic shock = Infection-driven; Crisis responds dramatically to hydrocortisone

Mnemonic: CRISIS MANAGEMENT

  • Cortisol (Hydrocortisone 100mg IV)
  • Resuscitate (1L 0.9% NaCl fast)
  • Investigate (After stabilisation: Cortisol, ACTH, U&Es)
  • Stress doses (Taper over days)
  • Identify precipitant (Sepsis, Trauma, Missed doses)
  • Steroid card + Alert bracelet

Mnemonic: ADDISON'S CRISIS FEATURES

  • Abdomen pain, Dehydration, Dizziness (hypotension)
  • Infection precipitant, Sodium low, Organism (infection)
  • Nausea/vomiting, 's (Hyperpigmentation), Shock

14. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Adrenal Crisis))
    Definition
      Acute AI decompensation
      Mortality 5-10%
    Pathophysiology
      GC deficiency → Hypotension, Hypoglycaemia, Hyponatraemia
      MC deficiency (Primary) → Hyperkalaemia, Volume depletion
      HPA suppression → No stress response
    Presentation
      Hypotension (refractory)
      Hypoglycaemia
      Hyponatraemia
      Hyperkalaemia (Primary)
      Nausea/Vomiting/Abdo pain
      Confusion/Coma
      Hyperpigmentation (Primary)
    Precipitants
      Infection (Sepsis)
      Trauma/Surgery
      Missed doses
      Steroid withdrawal
      DKA/HHS/Thyrotoxicosis
    Management
      IMMEDIATE: Hydrocortisone 100mg IV + 1L 0.9% NaCl
      NO DELAY FOR TESTS
      50-100mg q6h → Taper over days
      Fludrocortisone (Primary only)
      Glucose, Fluids, Monitoring
    Precipitants
      Infection (Sepsis)
      Trauma/Surgery
      Missed doses
      Steroid withdrawal
    Differential
      Septic shock (No hyperK, Lactate++)
      Cardiogenic (JVP++, EF--)
      Hypovolaemic (Low JVP, No hyperK)
      Anaphylaxis (Urticaria, Tryptase)
    Long-term
      GC replacement (Hydrocortisone 15-20mg AM + 5-10mg PM)
      MC replacement (Fludro 50-100mcg OD - Primary only)
      Stress dosing protocol
      Medical alert + Steroid card
      Education: Triple dose if ill

15. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionAcute life-threatening AI; Mortality 5-10%
Immediate TreatmentHydrocortisone 100mg IV STAT + 1L 0.9% NaCl over 1h
Core FeaturesHypotension, Hypoglycaemia, Hyponatraemia, Hyperkalaemia (Primary)
Primary vs SecondaryPrimary: Hyperkalaemia + Hyperpigmentation; Secondary: Neither
DiagnosisClinical — Do not delay hydrocortisone for tests
PrecipitantsInfection, Surgery, Trauma, Missed doses, Steroid withdrawal
Ongoing RxHydrocortisone 50-100mg q6h × 24-48h → Taper over days
FludrocortisonePrimary AI only: 50-100µg OD
Stress DosingMinor 25mg; Moderate 50-100mg; Major 100mg + 50mg q6h
Steroid Withdrawal>3 weeks = HPA suppression; Taper weeks-months
Patient EducationMedical alert bracelet, Steroid card, Emergency IM hydrocortisone

16. Spaced Repetition Trackers

Review IntervalDate CompletedConfidence (1-5)Notes
24 hours
7 days
15 days
30 days
90 days

17. Self-Test Scorecard

SectionScore /5Last Attempt
Definition & Pathophysiology
Clinical Features
Emergency Management
Precipitants
Differential Diagnosis
Steroid Withdrawal/Stress Dosing
Long-term Management

18. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)
