See also: [[Adrenal Insufficiency: Primary (Addison)]], [[Adrenal Insufficiency: Secondary/Tertiary]], [[Endocrine Emergencies]], [[Dynamic Testing Principles]]

19. MCQs (10)

  1. Adrenal crisis mortality:

A. 5-10% (despite treatment)

B. <1%

C. 20-30%

D. 50%

E. >50%

  1. Adrenal crisis immediate Rx:

A. Hydrocortisone 100mg IV STAT + 1L 0.9% NaCl

B. Fludrocortisone 100µg IV

C. Oral hydrocortisone 20mg

D. Dexamethasone 4mg IV

E. NSAIDs + fluids

  1. Do not delay hydrocortisone for:

A. Tests

B. IV access

C. Imaging

D. Consultant review

E. Family consent

  1. Primary AI crisis electrolytes:

A. Hyponatraemia + hyperkalaemia

B. Hyponatraemia only

C. Hyperkalaemia only

D. Normal electrolytes

E. Hypernatraemia

  1. Secondary AI crisis electrolytes:

A. Hyponatraemia only (no hyperkalaemia)

B. Hyponatraemia + hyperkalaemia

C. Normal

D. Hyperkalaemia only

E. Metabolic alkalosis

  1. Crisis vs septic shock diff:

A. Crisis: hyperK + hyponatraemia + responds to HC; Septic: lactate↑↑, normal K

B. Same

C. Crisis: high lactate

D. Septic: hyperK

E. Cannot differentiate

  1. Stress dose major surgery:

A. 100mg IV bolus → 50-100mg q6h × 48-72h

B. 25mg IV

C. 50mg OD

D. Oral double dose

E. No extra needed

  1. Fludrocortisone in crisis:

A. Only in primary AI (50-100µg OD)

B. In all AI

C. In secondary only

D. Not needed

E. IV only

  1. Steroid withdrawal >3 weeks:

A. HPA suppression → crisis risk if stressed

B. No risk

C. Immediate recovery

D. Only if high dose

E. Only if inhaled

  1. Adrenal crisis ABCDE + HC:

A. HC 100mg IV STAT + fluids BEFORE tests

B. Tests first then HC

C. HC after imaging

D. HC after consultant

E. HC after family consent

20. SBA Questions (10)

  1. Known Addison's on HC 15+5, fludro 100µg: vomiting, diarrhoea, hypotensive, Na+ 125, K+ 6.2. Immediate?

A. HC 100mg IV STAT + 1L 0.9% NaCl

B. Oral HC double dose

C. Fludrocortisone IV

D. Dexamethasone

E. NSAIDs + fluids

  1. On prednisolone 10mg 1yr for RA, stops 1 week ago: hypotension, hypoglycaemia. Diagnosis?

A. Tertiary adrenal crisis

B. Primary AI

C. Septic shock

D. Cushing syndrome

E. Factitious

  1. Adrenal crisis: HC given, BP improves in 1-2h. Confirms?

A. Adrenal crisis

B. Septic shock

C. Cardiogenic shock

D. Hypovolaemic shock

E. Anaphylaxis

  1. Fludrocortisone in secondary AI crisis?

A. NOT needed (ACTH low → aldosterone preserved)

B. Needed

C. High dose needed

D. IV only

E. Only if hyperK

  1. Post-crisis taper:

A. HC 50-100mg q6h × 24-48h → taper over days to maintenance

B. Stop HC

C. Continue 100mg q6h indefinitely

D. Switch to oral immediately

E. Fludrocortisone only

21. Flashcards

  • Q: Crisis mortality

A: 5-10% despite treatment

  • Q: Immediate Rx

A: HC 100mg IV STAT + 1L 0.9% NaCl; NEVER DELAY FOR TESTS

  • Q: Primary crisis

A: Hyponatraemia + hyperkalaemia + hypotension + hypoglycaemia

  • Q: Secondary crisis

A: Hyponatraemia + hypotension + hypoglycaemia (NO hyperK)

  • Q: Crisis vs septic

A: Crisis: hyperK, responds to HC; Septic: lactate↑↑, normal K, no HC response

  • Q: Stress dose major

A: 100mg IV bolus → 50-100mg q6h × 48-72h → taper

  • Q: Fludrocortisone crisis

A: Primary AI only: 50-100µg OD

  • Q: Steroid withdrawal >3wk

A: HPA suppression; stress doses essential

  • Q: Taper post-crisis

A: 50-100mg q6h × 24-48h → taper over days

  • Q: Medical alert

A: Bracelet + steroid card + IM HC 100mg pen

22. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. 5-10% (despite treatment) — Adrenal crisis mortality:
  1. Hydrocortisone 100mg IV STAT + 1L 0.9% NaCl — Adrenal crisis immediate Rx:
  1. Tests — Do not delay hydrocortisone for:
  1. Hyponatraemia + hyperkalaemia — Primary AI crisis electrolytes:
  1. Hyponatraemia only (no hyperkalaemia) — Secondary AI crisis electrolytes:
  1. Crisis: hyperK + hyponatraemia + responds to HC; Septic: lactate↑↑, normal K — Crisis vs septic shock diff:
  1. 100mg IV bolus → 50-100mg q6h × 48-72h — Stress dose major surgery:
  1. Only in primary AI (50-100µg OD) — Fludrocortisone in crisis:
  1. HPA suppression → crisis risk if stressed — Steroid withdrawal >3 weeks:
  1. HC 100mg IV STAT + fluids BEFORE tests — Adrenal crisis ABCDE + HC:

SBAs

  1. HC 100mg IV STAT + 1L 0.9% NaCl — Known Addison's on HC 15+5, fludro 100µg: vomiting, diarrhoea, hypotensive, Na+ 125, K+ 6.2. Immediate?
  1. Tertiary adrenal crisis — On prednisolone 10mg 1yr for RA, stops 1 week ago: hypotension, hypoglycaemia. Diagnosis?
  1. Adrenal crisis — Adrenal crisis: HC given, BP improves in 1-2h. Confirms?
  1. NOT needed (ACTH low → aldosterone preserved) — Fludrocortisone in secondary AI crisis?
  1. HC 50-100mg q6h × 24-48h → taper over days to maintenance — Post-crisis taper:
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.4.02 Adrenal Cushing
[!info]
Adrenal Cushing = ACTH-Independent Cortisol Excess from Adrenal Tumour/Hyperplasia. Accounts for 10-15% of Endogenous Cushing. ACTH Suppressed (<10 pg/mL). Subtypes: Adrenal Adenoma (Commonest), Adrenal Carcinoma, PPNAD, AIMAH.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Differentiate adrenal Cushing from ACTH-dependent causes using ACTH and HDDST
  • [ ] Distinguish adrenal adenoma from carcinoma clinically and radiologically
  • [ ] Recognise rare causes: PPNAD, AIMAH, macronodular hyperplasia
  • [ ] Outline management algorithm (surgery vs medical vs bilateral adrenalectomy)
  • [ ] Apply incidentaloma workup algorithm

2. Classification of ACTH-Independent Cushing

SubtypeFrequencyKey Features
Adrenal AdenomaMost Common (50-60% of Adrenal Cushing)Unilateral, Benign, Cortisol-Secreting
Adrenal Carcinoma5-10%Large (>6cm), Heterogeneous, Invasion, Metastases
Primary Pigmented Nodular Adrenal Disease (PPNAD)RareBilateral Micronodules, Carney Complex, ACTH Suppressed
ACTH-Independent Macronodular Adrenal Hyperplasia (AIMAH)RareBilateral Macronodules, Aberrant Receptors (GIP, LH, b-AR)
McCune-Albright SyndromeRareFibrous Dysplasia, Café-au-lait, GnRH-Independent Precocious Puberty

3. Clinical Presentation — Adrenal vs ACTH-Dependent Cushing

FeatureAdrenal CushingACTH-Dependent (Cushing Disease / Ectopic)
ACTHSuppressed (<10 pg/mL)Normal / Elevated (10-50 pg/mL in Disease; >200 in Ectopic)
HDDST (2mg q6h × 48h)No Suppression (Same as Ectopic)>50% Suppression = Cushing Disease; No Suppression = Ectopic
CortisolHighHigh
HypokalaemiaMild/ModerateSevere in Ectopic
HyperpigmentationAbsent (ACTH Low)Present in Ectopic (High ACTH)
OnsetInsidiousVariable
AetiologyAdrenal Tumour/HyperplasiaPituitary / Ectopic / Adrenal

4. Adrenal Adenoma — Most Common Adrenal Cushing

Clinical & Biochemical Profile

FeatureDetails
Prevalence50-60% of Adrenal Cushing
SizeUsually 2-5 cm (Solitary, Unilateral)
LateralityUnilateral (Right = Left)
Cortisol SecretionAutonomous, ACTH-Independent
ACTHSuppressed (<10 pg/mL)
DHEASNormal or Slightly Elevated
AndrogensNormal (Unless Mixed Secretion)

CT Adrenal Findings

FeatureAdenomaCarcinoma
Size<6 cm (Usually 2-5 cm)>6 cm (Often >10 cm)
Attenuation (Non-Contrast)<10 HU (Lipid-Rich)>20 HU (Often >30-40 HU)
Washout>60% Absolute / >40% Relative (Rapid)<60% Absolute / <40% Relative (Delayed)
EnhancementHomogeneousHeterogeneous, Necrosis, Calcification
MarginsSmooth, Well-DefinedIrregular, Infiltrative, Invasion

5. Adrenal Carcinoma — Rare But Aggressive

Key Features

FeatureDetails
Incidence5-10% of Adrenal Cushing; 1-2/million/year
Size>6 cm (Mean 10-13 cm)
Malignant FeaturesInvasion (Vena Cava, Kidney, Liver), Metastases (Lung, Liver, Bone, Lymph Nodes)
Hormonal ProfileCortisol + Androgens (DHEAS, Testosterone ↑); Mixed Secretion Common
Hormone ExcessOften Mixed (Cortisol + Androgens ± Oestrogens)
Biochemical CluesDHEAS ↑↑, Testosterone ↑, 17-OHP ↑

Imaging Red Flags

FindingSignificance
Size >6 cmHigh Suspicion
Heterogeneous EnhancementNecrosis, Haemorrhage
Irregular Margins / InvasionVena Cava, Kidney, Liver
LymphadenopathyRegional Nodes
Distant MetastasesLung, Liver, Bone, Lymph Nodes

Weiss Score (Histopathological) — >3 = Carcinoma

CriterionPoints
High Nuclear Grade1
Mitotic Rate >5/50 HPF1
Atypical Mitoses1
Clear Cells <25%1
Necrosis1
Venous Invasion1
Sinusoidal Invasion1
Capsular Invasion1
Total >3 = Carcinoma

6. Adrenal Carcinoma — Management

Surgical Management

StageProcedure
Localized (T1-2, N0, M0)Radical Adrenalectomy (Open Preferred) + Lymphadenectomy
Locally Advanced (T3-4, N0, M0)Radical Adrenalectomy + En Bloc Resection (IVC, Kidney, Liver) + Lymphadenectomy
Metastatic (M1)Palliative Resection (Symptom Control) + Mitotane

Adjuvant Therapy

AgentIndicationDose
Mitotane (o,p'-DDD)Adjuvant (All Stages) / Metastatic2-6 g/day (Titrate to 14-20 mg/L Serum Level)
EDP Chemotherapy (Etoposide, Doxorubicin, Cisplatin)Metastatic / UnresectableStandard Regimens
RadiotherapyPalliation (Bone Mets)Palliative Doses

Mitotane Monitoring

ParameterFrequencyTarget
Mitotane Levelq4-6wk14-20 mg/L (Therapeutic)
LFTs, Cortisolq4-6wkAdrenal Insufficiency Monitoring
Lipid Profileq3moHypercholesterolaemia Common

7. Rare Causes of ACTH-Independent Cushing

Primary Pigmented Nodular Adrenal Disease (PPNAD)

FeatureDetails
PathologyBilateral Micronodules (<1cm), Pigmented (Lipofuscin)
SyndromeCarney Complex (PRKAR1A Mutation) — Spotty Pigmentation, Myxomas, Schwannomas
HormonalCortisol Secretion; ACTH Suppressed; May Have Periodic Cushing
GeneticsPRKAR1A (Chromosome 17q24); Autosomal Dominant
ManagementBilateral Adrenalectomy (Curative); Pre-op Steroid Cover

ACTH-Independent Macronodular Adrenal Hyperplasia (AIMAH)

FeatureDetails
PathologyBilateral Macronodules (>1cm); Adrenal Hyperplasia
MechanismAberrant Membrane Receptors (Ectopic Hormone Response)
Common ReceptorsGIP (Post-Prandial Cortisol Spike), LH/hCG, β-AR, Vasopressin, Serotonin
GeneticsARMC5 (Tumour Suppressor, Chromosome 16) — 25-50% Familial
ManagementBilateral Adrenalectomy (Curative); Pre-op Steroid Cover; Receptor Studies

McCune-Albright Syndrome

FeatureDetails
TriadFibrous Dysplasia (Bone), Café-au-lait Spots (Jagged Edges), Endocrine Hyperfunction
EndocrinePrecocious Puberty (GnRH-Independent), Hyperthyroidism, Cushing (Adrenal Hyperplasia), Acromegaly
GeneticsGNAS Mutation (Post-Zygotic, Mosaic) — Activating Gsα
ManagementAdrenal: Surgery/Medical; Others: Disease-Specific

8. Adrenal Incidentaloma — Workup Algorithm

Adrenal Incidentaloma on Imaging
         │
         ▼
HORMONAL WORKUP (All Three Required)
         │
         ├── 1mg DST (Cortisol <50 nmol/L = Excluded)
         ├── Plasma Free Metanephrines (Normal = Excluded)
         └── Aldosterone/Renin Ratio (Normal = Excluded)
         │
         ├── ALL NORMAL → NON-FUNCTIONING
         │       │
         │       ├── SIZE <4cm + BENIGN FEATURES (<10 HU, Washout >60%) → SURVEILLANCE
         │       │       CT 6-12mo → Yearly ×2 → Hormonal Recheck 1yr
         │       │
         │       └── SIZE ≥4cm OR SUSPICIOUS (>10 HU, Washout <40%, Heterogeneous) → SURGERY
         │
         └── ANY ABNORMAL → FUNCTIONING → SPECIFIC MANAGEMENT
                 ├── Cushing → Surgery (Adrenalectomy)
                 ├── Phaeo → Alpha-Blockade → Surgery
                 └── Conn → AVS → Surgery (Unilateral) / Medical (Bilateral)

Surveillance Protocol (Non-Functioning, <4cm)

TimepointAction
6-12 MonthsCT Adrenal (1mm, Contrast)
Yearly ×2CT + Hormonal Recheck
ThenIf Stable → Every 2-3 Years; Hormones Yearly

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Adrenal Cushing ACTHSuppressed (<10 pg/mL)
Adrenal Cushing HDDSTNo Suppression (Same as Ectopic ACTH)
Adrenal Adenoma vs CarcinomaAdenoma: <6cm, <10 HU, Washout >60%; Carcinoma: >6cm, >20 HU, Invasion
Adrenal Carcinoma>6cm, Heterogeneous, Invasion, Metastases, ↑ DHEAS/Testosterone
Adrenal Incidentaloma Workup1mg DST + Metanephrines + Aldosterone/Renin
Non-Functioning <4cmSurveillance (CT 6-12mo → Yearly ×2 → 2-3yrly)
Non-Functioning ≥4cmSurgery (Laparoscopic Adrenalectomy)
Adrenal Cushing SurgeryLaparoscopic Adrenalectomy (Curative for Adenoma)
Adrenal Carcinoma>6cm, Heterogeneous, >20 HU, Invasion, ↑ DHEAS/Testosterone
PPNADCarney Complex, PRKAR1A, Bilateral Micronodules, ACTH Suppressed
AIMAHBilateral Macronodules, Aberrant Receptors (GIP, LH), ARMC5 Mutation
McCune-AlbrightFibrous Dysplasia, Café-au-lait, Precocious Puberty, GNAS Mutation
Incidentaloma Workup1mg DST + Metanephrines + ARR — All 3 Required
Adrenal Adenoma CT<10 HU, Washout >60% = Lipid-Rich Adenoma

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Adrenal Cushing vs Cushing DiseaseAdrenal: ACTH Suppressed, No HDDST Suppression; Cushing Disease: ACTH Normal/High, HDDST Suppresses
Adrenal Cushing vs Ectopic ACTHBoth: ACTH-Independent (Suppressed ACTH) vs ACTH-Dependent (High ACTH); HDDST No Suppression Both
Adenoma vs Carcinoma Size<6cm vs >6cm; <10 HU vs >20 HU; Washout >60% vs <60%
Adrenal IncidentalomaMust Check ALL 3: DST, Metanephrines, ARR
PPNADCarney Complex, Bilateral Micronodules, PRKAR1A
AIMAHBilateral Macronodules, Aberrant Receptors (GIP, LH), ARMC5
Adrenal Carcinoma SurgeryOpen Radical + Lymphadenectomy + Mitotane Adjuvant
Adrenal Incidentaloma Size<4cm = Observe; ≥4cm = Surgery (If Benign Features Absent)

11. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Adrenal Cushing))
    ACTH
      Suppressed (<10 pg/mL)
    Differentiation
      ACTH-Dependent: ACTH N/↑, HDDST Suppression
      ACTH-Independent: ACTH Suppressed, No HDDST Suppression
    Subtypes
      Adenoma (50-60%) → <6cm, <10 HU, Washout >60%
      Carcinoma (5-10%) → >6cm, >20 HU, Invasion, ↑ DHEAS/Testosterone
      PPNAD → Carney Complex, PRKAR1A, Bilateral Micronodules
      AIMAH → Bilateral Macronodules, Aberrant Receptors (GIP, LH), ARMC5
      McCune-Albright → Fibrous Dysplasia, Café-au-lait, Precocious Puberty, GNAS
    Incidentaloma Workup
      1mg DST + Metanephrines + ARR (All 3)
      Non-Func <4cm → Surveillance
      Non-Func ≥4cm or Suspicious → Surgery
      Functional → Specific Management
    Adenoma vs Carcinoma
      Size: <6cm vs >6cm
      HU: <10 vs >20
      Washout: >60% vs <60%
      Invasion: No vs Yes
    Carcinoma Management
      Radical Adrenalectomy + Lymphadenectomy
      Mitotane Adjuvant (14-20 mg/L)
      EDP Chemo if Metastatic

12. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Adrenal Cushing ACTHSuppressed (<10 pg/mL)
Adrenal Adenoma CT<6cm, <10 HU, Washout >60%
Adrenal Carcinoma CT>6cm, >20 HU, Washout <60%, Invasion, Metastases
Adrenal Carcinoma Biochem↑ DHEAS, ↑ Testosterone (Mixed Secretion)
Weiss Score>3 = Carcinoma (9 Criteria)
Adrenal Incidentaloma1mg DST + Metanephrines + ARR (All 3)
Non-Functioning <4cmSurveillance (CT 6-12mo → Yearly ×2)
Non-Functioning ≥4cmSurgery (Laparoscopic Adrenalectomy)
Adrenal Adenoma SurgeryLaparoscopic Adrenalectomy (Curative)
Adrenal Carcinoma SurgeryOpen Radical + Lymphadenectomy + Mitotane
PPNADCarney Complex, PRKAR1A, Bilateral Micronodules
AIMAHBilateral Macronodules, Aberrant Receptors (GIP, LH), ARMC5
McCune-AlbrightFibrous Dysplasia + Café-au-lait + Precocious Puberty + GNAS
Incidentaloma WorkupAll 3: 1mg DST, Metanephrines, ARR
