See also: [[Adrenal Insufficiency- Primary (Addison)]], [[Adrenal Crisis]], [[Drug-Induced Thyroid Dysfunction]], [[Adrenal Insufficiency- Secondary & Tertiary]]

16. MCQs (10)

  1. Commonest CAH:

A. 21-hydroxylase deficiency (95%)

B. 11β-hydroxylase deficiency

C. 17α-hydroxylase deficiency

D. 3β-HSD deficiency

E. Lipoid CAH

  1. 21-OH deficiency classic salt-wasting:

A. Hyponatraemia + hyperkalaemia + virilisation (46,XX)

B. Hypertension + virilisation

C. Sexual infantilism + HTN

D. Isolated cortisol deficiency

E. Isolated aldosterone deficiency

  1. 21-OH deficiency simple virilising:

A. Virilisation (46,XX) without salt-wasting

B. Salt-wasting only

C. Hypertension

D. Sexual infantilism

E. Isolated cortisol deficiency

  1. 11β-OH deficiency:

A. Hypertension (DOC↑) + virilisation (androgens↑)

B. Salt-wasting + virilisation

C. Sexual infantilism + HTN

D. Isolated aldosterone deficiency

E. No virilisation

  1. 17α-OH deficiency:

A. Hypertension (mineralocorticoid excess) + sexual infantilism (sex steroids↓)

B. Salt-wasting + virilisation

C. Hypertension + virilisation

D. Isolated cortisol deficiency

E. No HTN

  1. 21-OH diagnosis:

A. 17-OH progesterone markedly elevated

B. 11-deoxycortisol elevated

C. DOC elevated

D. Cortisol elevated

E. Aldosterone elevated

  1. CAH treatment glucocorticoid:

A. Hydrocortisone 10-15mg/m²/day divided TDS

B. Prednisolone 5mg OD

C. Dexamethasone 0.5mg OD

D. Hydrocortisone 20mg OD

E. Fludrocortisone only

  1. CAH treatment mineralocorticoid:

A. Fludrocortisone 50-200µg/day (salt-wasting)

B. Hydrocortisone only

C. Spironolactone

D. Eplerenone

E. Not needed

  1. CAH monitoring:

A. 17-OHP, androstenedione, renin, electrolytes, BP, growth

B. Only cortisol

C. Only ACTH

D. Only electrolytes

E. Only BP

  1. Lipoid CAH:

A. STAR or CYP11A1 mutation; lethal salt-wasting; adrenal lipid accumulation

B. Mild CAH

C. Only virilising

D. Only HTN

E. Adult onset

17. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 46,XX neonate: ambiguous genitalia, Na+ 125, K+ 6.5, 17-OHP 1500 nmol/L. Diagnosis?

A. 21-OH deficiency (salt-wasting)

B. 11β-OH deficiency

C. 17α-OH deficiency

D. 3β-HSD deficiency

E. Lipoid CAH

  1. Same neonate: immediate management?

A. HC 25mg IV + 0.9% saline + fludrocortisone 50µg

B. Surgery first

C. Observation

D. Dexamethasone

E. Fludrocortisone only

  1. 46,XX child: virilisation, normal electrolytes, 17-OHP elevated. Type?

A. 21-OH simple virilising (non-salt-wasting)

B. 21-OH salt-wasting

C. 11β-OH deficiency

D. 17α-OH deficiency

E. 3β-HSD deficiency

  1. 46,XY neonate: normal genitalia, hypertension, hypokalaemia, 11-deoxycortisol↑. Diagnosis?

A. 11β-OH deficiency

B. 21-OH deficiency

C. 17α-OH deficiency

D. 17β-HSD deficiency

E. Apparent mineralocorticoid excess

  1. CAH long-term monitoring:

A. 17-OHP, androstenedione, renin, electrolytes, BP, growth q3-6mo

B. Only cortisol

C. Only ACTH

D. Only electrolytes

E. Only BP

18. Flashcards

  • Q: 21-OH deficiency (95% CAH)

A: ↓Cortisol, ↓Aldo → ↑17-OHP → ↑Androgens → salt-wasting + virilisation (46,XX)

  • Q: 11β-OH deficiency

A: ↓Cortisol → ↑11-deoxycortisol + ↑DOC (mineralocorticoid) → HTN + virilisation

  • Q: 17α-OH deficiency

A: ↓Sex steroids → sexual infantilism; ↑Mineralocorticoids (DOC) → HTN

  • Q: 21-OH salt-wasting

A: 46,XX: ambiguous genitalia + salt-wasting; 46,XY: normal genitalia + salt-wasting

  • Q: 21-OH simple virilising

A: Virilisation without salt-wasting; 46,XX ambiguous genitalia; electrolytes normal

  • Q: Diagnosis

A: 17-OHP ↑↑ (markedly in salt-wasting); genetic testing CYP21A2

  • Q: Glucocorticoid Rx

A: HC 10-15mg/m²/day TDS (mimic circadian); suppress 17-OHP/androgens

  • Q: Mineralocorticoid Rx

A: Fludrocortisone 50-200µg/day (salt-wasting); titrate to renin/BP/electrolytes

  • Q: Monitoring

A: 17-OHP, androstenedione, renin, electrolytes, BP, growth q3-6mo

  • Q: Lipoid CAH

A: STAR/CYP11A1 mutation; lethal salt-wasting; adrenal lipid accumulation; 46,XY female genitalia

  • Q: Prenatal Rx

A: Dexamethasone to mother if 46,XX fetus at risk (prevents virilisation)

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. 21-hydroxylase deficiency (95%) — Commonest CAH:
  1. Hyponatraemia + hyperkalaemia + virilisation (46,XX) — 21-OH deficiency classic salt-wasting:
  1. Virilisation (46,XX) without salt-wasting — 21-OH deficiency simple virilising:
  1. Hypertension (DOC↑) + virilisation (androgens↑) — 11β-OH deficiency:
  1. Hypertension (mineralocorticoid excess) + sexual infantilism (sex steroids↓) — 17α-OH deficiency:
  1. 17-OH progesterone markedly elevated — 21-OH diagnosis:
  1. Hydrocortisone 10-15mg/m²/day divided TDS — CAH treatment glucocorticoid:
  1. Fludrocortisone 50-200µg/day (salt-wasting) — CAH treatment mineralocorticoid:
  1. 17-OHP, androstenedione, renin, electrolytes, BP, growth — CAH monitoring:
  1. STAR or CYP11A1 mutation; lethal salt-wasting; adrenal lipid accumulation — Lipoid CAH:

SBAs

  1. 21-OH deficiency (salt-wasting) — 46,XX neonate: ambiguous genitalia, Na+ 125, K+ 6.5, 17-OHP 1500 nmol/L. Diagnosis?
  1. HC 25mg IV + 0.9% saline + fludrocortisone 50µg — Same neonate: immediate management?
  1. 21-OH simple virilising (non-salt-wasting) — 46,XX child: virilisation, normal electrolytes, 17-OHP elevated. Type?
  1. 11β-OH deficiency — 46,XY neonate: normal genitalia, hypertension, hypokalaemia, 11-deoxycortisol↑. Diagnosis?
  1. 17-OHP, androstenedione, renin, electrolytes, BP, growth q3-6mo — CAH long-term monitoring:
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.4.07 Cushing Disease
[!info]
Cushing Disease = ACTH-Secreting Pituitary Microadenoma causing Cushing Syndrome. Commonest cause of endogenous Cushing Syndrome (70% of ACTH-dependent cases). Microadenoma in 80-90%. Gold standard treatment: Transsphenoidal Surgery (TSS).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Diagnose Cushing disease using ACTH, HDDST, CRH test, IPSS
  • [ ] Interpret MRI pituitary findings
  • [ ] Outline surgical approach (TSS) and remission criteria
  • [ ] Manage residual/recurrent disease and medical therapy options
  • [ ] Differentiate from ectopic ACTH and adrenal Cushing

2. Pathophysiology & Genetics

AspectDetails
Cell OriginCorticotroph Adenoma (Pituitary Anterior Lobe)
SizeMicroadenoma <10mm (80-90%); Macroadenoma 10-20%
PathogenesisUSP8 Mutations (40%); USP48 (Rare); ATP1A1, BRAF (Rare) → ↑ EGFR Signalling → ↑ POMC Transcription
GeneticsSporadic (Most); MEN1 (Rare); Carney Complex (PRKAR1A); FIP (Familial Isolated Pituitary Adenomas)
EpidemiologyWomen 3-5:1; Peak 20-50 Years; 70% of ACTH-Dependent Cushing

3. Clinical Presentation

FeatureFrequencyDetails
Weight Gain / Central Obesity>90%Moon Face, Buffalo Hump, Centripetal
Skin70-80%Striae (>1cm, Violaceous), Thin Skin, Bruising, Acne
Musculoskeletal60%Proximal Myopathy, Osteoporosis, Vertebral Fractures
Cardiovascular80%Hypertension, Metabolic Syndrome, Atherosclerosis
Glucose20-50%Diabetes / IGT
Psychiatric50-70%Depression, Anxiety, Cognitive Impairment
Reproductive50-70%Oligomenorrhoea, Amenorrhoea, Hirsutism, Low Libido
OphthalmologicalRareVisual Field Defects (If Macroadenoma >10mm)

4. Diagnosis — Confirmation & Differentiation

Diagnostic Algorithm

Cushing Syndrome Confirmed (2/3 Tests Positive)
         │
         ▼
PLASMA ACTH (09:00, EDTA, Ice)
         │
         ├── ACTH <10 pg/mL → ACTH-INDEPENDENT → Adrenal CT
         │
         └── ACTH ≥10-20 pg/mL → ACTH-DEPENDENT
                 │
                 ├── HDDST (2mg q6h × 48h)
                 │       ├── >50% Suppression → **Cushing Disease**
                 │       │       MRI Pituitary (3mm, Gadolinium)
                 │       │       IPSS if Equivocal
                 │       │
                 │       └── No Suppression → Ectopic ACTH / Adrenal Cushing
                 │
                 └── IF HDDST EQUIVOCAL
                         ├── CRH Test (ACTH ↑ >50% = Cushing Disease)
                         ├── IPSS (Central:Peripheral ACTH Gradient >2 Basal / >3 Post-CRH)
                         └── ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET (Ectopic Localisation)

Key Differentiators

FeatureCushing DiseaseEctopic ACTHAdrenal Cushing
ACTHNormal / HighHigh (>200 pg/mL)Suppressed (<10 pg/mL)
HDDST (>50% Suppression)YesNoNo
CRH TestACTH ↑ >50%No RiseN/A
IPSSCentral:Peripheral >2 (Basal) >3 (Post-CRH)No GradientN/A
MRI PituitaryMicroadenoma (80-90%)Normal / Ectopic MassNormal
Lung/Chest CTNormalMass (SCLC, Carcinoid)Normal

5. MRI Pituitary — Imaging Protocol

ParameterSpecification
SequenceT1 (Pre/Post-Gadolinium), T2, Coronal/Sagittal
Slice Thickness3 mm (No Gap)
Dynamic ContrastT1 Post-Gadolinium at 30s, 60s, 90s (Microadenoma Enhancement Delay)
Sensitivity60-70% for Microadenoma; >95% for Macroadenoma

MRI Findings

FindingSignificance
Hypointense on T1 Post-GadMicroadenoma (Delayed Enhancement vs Normal Pituitary)
Marked EnhancementNormal Pituitary / Macroadenoma
Deviation of StalkContralateral to Microadenoma

6. Inferior Petrosal Sinus Sampling (IPSS) — Gold Standard

Indications

IndicationDetails
MRI Negative / EquivocalMicroadenoma Not Visualised / Incidentaloma
HDDST EquivocalDiscordant Results
Discordant Clinical/BiochemicalSuspicion of Ectopic Despite Positive HDDST

Procedure

StepDetails
CathetersBilateral Inferior Petrosal Sinuses (IPS) + Peripheral Vein
SamplingBasal ACTH ×2 → CRH 100µg IV → ACTH at 1, 2, 3, 5, 10, 15min
GradientsBasal Central:Peripheral >2 OR Post-CRH >3 = Pituitary Source

Interpretation

ResultInterpretation
Central:Peripheral >2 (Basal) or >3 (Post-CRH)Cushing Disease (Pituitary ACTH)
No GradientEctopic ACTH

7. Management — Cushing Disease

First-Line: Transsphenoidal Surgery (TSS)

AspectDetails
ApproachEndoscopic Endonasal (Preferred) / Microscopic Sub-labial
Cure RateMicroadenoma: 80-90%; Macroadenoma: 50-60%
Remission Criteria (Consensus)
Post-Op Day 1Cortisol <50 nmol/L (2 µg/dL)
6 WeeksUFC + Late-Night Salivary Cortisol
6 MonthsNormal UFC + LNSC + Normal ACTH/Cortisol Axis
Recurrence Rate10-20% at 10 Years

Post-Op Management

TimepointAction
Day 1Cortisol <50 nmol/L = Biochemical Remission
Week 1-2Hydrocortisone Replacement (15-20mg AM + 5-10mg PM) — Essential (Adrenal Suppression)
6 WeeksWean Hydrocortisone if UFC Normal; If Not → Assess Recurrence
6 MonthsConfirm Sustained Remission (UFC, LNSC, ACTH, Cortisol)
AnnualUFC, LNSC, MRI (If Residual/Recurrent)

8. Residual / Recurrent Disease — Management

ScenarioManagement
Residual Post-TSSRepeat TSS (If Accessible) OR Medical Therapy
Recurrent (After Remission)Repeat TSS (Preferred) / Medical / Radiotherapy
Medical TherapyKetoconazole 200-400mg q6h; Metyrapone; Osilodrostat; Pasireotide (SST Analog)
Bilateral AdrenalectomyLast Resort (If Medical Fails / Life-Threatening)
RadiotherapyStereotactic / Fractionated (If Surgery/Medical Fail)

Medical Therapy Options

DrugDoseMechanismMonitoring
Ketoconazole200-400mg q6hInhibits CYP11B1 (11β-hydroxylase)LFTs q2wk; Adrenal Insufficiency Risk
Metyrapone250-750mg q8hInhibits 11β-hydroxylaseHypotension, Oedema, Hirsutism
Osilodrostat2-30mg BDInhibits 11β-hydroxylaseECG (QT), Hypokalaemia
Pasireotide600-900µg q12h SCSSTR5 Agonist (↓ ACTH)Hyperglycaemia (Monitor Glucose)

9. Pituitary Radiotherapy

TypeIndicationDose
Stereotactic (SRS/GK)Small Residual (<2cm)15-25 Gy (Single Fraction)
Fractionated RTLarger Residual / Not SRS Candidate45-50 Gy / 25-28 Fractions
Onset of Effect5-10 Years (Delayed)
ComplicationHypopituitarism (30-50% at 10-15yr)

10. Recurrence & Long-term Surveillance

ScenarioSurveillance
Biochemical RemissionUFC + LNSC q6mo ×2yr → Annual ×5yr → Then 2-3 Yearly
Post-TSSMRI 3mo, 1yr, then Yearly ×5yr
Recurrence CriteriaUFC >ULN + LNSC >Upper Limit + ACTH Detectable
Recurrence Rate10-20% at 10 Years

11. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Cushing Disease = ACTH-Secreting Microadenoma70% of ACTH-Dependent Cushing
ACTH LevelNormal/High (N/↑) — Not Suppressed
HDDST>50% Cortisol Suppression = Cushing Disease
IPSS GradientCentral:Peripheral >2 Basal / >3 Post-CRH
MRI PituitaryMicroadenoma 80-90% (<10mm); Delayed Gadolinium Enhancement
1st Line TreatmentTranssphenoidal Surgery (TSS)
Remission CriteriaDay 1 Cortisol <50 nmol/L; 6 Weeks UFC
Recurrence Rate10-20% at 10 Years
Recurrent DiseaseRepeat TSS → Medical (Ketoconazole, Pasireotide) → Radiotherapy
IPSS IndicationMRI Negative / Equivocal HDDST / Discordant
IPSS GradientCentral:Peripheral >2 (Basal) / >3 (Post-CRH)
Post-Op RexDay 1 Cortisol <50 = Remission; Hydrocortisone Replacement Essential
Medical RxKetoconazole, Metyrapone, Osilodrostat, Pasireotide
RadiotherapyDelayed Effect (5-10yr); Hypopituitarism Risk 30-50% at 10yr

12. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Cushing Disease vs SyndromeDisease = Pituitary ACTH Adenoma; Syndrome = All Causes of Hypercortisolism
HDDST >50% SuppressionCushing Disease; Ectopic/Adrenal = No Suppression
IPSS vs CRH TestIPSS = Gold Standard Localisation; CRH Test = Physiological (ACTH Rise >50% = Pituitary)
Micro vs MacroadenomaMicro <10mm (80-90%); Macro ≥10mm (Cavernous Sinus Invasion Risk)
Post-Op HydrocortisoneMandatory — Adrenal Suppression from Chronic ACTH Excess
Recurrence10-20% at 10yr; Monitor UFC/LNSC Annually
IPSS Gradient>2 Basal / >3 Post-CRH = Pituitary Source
PasireotideSST5 Agonist; ↓ ACTH; Hyperglycaemia (Monitor Glucose)

13. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Cushing Disease vs SyndromeDisease = Pituitary ACTH Adenoma; Syndrome = All Hypercortisolism
HDDST >50%Cushing Disease = Pituitary Source; No Suppression = Ectopic/Adrenal
Post-Op RemissionDay 1 Cortisol <50 nmol/L = Immediate Success
Recurrence10-20% at 10yr; Monitor UFC + LNSC Annually
IPSS vs MRIMRI First; IPSS If MRI Negative/Equivoal
Medical TherapyKetoconazole (1st), Metyrapone, Osilodrostat, Pasireotide (2nd)
RadiotherapyDelayed Effect (5-10yr); Hypopituitarism 30-50% at 10yr

14. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Cushing Disease))
    Pathophysiology
      Corticotroph Microadenoma (80-90%)
      USP8 Mutation (40%) → EGFR → POMC ↑
    Diagnosis
      ACTH N/↑
      HDDST >50% Suppression
      MRI: Microadenoma <10mm (60-70% visible)
      IPSS: Central:Peripheral >2 Basal / >3 Post-CRH
    Treatment
      1st: Transsphenoidal Surgery (TSS)
      Cure: Micro 80-90%, Macro 50-60%
      Remission: Day1 Cortisol <50 nmol/L
    Recurrence
      10-20% at 10yr
      UFC + LNSC Annually
    Medical Rx
      Ketoconazole, Metyrapone, Osilodrostat, Pasireotide
    Radiotherapy
      SRS / Fractionated
      Delayed Effect (5-10yr)
      Hypopit 30-50% at 10yr
    IPSS
      Gold Standard Localisation
      Gradient >2 Basal / >3 Post-CRH
    Complications
      DI (10-20% Transient)
      New Hypopituitarism (20-30%)

15. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Cushing DiseasePituitary ACTH Microadenoma (70% ACTH-Dep)
ACTH LevelNormal / High (Not Suppressed)
HDDST>50% Suppression = Cushing Disease
IPSSCentral:Peripheral >2 Basal / >3 Post-CRH
MRIMicroadenoma <10mm (80-90%); Delayed Enhancement
1st Line TxTranssphenoidal Surgery (TSS)
RemissionDay 1 Cortisol <50 nmol/L; UFC Normal at 6 Weeks
Recurrence10-20% at 10 Years
Medical TherapyKetoconazole, Metyrapone, Osilodrostat, Pasireotide
IPSS IndicationMRI Negative / Equivocal HDDST
Post-Op HydrocortisoneMandatory (Adrenal Suppression)
PasireotideSSTR5 Agonist; ↑ Hyperglycaemia Risk
Radiotherapy5-10yr Delay; Hypopituitarism 30-50% at 10yr

17. MCQs (10)

  1. Cushing disease is:

A. Pituitary ACTH-secreting microadenoma (>90% micro)

B. Adrenal adenoma

C. Ectopic ACTH

D. Adrenal carcinoma

E. Exogenous steroids

  1. Cushing disease ACTH level:

A. Normal or mildly elevated (inappropriately)

B. Suppressed

C. Very high (>200)

D. Undetectable

E. Pulsatile only

  1. High-dose DST in Cushing disease:

A. Cortisol suppresses >50%

B. No suppression

C. ACTH suppresses only

D. Cortisol increases

E. Variable

  1. CRH test in Cushing disease:

A. ACTH rise >35-50%

B. No ACTH rise

C. Cortisol falls

D. ACTH falls

E. Variable

  1. IPSS in Cushing disease:

A. Central:peripheral ACTH gradient >3 (basal), >2 (post-CRH)

B. No gradient

C. Peripheral > central

D. Gradient only post-CRH

E. Not used

  1. First-line treatment Cushing disease:

A. Transsphenoidal surgery

B. Ketoconazole

C. Radiotherapy

D. Bilateral adrenalectomy

E. Pasireotide

  1. Remission rate after surgery:

A. 80-90% (microadenoma)

B. 50-60%

C. 30-40%

D. 95-100%

E. 20-30%

  1. Nelson syndrome:

A. Post-bilateral adrenalectomy: ACTH↑↑, pituitary tumour growth

B. Post-surgery remission

C. Adrenal carcinoma

D. Ectopic ACTH

E. CAH

  1. Medical Rx for Cushing disease:

A. Ketoconazole, metyrapone, pasireotide, cabergoline

B. Only surgery

C. Only radiotherapy

D. Hydrocortisone

E. Fludrocortisone

  1. Radiotherapy for Cushing disease:

A. If surgery fails/recurs; delayed effect (years)

B. 1st line

C. Immediate cure

D. Causes immediate remission

E. Not used

18. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 35yo woman: Cushing features, ACTH 55, overnight DST not suppressed, pituitary MRI 6mm microadenoma. Next?

A. Transsphenoidal surgery

B. High-dose DST

C. CRH test

D. IPSS

E. Ketoconazole

  1. Same patient post-surgery day 1: cortisol 40 nmol/L. Interpretation?

A. Remission (cortisol <50-100 nmol/L predicts remission)

B. Failure

C. Need repeat surgery

D. Start ketoconazole

E. Radiotherapy

  1. Post-surgery recurrence at 2 years. Management?

A. Repeat surgery or radiotherapy or medical therapy

B. Bilateral adrenalectomy only

C. Observation

D. Ketoconazole only

E. Pasireotide only

  1. Cushing disease with failed surgery. Medical options?

A. Ketoconazole, metyrapone, pasireotide, cabergoline

B. Hydrocortisone

C. Fludrocortisone

D. Surgery only

E. Radiotherapy only

  1. IPSS gradient criteria for Cushing disease:

A. >3 basal, >2 post-CRH

B. >2 basal, >1 post-CRH

C. >5 basal, >3 post-CRH

D. >1 basal, >0.5 post-CRH

E. Any gradient

19. Flashcards

  • Q: Cushing disease

A: Pituitary ACTH microadenoma (>90%); 70% endogenous Cushing

  • Q: ACTH in Cushing disease

A: Normal/mildly high (inappropriately); NOT suppressed

  • Q: HDDST

A: Cortisol suppresses >50% (pituitary responds to dexamethasone)

  • Q: CRH test

A: ACTH rises >35-50% (pituitary responds to CRH)

  • Q: IPSS

A: Central:peripheral ACTH >3 basal, >2 post-CRH; gold standard

  • Q: Surgery

A: Transsphenoidal 1st line; remission 80-90% microadenoma

  • Q: Recurrence

A: 10-20% at 10yr; repeat surgery/radiotherapy/medical

  • Q: Nelson syndrome

A: Post-bilateral adrenalectomy: ACTH↑↑, pituitary tumour growth

  • Q: Medical therapy

A: Ketoconazole (steroidogenesis inhibitor), metyrapone, pasireotide, cabergoline

  • Q: Radiotherapy

A: If surgery fails; delayed effect (years); hypopituitarism risk

20. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Pituitary ACTH-secreting microadenoma (>90% micro) — Cushing disease is:
  1. Normal or mildly elevated (inappropriately) — Cushing disease ACTH level:
  1. Cortisol suppresses >50% — High-dose DST in Cushing disease:
  1. ACTH rise >35-50% — CRH test in Cushing disease:
  1. Central:peripheral ACTH gradient >3 (basal), >2 (post-CRH) — IPSS in Cushing disease:
  1. Transsphenoidal surgery — First-line treatment Cushing disease:
  1. 80-90% (microadenoma) — Remission rate after surgery:
  1. Post-bilateral adrenalectomy: ACTH↑↑, pituitary tumour growth — Nelson syndrome:
  1. Ketoconazole, metyrapone, pasireotide, cabergoline — Medical Rx for Cushing disease:
  1. If surgery fails/recurs; delayed effect (years) — Radiotherapy for Cushing disease:

SBAs

  1. Transsphenoidal surgery — 35yo woman: Cushing features, ACTH 55, overnight DST not suppressed, pituitary MRI 6mm microadenoma. Next?
  1. Remission (cortisol <50-100 nmol/L predicts remission) — Same patient post-surgery day 1: cortisol 40 nmol/L. Interpretation?
  1. Repeat surgery or radiotherapy or medical therapy — Post-surgery recurrence at 2 years. Management?
  1. Ketoconazole, metyrapone, pasireotide, cabergoline — Cushing disease with failed surgery. Medical options?
  1. >3 basal, >2 post-CRH — IPSS gradient criteria for Cushing disease:
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.4.08 Cushing Syndrome Overview
[!info]
Cushing Syndrome = Chronic Excess Glucocorticoid Exposure. ACTH-Dependent (80%) vs ACTH-Independent (20%). Cushing Disease (Pituitary ACTH) = 70% of ACTH-Dependent. Diagnosis: Confirm Hypercortisolism → Determine ACTH Dependence → Localise Source.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify Cushing syndrome by aetiology (ACTH-dependent vs independent)
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic criteria (24h UFC, Late-night Salivary Cortisol, LDDST)
  • [ ] Interpret ACTH levels and dynamic tests (HDDST, CRH test, IPSS)
  • [ ] Outline management algorithms for Cushing disease, ectopic ACTH, adrenal Cushing
  • [ ] Recognise cyclical Cushing and pseudo-Cushing states

2. Classification

CategorySubtypeMechanismProportion
ACTH-Dependent (80%)Cushing Disease (Pituitary ACTH Microadenoma)Pituitary ACTH Autonomous70% of ACTH-Dep
Ectopic ACTH SyndromeNon-Pituitary Tumour (SCLC, Carcinoid, NET)10-15%
Ectopic CRH (Rare)Hypothalamic CRH Hypersecretion<1%
ACTH-Independent (20%)Adrenal AdenomaUnilateral Cortisol Secretion10-15%
Adrenal CarcinomaMalignant Cortisol SecretionRare
Primary Pigmented Nodular Adrenal Disease (PPNAD)Bilateral Hyperplasia; PRKAR1ARare
ACTH-Independent Macronodular Adrenal Hyperplasia (AIMAH)Aberrant Receptors (GIP, LH, etc.)Rare
ExogenousGlucocorticoid TherapyIatrogenicCommon

3. Clinical Features

SystemFeatures
MetabolicCentral Obesity, Moon Face, Buffalo Hump, Supraclavicular Fat, Purple Striae (>1cm), Thin Skin, Easy Bruising, Poor Wound Healing
MusculoskeletalProximal Myopathy (↑ Creatinine Kinase), Osteoporosis, Vertebral Fractures
CardiovascularHypertension (80%), Metabolic Syndrome, Atherosclerosis
GlucoseImpaired Glucose Tolerance / Diabetes (20-50%)
PsychiatricDepression, Anxiety, Cognitive Impairment, Psychosis
ReproductiveOligomenorrhoea, Amenorrhoea, Hirsutism (↑ Androgens), Low Libido
DermatologicalAcne, Striae (Violaceous, >1cm), Acanthosis Nigricans, Fungal Infections
RenalHypokalaemia (Mineralocorticoid Effect), Metabolic Alkalosis

4. Diagnostic Criteria — Confirmation of Hypercortisolism

TestCut-offSensitivitySpecificity
24h Urinary Free Cortisol (UFC)>2-3x ULN (≥220-300 nmol/24h)95%98% (×2)
Late-Night Salivary Cortisol (LNSC)>Upper Limit Night (≥150-220 nmol/L)95%95%
1mg Overnight Dexamethasone Suppression Test (LDDST)Cortisol ≥50 nmol/L (1.8 µg/dL)95%80%

Diagnostic Standard: 2 of 3 Positive (UFC ×2 + LNSC ×2 + LDDST) → Confirmed Cushing Syndrome

5. Pseudo-Cushing States (False Positives)

ConditionMechanismDifferentiating Features
Alcoholism↑ CRH, Impaired FeedbackImproves with Abstinence; Normal ACTH
Major DepressionHPA HyperactivityUsually No Clinical Cushing Features
Morbid Obesity↓ CBG, ↑ Cortisol ClearanceNormal LNSC, Normal 24h UFC
Pregnancy (3rd Trimester)↑ CBG, ↑ CortisolNormal LNSC, Normal 24h UFC
Uncontrolled DiabetesInsulin ResistanceImproves with Glycaemic Control

Key Differentiator: LDDST + Late-Night Salivary Cortisol ×2 — True Cushing: Both Abnormal

6. Dynamic Testing for Hypercortisolism

Low-Dose DST (LDDST) — Screening

Protocol1mg Dexamethasone at 23:00 → Serum Cortisol at 09:00 Next Day
Cut-offCortisol <50 nmol/L (1.8 µg/dL) = Normal Suppression
Positive (Non-Suppressed)Cortisol ≥50 nmol/L → Proceed to HDDST

High-Dose DST (HDDST) — Differential Diagnosis

Protocol2mg Dexamethasone q6h × 8 Doses (48h) OR Overnight 8mg
Cushing Disease (Pituitary ACTH)Cortisol Suppresses >50% (or <50% of Baseline)
Ectopic ACTH / Adrenal CushingNo Suppression (<50% Suppression)

CRH Stimulation Test

ProtocolCRH 100µg IV → ACTH/Cortisol at 0, 15, 30, 60, 60, 90, 120min
Cushing DiseaseACTH ↑ >50% from Baseline (Pituitary Response)
Ectopic ACTHNo Rise (No CRH Receptors)

Inferior Petrosal Sinus Sampling (IPSS)

IndicationDiscordant HDDST / Equivocal CRH Test
ProcedureCatheters in Both IPS + Peripheral Vein → ACTH Sampling
Positive (Pituitary Source)Central:Peripheral ACTH Gradient >2 (Basal) or >3 (Post-CRH)
False NegativeAnatomical Variants, Intermittent Secretion

7. ACTH-Dependent vs Independent — Diagnostic Algorithm

Confirmed Cushing Syndrome (2/3 Tests Positive)
         │
         ▼
MEASURE PLASMA ACTH (09:00, EDTA, On Ice)
         │
         ├── ACTH <10 pg/mL (Suppressed) → **ACTH-INDEPENDENT**
         │       │
         │       ├── Adrenal CT → Unilateral Mass → **Adrenal Adenoma/Carcinoma**
         │       ├── Bilateral Hyperplasia → PPNAD / AIMAH
         │       └── No Mass → Ectopic CRH (Rare)
         │
         └── ACTH ≥10-20 pg/mL (Normal/High) → **ACTH-DEPENDENT**
                 │
                 ├── HDDST (2mg q6h × 48h)
                 │       ├── Suppresses >50% → **Cushing Disease** (Pituitary ACTH)
                 │       │       MRI Pituitary (3mm Slices, Gadolinium)
                 │       │       IPSS if Equivocal / Microadenoma Not Seen
                 │       │
                 │       └── NO Suppression → **Ectopic ACTH / Adrenal Cushing**
                 │               │
                 │               ├── ACTH High → **Ectopic ACTH Syndrome**
                 │               │       CT Chest/Abd/Pelvis → ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET → Bronchoscopy
                 │               │
                 │               └── ACTH Low → **Adrenal Cushing** (Re-check Imaging)
                 │
                 └── IF HDDST EQUIVOCAL → CRH Test / IPSS / ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET

8. Cushing Disease — Specifics

FeatureDetails
Adenoma TypeMicroadenoma (<10mm) in 80-90%; Macroadenoma Rare
MRI Pituitary3mm Slices, Gadolinium; Microadenoma in 60-70%
IPSSGold Standard if MRI Negative / Equivocal; Central:Peripheral ACTH Gradient >2 (Basal) or >3 (Post-CRH)
TreatmentTranssphenoidal Surgery (TSS) — 1st Line
Cure Rate80-90% (Microadenoma); 50-60% (Macroadenoma)
Recurrence10-20% at 10 Years
Medical (If Surgery Fails/Not Candidate)Ketoconazole, Metyrapone, Osilodrostat, Pasireotide

9. Ectopic ACTH Syndrome

AspectDetails
Common SourcesSCLC (50%), Bronchial Carcinoid (20%), Pancreatic NET, Thymic Carcinoid, Medullary Thyroid Ca
ACTH LevelVery High (Often >200 pg/mL)
HDDSTNo Suppression
ImagingCT Chest/Abd/Pelvis → ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT (Gold Standard) → Bronchoscopy
Medical ManagementKetoconazole 200-400mg TDS, Metyrapone, Osilodrostat, Etoposide (SCLC)
Bilateral AdrenalectomyLast Resort (If Medical Fails / Life-Threatening)

10. Adrenal Cushing (ACTH-Independent)

SubtypeFeaturesManagement
Adrenal AdenomaUnilateral, Benign, Cortisol SecretingAdrenalectomy (Laparoscopic, Curative)
Adrenal CarcinomaLarge (>6cm), Heterogeneous, Invasion, MetastasesRadical Adrenalectomy ± Lymphadenectomy; Mitotane Adjuvant
PPNADBilateral Micronodules; Carney Complex; ACTH SuppressedBilateral Adrenalectomy ± Auto-transplant
AIMAHBilateral Macronodules; Aberrant Receptors (GIP, LH, β-AR)Bilateral Adrenalectomy ± Auto-transplant

11. Cyclical Cushing

FeatureDetails
DefinitionIntermittent Hypercortisolism with Normal Intervals
Prevalence5-15% of Cushing Syndrome
MechanismPeriodic ACTH Secretion (Pulsatile Tumour) / Variable Cortisol Binding
DiagnosisProlonged Monitoring: Daily UFC × 2-4 Weeks; Midnight Cortisol × 2-4 Weeks
Diagnostic Criteria>3 Peaks of UFC >ULN Separated by Normal UFC Periods
ManagementSame as Sustained Cushing; Surgery Timed to Peaks if Possible

12. Pseudo-Cushing — Key Differentiators

FeatureTrue CushingPseudo-Cushing
LDDSTNon-Suppressed (Cortisol ≥50)May Suppress
Late-Night Salivary CortisolPersistently ElevatedNormal on Repeat
24h UFCConsistently >2-3x ULNNormal / Borderline
Clinical FeaturesClassic CushingoidOften Atypical
ResolutionPersistsResolves with Treatment of Underlying Cause

Common Causes: Chronic Alcoholism, Severe Depression, Morbid Obesity, Uncontrolled Diabetes, Pregnancy (3rd Tri).

13. Management — Post-Diagnosis

Cushing Disease

StepTreatment
1st LineTranssphenoidal Surgery (TSS) — 80-90% Cure (Micro), 50-60% (Macro)
Recurrent/ResidualRepeat TSS / Bilateral Adrenalectomy / Medical (Ketoconazole, Metyrapone, Osilodrostat, Pasireotide)
RadiotherapyIf Surgery Fails/Not Candidate (Stereotactic/Fractionated)

Ectopic ACTH

PriorityTreatment
1stSurgical Resection of Source (If Localised)
2ndMedical (Ketoconazole, Metyrapone, Osilodrostat, Etomidate)
3rdBilateral Adrenalectomy (If Unresectable/Failed Medical)

Adrenal Cushing

SubtypeSurgery
AdenomaLaparoscopic Adrenalectomy (Curative)
CarcinomaOpen Adrenalectomy + Lymphadenectomy + Mitotane
PPNAD / AIMAHBilateral Adrenalectomy ± Auto-transplant

14. Follow-up & Long-term Surveillance

TimepointAssessments
Post-Op Day 1Cortisol, ACTH, Electrolytes, Glucose
6 WeeksCortisol, ACTH, TSH, fT4, Electrolytes, Weight, BP
3 MonthsFull Hormonal Panel, MRI (If Cushing Disease)
6 MonthsHormones, Weight, BP, Glucose, BMD (DEXA)
Annual (Lifelong)UFC, Late-Night Salivary Cortisol, MRI (Post-Cushing Disease)

15. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Cushing Syndrome2/3 Tests Positive (UFC×2, LNSC×2, LDDST)
Cushing Disease vs AdrenalACTH: Disease = N/↑; Adrenal = ↓↓; Ectopic = ↑↑
HDDST>50% Suppression = Cushing Disease; No Suppression = Ectopic/Adrenal
IPSSCentral:Peripheral ACTH Gradient >2 (Basal) / >3 (Post-CRH)
Ectopic ACTH SourcesSCLC (50%), Bronchial Carcinoid (20%), Pancreatic NET
Adrenal Carcinoma>6cm, Heterogeneous, Invasion, ↑ DHEAS, ↑ Testosterone
Cyclical CushingIntermittent Hypercortisolism; Daily UFC × 2-4 Weeks
Pseudo-CushingAlcoholism, Depression, Obesity, Uncontrolled DM
1st Line Cushing DiseaseTranssphenoidal Surgery (TSS)
Adrenal Incidentaloma1mg DST + Metanephrines + Aldosterone/Renin
Cyclical CushingDaily UFC × 2-4 Weeks for Diagnosis
Adrenal Crisis in Cushing Post-OpHydrocortisone 100mg IV + Fluids (Adrenal Suppression)

16. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Cushing Syndrome))
    Classification
      ACTH-Dependent (80%)
        Cushing Disease (70%)
        Ectopic ACTH (10-15%)
        Ectopic CRH (Rare)
      ACTH-Independent (20%)
        Adrenal Adenoma
        Adrenal Carcinoma
        PPNAD / AIMAH
    Diagnosis
      24h UFC x2, LNSC x2, 1mg DST
      2/3 Positive = Confirmed
      ACTH Level → Dependent vs Independent
    Differential
      ACTH-Dep: HDDST >50% = Pituitary; No Suppress = Ectopic
      ACTH-Indep: ACTH Suppressed → Adrenal CT
    Cushing Disease
      Microadenoma 80-90%
      TSS 1st Line (80-90% Cure)
      IPSS if MRI Equivocal
    Ectopic ACTH
      SCLC, Carcinoid, Pancreatic NET
      CT + DOTATATE PET
      Medical: Ketoconazole, Metyrapone
    Adrenal Cushing
      Adenoma: Lap Adrenalectomy
      Carcinoma: Radical + Mitotane
      PPNAD/AIMAH: Bilateral Adrenalectomy
    Cyclical Cushing
      Daily UFC x 2-4 Weeks
      >3 Peaks Separated by Normal Periods

17. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Diagnostic Confirmation2/3 Tests Positive (UFC×2, LNSC×2, LDDST)
LDDST Cut-offCortisol ≥50 nmol/L = Non-Suppressed
HDDST>50% Suppression = Cushing Disease; No Suppression = Ectopic/Adrenal
ACTH in CushingDisease: N/↑; Adrenal: ↓↓; Ectopic: ↑↑
IPSS GradientCentral:Peripheral >2 (Basal) or >3 (Post-CRH)
Cushing Disease SurgeryTranssphenoidal Surgery — 1st Line
Ectopic ACTHSCLC (50%), Bronchial Carcinoid, Pancreatic NET
Adrenal Carcinoma>6cm, Heterogeneous, Invasion, ↑ DHEAS/Testosterone
Cyclical CushingDaily UFC × 2-4 Weeks; >3 Peaks with Normal Intervals
Pseudo-CushingAlcoholism, Depression, Obesity, Uncontrolled DM
Adrenal Crisis Post-Cushing SurgeryHydrocortisone 100mg IV + Fluids (Adrenal Suppression)

19. MCQs (10)

  1. Commonest cause of Cushing syndrome:

A. Exogenous steroids

B. Cushing disease (pituitary)

C. Adrenal adenoma

D. Ectopic ACTH

E. Adrenal carcinoma

  1. Cushing disease =

A. ACTH-dependent Cushing from pituitary adenoma

B. Adrenal Cushing

C. Ectopic ACTH

D. Exogenous steroids

E. CAH

  1. Cushing syndrome biochemical hallmarks:

A. Loss of diurnal rhythm + failure to suppress on DST

B. High cortisol + high ACTH always

C. Low cortisol + high ACTH

D. Normal cortisol + high ACTH

E. High cortisol + normal ACTH always

  1. Midnight salivary cortisol in Cushing:

A. Elevated (loss of diurnal rhythm)

B. Normal

C. Low

D. Variable

E. Suppressed

  1. 24h urinary free cortisol (UFC) in Cushing:

A. Elevated (usually >3x ULN)

B. Normal

C. Low

D. Only elevated in ectopic

E. Only elevated in adrenal

  1. First-line screening test for Cushing:

A. Overnight 1mg DST or midnight salivary cortisol or 24h UFC

B. High-dose DST

C. CRH test

D. IPSS

E. Adrenal CT

  1. ACTH level in Cushing disease:

A. Normal/high (inappropriately)

B. Suppressed

C. Very high (>200 pg/mL)

D. Undetectable

E. Pulsatile only

  1. ACTH level in ectopic ACTH:

A. High (>200 pg/mL)

B. Suppressed

C. Normal

D. Low

E. Pulsatile only

  1. ACTH level in adrenal Cushing:

A. Suppressed (<5 pg/mL)

B. High

C. Normal

D. Very high

E. Pulsatile

  1. Cyclical Cushing:

A. Periodic cortisol excess with normal intervals

B. Constant cortisol excess

C. Only in pregnancy

D. Only in children

E. Factitious

20. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 40yo woman: weight gain, striae, HTN, glucose intolerance. Midnight salivary cortisol high. Next?

A. 24h UFC + 09:00 cortisol + ACTH

B. High-dose DST

C. Pituitary MRI

D. Adrenal CT

E. CRH test

  1. Cushing confirmed. ACTH 80 pg/mL. Next?

A. High-dose DST or CRH test or IPSS

B. Adrenal CT

C. Pituitary MRI immediately

D. Ectopic workup

E. Stop steroids

  1. Cushing confirmed. ACTH <5 pg/mL. Next?

A. Adrenal CT

B. Pituitary MRI

C. High-dose DST

D. CRH test

E. IPSS

  1. Cushing confirmed. ACTH 250 pg/mL. Next?

A. Chest/abdomen CT for ectopic source

B. Pituitary MRI

C. High-dose DST

D. CRH test

E. Adrenal CT

  1. Cyclical Cushing suspected. Best approach?

A. Repeated 24h UFC / midnight salivary cortisol over weeks

B. Single UFC

C. Single DST

D. Single ACTH

E. Random cortisol

  1. Pseudo-Cushing (alcohol/depression):

A. May have mild UFC elevation but suppresses on low-dose DST

B. Does not suppress on DST

C. High ACTH

D. Requires IPSS

E. Always needs surgery

  1. Cushing in pregnancy:

A. Very rare; fetal risk; surgery 2nd trimester if needed

B. Treat with ketoconazole

C. RAI

D. Observation only

E. Deliver early

  1. Adrenal incidentaloma with possible Cushing:

A. 1mg DST → if not suppressed, 24h UFC + ACTH

B. Immediate surgery

C. Observation only

D. RAI

E. Ketoconazole

  1. Ectopic ACTH: commonest tumours?

A. Small cell lung cancer, bronchial carcinoid, thymic carcinoid

B. Pituitary adenoma

C. Adrenal adenoma

D. Renal cell cancer

E. Thyroid cancer

21. Flashcards

  • Q: Cushing syndrome

A: Cortisol excess (any cause); exogenous commonest

  • Q: Cushing disease

A: Pituitary ACTH-dependent (~70% endogenous)

  • Q: Ectopic ACTH

A: ACTH >200 pg/mL; SCLC, carcinoid; no DST suppression

  • Q: Adrenal Cushing

A: ACTH suppressed (<5); adrenal adenoma/carcinoma

  • Q: Cyclical Cushing

A: Episodic cortisol excess; needs repeated testing

  • Q: Screening

A: Overnight 1mg DST / midnight salivary cortisol / 24h UFC

  • Q: ACTH in Cushing disease

A: Normal/high (inappropriately); not suppressed

  • Q: ACTH in ectopic

A: High (>200 pg/mL); no suppression on HDDST

  • Q: ACTH in adrenal

A: Suppressed (<5 pg/mL)

  • Q: Midnight salivary cortisol

A: Loss of diurnal rhythm = sensitive screening

  • Q: IPSS

A: Gold standard for Cushing disease vs ectopic; central:peripheral ACTH gradient >3 basal, >2 post-CRH

  • Q: HDDST

A: Cushing disease: cortisol suppresses >50%; Ectopic/adrenal: no suppression

  • Q: Treatment Cushing disease

A: Transsphenoidal surgery (1st line)

  • Q: Treatment adrenal

A: Adrenalectomy (unilateral for adenoma)

  • Q: Treatment ectopic

A: Resection if localised; medical (ketoconazole, metyrapone) if not

22. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Exogenous steroids — Commonest cause of Cushing syndrome:
  1. ACTH-dependent Cushing from pituitary adenoma — Cushing disease =
  1. Loss of diurnal rhythm + failure to suppress on DST — Cushing syndrome biochemical hallmarks:
  1. Elevated (loss of diurnal rhythm) — Midnight salivary cortisol in Cushing:
  1. Elevated (usually >3x ULN) — 24h urinary free cortisol (UFC) in Cushing:
  1. Overnight 1mg DST or midnight salivary cortisol or 24h UFC — First-line screening test for Cushing:
  1. Normal/high (inappropriately) — ACTH level in Cushing disease:
  1. High (>200 pg/mL) — ACTH level in ectopic ACTH:
  1. Suppressed (<5 pg/mL) — ACTH level in adrenal Cushing:
  1. Periodic cortisol excess with normal intervals — Cyclical Cushing:

SBAs

  1. 24h UFC + 09:00 cortisol + ACTH — 40yo woman: weight gain, striae, HTN, glucose intolerance. Midnight salivary cortisol high. Next?
  1. High-dose DST or CRH test or IPSS — Cushing confirmed. ACTH 80 pg/mL. Next?
  1. Adrenal CT — Cushing confirmed. ACTH <5 pg/mL. Next?
  1. Chest/abdomen CT for ectopic source — Cushing confirmed. ACTH 250 pg/mL. Next?
  1. Repeated 24h UFC / midnight salivary cortisol over weeks — Cyclical Cushing suspected. Best approach?
  1. May have mild UFC elevation but suppresses on low-dose DST — Pseudo-Cushing (alcohol/depression):
  1. Very rare; fetal risk; surgery 2nd trimester if needed — Cushing in pregnancy:
  1. 1mg DST → if not suppressed, 24h UFC + ACTH — Adrenal incidentaloma with possible Cushing:
  1. Small cell lung cancer, bronchial carcinoid, thymic carcinoid — Ectopic ACTH: commonest tumours?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.4.09 Cyclical Cushing
[!info]
Cyclical Cushing Syndrome = Intermittent Hypercortisolism with periods of normal cortisol secretion. Prevalence 5-15% of Cushing Syndrome. Diagnostic challenge: requires prolonged monitoring. Management same as sustained Cushing but timing of surgery critical.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Define cyclical Cushing and its prevalence
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic criteria using prolonged monitoring protocols
  • [ ] Differentiate from pseudo-cyclical patterns and pseudo-Cushing
  • [ ] Time surgical intervention appropriately
  • [ ] Manage medical therapy during off-cycles

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetails
DefinitionIntermittent Hypercortisolism with alternating periods of biochemical hypercortisolism and normocortisolism
Prevalence5-15% of Cushing Syndrome
Age & SexSimilar to Sustained Cushing (Female 3-5:1, Peak 20-50y)
AetiologyCushing Disease (Pituitary) ~70%; Adrenal Adenoma ~20%; Ectopic ACTH ~10%

3. Pathophysiology

MechanismDetails
Pulsatile ACTH SecretionCorticotroph Adenoma with Burst Firing Pattern → Intermittent ACTH Bursts
Variable Cortisol ClearanceAltered Hepatic Metabolism / CBG Fluctuations
Feedback Resistance FluctuationVariable Glucocorticoid Receptor Sensitivity
Tumour BiologyPulsatile Secretion from Adenoma (Intrinsic Pacemaker)
Feedback ThresholdVariable Set-Point of HPA Axis Negative Feedback

4. Clinical Presentation

FeatureCyclical vs Sustained Cushing
SymptomsIntermittent — Waxing and Waning Over Days-Weeks
Weight GainFluctuating (Rapid Gain → Plateau/Loss)
Glucose ControlBrittle Glycaemic Control (Episodic Hyperglycaemia)
HypertensionLabile BP (Episodic Severe HTN)
MoodCyclical Mood Swings (Euphoria → Depression)
Skin ChangesStriae/Bruising Appear During Active Phase
Asymptomatic IntervalsComplete Resolution of Symptoms/Biochemistry Between Cycles

5. Diagnostic Criteria — Prolonged Monitoring

Diagnostic Standard

CriterionRequirement
Monitoring Duration≥2-4 Weeks (Usually 4 Weeks Daily Sampling)
Biochemical Criteria>3 Peaks of UFC >ULN Separated by Periods of Normal UFC
Cycle DurationTypically 3-10 Days (Variable: 1 Day to Several Weeks)
Nadir UFCNormal (<70-90 nmol/24h) During Off-Cycle

Monitoring Protocol

ScheduleTests
Daily24h UFC, Morning Cortisol, ACTH (If Possible)
Alternate DaysLate-Night Salivary Cortisol (LNSC)
WeeklySerum Cortisol (09:00), ACTH, Electrolytes
DurationMinimum 2 Weeks (Ideally 4 Weeks)

6. Diagnostic Criteria (Consensus)

CriterionThreshold
Peak UFC>ULN (Upper Limit of Normal)
Nadir UFC (Normal)
Number of Peaks≥3 Discrete Peaks
Inter-Peak IntervalNormal UFC Between Peaks
Cycle RegularityMay Be Regular (e.g., Every 3-7 Days) or Irregular

7. Differentiation — Cyclical vs Sustained vs Pseudo-Cushing

FeatureCyclical CushingSustained CushingPseudo-Cushing
UFC PatternPeaks >ULN + Normal NadirsPersistently >ULNVariable (Often Normal/Near-ULN)
Cycle DurationDays-Weeks (Regular/Irregular)ContinuousN/A
SymptomsWaxing-WaningPersistentOften Absent/Mild
LDDSTVariable (May Suppress During Nadir)Non-SuppressedMay Suppress
UFC BaselineAlways >ULNNormal/Near-ULN

8. Diagnostic Protocol

Standard Monitoring Protocol (4 Weeks)

DayTests
Daily (1-28)24h UFC, 09:00 Cortisol, ACTH (If Feasible)
Alternate DaysLate-Night Salivary Cortisol (LNSC)
WeeklySerum Electrolytes, Glucose, HbA1c, Lipids
As Needed1mg DST (During Suspected Nadir)

Interpretation Rules

PatternInterpretation
≥3 Peaks UFC >ULN + Normal UFC BetweenDiagnostic
<3 Peaks or No Normal NadirInconclusive → Extend Monitoring / Consider Pseudo-Cushing
Continuous >ULNSustained Cushing (Not Cyclical)
<2 PeaksInsufficient Data → Extend Monitoring

9. Aetiology of Cyclical Cushing

SourceFrequencyCycle Characteristics
Cushing Disease (Pituitary)~70%Often Regular (3-7 Days); Pituitary Adenoma Pulsatility
Adrenal Adenoma~20%Irregular; Adrenal Autonomous Cycling
Ectopic ACTH (Carcinoid)~10%Irregular; Tumour Burden Fluctuation
Adrenal CarcinomaRareVariable

10. Management — Timing is Critical

Surgical Timing

PrincipleDetails
Operate During Active PhaseBiochemical Confirmation During Peak → ↑ Surgical Success
Avoid Surgery During NadirRisk: False Negative Intra-Op Cortisol; Missed Tumour
Pre-Op ConfirmationConfirm Active Phase with UFC/LNSC Day Before Surgery

Surgical Outcomes

Phase at SurgeryRemission Rate
Active Phase (Peak)80-90% (Similar to Sustained)
Nadir Phase<30% (High False Negative Rate)

Medical Therapy During Nadir

SituationManagement
Waiting for PeakKetoconazole (200-400mg q6h) — Suppresses Peak Without Masking Nadir
Severe Symptoms During PeakMetyrapone (Bridge to Surgery)
Do NOTGive Steroids/Cortisol-Lowering During Nadir (Masks Cycle)

11. Diagnostic Challenges

Pseudo-Cyclical Patterns (False Positives)

PatternMechanismDifferentiation
Stress-InducedAcute Stress → Transient ↑ UFCNon-Recurrent, Resolves with Stress Removal
Drug-InducedOral Contraceptives, Oestrogen, MirtazapineDrug History; Repeats After Stopping
Lab ErrorCollection Errors, Improper TimingRepeat Sets; Standardised Protocol
Obese/DepressedPseudo-Cushing PatternResolves with Treatment of Primary Condition

Distinguishing Features

FeatureTrue CyclicalPseudo-Cyclical
RegularityOften Cyclic (e.g., Q3-7 Days)Irrandom/Non-Recurrent
DurationWeeks-MonthsDays (Acute Stressors)
TriggersSpontaneousIdentifiable (Stress, Drugs)
ResolutionSpontaneous CyclicResolves with Trigger Removal

12. Management Algorithm

Cyclical Cushing Suspected
         │
         ▼
4-Week Monitoring (Daily UFC + Alternate LNSC)
         │
         ├── ≥3 Peaks UFC >ULN + Normal Nadirs → CONFIRMED
         │       │
         │       ├── IDENTIFY SOURCE (ACTH, HDDST, MRI, CT)
         │       │       ├── Pituitary → Cushing Disease
         │       │       ├── Adrenal → Adrenal Adenoma/Carcinoma
         │       │       └── Ectopic → Ectopic ACTH
         │       │
         │       └── SURGERY DURING ACTIVE PHASE (Confirmed Peak)
         │
         ├── <3 Peaks / No Clear Nadir → EXTEND MONITORING (6-8 WEEKS)
         │
         └── CONTINUOUS ELEVATION → SUSTAINED CUSHING

13. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Cyclical Cushing Prevalence5-15% of Cushing Syndrome
Diagnostic Gold StandardDaily UFC × 2-4 Weeks
Diagnostic Criteria>3 Peaks UFC >ULN + Normal UFC Between Peaks
Cycle Duration3-10 Days (Typically 3-7 Days)
Most Common CauseCushing Disease (~70%)
Surgery TimingDuring Active Phase (Peak) — 80-90% Remission vs <30% if Nadir
Monitoring DurationMinimum 2 Weeks (Ideally 4 Weeks)
Diagnostic Criteria≥3 Peaks UFC >ULN + Normal UFC Between
Medical BridgeKetoconazole During Nadir (Suppresses Peak)
Surgery AvoidDuring Nadir (<30% Remission)
Pseudo-CyclicalStress, Drugs, Lab Error — Non-Recurrent, Resolves with Trigger Removal

14. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Cyclical vs SustainedCyclical = Fluctuating UFC (Peaks + Normal Nadir); Sustained = Persistently Elevated
Cyclical vs Pseudo-CushingTrue Cyclical = Recurrent Peaks + Normal Nadir; Pseudo = Stress/Drug-Induced, Non-Recurrent
Surgery TimingCritical: Operate at Peak (80-90% Cure); At Nadir = <30% Cure
Monitoring DurationMinimum 2 Weeks; Ideally 4 Weeks (Catch Irregular Cycles)
Medical BridgeKetoconazole During Nadir (Prevents Peak Overshoot) — Not During Peak

Mnemonic: CYCLICAL CUSHING

  • Cycles: 3-10 Days Typical
  • Yes to ≥3 Peaks UFC >ULN
  • Cortisol Normal Between Peaks
  • Length: Monitor 2-4 Weeks
  • Avoid Surgery at Nadir (<30% Cure)
  • Leak: Day 1 Cortisol <50 at Surgery = Good
  • Avoid Steroids During Nadir (Masks Cycle)
  • L percutaneous? No, Look for Ipss/HDDST for Source

15. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Cyclical Cushing))
    Definition
      Intermittent Hypercortisolism
      Peaks + Normal Nadirs
      5-15% of Cushing
    Diagnosis
      Daily UFC x 2-4 Weeks
      ≥3 Peaks >ULN + Normal Nadirs
      Cycle: 3-10 Days (Regular/Irregular)
    Aetiology
      Pituitary (70%) - Regular
      Adrenal (20%) - Irregular
      Ectopic (10%) - Irregular
    Management
      Surgery at PEAK (80-90% Cure)
      Avoid NADIR Surgery (<30%)
      Monitor 2-4 Weeks Pre-Op
      Ketoconazole Bridge at Nadir
    Differential
      Pseudo-Cushing: Non-Recurrent, Stress/Drugs
      Sustained: Continuous >ULN
      True Cyclical: Recurrent Peaks + Normal Nadirs

16. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Prevalence5-15% of Cushing Syndrome
Diagnostic StandardDaily UFC × 2-4 Weeks
Diagnostic Criteria≥3 Peaks UFC >ULN + Normal UFC Between
Cycle Duration3-10 Days (Typically 3-7 Days)
Surgery TimingAt Peak (80-90% Cure); Avoid Nadir (<30%)
Monitoring DurationMinimum 2 Weeks; Ideally 4 Weeks
Bridge TherapyKetoconazole at Nadir (Suppresses Peaks)
Pseudo-CyclicalNon-Recurrent; Stress/Drug-Induced
Key DifferentialSustained = Continuous >ULN; Cyclical = Peaks + Normal Nadir

17. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Cushing Syndrome Overview]], [[Cushing Disease]], [[Ectopic ACTH Syndrome]], [[Adrenal Cushing]], [[Dynamic Testing Principles]]

18. MCQs (10)

  1. Cyclical Cushing =

A. Periodic cortisol excess with normal intervals

B. Constant cortisol excess

C. Only in pregnancy

D. Factitious Cushing

E. Pseudo-Cushing

  1. Diagnosis requires:

A. Repeated testing (UFC, salivary cortisol) over weeks/months

B. Single UFC

C. Single DST

D. Single ACTH

E. Single cortisol

  1. Cyclical Cushing disease:

A. Pituitary adenoma with periodic ACTH secretion

B. Adrenal adenoma cycling

C. Ectopic ACTH cycling

D. Factitious

E. Pseudo-Cushing

  1. Cyclical Cushing frequency:

A. Days to months

B. Hours only

C. Years only

D. Random

E. Fixed monthly

  1. Best diagnostic approach:

A. Multiple 24h UFC / midnight salivary cortisol over time

B. Single DST

C. Single ACTH

D. IPSS once

E. CRH test once

  1. If single test during remission:

A. False negative (normal cortisol, ACTH, DST suppression)

B. Positive

C. Indeterminate

D. False positive

E. Always positive

  1. Treatment same as non-cyclical:

A. Yes (surgery for pituitary/adrenal/ectopic)

B. No - observation only

C. Only medical

D. Only radiotherapy

E. Only bilateral adrenalectomy

  1. Cyclical Cushing vs Pseudo-Cushing:

A. Cyclical: true cortisol excess periods; Pseudo-Cushing: stress-related

B. Same

C. Pseudo-Cushing has cycles

D. Cyclical is milder

E. Pseudo-Cushing always cyclical

  1. Diagnostic challenge:

A. May miss diagnosis if tested during remission

B. Easy to diagnose

C. Always obvious

D. Only needs one test

E. Never misses

  1. Recognition important for:

A. Avoiding false negative workup; timing of tests

B. Insurance

C. Research only

D. No clinical impact

E. Family screening

19. SBA Questions (10)

  1. Patient with intermittent Cushing features. Single UFC normal. Next?

A. Repeat UFC/salivary cortisol weekly x4-8 weeks

B. Diagnose pseudo-Cushing

C. Discharge

D. High-dose DST

E. IPSS

  1. Cyclical Cushing confirmed. Pituitary MRI shows 4mm adenoma. Treatment?

A. Transsphenoidal surgery (same as non-cyclical)

B. Observation

C. Ketoconazole only

D. Radiotherapy

E. Bilateral adrenalectomy

  1. Cyclical Cushing: how to time surgery?

A. During cortisol excess phase (easier localisation)

B. During remission

C. Any time

D. Only after 3 cycles

E. Never operate

  1. Cyclical ectopic ACTH:

A. Very rare; same diagnostic challenge

B. Common

C. Does not exist

D. Always SCLC

E. Always carcinoid

  1. Cyclical Cushing recurrence post-surgery:

A. May be harder to detect; need repeated testing

B. Never recurs

C. Always immediate

D. Only if Nelson syndrome

E. Only if radiotherapy

20. Flashcards

  • Q: Cyclical Cushing

A: Episodic cortisol excess with normal intervals (days-months)

  • Q: Diagnosis

A: Repeated 24h UFC / midnight salivary cortisol over weeks; single test may miss

  • Q: Can be

A: Pituitary (disease), adrenal, or ectopic

  • Q: HDDST/CRH/IPSS

A: May be falsely normal if tested in remission phase

  • Q: Treatment

A: Same as non-cyclical (surgery for source)

  • Q: Key pitfall

A: Testing during remission → false negative; need serial testing

  • Q: Pseudo-Cushing

A: Stress-related cortisol variability; not true cycles

21. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Periodic cortisol excess with normal intervals — Cyclical Cushing =
  1. Repeated testing (UFC, salivary cortisol) over weeks/months — Diagnosis requires:
  1. Pituitary adenoma with periodic ACTH secretion — Cyclical Cushing disease:
  1. Days to months — Cyclical Cushing frequency:
  1. Multiple 24h UFC / midnight salivary cortisol over time — Best diagnostic approach:
  1. False negative (normal cortisol, ACTH, DST suppression) — If single test during remission:
  1. Yes (surgery for pituitary/adrenal/ectopic) — Treatment same as non-cyclical:
  1. Cyclical: true cortisol excess periods; Pseudo-Cushing: stress-related — Cyclical Cushing vs Pseudo-Cushing:
  1. May miss diagnosis if tested during remission — Diagnostic challenge:
  1. Avoiding false negative workup; timing of tests — Recognition important for:

SBAs

  1. Repeat UFC/salivary cortisol weekly x4-8 weeks — Patient with intermittent Cushing features. Single UFC normal. Next?
  1. Transsphenoidal surgery (same as non-cyclical) — Cyclical Cushing confirmed. Pituitary MRI shows 4mm adenoma. Treatment?
  1. During cortisol excess phase (easier localisation) — Cyclical Cushing: how to time surgery?
  1. Very rare; same diagnostic challenge — Cyclical ectopic ACTH:
  1. May be harder to detect; need repeated testing — Cyclical Cushing recurrence post-surgery:
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.4.10 Ectopic Acth Syndrome
[!info]
Ectopic ACTH Syndrome = ACTH Secretion from Non-Pituitary Tumour. Accounts for 10-15% of ACTH-Dependent Cushing. SCLC (50%), Bronchial Carcinoid (20%), Pancreatic NET, Thymic Carcinoid. High ACTH, No HDDST Suppression, Localisation with CT/DOTATATE PET.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Differentiate ectopic ACTH from Cushing disease using HDDST, IPSS, CRH test
  • [ ] Identify common ectopic ACTH sources and their clinical features
  • [ ] Apply localisation strategies (CT, ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET, Bronchoscopy)
  • [ ] Outline medical management (ketoconazole, metyrapone, bilateral adrenalectomy)
  • [ ] Recognise cyclical ectopic ACTH and paraneoplastic features

2. Aetiology & Common Sources

SourceFrequencyACTH LevelClinical Features
Small Cell Lung Cancer (SCLC)50%Very High (>500 pg/mL)Rapid Onset, Severe Hypokalaemia, Hyperpigmentation, Weight Loss
Bronchial Carcinoid20-25%HighIndolent Course, Often Resectable, Atypical Carcinoid
Pancreatic NET10-15%HighMay Co-secrete Other Hormones (Insulin, Glucagon)
Thymic Carcinoid5-10%HighAssociated with MEN1
Phaeochromocytoma / Paraganglioma5%VariableParoxysmal Hypertension, Catecholamine Excess
Medullary Thyroid Cancer2-5%ElevatedDiarrhoea, Flushing, MEN2
Other (Ovarian, Gastric, Renal)RareVariableOccult Primary

3. Clinical Features — Ectopic ACTH vs Cushing Disease

FeatureEctopic ACTHCushing Disease
ACTH LevelVery High (Often >200 pg/mL, up to >1000)Normal / Mildly Elevated (10-50 pg/mL)
OnsetRapid (Weeks)Insidious (Months-Years)
HypokalaemiaSevere (<2.5 mmol/L, 80-90%)Mild/Moderate (10-20%)
Metabolic AlkalosisMarked (HCO₃⁻ >30)Mild
HyperpigmentationProminent (High ACTH → MSH Cross-Reactivity)Mild
Weight LossCommon (Cancer Cachexia)Weight Gain (Central Obesity)
HypertensionSevere, ResistantModerate
Glucose IntoleranceSevere (Insulin Resistance)Moderate
Mental StatusConfusion, Lethargy (Electrolyte Disturbance)Depression, Cognitive Changes

4. Diagnostic Differentiation

Key Investigations

TestEctopic ACTHCushing Disease
Plasma ACTH>100-200 pg/mL (Often >500)10-50 pg/mL
HDDST (2mg q6h × 48h)No Suppression (<50%)>50% Suppression
CRH Test (100µg IV)No ACTH RiseACTH ↑ >50%
IPSSNo Central:Peripheral GradientCentral:Peripheral >2 (Basal) / >3 (Post-CRH)
MRI PituitaryNormal / IncidentalomaMicroadenoma (80-90%)
ImagingCT Chest/Abd/Pelvis + ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PETMRI Pituitary (Microadenoma)

5. Diagnostic Algorithm

Cushing Syndrome Confirmed + ACTH ≥10-20 pg/mL
         │
         └── HDDST (2mg q6h × 48h)
                 │
                 ├── NO SUPPRESSION (<50%) → ECTOPIC ACTH
                 │       │
                 │       ├── ACTH Very High (>200 pg/mL) → Strongly Suggests Ectopic
                 │       │
                 │       └── LOCALISATION WORKUP
                 │               ├── CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis (Contrast)
                 │               ├── ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT (Somatostatin Receptor Imaging)
                 │               ├── Bronchoscopy (If Lung Lesion)
                 │               └── Octreotide Scan (If DOTATATE Unavailable)
                 │
                 └── SUPPRESSION >50% → CUSHING DISEASE
                         MRI Pituitary → TSS

6. Localisation Strategy

Step 1: CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis (Contrast)

TargetDetails
LungSCLC, Carcinoid, Metastases
MediastinumThymic Carcinoid, Lymph Nodes
Abdomen/PelvisPancreatic NET, Adrenal (Rule Out Adrenal Cushing), Phaeochromocytoma
ResolutionThin Slice (1-2mm), Arterial + Venous Phase

Step 2: Functional Imaging

ModalitySensitivitySpecificityIndication
⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT90-95% (SSTR2+)90%+First-Line (SCLC, Carcinoid, Pancreatic NET, Phaeo)
Octreotide Scan (Octreoscan)80-90%80%If DOTATATE Unavailable
FDG-PET/CTHigh (SCLC)Low (Net)SCLC Staging; Not for Well-Differentiated NET
Bronchoscopy——Central Bronchial Lesions (Carcinoid, SCLC)

7. Common Ectopic ACTH Sources

Tumour Type% of Ectopic ACTHACTH LevelKey Features
SCLC50%Very High (>500 pg/mL)Smoker, Weight Loss, Paraneoplastic (SIADH, LEMS)
Bronchial Carcinoid (Typical/Atypical)20-25%HighIndolent, Long Survival, Often Resectable
Pancreatic NET10-15%HighMay Co-secrete Insulin/Glucagon/PP
Thymic Carcinoid5-10%HighYoung, MEN1 Association
Phaeochromocytoma/Paraganglioma5%VariableParoxysmal HTN, Catecholamine Excess
Medullary Thyroid Cancer2-5%ElevatedDiarrhoea, Flushing, MEN2
Other (Ovarian, Gastric, Renal)<5%VariableOccult Primary

8. Medical Management

First-Line: Surgical Resection (If Localised/Resectable)

TumourApproach
Bronchial CarcinoidSurgical Resection (Lobectomy/Sleeve) — Curative if Localised
Thymic CarcinoidThymectomy ± Mediastinal Lymphadenectomy
Pancreatic NETDistal Pancreatectomy / Whipple (Per Location)
SCLCSurgery Rarely Curative (Usually Metastatic)

Medical Therapy (Unresectable/Metastatic/Pre-Op Bridge)

DrugDoseMechanismMonitoring
Ketoconazole200-400mg Q6H (Max 1200mg/day)CYP11B1 Inhibition (11β-Hydroxylase)LFTs q2wk, Adrenal Insufficiency Risk, QTc
Metyrapone250-750mg Q8H (Max 6g/day)11β-Hydroxylase InhibitionHypotension, Oedema, Drowsiness, GI
Osilodrostat2-30mg BD11β-Hydroxylase InhibitionECG (QT), Hypokalaemia, Liver Enzymes
EtoposideSCLC ProtocolChemotherapyCBC, LFTs, Neuropathy
Somatostatin AnaloguesOctreotide LAR 20-40mg IM q4wkSSTR2 Agonist (↓ ACTH Secretion)GI, Gallstones, Hyperglycaemia
Mifepristone300-1200mg/dayGlucocorticoid Receptor AntagonistEndometrial Changes, K+ Monitoring

9. Bilateral Adrenalectomy

Indications

IndicationDetails
Failed Medical TherapyUncontrolled Hypercortisolism Despite Maximal Medical Rx
Life-Threatening ComplicationsSevere Hypokalaemia, Resistant HTN, Psychosis, Cardiomyopathy
Unresectable Ectopic SourceMetastatic SCLC, Occult Primary
Patient PreferenceDefinitive Cure After Failed Options

Procedure

AspectDetails
ProcedureLaparoscopic Bilateral Adrenalectomy (Preferred)
Immediate Post-OpHydrocortisone 100mg IV q6h + Fludrocortisone 0.1mg OD
Fludrocortisone0.05-0.2mg OD (Life-Long Mineralocorticoid Replacement)
GlucocorticoidHydrocortisone 15-20mg AM + 5-10mg PM (Life-Long)
Follow-upLifelong Endocrine Follow-up; Crisis Card; Medic Alert

10. Complications & Prognosis

ComplicationIncidenceManagement
Severe Hypokalaemia80-90%Aggressive K⁺ Replacement (IV KCl), Spironolactone/Amiloride
Metabolic AlkalosisCommonKCl, Acetazolamide (Rare)
Hyperglycaemia / Diabetes70-80%Insulin Sliding Scale / Basal-Bolus
Hypertension90%Multiple Agents (Including Spironolactone)
Osteoporosis50%Bisphosphonates, Calcium, Vitamin D
Psychiatric30-50%Antidepressants, Antipsychotics
Infection Risk↑ (Immunosuppression)Prophylactic TMP-SMX if Prolonged Steroids

Prognosis by Source

SourceMedian SurvivalCurative Potential
Bronchial Carcinoid10-20 YearsHigh (If Resected Early)
Pancreatic NET5-10 YearsModerate
Thymic Carcinoid5-10 YearsModerate (MEN1 Association)
SCLC6-12 MonthsPoor (Palliative)
PhaeochromocytomaVariableGood if Resected
Medullary Thyroid Cancer5-10 YearsRET Mutation Guides Surgery

11. Examination Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Ectopic ACTH %10-15% of ACTH-Dependent Cushing
ACTH Level>200 pg/mL (Often >500) vs Cushing Disease (10-50)
HDDSTNo Suppression (<50%) — Differentiates from Cushing Disease
SCLC50% of Ectopic ACTH; Very High ACTH, Severe Hypokalaemia
Bronchial Carcinoid20-25%; Indolent, Resectable, Better Prognosis
HDDSTNo Suppression = Ectopic/Adrenal; >50% Suppression = Cushing Disease
IPSSNo Central:Peripheral Gradient = Ectopic
LocalisationCT Chest/Abd → ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET (Best) → Bronchoscopy
Medical RxKetoconazole, Metyrapone, Osilodrostat, Etoposide (SCLC)
Bilateral AdrenalectomyLast Resort (Life-Long Gluco + Mineralocorticoid Replacement)
Cyclical Ectopic ACTHDaily UFC × 2-4 Weeks for Diagnosis

12. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Ectopic vs Cushing DiseaseEctopic: ACTH Very High, No HDDST Suppression; Cushing Disease: ACTH Normal/High, HDDST Suppresses
Ectopic vs Adrenal CushingEctopic: ACTH High; Adrenal: ACTH Suppressed
SCLC vs CarcinoidSCLC: Aggressive, High ACTH, Short Survival; Carcinoid: Indolent, Resectable
IPSS vs CRH TestIPSS = Gold Standard Localisation; CRH Test = Functional (ACTH Rise >50% = Pituitary)
Bilateral AdrenalectomyLast Resort; Life-Long Gluco + Mineralo Replacement; Medical Alert Bracelet
Ectopic CRHRare (<1%); CRH High, ACTH High; CRH Stimulation Test Positive

13. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Ectopic ACTH Syndrome))
    Frequency
      10-15% of ACTH-Dependent Cushing
    Sources
      SCLC (50%) - Aggressive, High ACTH
      Bronchial Carcinoid (20-25%) - Indolent, Resectable
      Pancreatic NET (10-15%)
      Thymic Carcinoid (5-10%) - MEN1
      Phaeo/Paraganglioma (5%)
      MTC (2-5%) - MEN2
    Diagnosis
      ACTH >200 pg/mL
      HDDST No Suppression
      IPSS No Gradient
      MRI Pituitary Normal
    Localisation
      CT Chest/Abd/Pelvis
      68Ga-DOTATATE PET (Gold Standard)
      Bronchoscopy for Central Lesions
    Management
      Resectable → Surgery (Cure)
      Unresectable → Ketoconazole/Metyrapone/Osilodrostat
      Refractory → Bilateral Adrenalectomy
    Complications
      Hypokalaemia (80-90%)
      Metabolic Alkalosis
      Hyperglycaemia/Diabetes
      Hypertension
      Osteoporosis
    Prognosis
      Carcinoid: 10-20yr
      SCLC: 6-12mo

14. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Ectopic ACTH %10-15% of ACTH-Dependent Cushing
ACTH Level>200 pg/mL (Often >500) vs Cushing Disease (10-50)
HDDST ResultNo Suppression (<50%) — Differentiates from Cushing Disease
Most Common SourceSCLC (50%)
Best Localisation⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT (Gold Standard)
SCLC FeaturesSmoker, Weight Loss, Severe Hypokalaemia, Paraneoplastic
Bronchial CarcinoidIndolent, Resectable, 10-20yr Survival
Medical TherapyKetoconazole, Metyrapone, Osilodrostat, Etoposide (SCLC)
Bilateral AdrenalectomyLast Resort (Life-Long Replacement)
Ectopic vs Cushing DiseaseACTH Very High, No HDDST Suppression, No IPSS Gradient
Adrenal vs EctopicAdrenal: ACTH Suppressed; Ectopic: ACTH High
IPSSNo Central:Peripheral Gradient = Ectopic

15. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Cushing Syndrome Overview|Cushing Syndrome Overview]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Cushing Syndrome Overview]], [[Cushing Disease]], [[Adrenal Cushing]], [[Cyclical Cushing]], [[Dynamic Testing Principles]], [[Neuroendocrine Tumours]]

16. MCQs (10)

  1. Ectopic ACTH ACTH level:

A. >200 pg/mL (often >500)

B. Suppressed

C. Normal

D. Low

E. Variable

  1. Commonest ectopic ACTH tumours:

A. Small cell lung cancer, bronchial/thymic carcinoid

B. Pituitary adenoma

C. Adrenal adenoma

D. Renal cell cancer

E. Medullary thyroid cancer

  1. HDDST in ectopic ACTH:

A. No suppression

B. Suppresses >50%

C. Partial

D. Variable

E. Cortisol decreases

  1. CRH test in ectopic ACTH:

A. No ACTH rise

B. ACTH rise >50%

C. Variable

D. Cortisol rise

E. ACTH fall

  1. Ectopic ACTH hypokalaemia:

A. Severe (mineralocorticoid effect of high cortisol)

B. Mild

C. Absent

D. Hyperkalaemia

E. Variable

  1. Ectopic ACTH skin hyperpigmentation:

A. Marked (very high ACTH/MSH)

B. Absent

C. Mild

D. Only in Addison's

E. Variable

  1. Treatment ectopic ACTH:

A. Resection if localised; medical (ketoconazole, metyrapone) if metastatic

B. Pituitary surgery

C. Adrenalectomy

D. Radiotherapy

E. Observation only

  1. Ectopic ACTH vs Cushing disease:

A. ACTH >200 + no HDDST suppression + no CRH response

B. ACTH <50 + HDDST suppression

C. ACTH suppressed

D. Same features

E. Only imaging differs

  1. Carcinoid syndrome vs ectopic ACTH:

A. Carcinoid: serotonin (5-HIAA); Ectopic ACTH: cortisol

B. Same

C. Both cause Cushing

D. Both cause flushing

E. Both cause diarrhoea

  1. Paraneoplastic ectopic ACTH:

A. Can occur with any neuroendocrine tumour

B. Only SCLC

C. Only carcinoid

D. Only thymoma

E. Only pancreatic NET

17. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 55yo smoker: Cushing features, severe hypokalaemia 2.5, ACTH 450, chest CT 3cm spiculated mass. Diagnosis?

A. Ectopic ACTH (small cell lung cancer)

B. Cushing disease

C. Adrenal Cushing

D. Phaeochromocytoma

E. CAH

  1. Same patient: surgical candidate?

A. Usually metastatic at diagnosis → medical therapy (ketoconazole, metyrapone)

B. Pituitary surgery

C. Adrenalectomy

D. Curative surgery always

E. Observation

  1. 30yo woman: Cushing, ACTH 300, pituitary MRI normal, chest CT shows 2cm bronchial carcinoid. Treatment?

A. Surgical resection of carcinoid (curative if localised)

B. Pituitary surgery

C. Bilateral adrenalectomy

D. Ketoconazole only

E. Observation

  1. Ectopic ACTH medical management:

A. Ketoconazole, metyrapone, etomidate (IV), mitotane

B. Hydrocortisone

C. Fludrocortisone

D. Dexamethasone

E. Carbimazole

  1. Cyclical ectopic ACTH:

A. Rare; repeated testing needed

B. Does not exist

C. Same as cyclical Cushing disease

D. Always SCLC

E. Always carcinoid

18. Flashcards

  • Q: Ectopic ACTH

A: ACTH >200 pg/mL; no DST suppression; no CRH response

  • Q: Common tumours

A: SCLC, bronchial/thymic carcinoid, pancreatic NET, medullary thyroid Ca

  • Q: HDDST

A: No cortisol suppression

  • Q: CRH test

A: No ACTH rise

  • Q: Hypokalaemia

A: Severe (cortisol mineralocorticoid effect at high levels)

  • Q: Hyperpigmentation

A: Marked (very high ACTH → MSH cross-reactivity)

  • Q: Treatment

A: Resection if localised; medical (ketoconazole, metyrapone) if metastatic

  • Q: SCLC ectopic ACTH

A: Usually advanced; medical Rx; poor prognosis

  • Q: Carcinoid ectopic ACTH

A: May be localised → surgical cure possible

  • Q: Differentiation from Cushing disease

A: ACTH >200 + no HDDST suppression + no CRH response

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. >200 pg/mL (often >500) — Ectopic ACTH ACTH level:
  1. Small cell lung cancer, bronchial/thymic carcinoid — Commonest ectopic ACTH tumours:
  1. No suppression — HDDST in ectopic ACTH:
  1. No ACTH rise — CRH test in ectopic ACTH:
  1. Severe (mineralocorticoid effect of high cortisol) — Ectopic ACTH hypokalaemia:
  1. Marked (very high ACTH/MSH) — Ectopic ACTH skin hyperpigmentation:
  1. Resection if localised; medical (ketoconazole, metyrapone) if metastatic — Treatment ectopic ACTH:
  1. ACTH >200 + no HDDST suppression + no CRH response — Ectopic ACTH vs Cushing disease:
  1. Carcinoid: serotonin (5-HIAA); Ectopic ACTH: cortisol — Carcinoid syndrome vs ectopic ACTH:
  1. Can occur with any neuroendocrine tumour — Paraneoplastic ectopic ACTH:

SBAs

  1. Ectopic ACTH (small cell lung cancer) — 55yo smoker: Cushing features, severe hypokalaemia 2.5, ACTH 450, chest CT 3cm spiculated mass. Diagnosis?
  1. Usually metastatic at diagnosis → medical therapy (ketoconazole, metyrapone) — Same patient: surgical candidate?
  1. Surgical resection of carcinoid (curative if localised) — 30yo woman: Cushing, ACTH 300, pituitary MRI normal, chest CT shows 2cm bronchial carcinoid. Treatment?
  1. Ketoconazole, metyrapone, etomidate (IV), mitotane — Ectopic ACTH medical management:
  1. Rare; repeated testing needed — Cyclical ectopic ACTH:
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.4.11 Phaeochromocytoma Paraganglioma
[!info]
Phaeochromocytoma = Catecholamine-Secreting Tumour of Adrenal Medulla. Paraganglioma = Extra-Adrenal (Sympathetic/Parasympathetic). "Rule of 10s" (Classic Teaching). Alpha-Blockade BEFORE Beta-Blockade — Mandatory Pre-Op Prep. Genetic Testing Mandatory (30-40% Hereditary).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise clinical presentation (paroxysmal hypertension, headaches, sweating, palpitations)
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic criteria (plasma free metanephrines, 24h urine metanephrines)
  • [ ] Apply localisation imaging (CT/MRI, ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET, ¹²³I-MIBG)
  • [ ] Execute pre-operative alpha-blockade protocol (phenoxybenzamine)
  • [ ] Indicate genetic testing and recognise hereditary syndromes (MEN2, VHL, NF1, SDHx)
  • [ ] Differentiate phaeochromocytoma vs paraganglioma

2. Epidemiology & "Rule of 10s" (Classic Teaching)

Rule of 10sDetail
10% BilateralAdrenal Phaeochromocytoma
10% MalignantPhaeochromocytoma; Paraganglioma Higher (30-50%)
10% Extra-AdrenalParaganglioma (Sympathetic Chain, Organ of Zuckerkandl)
10% FamilialActually 30-40% (MEN2, VHL, NF1, SDHx, TMEM127, MAX)
10% Pediatric<18 Years
10% Extra-AbdominalParaganglioma (Head/Neck)
Modern View: "Rule of 10s" is an Approximation; Heritability 30-40% is Key Takeaway.

3. Clinical Presentation

Classic Triad (Episodic)

SymptomFrequencyMechanism
Headache90%Severe, Throbbing, Often Precipitated
Sweating70-80%Profuse, Generalised
Palpitations60-70%Tachycardia, Arrhythmias

Paroxysmal vs Sustained Hypertension

PatternFrequencyFeatures
Paroxysmal50-60%Episodic SBP >200, Duration Minutes-Hours
Sustained30-40%Persistent HTN, Paroxysms Superimposed
Normotensive5-10%Normotensive Between Spells

Additional Features

SystemFeatures
NeurologicalTremor, Anxiety, Panic Attacks, Tremor
GINausea, Vomiting, Abdominal Pain, Constipation
DermatologicalFlushing, Pallor (Post-Attack)
OphthalmicBlurred Vision, Hypertensive Retinopathy
MetabolicHypoglycaemia (Rare), Hyperglycaemia, Weight Loss

4. Diagnosis — Biochemical Confirmation

Gold Standard: Plasma Free Metanephrines

TestSensitivitySpecificityCut-off
Plasma Free Metanephrines99%89%Metanephrine ≥ Upper Limit OR Normetanephrine ≥ Upper Limit
24h Urine Metanephrines95%95%Metanephrine + Normetanephrine >ULN

Sample Collection Protocol (Critical)

RequirementDetails
Fasting≥8 Hours (Preferably Morning)
PositionSupine ≥30 Minutes Prior
Medications to AvoidL-DOPA, SSRIs, TCAs, MAOIs, Cocaine, Amphetamines, Sympathomimetics (≥7 Days)
Drugs Causing False PositivesL-DOPA, Metoclopramide, Antidepressants (TCA, SSRI), Cocaine, Amphetamines, Buspirone, Reserpine

24h Urine Metanephrines (Alternative)

Collection24h Urine + Acid Preservative (HCl)
AdvantagesLess Affected by Acute Stress; Integrates Over 24h
False NegativesIncomplete Collection, Acidification Failure

5. Localisation Imaging

First-Line: CT/MRI Adrenal

ModalityProtocol
CT (Adrenal Protocol)1mm Slices, Arterial + Venous Phase, Non-Contrast for HU
MRI (Adrenal Protocol)T1/T2, In/Out Phase (Chemical Shift), Post-Gadolinium

Functional Imaging (Gold Standard for Localisation)

ModalitySensitivitySpecificityIndication
⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT90-95%90%+First-Line (SSTR2+)
¹²³I-MIBG Scintigraphy80-90%80-90%If DOTATATE Unavailable; MIBG-Avid Tumours
¹⁸F-FDG PETHigh (Malignant)LowerSCLC, Poorly Differentiated NET
Octreoscan (¹¹¹In-Octreotide)80-85%80%If DOTATATE/MIBG Unavailable

6. Genetic Testing — Mandatory (30-40% Hereditary)

SyndromeGenePhaeo FeaturesOther Features
MEN2ARET (Codon 634)Phaeo 50% (Often Bilateral), MTC 95%, pHPT 20-30%
MEN2BRET M918T (95%)Phaeo 50%, MTC 100%Marfanoid, Mucosal Neuromas, Café-au-lait
VHLVHLPhaeo (Often Bilateral), RCC, Haemangioblastomas (CNS/Retina)
NF1NF1Phaeo (Unilateral), Café-au-lait Spots, Neurofibromas
SDHBSDHBPGL (Extra-Adrenal), High Malignancy (30-50%), GIST, RCC
SDHDSDHDHNPGL (Head/Neck PGL), Phaeo
SDHA/SDHC/SDHCSDHA/CVariable
TMEM127/MAXTMEM127/MAXPhaeo/PGL

Genetic Testing Indications

IndicationAction
All Phaeo/PGL PatientsMandatory (30-40% Hereditary)
Bilateral/MultifocalHigh Probability Genetic
Family HistoryStrong Indication
Young Age (<40)Higher Genetic Yield
Malignant PhaeoSDHB Suspicion High

7. Pre-Operative Preparation — Mandatory Protocol

Golden Rule: ALPHA-BLOCKADE BEFORE BETA-BLOCKADE

Alpha-Blockade Protocol (Phenoxybenzamine)

StepDoseTarget
Starting10mg BD (Oral)—
TitrationIncrease by 10mg BD every 2-3 DaysSBP <130/80 Supine, <120/70 Standing
Maintenance20-80mg/day (Divided BD/TDS)HR 60-80 bpm
Duration≥2 Weeks (Ideally 3-4 Weeks)Before Surgery

Beta-Blockade (ONLY After Alpha-Blockade Established)

AgentDoseTiming
Propranolol20-40mg TDSAFTER Alpha-Blockade Established
Atenolol/Metoprolol50-100mg OD/BDAfter Alpha-Blockade Adequate

Volume Expansion

InterventionDetails
IV Fluids2-3L 0.9% NaCl/Day (Start with Alpha-Blockade)
GoalCVP 8-12 mmHg, UOP >0.5 mL/kg/h
MonitorNa+, K+, Glucose, CVP, UOP q1-2h

8. Paraganglioma (Extra-Adrenal) — Key Differences

FeaturePhaeochromocytoma (Adrenal)Paraganglioma (Extra-Adrenal)
LocationAdrenal MedullaSympathetic Chain (Organ of Zuckerkandl — Most Common)
Malignancy Rate~10%30-50% (SDHB Highest)
Malignancy DefinitionMetastases (Non-Chromaffin Tissue)Metastases (Bone, Liver, Lung, Lymph Nodes)
GeneticsMEN2, VHL, NF1, SDHxSDHB (High Malignancy), SDHD (HNPGL)
ImagingAdrenal CT/MRIWhole Body (Neck to Pelvis)

Head & Neck Paraganglioma (HNPGL)

FeatureDetails
LocationCarotid Body, Jugulotympanic, Vagal, Laryngeal
GeneSDHD (Maternal Imprinting — Paternal Transmission), SDHC, SDHAF2
MalignancyRare (<5%)
SymptomsMass Effect (Cranial Nerve Palsies, Tinnitus, Dysphagia)
TreatmentSurgery ± Radiotherapy (Stereotactic)

9. Pre-Operative Optimisation Checklist

ParameterTargetTimeline
Alpha-BlockadePhenoxybenzamine 20-80mg/day≥2 Weeks (Ideally 3-4 Weeks)
BP ControlSupine <130/80; Standing <120/70Achieved Before Surgery
Heart Rate60-80 bpm (Add Beta-Blocker After Alpha)After Alpha-Blockade
Volume StatusEuvolaemic (CVP 8-12, UOP >0.5 mL/kg/h)At Surgery
GlucoseNormoglycaemia (Insulin Sliding Scale if Diabetic)Peri-operative
ElectrolytesK+ 3.5-5.0 (Correct Hypokalaemia Pre-Op)Pre-Op

10. Surgical Management

Laparoscopic Adrenalectomy — Gold Standard

ApproachIndication
Laparoscopic TransabdominalStandard (<8-10cm, No Invasion)
Posterior Retroperitoneoscopic (PRA)Small Tumours, Prior Abdominal Surgery
Open Adrenalectomy>10cm, Suspected Carcinoma, Invasion, Prior Surgery

Intraoperative Management

ChallengeManagement
Tumour ManipulationCatecholamine Surge → Deepen Anaesthesia, Esmolol/Nitroprusside
Venous SamplingAdrenal Vein Sampling (If Bilateral/Uncertain Laterality)
Bilateral PhaeoStaged (6-8 Weeks Apart) or Simultaneous (Experienced Centre)

Post-Operative Care

ComplicationMonitoring
HypotensionVasopressors (Norepinephrine), Fluid Resuscitation
HypoglycaemiaGlucose Monitoring q1-2h (First 24h)
Adrenal InsufficiencyHydrocortisone 50-100mg IV q6h (If Bilateral/Contra-lateral Suppressed)

11. Malignancy & Follow-up

Malignancy Criteria (No Histological Benign/Malignant)

CriterionDefinition
MetastasesNon-Chromaffin Tissue (Bone, Liver, Lung, Lymph Nodes)
Local InvasionAdjacent Organ Invasion
RecurrenceLocal/Distant Recurrence Post-Resection

SDHB Mutation Carriers — High Malignancy Risk

FeatureDetails
PGL (Extra-Adrenal)Most Common in SDHB Carriers
Malignancy Rate30-50% (Highest of All SDHx)
SurveillanceAnnual ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT + Biochemical

12. Follow-up & Surveillance

TimepointBiochemicalImaging
Post-Op 2 WeeksMetanephrines—
3 MonthsMetanephrinesCT/MRI if Residual Concern
6 MonthsMetanephrinesMRI/CT (If Residual)
Annual (×5yrs)MetanephrinesMRI/CT Alternating
Lifelong (If Hereditary)Annual MetanephrinesMRI/CT q1-2yr

13. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Rule of 10s10% Bilateral, 10% Malignant, 10% Extra-Adrenal, 10% Familial (Actually 30-40%)
Classic TriadHeadache, Sweating, Palpitations (Episodic) + Hypertension
Biochemical Gold StandardPlasma Free Metanephrines (Sensitivity 99%)
False Positive MetanephrinesDrugs (L-DOPA, Antidepressants), Stress, Renal Failure
Alpha-Blockade FirstPhenoxybenzamine 10mg BD → Titrate 2+ Weeks; Beta-Blocker ONLY After Alpha
Phenoxybenzamine Dose10mg BD → Titrate to 20-80mg/day; Target SBP <130/80
Beta-Blocker TimingONLY After Alpha-Blockade (Else Unopposed Alpha → Hypertensive Crisis)
Pre-Op Volume Expansion2-3L 0.9% NaCl/Day During Alpha-Blockade
Genetic TestingMandatory for All (30-40% Hereditary); MEN2, VHL, NF1, SDHx
SDHBHighest Malignancy Risk (30-50%); Extra-Adrenal PGL Common
SDHDHNPGL (Head/Neck PGL); Maternal Imprinting (Paternal Transmission)
Malignancy DefinitionMetastases to Non-Chromaffin Tissue (Bone, Liver, Lung, Nodes)
Malignancy SurveillanceAnnual ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET + Metanephrines (SDHB Carriers)
Paraganglioma vs PhaeoExtra-Adrenal; Higher Malignancy (30-50%); SDHB/SDHD Genetics
Pre-Op Alpha-BlockadePhenoxybenzamine 10mg BD → Titrate 2+ Weeks; Beta After Alpha
Bilateral PhaeoStaged Surgery (6-8 Weeks) or Simultaneous (Expert Centre)
Malignancy CriteriaMetastases to Non-Chromaffin Tissue (Not Size/Histology)

14. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Phaeo vs ParagangliomaPhaeo = Adrenal; PGL = Extra-Adrenal (Sympathetic Chain/Organ of Zuckerkandl)
MalignancyMetastases to Non-Chromaffin Tissue = Malignant (Not Size/Histology)
Alpha vs Beta BlockadeAlpha FIRST (Phentolamine/Phenoxybenzamine); Beta ONLY After Alpha
Beta-Blocker FirstDANGEROUS → Unopposed Alpha → Hypertensive Crisis
SDHB vs SDHDSDHB = Malignant PGL (Abdomen/Pelvis); SDHD = HNPGL (Head/Neck)
MEN2A vs MEN2BMEN2A: MTC + Phaeo + pHPT; MEN2B: MTC + Phaeo + Marfanoid/Neuromas
MIBG vs DOTATATEDOTATATE Superior Sensitivity (90-95% vs 80-90%); MIBG for MIBG-Avid Tumours
Genetic TestingMandatory for ALL (30-40% Hereditary) — Not Just Syndromic

15. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Phaeochromocytoma/Paraganglioma))
    Clinical
      Triad: Headache, Sweating, Palpitations
      Hypertension: Paroxysmal (50%) / Sustained (30%)
    Biochemistry
      Plasma Free Metanephrines (99% Sens)
      24h Urine Metanephrines
      False +: Drugs, Stress, Renal Failure
    Localisation
      CT/MRI Adrenal Protocol
      68Ga-DOTATATE PET (Gold Standard)
      MIBG (If DOTATATE Unavailable)
    Genetics (30-40% Hereditary)
      MEN2A/B (RET)
      VHL (Bilateral Phaeo + RCC)
      NF1 (Unilateral Phaeo)
      SDHB (Malignant PGL)
      SDHD (HNPGL)
      TMEM127/MAX
    Pre-Op Prep
      Alpha-Blockade FIRST (Phenoxybenzamine)
      Beta-Blocker AFTER Alpha
      Volume Expansion (2-3L Saline)
    Surgery
      Lap Adrenalectomy (Gold Standard)
      Open if >10cm/Invasion
    Genetics
      MEN2A/B (RET), VHL, NF1, SDHx, TMEM127, MAX
      SDHB = Malignant PGL
      SDHD = HNPGL
    Malignancy
      Metastases to Non-Chromaffin Tissue
      SDHB: 30-50% Malignant
    Surveillance
      Annual Metanephrines + DOTATATE PET (Hereditary)

16. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Classic TriadHeadache, Sweating, Palpitations + Paroxysmal Hypertension
Biochemical Gold StandardPlasma Free Metanephrines (Sensitivity 99%)
Rule of 10s10% Bilateral, 10% Malignant, 10% Extra-Adrenal, 10% Familial (Actually 30-40%)
Alpha-Blockade FirstPhenoxybenzamine 10mg BD → Titrate; Beta-Blocker ONLY After Alpha
Phenoxybenzamine Dose10mg BD → Titrate 20-80mg/day; Target SBP <130/80 Supine
Beta-Blocker TimingAFTER Alpha-Blockade (Else Unopposed Alpha → Hypertensive Crisis)
Pre-Op Volume2-3L 0.9% NaCl/Day During Alpha-Blockade
Genetic TestingMandatory for ALL (30-40% Hereditary)
SDHBHighest Malignancy Risk (30-50%); Extra-Adrenal PGL Common
SDHDHNPGL (Head/Neck PGL); Maternal Imprinting
Malignancy DefinitionMetastases to Non-Chromaffin Tissue (Bone, Liver, Lung, Nodes)
SDHB SurveillanceAnnual ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET + Metanephrines
Beta-Blocker TimingAFTER Alpha-Blockade (Else Unopposed Alpha → Crisis)
Phaeo vs PGLPhaeo = Adrenal; PGL = Extra-Adrenal; PGL Higher Malignancy
Pre-Op Alpha-BlockadeMandatory ≥2 Weeks; Target SBP <130/80 Supine

17. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Adrenal Disorders|Adrenal Disorders]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
