See also: [[Hypoparathyroidism]], [[Vitamin D Metabolism & Deficiency]], [[Pseudohypoparathyroidism]], [[Acute Pancreatitis]], [[Massive Transfusion]]

17. MCQs (10)

  1. Hypocalcaemia definition:

A. Adjusted Ca <2.15 mmol/L (or ionised Ca <1.15)

B. Ca <2.5

C. Ca <2.0

D. Ca <2.3

E. Ionised Ca <1.3

  1. Commonest cause hypocalcaemia:

A. Hypoparathyroidism (post-surgical)

B. Vit D deficiency

C. CKD

D. Acute pancreatitis

E. Massive transfusion

  1. Hypocalcaemia symptoms:

A. Tetany (Chvostek/Trousseau), seizures, QT prolongation, laryngospasm, bronchospasm

B. Hypertension

C. Tachycardia

D. Hyperreflexia only

E. Asymptomatic

  1. Acute severe hypocalcaemia Rx:

A. IV Ca gluconate 10-20ml 10% over 10min (cardiac monitor)

B. Oral Ca only

C. IV phosphate

D. Vit D only

E. Cinacalcet

  1. Chronic hypocalcaemia Rx:

A. Oral Ca + active Vit D (calcitriol/alfacalcidol) + Mg

B. IV Ca lifelong

C. PTH injection

D. Cinacalcet

E. Bisphosphonates

  1. Hypocalcaemia in acute pancreatitis:

A. Ca soaps in fat necrosis + hypoalbuminaemia + glucagon -> dCa

B. Hypercalcaemia

C. Normal Ca

D. Only in severe

E. Due to hyperparathyroidism

  1. Hypocalcaemia in massive transfusion:

A. Citrate chelation of Ca -> hypocalcaemia; give Ca if prolonged

B. Hypercalcaemia

C. No effect

D. Only if liver failure

E. Not relevant

  1. Mg and hypocalcaemia:

A. Low Mg -> PTH resistance + impaired PTH secretion -> refractory hypocalcaemia; replete Mg first

B. Mg has no effect

C. High Mg causes hypocalcaemia

D. Only in CKD

E. Not relevant

  1. Alkalosis and hypocalcaemia:

A. Alkalosis u albumin binding -> d ionised Ca -> tetany; correct pH

B. Alkalosis u ionised Ca

C. No effect

D. Only in respiratory

E. Acidosis causes hypocalcaemia

  1. Pseudohypocalcaemia:

A. Low albumin -> low total Ca but normal ionised Ca; measure ionised Ca

B. True hypocalcaemia

C. High albumin

D. High total Ca

E. Low ionised Ca

18. SBA Questions (10)

  1. Post-thyroidectomy: perioral tingling, Chvostek+, adj Ca 1.8. Immediate?

A. IV Ca gluconate 10ml 10% over 10min (cardiac monitor) -> oral Ca/calcitriol

B. Oral Ca only

C. Observation

D. IV phosphate

E. Cinacalcet

  1. Acute pancreatitis: Ca 1.9, albumin 25. Ionised Ca 0.9. Rx?

A. IV Ca gluconate + monitor Mg/albumin

B. Oral Ca only

C. Albumin infusion

D. Cinacalcet

E. Observation

  1. Massive transfusion: citrate toxicity, Ca 1.7. Management?

A. IV Ca gluconate 10ml 10% per 4 units blood; monitor ionised Ca

B. Oral Ca

C. Heparin instead

D. Albumin

E. Stop transfusion

  1. Hypocalcaemia + low Mg: Ca not correcting despite IV Ca. Next?

A. Replete Mg first (Mg needed for PTH secretion/action)

B. More IV Ca

C. Cinacalcet

D. Calcitonin

E. Stop Ca

  1. Alkalosis + tetany: pH 7.55, ionised Ca 0.9. Management?

A. Correct alkalosis (breathing control/paper bag) + IV Ca if severe

B. More IV Ca

C. Cinacalcet

D. Sedation

E. Intubation

19. Flashcards

  • Q: Hypocalcaemia

A: Adj Ca <2.15 mmol/L; ionised Ca <1.15 mmol/L

  • Q: Commonest cause

A: Hypoparathyroidism (post-surgical); Vit D deficiency; CKD; pancreatitis

  • Q: Acute Rx

A: IV Ca gluconate 10-20ml 10% over 10min (cardiac monitor)

  • Q: Chronic Rx

A: Oral Ca + active Vit D (calcitriol/alfacalcidol) + Mg

  • Q: Mg importance

A: Low Mg -> refractory hypocalcaemia; replete Mg first

  • Q: Pancreatitis

A: Ca soaps + hypoalbuminaemia + glucagon -> dCa

  • Q: Massive transfusion

A: Citrate chelation -> hypocalcaemia; Ca per 4 units blood

  • Q: Alkalosis

A: u albumin binding -> d ionised Ca -> tetany; correct pH

  • Q: Pseudohypocalcaemia

A: Low albumin -> low total Ca, normal ionised Ca; measure ionised Ca

  • Q: Chvostek/Trousseau

A: Tetany signs; low ionised Ca

20. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Adjusted Ca <2.15 mmol/L (or ionised Ca <1.15) — Hypocalcaemia definition:
  1. Hypoparathyroidism (post-surgical) — Commonest cause hypocalcaemia:
  1. Tetany (Chvostek/Trousseau), seizures, QT prolongation, laryngospasm, bronchospasm — Hypocalcaemia symptoms:
  1. IV Ca gluconate 10-20ml 10% over 10min (cardiac monitor) — Acute severe hypocalcaemia Rx:
  1. Oral Ca + active Vit D (calcitriol/alfacalcidol) + Mg — Chronic hypocalcaemia Rx:
  1. Ca soaps in fat necrosis + hypoalbuminaemia + glucagon -> dCa — Hypocalcaemia in acute pancreatitis:
  1. Citrate chelation of Ca -> hypocalcaemia; give Ca if prolonged — Hypocalcaemia in massive transfusion:
  1. Low Mg -> PTH resistance + impaired PTH secretion -> refractory hypocalcaemia; replete Mg first — Mg and hypocalcaemia:
  1. Alkalosis u albumin binding -> d ionised Ca -> tetany; correct pH — Alkalosis and hypocalcaemia:
  1. Low albumin -> low total Ca but normal ionised Ca; measure ionised Ca — Pseudohypocalcaemia:

SBAs

  1. IV Ca gluconate 10ml 10% over 10min (cardiac monitor) -> oral Ca/calcitriol — Post-thyroidectomy: perioral tingling, Chvostek+, adj Ca 1.8. Immediate?
  1. IV Ca gluconate + monitor Mg/albumin — Acute pancreatitis: Ca 1.9, albumin 25. Ionised Ca 0.9. Rx?
  1. IV Ca gluconate 10ml 10% per 4 units blood; monitor ionised Ca — Massive transfusion: citrate toxicity, Ca 1.7. Management?
  1. Replete Mg first (Mg needed for PTH secretion/action) — Hypocalcaemia + low Mg: Ca not correcting despite IV Ca. Next?
  1. Correct alkalosis (breathing control/paper bag) + IV Ca if severe — Alkalosis + tetany: pH 7.55, ionised Ca 0.9. Management?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.5.04 Hypocalcaemic Tetany
[!info]
Hypocalcaemic Tetany = Acute Neuromuscular Excitability due to Ionised Hypocalcaemia. Medical Emergency Requiring Immediate IV Calcium Gluconate. Key Differential: Hypoparathyroidism, Vitamin D Deficiency, Alkalosis, Magnesium Deficiency.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise clinical features of hypocalcaemic tetany (Trousseau's, Chvostek's signs)
  • [ ] Execute immediate IV calcium gluconate administration
  • [ ] Differentiate causes (hypoparathyroidism, vitamin D deficiency, alkalosis, magnesium deficiency)
  • [ ] Apply chronic replacement strategies (calcium + active vitamin D analogues)
  • [ ] Identify special causes (hungry bone syndrome, massive transfusion, pancreatitis)

2. Definitions & Diagnostic Criteria

ParameterNormalHypocalcaemiaSevere (Tetany)
Corrected Serum Calcium2.15-2.55 mmol/L (8.6-10.2 mg/dL)<2.15 mmol/L (<8.6 mg/dL)<1.75 mmol/L (<7.0 mg/dL)
Ionised Calcium1.15-1.30 mmol/L (4.5-5.2 mg/dL)<1.10 mmol/L<1.0 mmol/L
Correction Formula: Corrected Ca = Measured Ca + 0.02 × (40 - Albumin g/L) [mmol/L]

3. Aetiology & Differential Diagnosis

CategoryCauseKey Features
HypoparathyroidismPost-Thyroidectomy (75%), Autoimmune (APS-1), DiGeorge (22q11.2), Mg DeficiencyLow PTH, High Phosphate
Vitamin D DeficiencyHigh PTH (Secondary Hyperparathyroidism)Low 25-OH D, High ALP, Low Phosphate
Renal FailureHigh Phosphate, High PTH, Low 1,25-(OH)₂Duraemia
Acute PancreatitisSaponification (Ca²⁺ + FFA → Calcium Soaps)High Amylase/Lipase, Abdominal Pain
AlkalosisRespiratory (Hyperventilation) / Metabolic (Vomiting)↑ Protein Binding → ↓ Ionised Ca²⁺
Massive TransfusionCitrate Chelation (1 Unit = ~3g Citrate = 45 mmol Ca²⁺)Ionised Ca²⁺ Drop
RhabdomyolysisCa²⁺ Sequestration in Damaged MuscleHigh CK, Myoglobinuria
Tumour Lysis SyndromePhosphate Release → Ca²⁺ PrecipitationHigh Phosphate, Uric Acid, K⁺
Hungry Bone SyndromePost-PTX/Thyroidectomy; Rapid Bone MineralisationSevere HypoCa²⁺, HypoPhos, HypoMg
Mg²⁺ DeficiencyFunctional Hypoparathyroidism (Mg Needed for PTH Secretion/Action)Hypokalaemia, Hypocalcaemia Resistant to Ca²⁺

4. Clinical Presentation

Acute Tetany (Emergency)

SignDescriptionElicitation
Trousseau's SignCarpal Spasm (Adduction Thumb, Flexion Wrist/MCPs, Extension Fingers)BP Cuff Inflated >SBP for 3 Min
Chvostek's SignFacial Muscle Twitch (Lips, Nose, Eye)Percussion Over Facial Nerve (Anterior to Ear)
LaryngospasmStridor, Airway ObstructionEmergency — Intubation May Be Needed
Generalised SeizuresTonic-Clonic—
CardiacProlonged QT → Torsades de Pointes, VFECG Monitoring
NeuropsychiatricAnxiety, Confusion, Irritability, Depression—

Chronic Hypocalcaemia

FeatureDetails
Basal Ganglia CalcificationCT Brain (Fahr's Syndrome)
CataractsPosterior Subcapsular
DentalEnamel Hypoplasia, Caries
NeuropsychiatricDepression, Anxiety, Dementia, Parkinsonism
Skin/Hair/NailsDry Skin, Coarse Hair, Brittle Nails
CataractsPosterior Subcapsular

5. Aetiology & Differential Diagnosis

CausePTHPhosphateMagnesiumKey Feature
HypoparathyroidismLowHighNormalPost-Surgical (75%), Autoimmune, Genetic
Vitamin D DeficiencyHigh (2° HPT)LowNormalLow 25-OH D, High ALP
Renal FailureHigh (2° HPT)HighNormalHigh Creatinine
PseudohypoparathyroidismHigh (Resistance)HighNormalPHP1a = AHO (Short Stature, Obesity)
Vitamin D DeficiencyHigh (2° HPT)Low/NormalNormalLow 25-OH D
Acute PancreatitisNormal/HighHighNormalHigh Amylase/Lipase
AlkalosisNormal/HighNormalNormalRespiratory (>7.45) / Metabolic
HypomagnesaemiaLow/NormalLow/NormalLowCorrect Mg First

6. Acute Management — Hypocalcaemic Tetany (Emergency)

Immediate IV Calcium (Life-Saving)

DrugDoseAdministration
10% Calcium Gluconate10-20 mL (1-2g) IVSlow IV Over 10 Min (Cardiac Monitor)
RepeatIf Persistent TetanyEvery 10-20 Min Until Controlled

Follow-Up Infusion

InfusionRate
10% Calcium Gluconate1-2 g/hr (10-20 mL/hr) in 5% Dextrose / 0.9% Saline
TargetIonised Ca²⁺ >1.1 mmol/L; Symptom Relief

Adjuncts

DrugDoseIndication
Magnesium Sulphate2-4g IVMg <0.7 mmol/L
Vitamin D (Alfacalcidol/Calcitriol)1-2 µg IV/POAccelerate Ca²⁺ Absorption
Oral CalciumCalcium Carbonate 1-2g TDSAfter IV Stabilisation

7. Chronic Management

Calcium Supplementation

PreparationElemental Ca²⁺Dose
Calcium Carbonate40% (400mg/tablet)1.5-2g TDS with Meals
Calcium Citrate21%1-2g TDS (Better Absorption if Achlorhydria)
TargetTotal Elemental Ca²⁺ 1.5-2g/dayDivided Doses

Active Vitamin D Analogues (Essential for Absorption)

AgentDoseAdvantage
Alfacalcidol (1α-OH D3)0.25-1 µg/dayRapid Onset; No Renal Activation Needed
Calcitriol (1,25-(OH)₂D₃)0.25-0.5 µg/dayActive Form; Short Half-Life
Dose TitrationTarget: Ionised Ca²⁺ 1.1-1.3 mmol/LAdjust q2-4wk

Magnesium

IndicationDose
HypomagnesaemiaMgSO₄ 2-4g IV (Acute); Oral Mg 300-600mg/day (Chronic)

Thiazide Diuretic (Adjunct)

RoleDose
Reduce Urinary Ca²⁺ ExcretionHydrochlorothiazide 25mg OD (If Hypercalciuria)

8. Monitoring & Targets (Chronic)

ParameterTargetFrequency
Serum Calcium (Corrected)2.2-2.5 mmol/L (Slightly Low Normal)Monthly (Stable)
Ionised Calcium1.1-1.3 mmol/Lq1-2wk (Titration)
Phosphate<1.5 mmol/LMonthly
24h Urine Calcium<7.5 mmol/day (300mg)3-6 Monthly
Renal FunctioneGFR, Creatinine6-Monthly
25-OH Vitamin D>50 nmol/L (20 ng/mL)Annually
Bone DensityDEXA (Spine/Hip)q2-3 Years

9. Special Situations

Hypocalcaemic Tetany (Emergency)

StepAction
1. IV Calcium Gluconate 10%10-20 mL (1-2g) IV Over 10 Min (Cardiac Monitor)
2. RepeatEvery 10-20 Min Until Tetany Controlled
3. Infusion10% Calcium Gluconate 100mL (10g) in 500mL 5% Dextrose @ 50-100mL/hr
4. MagnesiumIf Mg <0.7 → 2-4g MgSO₄ IV
5. Vitamin DAlfacalcidol/Calcitriol 1-2µg IV/PO

Hungry Bone Syndrome (Post-Parathyroidectomy)

FeatureManagement
Severe HypocalcaemiaIV Calcium Gluconate Infusion (1-2g/hr)
DurationDays-Weeks (Until Bone Remineralisation)
PhosphateHypophosphataemia → Oral Phosphate Supplements
MagnesiumOften Co-existing Hypomagnesaemia

Magnesium Deficiency

FeatureManagement
Functional HypoparathyroidismCorrect Mg First
Prophylactic100mg/day Oral Mg (If on Loop Diuretics/PPI)
AcuteIV MgSO₄ 2-4g (Repeat if Needed)

Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)

AspectManagement
Phosphate BindersCalcium-Based (If Ca Normal) / Non-Ca (Sevelamer/Lanthanum) if Hypercalcaemia
Vitamin DAlfacalcidol/Calcitriol (Active Analogues)
CinacalcetIf PTH ↑↑ Despite Binders

10. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Hypocalcaemia DefinitionCorrected Ca <2.15 mmol/L; Ionised <1.10 mmol/L
Factitious HypocalcaemiaLow Albumin → Low Total Ca, Normal Ionised Ca
Corrected Ca FormulaMeasured Ca + 0.02 × (40 - Albumin g/L)
Acute Tetany RxIV 10% Ca Gluconate 10-20mL Over 10min (Cardiac Monitor)
Chronic ReplacementCa 1.5-2g/day + Alfacalcidol/Calcitriol 0.25-1µg/day
Target Ionised Ca²⁺1.1-1.3 mmol/L
Phosphate Target<1.5 mmol/L
24h Urine Ca Target<7.5 mmol/day (Avoid Nephrolithiasis)
PseudohypoparathyroidismHigh PTH + Hypocalcaemia; PHP1a = AHO Phenotype
Mg DeficiencyCorrect Mg First (Functional Hypoparathyroidism)
Post-ThyroidectomyTransient (<6mo) vs Permanent (>6-12mo)
Hungry Bone SyndromePost-PTX Severe Hypocalcaemia → IV Ca Infusion
Mg DeficiencyFunctional Hypoparathyroidism → Correct Mg First
Vitamin D in HypoparaAlfacalcidol/Calcitriol (Active Analogues) — Not Cholecalciferol

11. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Factitious HypocalcaemiaLow Albumin → Low Total Ca, Normal Ionised Ca
Hypoparathyroidism vs PHPHypopara: Low PTH; PHP: High PTH (Resistance)
Vit D Def vs HypoparaVit D Def: High PTH; Hypopara: Low PTH
Mg DeficiencyFunctional Hypoparathyroidism → Correct Mg First
Corrected Ca FormulaCa_corr = Measured Ca + 0.02 × (40 - Albumin g/L)
Acute Tetany RxIV 10% Ca Gluconate 10-20mL Over 10min (Cardiac Monitor)
Ionised Ca Target1.1-1.3 mmol/L
Phosphate Target<1.5 mmol/L
24h Urine Ca Target<7.5 mmol/day (Avoid Nephrolithiasis)
Mg DeficiencyFunctional Hypoparathyroidism → Correct Mg First
Trousseau's SignBP Cuff >SBP → Carpal Spasm
Chvostek's SignFacial Nerve Tap → Facial Twitch
LaryngospasmAirway Emergency → Intubation
Prolonged QTTorsades de Pointes Risk

12. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Hypocalcaemic Tetany))
    Definition
      Corrected Ca <2.15 mmol/L
      Ionised Ca <1.10 mmol/L
    Aetiology
      Hypoparathyroidism (Post-Surgical #1)
      Vit D Deficiency (High PTH)
      Renal Failure (High Phos, High PTH)
      Alkalosis (Resp/Metabolic)
      Massive Transfusion (Citrate)
      Pancreatitis (Saponification)
      Mg Deficiency (Functional Hypopara)
      Hungry Bone (Post-PTX)
    Acute Management
      IV 10% Ca Gluconate 10-20mL Over 10min
      Cardiac Monitor
      Mg if Low (<0.7)
      Vit D (Alfacalcidol/Calcitriol) IV
    Chronic Management
      Ca 1.5-2g/day + Alfacalcidol/Calcitriol 0.25-1µg/day
      Target Ionised Ca 1.1-1.3 mmol/L
      Phosphate <1.5 mmol/L
      24h Urine Ca <7.5mmol
    Special
      Hungry Bone: IV Ca Infusion
      Post-PTX: Acute → Chronic
      Mg Deficiency: Correct Mg First
      Alkosis: Correct pH First
    Signs
      Trousseau's (BP Cuff >SBP → Carpal Spasm)
      Chvostek's (Facial Tap → Facial Twitch)
      Laryngospasm (Airway Emergency)
      Prolonged QT → Torsades

13. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Hypocalcaemia DefinitionCorrected Ca <2.15 mmol/L; Ionised <1.10 mmol/L
Factitious HypocalcaemiaLow Albumin → Low Total Ca, Normal Ionised Ca
Corrected Ca FormulaCa_corr = Measured Ca + 0.02 × (40 - Albumin g/L)
Acute Tetany RxIV 10% Ca Gluconate 10-20mL Over 10min (Cardiac Monitor)
Chronic ReplacementCa 1.5-2g/day + Alfacalcidol/Calcitriol 0.25-1µg/day
Target Ionised Ca²⁺1.1-1.3 mmol/L
Phosphate Target<1.5 mmol/L
24h Urine Ca Target<7.5 mmol/day (Avoid Nephrolithiasis)
PseudohypoparathyroidismHigh PTH + Hypocalcaemia; PHP1a = AHO
Mg DeficiencyFunctional Hypoparathyroidism → Correct Mg First
Post-ThyroidectomyTransient (<6mo) vs Permanent (>6-12mo)
Hungry Bone SyndromePost-PTX Severe Hypocalcaemia → IV Ca Infusion

14. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Parathyroid & Calcium|Parathyroid & Calcium]]

15. MCQs (10)

  1. Hypocalcaemic tetany =

A. Neuromuscular irritability from low ionised Ca -> Chvostek/Trousseau signs, carpopedal spasm, laryngospasm, seizures

B. Hypercalcaemic crisis

C. Normal Ca with spasm

D. Mg deficiency only

E. K deficiency only

  1. Chvostek's sign:

A. Tap facial nerve -> ipsilateral facial muscle contraction (orbicularis oris)

B. Tap ulnar nerve -> hand spasm

C. BP cuff inflation -> carpal spasm

D. Knee tap -> ankle spasm

E. Ankle tap -> foot spasm

  1. Trousseau's sign:

A. BP cuff >SBP 3min -> carpal spasm (equine hand)

B. Facial tap -> facial spasm

C. Knee tap -> ankle spasm

D. Ankle tap -> foot spasm

E. Wrist tap -> finger spasm

  1. Acute tetany management:

A. IV Ca gluconate 10-20ml 10% over 10min (cardiac monitor) -> check Mg

B. Oral Ca only

C. IV phosphate

D. Cinacalcet

E. Observation

  1. Laryngospasm in tetany:

A. Life-threatening; IV Ca STAT + secure airway (intubation if needed)

B. Oral Ca

C. Nebulised salbutamol

D. IV Mg only

E. Observation

  1. Tetany in alkalosis:

A. Alkalosis u protein binding -> d ionised Ca -> tetany; correct pH + IV Ca

B. Alkalosis prevents tetany

C. No relation

D. Only in respiratory

E. Only in metabolic

  1. Tetany in hypomagnesaemia:

A. Mg needed for PTH secretion/action; replete Mg first

B. Mg not related

C. High Mg causes tetany

D. Only Ca needed

E. Tetany only with hypercalcaemia

  1. Chronic hypocalcaemia tetany prevention:

A. Oral Ca + active Vit D (calcitriol) + Mg; target ionised Ca low-normal

B. IV Ca lifelong

C. PTH injection

D. Cinacalcet

E. Bisphosphonates

  1. Tetany vs seizure:

A. Tetany: conscious, carpal spasm, Chvostek/Trousseau +; Seizure: LOC, tonic-clonic

B. Same

C. Tetany = seizure

D. Tetany: LOC

E. Seizure: carpal spasm

  1. Pseudohypoparathyroidism tetany:

A. PHP1a: PTH resistance (high PTH) + Albright's hereditary osteodystrophy

B. Low PTH

C. Normal PTH

D. Only Ca

E. No physical features

16. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 25yo woman: carpal spasm after BP cuff, perioral tingling, Ca 1.7. Immediate?

A. IV Ca gluconate 10ml 10% over 10min (cardiac monitor) -> check Mg -> oral Ca/calcitriol

B. Oral Ca

C. Observation

D. IV phosphate

E. Cinacalcet

  1. Same patient: Mg 0.5 (low). Ca not correcting after IV Ca. Next?

A. Replete Mg first (MgSO4 2-4g IV)

B. More IV Ca

C. Cinacalcet

D. Calcitonin

E. Stop Ca

  1. Alkalosis (hyperventilation): tetany, pH 7.58, ionised Ca 0.9. Management?

A. Correct pH (breathing control/paper bag) + IV Ca if severe

B. More IV Ca

C. Cinacalcet

D. Sedation

E. Intubation

  1. Hypomagnesaemic tetany: Mg 0.4, Ca 1.8. Mg replacement?

A. MgSO4 2-4g IV over 30-60min; then oral Mg

B. Oral Mg only

C. IV Ca only

D. Cinacalcet

E. Potassium

  1. PHP1a: short stature, round face, short 4th metacarpal, Ca 1.8, PTH 80. Diagnosis?

A. Pseudohypoparathyroidism Type 1a (Albright's hereditary osteodystrophy)

B. Hypoparathyroidism

C. Vit D deficiency

D. Primary HPT

E. FHH

17. Flashcards

  • Q: Tetany

A: Neuromuscular irritability from low ionised Ca -> Chvostek/Trousseau, carpopedal spasm, laryngospasm, seizures

  • Q: Chvostek's sign

A: Tap facial nerve -> ipsilateral facial muscle contraction

  • Q: Trousseau's sign

A: BP cuff >SBP 3min -> carpal spasm (equine hand)

  • Q: Acute Rx

A: IV Ca gluconate 10-20ml 10% over 10min (cardiac monitor); CHECK Mg

  • Q: Laryngospasm

A: Life-threatening; IV Ca STAT + secure airway

  • Q: Alkalosis tetany

A: u protein binding -> d ionised Ca -> tetany; correct pH + IV Ca

  • Q: Mg and tetany

A: Low Mg -> refractory hypocalcaemia; replete Mg FIRST

  • Q: Chronic prevention

A: Oral Ca + active Vit D (calcitriol) + Mg; target ionised Ca low-normal

  • Q: PHP1a

A: PTH resistance (high PTH) + Albright's hereditary osteodystrophy (short 4th metacarpal)

  • Q: Tetany vs seizure

A: Tetany: conscious, carpal spasm, Chvostek/Trousseau+; Seizure: LOC, tonic-clonic

18. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Neuromuscular irritability from low ionised Ca -> Chvostek/Trousseau signs, carpopedal spasm, laryngospasm, seizures — Hypocalcaemic tetany =
  1. Tap facial nerve -> ipsilateral facial muscle contraction (orbicularis oris) — Chvostek's sign:
  1. BP cuff >SBP 3min -> carpal spasm (equine hand) — Trousseau's sign:
  1. IV Ca gluconate 10-20ml 10% over 10min (cardiac monitor) -> check Mg — Acute tetany management:
  1. Life-threatening; IV Ca STAT + secure airway (intubation if needed) — Laryngospasm in tetany:
  1. Alkalosis u protein binding -> d ionised Ca -> tetany; correct pH + IV Ca — Tetany in alkalosis:
  1. Mg needed for PTH secretion/action; replete Mg first — Tetany in hypomagnesaemia:
  1. Oral Ca + active Vit D (calcitriol) + Mg; target ionised Ca low-normal — Chronic hypocalcaemia tetany prevention:
  1. Tetany: conscious, carpal spasm, Chvostek/Trousseau +; Seizure: LOC, tonic-clonic — Tetany vs seizure:
  1. PHP1a: PTH resistance (high PTH) + Albright's hereditary osteodystrophy — Pseudohypoparathyroidism tetany:

SBAs

  1. IV Ca gluconate 10ml 10% over 10min (cardiac monitor) -> check Mg -> oral Ca/calcitriol — 25yo woman: carpal spasm after BP cuff, perioral tingling, Ca 1.7. Immediate?
  1. Replete Mg first (MgSO4 2-4g IV) — Same patient: Mg 0.5 (low). Ca not correcting after IV Ca. Next?
  1. Correct pH (breathing control/paper bag) + IV Ca if severe — Alkalosis (hyperventilation): tetany, pH 7.58, ionised Ca 0.9. Management?
  1. MgSO4 2-4g IV over 30-60min; then oral Mg — Hypomagnesaemic tetany: Mg 0.4, Ca 1.8. Mg replacement?
  1. Pseudohypoparathyroidism Type 1a (Albright's hereditary osteodystrophy) — PHP1a: short stature, round face, short 4th metacarpal, Ca 1.8, PTH 80. Diagnosis?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.5.05 Hypoparathyroidism
[!info]
Hypoparathyroidism = Deficient PTH Secretion → Hypocalcaemia + Hyperphosphataemia. Post-Surgical (Post-Thyroidectomy) = Commonest Cause (75%). Autoimmune, Genetic, Infiltrative also. No PTH → ↓ Bone Resorption, ↓ Renal Ca²⁺ Reabsorption, ↓ 1,25-(OH)₂D Synthesis → Hypocalcaemia + Hyperphosphataemia.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify hypoparathyroidism by aetiology (post-surgical, autoimmune, genetic)
  • [ ] Recognise clinical features of hypocalcaemia (tetany, seizures, QT prolongation)
  • [ ] Execute acute management of hypocalcaemic tetany (IV calcium gluconate)
  • [ ] Prescribe chronic replacement (calcium, alfacalcidol/calcitriol)
  • [ ] Recognise pseudohypoparathyroidism (PTH resistance)

2. Aetiology & Classification

CategoryCauseFrequency
Post-SurgicalPost-Thyroidectomy (Total/Subtotal); Parathyroidectomy75% (Commonest)
AutoimmuneIsolated / APS-1 (Autoimmune Polyendocrine Syndrome Type 1)10-15%
GeneticDiGeorge Syndrome (22q11.2 Deletion); CASR (Activating); GCM2; PTH Gene5-10%
Infiltrative/InfiltrativeHaemochromatosis, Wilson's, Metastases, SarcoidosisRare
Radiation/RadiotherapyNeck Irradiation; Radioactive IodineRare
Magnesium DeficiencyFunctional Hypoparathyroidism (Mg Required for PTH Secretion)Reversible

3. Pathophysiology

DeficiencyConsequence
↓ PTH↓ Bone Resorption (↓ Ca²⁺ Release); ↓ Renal Ca²⁺ Reabsorption (Distal Tubule); ↓ Renal 1α-Hydroxylase → ↓ 1,25-(OH)₂D → ↓ Intestinal Ca²⁺ Absorption
Net EffectHypocalcaemia + Hyperphosphataemia (↓ Renal Phosphate Excretion)
Mg²⁺ DeficiencyImpairs PTH Secretion AND PTH Action (Functional Hypoparathyroidism)

4. Aetiology & Clinical Features

Post-Surgical (Commonest, 75%)

TypeOnsetFeatures
Transient<6 Months Post-ThyroidectomyUsually Recovers; Parathyroid Stunned
Permanent>6-12 MonthsPermanent PTH Deficiency; Requires Lifelong Replacement
Risk FactorsTotal Thyroidectomy, Central Neck Dissection, Re-operation, Thyroid Cancer

Autoimmune (Isolated / APS-1)

SyndromeComponents
IsolatedAutoimmune Hypoparathyroidism Alone; CAII, NACHT-LRR Antibodies
APS-1 (APECED)AIRE Gene (AR); Triad: Hypoparathyroidism + Adrenal Insufficiency + Chronic Mucocutaneous Candidiasis; Also: Enamel Hypoplasia, Hepatitis, Keratitis

Genetic

SyndromeGeneFeatures
DiGeorge Syndrome (22q11.2 Del)TBX1Hypoparathyroidism, Thymic Aplasia (T-cell Deficiency), Conotruncal Heart Defects, Cleft Palate, Facial Dysmorphism
CASR Activating MutationCASR (Gain-of-Function)Autosomal Dominant; Hypocalcaemia, Hypercalciuria, Bartter-Like Syndrome
GCM2GCM2Familial Isolated Hypoparathyroidism (AR/AD)
PTH GenePTHFamilial Isolated Hypoparathyroidism (AR)

Magnesium Deficiency

FeatureDetails
MechanismMg²⁺ Required for PTH Secretion (Adenylate Cyclase) + PTH Action (G-Protein Coupling)
PresentationHypocalcaemia (Resistant to Ca²⁺), Hypokalaemia, Hypomagnesaemia, Seizures, Tetany
TreatmentIV Magnesium Sulphate (Correct Mg²⁺ First → PTH Recovers)

5. Clinical Features

Acute Hypocalcaemia (Tetany)

FeatureDetails
Perioral ParaesthesiaNumbness/Tingling Lips, Fingers, Toes
Carpopedal SpasmTrousseau's Sign (Carpal Spasm on BP Cuff Inflation >20mmHg Above SBP); Chvostek's Sign (Facial Twitch on Facial Nerve Tap)
LaryngospasmStridor, Airway Obstruction (Emergency)
Generalised SeizuresGeneralised Tonic-Clonic
CardiacProlonged QT → Torsades de Pointes, VF
NeuropsychiatricAnxiety, Irritability, Confusion, Seizures, Depression
EctodermalDry Skin, Brittle Nails, Hair Loss, Cataracts (Chronic)

Chronic Hypocalcaemia

FeatureDetails
Basal Ganglia CalcificationCT Brain: Bilateral Basal Ganglia Calcification
CataractsPosterior Subcapsular
DentalEnamel Hypoplasia, Caries
NeuropsychiatricDepression, Anxiety, Dementia, Parkinsonism
Skin/Hair/NailsDry Skin, Coarse Hair, Brittle Nails

6. Diagnosis & Investigations

Biochemical Confirmation

TestExpected in Hypoparathyroidism
Serum Calcium (Corrected)Low (<2.15 mmol/L / 8.6 mg/dL)
Ionised CalciumLow (<1.1 mmol/L)
PhosphateHigh (>1.5 mmol/L)
PTH (Intact)Low / Undetectable (<1.6 pmol/L)
MagnesiumNormal (Unless Mg²⁺ Deficiency)
25-OH Vitamin DUsually Normal/Low (Secondary to Low PTH)
CreatinineNormal (Exclude Renal Failure)
24h Urine CalciumLow (Tubular Reabsorption ↑)

Differential Diagnosis

ConditionCalciumPhosphatePTHKey Differentiator
HypoparathyroidismLowHighLow/UndetectableLow PTH
PseudohypoparathyroidismLowHighHighPTH Resistance
Vitamin D DeficiencyLowLow/NormalHigh (Secondary)Low 25-OH D
Renal FailureLowHighHigh (Secondary)Elevated Creatinine
Pseudohypoparathyroidism Type 1aLowHighHighAlbright Hereditary Osteodystrophy (Short Stature, Obesity, Short Metacarpals)

7. Acute Management — Hypocalcaemic Tetany (Emergency)

Immediate IV Calcium

DrugDoseAdministration
10% Calcium Gluconate10-20 mL (1-2g) IVSlow IV Over 10 Min (Cardiac Monitor)
RepeatIf Persistent TetanyEvery 10-20 Min Until Controlled

Follow-Up Infusion

InfusionRate
10% Calcium Gluconate1-2 g/hr (10-20 mL/hr) in 5% Dextrose / 0.9% Saline
TargetIonised Ca²⁺ >1.1 mmol/L; Symptom Relief

Adjuncts

DrugDoseIndication
Magnesium Sulphate2-4g IV (If Hypomagnesaemia)Mg <0.7 mmol/L
Vitamin D (Alfacalcidol/Calcitriol)1-2 µg IV/POAccelerate Ca²⁺ Absorption
Oral CalciumCalcium Carbonate 1-2g TDSAfter IV Stabilisation

8. Chronic Management

Calcium Supplementation

PreparationElemental Ca²⁺Dose
Calcium Carbonate40% (400mg/tablet)1-2g TDS with Meals
Calcium Citrate21%1-2g TDS (Better Absorption if Achlorhydria)
TargetTotal Elemental Ca²⁺ 1.5-2g/dayDivided Doses

Vitamin D Analogues (Essential for Absorption)

AgentDoseAdvantage
Alfacalcidol (1α-OH D3)0.25-1 µg/dayRapid Onset; No Renal Activation Needed
Calcitriol (1,25-(OH)₂D₃)0.25-0.5 µg/dayActive Form; Short Half-Life
Dose TitrationTarget: Ionised Ca²⁺ 1.1-1.3 mmol/L; Adjust q2-4wk

Magnesium

IndicationDose
HypomagnesaemiaMgSO₄ 2-4g IV (Then Oral Mg 300-600mg/day)

Thiazide Diuretic (Adjunct)

RoleDose
Reduce Urinary Ca²⁺ ExcretionHydrochlorothiazide 25mg OD (If Hypercalciuria)

9. Monitoring & Targets

ParameterTargetFrequency
Serum Calcium (Corrected)2.2-2.5 mmol/L (Slightly Low Normal)Monthly (Stable); Weekly (Titration)
Ionised Calcium1.1-1.3 mmol/Lq1-2wk (Titration)
Phosphate<1.5 mmol/L (Avoid Hyperphosphataemia)Monthly
24h Urine Calcium<7.5 mmol/day (300mg)3-6 Monthly (Monitor Nephrolithiasis)
Renal FunctioneGFR, Creatinine6-Monthly
25-OH Vitamin D>50 nmol/L (20 ng/mL)Annually
Bone DensityDEXA (Spine/Hip)q2-3 Years

10. Special Situations

Pregnancy

AspectManagement
Calcium Demand↑ Fetal Demand → ↑ Maternal Requirement
Vitamin DAlfacalcidol/Calcitriol Safe; Increase Dose 50-100%
TargetIonised Ca²⁺ 1.1-1.3 mmol/L (Maternal); Fetal Ca²⁺ Dependent on Maternal
BreastfeedingContinue Replacement; Monitor Infant Ca²⁺

Post-Thyroidectomy (Transient vs Permanent)

TypeDurationManagement
Transient<6 MonthsCalcium + Vit D; Wean as PTH Recovers
Permanent>6-12 MonthsLifelong Replacement; PTH Undetectable

11. Pseudohypoparathyroidism (PHP) — PTH Resistance

TypeGeneFeaturesBiochemistry
PHP Type 1aGNAS (Maternal Imprinting)Albright Hereditary Osteodystrophy (AHO): Short Stature, Obesity, Round Face, Short Metacarpals (4th/5th), Subcutaneous OssificationsHigh PTH, Hypocalcaemia, Hyperphosphataemia
PHP Type 1bGNAS (Methylation Defect)Renal Resistance Only (No AHO)High PTH, Hypocalcaemia
Pseudopseudohypoparathyroidism (PPHP)GNAS (Paternal)AHO Features OnlyNormal Ca/Phos/PTH

12. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Commonest CausePost-Thyroidectomy (75%)
Acute TetanyIV Calcium Gluconate 10-20mL 10% IV (Cardiac Monitor)
Chronic ReplacementCalcium 1-2g/day + Alfacalcidol 0.25-1µg/day
Target Ionised Ca²⁺1.1-1.3 mmol/L (Slightly Low Normal)
Phosphate Target<1.5 mmol/L (Avoid Hyperphosphataemia)
24h Urine Calcium<7.5 mmol/day (Avoid Nephrolithiasis)
PseudohypoparathyroidismHigh PTH + Hypocalcaemia + PHP1a = AHO Features
Mg²⁺ DeficiencyFunctional Hypoparathyroidism → Correct Mg First
Post-ThyroidectomyTransient (<6mo) vs Permanent (>6-12mo)
Basal Ganglia CalcificationChronic Hypocalcaemia → CT Brain
Acute Tetany Rx10% Calcium Gluconate 10-20mL IV Over 10min (Cardiac Monitor)
Target Ionised Ca1.1-1.3 mmol/L (Slightly Low Normal to Avoid Hypercalciuria)
Mg DeficiencyCauses Functional Hypoparathyroidism → Correct Mg First

13. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Hypoparathyroidism vs PseudohypoparathyroidismHypopara: Low PTH; PHP: High PTH (Resistance)
PhosphateHypopara: High; Vit D Def: Low
PHP Type 1a vs 1b1a = AHO Phenotype (Short Stature, Round Face, Short Metacarpals); 1b = Renal Resistance Only
PPHPPseudopseudohypoparathyroidism = AHO Features + Normal Biochemistry
Mg DeficiencyCauses Functional Hypoparathyroidism; Correct Mg First Before Ca
Vitamin D in HypoparaAlfacalcidol/Calcitriol (Active Analogues) — Not Cholecalciferol

14. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Hypoparathyroidism))
    Aetiology
      Post-Surgical (75%) → Thyroidectomy/Parathyroidectomy
      Autoimmune (APS-1, Isolated)
      Genetic (DiGeorge 22q11, CASR, GCM2, PTH)
      Magnesium Deficiency (Functional)
      Infiltrative (Haemochromatosis, Metastases)
      Radiation
    Clinical
      Acute: Tetany (Trousseau, Chvostek), Seizures, Laryngospasm, Prolonged QT
      Chronic: Basal Ganglia Calcification, Cataracts, Dental, Neuropsychiatric
    Biochemistry
      Hypocalcaemia + Hyperphosphataemia + LOW PTH
    Acute Management
      IV Calcium Gluconate 10-20mL 10% STAT
      Cardiac Monitor
      Mg if Hypomagnesaemia
      Vit D (Alfacalcidol/Calcitriol) IV
    Chronic Management
      Calcium 1.5-2g/day (Elemental)
      Alfacalcidol/Calcitriol 0.25-1µg/day
      Target Ionised Ca 1.1-1.3 mmol/L
      Target Phosphate <1.5 mmol/L
      Monitor 24h Urine Ca <7.5mmol
    PHP
      Type 1a: GNAS (Maternal) → AHO + High PTH
      Type 1b: GNAS (Methylation) → Renal Resistance Only
      PPHP: GNAS (Paternal) → AHO Features Only

15. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Commonest CausePost-Thyroidectomy (75%)
Acute Tetany RxIV Calcium Gluconate 10-20mL 10% Over 10 Min (Cardiac Monitor)
Chronic ReplacementCalcium 1-2g/day + Alfacalcidol/Calcitriol 0.25-1µg/day
Target Ionised Ca²⁺1.1-1.3 mmol/L (Slightly Low Normal)
Phosphate Target<1.5 mmol/L
24h Urine Calcium<7.5 mmol/day (Avoid Nephrolithiasis)
PseudohypoparathyroidismHigh PTH + Hypocalcaemia; PHP1a = AHO Phenotype
Mg DeficiencyFunctional Hypoparathyroidism → Correct Mg First
Post-ThyroidectomyTransient (<6mo) vs Permanent (>6-12mo)
Basal Ganglia CalcificationChronic Hypocalcaemia Marker
Acute Tetany RxIV 10% Ca Gluconate 10-20mL Over 10min (Cardiac Monitor)
Target Ionised Ca²⁺1.1-1.3 mmol/L
Mg DeficiencyCorrect First (Functional Hypopara)
Vitamin D in HypoparaAlfacalcidol/Calcitriol (Active Analogues) — Not Cholecalciferol

16. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Parathyroid & Calcium|Parathyroid & Calcium]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Primary Hyperparathyroidism]], [[Secondary Hyperparathyroidism (CKD)]], [[Tertiary Hyperparathyroidism]], [[FHH (Familial Hypocalciuric Hypercalcaemia)]], [[Hypocalcaemia]], [[Pseudohypoparathyroidism]]

17. MCQs (10)

  1. Commonest cause of hypoparathyroidism:

A. Post-thyroidectomy (surgery)

B. Autoimmune

C. Genetic

D. Radiotherapy

E. Infiltrative

  1. Hypoparathyroidism biochemistry:

A. Hypocalcaemia + hyperphosphataemia + low/undetectable PTH

B. Hypercalcaemia + hypophosphataemia + high PTH

C. Hypocalcaemia + low phosphate + high PTH

D. Normal calcium + low PTH

E. Hypercalcaemia + low phosphate

  1. Acute hypocalcaemia treatment:

A. IV calcium gluconate 10-20ml 10% over 10min (cardiac monitor)

B. Oral calcium only

C. IV phosphate

D. Vitamin D only

E. Cinacalcet

  1. Chronic hypoparathyroidism treatment:

A. Calcium carbonate + active Vit D (calcitriol/alfacalcidol) + magnesium

B. IV calcium lifelong

C. PTH replacement only

D. Cinacalcet

E. Bisphosphonates

  1. PTH replacement therapy:

A. Recombinant PTH (1-84) / Natpara; for refractory cases

B. First-line

C. Not available

D. Only for acute

E. Only for post-surgical

  1. Hypocalcaemia symptoms:

A. Tetany (Chvostek/Trousseau), seizures, QT prolongation, cataracts, basal ganglia calcification

B. Hypertension

C. Tachycardia

D. Hyperreflexia only

E. Asymptomatic

  1. Post-thyroidectomy hypocalcaemia risk:

A. Total thyroidectomy + central neck dissection

B. Hemithyroidectomy

C. Lobectomy only

D. No risk

E. Only if cancer

  1. Magnesium in hypoparathyroidism:

A. Often low; replete Mg first (otherwise Ca won't correct)

B. Always normal

C. Not needed

D. Contraindicated

E. High Mg causes hypocalcaemia

  1. Complications chronic hypoparathyroidism:

A. Cataracts, basal ganglia calcification, renal stones, renal failure, seizures

B. Only cataracts

C. Only stones

D. No complications

E. Only seizures

  1. Autoimmune hypoparathyroidism:

A. APS-1 (AIRE): candidiasis + hypoparathyroidism + Addison's + others

B. APS-2

C. Isolated

D. Only in children

E. Always with thyroid disease

18. SBA Questions (10)

  1. Post-total thyroidectomy day 1: perioral tingling, Chvostek+, Ca 1.8. Immediate?

A. IV calcium gluconate 10ml 10% over 10min -> then oral Ca/calcitriol

B. Oral Ca only

C. Observation

D. IV phosphate

E. Cinacalcet

  1. Chronic hypoparathyroidism: Ca 2.0, PO4 1.8, PTH <5. Maintenance?

A. Ca carbonate 1-2g TDS + calcitriol 0.5-1mcg/day + Mg

B. IV Ca lifelong

C. PTH injection only

D. Cinacalcet

E. Bisphosphonates

  1. Hypocalcaemia + low Mg: Ca not correcting despite IV Ca. Next?

A. Replete Mg first (Mg needed for PTH secretion/action)

B. More IV Ca

C. Cinacalcet

D. Calcitonin

E. Stop Ca

  1. Pseudohypoparathyroidism (PHP1a): Albright's hereditary osteodystrophy. Features?

A. Short stature, round face, short 4th/5th metacarpals, PTH resistance (high PTH)

B. Low PTH

C. Normal PTH

D. Only hypocalcaemia

E. No physical features

  1. Autoimmune hypoparathyroidism: screen for?

A. APS-1: Candida + Addison's + others; AIRE mutation

B. APS-2

C. Only thyroid

D. Only diabetes

E. No screening needed

19. Flashcards

  • Q: Hypoparathyroidism

A: Hypocalcaemia + hyperphosphataemia + low PTH

  • Q: Commonest cause

A: Post-thyroidectomy surgery

  • Q: Acute Rx

A: IV Ca gluconate 10-20ml 10% over 10min (cardiac monitor)

  • Q: Chronic Rx

A: Oral Ca + active Vit D (calcitriol/alfacalcidol) + Mg

  • Q: PTH replacement

A: Natpara (recombinant PTH 1-84) for refractory

  • Q: Mg importance

A: Low Mg -> refractory hypocalcaemia; replete Mg first

  • Q: Complications

A: Cataracts, basal ganglia calcification, renal stones, renal failure

  • Q: Pseudohypoparathyroidism

A: PTH resistance (high PTH); Albright's hereditary osteodystrophy (PHP1a)

  • Q: Autoimmune

A: APS-1 (AIRE): candidiasis + hypoparathyroidism + Addison's

  • Q: Post-thyroidectomy

A: Risk: total thyroidectomy + central neck dissection; monitor Ca day 1

20. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Post-thyroidectomy (surgery) — Commonest cause of hypoparathyroidism:
  1. Hypocalcaemia + hyperphosphataemia + low/undetectable PTH — Hypoparathyroidism biochemistry:
  1. IV calcium gluconate 10-20ml 10% over 10min (cardiac monitor) — Acute hypocalcaemia treatment:
  1. Calcium carbonate + active Vit D (calcitriol/alfacalcidol) + magnesium — Chronic hypoparathyroidism treatment:
  1. Recombinant PTH (1-84) / Natpara; for refractory cases — PTH replacement therapy:
  1. Tetany (Chvostek/Trousseau), seizures, QT prolongation, cataracts, basal ganglia calcification — Hypocalcaemia symptoms:
  1. Total thyroidectomy + central neck dissection — Post-thyroidectomy hypocalcaemia risk:
  1. Often low; replete Mg first (otherwise Ca won't correct) — Magnesium in hypoparathyroidism:
  1. Cataracts, basal ganglia calcification, renal stones, renal failure, seizures — Complications chronic hypoparathyroidism:
  1. APS-1 (AIRE): candidiasis + hypoparathyroidism + Addison's + others — Autoimmune hypoparathyroidism:

SBAs

  1. IV calcium gluconate 10ml 10% over 10min -> then oral Ca/calcitriol — Post-total thyroidectomy day 1: perioral tingling, Chvostek+, Ca 1.8. Immediate?
  1. Ca carbonate 1-2g TDS + calcitriol 0.5-1mcg/day + Mg — Chronic hypoparathyroidism: Ca 2.0, PO4 1.8, PTH <5. Maintenance?
  1. Replete Mg first (Mg needed for PTH secretion/action) — Hypocalcaemia + low Mg: Ca not correcting despite IV Ca. Next?
  1. Short stature, round face, short 4th/5th metacarpals, PTH resistance (high PTH) — Pseudohypoparathyroidism (PHP1a): Albright's hereditary osteodystrophy. Features?
  1. APS-1: Candida + Addison's + others; AIRE mutation — Autoimmune hypoparathyroidism: screen for?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.5.06 Osteoporosis And Metabolic Bone Disease
[!info]
Osteoporosis = Systemic Skeletal Disease Characterised by Low Bone Mass & Microarchitectural Deterioration → Increased Fragility Fracture Risk. Commonest Metabolic Bone Disease. DEXA T-Score ≤-2.5 = Osteoporosis. FRAX Tool for 10-Year Fracture Probability.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Diagnose osteoporosis using DEXA T-scores and clinical risk factors
  • [ ] Apply FRAX tool for 10-year major osteoporotic/hip fracture probability
  • [ ] Apply treatment thresholds (NOF, NICE, ESCEO guidelines)
  • [ ] Prescribe first-line (bisphosphonates) and second-line (denosumab, teriparatide, romosozumab) therapies
  • [ ] Manage glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis (GIOP)
  • [ ] Monitor treatment efficacy and duration (drug holidays)

2. Definition & Diagnosis

WHO Criteria (DEXA T-Scores)

CategoryT-Score (SD from Young Adult Mean)
Normal≥-1.0 SD
Osteopenia-1.0 to -2.5 SD
Osteoporosis≤-2.5 SD
Severe/Established Osteoporosis≤-2.5 SD + Fragility Fracture

Measurement Sites: Lumbar Spine (L1-L4) + Total Hip / Femoral Neck (Lowest T-Score Used for Diagnosis).

3. Fracture Risk Assessment — FRAX® Tool

FRAX® (WHO) — 10-Year Major Osteoporotic Fracture / Hip Fracture Probability

Input VariablesRequired
Age, Sex, Weight, HeightMandatory
Previous FractureYes/No
Parental Hip FractureYes/No
Current SmokingYes/No
GlucocorticoidsCurrent/Past (>5mg Pred >3mo)
Rheumatoid ArthritisYes/No
Secondary OsteoporosisT1DM, Untreated Hypothyroidism, etc.
Alcohol ≥3 units/dayYes/No
BMD (Femoral Neck)Optional (Improves Accuracy)

Treatment Thresholds (UK / NOGG)

ScenarioIntervention Threshold (10-Year Probability)
Major Osteoporotic Fracture≥7.5% (Age 50-59) → ≥15% (Age 70+)
Hip Fracture≥3% (Age 50-59) → ≥4.5% (Age 70+)
Note: Thresholds Vary by Country/Guideline (NOGG vs NICE vs ESCEO).

4. Clinical Risk Factors for Fracture

CategoryRisk Factors
Non-ModifiableAge >65, Female, Caucasian/Asian, Family History (Parental Hip Fracture), Previous Fragility Fracture, Early Menopause (<45), Low BMI (<19)
ModifiableLow BMI, Smoking, Alcohol >3 Units/Day, Sedentary, Low Calcium/Vit D Intake, Falls Risk
DiseasesRA, T1DM, Hyperthyroidism, Coeliac, IBD, Chronic Liver/Kidney Disease, Anorexia, Premature Menopause, Premature Andropause

5. Secondary Causes of Osteoporosis (Always Exclude)

CategoryConditions
EndocrineHyperthyroidism, Hyperparathyroidism, Cushing's, Hypogonadism (Male/Female), T1DM, Acromegaly
GICoeliac, IBD, Gastrectomy, Bariatric Surgery, Chronic Pancreatitis
HaematologicalMultiple Myeloma, Lymphoma, Leukaemia
RheumatologicalRA, Ankylosing Spondylitis, SLE
MedicationsGlucocorticoids (>5mg Pred >3mo), Aromatase Inhibitors, AEDs (Enzyme Inducers), PPIs (Long-Term), Heparin, Methotrexate, Depo-Provera, GnRH Analogues
OtherImmobilisation, Organ Transplant, HIV, HIV Meds (TDF), Loop Diuretics, Loop Diuretics

6. Diagnosis & Workup

Essential Investigations

TestPurpose
DEXA (Lumbar Spine + Hip)Gold Standard — T-Score Diagnosis
Lateral Spine X-Ray / VFAVertebral Fracture Assessment (VFA) — Silent Fractures
BloodsCa, Phos, ALP, 25-OH Vit D, TSH, TFTs, FBC, U&E, LFT, FSH/LH/Oestradiol (Women), Testosterone (Men), 25-OH Vit D
Fracture RiskFRAX® (With/Without BMD)
Lateral Spine X-RayIf Height Loss >3cm, Kyphosis, Back Pain

Laboratory Exclusion of Secondary Causes

TestTarget
Calcium, Phosphate, ALPNormal (Exclude Hyperparathyroidism/Osteomalacia)
TSH, fT4Normal (Exclude Hyperthyroidism)
25-OH Vit D>50 nmol/L (20 ng/mL)
TSH, Testosterone (Men)Exclude Hypogonadism
FSH/LH/OestradiolExclude Premature Menopause
Coeliac Screen (TTG-IgA)If GI Symptoms

7. Treatment — Pharmacological Therapy

Treatment Indications (NOGG / NICE / ESCEO)

ScenarioTreatment Indicated
Osteoporosis (T ≤-2.5)Yes
Osteopenia (T -1 to -2.5) + FRAX >ThresholdYes
Fragility Fracture (Any T-Score)Yes
Glucocorticoid-InducedYes (If ≥5mg Pred >3mo + T ≤-1.5)

8. Pharmacological Therapy — First-Line

Oral Bisphosphonates (First-Line)

DrugDoseAdministrationMonitoring
Alendronate70mg Weekly POFirst-Line; Fast 30min, Upright 30min, Water 200mLDEXA 2-3yr; eGFR if <35
Risedronate35mg Weekly / 150mg MonthlyAlternative (Better GI Tolerance)
Ibandronate150mg Monthly PO / 3mg IV q3moGI Intolerance
Zoledronic Acid5mg IV AnnualAlternative if Oral Contraindicated; Renal AdjusteGFR ≥35 (Avoid <35)

Bisphosphonate Monitoring

ParameterFrequency
DEXA2-3 Years After Initiation; Then 2-3 Yearly
Renal FunctionAnnual (eGFR) — Avoid if eGFR <35 (Oral); <35 (IV Zoledronic)
AdherenceAssess at Each Visit

Bisphosphonate Duration & Drug Holiday

DurationAction
3-5 Years (Oral) / 3 Years (IV)Reassess → Drug Holiday if Low Risk (T-Score >-2.5, No Fracture)
High Risk (Prior Fracture / T ≤-3.0 / Steroid)Continue Beyond 5-6 Years
Drug Holiday MonitoringDEXA q1-2yr; Restart if T-Score ≤-2.5 or Fracture

9. Second-Line / Alternative Therapies

DrugDoseIndicationAdvantage
Denosumab60mg SC q6moFirst-Line if Bisphosphonate Contraindicated/Intolerant; GIOPNo Renal Adjustment; Potent ↓ Fracture
Teriparatide20µg SC Daily (Max 24 Months)Severe Osteoporosis (T ≤-3.5, Prior Fracture, Failed Bisphosphonate)Anabolic (↑ Bone Formation)
Romosozumab210mg SC Monthly ×12moSevere (Very High Fracture Risk)Anabolic + Antiresorptive
Teriparatide20µg SC Daily (Max 24mo)Severe; Failed/Intolerant BisphosphonatesAnabolic
Raloxifene60mg ODPostmenopausal Women; Breast Cancer Risk ReductionSERM
HRTOestrogen ± ProgestogenEarly Postmenopause (<10yr)Symptom Relief + Bone

10. Glucocorticoid-Induced Osteoporosis (GIOP)

Risk Stratification

RiskCriteriaAction
High Risk≥5mg Pred >3mo + T-Score ≤-1.5 OR Prior Fracture OR Age >70Start Bone Protection Immediately
Moderate≥5mg Pred >3mo + T-Score >-1.5Consider (If High FRAX)
Low<5mg Pred OR <3moLifestyle + Calcium/Vit D

First-Line for GIOP

DrugDose
Oral Bisphosphonate (Alendronate/Risedronate)First-Line
Zoledronic Acid 5mg IV AnnualIf Oral Contraindicated
Denosumab 60mg SC q6moPreferred if High Fracture Risk / eGFR <35
TeriparatidePreferred if Very High Risk / Prior Fracture (Anabolic Superior in GIOP)

Calcium + Vitamin D Mandatory with All Therapies.

11. Treatment Duration & Monitoring

Bisphosphonate Holiday

ScenarioAction
Low Risk (No Fracture, T >-2.5 after 3-5yr)Drug Holiday (Stop 1-3 Years) → Reassess DEXA q1-2yr
High Risk (Prior Fracture, T ≤-2.5, Steroid)Continue (May Extend to 10yr)
DenosumabNO HOLIDAY — Rapid Bone Loss on Stopping → Transition to Bisphosphonate

Monitoring Schedule

ParameterFrequency
DEXA2-3 Years After Initiation; Then Q2-3yr
HeightAnnual (Vertebral Fracture Screen)
BiochemistryAnnual (Ca, Phos, ALP, 25-OH Vit D, Renal)
AdherenceEach Visit

12. Male Osteoporosis

AspectDetails
Prevalence20-25% of Hip Fractures in Men
Secondary CausesHypogonadism (30-50%), Glucocorticoids, Alcohol, Smoking, Malabsorption
WorkupTestosterone, LH, FSH, Prolactin, TSH, 25-OH Vit D
TreatmentTestosterone Replacement (If Hypogonadal) + Osteoporosis Therapy

13. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Osteoporosis DefinitionT-Score ≤-2.5 SD (Lumbar Spine / Femoral Neck)
OsteopeniaT-Score -1.0 to -2.5
FRAX ThresholdMajor Osteoporotic Fracture ≥7.5-15% (Age-Dependent); Hip ≥3-4.5%
First-Line RxAlendronate 70mg Weekly (Oral Bisphosphonate)
Zoledronic Acid5mg IV Annual; eGFR ≥35; Alternative if Oral Contraindicated
Denosumab60mg SC q6mo; No Renal Adjustment; No Drug Holiday (Rapid Bone Loss on Stop)
Teriparatide20µg SC Daily ×24mo Max; Anabolic; Severe Osteoporosis (T ≤-3.5, Fracture)
Romosozumab210mg SC Monthly ×12mo; Anabolic + Antiresorptive; Severe/High Risk
GIOP Threshold≥5mg Pred >3mo + T ≤-1.5 → Treat
Bisphosphonate HolidayAfter 3-5yr (Oral) / 3yr (IV) if Low Risk; No Holiday for Denosumab
Denosumab HolidayContraindicated → Rapid Bone Loss; Transition to Bisphosphonate
TeriparatideAnabolic; Max 24mo; Severe (T ≤-3.5, Prior Fracture)
Male OsteoporosisCheck Testosterone (Hypogonadism 30-50%); Testosterone + Bisphosphonate
DEXA Monitoringq2-3yr After Initiation; Height Annually (Vertebral Fracture)

14. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Osteopenia vs OsteoporosisOsteopenia: T -1 to -2.5; Osteoporosis: T ≤-2.5
T-Score vs Z-ScoreT-Score = vs Young Adult Mean; Z-Score = vs Age/Sex Matched (Use Z in <50yr)
Bisphosphonate HolidayOral: 3-5yr → Holiday; IV Zoledronic: 3yr → Holiday; Denosumab = NO HOLIDAY
Denosumab HolidayContraindicated — Rapid Bone Loss → Transition to Bisphosphonate
Teriparatide DurationMax 24 Months (Rat Osteosarcoma Risk); Anabolic
Romosozumab Duration12 Months Only → Follow with Antiresorptive
FRAX Without BMDValid (But Less Accurate); Add BMD for Accuracy
Zoledronic Acid RenaleGFR ≥35; <35 → Avoid/Reduce dose
Teriparatide Max Duration24 Months Lifetime Max (Rat Osteosarcoma Data)
Romosozumab Duration12 Months Only → Follow with Antiresorptive

15. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Osteoporosis))
    Definition
      T-Score ≤-2.5 (DXA)
      Fragility Fracture Risk
    Risk Factors
      Non-Mod: Age, Female, FHx, Low BMI, Early Menopause
      Modifiable: Smoking, EtOH, Low Ca/VitD, Falls, Steroid
      Secondary Causes: Endocrine, GI, Meds, Rheum, Haem
    Diagnosis
      DEXA (Spine + Hip) → T-Score
      VFA (Vertebral Fracture Assessment)
      FRAX (10yr Fracture Risk)
      Exclude Secondary Causes
    Treatment Threshold
      T ≤-2.5 → Treat
      T -1 to -2.5 + FRAX >Threshold → Treat
      GIOP: ≥5mg Pred >3mo + T ≤-1.5
    First-Line
      Oral Bisphosphonate (Alendronate 70mg/wk)
      Zoledronic Acid 5mg IV Annual (Alt)
    Second-Line
      Denosumab 60mg SC q6mo (No Renal Adj; No Holiday)
      Teriparatide 20µg SC Daily (24mo Max) — Anabolic
      Romosozumab 210mg SC Monthly ×12mo — Anabolic+Antiresorptive
    GIOP
      ≥5mg Pred >3mo + T≤-1.5 → Treat
      Teriparatide Preferred (Anabolic Superior)
    Monitoring
      DEXA q2-3yr; Height Annually (VFA)
      Drug Holiday: Oral 3-5yr → Stop; Denosumab NO Holiday

16. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Osteoporosis DefinitionT-Score ≤-2.5 SD (DEXA)
OsteopeniaT-Score -1.0 to -2.5
FRAX ThresholdMajor Fracture ≥7.5-15% (Age-Dep); Hip ≥3-4.5%
First-LineAlendronate 70mg Weekly (Oral)
Zoledronic Acid5mg IV Annual; eGFR ≥35
Denosumab60mg SC q6mo; No Renal Adjustment; No Drug Holiday
Teriparatide20µg SC Daily ×24mo Max; Anabolic; Severe Osteoporosis
Romosozumab210mg SC Monthly ×12mo; Anabolic + Antiresorptive; Severe
GIOP≥5mg Pred >3mo + T ≤-1.5 → Treat; Teriparatide Preferred
Drug HolidayOral BP: 3-5yr → Holiday; IV Zol: 3yr → Holiday; Denosumab = NO HOLIDAY
Teriparatide Max24 Months Max (Rat Osteosarcoma Risk)
Romosozumab Duration12 Months Only → Then Antiresorptive
DEXA Monitoringq2-3yr After Initiation
Male OsteoporosisCheck Testosterone (Hypogonadism 30-50%)
FRAX Without BMDValid but Less Accurate; Add BMD for Accuracy
Denosumab HolidayContraindicated — Rapid Bone Loss

17. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Parathyroid & Calcium|Parathyroid & Calcium]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Vitamin D Metabolism & Deficiency]], [[Hypocalcaemia]], [[Hypoparathyroidism]], [[Secondary Hyperparathyroidism (CKD)]], [[Primary Hyperparathyroidism]], [[Glucocorticoid-Induced Osteoporosis]]

18. MCQs (10)

  1. Osteoporosis definition:

A. BMD T-score <=-2.5 at hip/spine (WHO)

B. T-score <-1.0

C. T-score <-1.5

D. Any fracture

E. Low bone mass only

  1. Osteopenia definition:

A. T-score -1.0 to -2.5

B. T-score <-2.5

C. T-score >-1.0

D. Normal

E. Fracture

  1. Osteoporosis DXA site:

A. Hip (femoral neck) and lumbar spine (L1-L4)

B. Forearm only

C. Heel only

D. Whole body

E. Tibia only

  1. FRAX threshold for treatment (UK):

A. 10-year major osteoporotic fracture risk >=10% OR hip fracture risk >=3%

B. Always treat

C. Never treat

D. Only if T-score <-3.0

E. Only if fracture

  1. First-line osteoporosis treatment:

A. Oral bisphosphonate (alendronate 70mg weekly)

B. Denosumab

C. Zoledronic acid

D. Teriparatide

E. Raloxifene

  1. Bisphosphonate duration:

A. 5 years oral, 3 years IV; then drug holiday if low risk

B. Lifelong

C. 1 year

D. 2 years

E. Until fracture

  1. Denosumab:

A. Anti-RANKL 60mg SC q6mo; if bisphosphonate intolerant/contraindicated

B. First line

C. Oral

D. Daily

E. No monitoring needed

  1. Teriparatide:

A. Anabolic (PTH 1-34) 20mcg SC daily; max 2 years; severe osteoporosis + fractures

B. First line

C. Oral

D. Unlimited duration

E. Bisphosphonate

  1. Atypical femoral fracture:

A. Long-term bisphosphonate (>5yr); subtrochanteric, transverse, minimal trauma

B. Early bisphosphonate

C. Denosumab only

D. Teriparatide

E. No association

  1. ONJ (osteonecrosis jaw):

A. Bisphosphonate/denosumab risk; dental extraction trigger; exposed bone >8wk

B. No association

C. Only teriparatide

D. Only early treatment

E. Rare with denosumab

19. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 70yo woman: T-score -2.8 hip, no fracture, FRAX major 12%, hip 4%. First-line?

A. Alendronate 70mg weekly + Ca/Vit D

B. Denosumab

C. Zoledronic acid

D. Teriparatide

E. Raloxifene

  1. Same patient: on alendronate 5 years, T-score -2.2. Next?

A. Drug holiday (reassess in 1-2yr); continue Ca/Vit D

B. Continue alendronate

C. Switch to denosumab

D. Teriparatide

E. Zoledronic acid

  1. 75yo woman: severe osteoporosis, vertebral fractures, T-score -3.5. First-line?

A. Teriparatide 20mcg daily (max 2yr) or denosumab/zoledronic acid

B. Alendronate

C. Raloxifene

D. Ca/Vit D only

E. HRT

  1. On denosumab 3 years: stopping denosumab ->

A. Rapid bone loss -> transition to bisphosphonate

B. No rebound

C. Continue indefinitely

D. Switch to teriparatide

E. No further treatment

  1. Atypical femoral fracture risk: associated with

A. Long-term bisphosphonate (>5yr) or denosumab

B. Short-term bisphosphonate

C. Teriparatide

D. Ca/Vit D only

E. HRT

20. Flashcards

  • Q: Osteoporosis T-score

A: <=-2.5 at hip/spine (WHO)

  • Q: Osteopenia T-score

A: -1.0 to -2.5

  • Q: FRAX UK threshold

A: Major osteoporotic >=10% or hip >=3% -> treat

  • Q: First-line

A: Alendronate 70mg weekly + Ca/Vit D

  • Q: Bisphosphonate holiday

A: 5yr oral / 3yr IV -> reassess if low risk

  • Q: Denosumab

A: Anti-RANKL 60mg SC q6mo; if BP intolerant/contraindicated; STOP -> rapid bone loss

  • Q: Teriparatide

A: Anabolic PTH 1-34 20mcg SC daily; max 2yr; severe + fractures

  • Q: Zoledronic acid

A: 5mg IV yearly; if oral BP intolerant; 3yr then holiday

  • Q: Atypical femur fracture

A: Long-term BP (>5yr); subtrochanteric, transverse, minimal trauma

  • Q: ONJ

A: BP/denosumab risk; dental extraction trigger; exposed bone >8wk

  • Q: Denosumab stop

A: -> rapid bone loss; MUST transition to bisphosphonate

  • Q: Teriparatide limit

A: Max 2 years (osteosarcoma risk in rats)

  • Q: Romosozumab

A: Anti-sclerostin; anabolic + antiresorptive; 12mo only; CV risk

21. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. BMD T-score <=-2.5 at hip/spine (WHO) — Osteoporosis definition:
  1. T-score -1.0 to -2.5 — Osteopenia definition:
  1. Hip (femoral neck) and lumbar spine (L1-L4) — Osteoporosis DXA site:
  1. 10-year major osteoporotic fracture risk >=10% OR hip fracture risk >=3% — FRAX threshold for treatment (UK):
  1. Oral bisphosphonate (alendronate 70mg weekly) — First-line osteoporosis treatment:
  1. 5 years oral, 3 years IV; then drug holiday if low risk — Bisphosphonate duration:
  1. Anti-RANKL 60mg SC q6mo; if bisphosphonate intolerant/contraindicated — Denosumab:
  1. Anabolic (PTH 1-34) 20mcg SC daily; max 2 years; severe osteoporosis + fractures — Teriparatide:
  1. Long-term bisphosphonate (>5yr); subtrochanteric, transverse, minimal trauma — Atypical femoral fracture:
  1. Bisphosphonate/denosumab risk; dental extraction trigger; exposed bone >8wk — ONJ (osteonecrosis jaw):

SBAs

  1. Alendronate 70mg weekly + Ca/Vit D — 70yo woman: T-score -2.8 hip, no fracture, FRAX major 12%, hip 4%. First-line?
  1. Drug holiday (reassess in 1-2yr); continue Ca/Vit D — Same patient: on alendronate 5 years, T-score -2.2. Next?
  1. Teriparatide 20mcg daily (max 2yr) or denosumab/zoledronic acid — 75yo woman: severe osteoporosis, vertebral fractures, T-score -3.5. First-line?
  1. Rapid bone loss -> transition to bisphosphonate — On denosumab 3 years: stopping denosumab ->
  1. Long-term bisphosphonate (>5yr) or denosumab — Atypical femoral fracture risk: associated with
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.5.07 Primary Hyperparathyroidism
[!info]
Primary Hyperparathyroidism (PHPT) = Autonomous PTH Overproduction by Parathyroid Gland(s). Commonest Cause of Hypercalcaemia in Outpatients. Female:Male = 2:1, Peak 50-70y. Single Adenoma (80-85%) Commonest; Surgical Cure Definitive.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply diagnostic criteria (Hypercalcaemia + Inappropriately High/Normal PTH)
  • [ ] Differentiate PHPT from Familial Hypocalciuric Hypercalcaemia (FHH) and Malignancy
  • [ ] Apply surgical criteria (Asymptomatic vs Symptomatic)
  • [ ] Outline surgical approach (Focused Parathyroidectomy) and intraoperative PTH monitoring
  • [ ] Manage medical therapy for non-surgical candidates

2. Aetiology & Pathophysiology

FeatureDetails
AetiologySingle Adenoma (80-85%); Double Adenoma (5-10%); Hyperplasia (10-15%); Carcinoma (<1%)
GeneticsMEN1 (MEN1 Gene); Cyclin D1 (PRAD1) Rearrangement (15-40% Sporadic Adenomas); CDC73 (Parathyroid Carcinoma); CaSR (FHH)
PathophysiologyAutonomous PTH Secretion → ↑ Bone Resorption (Ca²⁺ Release), ↑ Renal Ca²⁺ Reabsorption, ↑ 1,25-(OH)₂D Synthesis → Hypercalcaemia
DemographicsFemale:Male = 2:1; Peak 50-70 Years

3. Clinical Presentation

Asymptomatic (Majority at Diagnosis)

PresentationFrequency
Incidental Hypercalcaemia80% Identified on Routine Bloods

Symptomatic (Classical "Moans, Groans, Stones, Bones")

SystemFeatures
Renal (Stones)Nephrolithiasis (15-20%); Nephrocalcinosis; Renal Impairment
Skeletal (Bones)Osteoporosis, Osteitis Fibrosa Cystica (Brown Tumours), Pathological Fractures, Bone Pain
GI (Moans)Nausea, Vomiting, Constipation, Anorexia, Peptic Ulcers, Pancreatitis
Neuropsychiatric (Groans)Fatigue, Weakness, Depression, Confusion, Cognitive Decline
CardiovascularHypertension, Shortened QT, Vascular Calcification

4. Diagnosis — Biochemical Criteria

TestExpected in PHPT
Albumin-Corrected Calcium>2.6 mmol/L (10.4 mg/dL)
Ionised Calcium>1.3 mmol/L (More Accurate if Albumin Low)
PTHElevated (>6.8 pmol/L) OR Inappropriately Normal (Inappropriately Normal = Inappropriately Normal for Degree of Hypercalcaemia)
PhosphateLow/Normal (PTH → Phosphaturia)
Alkaline Phosphatase (ALP)Normal or Elevated (Bone Turnover)
25-OH Vitamin DOften Low (Secondary to ↑ 1,25-(OH)₂D)
24h Urinary Calcium>7.5 mmol/day (300 mg/day) = Hypercalciuria

5. Differentiation — The "Big Three" Causes of Hypercalcaemia

FeaturePHPTMalignancyFamilial Hypocalciuric Hypercalcaemia (FHH)
PTHHigh / Inappropriately NormalSuppressed (<1 pmol/L)Normal / Mildly Elevated
CalciumHighHighMildly High (Often Normal)
PhosphateLow/NormalNormal/LowNormal/Low
24h Urine CalciumHigh (>7.5 mmol/24h)VariableLow (<2.5 mmol/24h)
Urinary Calcium/Creatinine Clearance Ratio>0.01Variable<0.01 (Key Discriminator)
PTHrPNegativePositive (Humoral Hypercalcaemia)Negative
25-OH Vit DLow/NormalLow/NormalNormal
Family HistoryRareRareAutosomal Dominant (Multiple Affected)

6. Diagnostic Algorithm

Hypercalcaemia (Corrected Ca >2.6 mmol/L)
         │
         ▼
Measure PTH (Intact)
         │
         ├── PTH HIGH / Inappropriately Normal → PRIMARY HYPERPARATHYROIDISM
         │       │
         │       ├── 24h Urine Ca/Cr >0.02 → CONFIRM PHPT
         │       └── Ca/Cr <0.01 → CONSIDER FHH
         │
         ├── PTH SUPPRESSED (<1 pmol/L) → MALIGNANCY / GRANULOMATOUS / VIT D TOXICITY
         │       ├── PTHrP Positive → HUMORAL HYPERCALCAEMIA OF MALIGNANCY
         │       ├── 1,25-(OH)₂ Vit D High → SARCOID / LYMPHOMA / TB
         │       └── 25-OH Vit D High → VIT D TOXICITY
         │
         └── PTH NORMAL / MILDLY ELEVATED + LOW URINE CA → FHH
                 Genetic Testing (CaSR Mutation) → Confirm

7. Localisation & Pre-Operative Imaging

ModalitySensitivitySpecificityRole
Sestamibi Scan (⁹⁹mTc-MIBI)70-90% (Single Adenoma)80-90%First-Line
Neck Ultrasound60-80%80-90%Complementary; Operator-Dependent
4D-CT85-95%90-95%Problem-Solving / Re-operative
MRI75-85%85-95%Alternative (No Radiation)
PET-CT (Choline/Choline-11/¹⁸F-Fluorocholine)90%+90%+Problem Cases

Key: No Single Test 100%; Concordant Dual Imaging (Sestamibi + US) → High Confidence for Focused Surgery.

8. Surgical Indications (NICE / AAES / KDOQI Guidelines)

Symptomatic PHPT (Absolute Indications)

CriterionThreshold
SymptomaticStones, Bone Disease (Fracture/Osteoporosis), Pancreatitis, Neuropsychiatric

Asymptomatic PHPT (Surgical If ANY Met)

CriterionThreshold (AAES 2022 / NICE)
Age<50 Years
Serum Calcium>2.85 mmol/L (11.5 mg/dL)
RenaleGFR <60 mL/min; Nephrolithiasis; Nephrocalcinosis
BoneT-score ≤-2.5 (Spine/Hip/Forearm); Vertebral Fracture (VFA)
24h Urine Calcium>10 mmol/day (400 mg/day)
Age >50Not an Absolute Indication (Individualise)

9. Surgical Management

Focused Parathyroidectomy (Minimally Invasive)

FeatureDetails
IndicationPre-Op Concordant Localisation (Sestamibi + US ± 4D-CT)
Incision2-3 cm Cervical Incision (or Endoscopic)
Intraoperative PTH (IOPTH)Miami Criterion: >50% Drop from Peak at 10min Post-Excision = Cure Prediction
Success Rate95-98% (Single Adenoma); Lower for Hyperplasia

Bilateral Neck Exploration (Traditional)

IndicationDetails
Discordant ImagingNo Concordant Localisation
Hyperplasia SuspectedMEN1, Familial, Renal Failure
Re-operationPrevious Failed Surgery
Carcinoma SuspectedEn Bloc Resection

Intraoperative PTH (IOPTH) Criteria

CriterionMiami Criterion (Most Common)
>50% Drop from Highest Pre-Excision PTH at 10 Minutes Post-ExcisionPredicts Cure
Alternative>50% Drop from Baseline at 10min

10. Medical Management (Non-Surgical Candidates)

IndicationCriteria
Age >75Frailty, Comorbidities
Refusal/Unfit for SurgeryPatient Choice / High Anaesthetic Risk
Mild DiseaseCa <2.85, No Renal/Bone/Stone Complications

Medical Therapy Options

DrugDoseMechanismMonitoring
Cinacalcet (Calcimimetic)30-90mg BD (Max 180mg/day)CaSR Agonist → ↓ PTH SecretionCa, PTH q1-3mo; Adverse: Nausea, Hypocalcaemia
Bisphosphonates (Alendronate/Zoledronate)Standard DosesInhibit Bone ResorptionBMD, Renal Function
Denosumab60mg SC 6-MonthlyRANKL InhibitionHypocalcaemia Risk (Supplement Ca/Vit D)
Hydration + Loop DiureticAcute Severe HypercalcaemiaAcute ManagementNot Chronic

11. Parathyroid Carcinoma (Rare, <1%)

FeatureDetails
ClinicalSevere Hypercalcaemia (Ca >3.5), Large Neck Mass, Hoarseness (RLN Invasion)
PTHVery High (Often >100 pmol/L)
Calcium>3.5 mmol/L (Often >4.0)
ImagingLarge Mass, Invasion (Trachea, Oesophagus, RLN), Distant Mets
GeneticsCDC73 (HRPT2) Mutation (Tumour Suppressor)
ManagementEn Bloc Resection (Thyroid Lobectomy + Lymphadenectomy); Adjuvant RT; No Role for Cinacalcet

12. Post-Operative Management

Immediate Post-Op (Hungry Bone Syndrome)

ComplicationMechanismManagement
HypocalcaemiaRapid Bone Mineralisation → Sudden Ca²⁺ UptakeIV Calcium Gluconate (10% 10-20mL Bolus) → Oral Ca/Vit D
HypomagnesaemiaCo-existentIV MgSO₄
HypophosphataemiaBone RemineralisationOral Phosphate

Post-Op Hypocalcaemia Protocol

Calcium LevelAction
Ionised Ca <1.0 mmol/L or Symptomatic10% Calcium Gluconate 10-20mL IV Bolus → Infusion 1-2mg/kg/hr
Ionised Ca 1.0-1.1Oral Calcium + Alfacalcidol/Calcitriol
MonitoringIonised Ca q2-4h (First 24-48h)

13. Post-Operative Follow-up

TimepointInvestigations
Day 1Ca, PTH, ALP, Phosphate, Mg, Renal Function
6 WeeksCalcium, PTH, 25-OH Vit D, Bone Profile
6 MonthsCalcium, PTH, 25-OH Vit D, DEXA (Baseline)
AnnualCalcium, PTH, Renal Function, 25-OH Vit D, DEXA (q2-3yr)

14. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
PHPT = Commonest CauseHypercalcaemia in Outpatients (80% Asymptomatic at Diagnosis)
Diagnostic HallmarkHigh Ca²⁺ + Inappropriately High/Normal PTH
FHH vs PHPTFHH: Low Urine Ca/Cr (<0.01); PHPT: High Urine Ca (>7.5 mmol/24h)
Malignancy vs PHPTMalignancy: PTH Suppressed; PHPT: PTH High/Inappropriately Normal
Surgical CriteriaAge <50; Ca >2.85; eGFR <60; BMD T-score ≤-2.5; Stones; Urine Ca >10 mmol
LocalisationSestamibi + US (Concordant → Focused Surgery); Discordant → 4D-CT / 4D-CT / MRI
IOPTH (Miami)>50% Drop from Peak at 10min = Cure
Hungry Bone SyndromePost-Op Hypocalcaemia (Rapid Bone Remineralisation) → IV Calcium Gluconate
PTH in MalignancySuppressed (<1 pmol/L); PTHrP Positive
FHHCaSR Mutation; Urine Ca/Cr <0.01; No Surgery
CinacalcetCalcimimetic; ↓ PTH; Hypocalcaemia Risk
CarcinomaRare (<1%); Ca >3.5, Mass, RLN Invasion, CDC73 Mutation

15. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
PHPT vs FHHUrine Ca/Cr Ratio: PHPT >0.02; FHH <0.01
PHPT vs MalignancyPHPT: PTH High; Malignancy: PTH Suppressed, PTHrP+
PTH in FHHNormal/Mildly ↑; In PHPT: Inappropriately High/Normal
CinacalcetCalcimimetic; ↓ PTH; Risk: Hypocalcaemia, Nausea
Carcinoma vs AdenomaCa >3.5, Mass >3cm, RLN Invasion, CDC73 Mutation
Surgical IndicationsAge <50, Ca >2.85, eGFR <60, BMD ≤-2.5, Stones, Urine Ca >10mmol
IOPTH Miami Criterion>50% Drop from Peak at 10min Post-Excision = Cure
Hungry Bone SyndromePost-Op Hypocalcaemia (Rapid Bone Uptake) → IV Ca Gluconate

Mnemonic: PHPT SURGICAL CRITERIA (AAES)

  • Age <50
  • Calcium >2.85
  • Kidney (eGFR <60 / Stones)
  • Bone (T-score ≤-2.5 / Fracture)
  • Urine Ca >10 mmol/day

Mnemonic: FHH vs PHPT DISCRIMINATORS

  • FHH: Family History (AD), Hypocalciuria (<0.01), FH
  • PHPT: High Urine Ca (>7.5), PTH High/Inappropriately Normal

16. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Primary Hyperparathyroidism))
    Aetiology
      Adenoma (80-85%)
      Hyperplasia (10-15%)
      Double Adenoma (5-10%)
      Carcinoma (<1%)
    Pathophysiology
      PTH → ↑ Bone Resorption → Ca Release
      PTH → ↑ Renal Ca Reabsorption
      PTH → ↑ 1,25-(OH)2D → ↑ Gut Ca Absorption
      Result: Hypercalcaemia, Hypophosphataemia
    Diagnosis
      Ca²⁺ High + PTH Inappropriately High/Normal
      24h Urine Ca >7.5 mmol/d
      Differentials: Malignancy (PTH Low), FHH (Urine Ca/Cr <0.01)
    Localisation
      Sestamibi + US (Concordant = Focused Surgery)
      4D-CT / MRI if Discordant
    Surgery
      Focused Parathyroidectomy (TSS-like)
      IOPTH: >50% Drop at 10min
      Complications: RLN Injury, Hypocalcaemia (Hungry Bone)
    Non-Surgical
      Cinacalcet (Calcimimetic)
      Bisphosphonates / Denosumab (Bone)
    Carcinoma
      Ca >3.5, Mass >3cm, RLN Invasion
      CDC73 Mutation

17. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
PHPT Commonest CauseHypercalcaemia in Outpatients (80% Asymptomatic)
Diagnostic HallmarkHigh Ca + Inappropriately High/Normal PTH
FHH vs PHPTFHH: Urine Ca/Cr <0.01; PHPT: Urine Ca >7.5 mmol/24h
Malignancy vs PHPTMalignancy: PTH Suppressed; PHPT: PTH High/Inappropriately Normal
Surgical Criteria (AAES)Age <50; Ca >2.85; eGFR <60; BMD T-score ≤-2.5; Stones; Urine Ca >10 mmol
IOPTH Miami Criterion>50% Drop from Peak at 10min Post-Excision = Cure
FHH DiscriminationUrine Ca/Cr <0.01; CaSR Mutation; No Surgery Needed
CarcinomaRare (<1%); Ca >3.5, Mass >3cm, RLN Invasion, CDC73 Mutation
IOPTH Miami Criterion>50% Drop from Peak at 10min = Predicts Cure
Hungry Bone SyndromePost-Op Hypocalcaemia → IV Calcium Gluconate
Refractory Hypocalcaemia Post-OpIV Calcium Gluconate 10-20mL 10% Bolus

18. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Parathyroid & Calcium|Parathyroid & Calcium]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Secondary Hyperparathyroidism (CKD)]], [[Tertiary Hyperparathyroidism]], [[Hypoparathyroidism]], [[FHH (Familial Hypocalciuric Hypercalcaemia)]], [[Hypercalcaemia of Malignancy]], [[Hypocalcaemia]], [[Vitamin D Metabolism & Deficiency]]

19. MCQs (10)

  1. Primary hyperparathyroidism =

A. PTH excess (autonomous) → hypercalcaemia + hypophosphataemia + high PTH

B. Aldosterone excess → hypercalcaemia

C. Vitamin D excess → hypercalcaemia

D. Renal failure → high PTH

E. Malignancy → high PTH

  1. Commonest cause primary HPT:

A. Parathyroid adenoma (80-85%)

B. Parathyroid hyperplasia (15-20%)

C. Parathyroid carcinoma (<1%)

D. MEN1

E. MEN2A

  1. Biochemical hallmark:

A. Hypercalcaemia + hypophosphataemia + elevated/inappropriately normal PTH

B. Hypocalcaemia + high PTH

C. Hypercalcaemia + low PTH

D. Normal calcium + high PTH

E. Hypercalcaemia + high phosphate

  1. Asymptomatic primary HPT criteria for surgery:

A. Age <50, Ca >0.25 above ULN, eGFR <60, kidney stones, BMD T-score <-2.5, <1yr fracture

B. Always operate

C. Only if symptomatic

D. Never operate

E. Only if Ca >3.0 mmol/L

  1. Post-parathyroidectomy hungry bone syndrome:

A. Hypocalcaemia + hypophosphataemia + hypomagnesaemia → prolonged Ca/Vit D

B. Hypercalcaemia

C. Normal calcium

D. Hypermagnesaemia

E. Hyperphosphataemia

  1. FHH vs primary HPT:

A. FHH: high Ca, normal/high PTH, LOW urinary Ca excretion (CCCR <0.01)

B. FHH: low Ca

C. FHH: low PTH

D. FHH: high urinary Ca

E. Same as primary HPT

  1. Parathyroid carcinoma features:

A. Ca >3.5, PTH very high, palpable mass, renal/bone mets

B. Same as adenoma

C. Always asymptomatic

D. Ca normal

E. PTH low

  1. Cinacalcet use:

A. Calcimimetic: ↓ PTH, ↓ Ca (secondary HPT in CKD, parathyroid carcinoma)

B. Increases PTH

C. Vitamin D analog

D. Bisphosphonate

E. Calcium supplement

  1. Primary HPT in MEN1:

A. Multigland hyperplasia; all 4 glands; recurrent

B. Single adenoma

C. Always carcinoma

D. No hypercalcaemia

E. Only in MEN2A

  1. Normocalcaemic primary HPT:

A. PTH elevated, Ca normal, high bone turnover, low Vit D

B. Ca high, PTH normal

C. Ca low, PTH high

D. Both normal

E. Diagnosis of exclusion

20. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 55yo woman: asymptomatic, Ca 2.85, PTH 12, phosphate 0.7, eGFR 80, no stones, BMD T-score -1.0. Management?

A. Surgery (meets asymptomatic criteria: age<50? no, but Ca>0.25)

B. Observation

C. Cinacalcet

D. Bisphosphonate

E. Hydration only

  1. Same but Ca 3.1, renal stones. Management?

A. Surgery (stones = indication)

B. Observation

C. Cinacalcet

D. Hydration

E. Thiazide

  1. Patient: Ca 2.9, PTH 15, 24h urinary Ca 1.0 mmol/day (low). CCCR 0.005. Diagnosis?

A. FHH (no surgery needed)

B. Primary HPT (surgery)

C. Secondary HPT

D. Malignancy

E. Vit D toxicity

  1. Post-parathyroidectomy day 1: Ca 1.9, P 0.5, Mg 0.6. Symptoms: tingling. Rx?

A. IV calcium gluconate → oral Ca/Vit D/Mg (hungry bone)

B. Observation

C. Calcitonin

D. Cinacalcet

E. Stop Ca supplements

  1. Parathyroid carcinoma: Ca 3.8, PTH 500, 4cm neck mass. Treatment?

A. En bloc resection (+ ipsilateral thyroid lobectomy)

B. Simple parathyroidectomy

C. Cinacalcet only

D. Observation

E. Radiotherapy

21. Flashcards

  • Q: Primary HPT

A: PTH excess → hypercalcaemia + hypophosphataemia + high PTH

  • Q: Commonest cause

A: Parathyroid adenoma (80-85%); hyperplasia 15-20%; carcinoma <1%

  • Q: Asymptomatic criteria for surgery

A: Age<50, Ca>0.25 ULN, eGFR<60, stones, BMD<-2.5, <1yr fracture

  • Q: FHH

A: High Ca, normal/high PTH, LOW urinary Ca (CCCR<0.01); NO surgery

  • Q: Post-op hungry bone

A: Hypocalcaemia + hypophosphataemia + hypomagnesaemia; prolonged Ca/Vit D

  • Q: Cinacalcet

A: Calcimimetic: ↓PTH, ↓Ca; secondary HPT (CKD), parathyroid carcinoma

  • Q: Parathyroid carcinoma

A: Ca>3.5, PTH very high, palpable mass; en bloc resection

  • Q: MEN1 HPT

A: Multigland hyperplasia; recurrent

  • Q: Normocalcaemic HPT

A: PTH↑, Ca normal, high bone turnover, low Vit D

  • Q: CCCR

A: Ca:Cr clearance ratio; <0.01 = FHH; >0.02 = primary HPT

22. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. PTH excess (autonomous) → hypercalcaemia + hypophosphataemia + high PTH — Primary hyperparathyroidism =
  1. Parathyroid adenoma (80-85%) — Commonest cause primary HPT:
  1. Hypercalcaemia + hypophosphataemia + elevated/inappropriately normal PTH — Biochemical hallmark:
  1. Age <50, Ca >0.25 above ULN, eGFR <60, kidney stones, BMD T-score <-2.5, <1yr fracture — Asymptomatic primary HPT criteria for surgery:
  1. Hypocalcaemia + hypophosphataemia + hypomagnesaemia → prolonged Ca/Vit D — Post-parathyroidectomy hungry bone syndrome:
  1. FHH: high Ca, normal/high PTH, LOW urinary Ca excretion (CCCR <0.01) — FHH vs primary HPT:
  1. Ca >3.5, PTH very high, palpable mass, renal/bone mets — Parathyroid carcinoma features:
  1. Calcimimetic: ↓ PTH, ↓ Ca (secondary HPT in CKD, parathyroid carcinoma) — Cinacalcet use:
  1. Multigland hyperplasia; all 4 glands; recurrent — Primary HPT in MEN1:
  1. PTH elevated, Ca normal, high bone turnover, low Vit D — Normocalcaemic primary HPT:

SBAs

  1. Surgery (meets asymptomatic criteria: age<50? no, but Ca>0.25) — 55yo woman: asymptomatic, Ca 2.85, PTH 12, phosphate 0.7, eGFR 80, no stones, BMD T-score -1.0. Management?
  1. Surgery (stones = indication) — Same but Ca 3.1, renal stones. Management?
  1. FHH (no surgery needed) — Patient: Ca 2.9, PTH 15, 24h urinary Ca 1.0 mmol/day (low). CCCR 0.005. Diagnosis?
  1. IV calcium gluconate → oral Ca/Vit D/Mg (hungry bone) — Post-parathyroidectomy day 1: Ca 1.9, P 0.5, Mg 0.6. Symptoms: tingling. Rx?
  1. En bloc resection (+ ipsilateral thyroid lobectomy) — Parathyroid carcinoma: Ca 3.8, PTH 500, 4cm neck mass. Treatment?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.5.08 Secondary Hyperparathyroidism Ckd
[!info]
Secondary Hyperparathyroidism (SHPT) = Compensatory PTH Hypersecretion driven by CKD-Mineral and Bone Disorder (CKD-MBD). Universal in CKD Stage 4-5. Pathophysiology: Phosphate Retention → FGF23 ↑ → 1,25-(OH)₂D ↓ → Hypocalcaemia → PTH ↑. KDIGO Target: PTH 2-9x ULN.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Explain the pathophysiology of SHPT in CKD (phosphate → FGF23 → Vit D → Ca²⁺ → PTH cascade)
  • [ ] Apply KDIGO diagnostic and treatment targets for PTH, Calcium, Phosphate
  • [ ] Sequence management: Phosphate binders → Vitamin D analogues → Calcimimetics
  • [ ] Identify criteria for parathyroidectomy in refractory SHPT
  • [ ] Differentiate from tertiary HPT and primary HPT

2. Pathophysiology — The CKD-MBD Cascade

CKD (↓ GFR)
    │
    ├─→ **Phosphate Retention** (↓ Filtered Load)
    │       │
    │       ├─→ **FGF23 ↑** (Osteocyte Response) → ↓ 1α-Hydroxylase → **↓ 1,25-(OH)₂D**
    │       │       │
    │       │       └─→ **↓ Intestinal Ca²⁺ Absorption** → **Hypocalcaemia**
    │       │
    │       └─→ **Direct PTH Stimulation** (Phosphate → PTH Gene Expression)
    │
    └─→ **Hypocalcaemia** (↓ Ca²⁺ Absorption + ↓ Vit D) → **PTH ↑↑** (Secondary HPT)

Key Mediators

MediatorChange in CKDEffect
Phosphate↑↑ (Retention)Direct PTH Stim; FGF23 ↑
FGF23↑↑↑ (Early Rise)↓ 1α-Hydroxylase; ↓ 1,25-(OH)₂D
1,25-(OH)₂D (Calcitriol)↓↓↓↓ Intestinal Ca²⁺ Absorption → Hypocalcaemia
Calcium↓↑ PTH Secretion
PTH↑↑↑ (Secondary HPT)Bone Resorption; Phosphate Excretion

3. Stages of CKD-MBD (KDIGO)

CKD StageGFR (mL/min)PhosphateFGF231,25-(OH)₂DPTHBone Turnover
G3a (45-59)MildNormal↑Normal/↓↑ (1-2x ULN)Normal/High
G3b (30-44)Moderate↑↑↑↓↑↑ (2-5x ULN)High
G4 (15-29)Severe↑↑↑↑↑↓↓↑↑↑ (5-10x ULN)High/Adynamic
G5 (<15) / DialysisFailure↑↑↑↑↑↑↑Absent↑↑↑↑ (10-50x ULN)Adynamic/Mixed

4. KDIGO 2017 Targets (CKD Stage 3-5D)

ParameterTarget (CKD 3-5D)Target (Dialysis)
PTH2-9x ULN2-9x ULN
Corrected Calcium2.1-2.5 mmol/L2.1-2.5 mmol/L
Phosphate0.8-1.5 mmol/L (Stage 3-4)1.1-1.5 mmol/L (Dialysis)
25-OH Vitamin D≥30 ng/mL (75 nmol/L)≥30 ng/mL
Alkaline PhosphataseMonitor TrendsMonitor Trends
Note: PTH Target is Wide (2-9x ULN) — Avoid Over-Suppression (Adynamic Bone Disease).

5. Management Algorithm (KDIGO 2017)

Stepwise Approach

SHPT Diagnosed (CKD G3-5, PTH >9x ULN or Rising)
         │
         ▼
STEP 1: **PHOSPHATE BINDERS** (Lower Phosphate → ↓ FGF23 → ↑ Vit D → ↓ PTH)
         │
         ├── **Calcium-Based** (Calcium Carbonate/Acetate) — **First-Line if Ca²⁺ Normal/Low**
         │       Dose: 0.5-1g with Meals; Max 1.5-2g Elemental Ca/day
         │       Watch: Hypercalcaemia, Vascular Calcification
         │
         ├── **Non-Calcium-Based** (Sevelamer, Lanthanum, Sucroferric Oxyhydroxide)
         │       **First-Line if Ca²⁺ High / Vascular Calcification / Adynamic Bone**
         │       Sevelamer 800-2400mg TDS with Meals
         │
         └── **Ferric Citrate** (Newer; Also Treats Anaemia)
                 Dose: 1g TDS with Meals
                 │
         ▼
STEP 2: **VITAMIN D ANALOGUES** (If PTH Still >9x ULN after Phosphate Control)
         │
         ├── **Alfacalcidol (1α-OH D3)** 0.25-1µg/day (or 1µg Alternate Days)
         ├── **Calcitriol (1,25-(OH)₂D)** 0.25-0.5µg/day (or 1µg Alternate Days)
         ├── **Paricalcitol** (Selective VDR Agonist) 1-2µg 3x/week (Post-HD)
         │       Advantage: Less Hypercalcaemia/Hyperphosphataemia
         │
         ▼
STEP 3: **CALCIMIMETICS** (If PTH Still >9x ULN Despite Above)
         │
         ├── **Cinacalcet** 30-90mg BD (Max 180mg/day)
         │       Mechanism: CaSR Agonist → ↓ PTH Secretion
         │       Adverse: Hypocalcaemia, Nausea, QTc Prolongation
         │       Advantage: ↓ PTH, ↓ Ca, ↓ Phosphate
         │
         └── **Etelcalcetide** (IV, Post-HD) 5-15mg 3x/week (Dialysis Only)
                 │
                 ▼
STEP 4: **PARATHYROIDECTOMY** (If Refractory SHPT)

6. Monitoring Targets (KDIGO)

ParameterCKD 3-5 (Non-Dialysis)Dialysis (HD/PD)
PTHq3-6mo (G3-4); q3mo (G5)Monthly
Calciumq3-6moMonthly
Phosphateq3-6moMonthly
PTH TrendAvoid <2x ULN (Adynamic Bone)Avoid <2x ULN
Alkaline PhosphataseMonitor Bone TurnoverMonitor Bone Turnover
25-OH Vit D≥30 ng/mL≥30 ng/mL

7. Vitamin D Therapy — Key Distinctions

AgentActive MetabolitesIndicationAdvantage
Cholecalciferol (D3) / Ergocalciferol (D2)Requires 25-OH → 1,25-(OH)₂D (Renal)Nutritional Deficiency (CKD G1-3)Cheap; Long Half-Life
Alfacalcidol (1α-OH D3)1,25-(OH)₂D (Rapid, No Renal Step)CKD G4-5 / DialysisPredictable; Short Half-Life
Calcitriol (1,25-(OH)₂D₃)Active HormoneCKD G4-5 / DialysisPotent; Short Half-Life
ParicalcitolSelective VDR AgonistCKD G4-5 / DialysisLess Hypercalcaemia/Phosphataemia
Doxercalciferol1α-OH D2 (Prohormone)CKD G4-5Less Hypercalcaemia
Key: Nutritional Vit D (D2/D3) for CKD G1-3; Active Analogues (Alfacalcidol/Calcitriol/Paricalcitol) for CKD G4-5/Dialysis.

8. Calcimimetics (Cinacalcet / Etelcalcetide)

DrugDoseMechanismAdverse EffectsMonitoring
Cinacalcet30-180mg/day (Divided BD)CaSR Agonist → ↓ PTH SecretionHypocalcaemia, Nausea, QTc ProlongationCa²⁺, PTH q1-2mo; ECG (QTc)
Etelcalcetide5-15mg 3x/week (IV Post-HD)CaSR Agonist (IV)Hypocalcaemia, HypotensionCa²⁺, PTH Monthly

Key Advantage: ↓ PTH + ↓ Ca²⁺ + ↓ Phosphate (Unique Triple Effect).

9. Parathyroidectomy (PTX) — Indications for Refractory SHPT

IndicationCriteria
Medical FailurePTH >800-1000 pg/mL (9x ULN) Despite Maximal Medical Therapy
HypercalcaemiaPersistent Ca²⁺ >2.75 mmol/L Despite Medical Therapy
HyperphosphataemiaPersistent Phosphate >1.8 mmol/L Despite Binders
SymptomsSevere Bone Pain, Pruritus, Calciphylaxis, Fractures
Adynamic BoneExclude (Low PTH <2x ULN + Low ALP) → Contraindicated

Surgical Approach

ProcedureDetails
Subtotal (3.5-Gland) PTXStandard for SHPT; Leave 50-70mg Remnant
Total PTX + AutotransplantForearm (Brachioradialis); Higher Recurrence Risk
Intraoperative PTH>50% Drop at 10min Post-Excision (Miami Criterion)

10. Complications of SHPT & Treatment

ComplicationMechanismMonitoring
Renal OsteodystrophyHigh Turnover (Osteitis Fibrosa) → FracturesDEXA q2-3yr; ALP, PTH
Adynamic Bone DiseasePTH <100-150 pg/mL (Over-Suppression)PTH >2x ULN; Avoid Over-Suppression
Vascular CalcificationCa × Phosphate Product >4.5; High PhosphateCXR/CT (Coronary/Valvular); Phosphate Control
CalciphylaxisMedial Calcification → Skin NecrosisUrgent PTX; Sodium Thiosulfate
Fracture RiskHigh Turnover + Adynamic BoneDEXA q2-3yr; Vertebral Fracture Assessment

11. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
SHPT PathophysiologyPhosphate ↑ → FGF23 ↑ → 1,25-(OH)₂D ↓ → Ca²⁺ ↓ → PTH ↑↑
FGF23Osteocyte Hormone; ↑ Early in CKD; ↓ 1α-Hydroxylase; ↑ Phosphate Excretion
KDIGO PTH Target2-9x ULN (Avoid <2x ULN = Adynamic Bone)
First-Line Phosphate BinderCalcium Carbonate/Acetate (If Ca Normal); Sevelamer if Ca High
Vitamin D in CKD G4-5Active Analogues (Alfacalcidol, Calcitriol, Paricalcitol) — Not Cholecalciferol
CalcimimeticCinacalcet (CaSR Agonist) → ↓ PTH, ↓ Ca, ↓ Phos; Hypocalcaemia Risk
Parathyroidectomy IndicationsPTH >800-1000 pg/mL (Refractory); Ca >2.75; Phos >1.8; Calciphylaxis
Adynamic Bone DiseasePTH <2x ULN (Over-Suppressed); Low Bone Turnover → Fracture Risk
CalciphylaxisUrgent PTX + Sodium Thiosulfate; Mortality 50-80%
FGF23↑ Early in CKD; ↓ 1,25-(OH)₂D; ↑ Phosphate Excretion

12. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Nutritional Vit D vs Active AnaloguesCholecalciferol (D3) needs renal 1α-hydroxylation — Useless in CKD G4-5; Use Alfacalcidol/Calcitriol/Paricalcitol
PTH Target2-9x ULN — Not Normalisation! Too Low = Adynamic Bone
Calcium BindersCalcium-Based = Cheapest; Non-Calcium (Sevelamer/Lanthanum) if Hypercalcaemia/Calcification
Cinacalcet vs Vitamin DCinacalcet = ↓ PTH + ↓ Ca + ↓ Phos; Vit D = ↑ Ca + ↑ Phos (Risk)
Alfacalcidol vs CalcitriolAlfacalcidol (1α-OH D3) → Rapid Conversion; Calcitriol = Active Form
ParicalcitolSelective VDR Agonist; Less Hypercalcaemia/Phosphataemia than Calcitriol
