Adynamic BonePTH <2x ULN; Low ALP; High Fracture Risk; Avoid Over-Suppression
CalciphylaxisUrgent PTX + Sodium Thiosulfate + Pain Control; Mortality 50-80%

13. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Secondary HPT in CKD))
    Pathophysiology
      Phosphate Retention → FGF23 ↑
      FGF23 → 1,25-(OH)2D ↓
      1,25-(OH)2D ↓ → Ca Absorption ↓
      Ca ↓ → PTH ↑↑
      FGF23 also directly stimulates PTH
    KDIGO Targets
      PTH: 2-9x ULN
      Ca: 2.1-2.5 mmol/L
      Phos: 0.8-1.5 (G3-4) / 1.1-1.5 (HD)
      Vit D: ≥30 ng/mL
    Treatment Cascade
      1. Phosphate Binders (Ca/Non-Ca)
      2. Vit D Analogues (Alfacalcidol/Calcitriol/Paricalcitol)
      3. Calcimimetics (Cinacalcet/Etelcalcetide)
      4. Parathyroidectomy (Refractory)
    Key Drugs
      Binders: CaCO3, Sevelamer, Lanthanum, Ferric Citrate
      Vit D: Alfacalcidol, Calcitriol, Paricalcitol
      Calcimimetic: Cinacalcet (CaSR Agonist)
    PTX Indications
      PTH >800-1000 pg/mL; Ca >2.75; Phos >1.8
      Calciphylaxis; Symptomatic Bone Disease
    Complications
      Adynamic Bone (PTH <2x ULN)
      Vascular Calcification (Ca x Phos >4.5)
      Calciphylaxis (Urgent PTX + Thiosulfate)

14. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
SHPT PathophysiologyPhosphate ↑ → FGF23 ↑ → 1,25-(OH)₂D ↓ → Ca²⁺ ↓ → PTH ↑↑
FGF23Osteocyte Hormone; ↑ Early CKD; ↓ 1α-Hydroxylase; ↑ Phosphate Excretion
KDIGO PTH Target2-9x ULN (Avoid <2x ULN = Adynamic Bone)
First-Line Phosphate BinderCalcium Carbonate/Acetate (If Ca Normal); Sevelamer (If Ca High)
Vitamin D in CKD G4-5Active Analogues (Alfacalcidol, Calcitriol, Paricalcitol) — Not Cholecalciferol
CinacalcetCaSR Agonist → ↓ PTH, ↓ Ca, ↓ Phos; Monitor Hypocalcaemia
Parathyroidectomy IndicationsPTH >800-1000 pg/mL (Refractory); Ca >2.75; Phos >1.8
Adynamic Bone DiseasePTH <2x ULN (Over-Suppressed); Low ALP; ↑ Fracture Risk
CalciphylaxisUrgent PTX + Sodium Thiosulfate; Mortality 50-80%
FGF23↑ Early CKD; ↓ 1,25-(OH)2D; ↑ Phosphate Excretion

15. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Parathyroid & Calcium|Parathyroid & Calcium]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Primary Hyperparathyroidism]], [[Tertiary Hyperparathyroidism]], [[CKD-MBD]], [[Vitamin D Metabolism & Deficiency]], [[Hypocalcaemia]]

16. MCQs (10)

  1. Secondary hyperparathyroidism =

A. PTH elevation in response to chronic hypocalcaemia (CKD, Vit D deficiency)

B. Autonomous PTH secretion

C. Malignancy-related PTHrP

D. FHH

E. Primary parathyroid adenoma

  1. CKD-MBD pathophysiology:

A. Low Vit D -> dCa -> uPTH + dPO4 excretion -> hyperphosphataemia -> vascular calcification

B. High Vit D -> uCa

C. Normal PTH

D. Low phosphate

E. High Ca

  1. CKD secondary HPT treatment:

A. Vitamin D analogues (calcitriol, paricalcitol) + phosphate binders + cinacalcet if refractory

B. Parathyroidectomy first line

C. Cinacalcet only

D. High calcium diet

E. Loop diuretics

  1. Target PTH in CKD stage 3-5:

A. 2-9x ULN (KDIGO)

B. Normal

C.

D. >20x ULN

E. Not monitored

  1. Tertiary hyperparathyroidism:

A. Autonomous PTH secretion after long-standing secondary HPT (post-transplant)

B. Primary HPT

C. FHH

D. Malignancy

E. Vit D excess

  1. Phosphate binders in CKD:

A. Calcium-based (carbonate/acetate) + non-calcium (sevelamer, lanthanum)

B. Only calcium

C. Only non-calcium

D. Aluminium only

E. Magnesium only

  1. Calcitriol in CKD:

A. Active Vit D; uCa, uPO4, dPTH suppression; monitor Ca*PO4 product

B. Lowers phosphate

C. Lowers calcium

D. No effect on PTH

E. Contraindicated

  1. Cinacalcet in secondary HPT:

A. Calcimimetic; dPTH, dCa, dPO4; use if PTH refractory to Vit D/binders

B. Vitamin D analog

C. Phosphate binder

D. Increases PTH

E. Contraindicated

  1. Adynamic bone disease:

A. Low PTH (<100-150) -> low bone turnover; fracture risk; avoid over-suppressing PTH

B. High PTH

C. Normal PTH

D. High bone turnover

E. Osteoporosis only

  1. CKD-MBD monitoring:

A. Ca, PO4, PTH, ALP, Vit D q3-6mo (stage 3-5)

B. Only Ca

C. Only PTH

D. Only PO4

E. Only ALP

17. SBA Questions (10)

  1. CKD stage 4: Ca 2.1, PO4 1.8, PTH 450 (ULN 65). On calcitriol. Next?

A. Add phosphate binder + consider cinacalcet

B. Increase calcitriol

C. Parathyroidectomy

D. Stop calcitriol

E. High calcium diet

  1. Post-renal transplant: Ca 2.6, PTH 300 (persistent). Diagnosis?

A. Tertiary HPT (autonomous post-transplant)

B. Secondary HPT

C. Primary HPT

D. FHH

E. Malignancy

  1. Adynamic bone disease: PTH 80, low ALP, fractures. Management?

A. Reduce/stop Vit D analogues; avoid oversuppression

B. Increase calcitriol

C. Cinacalcet

D. Parathyroidectomy

E. High calcium

  1. Cinacalcet side effect:

A. Hypocalcaemia; monitor Ca closely

B. Hypercalcaemia

C. Hyperphosphataemia

D. Hyperkalaemia

E. Hypertension

  1. CKD stage 5 on dialysis: PTH >800 despite max medical. Next?

A. Parathyroidectomy (subtotal/total + autotransplant)

B. Increase cinacalcet

C. Increase calcitriol

D. Observation

E. Phosphate binders only

18. Flashcards

  • Q: Secondary HPT

A: PTH up in response to hypocalcaemia (CKD, Vit D def); NOT autonomous

  • Q: CKD-MBD

A: Low Vit D -> dCa -> uPTH + dPO4 excretion -> hyperphosphataemia -> vascular calcification

  • Q: Treatment

A: Vit D analogues + phosphate binders + cinacalcet (refractory)

  • Q: Target PTH CKD

A: 2-9x ULN (KDIGO); avoid <100-150 (adynamic bone)

  • Q: Tertiary HPT

A: Autonomous PTH after long-standing secondary (post-transplant); surgery if severe

  • Q: Phosphate binders

A: Ca-based + non-Ca (sevelamer, lanthanum); avoid Al in dialysis

  • Q: Calcimimetic

A: Cinacalcet -> dPTH, dCa, dPO4

  • Q: Adynamic bone

A: PTH <100-150 -> low bone turnover; fracture risk

  • Q: Calcitriol

A: Active Vit D; uCa, uPO4; monitor Ca*PO4 product <4.4

  • Q: Post-transplant HPT

A: Tertiary -> persistent PTH up; parathyroidectomy if severe

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. PTH elevation in response to chronic hypocalcaemia (CKD, Vit D deficiency) — Secondary hyperparathyroidism =
  1. Low Vit D -> dCa -> uPTH + dPO4 excretion -> hyperphosphataemia -> vascular calcification — CKD-MBD pathophysiology:
  1. Vitamin D analogues (calcitriol, paricalcitol) + phosphate binders + cinacalcet if refractory — CKD secondary HPT treatment:
  1. 2-9x ULN (KDIGO) — Target PTH in CKD stage 3-5:
  1. Autonomous PTH secretion after long-standing secondary HPT (post-transplant) — Tertiary hyperparathyroidism:
  1. Calcium-based (carbonate/acetate) + non-calcium (sevelamer, lanthanum) — Phosphate binders in CKD:
  1. Active Vit D; uCa, uPO4, dPTH suppression; monitor Ca*PO4 product — Calcitriol in CKD:
  1. Calcimimetic; dPTH, dCa, dPO4; use if PTH refractory to Vit D/binders — Cinacalcet in secondary HPT:
  1. Low PTH (<100-150) -> low bone turnover; fracture risk; avoid over-suppressing PTH — Adynamic bone disease:
  1. Ca, PO4, PTH, ALP, Vit D q3-6mo (stage 3-5) — CKD-MBD monitoring:

SBAs

  1. Add phosphate binder + consider cinacalcet — CKD stage 4: Ca 2.1, PO4 1.8, PTH 450 (ULN 65). On calcitriol. Next?
  1. Tertiary HPT (autonomous post-transplant) — Post-renal transplant: Ca 2.6, PTH 300 (persistent). Diagnosis?
  1. Reduce/stop Vit D analogues; avoid oversuppression — Adynamic bone disease: PTH 80, low ALP, fractures. Management?
  1. Hypocalcaemia; monitor Ca closely — Cinacalcet side effect:
  1. Parathyroidectomy (subtotal/total + autotransplant) — CKD stage 5 on dialysis: PTH >800 despite max medical. Next?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.5.09 Tertiary Hyperparathyroidism
[!info]
Tertiary Hyperparathyroidism (THPT) = Autonomous PTH Hypersecretion after Long-Standing Secondary HPT. Autonomous PTH Secretion Persists Despite Correction of Stimulus (Hypercalcaemia). Occurs after Long-Standing SHPT (Usually >5-10 Years on Dialysis). Autonomy = PTH Fails to Suppress with Hypercalcaemia.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Define THPT and differentiate from primary and secondary HPT
  • [ ] Recognise the pathophysiology of autonomous PTH secretion
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic criteria (Hypercalcaemia + Inappropriately High PTH)
  • [ ] Apply parathyroidectomy criteria and surgical approach
  • [ ] Manage post-operative hypocalcaemia (Hungry Bone Syndrome)

2. Pathophysiology — From Secondary to Autonomous

StageMechanism
1. Secondary HPTPhosphate ↑ → FGF23 ↑ → 1,25-(OH)₂D ↓ → Hypocalcaemia → Reactive PTH ↑↑
2. Prolonged StimulationMonoclonal Expansion of Parathyroid Chief Cells → Clonal Autonomy
3. Autonomous SecretionPTH Secretion Becomes Calcium-Independent → PTH ↑ Despite Hypercalcaemia
GeneticsClonal Expansion (Monoclonal > Polyclonal); MEN1, CDC73, PRAD1 Mutations (Rare in Sporadic)

3. Natural History & Progression

StageDurationCalciumPTHAutonomy
Early SHPT<2 Years on DialysisLow/Normal↑↑ (Reactive)None (Calcium-Dependent)
Advanced SHPT5-10 YearsNormal/Mild ↑↑↑↑ (High)Developing Autonomy
Tertiary HPT>5-10 Years / Post-TransplantHigh (Hypercalcaemia)Inappropriately HighFull Autonomy (PTH Fails to Suppress)

4. Diagnostic Criteria — THPT vs Primary vs Secondary HPT

FeaturePrimary HPTSecondary HPTTertiary HPT
CalciumHighLow/NormalHigh (Hypercalcaemia)
PTHHigh / Inappropriately NormalHigh (Reactive)High (Autonomous)
PhosphateLowHigh (CKD)Low/Normal (Variable)
Calcium x PhosphateNormal/HighHigh (CKD)High
Vitamin DLow/NormalVery LowLow/Normal
Renal FunctionNormalCKD G4-5 / DialysisCKD G4-5 / Post-Transplant
PTH Response to CalciumSuppressesSuppresses (Initially)Fails to Suppress
AutonomyYes (Adenoma)No (Reactive)Yes (Autonomous)

5. Clinical Presentation

FeatureDetails
HypercalcaemiaPersistent (Ca²⁺ >2.6 mmol/L) Despite Dialysis/Transplant
PTHInappropriately High (Often >500-1000 pg/mL)
Bone DiseaseRenal Osteodystrophy — Osteitis Fibrosa, Fractures, Bone Pain
Vascular CalcificationSevere (Coronary, Valvular, Peripheral)
CalciphylaxisHigh Risk (Ca × Po₄ >4.5 mmol²/L²)
Post-TransplantPersistent Hypercalcaemia >3-6 Months Post-Transplant

6. Diagnosis — THPT vs Primary vs Secondary HPT

FeaturePrimary HPTSecondary HPTTertiary HPT
CalciumHighLow/NormalHigh
PTHHighHigh (Reactive)High (Autonomous)
PhosphateLowHighLow/Normal
Renal FunctionNormalCKD G4-5 / DialysisCKD G5 / Post-Transplant
PTH Response to High CaSuppressesSuppresses (Eventually)Fails to Suppress
AutonomyYes (Adenoma)No (Reactive)Yes (Autonomous)
SettingNormal Renal FunctionCKD G4-5 / DialysisLong-Standing SHPT / Post-Transplant

7. Diagnostic Algorithm

CKD G4-5 / Dialysis / Post-Transplant + Hypercalcaemia + High PTH
         │
         ▼
EXCLUDE PRIMARY HPT
         │
         ├── Renal Function NORMAL → PRIMARY HPT
         │
         └── Renal Function IMPAIRED
                 │
                 ├── HYPERCALCAEMIA + HIGH PTH + CKD/DIALYSIS HISTORY
                 │       → TERTIARY HPT
                 │
                 └── NORMAL CALCIUM + HIGH PTH + CKD/DIALYSIS
                         → SECONDARY HPT

Functional Autonomy Test

TestProtocolTertiary HPT Result
Calcium Infusion10% Ca Gluconate 10-20mL IV over 10min → PTH at 0, 15, 30, 60minPTH Fails to Suppress (<30% Drop)
Oral Calcium Load2g CaCO₃ Oral → PTH at 0, 30, 60, 120minPTH Fails to Suppress

8. Clinical Features Specific to THPT

FeatureDetails
HypercalcaemiaCa²⁺ >2.6 mmol/L (Often >3.0) Persisting Despite Dialysis/Transplant
PTH>500-1000 pg/mL (Inappropriately High for Calcium Level)
Bone DiseaseSevere Renal Osteodystrophy (Osteitis Fibrosa, Brown Tumours, Fractures)
Vascular CalcificationSevere — Coronary, Valvular, Peripheral Arteries
Calciphylaxis RiskHigh (Ca × Po₄ >4.5 mmol²/L²)
Post-TransplantHypercalcaemia Persists >3-6 Months Post-Transplant = THPT

9. Management — Parathyroidectomy (PTX) is Definitive

Indications for PTX in THPT

IndicationDetails
Persistent HypercalcaemiaCa²⁺ >2.75 mmol/L (Post-Transplant >3-6 Months)
Severe Bone DiseaseFractures, Bone Pain, Osteitis Fibrosa
Vascular CalcificationProgressive Coronary/Valvular/Peripheral Calcification
CalciphylaxisUrgent Indication
Symptomatic HypercalcaemiaPolyuria, Confusion, Renal Stones, Pancreatitis
Failed Medical ManagementRefractory Hypercalcaemia on Dialysis/Transplant

Surgical Approach

ProcedureDetails
Subtotal (3.5-Gland) PTXStandard for THPT — Leave 50-70mg Remnant (≈50mg)
Total PTX + AutotransplantForearm (Brachioradialis); If All 4 Glands Enlarged
IOPTH (Miami Criterion)>50% Drop from Peak at 10min Post-Excision

Post-Op Management (THPT Specific)

ComplicationManagement
HypocalcaemiaSevere & Prolonged (Hungry Bone Syndrome More Intense)
IV Calcium10% Ca Gluconate 10-20mL Bolus → Infusion 1-2mg/kg/hr
Oral Calcium + Vit DHigh-Dose (Calcitriol 0.5-1µg + Ca 2-3g/day)
PhosphateHypophosphataemia Common → Oral Phosphate Supplements

10. Post-Transplant THPT Management

TimeframeManagement
0-3 MonthsObserve (May Resolve Spontaneously)
3-6 MonthsIf Ca²⁺ >2.75 + PTH High → THPT Likely → Workup for PTX
>6 MonthsDefinitive THPT → PTX Indicated

Post-Transplant Monitoring

TimepointTests
Week 1Ca, PTH, Phosphate, Mg, Alk Phos Daily
Month 1Ca, PTH, Phosphate, ALP, 25-OH Vit D
Month 3Full Bone Profile; Assess for THPT
Month 6If Hypercalcaemia Persists → PTX Workup

11. Medical Management (If PTX Not Feasible)

DrugDoseRole
Cinacalcet30-180mg/day (BD)Calcimimetic → ↓ PTH; Lowers Ca & Phos
Phosphate BindersSevelamer/Lanthanum/Ferric CitrateControl Phosphate (Avoid Ca-Based if Hypercalcaemia)
Vitamin D AnaloguesAvoid (Worsens Hypercalcaemia)
BisphosphonatesZoledronic Acid 5mg IV AnnualBone Protection (Not for Acute Hypercalcaemia)

12. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
THPT DefinitionAutonomous PTH Secretion after Long-Standing SHPT → PTH Fails to Suppress with Hypercalcaemia
Key DifferentiatorHypercalcaemia + High PTH (vs Secondary: Normal/Low Ca; Primary: Normal Renal Function)
PathogenesisMonoclonal Parathyroid Expansion → Autonomous PTH Secretion
Key DiagnosticHypercalcaemia + Inappropriately High PTH + CKD/Dialysis/Post-Transplant History
Post-TransplantHypercalcaemia >3-6 Months Post-Tx = THPT (vs Transient <3 Months)
Surgical IndicationPersistent Hypercalcaemia >3-6mo Post-Tx; Ca >2.75; Bone Disease; Calciphylaxis
SurgerySubtotal (3.5-Gland) PTX (Standard); IOPTH >50% Drop at 10min
Post-Op HypocalcaemiaSevere & Prolonged (Hungry Bone Syndrome) → Aggressive Ca/Vit D Replacement
Medical Rx (If No Surgery)Cinacalcet (Calcimimetic) → ↓ PTH, ↓ Ca, ↓ Phos; Phosphate Binders (Non-Ca)
Key DifferentiatorPrimary HPT: Normal Renal Function; THPT: CKD/Dialysis/Post-Transplant History

13. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Tertiary vs Primary HPTTertiary = CKD/Dialysis/Post-Transplant History + Autonomous PTH; Primary = Normal Renal Function
Tertiary vs Secondary HPTSecondary: Ca Normal/Low + PTH High (Reactive); Tertiary: Ca High + PTH High (Autonomous)
Post-Transplant Hypercalcaemia<3 Months = Transient (Tertiary Hypoparathyroidism Recovery); >3-6 Months = THPT
THPT vs Primary HPTPrimary: Normal Renal Function; THPT: CKD/Dialysis/Post-Transplant History
Autonomy TestCalcium Infusion → Tertiary: PTH Fails to Suppress; Primary: PTH Suppresses

Mnemonic: TERTIARY HPT

  • Time: Long-Standing SHPT (>5-10 Years Dialysis)
  • Escape: PTH Escapes Calcium Control (Autonomous)
  • Renal: CKD/Dialysis/Post-Transplant Setting
  • Tertiary = Tertiary (Autonomous PTH)
  • Important: Immobilization of Calcium Control
  • Autonomy: PTH Fails to Suppress with High Calcium
  • Requires: Parathyroidectomy (Subtotal)
  • Year: >5-10 Years Dialysis History

14. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Tertiary Hyperparathyroidism))
    Definition
      Autonomous PTH after Long-Standing SHPT
      PTH Fails to Suppress with Hypercalcaemia
    Pathophysiology
      SHPT → Monoclonal Parathyroid Expansion
      Autonomous PTH Secretion (Calcium-Independent)
      PTH Fails to Suppress with Hypercalcaemia
    Progression
      SHPT (Reactive) → Advanced SHPT → THPT (Autonomous)
      >5-10 Years Dialysis / Post-Transplant
    Diagnosis
      Hypercalcaemia + High PTH + CKD/Dialysis/Post-Tx
      PTH Fails to Suppress with Calcium Load
      Post-Transplant >3-6mo Hypercalcaemia = THPT
    Management
      PTX (Subtotal 3.5-Gland) = Curative
      IOPTH >50% Drop at 10min
      Post-Op: Hungry Bone Syndrome (Severe)
    Post-Transplant
      <3mo: Observe
      3-6mo: Workup for THPT
      >6mo: PTX Indicated
    Medical (If No Surgery)
      Cinacalcet (Calcimimetic)
      Non-Ca Phosphate Binders
      Avoid Vit D Analogues

15. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
THPT DefinitionAutonomous PTH after Long-Standing SHPT; PTH Fails to Suppress with Hypercalcaemia
Key DifferentiatorHypercalcaemia + High PTH + CKD/Dialysis/Post-Transplant History
PathogenesisMonoclonal Parathyroid Expansion → Autonomous PTH Secretion
Post-Transplant THPTHypercalcaemia >3-6 Months Post-Transplant
Surgical IndicationCa >2.75; Bone Disease; Vascular Calcification; Calciphylaxis
SurgerySubtotal (3.5-Gland) PTX; IOPTH >50% Drop at 10min
Post-Op HypocalcaemiaSevere & Prolonged (Hungry Bone Syndrome) → Aggressive Ca/Vit D
Medical RxCinacalcet (Calcimimetic) → ↓ PTH, ↓ Ca, ↓ Phos
Key DifferentiatorPrimary HPT: Normal Renal Function; THPT: CKD/Dialysis/Post-Transplant
vs Secondary HPTSecondary: Normocalcaemic/Low Ca; Tertiary: Hypercalcaemia
Autonomy TestCalcium Load → THPT: PTH Fails to Suppress

16. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Parathyroid & Calcium|Parathyroid & Calcium]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Primary Hyperparathyroidism]], [[Secondary Hyperparathyroidism (CKD)]], [[Hypoparathyroidism]], [[FHH (Familial Hypocalciuric Hypercalcaemia)]], [[Hypercalcaemia of Malignancy]]

17. MCQs (10)

  1. Tertiary hyperparathyroidism =

A. Autonomous PTH hypersecretion after long-standing secondary HPT (usually post-renal transplant)

B. Primary parathyroid adenoma

C. Secondary HPT still responsive

D. FHH

E. Malignancy

  1. Tertiary HPT occurs after:

A. Renal transplant (persistent secondary HPT -> autonomy)

B. Parathyroidectomy

C. Vit D therapy

D. Cinacalcet therapy

E. Phosphate binders

  1. Tertiary HPT biochemistry:

A. Hypercalcaemia + high PTH (autonomous, not suppressible)

B. Hypocalcaemia + high PTH

C. Normal Ca + high PTH

D. Hypercalcaemia + low PTH

E. Normal Ca + normal PTH

  1. Tertiary HPT treatment:

A. Parathyroidectomy (subtotal 3.5 glands or total + autotransplant)

B. Cinacalcet only

C. Vit D analogues

D. Phosphate binders

E. Observation

  1. Tertiary vs primary HPT:

A. Tertiary: post-CKD/transplant history; Primary: no CKD history

B. Same

C. Tertiary: low Ca

D. Primary: history of CKD

E. Tertiary: low PTH

  1. Tertiary HPT recurrence:

A. Common if subtotal; monitor Ca/PTH lifelong

B. Never recurs

C. Only if total parathyroidectomy

D. Only if autotransplant fails

E. Only in MEN

  1. Autotransplant in tertiary HPT:

A. Forearm (brachioradialis); easy access for re-operation

B. Neck

C. Chest

D. Abdomen

E. Thigh

  1. Tertiary HPT indications for surgery:

A. Hypercalcaemia, symptomatic, bone disease, kidney stones

B. Asymptomatic normocalcaemic

C. Only if PTH>1000

D. Only if Ca>3.0

E. Never operate

  1. Post-parathyroidectomy in tertiary:

A. High hungry bone syndrome risk (severe osteodystrophy) -> prolonged Ca/Vit D

B. Low hungry bone risk

C. No hypocalcaemia

D. Immediate normocalcaemia

E. Cinacalcet needed

  1. Tertiary HPT in MEN:

A. MEN1: hyperplasia; MEN2A: hyperplasia; both can progress to tertiary

B. Only MEN1

C. Only MEN2A

D. Never in MEN

E. Always carcinoma

18. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 3yr post-renal transplant: Ca 2.7, PTH 400, PO4 1.0. Persistent hypercalcaemia. Management?

A. Parathyroidectomy (subtotal + autotransplant)

B. Cinacalcet

C. Increase prednisolone

D. Observation

E. Phosphate binders

  1. Post-transplant: Ca normal, PTH 250. Management?

A. Monitor (may resolve); surgery only if hypercalcaemic

B. Immediate surgery

C. Cinacalcet

D. Calcitriol

E. Parathyroidectomy

  1. Tertiary HPT post-surgery: Ca 1.8 day 1. Management?

A. Aggressive Ca/Vit D (hungry bone risk high in renal osteodystrophy)

B. Observation

C. Calcitonin

D. Cinacalcet

E. Stop Ca

  1. Tertiary HPT recurrence 2yrs post-subtotal parathyroidectomy. Next?

A. Re-exploration or cinacalcet

B. Observation

C. Calcitriol

D. Phosphate binders

E. Radiotherapy

  1. Tertiary HPT in MEN1: 4-gland hyperplasia. Surgery?

A. Subtotal (3.5 glands) + autotransplant; monitor for recurrence

B. Total parathyroidectomy only

C. Single gland removal

D. Cinacalcet only

E. Observation

19. Flashcards

  • Q: Tertiary HPT

A: Autonomous PTH after long-standing secondary HPT (post-renal transplant)

  • Q: Pathophysiology

A: Secondary HPT -> parathyroid hyperplasia -> autonomy -> hypercalcaemia

  • Q: Biochemistry

A: Hypercalcaemia + high PTH (not suppressible)

  • Q: Treatment

A: Parathyroidectomy (subtotal 3.5g + autotransplant)

  • Q: vs Primary HPT

A: Tertiary: post-CKD/transplant history

  • Q: Hungry bone risk

A: HIGH post-op (severe osteodystrophy); aggressive Ca/Vit D

  • Q: Autotransplant

A: Forearm (brachioradialis); easy re-access

  • Q: Recurrence

A: Common after subtotal; monitor Ca/PTH lifelong

  • Q: MEN1 tertiary

A: 4-gland hyperplasia; subtotal + autotransplant

  • Q: Indications

A: Hypercalcaemia, symptoms, bone disease, stones

20. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Autonomous PTH hypersecretion after long-standing secondary HPT (usually post-renal transplant) — Tertiary hyperparathyroidism =
  1. Renal transplant (persistent secondary HPT -> autonomy) — Tertiary HPT occurs after:
  1. Hypercalcaemia + high PTH (autonomous, not suppressible) — Tertiary HPT biochemistry:
  1. Parathyroidectomy (subtotal 3.5 glands or total + autotransplant) — Tertiary HPT treatment:
  1. Tertiary: post-CKD/transplant history; Primary: no CKD history — Tertiary vs primary HPT:
  1. Common if subtotal; monitor Ca/PTH lifelong — Tertiary HPT recurrence:
  1. Forearm (brachioradialis); easy access for re-operation — Autotransplant in tertiary HPT:
  1. Hypercalcaemia, symptomatic, bone disease, kidney stones — Tertiary HPT indications for surgery:
  1. High hungry bone syndrome risk (severe osteodystrophy) -> prolonged Ca/Vit D — Post-parathyroidectomy in tertiary:
  1. MEN1: hyperplasia; MEN2A: hyperplasia; both can progress to tertiary — Tertiary HPT in MEN:

SBAs

  1. Parathyroidectomy (subtotal + autotransplant) — 3yr post-renal transplant: Ca 2.7, PTH 400, PO4 1.0. Persistent hypercalcaemia. Management?
  1. Monitor (may resolve); surgery only if hypercalcaemic — Post-transplant: Ca normal, PTH 250. Management?
  1. Aggressive Ca/Vit D (hungry bone risk high in renal osteodystrophy) — Tertiary HPT post-surgery: Ca 1.8 day 1. Management?
  1. Re-exploration or cinacalcet — Tertiary HPT recurrence 2yrs post-subtotal parathyroidectomy. Next?
  1. Subtotal (3.5 glands) + autotransplant; monitor for recurrence — Tertiary HPT in MEN1: 4-gland hyperplasia. Surgery?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.5.10 Vitamin D Metabolism And Deficiency
[!info]
Vitamin D = Secosteroid Hormone Essential for Calcium/Phosphate Homeostasis & Bone Health. Deficiency = Global Pandemic (1 Billion+ Worldwide). 25-OH Vitamin D = Storage Form (Half-Life 2-3 Weeks); 1,25-(OH)₂D = Active Hormone (Half-Life 4-6 Hours). Deficiency = Rickets (Children) / Osteomalacia (Adults) + Secondary Hyperparathyroidism + Osteoporosis.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Describe vitamin D synthesis, metabolism, and mechanism of action
  • [ ] Define deficiency, insufficiency, and sufficiency thresholds
  • [ ] Identify risk factors and clinical manifestations (rickets, osteomalacia, myopathy)
  • [ ] Apply replacement strategies (cholecalciferol vs active analogues)
  • [ ] Manage vitamin D in special populations (CKD, pregnancy, obesity, drugs)

2. Vitamin D Metabolism

Synthesis Pathway

Sunlight (UVB 290-315nm) → 7-Dehydrocholesterol (Skin) → **Pre-Vitamin D3** → **Vitamin D3 (Cholecalciferol)**
         │
         ▼
Liver (25-Hydroxylase / CYP2R1) → **25-OH Vitamin D (Calcidiol)** — **Storage Form** (t½ 2-3 Weeks)
         │
         ▼
Kidney (1α-Hydroxylase / CYP27B1) → **1,25-(OH)₂D (Calcitriol)** — **Active Hormone** (t½ 4-6h)
         │
         ▼
Target Tissues (VDR) → Genomic (Genomic Transcription) + Non-Genomic (Rapid) Effects

Alternative Sources

SourceFormNotes
Sunlight (UVB)Vitamin D3 (Cholecalciferol)Major Source (80-90%)
Diet (Fatty Fish, Egg Yolk, Fortified Foods)D3 (Cholecalciferol)Minor Contribution
Diet (Mushrooms, Fortified Foods)Vitamin D2 (Ergocalciferol)Plant Source; Less Potent
SupplementsD3 (Cholecalciferol) / D2 (Ergocalciferol)D3 Preferred (More Potent)

3. Metabolic Activation & Catabolism

Activation Steps

StepEnzymeGeneLocationRegulation
1. 25-Hydroxylation25-Hydroxylase (CYP2R1, CYP27A1)CYP2R1, CYP27A1LiverSubstrate-Dependent (First-Order Kinetics)
2. 1α-Hydroxylation1α-Hydroxylase (CYP27B1)CYP27B1Kidney (Proximal Tubule)↑ PTH, ↓ Phosphate, ↓ Calcium, FGF23 ↓, PTH ↑
24-Hydroxylation (Catabolism)24-Hydroxylase (CYP24A1)CYP24A1Kidney, Target Tissues↑ 1,25-(OH)₂D, FGF23

Regulation of 1α-Hydroxylase (CYP27B1)

StimulatorInhibitor
PTH ↑ (Strongest)FGF23 ↑ (Strongest)
HypocalcaemiaHypercalcaemia
HypophosphataemiaHyperphosphataemia
Growth Hormone / Prolactin1,25-(OH)₂D (Feedback)
Low 1,25-(OH)₂DHigh 1,25-(OH)₂D

4. Vitamin D Forms & Activity

FormNameHalf-LifePotencySource
Vitamin D3 (Cholecalciferol)Pre-Vitamin D3 → D32-3 Weeks (25-OH D)ProhormoneSunlight, Diet, Supplements
Vitamin D2 (Ergocalciferol)D2 (Plant/Fungal)2-3 Weeks (25-OH D2)Less Potent (1/3 of D3)Plants, Fortified Foods
25-OH Vitamin D (Calcidiol)Storage Form2-3 WeeksStorage PoolMeasured as "Vitamin D Level"
1,25-(OH)₂D (Calcitriol)Active Hormone4-6 HoursMost PotentRenal 1α-Hydroxylation
Alfacalcidol (1α-OH D3)Synthetic Analogue12-24 HoursRapid ActiveBypasses Renal 1α-Hydroxylase
Calcitriol (1,25-(OH)₂D₃)Active Hormone4-6 HoursMost PotentRenal Failure / Hypoparathyroidism

5. Vitamin D Status — Definitions (Endocrine Society / IOF / NOF)

Status25-OH Vitamin D (ng/mL)25-OH Vitamin D (nmol/L)
Deficiency<20 ng/mL<50 nmol/L
Insufficiency20-29 ng/mL50-74 nmol/L
Sufficiency≥30 ng/mL≥75 nmol/L
Optimal (Bone Health)40-60 ng/mL100-150 nmol/L
Toxicity>150 ng/mL>375 nmol/L
Controversy: IOM (Institute of Medicine) uses ≥20 ng/mL (50 nmol/L) as Sufficient for Bone Health; Endocrine Society Recommends ≥30 ng/mL (75 nmol/L).

6. Risk Factors for Deficiency

CategoryRisk Factors
Limited Sun ExposureIndoor Lifestyle, High Latitude (>35°), Winter, Sunscreen (SPF>15), Covering Clothing, Dark Skin (Melanin ↓ UVB Penetration)
DietaryLow Intake (Vegan, Lactose Intolerance), No Fortified Foods
MalabsorptionCoeliac, IBD, Cystic Fibrosis, Bariatric Surgery, Short Bowel, Cholestatic Liver Disease
Renal DiseaseCKD (↓ 1α-Hydroxylase), Nephrotic Syndrome (Urinary Loss of DBP)
Hepatic Disease↓ 25-Hydroxylation
ObesitySequestration in Adipose Tissue (Volumetric Dilution); BMI >30 → 2x Risk
Ageing↓ Skin 7-Dehydrocholesterol; ↓ Renal 1α-Hydroxylase
DrugsEnzyme Inducers (Phenytoin, Carbamazepine, Rifampicin, Rifabutin, St John's Wort); Glucocorticoids, Antiretrovirals (EFV, NVP)
GeneticCYP2R1, CYP27B1, CYP24A1, DBP (GC), VDR Polymorphisms

7. Clinical Manifestations

Children — Rickets

FeatureDetails
SkeletalCraniotabes (Soft Skull), Harrison's Groove (Costochondral Junction), Rachitic Rosary, Wrist/Ankle Widening, Bow Legs (Genu Varum), Knock Knees (Genu Valgum)
GrowthFailure to Thrive, Delayed Walking, Stunted Growth
MuscularHypotonia, Proximal Myopathy, Delayed Motor Milestones
DentalDelayed Eruption, Enamel Hypoplasia, Caries
BiochemicalLow Ca²⁺, Low PO₄, High ALP, Low 25-OH D, High PTH, High ALP

Adults — Osteomalacia

FeatureDetails
Bone PainProximal Myopathy (Thighs, Shoulders), Diffuse Bone Pain (Ribs, Spine, Pelvis)
FracturesLooser's Zones (Pseudofractures), Pathological Fractures
MuscularProximal Myopathy (Waddling Gait, Difficulty Rising), Muscle Weakness
BiochemicalLow Ca²⁺, Low PO₄, High ALP, Low 25-OH D, High PTH, High ALP

Subclinical / Insufficiency

FeatureAssociation
OsteoporosisAccelerated Bone Loss, Fragility Fractures
Muscle WeaknessProximal Myopathy, Falls Risk (Elderly)
Immune↑ Infection Risk (TB, Respiratory), Autoimmune (MS, T1DM, IBD)
CardiovascularHypertension, Heart Failure, Cardiovascular Mortality
CancerColorectal, Breast, Prostate (Epidemiological)
AutoimmuneMS, T1DM, IBD, RA

8. Diagnosis & Thresholds

25-OH Vitamin D Assay

MethodNotes
Immunoassay (CLIA, ECLIA)Common; Variable Accuracy (Cross-Reactivity with 24,25-(OH)₂D)
LC-MS/MSGold Standard (Separates D2/D3, 25-OH D2/D3, 24,25-(OH)₂D)
StandardisationVDSP (Vitamin D Standardisation Program) — CDC/DEQAS

Interpretation (Endocrine Society / IOF)

25-OH D LevelStatusAction
<12 ng/mL (<30 nmol/L)Severe DeficiencyUrgent Treatment (High-Dose Loading)
12-19 ng/mL (30-49 nmol/L)DeficiencyTreatment (Loading + Maintenance)
20-29 ng/mL (50-74 nmol/L)InsufficiencyTreatment (Maintenance Dose)
30-100 ng/mL (75-250 nmol/L)SufficientMaintenance (If Risk Factors)
>100 ng/mL (>250 nmol/L)Potential ToxicityAssess for Granulomatous Disease / Excess Supplementation

9. Replacement Strategies

Vitamin D3 (Cholecalciferol) — Preferred

ScenarioRegimen
Severe Deficiency (<12 ng/mL)Loading: 50,000 IU Weekly × 6-8 Weeks → Maintenance 1,000-2,000 IU/day
Deficiency (12-19 ng/mL)Loading: 50,000 IU Weekly × 4-6 Weeks → Maintenance 1,000-2,000 IU/day
Insufficiency (20-29 ng/mL)Maintenance 1,000-2,000 IU/day (No Loading Needed)
Maintenance (Sufficient)800-2,000 IU/day (Age/Weight Dependent)
Obesity (BMI >30)2-3x Higher Dose (Sequestration)
MalabsorptionHigher Doses / IM Route / Active Analogues

Vitamin D2 (Ergocalciferol) vs D3

FeatureD3 (Cholecalciferol)D2 (Ergocalciferol)
Potency100% (Reference)~33% (1/3 of D3)
Half-Life (25-OH)Longer (2-3 Weeks)Shorter
SourceAnimal (Lanolin)Fungal/Yeast
PreferredYESOnly if D3 Unavailable

10. Special Populations

Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)

StageVitamin D Strategy
CKD G1-3Nutritional D3/D2 (Cholecalciferol/Ergocalciferol) → Monitor 25-OH D
CKD G4-5 / DialysisActive Analogues Required (Renal 1α-Hydroxylase Lost) → Alfacalcidol / Calcitriol / Paricalcitol
Dialysis (HD/PD)Paricalcitol / Alfacalcidol / Calcitriol Post-HD; Monitor Ca, Phos, PTH

Pregnancy & Lactation

TrimesterRequirementSupplementation
All Trimesters≥600 IU/day (15 µg)400-600 IU/day (Routine); Higher if Deficient
High Risk (Dark Skin, Obesity, Veiled)1000-2000 IU/day
Lactation600-800 IU/dayMaternal Supplementation → Breast Milk

Obesity (BMI >30)

FactorAdjustment
SequestrationVitamin D Sequestered in Adipose Tissue
Dose2-3x Standard Dose (Loading + Maintenance)
Surgery (Bariatric)High Risk — Monitor 25-OH D q3mo Post-Op

Drug Interactions (Enzyme Inducers)

DrugEffectManagement
Phenytoin, Carbamazepine, Phenobarbital, Primidone↑ CYP24A1 (Catabolism)2-3x Higher Dose
Rifampicin, Rifabutin
St John's Wort
Glucocorticoids↑ CatabolismMonitor 25-OH D
Antiretrovirals (EFV, NVP)↑ CYP24A1Monitor

11. Toxicity (Hypervitaminosis D)

ThresholdFeatures
25-OH D >150 ng/mL (>375 nmol/L)Hypervitaminosis D
Mechanism↑ 1,25-(OH)₂D → Hypercalcaemia, Hypercalciuria, Nephrocalcinosis, Renal Failure
SymptomsNausea, Vomiting, Polyuria, Polydipsia, Weakness, Confusion, AKI
TreatmentStop Vitamin D; Hydration; Loop Diuretics; Bisphosphonates (Severe)

12. Replacement Regimens

Deficiency Treatment (Adults)

SeverityLoading DoseMaintenance
Severe (<12 ng/mL)50,000 IU Weekly × 6-8 Weeks1,000-2,000 IU/day
Moderate (12-19 ng/mL)50,000 IU Weekly × 4-6 Weeks1,000-2,000 IU/day
Insufficiency (20-29 ng/mL)No Loading1,000-2,000 IU/day
Maintenance (Sufficient)—800-2,000 IU/day

Special Regimens

PopulationLoadingMaintenance
Obesity (BMI >30)2-3x Standard Loading2-3x Maintenance
MalabsorptionIM Vitamin D3 (300,000 IU q3-6mo) / High-Dose Oral2-3x Maintenance
CKD G4-5 / DialysisActive Analogues Only (Alfacalcidol, Calcitriol, Paricalcitol)Not Nutritional D2/D3

Paediatric Dosing (AAP/Endocrine Society)

AgeDeficiency TreatmentMaintenance
0-1 Year2,000 IU/day × 6 Weeks → 400-1,000 IU/day400-1,000 IU/day
1-18 Years50,000 IU Weekly × 6 Weeks → 600-1,000 IU/day600-1,000 IU/day

13. Monitoring & Toxicity

Monitoring

ParameterFrequencyTarget
25-OH Vitamin D3-4 Months Post-Loading → Then q6-12mo30-60 ng/mL (75-150 nmol/L)
Serum Calciumq3-6mo (If High Dose)<2.6 mmol/L (10.5 mg/dL)
24h Urine CalciumIf High Dose / Hypercalcaemia Risk<7.5 mmol/day (300 mg/day)

Toxicity Thresholds

25-OH D LevelClinical Significance
>100 ng/mL (250 nmol/L)Potential Toxicity — Monitor Calcium
>150 ng/mL (375 nmol/L)Hypervitaminosis D — Hypercalcaemia Risk
>200 ng/mL (500 nmol/L)Severe Toxicity — Stop Supplement; Hydration, Loop Diuretic, Bisphosphonate

14. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Vitamin D Deficiency Definition25-OH D <20 ng/mL (<50 nmol/L)
Insufficiency20-29 ng/mL (50-74 nmol/L)
Sufficiency≥30 ng/mL (≥75 nmol/L)
Preferred FormVitamin D3 (Cholecalciferol) — More Potent, Longer Half-Life
Loading Dose (Severe Def)50,000 IU Weekly × 6-8 Weeks
Maintenance Dose1,000-2,000 IU/day (Adults)
Obesity2-3x Higher Dose (Sequestration in Fat)
CKD G4-5Active Analogues Only (Alfacalcidol, Calcitriol, Paricalcitol)
Enzyme InducersPhenytoin, Carbamazepine, Rifampicin → 2-3x Dose
Toxicity25-OH D >150 ng/mL → Hypercalcaemia, Nephrocalcinosis
25-OH D AssayLC-MS/MS Gold Standard; Immunoassay Variable
Vitamin D2 vs D3D3 Preferred — More Potent, Longer Half-Life
Optimal Level40-60 ng/mL (100-150 nmol/L) for Bone Health
Pregnancy600-800 IU/day; Deficiency → 1,000-2,000 IU
CKD G4-5Active Analogues Only (Alfacalcidol/Calcitriol/Paricalcitol) — Not Nutritional D
Loading Dose50,000 IU Weekly × 6-8 Weeks (Severe Deficiency)
Active Analogues in CKDAlfacalcidol, Calcitriol, Paricalcitol (Bypass Renal 1α-Hydroxylase)

15. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
D2 vs D3D3 = Cholecalciferol (Animal, Potent); D2 = Ergocalciferol (Plant, 1/3 Potency)
25-OH D vs 1,25-(OH)₂D25-OH = Storage (Measure This); 1,25-(OH)₂D = Active Hormone (Short t½)
CKD Vitamin DCKD G1-3: Nutritional D3 OK; CKD G4-5: Only Active Analogues (Alfacalcidol/Calcitriol/Paricalcitol)
D2 vs D3 PotencyD3 ≈ 3x D3 Potency; D2 Shorter Half-Life
Loading vs MaintenanceLoading = 50,000 IU/wk ×6-8wk; Maintenance = 1,000-2,000 IU/day
Obesity Dosing2-3x Standard Dose (Sequestration in Fat)
Toxicity Threshold25-OH D >150 ng/mL = Hypervitaminosis D → Hypercalcaemia

Mnemonic: VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY

  • Vitamin D Deficiency = Rickets (Kids) / Osteomalacia (Adults)
  • Immune Dysfunction (Infections, Autoimmune)
  • Thyroid / Autoimmune (MS, T1DM, IBD)
  • Advanced Genetic (VDR, CYP24A1, CYP2R1)
  • Muscle Weakness (Proximal Myopathy)
  • Insulin Resistance / Diabetes
  • Nephrolithiasis (Paradoxical — High Doses)
  • Deficiency Epidemic (1 Billion+ Worldwide)

16. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Vitamin D Deficiency25-OH D <20 ng/mL (<50 nmol/L)
Insufficiency20-29 ng/mL (50-74 nmol/L)
Sufficiency≥30 ng/mL (≥75 nmol/L)
Preferred FormVitamin D3 (Cholecalciferol) — More Potent, Longer t½
Loading Dose (Severe)50,000 IU Weekly × 6-8 Weeks
Maintenance1,000-2,000 IU/day (Adults)
Obesity2-3x Standard Dose (Sequestration)
CKD G4-5Active Analogues Only (Alfacalcidol, Calcitriol, Paricalcitol)
Enzyme InducersPhenytoin, Carbamazepine, Rifampicin → 2-3x Dose
Toxicity25-OH D >150 ng/mL → Hypercalcaemia, Nephrocalcinosis
25-OH D AssayLC-MS/MS Gold Standard
Vitamin D2 vs D3D3 Preferred — More Potent, Longer t½
Optimal Level40-60 ng/mL (100-150 nmol/L) for Bone Health
Pregnancy600-800 IU/day; Deficiency → 1,000-2,000 IU
CKD G4-5Active Analogues Only (Alfacalcidol, Calcitriol, Paricalcitol)
Loading Dose50,000 IU Weekly × 6-8 Weeks
Active Analogues in CKDAlfacalcidol, Calcitriol, Paricalcitol (Bypass Renal 1α-Hydroxylase)

17. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Parathyroid & Calcium|Parathyroid & Calcium]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]

18. MCQs (10)

  1. Vitamin D deficiency definition:

A. 25-OH D <30 nmol/L (12 ng/mL); insufficiency 30-50 nmol/L

B. <50

C. <75

D. <100

E. <20

  1. Vitamin D sources:

A. Sunlight (UVB) -> skin 7-dehydrocholesterol -> Vit D3; diet (oily fish, fortified) minor

B. Diet only

C. Sunlight only

D. Supplements only

E. Endogenous synthesis only

  1. Vit D metabolism:

A. Skin (D3) -> liver 25-hydroxylase -> 25-OH D -> kidney 1a-hydroxylase -> 1,25(OH)2D (active)

B. Kidney only

C. Liver only

D. Intestine only

E. Adrenal only

  1. Vit D deficiency causes:

A. Rickets (children), osteomalacia (adults), secondary HPT, muscle weakness, falls

B. Osteoporosis only

C. Hypercalcaemia

D. Renal stones

E. No symptoms

  1. Vit D deficiency treatment:

A. Colecalciferol (D3) 20,000-40,000 IU weekly x8-12wk -> maintenance 800-2000 IU/day

B. Ergocalciferol single dose

C. Calcitriol first line

D. IV Vit D

E. Sunlight only

  1. Vit D toxicity:

A. Hypercalcaemia, hypercalciuria, renal stones, nephrocalcinosis; 25-OH D >250 nmol/L

B. Hypocalcaemia

C. Hyponatraemia

D. Hypertension

E. Weight loss

  1. Vit D in CKD:

A. Use active Vit D (calcitriol/paricalcitol) - kidney cannot 1a-hydroxylate

B. Colecalciferol sufficient

C. Ergocalciferol preferred

D. No Vit D needed

E. Toxic in CKD

  1. Vit D in obesity:

A. Sequestration in adipose tissue -> higher doses needed (2-3x)

B. Lower doses

C. Normal doses

D. Contraindicated

E. No adjustment

  1. Vit D and falls:

A. Deficiency -> proximal myopathy -> falls; supplementation d falls in elderly

B. No effect on falls

C. Increases falls

D. Only in osteoporosis

E. Only in children

  1. Rickets vs osteomalacia:

A. Rickets: children (growth plate); osteomalacia: adults (mineralisation defect)

B. Same

C. Rickets: adults

D. Osteomalacia: children

E. No difference

19. SBA Questions (10)

  1. Elderly woman: falls, proximal weakness, 25-OH D 18 nmol/L. Treatment?

A. Colecalciferol 20,000 IU weekly x8wk -> maintenance 1000 IU/day

B. Calcitriol

C. Ergocalciferol 50,000 IU monthly

D. IV Vit D

E. Sunlight only

  1. Same patient: after loading, maintenance dose?

A. 800-2000 IU/day (1000-2000 IU/day); monitor 25-OH D at 3-6mo

B. 400 IU/day

C. 50,000 IU monthly

D. No maintenance

E. Calcitriol

  1. CKD stage 4: 25-OH D 25, PTH 400. Vit D therapy?

A. Calcitriol/paricalcitol (active Vit D); kidney cannot 1a-hydroxylate

B. Colecalciferol high dose

C. Ergocalciferol

D. No Vit D

E. IV Vit D

  1. Vit D toxicity: 25-OH D 300, Ca 3.0. Management?

A. Stop Vit D; IV hydration; furosemide; avoid Ca; monitor Ca

B. Continue Vit D

C. Increase Ca

D. Cinacalcet

E. Calcitriol

  1. Obesity (BMI 40): 25-OH D 20. Loading dose?

A. 2-3x standard dose (60,000-120,000 IU weekly) due to sequestration

B. Standard dose

C. Half dose

D. No loading

E. Calcitriol

20. Flashcards

  • Q: Vit D deficiency

A: 25-OH D <30 nmol/L; insufficiency 30-50 nmol/L

  • Q: Vit D synthesis

A: Skin UVB -> D3 -> liver 25-OH D -> kidney 1a-OH -> 1,25(OH)2D (active)

  • Q: Deficiency causes

A: Rickets (children), osteomalacia (adults), secondary HPT, myopathy, falls

  • Q: Treatment

A: Colecalciferol (D3) 20-40k IU weekly x8-12wk -> maintenance 800-2000 IU/day

  • Q: Toxicity

A: Hypercalcaemia, hypercalciuria, stones; 25-OH D >250 nmol/L

  • Q: CKD Vit D

A: Use ACTIVE Vit D (calcitriol/paricalcitol); kidney cannot 1a-hydroxylate

  • Q: Obesity

A: Sequestration in fat -> 2-3x higher doses

  • Q: Falls

A: Deficiency -> proximal myopathy -> falls; D supplementation d falls in elderly

  • Q: Rickets vs osteomalacia

A: Rickets: children (growth plate); osteomalacia: adults (mineralisation defect)

  • Q: Vit D forms

A: D3 (colecalciferol) preferred over D2 (ergocalciferol); D3 more potent

21. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. 25-OH D <30 nmol/L (12 ng/mL); insufficiency 30-50 nmol/L — Vitamin D deficiency definition:
  1. Sunlight (UVB) -> skin 7-dehydrocholesterol -> Vit D3; diet (oily fish, fortified) minor — Vitamin D sources:
  1. Skin (D3) -> liver 25-hydroxylase -> 25-OH D -> kidney 1a-hydroxylase -> 1,25(OH)2D (active) — Vit D metabolism:
  1. Rickets (children), osteomalacia (adults), secondary HPT, muscle weakness, falls — Vit D deficiency causes:
  1. Colecalciferol (D3) 20,000-40,000 IU weekly x8-12wk -> maintenance 800-2000 IU/day — Vit D deficiency treatment:
  1. Hypercalcaemia, hypercalciuria, renal stones, nephrocalcinosis; 25-OH D >250 nmol/L — Vit D toxicity:
  1. Use active Vit D (calcitriol/paricalcitol) - kidney cannot 1a-hydroxylate — Vit D in CKD:
  1. Sequestration in adipose tissue -> higher doses needed (2-3x) — Vit D in obesity:
  1. Deficiency -> proximal myopathy -> falls; supplementation d falls in elderly — Vit D and falls:
  1. Rickets: children (growth plate); osteomalacia: adults (mineralisation defect) — Rickets vs osteomalacia:

SBAs

  1. Colecalciferol 20,000 IU weekly x8wk -> maintenance 1000 IU/day — Elderly woman: falls, proximal weakness, 25-OH D 18 nmol/L. Treatment?
  1. 800-2000 IU/day (1000-2000 IU/day); monitor 25-OH D at 3-6mo — Same patient: after loading, maintenance dose?
  1. Calcitriol/paricalcitol (active Vit D); kidney cannot 1a-hydroxylate — CKD stage 4: 25-OH D 25, PTH 400. Vit D therapy?
  1. Stop Vit D; IV hydration; furosemide; avoid Ca; monitor Ca — Vit D toxicity: 25-OH D 300, Ca 3.0. Management?
  1. 2-3x standard dose (60,000-120,000 IU weekly) due to sequestration — Obesity (BMI 40): 25-OH D 20. Loading dose?

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.6 20.6 6
▸ 20.6.01 Acromegaly
[!info]
Acromegaly = Chronic GH Excess (Usually Pituitary Macroadenoma). Insidious Onset → Coarse Features, Acral Enlargement, Organomegaly. IGF-1 = Screening Test; OGTT GH Suppression = Gold Standard Diagnosis. Transsphenoidal Surgery = First-Line Treatment.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise clinical features (acral enlargement, coarse features, organomegaly)
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic criteria (IGF-1, OGTT GH suppression)
  • [ ] Interpret pituitary MRI and plan surgical approach
  • [ ] Outline medical management (somatostatin analogues, pegvisomant, dopamine agonists)
  • [ ] Manage complications (cardiomyopathy, sleep apnoea, glucose intolerance, colonic polyps)

2. Epidemiology & Aetiology

FeatureDetails
Incidence3-4 per million/year
Prevalence40-70 per million
Peak Age40-50 Years
SexEqual (M:F = 1:1)
AetiologyPituitary GH-Secreting Adenoma (>95%) — Macroadenoma >10mm (80-90%)
Rare CausesEctopic GHRH (Pancreatic NET, Bronchial Carcinoid), Ectopic GH (Rare)
GeneticsAIP (Young-Onset, Familial), GPR101, GNAS (McCune-Albright), MEN1

3. Clinical Features

Acral & Facial Changes

FeatureDescription
Acral EnlargementHands/Feet Enlargement → Ring/Shoe Size Increase, Spade-Like Hands
FacialCoarse Features, Prognathism, Macroglossia, Wide Spacing Teeth, Prominent Supraorbital Ridges
SkinThickened, Oily, Hyperhidrosis, Skin Tags, Acanthosis Nigricans
Soft TissueSoft Tissue Swelling, Carpal Tunnel Syndrome, Arthralgias

Systemic Manifestations

SystemComplications
CardiovascularCardiomyopathy (LVH, Diastolic Dysfunction), Hypertension, Sleep Apnoea (Obstructive), Arrhythmias
RespiratoryObstructive Sleep Apnoea (50-80%), Upper Airway Obstruction
MetabolicImpaired Glucose Tolerance / Diabetes (30-50%), Insulin Resistance, Dyslipidaemia
GastrointestinalColonic Polyps (↑ Risk 2-3x, Adenomatous), Diverticulosis
MusculoskeletalArthralgias, Joint Swelling, Osteoarthritis, Carpal Tunnel Syndrome
ReproductiveHypogonadism (↓ LH/FSH), Menstrual Irregularities, Infertility, Galactorrhoea (If Co-Secreting PRL)

4. Diagnosis — Biochemical & Imaging

Screening & Confirmation

TestNormalAcromegaly
IGF-1 (Age-Adjusted)Normal↑↑ (2-3x ULN) — Best Screening Test
OGTT (75g Glucose)GH Suppressed <1 µg/L at 60-120minGH NOT Suppressed ≥1 µg/L (Gold Standard)
Random GH<0.4 µg/L (Nadir)>1 µg/L (If IGF-1 Elevated)
MRI PituitaryNormalMacroadenoma >10mm (80-90%)

Diagnostic Criteria

CriterionThreshold
IGF-1Age-Adjusted >ULN (Usually 2-3x ULN)
OGTT GHNot Suppressed <1 µg/L at 60-120min (Gold Standard)
Random GH>1 µg/L (If IGF-1 Elevated)
MRI PituitaryMacroadenoma >10mm (80-90%)

Differential Diagnosis of Elevated IGF-1

ConditionIGF-1OGTT GH SuppressionOther Features
Acromegaly↑↑Not Suppressed (≥1 µg/L)Pituitary Macroadenoma
Pubertal Gigantism↑↑Not SuppressedOpen Epiphyses, Age <18
Pregnancy↑ (Placental GH)N/APhysiological
Hepatic Failure↓ (Impaired Synthesis)N/ALiver Disease
Renal Failure↑ (↓ Clearance)Variable↑ Creatinine
Malnutrition/Anorexia↓↓Paradoxical ↑ GHLow BMI, Starvation

5. Pituitary MRI — Imaging Protocol

ParameterSpecification
Slice Thickness3mm (No Gap)
SequencesT1 Pre/Post-Gadolinium (Coronal, Sagittal), T2
Dynamic ContrastT1 Post-Gad at 30s, 60s, 90s (Microadenoma Delayed Enhancement)
Field of ViewSella + Suprasellar Region + Optic Chiasm

Adenoma Classification

TypeSizeFrequency
Microadenoma<10mm10-20%
Macroadenoma≥10mm80-90%
Giant>40mmRare

6. Complications — High Yield for Exams

SystemComplicationScreening
CardiovascularCardiomyopathy (LVH, Diastolic Dysfunction), Hypertension (30-50%), Sleep Apnoea (50-80%)Echo, Sleep Study, BP Monitoring
RespiratoryObstructive Sleep Apnoea (50-80%)Sleep Study (Polysomnography)
MetabolicImpaired Glucose Tolerance / Diabetes (30-50%), DyslipidaemiaHbA1c, Fasting Glucose, Lipids
GIColonic Polyps (↑ 2-3x Risk), DiverticulosisColonoscopy at Diagnosis, q3-5yr
JointsArthralgias, Osteoarthritis, Carpal Tunnel SyndromeClinical Exam
ReproductiveHypogonadism (↓ LH/FSH), Menstrual Irregularities, InfertilityLH, FSH, Testosterone/Oestradiol, Prolactin
Neoplastic RiskThyroid Nodules (↑), Colonic Polyps (↑ 2-3x)Thyroid US, Colonoscopy

7. Management Algorithm

Acromegaly Diagnosed (↑ IGF-1 + GH Not Suppressed on OGTT)
         │
         ▼
MRI PITUITARY (3mm Slices) → Macroadenoma >10mm (90%)
         │
         ▼
**TRANSSEPHENOIDAL SURGERY (TSS) — 1st LINE**
         │
         ├── CURE (Post-Op IGF-1 Normal + OGTT GH <1 µg/L)
         │       → SURVEILLANCE (Annual IGF-1 + OGTT)
         │
         └── RESIDUAL DISEASE (IGF-1 Elevated / GH Not Suppressed)
                 │
                 ├── RE-OPERATION (If Accessible Residual)
                 └── MEDICAL THERAPY
                         ├── SOMATOSTATIN ANALOGUES (1st Line Medical)
                         │       Octreotide LAR 20-40mg IM q4wk
                         │       Lanreotide Autogel 60-120mg SC q4wk
                         │       Pasireotide LAR 40-60mg IM q4wk (2nd Line)
                         │
                         ├── PEGVISOMANT (GH Receptor Antagonist)
                         │       10-30mg SC Daily (If SST Refractory)
                         │
                         ├── DOPAMINE AGONISTS (Adjunct)
                         │       Cabergoline 0.5-3mg/week
                         │
                         └── RADIOTHERAPY (Stereotactic / Fractionated)
                                 → If Surgery + Medical Fail

8. Medical Therapy — Agents & Dosing

Somatostatin Analogues (1st Line Medical)

AgentDoseRouteFrequencyKey Features
Octreotide LAR20-40mgIMq4 WeeksStandard 1st Line
Lanreotide Autogel60-120mgSCq4 WeeksAlternative; Self-Administration
Pasireotide LAR40-60mgIMq4 Weeks2nd Line; ↑ Hyperglycaemia Risk

Pegvisomant (GH Receptor Antagonist) — 2nd/3rd Line

FeatureDetails
MechanismGH Receptor Antagonist — Blocks GH Action (↓ IGF-1)
Dose10-30mg SC Daily (Titrate to IGF-1 Normalisation)
IndicationSST Refractory / Intolerant / Post-RT
AdvantageNormalises IGF-1 Without ↓ GH (GH ↑↑ on Pegvisomant)
MonitoringIGF-1 Normalisation (GH Levels Rise — Do Not Use for Monitoring)

Dopamine Agonists (Adjunct)

AgentDoseRole
Cabergoline0.5-3mg/weekAdjunct; Mild GH Suppression; Low Cost

9. Treatment Goals & Cure Criteria

Biochemical Cure (Consensus)

ParameterTarget
IGF-1Normal (Age-Adjusted)
OGTT GH<1 µg/L at 60-120min
Random GH<0.4 µg/L (Nadir)

Surgical Cure Rates

Tumour TypeCure Rate (Biochemical Remission)
Microadenoma (<10mm)80-90%
Macroadenoma (>10mm)50-60%
Giant (>40mm)<30%

10. Comorbidities & Surveillance

Surveillance Protocol

ParameterFrequency
IGF-13-6 Monthly (Active); Annually (Remission)
OGTT GH6Monthly (Active); Annually (Remission)
MRI Pituitary3mo, 1yr, then Annually ×5yr
Visual Fields6Monthly (If Chiasmal Compression)
Cardiac EchoBaseline, Then q1-2yr (Cardiomyopathy)
Sleep StudyBaseline + q1-2yr (Sleep Apnoea)
ColonoscopyAt Diagnosis → q3-5yr (↑ Colonic Polyps 2-3x)
Glucose/HbA1c6-Monthly (Diabetes Risk)
LipidsAnnual
Thyroid USq1-2yr (↑ Thyroid Nodules)

11. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Acromegaly ScreeningIGF-1 (Age-Adjusted) — Single Best Test
Acromegaly ConfirmationOGTT: GH Not Suppressed <1 µg/L (Gold Standard)
GH on OGTTNormal: <1 µg/L at 60-120min; Acromegaly: Not Suppressed
IGF-1 in Acromegaly2-3x ULN (Age-Adjusted)
MRI PituitaryMacroadenoma >10mm in 90%
1st Line TreatmentTranssphenoidal Surgery (TSS)
Surgical Cure RateMicro: 80-90%; Macro: 50-60%
Remission CriteriaIGF-1 Normal + OGTT GH <1 µg/L
Medical 1st LineSomatostatin Analogues (Octreotide LAR / Lanreotide)
PegvisomantGH Receptor Antagonist; Normalises IGF-1; GH ↑ on Treatment
ComplicationsCardiomyopathy, Sleep Apnoea, Diabetes, Colonic Polyps (↑2-3x)
Pasireotide2nd Line SST; ↑ Hyperglycaemia Risk
PegvisomantGH Receptor Antagonist; GH ↑ During Treatment; IGF-1 Normalises
Remission CriteriaIGF-1 Normal + OGTT GH <1 µg/L
Recurrence10-20% at 10 Years; Annual Surveillance

12. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
IGF-1 vs GH for DiagnosisIGF-1 = Screening (Stable); OGTT GH = Confirmation (Dynamic)
GH RandomUseless (Pulsatile); Use IGF-1 or OGTT
Gigantism vs AcromegalyGigantism = Pre-Epiphyseal Fusion; Acromegaly = Post-Fusion
Pegvisomant EffectGH ↑↑ on Treatment (Displaces GH from Receptor); IGF-1 Normalises
Pasireotide vs OctreotidePasireotide = SSTR1/2/3/5 (Broader); ↑ Hyperglycaemia Risk
Surgery Cure RateMicro 80-90%; Macro 50-60%
Remission DefinitionIGF-1 Normal + OGTT GH <1 µg/L
Sleep Apnoea50-80% in Acromegaly; Screen with Sleep Study
Colonic Polyps2-3x Increased Risk; Colonoscopy at Diagnosis
Pegvisomant MonitoringMonitor IGF-1 (NOT GH — GH Rises on Treatment)

13. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Acromegaly))
    Aetiology
      Pituitary GH Adenoma (95%)
      Macroadenoma >10mm (90%)
      Ectopic GHRH/GH Rare
    Clinical
      Acral Enlargement (Hands/Feet)
      Coarse Facial Features
      Organomegaly (Heart, Liver, Thyroid)
      Skin: Thick, Oily, Hyperhidrosis
      Cardiomyopathy, Sleep Apnoea, Diabetes
      Colonic Polyps (2-3x Risk)
    Diagnosis
      IGF-1 ↑↑ (Screen) → OGTT GH Not <1 µg/L (Confirm)
      MRI Macroadenoma >10mm (90%)
    Treatment
      1st Line: Transsphenoidal Surgery (TSS)
      Medical: SST Analogues (Octreotide/Lanreotide)
      2nd Line: Pegvisomant (GH Receptor Antagonist)
      Radiotherapy: If Surgery/Med Fail
    Surgery
      TSS (Endoscopic Preferred)
      Cure: Micro 80-90%, Macro 50-60%
      Remission: IGF-1 Normal + OGTT GH <1 µg/L
    Complications
      Cardiomyopathy, Sleep Apnoea, Diabetes
      Colonic Polyps (2-3x Risk)
      Hypogonadism, Arthralgia
    Surveillance
      IGF-1 + OGTT 6-12mo
      MRI Pituitary Annual 5yr
      Colonoscopy q3-5yr
      Echo + Sleep Study

14. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Acromegaly ScreeningIGF-1 (Age-Adjusted) — Best Single Test
Confirmatory TestOGTT: GH Not Suppressed <1 µg/L
IGF-1 in Acromegaly2-3x ULN (Age-Adjusted)
MRI FindingMacroadenoma >10mm (90%)
SurgeryTSS 1st Line; Micro 80-90% Cure; Macro 50-60%
RemissionIGF-1 Normal + OGTT GH <1 µg/L
Medical 1st LineSomatostatin Analogues (Octreotide LAR / Lanreotide)
PegvisomantGH Receptor Antagonist; GH ↑, IGF-1 Normalises
Pasireotide2nd Line SST; ↑ Hyperglycaemia Risk
ComplicationsCardiomyopathy, Sleep Apnoea, DM, Colonic Polyps (2-3x)
SurveillanceAnnual IGF-1 + OGTT; MRI 5yr; Colonoscopy q3-5yr
Pegvisomant MonitoringMonitor IGF-1 (NOT GH — GH Rises)
Remission CriteriaIGF-1 Normal + OGTT GH <1 µg/L
