16. MCQs (10)

  1. Acromegaly cause:

A. GH-secreting pituitary adenoma (>95%)

B. Adrenal adenoma

C. Ectopic GHRH

D. Hypothalamic tumour

E. GH-secreting pancreatic NET

  1. Acromegaly screening test:

A. IGF-1 (age-adjusted)

B. Random GH

C. OGTT with GH

D. Cranial MRI

E. Glucose

  1. OGTT in acromegaly:

A. GH fails to suppress <1 ug/L (or <0.4 ug/L modern)

B. GH suppresses normally

C. GH rises paradoxically

D. No GH change

E. GH increases 2x

  1. Acromegaly treatment 1st line:

A. Transsphenoidal surgery

B. Somatostatin analogues

C. Pegvisomant

D. Radiotherapy

E. Dopamine agonists

  1. Somatostatin analogues:

A. Octreotide LAR / lanreotide autogel; reduce GH/IGF-1; shrink tumour

B. IV only

C. Daily injection

D. Oral

E. Short-acting only

  1. Pegvisomant:

A. GH receptor antagonist; normalises IGF-1; no effect on tumour size

B. Somatostatin analogue

C. Dopamine agonist

D. Surgical

E. Radiation

  1. Acromegaly complications:

A. Cardiomyopathy, sleep apnoea, colonic polyps, diabetes, hypertension, arthropathy

B. Only diabetes

C. Only hypertension

D. Only arthropathy

E. No complications

  1. Acromegaly monitoring:

A. IGF-1, GH, glucose, echocardiogram, sleep study, colonoscopy

B. Only GH

C. Only IGF-1

D. Only glucose

E. Only MRI

  1. Acromegaly recurrence post-surgery:

A. IGF-1 rise; repeat MRI; medical therapy (SSA/pegvisomant) or radiotherapy

B. Observation

C. Repeat surgery only

D. Radiotherapy only

E. Pegvisomant only

  1. Acromegaly life expectancy:

A. Reduced if uncontrolled (CV disease); normal if controlled

B. Normal always

C. Reduced always

D. Depends on age

E. Unknown

17. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 45yo man: enlarged hands/feet, coarse features, IGF-1 3x ULN, MRI 12mm macroadenoma. Treatment?

A. Transsphenoidal surgery

B. Octreotide LAR

C. Pegvisomant

D. Radiotherapy

E. Cabergoline

  1. Same patient post-op: IGF-1 normalised. Follow-up?

A. IGF-1 q6mo; MRI at 3mo then annually

B. Observation only

C. Start octreotide

D. Radiotherapy

E. Pegvisomant

  1. Acromegaly with residual tumour post-surgery, IGF-1 elevated. Next?

A. Somatostatin analogue (octreotide LAR/lanreotide)

B. Repeat surgery

C. Pegvisomant

D. Radiotherapy

E. Cabergoline

  1. Acromegaly on octreotide LAR, IGF-1 still elevated. Add?

A. Pegvisomant (GH receptor antagonist)

B. Increase octreotide dose

C. Radiotherapy

D. Cabergoline

E. Repeat surgery

  1. Acromegaly with sleep apnoea, cardiomyopathy. Monitoring?

A. Echo, sleep study, colonoscopy, glucose, IGF-1 q6-12mo

B. Only IGF-1

C. Only glucose

D. Only colonoscopy

E. Only echo

18. Flashcards

  • Q: Acromegaly cause

A: GH-secreting pituitary adenoma (>95%)

  • Q: Screening

A: IGF-1 (age-adjusted); confirm with OGTT GH suppression <1 ug/L

  • Q: OGTT

A: Normal: GH suppresses <1 ug/L; acromegaly: no suppression

  • Q: Surgery

A: Transsphenoidal 1st line; remission if IGF-1 normal

  • Q: Medical Rx

A: SSA (octreotide/lanreotide) 2nd line; pegvisomant if SSA fails

  • Q: Pegvisomant

A: GH receptor antagonist; normalises IGF-1; no tumour shrinkage

  • Q: Complications

A: Cardiomyopathy, sleep apnoea, colonic polyps, diabetes, HTN, arthropathy

  • Q: Monitoring

A: IGF-1, GH, glucose, echo, sleep study, colonoscopy q6-12mo

  • Q: Recurrence

A: IGF-1 rise -> repeat MRI -> SSA/pegvisomant/radiotherapy

  • Q: Life expectancy

A: Reduced if uncontrolled; normal if controlled

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. GH-secreting pituitary adenoma (>95%) — Acromegaly cause:
  1. IGF-1 (age-adjusted) — Acromegaly screening test:
  1. GH fails to suppress <1 ug/L (or <0.4 ug/L modern) — OGTT in acromegaly:
  1. Transsphenoidal surgery — Acromegaly treatment 1st line:
  1. Octreotide LAR / lanreotide autogel; reduce GH/IGF-1; shrink tumour — Somatostatin analogues:
  1. GH receptor antagonist; normalises IGF-1; no effect on tumour size — Pegvisomant:
  1. Cardiomyopathy, sleep apnoea, colonic polyps, diabetes, hypertension, arthropathy — Acromegaly complications:
  1. IGF-1, GH, glucose, echocardiogram, sleep study, colonoscopy — Acromegaly monitoring:
  1. IGF-1 rise; repeat MRI; medical therapy (SSA/pegvisomant) or radiotherapy — Acromegaly recurrence post-surgery:
  1. Reduced if uncontrolled (CV disease); normal if controlled — Acromegaly life expectancy:

SBAs

  1. Transsphenoidal surgery — 45yo man: enlarged hands/feet, coarse features, IGF-1 3x ULN, MRI 12mm macroadenoma. Treatment?
  1. IGF-1 q6mo; MRI at 3mo then annually — Same patient post-op: IGF-1 normalised. Follow-up?
  1. Somatostatin analogue (octreotide LAR/lanreotide) — Acromegaly with residual tumour post-surgery, IGF-1 elevated. Next?
  1. Pegvisomant (GH receptor antagonist) — Acromegaly on octreotide LAR, IGF-1 still elevated. Add?
  1. Echo, sleep study, colonoscopy, glucose, IGF-1 q6-12mo — Acromegaly with sleep apnoea, cardiomyopathy. Monitoring?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.6.02 Diabetes Insipidus
[!info]
Diabetes Insipidus (DI) = Polyuria + Polydipsia due to ADH Deficiency (Central) or Renal Resistance (Nephrogenic). Central DI = ADH Deficiency; Nephrogenic DI = Renal Resistance. Water Deprivation Test + Desmopressin Challenge = Gold Standard Diagnostic Test.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Differentiate Central DI, Nephrogenic DI, and Primary Polydipsia
  • [ ] Perform and interpret Water Deprivation Test + Desmopressin Challenge
  • [ ] Apply desmopressin therapy for Central DI
  • [ ] Manage Nephrogenic DI (Thiazides, Amiloride, Low Salt Diet)
  • [ ] Recognise Gestational DI and its management

2. Overview & Classification

TypePathophysiologyADH LevelUrine OsmolalityDesmopressin Response
Central DIADH Deficiency (Pituitary/Stalk/Hypothalamic Lesion)LowLow (Dilute)Responds ↑ Urine Osm >50%
Nephrogenic DIRenal Resistance to ADH (V2 Receptor/AQP2 Defect)High/NormalLow (Dilute)No Response
Primary PolydipsiaExcessive Water Intake (Psychogenic/Dipsogenic)SuppressedLow (Concentrates on Deprivation)Concentrates on Deprivation
Gestational DIPlacental Vasopressinase Degrades ADHLow/NormalDiluteResponds to Desmopressin

3. Aetiology

Central DI (CDI)

CauseFrequencyDetails
Idiopathic~30%Autoimmune (Lymphocytic Infundibuloneurohypophysitis)
Pituitary Surgery / Trauma~20%Post-TSS, Head Trauma, Basal Skull Fracture
Infiltration~15%Sarcoidosis, Langerhans Cell Histiocytosis, Metastases, Haemochromatosis
GeneticRareAVP Gene Mutations (Autosomal Dominant)
Pituitary Surgery/Apoplexy~10%Post-TSS, Pituitary Apoplexy
Craniopharyngioma~5%Suprasellar Tumor Compressing Stalk

Nephrogenic DI (NDI)

CauseMechanismKey Features
LithiumMost Common Acquired↓ AQP2 Expression, ↓ cAMP; Dose/Duration Dependent
DrugsDemeclocycline, Amphotericin B, Foscarnet, Ofloxacin, IfosfamideV2 Receptor / AQP2 Inhibition
ElectrolytesHypercalcaemia, HypokalaemiaTubular Damage
Obstructive UropathyTubulointerstitial Damage
GeneticAVPR2 (X-Linked Recessive), AQP2 (AR)Congenital NDI

Primary Polydipsia (Psychogenic)

FeatureDetails
AetiologyPsychogenic (Schizophrenia), Habitual, Dipsogenic
MechanismExcess Water Intake → Suppresses ADH → Dilute Urine
Key FeatureUrine Concentrates During Deprivation (Unlike DI)

4. Clinical Presentation

FeatureCentral DINephrogenic DIPrimary Polydipsia
Polyuria>3-4 L/day>3-4 L/day>3-4 L/day
PolydipsiaProfound Thirst, NocturiaProfound Thirst, NocturiaCompulsive Drinking
Urine OsmolalityLow (<300 mOsm/kg)Low (<300 mOsm/kg)Low/Variable (Concentrates on Deprivation)
Serum OsmolalityHigh (>295 mOsm/kg)HighNormal/ Low
Serum SodiumHigh (>145 mmol/L)HighNormal/Low
ADH LevelLow/UndetectableHighSuppressed
NocturiaSevereSevereSevere

5. Diagnostic Algorithm — Water Deprivation Test (WDT)

Protocol

PhaseProcedureStop Criteria
1. Fluid DeprivationNPO + Fluid Restriction; Weight, Serum/Urine Osmolality q1-2hWeight Loss >5%; Urine Osm > Plasma Osm; Haemodynamic Instability
2. Desmopressin ChallengeDDAVP 2µg SC/IV (or 10-40µg Intranasal)Measure Urine Osm at 60min

Interpretation Matrix

ResultCentral DINephrogenic DIPrimary Polydipsia
Baseline Urine OsmLow (<300)Low (<300)Low/Variable
After DeprivationLow (<300)Low (<300)Concentrates (>600)
Post-DDAVP (60min)↑ >50%No Response (<10%)N/A (Already Concentrated)
Plasma ADHLowHighLow (Suppressed)

Stop Criteria (Safety)

CriterionAction
Weight Loss >5%STOP
Urine Osm > Plasma OsmSTOP
Haemodynamic Instability (SBP<90, HR>120)STOP
Time >8 HoursSTOP

6. Desmopressin (DDAVP) Therapy

Central DI

RouteDoseFrequency
Intranasal10-40µgq12-24h
Oral (Tablet)0.1-0.4mgq12-24h
Subcutaneous / IV2-4µgq12-24h
Dose TitrationTarget: Urine Output 1.5-2.5L/day, No Nocturia, Normal Na⁺

Nephrogenic DI

TreatmentDoseMechanism
Low Salt / Low Protein Diet—Reduces Solute Load
Thiazide DiureticHydrochlorothiazide 25-50mg BDInduces Mild Volume Depletion → ↑ Proximal Reabsorption
Amiloride5-10mg BDENaC Blocker; K⁺-Sparing
NSAIDs (Indomethacin)25-50mg TDS↑ Prostaglandin Inhibition → ↓ PGE2 → ↑ ADH Sensitivity
DesmopressinPartial Response (If Partial NDI)Limited Role

7. Gestational DI

FeatureDetails
CausePlacental Vasopressinase Degrades ADH
Onset2nd/3rd Trimester
ResolutionPostpartum (4-6 Weeks)
TreatmentDesmopressin (Safe in Pregnancy); Monitor Fluid Balance
RecurrenceHigh in Subsequent Pregnancies

8. Differential Diagnosis

FeatureCentral DINephrogenic DIPrimary Polydipsia
Serum Osmolality>295 mOsm/kg>295 mOsm/kgNormal/Low
Urine Osmolality<300 mOsm/kg<300 mOsm/kgConcentrates on Deprivation
Urine Osm Post-DDAVP↑ >50%No ChangeN/A
Plasma ADHLowHighSuppressed
Desmopressin TestPositive ResponseNo ResponseConcentrates on Deprivation

9. Management

Central DI

AspectDetails
Desmopressin (DDAVP)Intranasal 10-40µg q12-24h; Oral 0.1-0.4mg q12-24h; SC/IV 2-4µg q24h
Dose TitrationStart Low; Titrate to Urine Output 1.5-2.5L/day; Nocturia ≤1
Fluid IntakeNo Restriction (Ad Libitum)
Adverse EffectsHyponatraemia (Water Retention) — Monitor Na⁺

Nephrogenic DI

TherapyDoseRole
DietLow Salt, Low ProteinReduce Solute Load
ThiazideHCTZ 25-50mg BDInduces Mild Volume Depletion → ↑ Proximal Reabsorption
Amiloride5-10mg BDENaC Blocker; K⁺-Sparing
NSAIDsIndomethacin 25-50mg TDS↓ Prostaglandin → ↑ ADH Sensitivity
DesmopressinPartial Response OnlyIf Partial NDI

10. Complications & Monitoring

ComplicationMonitoring
HyponatraemiaSerum Na⁺ q6-12h (If on Desmopressin)
Water IntoxicationFluid Restriction if Severe; Stop Desmopressin
HypernatraemiaIf Desmopressin Missed/Dose Inadequate
Breakthrough PolyuriaDose Escalation / Split Dosing

11. Special: Gestational DI

FeatureManagement
Onset2nd/3rd Trimester (Placental Vasopressinase)
TreatmentDesmopressin (Safe in Pregnancy)
DeliveryMonitor Fluid Balance; Desmopressin Peripartum
PostpartumResolves 4-6 Weeks Postpartum
RecurrenceHigh Recurrence in Subsequent Pregnancies

12. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
DI vs SIADHDI = Polyuria + Dilute Urine + High Serum Osm; SIADH = Hyponatraemia + Concentrated Urine
Central vs Nephrogenic DICentral: ADH Low, Responds to DDAVP; Nephrogenic: ADH High, No DDAVP Response
WDT Stop CriteriaWeight Loss >5% OR Urine Osm > Plasma Osm OR Haemodynamic Instability
Central DI TreatmentDesmopressin (DDAVP) — Intranasal/Oral/SC/IV
Nephrogenic DI TreatmentThiazide + Amiloride + Low Salt Diet (Desmopressine Ineffective)
LithiumMost Common Cause of Acquired NDI (↓ AQP2, ↓ cAMP)
Primary PolydipsiaUrine Concentrates on Deprivation; DDAVP Not Needed
Gestational DIPlacental Vasopressinase; Desmopressin Safe; Resolves Postpartum
WDT Stop CriteriaWeight Loss >5%, Urine Osm > Plasma Osm, Haemodynamic Instability
Desmopressin HyponatraemiaMonitor Na⁺; Limit Fluids if on DDAVP
Lithium NDI↓ AQP2 Expression → Stop Lithium if Possible
Central vs Nephrogenic DIDDAVP Response: Central = ↑ Urine Osm >50%; Nephrogenic = No Response

13. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Central vs Nephrogenic DICentral = ADH Deficiency; Nephrogenic = Renal Resistance to ADH
DDAVP ResponseCentral = Works; Nephrogenic = Fails
Primary Polydipsia vs DIPolydipsia: Urine Concentrates on Deprivation; DI = Fails to Concentrate
Gestational DIPlacental Vasopressinase → ADH Degradation; Resolves Postpartum
Lithium NDI↓ AQP2 Expression; ↓ cAMP; Most Common Acquired NDI
Desmopressin vs VasopressinDDAVP = Pure V2 Agonist (No Pressor Effect); Vasopressin = V1+V2
Desmopressin in EnuresisIntranasal/Oral at Bedtime; No Fluid Restriction Needed
WDT StopWeight Loss >5% OR Urine Osm > Plasma Osm

14. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Diabetes Insipidus))
    Types
      Central DI (ADH Deficiency)
        Pituitary Surgery/Trauma
        Infiltration (Sarcoid, LCH, Mets)
        Genetic (AVP Mutation)
      Nephrogenic DI (Renal Resistance)
        Lithium (Most Common Acquired)
        Drugs (Demeclocycline, Amphotericin)
        Genetic (AVPR2, AQP2)
        Electrolytes (HyperCa, HypoK)
      Primary Polydipsia
        Psychogenic / Habitual
        Urine Concentrates on Deprivation
      Gestational DI
        Placental Vasopressinase
        Desmopressin Safe
    Diagnosis
      Water Deprivation Test (Gold Standard)
      Deprivation → DDAVP Challenge
      Central: Urine Osm ↑ >50% post DDAVP
      Nephrogenic: No Response
      Polydipsia: Concentrates on Deprivation
    Treatment
      Central: Desmopressin (DDAVP)
      Nephrogenic: Thiazide + Amiloride + Low Salt
      Gestational: DDAVP (Safe)
    WDT Protocol
      Deprivation until >5% weight loss or Uosm > Posm
      DDAVP 2µg SC/IV → Urine Osm at 60min
      Central: ↑>50%; Nephrogenic: No Response

15. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Central vs Nephrogenic DICentral: ADH Low → DDAVP Response; Nephrogenic: ADH High → DDAVP No Response
WDT Gold StandardDeprivation → DDAVP Challenge
Central DIDDAVP → Urine Osm ↑ >50%
Nephrogenic DIDDAVP → No Response
Primary PolydipsiaUrine Concentrates on Deprivation (No DDAVP Needed)
Gestational DIPlacental Vasopressinase → ADH Degradation → Desmopressin Safe
Lithium NDIMost Common Acquired NDI (↓ AQP2, ↓ cAMP)
DDAVP in Central DIIntranasal/Oral/SC/IV; Monitor Na+ (Hyponatraemia Risk)
NDI ManagementThiazide + Amiloride + Low Salt Diet; Desmopressine Not Effective
WDT Stop CriteriaWeight Loss >5% OR Urine Osm > Plasma Osm

16. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Posterior Pituitary- ADH-Vasopressin|Posterior Pituitary- ADH-Vasopressin]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Posterior Pituitary- ADH-Vasopressin]], [[SIADH]], [[Posterior Pituitary- Oxytocin]], [[Pituitary Apoplexy]], [[Craniopharyngioma]]

17. MCQs (10)

  1. Diabetes insipidus =

A. ADH deficiency (central) or renal resistance (nephrogenic); polyuria/polydipsia

B. ADH excess

C. Aldosterone deficiency

D. Cortisol deficiency

E. Thyroid hormone excess

  1. Central DI causes:

A. Pituitary surgery, trauma, tumours, infiltrative, idiopathic

B. Renal disease

C. Lithium

D. Hypercalcaemia

E. Hypokalaemia

  1. Nephrogenic DI causes:

A. Lithium, hypercalcaemia, hypokalaemia, renal disease, genetic (AQP2)

B. Pituitary surgery

C. Trauma

D. Tumours

E. Idiopathic

  1. Water deprivation test - central DI:

A. No urine concentration -> DDAVP -> urine osmolality rises >50%

B. No response to DDAVP

C. Normal concentration

D. Urine osmolality >800

E. Serum Na+ normal

  1. Water deprivation test - nephrogenic DI:

A. No urine concentration -> DDAVP -> NO response

B. Concentrates with DDAVP

C. Normal concentration

D. Urine osmolality >800

E. Serum Na+ normal

  1. Central DI treatment:

A. Desmopressin (DDAVP) oral/nasal/IV; monitor sodium

B. Fluid restriction

C. Thiazide

D. NSAIDs

E. Fludrocortisone

  1. Nephrogenic DI treatment:

A. Thiazide + low salt + NSAID (indomethacin); DDAVP ineffective

B. Desmopressin

C. Fluid restriction only

D. Furosemide

E. Spironolactone

  1. Gestational DI:

A. Placental vasopressinase degrades ADH; treat with desmopressin

B. Central DI

C. Nephrogenic DI

D. Primary polydipsia

E. SIADH

  1. Primary polydipsia:

A. Water deprivation -> partial concentration; DDAVP -> further concentration

B. Central DI

C. Nephrogenic DI

D. SIADH

E. No concentration

  1. Desmopressin dosing:

A. Central DI: 100-400mcg oral q12-24h; 1-4mcg IV/SC q8-12h; titrate to urine osmolality

B. Fixed 1mg daily

C. Only IV

D. Only nasal

E. Weight-based only

18. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 30yo man: polyuria 6L/day, polydipsia, serum Na+ 150, urine osmolality 100. After water dep: urine osmolality 150. DDAVP -> 600. Diagnosis?

A. Central DI

B. Nephrogenic DI

C. Primary polydipsia

D. SIADH

E. Gestational DI

  1. Same patient: treatment?

A. Desmopressin 100-200mcg oral/nasal q12h; monitor sodium

B. Fluid restriction

C. Thiazide

D. NSAIDs

E. Furosemide

  1. Patient on lithium: polyuria, Na+ 148, urine osmolality 150. Water dep no concentration. DDAVP no response. Diagnosis?

A. Nephrogenic DI (lithium)

B. Central DI

C. Primary polydipsia

D. SIADH

E. Gestational DI

  1. Same patient: treatment?

A. Thiazide + amiloride + low salt + NSAID; stop lithium if possible

B. Desmopressin

C. Fluid restriction

D. Furosemide

E. Spironolactone

  1. Pregnant woman: polyuria, Na+ 150. Water dep no concentration. DDAVP -> concentration. Diagnosis?

A. Gestational DI

B. Central DI

C. Nephrogenic DI

D. Primary polydipsia

E. SIADH

19. Flashcards

  • Q: Central DI

A: ADH deficiency; water dep no concentrate -> DDAVP -> urine concentrates

  • Q: Nephrogenic DI

A: Renal resistance to ADH; no concentration even with DDAVP

  • Q: Primary polydipsia

A: Water dep -> some concentration; DDAVP -> further concentration

  • Q: Water deprivation test

A: Central DI: DDAVP works; Nephrogenic: DDAVP fails; Polydipsia: some concentration

  • Q: Desmopressin

A: Central DI: 100-400mcg q12-24h oral/nasal; 1-4mcg q8-12h IV/SC

  • Q: Nephrogenic DI Rx

A: Thiazide + low salt + NSAID (indomethacin); stop offending drug

  • Q: Gestational DI

A: Placental vasopressinase; treat with desmopressin

  • Q: Primary polydipsia

A: Water dep partial concentration; DDAVP further concentrates

  • Q: Lithium DI

A: Nephrogenic; thiazide + amiloride + low salt + NSAID

  • Q: SIADH vs DI

A: SIADH: euvolaemic hyponatraemia, Uosm >100, UNa+ >20; DI: hypernatraemia, polyuria

20. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. ADH deficiency (central) or renal resistance (nephrogenic); polyuria/polydipsia — Diabetes insipidus =
  1. Pituitary surgery, trauma, tumours, infiltrative, idiopathic — Central DI causes:
  1. Lithium, hypercalcaemia, hypokalaemia, renal disease, genetic (AQP2) — Nephrogenic DI causes:
  1. No urine concentration -> DDAVP -> urine osmolality rises >50% — Water deprivation test - central DI:
  1. No urine concentration -> DDAVP -> NO response — Water deprivation test - nephrogenic DI:
  1. Desmopressin (DDAVP) oral/nasal/IV; monitor sodium — Central DI treatment:
  1. Thiazide + low salt + NSAID (indomethacin); DDAVP ineffective — Nephrogenic DI treatment:
  1. Placental vasopressinase degrades ADH; treat with desmopressin — Gestational DI:
  1. Water deprivation -> partial concentration; DDAVP -> further concentration — Primary polydipsia:
  1. Central DI: 100-400mcg oral q12-24h; 1-4mcg IV/SC q8-12h; titrate to urine osmolality — Desmopressin dosing:

SBAs

  1. Central DI — 30yo man: polyuria 6L/day, polydipsia, serum Na+ 150, urine osmolality 100. After water dep: urine osmolality 150. DDAVP -> 600. Diagnosis?
  1. Desmopressin 100-200mcg oral/nasal q12h; monitor sodium — Same patient: treatment?
  1. Nephrogenic DI (lithium) — Patient on lithium: polyuria, Na+ 148, urine osmolality 150. Water dep no concentration. DDAVP no response. Diagnosis?
  1. Thiazide + amiloride + low salt + NSAID; stop lithium if possible — Same patient: treatment?
  1. Gestational DI — Pregnant woman: polyuria, Na+ 150. Water dep no concentration. DDAVP -> concentration. Diagnosis?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.6.03 Gonadotroph Adenoma
[!info]
Gonadotroph Adenoma = Pituitary Adenoma Secreting FSH and/or LH (and α-Subunit). Usually Clinically Silent / Non-Functioning. Most Present as Macroadenomas with Mass Effect. No Classical Hormone Excess Syndrome (Unlike Prolactinoma/Acromegaly/Cushing).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise the clinical presentation (mass effect rather than hormone excess)
  • [ ] Interpret hormonal profile (FSH/LH/α-subunit elevation without clinical syndrome)
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic workup (MRI, hormonal panel, visual fields)
  • [ ] Apply management algorithm (observation vs surgery)
  • [ ] Differentiate from other pituitary adenomas

2. Pathophysiology & Classification

FeatureDetails
Cell OriginGonadotrophs (FSH/LH Producing Cells) — ~10-15% of Anterior Pituitary
Hormones SecretedFSH, LH, α-Subunit (Common), β-Subunits (FSH-β, LH-β)
Clinical SyndromeUsually NONE ("Silent" / Non-Functioning)
Size at PresentationMacroadenoma (>10mm) in >80% — Presents Late Due to Lack of Hormone Excess

Hormone Profile

HormoneTypical Pattern
FSH↑↑ (Often Disproportionately High vs LH)
LH↑ or Normal
α-SubunitConsistently Elevated (Most Sensitive Marker)
Free α-SubunitMarkedly Elevated (Specific Marker)
Testosterone/OestradiolLow/Normal (Due to ↓ Pulsatile LH) — Not Elevated

3. Clinical Presentation — Mass Effect Dominates

PresentationFrequencyDetails
Incidentaloma20-30%Found on MRI for Headache/Trauma
Visual Field Defects50-70%Bi-temporal Hemianopia (Chiasmal Compression)
Headache40-60%Frontal/Retro-orbital
Hypopituitarism40-50%GH → Gonadotrophs → TSH → ACTH Order
Cranial Nerve Palsies10-20%CN III/IV/VI (Cavernous Sinus Invasion)
Hormone Excess SymptomsAbsentNo Classical Syndrome (No Hyperprolactinaemia, Acromegaly, Cushing)

4. Biochemical Profile

Hormonal Pattern

HormoneTypical FindingSignificance
FSH↑↑ (Often Disproportionately > LH)Gonadotroph Hyperplasia/Neoplasia
LH↑ or NormalOften Less Than FSH
α-Subunit↑↑ (Markedly Elevated)Most Sensitive Marker
Free α-SubunitMarkedly ElevatedSpecific for Gonadotroph Adenoma
LH/FSH Ratio<1 (FSH Predominates)Opposite to PCOS / Menopause
Testosterone / OestradiolLow / NormalDysregulation; Not Elevated
ProlactinNormal / Mildly ↑ (Stalk Effect)Stalk Compression (Disinhibition)

5. Diagnosis — Workup Algorithm

Pituitary Incidentaloma / Mass Effect Symptoms
         │
         ▼
HORMONAL PANEL: FSH, LH, α-Subunit, Free α-Subunit, Testosterone/Oestradiol
         │
         ├── FSH ↑↑, α-Subunit ↑↑, LH Normal/↑, Testosterone/Oestradiol Low/Normal
         │       → **GONADOTROPH ADENOMA** (Silent)
         │
         ├── Prolactin ↑↑ → PROLACTINOMA
         ├── GH/IGF-1 ↑ → ACROMEGALY
         ├── ACTH/Cortisol ↑ → CUSHING DISEASE
         ├── TSH ↑ + fT4/fT3 ↑ → TSH-oma
         └── All Normal / Mild α-Subunit ↑ → NON-FUNCTIONING / NULL CELL ADENOMA

Key Diagnostic Features

FeatureGonadotroph AdenomaNon-Functioning (Null Cell)
FSH/LH↑↑ / ↑ (FSH > LH)Normal / Slightly ↑
α-SubunitMarkedly ElevatedNormal / Mildly ↑
Free α-SubunitMarkedly ↑Normal
Sex SteroidsLow / Normal (Not Elevated)Low (Due to Compression)
Clinical SyndromeNone (Silent)None (Silent)

6. Imaging & Localisation

MRI Pituitary (3mm, Gadolinium)

FeatureFindings
SizeMacroadenoma >10mm (>80%)
EnhancementHomogeneous / Heterogeneous
ExtensionSuprasellar (Chiasm), Cavernous Sinus (Knosp Grade)
SignalT1 Hypointense, T2 Variable

Cavernous Sinus Invasion (Knosp Classification)

GradeDescriptionSurgical Resectability
Grade 0Tumour ≤ Intercarotid LineComplete Resection Likely
Grade 1> Intercarotid, ≤ Tangent to Lateral ICAComplete Possible
Grade 2> Lateral ICA, ≤ Tangent to Medial ICAPartial Resection
Grade 3> Medial ICA, ≤ Tangent to MCCSubtotal
Grade 4> Tangent to MCCGross Total Unlikely

7. Management Algorithm

Gonadotroph Adenoma Confirmed (Hormones + MRI)
         │
         ├── MICROADENOMA (<10mm) + ASYMPTOMATIC
         │       → OBSERVATION
         │       MRI 6-12mo → Yearly ×2 → Then 2-5yrly
         │       Hormonal Surveillance Annual
         │
         ├── MACROADENOMA (≥10mm) + ASYMPTOMATIC
         │       → SURGERY RECOMMENDED (High Risk Future Mass Effect)
         │       Transsphenoidal Surgery (TSS)
         │
         ├── VISUAL FIELD DEFECTS (Any Size)
         │       → URGENT TRANSSEPHENOIDAL SURGERY (TSS)
         │
         ├── HYPOPITUITARISM (Symptomatic)
         │       → SURGERY (TSS) + Hormone Replacement
         │
         └── RECURRENT / RESIDUAL POST-SURGERY
                 ├── ASYMPTOMATIC RESIDUAL (<1cm) → Observation
                 ├── SYMPTOMATIC / GROWING → Re-operation / Radiotherapy
                 └── NOT SURGICAL CANDIDATE → Stereotactic Radiosurgery (SRS)

8. Surgical Management

Transsphenoidal Surgery (TSS)

AspectDetails
ApproachEndoscopic Endonasal (Preferred) / Microscopic Sub-labial
GoalGross Total Resection (GTR) if Possible; Decompression if Cavernous Sinus Invasion
Success RatesGTR 70-80% (Knosp 0-1); Decompression 90-95%
ComplicationsCSF Leak (2-5%), DI (10-20% Transient), New Hypopituitarism (5-10%), Epistaxis

Cavernous Sinus Invasion (Knosp)

GradeDescriptionResection
Grade 0≤ Intercarotid LineComplete Likely
Grade 1> Intercarotid, ≤ Tangent Lateral ICAComplete Possible
Grade 2> Lateral ICA, ≤ Medial ICASubtotal Expected
Grade 3> Medial ICA, ≤ MCCSubtotal
Grade 4> MCCDecompression Only

9. Post-Operative Management

TimepointAssessments
Day 1-2Hormones (FSH, LH, α-Subunit, Testosterone/Oestradiol), Cortisol, Electrolytes, Visual Fields
6 WeeksFull Hormone Panel, Visual Fields
3 MonthsFull Hormones, MRI, Visual Fields
6 MonthsHormones, MRI (If Residual), Visual Fields
Annual ×5yrHormones, MRI, Visual Fields
Long-term2-3 Yearly MRI + Hormones + Visual Fields

Post-Op Hormone Replacement

DeficiencyReplacement
ACTH/CortisolHydrocortisone 15-20mg AM + 5-10mg PM
TSH/fT4Levothyroxine 1.6µg/kg/day
GonadotrophsTestosterone / HRT (If Hypogonadal)
GHRH-GH if Confirmed GHD (ITT Peak <3 µg/L)
ProlactinNot Replaced (Except Lactation)

10. Recurrence & Surveillance

OutcomeRate
Recurrence (GTR)10-15% at 10 Years
Recurrence (Subtotal)30-50% at 5 Years
New Hypopituitarism10-20% (Post-Surgery)

Surveillance Protocol

TimepointInvestigations
Pre-OpFull Hormones, MRI 3mm, Visual Fields
Day 1-2 Post-OpHormones, Electrolytes, Visual Fields
6 WeeksFull Hormones, Visual Fields
3 MonthsHormones, MRI, Visual Fields
Annual (×5yr)Hormones, MRI, Visual Fields
Long-Termq2-3yr MRI + Hormones + Visual Fields

11. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Gonadotroph AdenomaSilent (No Hormone Excess Syndrome); FSH > LH; α-Subunit ↑↑
α-SubunitMarkedly Elevated (Most Sensitive Marker); Free α-Subunit Best
FSH vs LHFSH > LH (Disproportionate) — Opposite to PCOS
Sex SteroidsLow/Normal (Not Elevated) — Key Differentiator
Clinical PresentationMass Effect Only (Visual Fields, Headache, Hypopituitarism)
Incidentaloma Rate20-30% of Pituitary Adenomas
Size at PresentationMacroadenoma >10mm in >80% (Due to Silent Nature)
Surgery IndicationMacroadenoma + Asymptomatic → Surgery Recommended
Knosp ClassificationGrade 0-1 = GTR Likely; Grade 3-4 = Subtotal/Decompression
IOPTH EquivalentNo Standard IOPTH for Gonadotroph (Unlike Cushing)
Recurrence10-15% (GTR); 30-50% (Subtotal) → Adjuvant RT

12. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Gonadotroph vs Non-FunctioningGonadotroph = FSH/LH/α-Subunit ↑ (Biochemically Active); Non-Func = All Hormones Normal
Gonadotroph vs ProlactinomaProlactinoma = PRL ↑↑ + Galactorrhoea/Amenorrhoea; Gonadotroph = FSH/LH/α-Subunit ↑, PRL Normal
Gonadotroph vs TSH-omaTSH-oma: TSH ↑ + fT4/fT3 ↑; Gonadotroph: TSH Normal
Gonadotroph vs Null CellGonadotroph = α-Subunit/FSH/LH ↑; Null Cell = All Hormones Normal
FSH > LHCharacteristic of Gonadotroph Adenoma (vs PCOS LH>FSH)
α-SubunitBest Marker for Gonadotroph Adenoma (More Sensitive than FSH/LH)
Free α-SubunitMore Specific than Total α-Subunit
SurgeryRecommended for All Macroadenomas (Even Asymptomatic)
Knosp ≥3Subtotal Resection Expected; Adjuvant RT Considered

13. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Gonadotroph Adenoma))
    Definition
      FSH/LH/α-Subunit Secreting
      Usually Silent (No Hormone Syndrome)
    Hormones
      FSH ↑↑ (Disproportionate)
      LH ↑/Normal
      α-Subunit ↑↑ (Marker)
      Free α-Subunit ↑↑
      Sex Steroids: Low/Normal
    Presentation
      Mass Effect: Visual Fields, Headache, Hypopituitarism
      NO Hormone Excess Syndrome
      Incidentaloma (20-30%)
    Diagnosis
      FSH↑ + LH↑/N + α-Subunit↑↑ + Steroid Low/Normal
      MRI: Macroadenoma >10mm (80%)
      α-Subunit ↑↑ = Best Marker
    Management
      Micro + Asymptomatic → Observe
      Macro + Asymptomatic → Surgery
      Visual Fields / Hypopituitarism → Urgent Surgery
      TSS (Endoscopic) Preferred
      Knosp Grade Guides Resectability
    Surgery
      TSS (Endoscopic Preferred)
      GTR 70-80% (Knosp 0-1)
      Complications: CSF Leak, DI, New Hypopit
    Surveillance
      Hormones + MRI + Visual Fields
      Annual ×5yr → 2-3yrly
      Recurrence: GTR 10-15%, Subtotal 30-50%

14. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Gonadotroph AdenomaSilent Adenoma; FSH > LH; α-Subunit Markedly Elevated
Clinical PresentationMass Effect Only (Visual Fields, Headache, Hypopituitarism)
No Hormone Excess SyndromeUnlike Prolactinoma/Acromegaly/Cushing
FSH > LHCharacteristic (Opposite to PCOS where LH>FSH)
α-SubunitMost Sensitive Marker (Free α-Subunit Best)
Size at PresentationMacroadenoma >10mm in >80% (Silent → Late Detection)
Surgery IndicationAll Macroadenomas (Even Asymptomatic) → TSS
Knosp Grading0-1 = GTR Likely; 2 = Partial; 3-4 = Decompression Only
IOPTHNot Standard for Gonadotroph (Unlike Cushing)
Recurrence10-15% (GTR); 30-50% (Subtotal)
Free α-SubunitMore Sensitive/Specific than Total α-Subunit

15. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Pituitary Adenomas- Functioning|Pituitary Adenomas- Functioning]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Pituitary Adenomas: Functioning]], [[Pituitary Adenomas: Non-Functioning]], [[FSH-LH-Gonadal Axis]], [[Pituitary Apoplexy]], [[Hypopituitarism]], [[Reproductive Endocrinology]]

16. MCQs (10)

  1. Gonadotroph adenomas are typically:

A. Non-functioning (clinically silent) or minimally functioning; FSH/LH subunits elevated but no clinical syndrome

B. Functioning with hypergonadism

C. Functioning with hypogonadism

D. Prolactin-secreting

E. ACTH-secreting

  1. Most common hormone immunohistochemically detected in gonadotroph adenomas:

A. FSH > LH (FSHβ subunit)

B. LH > FSH

C. TSH

D. GH

E. ACTH

  1. Clinical presentation of gonadotroph adenoma:

A. Mass effect (headache, visual field defects, hypopituitarism) — NOT hormonal syndrome

B. Precocious puberty

C. Ovarian hyperstimulation

D. Testicular enlargement

E. Gynaecomastia

  1. Gonadotroph adenoma in pre-pubertal child may cause:

A. Precocious puberty (rare; LH/FSH secretion)

B. Delayed puberty

C. No effect

D. Growth failure

E. Diabetes insipidus

  1. Post-menopausal women: gonadotroph adenoma may present with:

A. Ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (high FSH → multiple follicles)

B. Breast cancer

C. Endometrial cancer

D. Osteoporosis

E. Thrombosis

  1. Immunohistochemistry for gonadotroph adenoma:

A. FSHβ, LHβ, α-subunit, GATA3, SF1, PITX1

B. PRL, GH, ACTH, TSH

C. Only α-subunit

D. Only LHβ

E. Only FSHβ

  1. Transsphenoidal surgery for gonadotroph adenoma:

A. Indicated for mass effect; gross total resection ~70-80%; recurrence 10-20%

B. Never indicated

C. Only for hormonal syndrome

D. Radiotherapy first

E. Medical therapy first

  1. Post-op surveillance for gonadotroph adenoma:

A. MRI 3mo, 1yr then annually ×5yr; visual fields if chiasmal compression

B. No surveillance needed

C. Only hormone levels

D. PET-CT annually

E. Only if symptomatic

  1. Gonadotroph adenoma vs non-functioning pituitary adenoma:

A. Gonadotroph = IHC positive for FSH/LH subunits; NFPA = negative for all anterior pituitary hormones

B. Same thing

C. Gonadotroph secretes prolactin

D. NFPA secretes GH

E. No difference

  1. Medical treatment for gonadotroph adenoma:

A. No effective medical therapy (dopamine agonists, somatostatin analogues ineffective)

B. Cabergoline

C. Octreotide

D. Pegvisomant

E. Temozolomide

17. SBA Questions (5)

  1. 55yo man: bitemporal hemianopia, headache. MRI: 2.5cm pituitary macroadenoma, suprasellar extension. IHC: FSHβ+, LHβ+, α-subunit+. Diagnosis?

A. Gonadotroph adenoma

B. Non-functioning pituitary adenoma

C. Prolactinoma

D. Somatotroph adenoma

E. Corticotroph adenoma

  1. Same patient: post-transsphenoidal surgery, residual tumour on 3mo MRI. Management?

A. Surveillance MRI 6-12mo; radiotherapy if progression; no medical therapy

B. Cabergoline

C. Octreotide

D. Temozolomide

E. Re-operation immediately

  1. 8yo girl: breast development, vaginal bleeding. MRI: 1.8cm pituitary tumour. IHC: LHβ+, FSHβ+. Diagnosis?

A. Gonadotroph adenoma causing precocious puberty

B. Hypothalamic hamartoma

C. Ovarian tumour

D. Adrenal tumour

E. McCune-Albright

  1. 65yo woman: incidentally found 8mm pituitary microadenoma. IHC: FSHβ+, LHβ+. Asymptomatic, normal visual fields. Management?

A. Conservative: MRI 6-12mo then annually; no surgery unless growth/mass effect

B. Transsphenoidal surgery

C. Radiotherapy

D. Cabergoline

E. Octreotide

  1. Gonadotroph adenoma recurrence post-surgery: typical timing:

A. 5-10 years; surveillance MRI annually ×5yr then q2-3yr

B. 6 months

C. 1 year

D. 20 years

E. Never recurs

18. Flashcards

  • Q: Gonadotroph adenoma

A: Pituitary adenoma expressing FSH/LH/α-subunit; usually clinically NON-functioning

  • Q: Clinical presentation

A: Mass effect: headache, visual field defects (bitemporal hemianopia), hypopituitarism

  • Q: IHC markers

A: FSHβ, LHβ, α-subunit, GATA3, SF1, PITX1

  • Q: Prevalence

A: ~15-20% of pituitary adenomas (2nd after NFPA); but many are clinically silent

  • Q: Pre-pubertal

A: Rare: precocious puberty (LH/FSH secretion)

  • Q: Post-menopausal

A: Rare: ovarian hyperstimulation (high FSH)

  • Q: Surgery

A: TSS for mass effect; GTR 70-80%; recurrence 10-20% at 10yr

  • Q: Medical therapy

A: NONE effective (DA, SST, pegvisomant all ineffective)

  • Q: Radiotherapy

A: If residual/progressive post-surgery; stereotactic or fractionated

  • Q: Surveillance

A: MRI 3mo, 1yr, then annually ×5yr; visual fields if chiasmal compression

  • Q: vs NFPA

A: Gonadotroph: IHC+ for gonadotropin subunits; NFPA: IHC- for all anterior hormones

  • Q: Silent gonadotroph

A: Most gonadotroph adenomas are 'silent' — no clinical hormonal syndrome

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Non-functioning (clinically silent) or minimally functioning; FSH/LH subunits elevated but no clinical syndrome — Gonadotroph adenomas are typically:
  1. FSH > LH (FSHβ subunit) — Most common hormone immunohistochemically detected in gonadotroph adenomas:
  1. Mass effect (headache, visual field defects, hypopituitarism) — NOT hormonal syndrome — Clinical presentation of gonadotroph adenoma:
  1. Precocious puberty (rare; LH/FSH secretion) — Gonadotroph adenoma in pre-pubertal child may cause:
  1. Ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (high FSH → multiple follicles) — Post-menopausal women: gonadotroph adenoma may present with:
  1. FSHβ, LHβ, α-subunit, GATA3, SF1, PITX1 — Immunohistochemistry for gonadotroph adenoma:
  1. Indicated for mass effect; gross total resection ~70-80%; recurrence 10-20% — Transsphenoidal surgery for gonadotroph adenoma:
  1. MRI 3mo, 1yr then annually ×5yr; visual fields if chiasmal compression — Post-op surveillance for gonadotroph adenoma:
  1. Gonadotroph = IHC positive for FSH/LH subunits; NFPA = negative for all anterior pituitary hormones — Gonadotroph adenoma vs non-functioning pituitary adenoma:
  1. No effective medical therapy (dopamine agonists, somatostatin analogues ineffective) — Medical treatment for gonadotroph adenoma:

SBAs

  1. Gonadotroph adenoma — 55yo man: bitemporal hemianopia, headache. MRI: 2.5cm pituitary macroadenoma, suprasellar extension. IHC: FSHβ+, LHβ+, α-subunit+. Diagnosis?
  1. Surveillance MRI 6-12mo; radiotherapy if progression; no medical therapy — Same patient: post-transsphenoidal surgery, residual tumour on 3mo MRI. Management?
  1. Gonadotroph adenoma causing precocious puberty — 8yo girl: breast development, vaginal bleeding. MRI: 1.8cm pituitary tumour. IHC: LHβ+, FSHβ+. Diagnosis?
  1. Conservative: MRI 6-12mo then annually; no surgery unless growth/mass effect — 65yo woman: incidentally found 8mm pituitary microadenoma. IHC: FSHβ+, LHβ+. Asymptomatic, normal visual fields. Management?
  1. 5-10 years; surveillance MRI annually ×5yr then q2-3yr — Gonadotroph adenoma recurrence post-surgery: typical timing:
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.6.04 Prolactinoma
[!info]
Prolactinoma = Prolactin-Secreting Pituitary Adenoma. Commonest Functioning Pituitary Adenoma (40-50%). Microprolactinoma (<10mm) 60%; Macroprolactinoma (≥10mm) 40%. Cabergoline = 1st Line Treatment. Macroprolactinoma in Pregnancy = Continue Cabergoline.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Diagnose prolactinoma using prolactin levels and MRI
  • [ ] Differentiate true prolactinoma from macroprolactinaemia and stalk effect
  • [ ] Apply cabergoline dosing and titration protocols
  • [ ] Manage prolactinoma in pregnancy (micro vs macro)
  • [ ] Apply withdrawal criteria and monitor for recurrence

2. Classification & Epidemiology

TypeSizeProlactin LevelPrevalence
Microprolactinoma<10mm500-5000 mIU/L~60%
Macroprolactinoma≥10mm>5000 mIU/L (Often >10,000)~40%
Giant>40mmVery HighRare
FeatureDetails
IncidenceMost Common Functioning Pituitary Adenoma (40-50%)
Sex RatioFemale > Male (5:1)
Peak Age20-40 Years
GeneticsMEN1 (Rare), AIP (Young-Onset Familial), GPR101

3. Clinical Presentation

FeatureMicroprolactinomaMacroprolactinoma
Prolactin Level500-5000 mIU/L>5000 mIU/L (Often >10,000)
PresentationGalactorrhoea, Amenorrhoea, Infertility, Low LibidoVisual Field Defects (Bi-temporal Hemianopia), Headache, Hypopituitarism
Visual FieldsNormalBi-temporal Hemianopia (Chiasmal Compression)
HeadacheRareCommon

Other Clinical Features

SystemFeatures
ReproductiveGalactorrhoea, Amenorrhoea, Oligomenorrhoea, Infertility, Low Libido, Erectile Dysfunction
BoneOsteoporosis (Oestrogen/Testosterone Deficiency)
MetabolicWeight Gain, Insulin Resistance

4. Differential Diagnosis of Hyperprolactinaemia

Prolactin >500 mIU/L
         │
         ▼
EXCLUDE PHYSIOLOGICAL / PHARMACOLOGICAL CAUSES:
         │
         ├── DRUGS: Antipsychotics (Risperidone > Olanzapine > Quetiapine), Metoclopramide, Antidepressants, OCP, Verapamil, Opioids
         ├── HYPOTHYROIDISM (TRH-driven) → Check TSH
         ├── RENAL FAILURE (Reduced Clearance)
         ├── PREGNANCY
         ├── NIPPLE STIMULATION / STRESS (Recent)
         └── MACROPROLACTINAEMIA
         │
         ▼
REPEAT PROLACTIN (Fasting, Non-stressed, Avoid Nipple Stimulation)
         │
         ├── NORMAL → Transient/Physiological
         │
         └── PERSISTENTLY ELEVATED
                 │
                 ▼
         PEG PRECIPITATION TEST → MACROPROLACTINAEMIA?
                 │
                 ├── YES → MACROPROLACTINAEMIA (Benign, No Treatment)
                 │
                 └── NO → TRUE HYPERPROLACTINAEMIA
                         │
                         ▼
                 MRI PITUITARY (3mm Slices, Gadolinium)
                         │
                         ├── NO TUMOUR + Prolactin <1000 → IDIOPATHIC / STALK EFFECT
                         ├── MICROPROLACTINOMA (<10mm) → CABERGOLINE
                         ├── MACROPROLACTINOMA (≥10mm) → CABERGOLINE / SURGERY
                         └── NO TUMOUR + Prolactin >1000 → STALK EFFECT / ECTOPIC (Rare)

5. Macroprolactinaemia

FeatureDetails
DefinitionProlactin Complexed with IgG Autoantibody (PRL-IgG Complex)
CharacteristicsHigh Molecular Weight (>150 kDa); Biologically Inactive
Prevalence10-25% of Hyperprolactinaemia
DiagnosisPEG (Polyethylene Glycol) Precipitation → Recovery <60% = Macroprolactinaemia
Clinical SignificanceBenign; No Pituitary Tumour; No Treatment Needed
PitfallCan Coexist with True Hyperprolactinaemia → Measure Monomeric PRL Post-PEG

6. Prolactinoma — Management

Microprolactinoma (<10mm)

AspectProtocol
1st LineCabergoline 0.25mg BD → Titrate Weekly by 0.25mg BD
Target Dose0.5-1mg BD (Max 2mg BD)
GoalProlactin Normalisation + Tumour Shrinkage (>50% Volume Reduction)
MonitoringProlactin q4-8w During Titration; MRI at 6-12mo if Macro Features
WithdrawalAfter 2-3 Years Normal Prolactin + No Tumour on MRI → Taper Over 3-6 Months

Macroprolactinoma (≥10mm)

AspectProtocol
1st LineCabergoline 0.5mg BD → Titrate to 1-2mg BD (Max 3mg BD)
TargetProlactin Normalisation + >50% Volume Reduction
Surgery IndicationsCabergoline Resistant/Intolerant; Apoplexy; Chiasmal Compression with Visual Loss Despite Medical Rx
Visual FieldsMandatory (Bi-temporal Hemianopia = Urgent Decompression)

Cabergoline Dosing Schedule

WeekMicroprolactinomaMacroprolactinoma
1-20.25mg BD0.5mg BD
3-40.5mg BD1mg BD
5-81mg BD1.5-2mg BD
Max2mg BD3mg BD (Rarely >2mg)

7. Cabergoline — Key Pharmacology

PropertyDetails
MechanismD2 Dopamine Receptor Agonist → Inhibits Prolactin Secretion + Shrinks Tumour
Half-Life63-69 Hours (Long; Allows BD/Weekly Dosing)
OnsetProlactin Falls Within Hours; Tumour Shrinkage Over Weeks-Months
Side EffectsNausea (40%), Postural Hypotension, Nasal Congestion, Impulse Control Disorders (Rare), Valvulopathy (High Dose, Chronic)
MonitoringEchocardiogram If Dose >2mg/day or Duration >5 Years; Prolactin q3-6mo

8. Withdrawal / Discontinuation Criteria

CriteriaRequired
Duration≥2-3 Years on Stable Dose
BiochemicalProlactin Normalised on Stable Dose for ≥2 Years
RadiologicalNo Tumour Visible on MRI (or Microadenoma Resolved)
ProcessTaper Cabergoline Over 3-6 Months; Recheck Prolactin q3mo; Restart if Recurrence

9. Pregnancy & Prolactinoma

ScenarioMicroprolactinomaMacroprolactinoma
Pre-ConceptionStop Cabergoline if Prolactin Normal + MRI StableContinue Cabergoline (Growth Risk >10%)
During PregnancyStop Cabergoline (Low Growth Risk)Continue Cabergoline (Growth Risk >10%)
MonitoringProlactin q Trimester; Visual Fields q Trimester (If Macro)Prolactin q Trimester; Visual Fields q Trimester; MRI if Symptoms
DeliveryVaginal PreferredC-Section if Chiasmal Compression Risk
PostpartumBreastfeeding → Prolactin ↑; Cabergoline Suppresses LactationBreastfeeding → Cabergoline Suppresses Lactation

Key Point: Microprolactinoma → Stop Cabergoline; Macroprolactinoma → Continue Cabergoline (Growth Risk >10%).

10. Stalk Effect / Disconnection Hyperprolactinaemia

FeatureDetails
MechanismPituitary Stalk Compression → ↓ Dopamine Delivery to Lactotrophs → Disinhibition
Prolactin LevelUsually <2000 mIU/L (Rarely >3000)
MRINon-Functioning Macroadenoma Compressing Stalk / Suprasellar Mass
ManagementTreat Underlying Mass (Surgery if Macroadenoma); Prolactin Normalises Post-Decompression
Key DifferentiatorVisual Field Defects Common; Other Pituitary Deficits Often Coexist

11. Drug-Induced Hyperprolactinaemia

Drug ClassTypical Prolactin LevelManagement
Antipsychotics (Typical > Atypical)Risperidone > Olanzapine > Quetiapine > Clozapine > Aripiprazole (Low)Switch to Lower-Risk Agent (Aripiprazole); If Essential → Monitor
Metoclopramide / DomperidoneD2 Antagonism (GI Motility)Switch if Possible; Reduce Dose
Antidepressants (SSRIs, TCAs)Serotonergic ModulationSwitch if Possible
Opioidsμ-Receptor → ↓ DopamineReduce Dose / Switch
Verapamil / Alpha-MethyldopaCentral D2 EffectSwitch if Possible
OCPOestrogen → Lactotroph HyperplasiaSwitch to POP / Non-Hormonal

12. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Prolactin RegulationDopamine (PIH) = Tonic Inhibition; TRH = Stimulatory; Oestrogen = Stimulatory
Micro vs MacroMicro <10mm (PRL 500-5000); Macro ≥10mm (PRL >5000)
MacroprolactinaemiaPEG Precipitation; Recovery <60% = Macro; Benign, No Treatment
Cabergoline 1st LineD2 Agonist; Start 0.25mg BD (Micro) / 0.5mg BD (Macro); Titrate
Cabergoline MaxMicro: 2mg BD; Macro: 3mg BD
WithdrawalAfter 2-3yr Normal PRL + No Tumour on MRI
Pregnancy + MicroStop Cabergoline (Low Growth Risk)
Pregnancy + MacroContinue Cabergoline (Growth Risk >10%)
Stalk EffectNon-Funct Macroadenoma → ↓ Dopamine Delivery → PRL <2000
MacroprolactinaemiaPEG Precipitation; Recovery <60% = Macro; Benign, No Treatment
Cabergoline vs BromocriptineCabergoline Preferred: Longer t½, Better Tolerability, Higher Efficacy, Weekly Dosing
Prolactin in PregnancyPhysiological ↑ 10-20x; Not Pathological
Stalk EffectNon-Functioning Macro → ↓ Dopamine Delivery → PRL <2000

13. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Prolactinoma vs Stalk EffectStalk Effect: Non-Functioning Macro + PRL <2000 + Visual Fields + Other Deficits
Macroprolactinaemia vs True HyperPRLProlactin = PRL-IgG Complex; PEG Precip → Recovery <60% = Macro; True Hyper = Monomeric PRL > Upper Limit
Cabergoline vs BromocriptineCabergoline Preferred: Longer t½, Better Tolerability, Higher Efficacy, Weekly Dosing
Prolactin in PregnancyPhysiological ↑ 10-20x; Not Pathological
Micro vs Macro ProlactinMicro: 500-5000; Macro: >5000 (Often >10,000)
Cabergoline StopAfter 2-3yr Normal PRL + MRI Resolution
Pregnancy + MacroContinue Cabergoline (Growth Risk >10%)
Pregnancy + MicroStop Cabergoline (Low Risk)
Stalk EffectNon-Functioning Macro → ↓ Dopamine Delivery → PRL <2000
MacroprolactinaemiaPEG Precipitation; Recovery <60% = Macro; Benign, No Treatment

14. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Prolactinoma))
    Physiology
      Lactotroph (15-20%)
      Dopamine (PIH) = Tonic Inhibition
      TRH = Stimulatory
      Oestrogen = Stimulatory
      Suckling → ↑ PRL
    Hyperprolactinaemia
      Causes: Drugs, Hypothyroid, Renal, Pregnancy, Macroprolactin, Stalk Effect
      Drug causes: Antipsychotics > Metoclopramide > Antidepressants
    Macroprolactinaemia
      PRL-IgG Complex; PEG precip; Recovery <60% = Macro
      Benign; No treatment
    Prolactinoma
      Micro <10mm (PRL 500-5000) → Cabergoline
      Macro ≥10mm (PRL >5000) → Cabergoline/Surgery
      Giant >40mm
    Cabergoline
      D2 Agonist; 0.25mg BD (Micro) / 0.5mg BD (Macro)
      Max: 2mg BD (Micro), 3mg BD (Macro)
      Withdrawal: 2-3yr normal PRL + No tumour
    Pregnancy
      Micro: Stop Cabergoline
      Macro: Continue Cabergoline (Growth risk)
    Stalk Effect
      Non-functioning macro → ↓ Dopamine delivery → PRL <2000
    Macroprolactinaemia
      PRL-IgG complex; PEG precip; Recovery <60% = Macro
      Benign; No treatment
    Stalk Effect
      Non-functioning macro → ↓ Dopamine delivery → PRL <2000
    Macroprolactinaemia
      PEG Precipitation; Recovery <60% = Macro
      Benign; No treatment

15. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Prolactin RegulationDopamine (PIH) = Tonic Inhibition; TRH = Stimulatory; Oestrogen = Stimulatory
Micro vs MacroMicro <10mm; Macro ≥10mm (Chiasmal Compression Risk)
Prolactin in Micro500-5000 mIU/L
Prolactin in Macro>5000 mIU/L (Often >10,000)
MacroprolactinaemiaPEG Precipitation; Recovery <60% = Macroprolactin; Benign, No Treatment
Cabergoline 1st LineD2 Agonist; Start 0.25mg BD (Micro) / 0.5mg BD (Macro); Titrate
Cabergoline MaxMicro: 2mg BD; Macro: 3mg BD
WithdrawalAfter 2-3yr Normal PRL + No Tumour on MRI
Pregnancy + MicroStop Cabergoline (Low Growth Risk)
Pregnancy + MacroContinue Cabergoline (Growth Risk >10%)
Stalk EffectNon-Functioning Macro → ↓ Dopamine Delivery → PRL <2000
MacroprolactinaemiaPEG Precipitation; Recovery <60% = Macro; Benign, No Treatment

16. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Pituitary Adenomas- Functioning|Pituitary Adenomas- Functioning]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Pituitary Adenomas: Functioning]], [[Pituitary Adenomas: Non-Functioning]], [[Pituitary Apoplexy]], [[Pregnancy & Endocrine]]

17. MCQs (10)

  1. Prolactinoma =

A. Prolactin-secreting pituitary adenoma; commonest functioning adenoma (~40%)

B. GH-secreting

C. ACTH-secreting

D. TSH-secreting

E. Non-functioning

  1. Prolactinoma prolactin levels:

A. Microadenoma: 100-2000 mIU/L; Macroadenoma: >2000 mIU/L (often >5000)

B. Always <100

C. Always >5000

D. Inversely related to size

E. Normal in macroadenoma

  1. Prolactinoma diagnosis:

A. Prolactin >2000 + pituitary adenoma on MRI

B. Prolactin >100

C. Prolactin <100

D. Prolactin >5000 required

E. MRI only

  1. First-line treatment:

A. Cabergoline 0.25mg BD titrate; normalises prolactin; shrinks tumour 80-90%

B. Surgery

C. Radiotherapy

D. Bromocriptine

E. Observation

  1. Surgery indication:

A. Macroadenoma with visual loss; resistant to cabergoline; pregnant & intolerant

B. All microadenomas

C. All macroadenomas

D. Only if cabergoline fails

E. Never indicated

  1. Prolactinoma in pregnancy:

A. Stop cabergoline at confirmation; monitor prolactin/visual fields; restart postpartum if needed

B. Continue cabergoline

C. RAI

D. Surgery in 1st tri

E. Stop all meds

  1. Prolactinoma post-partum:

A. Restart cabergoline if macroadenoma; monitor prolactin; surgery if visual loss

B. Surgery

C. RAI

D. Observation only

E. Bromocriptine only

  1. Cabergoline dose:

A. Start 0.25mg BD; titrate to max 1-2mg/week; usual dose 0.5-1mg/week

B. 1mg OD

C. 5mg weekly

D. 0.5mg OD

E. 0.1mg OD

  1. Macroadenoma visual loss
