18. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 30yo woman: amenorrhoea, galactorrhoea, prolactin 2000, MRI 6mm microadenoma.

A. Cabergoline 0.25mg BD titrate

B. Surgery

C. RAI

D. Observation

E. Bromocriptine

  1. Same: macroadenoma 15mm, visual field defect. Urgent?

A. Surgery (decompression) -> then cabergoline

B. Cabergoline only

C. RAI

D. Observation

E. Radiotherapy

  1. Prolactinoma in pregnancy 8wks on cabergoline.

A. Stop cabergoline; monitor prolactin/visual fields; restart postpartum if needed

B. Continue cabergoline

C. RAI

D. Surgery

E. Stop all meds

  1. Pregnant prolactinoma restart postpartum?

A. If macroadenoma -> restart cabergoline; monitor PRL; surgery if visual loss

B. Never restart

C. Surgery

D. RAI

E. Bromocriptine

  1. Prolactinoma resistant to cabergoline.

A. Switch to bromocriptine / surgery / radiotherapy

B. Increase cabergoline indefinitely

C. Surgery only

D. RAI

E. Observation

19. Flashcards

  • Q: Prolactinoma

A: Prolactin-secreting pituitary adenoma (~40% functioning adenomas)

  • Q: Prolactin levels

A: Micro: 100-2000; Macro: >2000 (often >5000)

  • Q: Diagnosis

A: Prolactin >2000 + pituitary adenoma on MRI

  • Q: Cabergoline

A: 0.25mg BD titrate; normalise PRL; shrink 80-90%

  • Q: Surgery

A: If visual loss / resistant / pregnant intolerant

  • Q: Pregnancy

A: Stop cabergoline; monitor PRL/visual fields; restart postpartum if macro

  • Q: Dopamine agonist resistance

A: PRL not normalising on max dose; switch bromocriptine / surgery / RT

  • Q: Macroprolactin

A: High MW; biologically inactive; PEG precipitation to exclude

  • Q: Stalk effect

A: PRL <100 mIU/L; dopamine dishibition; NOT prolactinoma

  • Q: Bromocriptine

A: Ergot derivative; more SE; 2nd line if cabergoline fails

20. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Prolactin-secreting pituitary adenoma; commonest functioning adenoma (~40%) — Prolactinoma =
  1. Microadenoma: 100-2000 mIU/L; Macroadenoma: >2000 mIU/L (often >5000) — Prolactinoma prolactin levels:
  1. Prolactin >2000 + pituitary adenoma on MRI — Prolactinoma diagnosis:
  1. Cabergoline 0.25mg BD titrate; normalises prolactin; shrinks tumour 80-90% — First-line treatment:
  1. Macroadenoma with visual loss; resistant to cabergoline; pregnant & intolerant — Surgery indication:
  1. Stop cabergoline at confirmation; monitor prolactin/visual fields; restart postpartum if needed — Prolactinoma in pregnancy:
  1. Restart cabergoline if macroadenoma; monitor prolactin; surgery if visual loss — Prolactinoma post-partum:
  1. Start 0.25mg BD; titrate to max 1-2mg/week; usual dose 0.5-1mg/week — Cabergoline dose:
  1. U — Macroadenoma visual loss
  1. P — Dopamine agonist resistance
  1. R — Prolactinoma recurrence

SBAs

  1. Cabergoline 0.25mg BD titrate — 30yo woman: amenorrhoea, galactorrhoea, prolactin 2000, MRI 6mm microadenoma.
  1. Surgery (decompression) -> then cabergoline — Same: macroadenoma 15mm, visual field defect. Urgent?
  1. Stop cabergoline; monitor prolactin/visual fields; restart postpartum if needed — Prolactinoma in pregnancy 8wks on cabergoline.
  1. If macroadenoma -> restart cabergoline; monitor PRL; surgery if visual loss — Pregnant prolactinoma restart postpartum?
  1. Switch to bromocriptine / surgery / radiotherapy — Prolactinoma resistant to cabergoline.
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.6.05 Siadh
[!info]
SIADH = Syndrome of Inappropriate ADH Secretion. Commonest Cause of Euvolaemic Hyponatraemia. Inappropriate ADH Secretion → Water Retention → Dilutional Hyponatraemia. Diagnosis = Exclusion (Schwartz-Bartter Criteria). Treatment: Fluid Restriction → Demeclocycline → Tolvaptan (V2 Antagonist).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply Schwartz-Bartter diagnostic criteria for SIADH
  • [ ] Differentiate SIADH from Cerebral Salt Wasting (CSW) and Hypovolaemic Hyponatraemia
  • [ ] Apply stepwise management: Fluid Restriction → Demeclocycline → Tolvaptan
  • [ ] Manage severe symptomatic hyponatraemia with hypertonic saline
  • [ ] Identify and treat underlying causes (drugs, malignancy, CNS, pulmonary)

2. Diagnostic Criteria (Schwartz-Bartter)

CriterionRequirement
1. HyponatraemiaSerum Na+ <135 mmol/L
2. Hypo-osmolalitySerum Osm <275 mOsm/kg
3. Inappropriately Concentrated UrineUrine Osm >100 mOsm/kg (Usually >200)
4. EuvolaemiaNormal Volume Status (No Oedema, Normal JVP, Normal BP)
5. Elevated Urine SodiumUrine Na+ >20 mmol/L (Euvolaemic)
6. Normal Thyroid/Adrenal/RenalTSH Normal, Cortisol Normal, Creatinine Normal
7. No DiureticsExclude Recent Diuretic Use (Within 1-2 Weeks)

All 6 Criteria Must Be Met for Diagnosis.

3. Aetiology — SIADH Causes

CategoryCauses
Drugs (Commonest)SSRIs, SNRIs, Tricyclics, Carbamazepine, Oxcarbazepine, Chlorpropamide, Cyclophosphamide, Vincristine, MDMA
MalignancySmall Cell Lung Cancer (Most Common), Pancreatic, Thymoma, Lymphoma, Head & Neck
CNS DisordersStroke, Haemorrhage, Meningitis, Encephalitis, Guillain-Barré, Tumours, Trauma, Neurosurgery
PulmonaryPneumonia, TB, Lung Abscess, Positive Pressure Ventilation
EndocrineHypothyroidism, Adrenal Insufficiency (Addison's)
Post-OperativePost-Neurosurgery, Post-Abdominal Surgery
OtherPorphyria, Acute Intermittent Porphyria, Nausea/Vomiting

4. Diagnostic Approach

Stepwise Diagnosis

Hyponatraemia (Serum Na+ <135 mmol/L)
         │
         ├── ASSESS VOLUME STATUS
         │       ├── HYPOVOLAEMIC → Hypovolaemic Hyponatraemia
         │       │       (Diuretics, GI Losses, Cerebral Salt Wasting)
         │       │
         │       ├── HYPERVOLAEMIC → Hypervolaemic Hyponatraemia
         │       │       (HF, Cirrhosis, Nephrotic Syndrome)
         │       │
         └── EUVOLAEMIC → **SIADH** (Most Common)
                 │
                 ├── EXCLUDE: Hypothyroidism, Adrenal Insufficiency, Renal Failure
                 ├── EXCLUDE: Diuretic Use (Recent)
                 ├── APPLY SCHWARTZ-BARTTER CRITERIA (All 6 Must Be Met)
                 └── CONFIRM SIADH

Diagnostic Criteria (Schwartz-Bartter) — All 6 Must Be Met

#CriterionRequirement
1HyponatraemiaSerum Na+ <135 mmol/L
2Hypo-osmolalitySerum Osm <275 mOsm/kg
3Inappropriately Concentrated UrineUrine Osm >100 mOsm/kg (Usually >200)
4EuvolaemiaNormal JVP, No Oedema, Normal BP
5Elevated Urine Na+Urine Na+ >20 mmol/L (Euvolaemic)
6Normal Endocrine/RenalTSH Normal, Cortisol Normal, Creatinine Normal; No Diuretics

5. Differential Diagnosis — Euvolaemic Hyponatraemia

ConditionSerum Na+Urine OsmUrine Na+Volume StatusKey Differentiator
SIADHLow>100>20EuvolaemicInappropriate ADH
Cerebral Salt Wasting (CSW)Low>100>20 (Often Higher)HypovolaemicHypovolaemia + High Urine Na+
HypothyroidismLowVariableVariableEuvolaemicTSH ↑, Low fT4
Adrenal InsufficiencyLowHighHighHypovolaemicCortisol Low, ACTH ↑, Hyperpigmentation
Glucocorticoid DeficiencyLow>100>20HypovolaemicCortisol Low
Reset OsmostatStable Low (125-130)Inappropriately High>20EuvolaemicStable Na+ 125-130
Diuretic UseLowVariable>20Euvo/HypovolaemicRecent Diuretic History
Beer PotomaniaLowLowLowEuvolaemicLow Solute Intake History

6. SIADH vs Cerebral Salt Wasting (CSW) — Critical Distinction

FeatureSIADHCerebral Salt Wasting (CSW)
Volume StatusEuvolaemicHypovolaemic
Urine Na+>20 mmol/L>20 (Often Higher)
Urine Osm>100>100 (Often Higher)
Serum Na+LowLow
JVPNormalLow
Skin TurgorNormalDry
Postural BPNormalPostural Drop >20 mmHg
Urine OutputLow/NormalHigh (Polyuria)
Uric AcidLow/NormalHigh
ManagementFluid RestrictionSaline Resuscitation (+ Fludrocortisone)
PathophysiologyADH Excess → Water RetentionRenal Salt Wasting → Volume Depletion → ADH Release
Key: Volume Status is the Key Discriminator — SIADH = Euvolaemic; CSW = Hypovolaemic.

7. Management Algorithm

Stepwise Management (Euvoleaemic Hyponatraemia = SIADH)

SIADH Confirmed (All 6 Criteria Met)
         │
         ▼
IDENTIFY & TREAT UNDERLYING CAUSE
         │
         ├── Drug-Induced → Stop/Reduce Offending Drug
         ├── Malignancy → Treat Cancer
         ├── CNS/Pulmonary → Treat Underlying
         │
         ▼
STEP 1: FLUID RESTRICTION (<1 L/day)
         │
         ├── Effective → Goal Na+ Rise 0.5-1 mmol/L/day; Target Na+ >130
         ├── IF Na+ Rise <0.5 mmol/L/day OR Severe Symptoms → STEP 2
         │
         ▼
STEP 2: DEMECLOCYCLINE 600-1200mg/day (600-1200mg/day in Divided Doses)
         │       MoA: Induces Nephrogenic DI (ADH Resistance)
         │       Side Effects: Photosensitivity, Nephrotoxicity, GI
         │       Monitor: Renal Function, Photosensitivity
         │
         ▼
STEP 3: TOLVAPTAN (V2 Receptor Antagonist) — **Severe/Refractory**
         │       Dose: 15mg OD PO → Titrate to 30, 45, 60mg OD
         │       Target: Na+ Rise 4-6 mmol/L/24h (Max 8-10 mmol/L/24h)
         │       Monitoring: Na+ q4-6h, Liver Function (Hepatotoxicity Risk)
         │       Max Duration: 30 Days (US FDA); Longer in Some Countries
         │
         ▼
STEP 4: HYPERTONIC SALINE (3% NaCl) — **SEVERE SYMPTOMATIC HYPONATRAEMIA**
         │       Na+ <120 mmol/L OR Seizures/Coma
         │       3% NaCl 100-150mL over 1-2h (Target ↑Na+ 4-6 mmol/L/24h)
         │       Monitor: Na+ q1-2h, Fluid Balance, Neurological Status

8. Severe Symptomatic Hyponatraemia — Emergency Protocol

SeveritySerum Na+Neurological StatusImmediate Treatment
Mild130-134Asymptomatic/MildFluid Restriction
Moderate125-129Nausea, Headache, Mild ConfusionFluid Restriction ± Demeclocycline
Severe<125Seizures, Coma, Severe Confusion3% Hypertonic Saline 100-150mL over 1-2h
Life-Threatening<115Coma, Seizures, Respiratory Arrest3% Saline 100-150mL over 1h + ICU

Hypertonic Saline Protocol

ParameterTarget
Na+ Rise Rate4-6 mmol/L in First 24h (Max 8 mmol/L/24h)
Max Correction8-10 mmol/L in 24h (Avoid Central Pontine Myelinolysis)
Infusion3% NaCl 100-150mL over 1-2h (Repeat if Needed)
MonitorNa+ q1-2h, Neuro Checks, Fluid Balance, Urine Output

9. Specific Causes & Management

Drug-Induced SIADH

Drug ClassExamplesManagement
AntidepressantsSSRIs (Sertraline, Fluoxetine), TCAs, MAOIsStop/Reduce; Switch to Alternative
Carbamazepine / OxcarbazepineCommon CauseSwitch to Alternative AED
Chemotherapy (Cyclophosphamide, Vincristine)ChemoSupportive; Demeclocycline if Needed
Opioids / MDMAAcuteStop Drug; Supportive

Malignancy-Related SIADH

TumourFrequencyManagement
Small Cell Lung CancerMost CommonTreat Cancer; Demeclocycline / Tolvaptan
Pancreatic / Thymoma / LymphomaLess CommonTreat Cancer

SIADH in CNS Disorders

ConditionManagement
Stroke / HaemorrhageSupportive; Fluid Restrict; Demeclocycline if Persistent
Meningitis / EncephalitisTreat Infection; Manage SIADH
NeurosurgeryPost-Op SIADH Common; Transient Usually
TraumaMonitor; Fluid Restrict; Demeclocycline if Persistent

10. Cerebral Salt Wasting (CSW) — Detailed

Diagnostic Criteria

FeatureRequirement
CNS DiseasePresent (SAH, TBI, Neurosurgery, Tumour)
HyponatraemiaSerum Na+ <135
Volume StatusHypovolaemic (Low JVP, Dry Mucosa, Postural Hypotension)
Urine Na+>20 mmol/L (Often >100)
Urine Osm>100 mOsm/kg (Often >300)
Urine OutputHigh (Polyuria)

Management of CSW

StepAction
1. Volume ResuscitationIV 0.9% NaCl 1-2L Bolus → Then Maintenance
2. Fludrocortisone0.05-0.2mg OD (Mineralocorticoid Replacement)
3. Salt SupplementationNaCl Tablets 1-2g TDS
4. MonitorNa+, K+, Volume Status, UOP

11. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
SIADH = Euvolaemic HyponatraemiaMost Common Cause of Hyponatraemia in Hospital
Schwartz-Bartter Criteria6 Criteria — All Must Be Met
SIADH vs CSWVolume Status = Key Discriminator (Euvolaemic vs Hypovolaemic)
CSWHypovolaemic + High Urine Na+ + High Urine Osm → Saline + Fludrocortisone
First-Line SIADHFluid Restriction <1L/day
Second-LineDemeclocycline 600-1200mg/day (Induces Nephrogenic DI)
Third-LineTolvaptan 15-60mg OD (V2 Antagonist); Max 30 Days (US)
Severe Hyponatraemia3% NaCl 100-150mL/1-2h (Target ↑ Na 4-6 mmol/L/24h)
Correction Rate4-6 mmol/L/24h Max (Avoid Central Pontine Myelinolysis)
CSW vs SIADHCSW = Hypovolaemic; SIADH = Euvolaemic
CSW ManagementIV Saline + Fludrocortisone + Salt Tablets
TolvaptanV2 Antagonist; Max 30 Days (US); Monitor LFTs
DemeclocyclineInduces Nephrogenic DI; Photosensitivity, Nephrotoxicity
Rapid Correction>8-10 mmol/L/24h → Central Pontine Myelinolysis (Osmotic Demyelination)

12. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
SIADH vs CSWVolume Status is Key: SIADH = Euvolaemic; CSW = Hypovolaemic
Urine Na+ in BothBoth Have High Urine Na+ (>20); Volume Status Differentiates
CSW ManagementSaline + Fludrocortisone (Not Fluid Restriction!)
Rapid Correction>8-10 mmol/L/24h → Central Pontine Myelinolysis (Osmotic Demyelination)
DemeclocyclineInduces Nephrogenic DI; Photosensitivity / Nephrotoxicity
TolvaptanV2 Antagonist; Max 30 Days (US); Hepatotoxicity Risk
Fludrocortisone in CSWMineralocorticoid Replacement → Salt Retention
Reset OsmostatStable Mild Hyponatraemia (125-130); No Treatment Needed
Beer PotomaniaLow Solute Intake → Low Urine Osm; Fluid Restriction Works
Tea & Toast DietLow Solute → Hyponatraemia; Improve Nutrition

13. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((SIADH))
    Criteria (Schwartz-Bartter)
      1. Hyponatraemia (<135)
      2. Hypo-osmolality (<275)
      3. Urine Osm >100
      4. Euvolaemic
      5. Urine Na+ >20
      6. Normal Thyroid/Adrenal/Renal
    Aetiology
      Drugs: SSRIs, Carbamazepine, Cyclophosphamide
      Malignancy: SCLC (Most Common)
      CNS: Stroke, Meningitis, Trauma
      Pulmonary: Pneumonia, TB
      Endocrine: Hypothyroidism, Adrenal Insufficiency
    Differential
      SIADH: Euvolaemic
      CSW: Hypovolaemic (Hypovolaemic + High Urine Na+)
      Hypovolaemic: Urine Na+ <20
      Hypervolaemic: Oedema + Hyponatraemia
    Management
      1. Fluid Restriction <1L/day
      2. Demeclocycline 600-1200mg/day
      3. Tolvaptan 15-60mg OD (V2 Antagonist)
      4. Hypertonic Saline (Severe)
    CSW
      Hypovolaemic + High Urine Na+
      Rx: Saline + Fludrocortisone + Salt
    Severe Hyponatraemia
      3% NaCl 100-150mL/1-2h
      Target ↑Na 4-6 mmol/L/24h
      Max 8-10 mmol/L/24h

14. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
SIADH DefinitionEuvolaemic Hyponatraemia + Inappropriate ADH
Schwartz-Bartter 6 CriteriaHyponatraemia, Hypo-osmolality, Urine Osm >100, Euvolaemia, Urine Na+ >20, Normal Endocrine/Renal
SIADH vs CSWVolume Status = Key (Euvolaemic vs Hypovolaemic)
CSW ManagementSaline + Fludrocortisone + Salt (Opposite of SIADH)
First-Line SIADHFluid Restriction <1L/day
DemeclocyclineInduces Nephrogenic DI; 600-1200mg/day; Photosensitivity Risk
TolvaptanV2 Antagonist; 15-60mg OD; Hepatotoxicity Risk; Max 30 Days (US)
Severe Hyponatraemia3% NaCl 100-150mL/1-2h; Target ↑Na 4-6 mmol/L/24h
Rapid Correction LimitMax 8-10 mmol/L/24h (Avoid CPM)
CSW TreatmentVolume Resuscitation + Fludrocortisone (Not Fluid Restrict)
CSW vs SIADHVolume Status: CSW = Hypovolaemic; SIADH = Euvolaemic
Rapid Correction Risk>10 mmol/L/24h → Central Pontine Myelinolysis
Drug CausesSSRIs, Carbamazepine, Cyclophosphamide, MDMA
Malignancy SIADHSCLC (Most Common)
CSW AetiologySAH, TBI, Neurosurgery, CNS Tumours
Tolvaptan MonitoringLFTs (Hepatotoxicity); Na+ q4-6h

15. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Diabetes Insipidus|Diabetes Insipidus]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Diabetes Insipidus]], [[Posterior Pituitary- ADH-Vasopressin]], [[Hyponatraemia]], [[Cerebral Salt Wasting]], [[Adrenal Insufficiency]]

16. MCQs (10)

  1. SIADH diagnostic criteria (Schwartz-Bartter):

A. Hyponatraemia + hypo-osmolality + urine osm >100 + urine Na>20 + euvolaemia + normal thyroid/adrenal/renal

B. High urine output

C. Hypervolaemia

D. High serum osm

E. Low urine sodium

  1. SIADH urine osmolality is typically:

A. >100 mOsm/kg (inappropriately concentrated relative to serum)

B. <100 mOsm/kg

C. Equal to serum

D. >500 mOsm/kg always

E. Variable

  1. Most common cause of SIADH:

A. Drugs (SSRIs, SNRIs, carbamazepine, cyclophosphamide, PPIs, opioids)

B. Malignancy

C. CNS disease

D. Pulmonary disease

E. Post-op

  1. Malignancy-associated SIADH most common:

A. Small cell lung cancer (SCLC) — ADH ectopic production

B. Breast cancer

C. Lymphoma

D. Pancreatic cancer

E. Prostate cancer

  1. SIADH management step 1:

A. Fluid restriction <1-1.5 L/day

B. 3% saline

C. Tolvaptan

D. Demeclocycline

E. Furosemide

  1. Demeclocycline for SIADH:

A. Inhibits ADH action on V2 receptor → nephrogenic DI-like state; onset 3-5 days

B. V2 agonist

C. V1a antagonist

D. Increases ADH

E. Loop diuretic

  1. Tolvaptan (vaptan) in SIADH:

A. V2 antagonist → aquaresis (water excretion without Na+ loss); hepatotoxicity risk

B. V2 agonist

C. V1a agonist

D. Increases ADH

E. Causes hyponatraemia

  1. Acute severe symptomatic hyponatraemia (seizures/coma) in SIADH:

A. 3% NaCl 100-150mL over 1-2h (target Na+ rise 4-6 mmol/L/24h, max 8 in 24h)

B. Fluid restriction

C. Tolvaptan

D. Demeclocycline

E. Furosemide alone

  1. SIADH vs Cerebral Salt Wasting (CSW):

A. SIADH = euvolaemic; CSW = hypovolaemic + polyuria + high urine Na+

B. Both euvolaemic

C. Both hypovolaemic

D. SIADH = polyuria

E. CSW = low urine Na+

  1. SIADH monitoring on fluid restriction:

A. Daily weights, serum Na+ q1-2d, fluid balance, urine output, urine Na+/osm

B. Weekly Na+ only

C. No monitoring

D. Only weight

E. Only urine output

17. SBA Questions (5)

  1. 70yo woman on sertraline: confused, Na+ 118, euvolaemic, serum osm 250, urine osm 380, urine Na+ 55, normal TSH/cortisol/creatinine. Management?

A. Fluid restriction <1L + stop sertraline; if Na+<120/seizures → 3% saline 100-150mL

B. 3% saline rapid correction

C. Tolvaptan immediately

D. Demeclocycline

E. Furosemide + saline

  1. 60yo man with SCLC: Na+ 122, asymptomatic. Urine osm 450, urine Na+ 60, euvolaemic. Step 1?

A. Fluid restriction 800-1000 mL/day; treat underlying malignancy

B. Tolvaptan

C. Demeclocycline

D. 3% saline

E. Continue fluids

  1. Same patient on fluid restriction: Na+ 120 after 48h. Next?

A. Tolvaptan 15mg OD (titrate to 30-60mg); monitor Na+ q4-6h, LFTs

B. Demeclocycline

C. 3% saline

D. Increase fluid restriction

E. Furosemide

  1. Post-op day 2: hip fracture repair, Na+ 125, oliguric, urine osm 500, urine Na+ 80, JVP low, postural drop. Diagnosis?

A. Cerebral Salt Wasting (hypovolaemic) — NOT SIADH

B. SIADH

C. Reset osmostat

D. Diuretic effect

E. Adrenal insufficiency

  1. Tolvaptan contraindications:

A. Anuria, hypovolaemia, pregnancy, concomitant strong CYP3A4 inhibitors

B. Hypertension

C. Diabetes

D. CKD

E. Liver disease only

18. Flashcards

  • Q: SIADH criteria

A: Hyponatraemia + hypo-osmolality + urine osm >100 + urine Na>20 + euvolaemia + normal TSH/cortisol/renal

  • Q: Pathophysiology

A: Inappropriate ADH → water retention → dilutional hyponatraemia + natriuresis

  • Q: Causes

A: Drugs (SSRIs #1), SCLC, CNS (stroke, TBI), pulmonary (pneumonia), post-op, endocrine (hypothyroid, AI)

  • Q: Diagnosis

A: Exclusion: euvolaemic hyponatraemia + concentrated urine + high urine Na+

  • Q: Fluid restriction

A: 1st line: <1-1.5 L/day; target Na+ rise 1-2 mmol/L/day

  • Q: Demeclocycline

A: Tetracycline; V2 inhibition → nephrogenic DI; 150mg QDS; onset 3-5d; photosensitivity, nephrotoxicity

  • Q: Tolvaptan

A: V2 antagonist; aquaresis; 15-60mg OD; monitor Na+ q4-6h, LFTs; hepatotoxicity; max 30d US

  • Q: Acute severe

A: Seizures/coma: 3% NaCl 100-150mL/1-2h; target ↑4-6 mmol/L/24h (max 8); avoid overcorrection

  • Q: CSW vs SIADH

A: CSW: hypovolaemic + polyuria + high urine Na+ (post-neurosurg/SAH/TBI); Rx: IV saline + fludrocortisone

  • Q: Reset osmostat

A: SIADH variant: regulates at lower set-point; stable mild hyponatraemia; no treatment needed

  • Q: Drug SIADH

A: SSRIs/SNRIs most common; carbamazepine, PPIs, opioids, cyclophosphamide

  • Q: Malignancy

A: SCLC (ADH ectopic); monitor Na+ during chemo; treat cancer = treat SIADH

  • Q: Monitoring

A: Daily weights, serum Na+ q1-2d, fluid balance, urine output, urine Na+/osm

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Hyponatraemia + hypo-osmolality + urine osm >100 + urine Na>20 + euvolaemia + normal thyroid/adrenal/renal — SIADH diagnostic criteria (Schwartz-Bartter):
  1. >100 mOsm/kg (inappropriately concentrated relative to serum) — SIADH urine osmolality is typically:
  1. Drugs (SSRIs, SNRIs, carbamazepine, cyclophosphamide, PPIs, opioids) — Most common cause of SIADH:
  1. Small cell lung cancer (SCLC) — ADH ectopic production — Malignancy-associated SIADH most common:
  1. Fluid restriction <1-1.5 L/day — SIADH management step 1:
  1. Inhibits ADH action on V2 receptor → nephrogenic DI-like state; onset 3-5 days — Demeclocycline for SIADH:
  1. V2 antagonist → aquaresis (water excretion without Na+ loss); hepatotoxicity risk — Tolvaptan (vaptan) in SIADH:
  1. 3% NaCl 100-150mL over 1-2h (target Na+ rise 4-6 mmol/L/24h, max 8 in 24h) — Acute severe symptomatic hyponatraemia (seizures/coma) in SIADH:
  1. SIADH = euvolaemic; CSW = hypovolaemic + polyuria + high urine Na+ — SIADH vs Cerebral Salt Wasting (CSW):
  1. Daily weights, serum Na+ q1-2d, fluid balance, urine output, urine Na+/osm — SIADH monitoring on fluid restriction:

SBAs

  1. Fluid restriction <1L + stop sertraline; if Na+<120/seizures → 3% saline 100-150mL — 70yo woman on sertraline: confused, Na+ 118, euvolaemic, serum osm 250, urine osm 380, urine Na+ 55, normal TSH/cortisol/creatinine. Management?
  1. Fluid restriction 800-1000 mL/day; treat underlying malignancy — 60yo man with SCLC: Na+ 122, asymptomatic. Urine osm 450, urine Na+ 60, euvolaemic. Step 1?
  1. Tolvaptan 15mg OD (titrate to 30-60mg); monitor Na+ q4-6h, LFTs — Same patient on fluid restriction: Na+ 120 after 48h. Next?
  1. Cerebral Salt Wasting (hypovolaemic) — NOT SIADH — Post-op day 2: hip fracture repair, Na+ 125, oliguric, urine osm 500, urine Na+ 80, JVP low, postural drop. Diagnosis?
  1. Anuria, hypovolaemia, pregnancy, concomitant strong CYP3A4 inhibitors — Tolvaptan contraindications:
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.6.06 Tsh Secreting Adenoma
[!info]
TSH-oma = TSH-Secreting Pituitary Adenoma. Rare (1-2% of Pituitary Adenomas). Causes Central Hyperthyroidism: fT4/fT3 ↑ with Inappropriately Normal/High TSH. TRH Test: Paradoxical TSH Rise. Surgery = 1st Line.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise clinical presentation of central hyperthyroidism (thyrotoxicosis with non-suppressed TSH)
  • [ ] Differentiate TSH-oma from Graves disease and thyroid hormone resistance (RTH)
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic criteria (TSH inappropriately normal/high with elevated fT4/fT3)
  • [ ] Apply TRH stimulation test and MRI pituitary for diagnosis
  • [ ] Outline management (surgery 1st line, somatostatin analogues, radiotherapy)

2. Epidemiology & Pathophysiology

FeatureDetails
Frequency1-2% of Pituitary Adenomas
DemographicsAdults (Peak 40-50y); No Sex Predilection
Cell OriginThyrotroph Adenoma (TSH-Secreting)
GeneticsGNAS (Activating), GNA11, USP8, ATP1A1; MEN1 (Rare)
SecretionTSH + α-Subunit (Often Co-Secretion)
Feedback ResistanceImpaired Negative Feedback — TSH Fails to Suppress with High fT4/fT3

3. Clinical Presentation

FeatureTSH-omaGraves Disease
Thyrotoxic SymptomsWeight Loss, Tachycardia, Tremor, Heat IntoleranceIdentical
GoitrePresent (Usually)Present
OphthalmopathyAbsent (Rare)Present (25-50%)
Dermopathy/AcropachyAbsentMay Be Present
TSHInappropriately Normal/HighSuppressed (<0.01 mIU/L)
TRAbNegativePositive (>95%)
α-SubunitElevated (Co-Secreted)Normal
TRH TestParadoxical TSH RiseBlunted (No Rise)

4. Biochemical Profile

ParameterTSH-omaGraves DiseaseRTH (THRβ Mutation)
TSHNormal / High (Inappropriately Normal)Suppressed (<0.01)Normal / High
fT4 / fT3ElevatedElevatedElevated
TRAbNegativePositive (>95%)Negative
α-SubunitElevated (Co-Secreted)NormalNormal
α-Subunit/TSH Ratio>1.0 (Often >1.0)<1.0Variable
TRH Test (100µg IV)Paradoxical TSH Rise (>2mIU/L or >50% ↑)Blunted (No Rise)Exaggerated (Central Resistance)
RAI UptakeDiffuse ↑Diffuse ↑Normal/↑
MRI PituitaryMacroadenoma (Usually >10mm)NormalNormal

5. Diagnostic Algorithm

Thyrotoxicosis (fT4/fT3 ↑ + Symptoms)
         │
         ▼
TSH LEVEL
         │
         ├── TSH SUPPRESSED (<0.01) → **GRAVES DISEASE** (TRAb+)
         │
         └── TSH NORMAL / HIGH → CENTRAL HYPERTHYROIDISM
                 │
                 ├── TRAb POSITIVE → GRAVES (Rare Co-Existence)
                 │
                 ├── TRAb NEGATIVE + **TSH NORMAL/HIGH**
                 │       │
                 │       ├── **TRH TEST: PARADOXICAL TSH RISE** → TSH-OMA
                 │       │
                 │       └── **MRI PITUITARY** → Macroadenoma (>10mm Usually)
                 │
                 └── **THRA MUTATION (RTH)** → TSH Normal/High + fT4/fT3 ↑ + TRAb Negative + FHx

6. Diagnostic Criteria (Consensus)

CriterionTSH-oma
TSHInappropriately Normal/High (≥1.0 mIU/L) with fT4/fT3 ↑
fT4/fT3Elevated
TRAbNegative
α-SubunitElevated (Often >TSH)
α-Subunit/TSH Ratio>1.0 (Often >>1.0)
TRH TestParadoxical TSH Rise (>2 mIU/L or >50% Rise)
MRI PituitaryMacroadenoma (>10mm) Usually

7. TRH Stimulation Test

ProtocolDetails
DoseTRH 100-200µg IV Bolus
SamplingTSH at 0, 20, 40, 60, 90, 120min
Normal ResponseTSH Peak 5-15 mIU/L at 20-30min; Return to Baseline by 60-120min
TSH-oma ResponseParadoxical Rise → TSH ↑↑ (Often >50% Rise or >2 mIU/L Above Baseline)
GravesBlunted/No Rise (TSH Suppressed)
RTHExaggerated Rise (Central Resistance)

8. Diagnosis & Localisation

MRI Pituitary (Gold Standard)

FeatureFinding
LesionMacroadenoma (>10mm) in >90%
EnhancementHypointense on T1 Post-Gad (Delayed Enhancement vs Normal Pituitary)
ExtensionSuprasellar Extension, Cavernous Sinus Invasion (Knosp Grading)

α-Subunit

FeatureSignificance
ElevatedCo-Secreted with TSH by Thyrotroph Adenoma
α-Subunit/TSH Ratio>1.0 (Often >>1.0) — Highly Suggestive

9. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionTSHfT4/fT3TRAbα-SubunitTRH Test
TSH-omaNormal/HighHighNegativeHighParadoxical Rise
GravesSuppressed (<0.01)HighPositiveNormalBlunted
RTH (THRβ)Normal/HighHighNegativeNormalExaggerated Rise
T3 ThyrotoxicosisSuppressedfT3↑, fT4 NormalNegativeNormalBlunted

10. Management

First-Line: Transsphenoidal Surgery (TSS)

AspectDetails
IndicationAll TSH-omas (Micro/Macro)
ApproachEndoscopic Endonasal TSS (Preferred)
GoalGross Total Resection (GTR)
Cure CriteriaTSH Normalisation + fT4/fT3 Normalisation

Surgery Outcomes

Tumour SizeGTR RateRemission Rate
Microadenoma (<10mm)80-90%70-80%
Macroadenoma (>10mm)50-70%40-60%

Pre-Op Medical Preparation

AgentDosePurpose
Somatostatin Analogues (Octreotide/Lanreotide)Standard DosesPre-Op ↓ TSH → ↓ Vascularity; ↑ Resection Rate
Beta-BlockersPropranolol 40-80mg QDSSymptomatic Control (Tachycardia)
Antithyroid DrugsNOT Routine (May Worsen Goitre)

11. Medical Therapy (If Surgery Not Feasible / Residual Disease)

Somatostatin Analogues (1st Line Medical)

AgentDoseRegimen
Octreotide LAR20-40mg IMq4 Weeks
Lanreotide Autogel60-120mg SCq4 Weeks
Pasireotide LAR40-60mg IMq4 Weeks (2nd Line; ↑ Hyperglycaemia Risk)
EffectDetails
TSH Suppression50-70% Normalise TSH/fT4
Tumour Shrinkage30-50% Volume Reduction
α-SubunitNormalises with TSH

Dopamine Agonists (Adjunct)

DrugDoseEfficacy
Cabergoline0.5-2mg BDModest TSH Suppression (30-40%)
Bromocriptine2.5-15mg/dayLess Effective

12. Radiotherapy

IndicationModalityDose
Residual/Recurrent Post-TSSStereotactic Radiosurgery (SRS)15-25 Gy (Single Fraction)
Unresectable / RecurrentFractionated RT45-50 Gy / 25-28 Fractions
Latency5-10 Years for Biochemical Control

13. Post-Operative Follow-up

TimepointInvestigations
Day 1TSH, fT4, fT3, α-Subunit
Week 1-2TSH, fT4, fT3, α-Subunit
6 WeeksTSH, fT4, fT3, α-Subunit, MRI
6 MonthsFull Panel + MRI
Annual (Lifelong)TSH, fT4, fT3, α-Subunit, MRI (Yearly ×5yr, then 2-3yrly)

Remission Criteria

ParameterTarget
TSHNormalised (Within Reference Range)
fT4/fT3Normal
α-SubunitNormalised

14. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
TSH-oma Prevalence1-2% of Pituitary Adenomas
Key Biochemical FeatureTSH Inappropriately Normal/High with fT4/fT3 ↑
TRAbNegative (Differentiates from Graves)
α-SubunitMarkedly Elevated (Co-Secreted); α/TSH Ratio >1
TRH TestParadoxical TSH Rise (>50% Rise or >2mIU/L Rise)
TSH-oma vs GravesTSH-oma: TSH Normal/High + TRAb (-); Graves: TSH Suppressed + TRAb (+)
TSH-oma vs RTHTSH-oma: Pituitary Adenoma on MRI; RTH: THRβ Mutation, No Pituitary Mass
α-Subunit/TSH Ratio>1.0 Suggests TSH-oma
MRIMacroadenoma >10mm (Usually); Hypointense on T1 Post-Gad
1st Line TreatmentTranssphenoidal Surgery (TSS)
Medical TherapySomatostatin Analogues (Octreotide/Lanreotide) 1st Line; Cabergoline Adjunct
Radiotherapy2nd Line (Residual/Recurrent); Delayed Effect (5-10yr)
TRH TestParadoxical TSH Rise = TSH-oma; Blunted = Graves; Exaggerated = RTH
α-SubunitCo-Secreted with TSH; ↑ in TSH-oma
TSH-oma vs RTHRTH: THRβ Mutation, Generalised Resistance, Goitre, Tachycardia

15. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
TSH-oma vs GravesTSH-oma: TSH Normal/High + TRAb(-); Graves: TSH Suppressed + TRAb+
TSH-oma vs RTHTSH-oma: Pituitary Adenoma; RTH: THRβ Mutation, No Pituitary Mass
TRH TestTSH-oma: ↑ TSH (Paradoxical); Graves: Blunted; RTH: Exaggerated
α-Subunit Ratioα/TSH >1.0 = TSH-oma; <1.0 = Normal/Other
α-Subunit SourceCo-Secreted with TSH by Thyrotroph Adenoma
Medical RxSomatostatin Analogues 1st Line (Octreotide/Lanreotide)
CabergolineModest TSH Suppression (30-40%); Adjunct
RadiotherapyDelayed Effect (5-10yr); For Residual/Recurrent

16. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((TSH-Secreting Adenoma))
    Epidemiology
      Rare (1-2% Pituitary Adenomas)
      Adults 40-50y; No Sex Predilection
    Pathophysiology
      Thyrotroph Adenoma
      TSH + α-Subunit Co-Secretion
      Impaired Negative Feedback (TRH/Thyroid Hormone)
    Clinical
      Thyrotoxicosis (Weight Loss, Tachycardia, Tremor)
      Goitre (Usually Present)
      **NO Ophthalmopathy** (vs Graves)
      **NO Dermopathy/Acropachy**
    Biochemistry
      TSH Normal/High (Inappropriately)
      fT4/fT3 ↑
      TRAb Negative
      α-Subunit ↑↑ (α/TSH >1)
    TRH Test
      Paradoxical TSH Rise (>50% or >2mIU/L)
    MRI
      Macroadenoma >10mm (90%)
      Hypointense T1 Post-Gad
      Suprasellar Extension
    Treatment
      1st Line: TSS (Micro 80-90%; Macro 50-60%)
      Medical: SST Analogues (Octreotide/Lanreotide) 1st Line
      Adjunct: Cabergoline
      RT: Residual/Recurrent
    Differential
      Graves: TSH Suppressed, TRAb+
      RTH: THRβ Mutation, No Pituitary Mass
      T3 Thyrotoxicosis: TSH Suppressed

17. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
TSH-oma Frequency1-2% of Pituitary Adenomas
TSH in TSH-omaInappropriately Normal/High (Not Suppressed)
TRAbNegative (Differentiates from Graves)
α-SubunitElevated; α/TSH Ratio >1
TRH TestParadoxical TSH Rise (>50% or >2mIU/L Rise)
MRIMacroadenoma >10mm (90%); Hypointense T1 Post-Gad
TSH-oma vs GravesTSH Normal/High + TRAb(-) vs TSH Suppressed + TRAb(+)
TSH-oma vs RTHTSH-oma = Pituitary Adenoma; RTH = THRβ Mutation, No Mass
α-Subunit/TSH Ratio>1.0 = TSH-oma
TRH Test TSH-omaParadoxical Rise (>50% or >2mIU/L)
1st Line TreatmentTranssphenoidal Surgery
Medical TherapySomatostatin Analogues 1st Line; Cabergoline Adjunct
RadiotherapyResidual/Recurrent; 5-10yr Latency
α-SubunitCo-Secreted with TSH; Markedly Elevated in TSH-oma

18. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Pituitary Adenomas- Functioning|Pituitary Adenomas- Functioning]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Pituitary Adenomas: Functioning]], [[Graves Disease]], [[Hyperthyroidism Overview]], [[TSH-Thyroid Axis]], [[Pituitary Adenomas: Functioning/TSH-Secreting Adenoma]]

19. MCQs (10)

  1. TSH-secreting pituitary adenoma (TSHoma) prevalence:

A. ~1-2% of pituitary adenomas; rare

B. 10-15%

C. 30-40%

D. 50%

E. Most common

  1. TSHoma biochemical hallmark:

A. Inappropriately normal/elevated TSH with elevated fT4/fT3 (loss of negative feedback)

B. Low TSH + high fT4

C. High TSH + low fT4

D. Normal TSH + normal fT4

E. Undetectable TSH

  1. Alpha-subunit in TSHoma:

A. Elevated; α-subunit:TSH molar ratio >1 (normal <1)

B. Low

C. Normal

D. Only TSH elevated

E. Only α-subunit elevated

  1. TSHoma TRH test:

A. Blunted/absent TSH response to TRH (autonomous secretion)

B. Exaggerated response

C. Normal response

D. Prolactin rise

E. GH rise

  1. TSHoma T3 suppression test:

A. Failure of TSH suppression with T3 (autonomous)

B. Normal suppression

C. Paradoxical rise

D. No test available

E. Only for acromegaly

  1. TSHoma imaging:

A. MRI pituitary: macroadenoma >10mm (80%); may have cystic component

B. Normal pituitary

C. Microadenoma only

D. No imaging needed

E. CT only

  1. TSHoma treatment 1st line:

A. Transsphenoidal surgery (TSS); gross total resection 60-70%

B. Somatostatin analogues

C. Thionamides

D. Radioactive iodine

E. Observation

  1. Pre-op preparation for TSHoma surgery:

A. Euthyroid state: thionamides (carbimazole) ± beta-blocker ± potassium iodide (short-term)

B. SST analogues only

C. RAI

D. No preparation

E. Steroids only

  1. Somatostatin analogues for TSHoma:

A. Octreotide/lanreotide: ↓ TSH, ↓ thyroid hormones; tumour shrinkage in 30-50%

B. No effect

C. Worsens hyperthyroidism

D. Only for acromegaly

E. Only for carcinoid

  1. TSHoma vs Resistance to Thyroid Hormone (RTH):

A. TSHoma: pituitary tumour, α-subunit↑, α:TSH>1, TRH blunted; RTH: genetic (THRB), goitre, tachycardia, normal pituitary

B. Same

C. RTH has pituitary tumour

D. TSHoma genetic

E. No difference

20. SBA Questions (5)

  1. 45yo woman: weight loss, tremor, tachycardia, fT4 35, fT3 12, TSH 2.5 (inappropriately normal). MRI: 1.2cm pituitary macroadenoma. α-subunit:TSH ratio 2.5. Diagnosis?

A. TSH-secreting pituitary adenoma (TSHoma)

B. Graves disease

C. Thyroid hormone resistance (RTH)

D. T3 toxicosis

E. Factitious thyrotoxicosis

  1. Same patient: pre-op preparation for TSS?

A. Carbimazole 20-40mg/day + propranolol until euthyroid; short-term KI (7-10d) pre-op

B. RAI

C. Octreotide only

D. No preparation

E. Thyroidectomy first

  1. TSHoma post-TSS: residual tumour, TSH 5, fT4 28. Next?

A. Somatostatin analogue (octreotide LAR/lanreotide) → consider re-op or RT if progressive

B. Carbimazole long-term

C. RAI

D. Observation

E. Tyrosine kinase inhibitor

  1. TSHoma vs RTH: key discriminating test:

A. α-subunit:TSH molar ratio (>1 in TSHoma, <1 in RTH); TRH test (blunted in TSHoma); pituitary MRI

B. TSH level only

C. fT4 only

D. Family history only

E. Genetic testing only

  1. TSHoma genetic association:

A. GNAS mutations (rare); MEN1 (rare); mostly sporadic

B. RET

C. MEN1 always

D. VHL

E. SDHx

21. Flashcards

  • Q: TSHoma prevalence

A: 1-2% of pituitary adenomas; rare

  • Q: Biochemical hallmark

A: Inappropriately normal/↑ TSH with ↑ fT4/fT3 (lost negative feedback)

  • Q: Alpha-subunit

A: Elevated; α:TSH molar ratio >1 (normal <1)

  • Q: TRH test

A: Blunted/absent TSH response (autonomous secretion)

  • Q: T3 suppression

A: Failure of TSH suppression with T3

  • Q: Imaging

A: MRI: macroadenoma >10mm (80%); may be cystic

  • Q: Surgery 1st line

A: TSS; GTR 60-70%; remission: normal TSH + normal fT4

  • Q: Pre-op prep

A: Euthyroid: carbimazole + propranolol ± KI (7-10d)

  • Q: SST analogues

A: Octreotide/lanreotide: ↓ TSH, ↓ thyroid hormones; shrinkage 30-50%

  • Q: RTH vs TSHoma

A: RTH: THRB mutation, goitre, tachycardia, normal pituitary, α:TSH<1, TRH normal

  • Q: Genetics

A: Mostly sporadic; rare GNAS/MEN1

  • Q: Surveillance

A: TSH, fT4, α-subunit q6-12mo; MRI annually ×5yr

22. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. ~1-2% of pituitary adenomas; rare — TSH-secreting pituitary adenoma (TSHoma) prevalence:
  1. Inappropriately normal/elevated TSH with elevated fT4/fT3 (loss of negative feedback) — TSHoma biochemical hallmark:
  1. Elevated; α-subunit:TSH molar ratio >1 (normal <1) — Alpha-subunit in TSHoma:
  1. Blunted/absent TSH response to TRH (autonomous secretion) — TSHoma TRH test:
  1. Failure of TSH suppression with T3 (autonomous) — TSHoma T3 suppression test:
  1. MRI pituitary: macroadenoma >10mm (80%); may have cystic component — TSHoma imaging:
  1. Transsphenoidal surgery (TSS); gross total resection 60-70% — TSHoma treatment 1st line:
  1. Euthyroid state: thionamides (carbimazole) ± beta-blocker ± potassium iodide (short-term) — Pre-op preparation for TSHoma surgery:
  1. Octreotide/lanreotide: ↓ TSH, ↓ thyroid hormones; tumour shrinkage in 30-50% — Somatostatin analogues for TSHoma:
  1. TSHoma: pituitary tumour, α-subunit↑, α:TSH>1, TRH blunted; RTH: genetic (THRB), goitre, tachycardia, normal pituitary — TSHoma vs Resistance to Thyroid Hormone (RTH):

SBAs

  1. TSH-secreting pituitary adenoma (TSHoma) — 45yo woman: weight loss, tremor, tachycardia, fT4 35, fT3 12, TSH 2.5 (inappropriately normal). MRI: 1.2cm pituitary macroadenoma. α-subunit:TSH ratio 2.5. Diagnosis?
  1. Carbimazole 20-40mg/day + propranolol until euthyroid; short-term KI (7-10d) pre-op — Same patient: pre-op preparation for TSS?
  1. Somatostatin analogue (octreotide LAR/lanreotide) → consider re-op or RT if progressive — TSHoma post-TSS: residual tumour, TSH 5, fT4 28. Next?
  1. α-subunit:TSH molar ratio (>1 in TSHoma, <1 in RTH); TRH test (blunted in TSHoma); pituitary MRI — TSHoma vs RTH: key discriminating test:
  1. GNAS mutations (rare); MEN1 (rare); mostly sporadic — TSHoma genetic association:
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.7 20.7 6
▸ 20.7.01 Contraception
[!info]
Contraception = Prevention of Pregnancy. UKMEC (Medical Eligibility Criteria) guides safe prescribing. LARC (Long-Acting Reversible Contraception) = First-Line for Most Women. Tiered Efficacy: LARC > COCP/POP > Barrier > Natural Methods.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply UKMEC categories for contraceptive eligibility
  • [ ] Compare efficacy, mechanisms, and side effects of all methods
  • [ ] Select appropriate method based on patient factors (age, BMI, medical history, preferences)
  • [ ] Manage emergency contraception
  • [ ] Counsel on non-contraceptive benefits and side effects

2. Contraceptive Efficacy — Tiered by Typical Use Failure Rate

TierMethodTypical Failure Rate (1st Year)Perfect Use Failure Rate
Tier 1 (LARC)Implant (ENG)0.05%0.05%
IUS (LNG 52mg)0.2%0.2%
Copper IUD0.8%0.6%
Injectable (DMPA)3-6%0.2%
Tier 2COCP7-9%0.3%
POP (Desogestrel)7-9%0.3%
Patch / Ring7-9%0.3%
Tier 3Male Condom13-18%2%
Female Condom21%5%
Diaphragm/Cap17%4%
Tier 4Fertility Awareness12-24%1-5%
Withdrawal20-22%4%
Spermicides21-28%18%
LARC = First-Line for Most Women (Highest Efficacy, Long Duration, User-Independent)

3. UKMEC (Medical Eligibility Criteria) — Key Categories

CategoryDefinitionClinical Action
UKMEC 1No RestrictionUse Without Restriction
UKMEC 2Advantages > RisksGenerally Use
UKMEC 3Risks > AdvantagesGenerally Avoid (Unless Other Methods Unacceptable)
UKMEC 4Unacceptable RiskDo Not Use

Key UKMEC 4 Contraindications (Selected)

ConditionCOCPPOPImplantIUS/IUDInjectable
Breast Cancer (Current)44444
VTE (Current/History)42223
Stroke / TIA42223
Ischaemic Heart Disease42223
Uncontrolled HTN (≥160/100)42223
Migraine with Aura42222
Smoker ≥35, ≥15/day42222
Major Surgery (Immobility)41112
Postpartum <21 days (Non-Breastfeeding)41111
Postpartum <21 days (Breastfeeding)41111
Postpartum 21-42 days (Breastfeeding)31111
Liver Tumour43333
Acute Liver Disease42223

4. Contraceptive Methods — Quick Reference

Long-Acting Reversible Contraception (LARC)

MethodMechanismDurationPearl Index (Typical)Key Features
Copper IUDCu²⁺ Toxic to Sperm/Ova; Endometrial Inflammation5-10 Years0.6-0.8%Non-Hormonal; Heavy Periods; Emergency Contraception
LNG-IUS (52mg)LNG Release → Endometrial Suppression5 Years0.2%↓ Menstrual Bleeding; Local Progestogen; LNG 20µg/day
LNG-IUS (19.5mg/13.5mg)Lower Dose3-5 Years0.2%Smaller Frame; Less Bleeding
Implant (ENG 68mg)ENG Release → Oestrogen Suppression3 Years0.05%Subdermal; Predictable Bleeding
DMPA (Injectable)MPA → Ovulation Suppression; Endometrial Thinning12-13 Weeks3-6%Weight Gain; Delayed Fertility Return (9-10mo)

5. Combined Oral Contraceptive Pill (COCP)

AspectDetails
CompositionEE 20-35µg + Progestogen (Levonorgestrel, Norethisterone, Desogestrel, Gestodene, Drospirenone, Dienogest)
Regimen21 Days On / 7 Days Off (Withdrawal Bleed)
Extended RegimensTricycling (63/7), Continuous (No Break)
MechanismSuppress Ovulation (E2 → ↓ FSH/LH); Endometrial Thinning; Cervical Mucus Thickening
Failure Rate7-9% (Typical); 0.3% (Perfect)

COCP — Non-Contraceptive Benefits

BenefitEvidence
Menstrual RegulationRegular, Lighter, Less Painful Periods
Acne / HirsutismDrospirenone/Dienogest/Cyproterone (Anti-Androgenic)
Endometrial Cancer↓ 50% (Long-Term)
Ovarian Cancer↓ 40-50% (Long-Term)
Colorectal Cancer↓ 20%
PID↓ Risk
Functional Ovarian Cysts↓ Incidence

COCP — Risks (UKMEC 3/4)

RiskMagnitudeMitigation
VTE3-6x (Baseline 5-10/100,000 → 20-40/100,000)Transdermal Preferred if Risk Factors
Stroke1.5-2x (Ischaemic)Avoid if Migraine with Aura
MISlight ↑Avoid if CVD
Breast CancerSlight ↑ (Current/Recent Use)↓ After Stopping
Cervical CancerSlight ↑ (HPV Co-Factor)HPV Vaccination
Benefits vs RisksNet Benefit for Most Women <35, Non-Smoker

COCP Contraindications (UKMEC 4)

ConditionRationale
Current/Recent Breast CancerOestrogen-Dependent Tumour
VTE (Current/History)↑ Thrombotic Risk
Stroke / TIA / MIThrombotic Risk
Uncontrolled HTN (≥160/100)↑ Stroke/MI Risk
Migraine with Aura↑ Stroke Risk (2-4x)
Smoker ≥35y, >15/daySynergistic VTE Risk
Major Surgery (Immobility)Post-Op VTE Risk
Major Surgery (Planned)Stop 4 Weeks Pre-Op
Liver Tumour / Severe Liver DiseaseMetabolism Impaired

6. Progestogen-Only Pill (POP)

FeatureDetails
TypesDesogestrel 75µg (Newer, Ovulation Inhibition); Traditional (Norethisterone 350µg, Levonorgestrel 30µg) — Cervical Mucus Effect
MechanismDesogestrel: Ovulation Inhibition (97%); Traditional: Cervical Mucus + Endometrial Thinning
RegimenStrict Timing (Same Time Daily; Desogestrel 12h Window)
Bleeding PatternUnpredictable (Amenorrhoea, Spotting, Prolonged Bleeding)
UKMEC 4Breast Cancer, Undiagnosed Bleeding, Severe Liver Disease

7. Emergency Contraception (EC)

MethodWindowEfficacyDetails
Cu-IUD≤5 Days (120h)>99% (Most Effective)First-Line; Also Ongoing Contraception
Ulipristal Acetate (UPA 30mg)≤120h (5 Days)~98% (More Effective Than LNG)Prescription; Delay Ovulation
Levonorgestrel (LNG 1.5mg)≤72h (3 Days)~95% (Within 24h)OTC; Less Effective After 72h

EC Decision Algorithm

Unprotected Intercourse / Contraceptive Failure
         │
         ▼
Time Since Intercourse
         │
         ├── ≤120h (5 Days) → **Cu-IUD** (1st Line) → If Unsuitable/Unavailable → UPA 30mg
         │
         └── ≤72h (3 Days) → **LNG 1.5mg** (If UPA Unavailable/Contraindicated)
         │
         ▼
Post-EC Care
         │
         ├── Pregnancy Test if Next Period Delayed >7 Days
         ├── Start/Resume Regular Contraception Immediately
         ├── Cu-IUD → Ongoing Contraception
         └── STI Screen if Indicated

8. Male Contraception

MethodMechanismEfficacyStatus
Condom (Male)Barrier82-98% (Typical)Only User-Controlled Male Method
VasectomyVas Deferens Occlusion>99% (Permanent)Surgical; Reversal Possible (50-70% Success)
HormonalTestosterone + Progestogen (Gel/Injectable)Trials OngoingNot Yet Licensed (Phase 3)

9. Quick Reference — Method Selection

Patient ProfileFirst-Line ChoiceAlternative
Nulliparous, No Risk FactorsImplant or IUSCu-IUD
Postpartum (Breastfeeding)Implant or POP or Cu-IUD (After 4wks)POP
Postpartum (Non-Breastfeeding)Implant, Cu-IUD, DMPAPOP
Post-AbortionAll Methods (Including IUD Immediate)—
Postpartum <21 Days (Non-BF)Cu-IUD, Implant, DMPA, POPCOCP (UKMEC 4)
Postpartum <21 Days (BF)Implant, POP, Cu-IUDDMPA (UKMEC 2)
Obesity (BMI ≥30)Implant, Cu-IUD, IUSPOP, DMPA
AdolescentImplant, Cu-IUD, IUSPOP, Condoms
HIV (On ART)Most Methods OK; Check Interactions (Rifampicin, EFV)POP if EFV
Breast CancerCu-IUD, POP, LNG-IUS (Caution)
VTE HistoryCu-IUD, POP, LNG-IUS (Caution)COCP, DMPA (UKMEC 3-4)
Smoker >35, >15/dayCu-IUD, POP, Implant, LNG-IUSCOCP (UKMEC 4)

10. Contraception After Pregnancy Events

EventTimingMethods
Delivery (Non-BF)ImmediateAll Methods (COPC UKMEC 4 <21d)
Delivery (Breastfeeding)Immediate (POP, Implant, Cu-IUD)DMPA (21-42d UKMEC 2)
Delivery <21d (BF)DMPA (UKMEC 2)
Delivery 21-42d (BF)DMPA (UKMEC 2)
Post-AbortionImmediateAll Methods (Including IUD)
Post-EctopicAfter hCG <5All Methods
Post-MiscarriageImmediateAll Methods

11. Follow-Up & Counselling

Initiation Counselling

TopicKey Points
MechanismHow It Works
EffectivenessPearl Index (Typical vs Perfect)
Side EffectsCommon (Bleeding Changes, Weight, Mood, Headache)
Warning SignsWhen to Seek Help (Severe Pain, Heavy Bleeding, Signs of VTE)
Follow-Up3-6 Month Review (Bleeding Pattern, Side Effects, Satisfaction)
STI ProtectionCondoms Only (Dual Protection)

Common Side Effects & Management

Side EffectMethodManagement
Irregular BleedingPOP, Implant, IUS, DMPAReassurance; Mefenamic Acid (IUS); Switch if Persistent >6mo
Weight GainDMPA (Mean +2-3kg)Counselling; Switch if >5kg
Mood ChangesCOCP, POP, DMPASwitch Method; SSRI if Depressive
AcneCOCP (Anti-Androgenic Progestogen)Switch Progestogen
AmenorrhoeaIUS, Implant, DMPAReassurance (Physiological)

12. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
LARC = First-LineImplant, IUS, Cu-IUD; Highest Efficacy, Lowest User-Dependence
COCP Failure Rate7-9% Typical; 0.3% Perfect Use
Cu-IUDMost Effective EC (≤5 Days); 10-Year Duration
UPA (UPA 30mg)120h Window (vs LNG 72h); More Effective
LNG-EC1.5mg Single Dose; ≤72h (Best ≤24h)
Cu-IUD Post-AbortionImmediate Insertion Safe & Effective
DMPADelayed Fertility Return (Median 9-10 Months)
UKMEC 4 COCPBreast Ca, VTE, Stroke, MI, Uncontrolled HTN, Migraine with Aura, Smoker ≥35 >15/day
UKMEC 4 POPBreast Cancer, Unexplained Bleeding, Severe Liver Disease
DMPA UKMECBreast Cancer (4), Active VTE (3), Stroke/MI (3), Severe Liver (3)
POP TimingStrict (Desogestrel 12h Window; Traditional 3h Window)
Cu-IUDEmergency Contraception ≤5 Days; 10-Year Duration
LNG-IUS52mg (5yr), 19.5mg (3-5yr); ↓ Menstrual Bleeding; Local Progestogen
Implant68mg ENG; 3 Years; 0.05% Failure
Vasectomy>99% Effective; Permanent; Reversal 50-70%
COCP VTE Risk3-6x Baseline; Transdermal ↓ Risk
COPC Migraine AuraUKMEC 4 (Contraindicated)
Postpartum ECCu-IUD (1st Line), UPA 30mg (120h), LNG-EC (72h)
UKMEC Categories1=No Restriction, 2=Advantages>Risks, 3=Risks>Advantages, 4=Unacceptable

13. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
LARC vs Non-LARCLARC = Implant, IUS, Cu-IUD; Set-and-Forget; Non-LARC = User-Dependent
COCP vs POPCOCP = Oestrogen + Progestogen (Suppress Ovulation); POP = Progestogen Only (Cervical Mucus/Endometrium)
Emergency ContraceptionCu-IUD = Most Effective (≤5 Days); UPA = 120h; LNG = 72h
DMPA DelayFertility Return 9-10 Months (Not Immediate)
IUD vs IUSCu-IUD = Copper; LNG-IUS = Levonorgestrel-Releasing
POP TimingDesogestrel: 12h Window; Traditional POP: 3h Window
UKMEC Categories1=Use, 2=Use, 3=Avoid, 4=Do Not Use
EC WindowCu-IUD ≤5 Days; UPA ≤5 Days; LNG ≤72h (Best ≤24h)
DMPA DelayReturn to Fertility 9-10 Months (Plan Ahead)
Breastfeeding + COCPSuppresses Lactation; Avoid <6 Weeks
Vasectomy>99% Effective; Reversal 50-70% (Not Guaranteed)

14. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Most Effective ContraceptionLARC (Implant, IUS, Cu-IUD) — Failure Rate <1%
Cu-IUDEmergency Contraception ≤5 Days; 10-Year Duration
UPA (EllaOne)120h Window; More Effective Than LNG-EC
LNG-EC72h Window (Best ≤24h); Less Effective Than UPA
Cu-IUD Post-AbortionImmediate Insertion Safe & Effective
DMPADelayed Fertility Return (9-10 Months Median)
COCP UKMEC 4Breast Ca, VTE, Stroke, MI, Uncontrolled HTN, Migraine with Aura, Smoker ≥35 >15/day
POP UKMEC 4Breast Cancer, Unexplained Bleeding, Severe Liver Disease
DMPA UKMECVTE/Stroke/MI (3), Breast Ca (4)
POP TimingDesogestrel 12h Window; Traditional 3h
Cu-IUDEC ≤5 Days; 10-Year Contraception
LNG-IUS52mg (5yr), 19.5mg (3-5yr); ↓ Bleeding; Local Effect
ImplantENG 68mg; 3 Years; 0.05% Failure Rate
Vasectomy>99% Effective; Reversal 50-70%; Not Immediate
COCP VTE Risk3-6x Baseline; Transdermal ↓ Risk
Migraine + Aura + COCPUKMEC 4 (Contraindicated)
Postpartum ECCu-IUD (1st), UPA (2nd), LNG (3rd)
UKMEC1=Use, 2=Use, 3=Avoid, 4=Contraindicated

15. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Reproductive Endocrinology|Reproductive Endocrinology]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]

16. MCQs (10)

  1. Combined OCP mechanism:

A. Oestrogen + progestin; inhibits ovulation; thickens cervical mucus; thins endometrium

B. Progestin only

C. Oestrogen only

D. Barrier only

E. Spermicide only

  1. COCP effectiveness:

A. Perfect use: >99%; Typical use: ~91%

B. 100%

C. 50%

D. 80%

E. 70%

  1. COCP contraindications:

A. VTE risk (personal/family), migraine with aura, smoking >35yo, hypertension, liver disease, breast cancer

B. Age <18

C. Nulliparity

D. Smoking <10/day

E. BMI <30

  1. Progestin-only pill:

A. Desogestrel 75mcg daily; inhibits ovulation; safe in breastfeeding; no VTE risk

B. Contains oestrogen

C. Less effective

D. Daily at same time not required

E. Contraindicated in breastfeeding

  1. IUD copper:

A. T-shaped copper device; >99% effective; 5-10yr duration; non-hormonal; increases menstrual bleeding

B. Hormonal

C. Less effective

D. Lasts 2yr

E. Contraindicated in nulliparity

  1. IUD hormonal (LNG-IUS):

A. Levonorgestrel 20mcg/day; >99%; 5-8yr; reduces bleeding; treats menorrhagia

B. Copper

C. Less effective

D. Lasts 2yr

E. Increases bleeding

  1. Implant (etonogestrel):

A. Subdermal rod; 3yr; >99%; inhibits ovulation; may cause irregular bleeding

B. IUD

C. Oral pill

D. Injection

E. Vaginal ring

  1. Depot medroxyprogesterone (DMPA):

A. IM 150mg q12wk; inhibits ovulation; weight gain; bone density loss; delayed return fertility

B. Oral

C. IUD

D. Implant

E. Vaginal ring

  1. Emergency contraception - levonorgestrel:

A. 1.5mg within 72h post-coitus; inhibits/delays ovulation; not abortifacient

B. Within 24h only

C. After 120h

D. Abortifacient

E. Requires prescription

  1. Emergency contraception - ulipristal:

A. 30mg within 120h; more effective than LNG; inhibits ovulation; not in breastfeeding

B. Within 72h only

C. Less effective than LNG

D. Abortifacient

E. OTC

17. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 30yo woman: migraine with aura, wants contraception. Best option?

A. Progestin-only pill or LNG-IUS or copper IUD (no oestrogen)

B. COCP

C. DMPA

D. Vaginal ring

E. Diaphragm

  1. 25yo woman: missed 2 COCP pills in week 1. Advice?

A. Take 2 pills today + 2 tomorrow + condom x7d

B. Skip missed

C. Stop pack

D. Emergency contraception only

E. Continue as normal

  1. 40yo smoker: wants COCP. Risk?

A. VTE risk increased; avoid COCP; offer progestin-only or IUD

B. Safe

C. Reduce cigarettes

D. Low-dose COCP OK

E. Patch preferred

  1. Breastfeeding mother: 6wks pp, wants contraception. Best?

A. Progestin-only pill or LNG-IUS or copper IUD or implant

B. COCP

C. DMPA

D. Vaginal ring

E. Emergency contraception

  1. Woman: copper IUD for emergency contraception. Timeframe?

A. Within 120h (5 days) post-coitus; >99% effective; can remain for ongoing contraception

B. Within 72h only

C. Within 24h

D. After 5 days

E. Not effective

18. Flashcards

  • Q: COCP

A: Oestrogen + progestin; inhibits ovulation; >99% perfect use; contraindicated: VTE, migraine aura, smoking>35

  • Q: POP

A: Desogestrel 75mcg; inhibits ovulation; safe breastfeeding; no VTE risk

  • Q: LNG-IUS

A: Levonorgestrel 20mcg/day; 5-8yr; reduces bleeding; treats menorrhagia

  • Q: Copper IUD

A: Non-hormonal; >99%; 5-10yr; increases bleeding

  • Q: Implant

A: Etonogestrel rod; 3yr; >99%; may cause irregular bleeding

  • Q: DMPA

A: 150mg IM q12wk; weight gain; bone loss; delayed fertility return

  • Q: Emergency contraception

A: LNG 1.5mg 72h; Ulipristal 30mg 120h; not abortifacient

  • Q: Ulipristal

A: 30mg 120h; more effective than LNG; not in breastfeeding

  • Q: Copper IUD EC

A: Within 120h; >99%; ongoing contraception

  • Q: Contraindications COCP

A: VTE, migraine aura, smoking>35, HTN, liver disease, breast cancer

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Oestrogen + progestin; inhibits ovulation; thickens cervical mucus; thins endometrium — Combined OCP mechanism:
  1. Perfect use: >99%; Typical use: ~91% — COCP effectiveness:
  1. VTE risk (personal/family), migraine with aura, smoking >35yo, hypertension, liver disease, breast cancer — COCP contraindications:
  1. Desogestrel 75mcg daily; inhibits ovulation; safe in breastfeeding; no VTE risk — Progestin-only pill:
  1. T-shaped copper device; >99% effective; 5-10yr duration; non-hormonal; increases menstrual bleeding — IUD copper:
  1. Levonorgestrel 20mcg/day; >99%; 5-8yr; reduces bleeding; treats menorrhagia — IUD hormonal (LNG-IUS):
  1. Subdermal rod; 3yr; >99%; inhibits ovulation; may cause irregular bleeding — Implant (etonogestrel):
  1. IM 150mg q12wk; inhibits ovulation; weight gain; bone density loss; delayed return fertility — Depot medroxyprogesterone (DMPA):
  1. 1.5mg within 72h post-coitus; inhibits/delays ovulation; not abortifacient — Emergency contraception - levonorgestrel:
  1. 30mg within 120h; more effective than LNG; inhibits ovulation; not in breastfeeding — Emergency contraception - ulipristal:

SBAs

  1. Progestin-only pill or LNG-IUS or copper IUD (no oestrogen) — 30yo woman: migraine with aura, wants contraception. Best option?
  1. Take 2 pills today + 2 tomorrow + condom x7d — 25yo woman: missed 2 COCP pills in week 1. Advice?
  1. VTE risk increased; avoid COCP; offer progestin-only or IUD — 40yo smoker: wants COCP. Risk?
  1. Progestin-only pill or LNG-IUS or copper IUD or implant — Breastfeeding mother: 6wks pp, wants contraception. Best?
  1. Within 120h (5 days) post-coitus; >99% effective; can remain for ongoing contraception — Woman: copper IUD for emergency contraception. Timeframe?

MCQs

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.7.02 Female Hypogonadism
[!info]
Female Hypogonadism = Ovarian Failure or Insufficient Gonadotropin Drive. Presents as Primary Amenorrhoea (No Menarche by 15) or Secondary Amenorrhoea (Absence of Menses ≥3-6 Months). Primary (Hypergonadotropic) = Ovarian Failure. Secondary (Hypogonadotropic) = Pituitary/Hypothalamic Failure.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Differentiate primary vs secondary hypogonadism using FSH/LH
  • [ ] Diagnose and manage Premature Ovarian Insufficiency (POI)
  • [ ] Manage hypothalamic amenorrhoea and PCOS overlap
  • [ ] Apply HRT in POI and menopause
  • [ ] Address fertility concerns and contraception

2. Classification & Aetiology

TypeFSH/LHOestradiolCommon Causes
Primary (Hypergonadotropic)↑↑LowTurner Syndrome (45,X), Premature Ovarian Insufficiency (POI), Chemo/Radiotherapy, Autoimmune, Surgery (Oophorectomy), Galactosaemia, Infections (Mumps)
Secondary (Hypogonadotropic)↓/NormalLowHypothalamic Amenorrhoea (Weight Loss, Stress, Exercise), Pituitary Adenoma, Hyperprolactinaemia, Kallmann Syndrome, Sheehan Syndrome, Craniopharyngioma

3. Clinical Presentation

FeaturePrimary (POI/Turner)Secondary (Hypothalamic/Pituitary)
Primary AmenorrhoeaTurner Syndrome, Gonadal DysgenesisKallmann Syndrome, CNS Tumours
Secondary AmenorrhoeaPOI (Autoimmune, Chemo, Idiopathic)Weight Loss, Stress, Exercise, Prolactinoma, Sheehan, Craniopharyngioma
InfertilityBoth TypesBoth Types
Oestrogen Deficiency SignsHot Flushes, Vaginal Dryness, Osteoporosis, Low LibidoSame
Additional FeaturesTurner: Short Stature, Webbed Neck, CoarctationProlactinoma: Galactorrhoea; Kallmann: Anosmia

4. Primary Hypogonadism — Key Entities

Premature Ovarian Insufficiency (POI)

FeatureDetails
DefinitionOvarian Failure <40 Years (FSH >40 IU/L on 2 Occasions 4-6 Weeks Apart)
Incidence1% by Age 40; 0.1% by Age 30
AetiologyIdiopathic (90%); Autoimmune (Adrenal, Thyroid), Genetic (FMR1 Premutation, BMP15), Chemo/Radiotherapy, Surgery, Infections (Mumps), Galactosaemia
AutoimmuneAdrenal (Addison's) 10-20%, Thyroid (Hashimoto), Coeliac, T1DM, Pernicious Anaemia
GeneticFMR1 Premutation (5-10%), BMP15, FOXL2; Turner (45,X) / Mosaic
DiagnosisFSH >40 IU/L on 2 Occasions 4-6 Weeks Apart + Amenorrhoea >4 Months + Age <40

Turner Syndrome (45,X / Mosaic)

FeatureDetails
Karyotype45,X (50%); Mosaic 45,X/46,XX (15-20%)
PhenotypeShort Stature, Webbed Neck, Low Hairline, Low-Set Ears, Shield Chest, Cubitus Valgus
CardiacCoarctation of Aorta (10-20%), Bicuspid Aortic Valve
RenalHorseshoe Kidney (30%), Collecting System Anomalies
EndocrineStreak Ovaries → Primary Amenorrhoea, Infertility
ScreeningEchocardiogram, Renal US, Audiology, Ophthalmology, BP Monitoring

5. Secondary Hypogonadism — Key Entities

Hypothalamic Amenorrhoea (Functional)

CauseMechanismFeatures
Weight Loss / Anorexia↓ Leptin → ↓ GnRH Pulse GeneratorLow BMI (<18.5), Fear of Weight Gain
Excessive Exercise↓ Leptin, ↑ Endorphins → ↓ GnRH Pulse FrequencyAthletes, Dancers, Low Body Fat
Psychological Stress↑ CRH, ↑ Cortisol → ↓ GnRHStress, Life Events
Pituitary AdenomaProlactinoma (Most Common)Galactorrhoea, Headache, Visual Field Defects
CraniopharyngiomaSuprasellar MassVisual Field Defects, Hormone Deficiencies
Sheehan SyndromePostpartum Pituitary NecrosisAgalactorrhoea, Amenorrhoea, Loss of Pubic/Axillary Hair
Kallmann SyndromeAnosmia + Hypogonadotropic HypogonadismANOS1/KAL1 Gene

Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS) — See Separate Note

FeaturePCOS
Diagnosis2/3 Rotterdam: Oligo/Anovulation + Hyperandrogenism + PCO Morphology
EndocrineLH:FSH >2:1, Hyperandrogenism, Insulin Resistance
ManagementLifestyle, COCP, Metformin, Letrozole (Ovulation Induction)

6. Investigation Algorithm

Amenorrhoea (Primary or Secondary)
         │
         ▼
PREGNANCY TEST (β-hCG) → Exclude Pregnancy
         │
         ▼
FSH, LH, OESTRADIOL, PROLACTIN, TSH
         │
         ├── FSH/LH HIGH + OESTRADIOL LOW → PRIMARY HYPOGONADISM
         │       ├── TURNER (45,X) → Karyotype, Cardiac/Renal US
         │       ├── POI (FSH >40 IU/L x2) → Autoimmune Screen, FMR1, Bone Density
         │       └── POST-SURGICAL / CHEMO / RADIO → History
         │
         ├── FSH/LH LOW/NORMAL + OESTRADIOL LOW → SECONDARY
         │       ├── PROLACTIN HIGH → PROLACTINOMA (MRI Pituitary)
         │       ├── LOW BMI / EXERCISE / STRESS → FUNCTIONAL HYPOTHALAMIC AMENORRHOEA
         │       ├── PCOS (LH:FSH >2:1, ANDROGENS ↑) → ROTTERDAM CRITERIA
         │       ├── SHEEHAN SYNDROME (POSTPARTUM) → EMPTY SELLA MRI
         │       └── CRANIOPHARYNGIOMA / TUMOUR → MRI BRAIN
         │
         └── ANDROGENS HIGH + OLIGO/ANOVULATION → PCOS (See PCOS Note)

7. Premature Ovarian Insufficiency (POI) — Management

Hormone Replacement Therapy (HRT) — Until Age 50

ComponentRegimen
OestrogenTransdermal 50-100µg/day (Patch/Gel) OR Oral 1-2mg
ProgestogenCyclical (Medroxyprogesterone 10mg Days 1-12) or Continuous (If Hysterectomy)
DurationUntil Natural Menopause Age (~50-51 Years)
Bone ProtectionBisphosphonate if T-score ≤-2.5; Calcium/Vit D
CardiovascularMonitor BP, Lipids; Transdermal Preferred (↓ VTE Risk)

Fertility in POI

OptionDetails
Spontaneous Pregnancy5-10% (Intermittent Ovarian Activity)
Donor Oocyte IVFGold Standard (Success 40-50%)
Own Oocyte IVFRarely Successful (Low AMH, High FSH)
Fertility PreservationOocyte/Embryo Cryopreservation Before Chemo/Radiotherapy

Autoimmune Screening (POI)

TestFrequency
21-Hydroxylase AbAdrenal (Addison's) — 10-20%
TPOAb / TgAbThyroid (Hashimoto) — 20-30%
Coeliac (TTG-IgA)5-10%
Pernicious AnaemiaB12, IF-Ab — 5-10%

8. Turner Syndrome Management

SystemSurveillance / Intervention
CardiacEchocardiogram at Diagnosis; Annual BP; MRI Aorta if Dilated
RenalRenal US at Diagnosis; Hypertension Screening
GrowthGH Therapy (0.035 mg/kg/day) — Start Early (Age 4-6)
Puberty InductionOestrogen (Low Dose → Adult Dose over 2-3 Years)
FertilityDonor Oocyte IVF (Own Oocytes Rarely Viable)
Bone HealthCalcium/Vit D; DEXA q2-3yr; Bisphosphonate if T-score ≤-2.5
ThyroidAnnual TSH (Autoimmune Thyroiditis Common)
Audiology/OphthalmologyBaseline + q2-3yr

9. Hypothalamic Amenorrhoea Management

CauseManagement
Weight Loss / AnorexiaWeight Restoration (BMI >18.5); Multidisciplinary (Dietitian, Psych)
Excessive ExerciseReduce Intensity/Volume; ↑ Energy Availability (>45 kcal/kg FFM/day)
StressCBT, Stress Management, ↓ Training Load
ProlactinomaCabergoline (0.25mg BD → Titrate); Surgery if Macro/Visual Defects
Kallmann SyndromehCG + rFSH for Fertility; Testosterone/Oestradiol Replacement

10. Premature Ovarian Insufficiency (POI) — Long-Term Risks

ComplicationScreening / Prevention
OsteoporosisDEXA at Diagnosis; Repeat q2-3yr; Bisphosphonate if T-score ≤-2.5
CardiovascularEarly Menopause → ↑ CVD Risk; HRT Until Age 50
Cognitive DeclineEarly Menopause → ↑ Dementia Risk; HRT Neuroprotective?
Autoimmune RiskAnnual TSH, 21-OH Ab, Coeliac Screen
Fertility5-10% Spontaneous Pregnancy; Donor Oocyte IVF Main Option

11. Hormone Replacement Therapy (HRT) in POI / Turner

RegimenDetails
OestrogenTransdermal 50-100µg/day (Patch/Gel) — Preferred (↓ VTE Risk)
ProgestogenCyclical (Medroxyprogesterone 10mg Days 1-12) or Continuous (If Hysterectomy)
DurationUntil Age 50-51 (Natural Menopause Age)
Non-HormonalIf HRT Contraindicated: Calcium/Vit D, Bisphosphonates, Lifestyle

12. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Primary Amenorrhoea CausesTurner (45,X), Müllerian Agenesis, Androgen Insensitivity, Kallmann
Secondary Amenorrhoea Investigationβ-hCG → FSH/LH/Oestradiol/Prolactin/TSH
POI DefinitionFSH >40 IU/L on 2 Occasions (4-6 Weeks Apart) + Age <40 + Amenorrhoea >4 Months
POI AetiologyIdiopathic (90%), Autoimmune (Adrenal/Thyroid), FMR1 Premutation, Chemo/Radiotherapy
POI HRTUntil Age 50; Transdermal Oestrogen + Cyclical Progestogen
Turner Syndrome45,X; Short Stature, Webbed Neck, Coarctation, Streak Ovaries; GH Therapy; Coarctation Repair
Hypothalamic AmenorrhoeaFunctional (Weight Loss, Exercise, Stress) → Weight Gain, ↓ Exercise
Kallmann SyndromeAnosmia + Hypogonadotropic Hypogonadism; ANOS1/KAL1
Sheehan SyndromePostpartum Pituitary Necrosis; Agalactorrhoea + Amenorrhoea + Hair Loss; Empty Sella
POI HRT DurationUntil Age 50 (Natural Menopause Age)
Turner CardiacCoarctation of Aorta (10-20%), Bicuspid Aortic Valve
Kallmann SyndromeAnosmia + Hypogonadotropic Hypogonadism; ANOS1/KAL1 Gene
POI Autoimmune Screen21-OH Ab (Adrenal), TPOAb (Thyroid), TTG-IgA (Coeliac)
Fertility in POI5-10% Spontaneous; Donor Oocyte IVF → Main Option
Turner RenalHorseshoe Kidney (30%); Renal US at Diagnosis

13. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Primary vs SecondaryPrimary = FSH/LH ↑↑; Secondary = FSH/LH Low/Normal
POI vs Early MenopausePOI = <40 Years + FSH >40; Early Menopause = 40-45 Years
Turner vs NoonanTurner: 45,X, Webbed Neck, Coarctation; Noonan: Normal Karyotype, Pyloric Stenosis, Pulmonary Stenosis
POI vs Early MenopausePOI = Before 40; Early Menopause = 40-45
Functional vs OrganicFunctional = Weight Loss/Exercise/Stress; Organic = Prolactinoma, Craniopharyngioma, Kallmann
Kallmann vs Isolated HHKallmann = Anosmia + HH; Isolated HH = No Anosmia
POI HRT DurationUntil Age 50 (Natural Menopause Age)
Turner FertilityDonor Oocyte IVF (Own Oocytes Non-Viable)
Sheehan SyndromePostpartum Pituitary Necrosis → Agalactorrhoea + Amenorrhoea + Empty Sella
Turner RenalHorseshoe Kidney (30%); Renal US at Diagnosis

14. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Female Hypogonadism))
    Primary (Hypergonadotropic)
      Turner Syndrome (45,X)
      POI (<40y, FSH>40 x2)
      Autoimmune (Adrenal, Thyroid)
      Chemo/Radiotherapy
      Genetic (FMR1, BMP15)
    Secondary (Hypogonadotropic)
      Functional (Weight Loss, Exercise, Stress)
      Prolactinoma
      Craniopharyngioma
      Kallmann (Anosmia)
      Sheehan (Postpartum Necrosis)
      Craniopharyngioma
    PCOS
      Rotterdam (2/3): Oligo + Hyperandrogenism + PCO
      LH:FSH >2:1
      Metabolic: IR, Obesity
    Turner Syndrome
      45,X / Mosaic
      Short Stature, Webbed Neck, Coarctation
      GH Therapy; Oestrogen Puberty Induction
      Donor Oocyte IVF
    POI
      FSH>40 x2 <40y
      Autoimmune Screen (Adrenal, Thyroid)
      HRT Until 50y
      Donor Oocyte IVF
    Kallmann
      Anosmia + HH
      ANOS1/KAL1 Gene
    Sheehan
      Postpartum Necrosis
      Empty Sella MRI

15. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Primary Amenorrhoea CausesTurner, Müllerian Agenesis, Androgen Insensitivity, Kallmann
Secondary AmenorrhoeaPregnancy → FSH/LH/Oestradiol/Prolactin/TSH
POI DefinitionFSH >40 IU/L x2 (4-6wk Apart) + Amenorrhoea >4mo + Age <40
Turner Syndrome45,X; Short Stature, Webbed Neck, Coarctation; GH Therapy; Donor Oocyte IVF
POI HRTUntil Age 50; Transdermal Oestrogen + Cyclical Progestogen
Kallmann SyndromeAnosmia + Hypogonadotropic Hypogonadism (ANOS1/KAL1)
Sheehan SyndromePostpartum Necrosis; Agalactorrhoea + Amenorrhoea + Hair Loss; Empty Sella
POI Autoimmune Screen21-OH Ab (Adrenal), TPOAb (Thyroid), TTG-IgA (Coeliac)
Turner CardiacCoarctation (10-20%), Bicuspid Aortic Valve; Echo at Diagnosis
POI Fertility5-10% Spontaneous; Donor Oocyte IVF Main Option
KallmannAnosmia + HH; ANOS1/KAL1 Gene; X-Linked Recessive
Functional Hypothalamic AmenorrhoeaWeight Loss, Exercise, Stress → Weight Gain, ↓ Exercise
Turner RenalHorseshoe Kidney (30%); Renal US at Diagnosis
POI Autoimmune21-OH Ab (Adrenal 10-20%), TPOAb (Thyroid 20-30%), Coeliac (5-10%)
Turner GH TherapyStart Age 4-6; 0.035 mg/kg/day
Turner PubertyOestrogen 6.25µg → Adult Dose Over 2-3 Years
