TopicKey Point
Menopause Definition12 Months Amenorrhoea (Mean Age 51)
PerimenopauseIrregular Cycles + Vasomotor Symptoms; Clinical Diagnosis
HRT 1st Line IndicationVasomotor Symptoms (Hot Flushes/Night Sweats)
Route of ChoiceTransdermal (↓ VTE, Stroke, Gallbladder)
Progestogen ChoiceMicronised Progesterone (Breast Safety); LNG-IUS (Endometrial Protection)
ContraindicationsBreast Ca, VTE, Stroke, MI, Unexplained Bleeding, Active Liver Disease
WHI Key Finding<10yr Post-Menopause / Age <60 → Net Benefit; >10yr / Age >60 → Net Harm
Window of OpportunityStart HRT <10yr Post-Menopause / Age <60 for CVD Benefit
Breast Cancer RiskCombined HRT > Oestrogen-Only > None; Duration-Dependent
VTE RiskOral > Transdermal; Highest 1st Year
Premature MenopauseHRT Until Age 50-51 (Mandatory for Bone/Heart/Brain)
Breast Cancer SurvivorsSystemic HRT Contraindicated; Vaginal Oestrogen Safe
Tibolone2.5mg OD; No Bleed; ↑ Libido; ↑ Stroke Risk >60
Non-Hormonal 1st LineCBT (NICE); SSRIs/SNRIs/Fezolinetant/Gabapentin
Vaginal OestrogenSafe in Breast Cancer; Long-Term Use OK
Window of Opportunity<10yr Post-Menopause / Age <60 for Net Benefit

17. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Reproductive Endocrinology|Reproductive Endocrinology]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Female Hypogonadism]], [[Premature Ovarian Insufficiency]], [[Osteoporosis]], [[Cardiovascular Disease]], [[Breast Cancer]]

18. MCQs (10)

  1. Menopause definition:

A. 12 months amenorrhoea after age 45; average 51yr; FSH >30, oestradiol low

B. Any amenorrhoea

C. Age <40

D. 6 months amenorrhoea

E. FSH <10

  1. Perimenopause:

A. Irregular cycles, vasomotor symptoms, mood changes; FSH fluctuating; 4-8yr before menopause

B. Post-menopause

C. Premature menopause

D. POI

E. Pregnancy

  1. HRT - oestrogen only:

A. For hysterectomised women; oral/transdermal; reduces vasomotor symptoms, bone loss, urogenital atrophy

B. With progestin

C. Contraindicated in uterus

D. Only vaginal

E. No bone benefit

  1. HRT - combined:

A. Oestrogen + progestin (protects endometrium); sequential (cyclical) or continuous

B. Oestrogen only

C. Progestin only

D. No endometrial protection

E. Only for hysterectomy

  1. HRT risks:

A. VTE (oral > transdermal), breast cancer (combined >5yr), stroke, gallbladder disease

B. No risks

C. Only osteoporosis benefit

D. No cancer risk

E. Only VTE

  1. HRT contraindications:

A. Active VTE, breast cancer, endometrial cancer, active liver disease, undiagnosed vaginal bleeding

B. Osteoporosis

C. Vasomotor symptoms only

D. Age <50

E. Hysterectomy

  1. HRT duration:

A. Short-term (2-5yr) for symptoms; lowest dose; annual review; no arbitrary limit

B. Lifelong

C. 1 year max

D. 2 years max

E. 10 years max

  1. HRT alternatives:

A. SSRIs/SNRIs (venlafaxine), gabapentin, clonidine, CBT, lifestyle; for vasomotor if HRT contraindicated

B. Only HRT

C. Only herbal

D. No alternatives

E. Surgery only

  1. Premature menopause (<40):

A. POI; HRT until age 50 (bone, CV, cognitive); fertility referral; genetic counselling

B. No treatment

C. COCP only

D. No HRT

E. Surgery only

  1. Vaginal oestrogen:

A. Low-dose vaginal oestrogen (cream/pessary/ring); urogenital atrophy; minimal systemic absorption

B. Systemic HRT

C. Contraindicated in breast cancer

D. No benefit

E. Oral only

19. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 52yo woman: flushes x10/day, sleep disturbance, no uterus. HRT?

A. Oestrogen-only (transdermal preferred)

B. Combined HRT

C. Progestin only

D. No HRT

E. SSRI only

  1. Same patient: breast cancer history. Alternative?

A. Venlafaxine/gabapentin/clonidine/CBT; avoid HRT

B. HRT

C. COCP

D. Progestin only

E. No treatment

  1. 50yo woman: uterus intact, flushes, bone loss. HRT?

A. Combined continuous HRT (oestrogen + progestin)

B. Oestrogen only

C. Progestin only

D. Raloxifene

E. HRT

  1. 52yo woman: VTE history, flushes. Management?

A. Transdermal oestrogen + micronised progesterone (lower VTE) OR non-hormonal

B. Oral combined HRT

C. Oral oestrogen only

D. No treatment

E. COCP

  1. Premature menopause 35yo: management?

A. HRT until age 50 (bone/CV/cognitive); fertility referral; genetic counselling

B. No treatment

C. COCP only

D. Surgery

E. Observation

20. Flashcards

  • Q: Menopause

A: 12mo amenorrhoea >45yr; avg 51yr; FSH>30, oestradiol low

  • Q: Perimenopause

A: Irregular cycles, vasomotor symptoms, mood changes; 4-8yr before menopause

  • Q: HRT oestrogen only

A: Hysterectomised women; oral/transdermal; vasomotor, bone, urogenital

  • Q: HRT combined

A: Oestrogen + progestin (endometrial protection); sequential or continuous

  • Q: HRT risks

A: VTE (oral>transdermal), breast cancer (combined>5yr), stroke, gallbladder

  • Q: HRT contraindications

A: Active VTE, breast/endometrial Ca, active liver disease, undiagnosed vaginal bleeding

  • Q: HRT duration

A: Short-term (2-5yr) for symptoms; lowest dose; annual review

  • Q: HRT alternatives

A: SSRIs/SNRIs (venlafaxine), gabapentin, clonidine, CBT, lifestyle

  • Q: Premature menopause

A: <40yr (POI); HRT until 50; fertility referral; genetic counselling

  • Q: Vaginal oestrogen

A: Low-dose (cream/pessary/ring); urogenital atrophy; minimal systemic absorption

21. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. 12 months amenorrhoea after age 45; average 51yr; FSH >30, oestradiol low — Menopause definition:
  1. Irregular cycles, vasomotor symptoms, mood changes; FSH fluctuating; 4-8yr before menopause — Perimenopause:
  1. For hysterectomised women; oral/transdermal; reduces vasomotor symptoms, bone loss, urogenital atrophy — HRT - oestrogen only:
  1. Oestrogen + progestin (protects endometrium); sequential (cyclical) or continuous — HRT - combined:
  1. VTE (oral > transdermal), breast cancer (combined >5yr), stroke, gallbladder disease — HRT risks:
  1. Active VTE, breast cancer, endometrial cancer, active liver disease, undiagnosed vaginal bleeding — HRT contraindications:
  1. Short-term (2-5yr) for symptoms; lowest dose; annual review; no arbitrary limit — HRT duration:
  1. SSRIs/SNRIs (venlafaxine), gabapentin, clonidine, CBT, lifestyle; for vasomotor if HRT contraindicated — HRT alternatives:
  1. POI; HRT until age 50 (bone, CV, cognitive); fertility referral; genetic counselling — Premature menopause (<40):
  1. Low-dose vaginal oestrogen (cream/pessary/ring); urogenital atrophy; minimal systemic absorption — Vaginal oestrogen:

SBAs

  1. Oestrogen-only (transdermal preferred) — 52yo woman: flushes x10/day, sleep disturbance, no uterus. HRT?
  1. Venlafaxine/gabapentin/clonidine/CBT; avoid HRT — Same patient: breast cancer history. Alternative?
  1. Combined continuous HRT (oestrogen + progestin) — 50yo woman: uterus intact, flushes, bone loss. HRT?
  1. Transdermal oestrogen + micronised progesterone (lower VTE) OR non-hormonal — 52yo woman: VTE history, flushes. Management?
  1. HRT until age 50 (bone/CV/cognitive); fertility referral; genetic counselling — Premature menopause 35yo: management?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.7.06 Pcos
[!info]
PCOS = Most Common Endocrine Disorder in Women of Reproductive Age (10-15%). Rotterdam Criteria (2/3): Oligo/Anovulation + Hyperandrogenism + Polycystic Ovaries. Insulin Resistance = Core Pathophysiology. Long-term Risks: T2DM, CVD, Endometrial Cancer, Infertility.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply Rotterdam diagnostic criteria (2/3) and phenotypic classification
  • [ ] Differentiate PCOS from other hyperandrogenic states
  • [ ] Assess metabolic risk and screen for long-term complications
  • [ ] Apply management algorithms (lifestyle, COCP, metformin, letrozole, IVF)
  • [ ] Counsel on long-term health risks (T2DM, CVD, endometrial cancer)

2. Diagnostic Criteria — Rotterdam (2/3 Required)

CriterionDefinition
1. Oligo/Anovulation<8 Cycles/Year or Cycle Length >35 Days
2. HyperandrogenismClinical (Hirsutism mFG >8, Acne, Alopecia) OR Biochemical (Total Testosterone >2.5-3 nmol/L, FAI >5)
3. Polycystic Ovarian Morphology (PCOM)≥20 Follicles (2-9mm) Per Ovary OR Ovarian Volume >10 mL (US)

Diagnosis = 2 of 3 Criteria (After Excluding Other Aetiologies)

Phenotypic Classification (4 Phenotypes)

PhenotypeCriteriaMetabolic Risk
A (Classic/Full)Oligo/Anovulation + Hyperandrogenism + PCOMHighest
B (Classic Non-PCO)Oligo/Anovulation + HyperandrogenismHigh
C (Ovulatory PCOS)Hyperandrogenism + PCOMModerate
D (Non-Hyperandrogenic)Oligo/Anovulation + PCOMLowest

3. Differential Diagnosis — Exclusion of Mimics

ConditionKey Differentiators
NCCAH (21-OH Deficiency)↑ 17-OHP (Basal >200, Stim >1000 ng/dL); Genetic Testing (CYP21A2)
Cushing SyndromeCortisol Excess (Dex Suppression Test); Striae, Moon Face, HTN
Androgen-Secreting TumourRapid Onset, Very High Testosterone (>5 nmol/L); DHEAS ↑; Imaging
Thyroid DysfunctionTSH Abnormal; Prolactin Normal
HyperprolactinaemiaProlactin ↑; Galactorrhoea; MRI Pituitary
Drug-InducedAnabolic Steroids, Danazol, Valproate, CYP21A2 Inhibitors
AcromegalyGH/IGF-1 ↑; OGTT GH Not Suppressed
Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia17-OHP ↑; 21-Hydroxylase Deficiency

4. Clinical Features & Phenotypes

FeatureFrequencyDetails
Oligomenorrhoea/Amenorrhoea70-80%<8 Cycles/Year or >35 Day Cycles
Hirsutism (mFG >8)60-70%Ferriman-Gallwey Score; Chin, Upper Lip, Chest, Abdomen, Thighs
Acne40-50%Jawline, Jaw, Back
Alopecia10-20%Female Pattern (Ludwig)
Acanthosis Nigricans30-40%Neck, Axillae, Groins (Insulin Resistance Marker)
Obesity40-80% (Central)BMI >30 in 40-50%; Waist >88cm
Infertility70-80%Anovulation; ↑ Miscarriage Risk
MetabolicInsulin Resistance (70-80%); Dyslipidaemia; ↑ T2DM Risk

5. Diagnostic Algorithm

Clinical Suspicion (Oligomenorrhoea + Hyperandrogenism)
         │
         ▼
EXCLUDE: Pregnancy, Thyroid, Prolactin, Cushing, NCCAH, Androgen-Secreting Tumour
         │
         ▼
APPLY ROTTERDAM CRITERIA (2/3)
         │
         ├── Oligo/Anovulation (<8 cycles/yr or >35d cycle)
         ├── Hyperandrogenism (Clinical mFG>8 OR Biochemical T >2.5-3 nmol/L)
         └── PCOM on US (≥20 Follicles 2-9mm OR Volume >10mL)
         │
         └── 2/3 CRITERIA MET → DIAGNOSE PCOS
         │
         ▼
PHENOTYPING (A/B/C/D) → Guides Metabolic Risk & Management

6. Investigations

Hormonal Profile

TestTimingExpected in PCOS
Total TestosteroneDay 2-4 (or Random)>2.5-3 nmol/L (or FAI >5)
Free Androgen Index (FAI)(Total T × 100) / SHBG>5
SHBGLow (Insulin ↓ SHBG Synthesis)
LH/FSHDay 2-4LH:FSH >2:1 (Classic but Not Diagnostic)
17-OHPDay 2-4Exclude NCCAH (Basal <200 ng/dL; Stim >1000 ng/dL)
TSH, ProlactinExclude Thyroid / Prolactinoma
17-OHP (Basal)<200 ng/dL (Exclude NCCAH)
DHEASMildly ↑ OftenExclude Adrenal Tumour if Markedly ↑

Metabolic Workup

TestTarget / Threshold
Fasting Glucose / HbA1c<5.6 mmol/L / <5.7%
OGTT (75g)120min <7.8 mmol/L (Normal); 7.8-11.0 = IGT; ≥11.1 = DM
Fasting Insulin / HOMA-IRHOMA-IR >2.5 = Insulin Resistance
Lipid ProfileTG >1.7, HDL <1.3 (Women)
Liver FunctionNAFLD Common (ALT ↑)

Pelvic Ultrasound

FindingPCOM Criteria
Follicle Count≥20 Follicles (2-9mm) Per Ovary
Ovarian Volume>10 mL
StromaIncreased Echogenicity

7. Long-Term Health Risks

ComplicationRelative RiskScreening
Type 2 Diabetes4-7xOGTT q1-3yr (Age >30 / BMI >25 / Prior GDM)
Cardiovascular Disease2-3xBP, Lipids, HbA1c Annually
Endometrial Cancer3-4x (Unopposed Oestrogen)Ultrasound if Abnormal Bleeding; Progestogen Challenge
Obstructive Sleep Apnoea5-10xSTOP-BANG; Sleep Study if Symptomatic
Mood Disorders2-3x (Depression/Anxiety)PHQ-9/GAD-7 Screening
NAFLD2-3xLFTs, Ultrasound Liver

8. Management — Phenotype-Guided

Universal First-Line: Lifestyle Modification

TargetIntervention
Weight Loss5-10% Body Weight → Restores Ovulation in 50-60%
DietLow GI, High Fibre, Moderate Protein, Mediterranean
Exercise150 min/week Moderate + Resistance 2x/week
BehaviouralCBT, Self-Monitoring, Stress Management

Pharmacotherapy — By Goal

GoalFirst-LineSecond-LineThird-Line
Menstrual RegulationCOCP (Low-Dose, EE 20-30µg + Anti-Androgen Progestogen)Cyclical Progestogen (MPA 10mg Days 1-12)—
Hirsutism/AcneCOCP (Cyproterone/Drospirenone)Anti-Androgens (Spironolactone 50-100mg, Finasteride, Flutamide)Laser/Electrolysis
Ovulation InductionLetrozole 2.5-5mg Days 2-6 (1st Line)Clomifene 50-150mg Days 2-6Gonadotrophins (rFSH/hMG) → IVF
Metabolic/IRMetformin 500mg BD → 1g BD (If BMI ≥25 / IGT)Pioglitazone (If Metformin Intolerant)GLP-1 RA (Semaglutide/Tirzepatide) if Obese
Weight Loss (BMI ≥25)Lifestyle 5-10% Weight LossGLP-1 RA (Semaglutide 2.4mg / Tirzepatide 15mg)Bariatric Surgery (BMI ≥35/40)

9. Ovulation Induction — Algorithm

Anovulatory PCOS Desiring Pregnancy
         │
         ▼
PRE-CONCEPTION: Folic Acid 5mg/day; Rubella/Varicella Immune; TSH/TSH/HbA1c
         │
         └── LIFESTYLE OPTIMISATION (3-6 Months)
                 │
                 ├────────────────┬─────────────────────┐
                 │                │                     │
          LETROZOLE 2.5-5mg    CLOMIFENE 50-150mg      GONADOTROPHINS
          Days 2-6 (1st Line)   Days 2-6 (If Letrozole Fail)   rFSH/hMG (If Oral Fail)
          (↑ Live Birth, ↓       (Traditional 1st Line)         (Monitor: US + E2)
           Multiples, ↓ OHSS)
                 │                │                      │
                 └────────────────┴──────────────────────┘
                              │
                              ▼
                        MONITORING: US Follicular Tracking (Day 10-12)
                        TRIGGER: hCG 5000-10000 IU (When Lead Follicle ≥18mm)
                        TIMING: IUI 36h Post-hCG / Timed Intercourse
                        LUTEAL SUPPORT: Progesterone (If IUI/IVF)

10. Long-Term Health Surveillance

ComplicationScreening IntervalThreshold for Action
Type 2 DiabetesOGTT q1-3yr (Age >30 / BMI >25 / Prior GDM)HbA1c ≥5.7% → Preventive Metformin
CardiovascularBP, Lipids Annually; Carotid IMT if High RiskLDL >3.0 → Statin
Endometrial CancerUltrasound if Abnormal Bleeding; Progestogen Challenge q1-2yr if AmenorrhoeicEndometrial Thickness >5mm → Biopsy
Sleep ApnoeaSTOP-BANG Questionnaire; Sleep Study if High RiskCPAP if AHI >15
Mood DisordersPHQ-9/GAD-7 AnnuallyRefer if Positive
Bone HealthDEXA if Amenorrhoeic >1yr / Steroid UseT-score ≤-2.5 → Bisphosphonate

11. Special Situations

PCOS in Adolescence

ChallengeApproach
Diagnosis DelayWait 2 Years Post-Menarche; PCOM Common in Normal Teens
DiagnosisOligo/Anovulation ≥2 Years Post-Menarche + Hyperandrogenism
ManagementCOCP (1st Line); Metformin if IGT; Lifestyle Emphasis

Pregnancy in PCOS

RiskManagement
Miscarriage1.5-2x HigherProgestogen Support (If Prior Loss)
GDM2-3x RiskEarly OGTT (16-18wk); Metformin if IGT
Pre-eclampsia2-3x RiskAspirin 75-150mg OD from 12w (If >1 Risk Factor)
Preterm Birth↑ RiskCervical Length Monitoring

12. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Rotterdam Criteria2/3: Oligo/Anovulation + Hyperandrogenism + PCOM
Phenotype A (Full)Highest Metabolic Risk
Hirsutism mFG>8 = Significant
Biochemical HyperandrogenismTestosterone >2.5-3 nmol/L OR FAI >5
LH:FSH Ratio>2:1 in PCOS (Not Diagnostic Alone)
17-OHPBasal <200 ng/dL; Stim >1000 ng/dL = NCCAH
Letrozole vs ClomifeneLetrozole 1st Line (Higher Live Birth, Lower Multiples, ↓ OHSS)
MetforminBMI ≥25 or IGT → 500-1000mg BD; ↓ IR, ↓ Androgens, ↑ Ovulation
GLP-1 RASemaglutide 2.4mg / Tirzepatide 15mg for Weight Loss in Obese PCOS
Bariatric SurgeryBMI ≥35 (or ≥30 + Comorbidities) → Resolution of PCOS Features
Endometrial Cancer Risk3-4x (Unopposed Oestrogen); Progestogen Challenge q1-2yr
Sleep Apnoea5-10x Risk; STOP-BANG Screen; CPAP if AHI >15
Letrozole vs ClomifeneLetrozole Superior (↑ Live Birth, ↓ Multiples, ↓ OHSS)
Metformin in PregnancyContinue if Prior GDM/IR; Not for Ovulation Induction Alone
Vitamin DDeficiency Common in PCOS; Supplement if <30 ng/mL

13. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
PCOS vs NCCAHNCCAH: 17-OHP >200 (Basal) / >1000 (Stim); Genetic CYP21A2
PCOS vs CushingCushing: Striae, Moon Face, Hypertension, Easy Bruising; Dex Suppression Test
PCOS vs Androgen-Secreting TumourTumour: Rapid Onset, Very High Testosterone (>5 nmol/L), DHEAS ↑↑
Letrozole vs ClomifeneLetrozole: ↑ Live Birth, ↓ Multiples, ↓ OHSS; Preferred 1st Line
Metformin in PCOSBMI ≥25 + IGT → Metformin; Not for All PCOS
Ovulation InductionLetrozole 1st Line; Clomifene 2nd; Gonadotrophins 3rd
Endometrial Cancer3-4x Risk → Progestogen Challenge q1-2yr if Oligomenorrhoea
Insulin ResistanceHOMA-IR >2.5; Central Obesity + Acanthosis Nigricans
Acanthosis NigricansInsulin Resistance Marker; Neck, Axillae, Groins

14. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((PCOS))
    Diagnosis
      Rotterdam 2/3: Oligo/Anovulation + Hyperandrogenism (+Clinical/Biochem) + PCOM
      Phenotypes: A (Full), B (Classic), C (Ovulatory), D (Non-Hyperandrogenic)
    Differential
      NCCAH: 17-OHP >200/1000 stim
      Cushing: Dex Supp; Striae, Moon Face
      Tumour: Rapid Virilisation, T >5nmol/L
    Phenotypes
      A: Oligo + HA + PCOM (Highest Risk)
      B: Oligo + HA (No PCOM)
      C: HA + PCOM (Ovulatory)
      D: Oligo + PCOM (Non-HA)
    Management
      Lifestyle: 5-10% Wt Loss → 50-60% Ovulation
      COCP: 1st Line (Cycle Reg, Hirsutism)
      Metformin: BMI≥25/IGT; ↓IR, ↓Androgen
      Ovulation: Letrozole (1st) > Clomifene (2nd) > Gonadotrophins
      Weight Loss: GLP-1 RA (Sema/Tirzepatide) / Bariatric
    Complications
      T2DM (4-7x), CVD (2-3x), Endo Ca (3-4x), OSA (5-10x)
      Infertility (70-80%)
      Mood Disorders (2-3x)
    Monitoring
      OGTT q1-3yr; Lipids/BP Annual; Endo US if Bleed; DEXA if Amenorrhoeic

15. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
PCOS DiagnosisRotterdam 2/3: Oligo/Anovulation + Hyperandrogenism + PCOM
Hirsutism ScoreModified Ferriman-Gallwey >8 = Significant
Biochemical HyperandrogenismTestosterone >2.5-3 nmol/L OR FAI >5
LH:FSH Ratio>2:1 in PCOS (Not Diagnostic Alone)
17-OHP (NCCAH)Basal <200 ng/dL; Stimulated >1000 ng/dL
Letrozole vs ClomifeneLetrozole 1st Line (↑ Live Birth, ↓ Multiples, ↓ OHSS)
Metformin IndicationBMI ≥25 OR IGT; Dose 500mg BD → 1g BD
GLP-1 RA in PCOSSemaglutide 2.4mg / Tirzepatide 15mg for Weight Loss
Endometrial Cancer Risk3-4x (Unopposed Oestrogen); Progestogen Challenge q1-2yr
Sleep Apnoea5-10x Risk; STOP-BANG Screen; CPAP if AHI >15
Letrozole vs ClomifeneLetrozole: ↑ Live Birth, ↓ Multiples, ↓ OHSS
Vitamin DDeficiency Common in PCOS; Supplement if <30 ng/mL
Metabolic SyndromeCentral Obesity + IR + HTN + Dyslipidaemia = MetSyn (Common in PCOS)
Bariatric SurgeryBMI ≥35 (or ≥30 + Comorbidity) → Resolution of PCOS Features
Vitamin D<30 ng/mL → Supplement (↑ Insulin Sensitivity, ↓ Androgens)

16. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Reproductive Endocrinology|Reproductive Endocrinology]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Female Hypogonadism]], [[Infertility]], [[Infertility]], [[Insulin Resistance]], [[Metabolic Syndrome]], [[Thyroid Disorders]]

17. MCQs (10)

  1. PCOS diagnostic criteria (Rotterdam):

A. 2 of 3: oligo/anovulation, hyperandrogenism (clinical/biochemical), polycystic ovaries on US

B. All 3 required

C. Only hyperandrogenism

D. Only PCO on US

E. Only anovulation

  1. PCOS hyperandrogenism:

A. Clinical (hirsutism, acne, alopecia) or biochemical (testosterone, androstenedione)

B. Only clinical

C. Only biochemical

D. Only DHEAS

E. Only androstenedione

  1. PCO morphology on US:

A. >=20 follicles (2-9mm) per ovary OR ovarian volume >10mL

B. >=10 follicles

C. >=10 follicles per ovary

D. Volume >15mL

E. Single cyst >2cm

  1. PCOS LH:FSH ratio:

A. >2:1 (common but not diagnostic)

B. <1:1

C. Normal

D. No change

E. FSH > LH

  1. PCOS insulin resistance:

A. Present in 50-70% (obese > lean); drives hyperandrogenism; metformin may help

B. Absent

C. Only in obese

D. Only in lean

E. Diagnostic criterion

  1. PCOS management - anovulation/infertility:

A. Letrozole 1st line; clomiphene 2nd; metformin adjunct; gonadotrophins/IVF if resistant

B. Clomiphene 1st line

C. Metformin alone

D. IVF only

E. Surgery only

  1. PCOS management - hirsutism:

A. COCP 1st line (anti-androgenic progestin); spironolactone/finasteride adjunct; laser

B. Metformin only

C. Spironolactone alone

D. Laser only

E. No treatment

  1. PCOS metabolic risk:

A. Increased T2DM, CVD, endometrial cancer; screen glucose/lipids; lifestyle 1st line

B. No increased risk

C. Only T2DM

D. Only CVD

E. Only endometrial cancer

  1. PCOS in adolescence:

A. Diagnosis cautious; anovulation + hyperandrogenism (US unreliable); treat symptoms

B. Same as adults

C. Not diagnosed

D. Only if obese

E. Resolves spontaneously

  1. PCOS and endometrial cancer:

A. Unopposed oestrogen -> endometrial hyperplasia -> cancer; protect with progestin/COCP/MPA

B. No increased risk

C. Only if obese

D. Only if diabetic

E. Only if nulliparous

18. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 25yo woman: oligomenorrhoea, hirsutism, acne, T=3.5, LH:FSH 3:1, PCO on US. Diagnosis?

A. PCOS (Rotterdam criteria)

B. HAIR-AN

C. Cushing

D. Adrenal hyperplasia

E. Ovarian tumour

  1. Same patient: wants pregnancy. 1st line ovulation induction?

A. Letrozole 2.5-7.5mg days 3-7

B. Clomiphene

C. Metformin alone

D. Gonadotrophins

E. IVF

  1. Same patient: hirsutism management?

A. COCP (drosperinone/cyproterone) 1st line; spironolactone adjunct

B. Metformin

C. Spironolactone alone

D. Laser only

E. No treatment

  1. PCOS patient: BMI 35, T2DM risk. 1st line?

A. Lifestyle (diet/exercise) 5-10% weight loss; metformin if pre-diabetic

B. Metformin alone

C. Bariatric surgery

D. COCP

E. No intervention

  1. PCOS with endometrial hyperplasia. Management?

A. Progestin (MPA/COCP) to counteract unopposed oestrogen; hysteroscopy if atypical

B. Hysterectomy

C. Oestrogen

D. Clomiphene

E. Observation

19. Flashcards

  • Q: PCOS criteria

A: Rotterdam: 2/3 (oligo/anovulation, hyperandrogenism, PCO on US)

  • Q: Hyperandrogenism

A: Clinical (hirsutism/acne) or biochemical (testosterone/androstenedione)

  • Q: PCO morphology

A: >=20 follicles (2-9mm) per ovary OR volume >10mL

  • Q: LH:FSH

A: >2:1 (common but not diagnostic)

  • Q: Insulin resistance

A: 50-70% (obese > lean); drives hyperandrogenism; metformin may help

  • Q: Ovulation induction

A: Letrozole 1st line; clomiphene 2nd; metformin adjunct

  • Q: Hirsutism Rx

A: COCP 1st line (anti-androgenic progestin); spironolactone/finasteride adjunct

  • Q: Metabolic risk

A: Increased T2DM, CVD, endometrial cancer; lifestyle 1st line

  • Q: Adolescent PCOS

A: Diagnosis cautious; anovulation + hyperandrogenism; US unreliable

  • Q: Endometrial risk

A: Unopposed oestrogen -> hyperplasia -> cancer; progestin/COCP/MPA protection

20. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. 2 of 3: oligo/anovulation, hyperandrogenism (clinical/biochemical), polycystic ovaries on US — PCOS diagnostic criteria (Rotterdam):
  1. Clinical (hirsutism, acne, alopecia) or biochemical (testosterone, androstenedione) — PCOS hyperandrogenism:
  1. >=20 follicles (2-9mm) per ovary OR ovarian volume >10mL — PCO morphology on US:
  1. >2:1 (common but not diagnostic) — PCOS LH:FSH ratio:
  1. Present in 50-70% (obese > lean); drives hyperandrogenism; metformin may help — PCOS insulin resistance:
  1. Letrozole 1st line; clomiphene 2nd; metformin adjunct; gonadotrophins/IVF if resistant — PCOS management - anovulation/infertility:
  1. COCP 1st line (anti-androgenic progestin); spironolactone/finasteride adjunct; laser — PCOS management - hirsutism:
  1. Increased T2DM, CVD, endometrial cancer; screen glucose/lipids; lifestyle 1st line — PCOS metabolic risk:
  1. Diagnosis cautious; anovulation + hyperandrogenism (US unreliable); treat symptoms — PCOS in adolescence:
  1. Unopposed oestrogen -> endometrial hyperplasia -> cancer; protect with progestin/COCP/MPA — PCOS and endometrial cancer:

SBAs

  1. PCOS (Rotterdam criteria) — 25yo woman: oligomenorrhoea, hirsutism, acne, T=3.5, LH:FSH 3:1, PCO on US. Diagnosis?
  1. Letrozole 2.5-7.5mg days 3-7 — Same patient: wants pregnancy. 1st line ovulation induction?
  1. COCP (drosperinone/cyproterone) 1st line; spironolactone adjunct — Same patient: hirsutism management?
  1. Lifestyle (diet/exercise) 5-10% weight loss; metformin if pre-diabetic — PCOS patient: BMI 35, T2DM risk. 1st line?
  1. Progestin (MPA/COCP) to counteract unopposed oestrogen; hysteroscopy if atypical — PCOS with endometrial hyperplasia. Management?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.8 20.8 4
▸ 20.8.01 Men1
[!info]
MEN1 = Wermer Syndrome = Autosomal Dominant (Penetrance >95% by Age 50). "3 Ps": Parathyroid Hyperplasia, Pituitary Adenoma, Pancreatic NETs. MEN1 Gene (Menin, Tumour Suppressor) on Chromosome 11q13. Screening: Annual Biochemical + 5-Yearly Imaging.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Define MEN1 diagnostic criteria
  • [ ] Apply screening and surveillance protocols
  • [ ] Manage each component (Parathyroid, Pituitary, Pancreas)
  • [ ] Counsel on genetic testing and family screening
  • [ ] Recognise associated tumours (Adrenal, Thymus, Lung, Skin)

2. Diagnostic Criteria

Clinical CriterionDefinition
Classic≥2 of 3 Ps (Parathyroid, Pituitary, Pancreas)
Familial1 of 3 Ps + 1st-Degree Relative with MEN1

Genetic Confirmation: MEN1 Mutation (Menin, Tumour Suppressor, Chromosome 11q13)

3. The "3 Ps" — Core Manifestations

1. Primary Hyperparathyroidism (PHPT) — Most Common (90-95%)

FeatureDetails
Frequency90-95% (Earliest, Often First Manifestation)
PathologyMultigland Hyperplasia (90%) / Single Adenoma (10%)
BiochemistryHypercalcaemia, Inappropriately Normal/High PTH, Low Phosphate
Age of Onset20-40 Years (Earlier Than Sporadic PHPT)
ManagementSubtotal (3.5-Gland) Parathyroidectomy (Standard)

2. Pituitary Adenoma

FeatureDetails
Frequency30-40%
TypesProlactinoma (60%) > GH (Somatotroph) > Non-Functioning > ACTH (Cushing) > TSH-oma
ProlactinomaOften Macroadenoma; Cabergoline 1st Line
GH-Secreting (Acromegaly)10-20% of MEN1 Pituitary; Earlier Than Sporadic
Cushing DiseaseRare in MEN1
SurgeryTSS (Transsphenoidal) for Macroadenomas / Functioning Microadenomas

3. Pancreatic NETs (pNETs)

FeatureDetails
Frequency30-50% (Often Multiple)
FunctioningGastrinoma (ZES) (40%) → Duodenal (Multiple, Small); Insulinoma (10-20%); Non-Functioning (30-40%)
Gastrinoma (ZES)Duodenal > Pancreatic; Multiple Small Tumours; Secretin Stimulation Test (+ve)
InsulinomaBenign (90%); Whipple's Triad; Surgical Enucleation
Non-FunctioningMalignant Potential High if >2cm; Surgical Resection if >2cm
Malignant PotentialpNETs >2cm → Higher Malignant Risk; Lymph Node Metastases Common

4. Associated Tumours (Beyond the 3 Ps)

TumourFrequencyClinical Significance
Adrenal20-40%Usually Non-Functioning Incidentalomas; Rare Cortisol/Androgen Excess
Thymic Carcinoid5-10%MEN1-Associated; Often Malignant; Male Predominance
Bronchial Carcinoid5-10%Female Predominance; Less Aggressive than Thymic
Lipomas30%Subcutaneous, Visceral
Angiofibromas / CollagenomasSkinFacial, Trunk
MeningiomaRareSuprasellar / Convexity

5. Screening & Surveillance Protocol

ComponentFrequencyModality
Calcium + PTHAnnual (From Age 5-10)Blood
Pituitary Hormones (PRL, IGF-1, ACTH, TSH, fT4, LH/FSH)AnnualBlood
Fasting Gut Hormones (Gastrin, Insulin, Glucagon, PP, Chromogranin A)Annual (From Age 20)Blood
MRI PituitaryEvery 3 Years (Or Sooner if Functioning)MRI (3mm, Gadolinium)
CT/MRI PancreasEvery 1-3 Years (From Age 20)Multiphasic CT / MRI
Chest CTEvery 3-5 Years (Thymic/Bronchial Carcinoid)CT Chest
Adrenal ImagingEvery 2-3 YearsCT Abdomen
Skin ExamAnnualClinical

6. Genetic Testing & Counselling

AspectDetails
GeneMEN1 (Menin) — Chromosome 11q13, Tumour Suppressor
InheritanceAutosomal Dominant; Penetrance >95% by Age 50
Testing IndicationClinical Diagnosis + 1st-Degree Relatives
Family ScreeningPredictive Testing (If Mutation Known); Offer to 1st-Degree Relatives
Genetic CounsellingMandatory Pre/Post-Test; Reproductive Options (PGD)

7. Management by Organ System

Parathyroid (PHPT in MEN1)

AspectDetails
Pathology4-Gland Hyperplasia (90%) (vs 80-85% Single Adenoma in Sporadic)
SurgerySubtotal (3.5-Gland) Parathyroidectomy + Autotransplant (Forearm)
Recurrence15-30% (Due to Hyperplasia / Remnant Tissue)
IOPTH>50% Drop at 10min Post-Excision (Miami Criterion)

Pituitary Adenoma

TypeManagement
ProlactinomaCabergoline 1st Line (Dose Titration); Surgery if Macro + Visual Defects
Acromegaly (GH)TSS (1st Line) → SST Analogues / Pegvisomant
Non-FunctioningSurgery if Macro + Mass Effect / Apoplexy Risk

Pancreatic NETs

Tumour TypeManagement
Gastrinoma (ZES)High-Dose PPI + Surgical Resection (Duodenotomy + Enucleation)
InsulinomaEnucleation (Curative; 90% Benign)
Non-Functioning >2cmSurgical Resection (Malignant Potential)
<2cmSurveillance (EUS/MRI q6-12mo)

8. Surveillance — Lifetime Protocol

Age to StartSurveillanceFrequency
5-10 YearsSerum Calcium, PTHAnnual
10-15 YearsPituitary Hormones, MRI PituitaryAnnual / 3-Yearly
20 YearsFasting Gut Hormones (Gastrin, Insulin, Glucagon, PP, CgA)Annual
20 YearsCT/MRI Pancreas, Chest CT1-3 Years
All AgesAdrenal ImagingEvery 2-3 Years
Post-Transplant / Post-PTXLifelong Surveillance

9. Genetic Counselling

AspectRecommendation
TestingMEN1 Sequencing (If Clinical Diagnosis / Family History)
Predictive TestingOffer to 1st-Degree Relatives (If Mutation Known)
PrenatalCVS/Amniocentesis (If Parental Mutation Known)
ReproductivePGD (Preimplantation Genetic Diagnosis) Available

10. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
MEN1 = 3 PsParathyroid, Pituitary, Pancreas
GeneMEN1 (Menin) — Chromosome 11q13, Tumour Suppressor
InheritanceAD; Penetrance >95% by 50
PHPT in MEN1Multigland Hyperplasia (90%) → Subtotal Parathyroidectomy
PituitaryProlactinoma > Acromegaly > NFPA > Cushing
Pancreatic NETsGastrinoma (ZES) Most Common Functional; Non-Func >2cm → Surgery
GastrinomaDuodenal > Pancreatic; Multiple; Secretin Test Positive
AdrenalIncidentalomas Common (20-40%); Usually Non-Functioning
Thymic CarcinoidMEN1-Associated; Malignant; Male > Female
Carcinoid SyndromeSee NET Note
SurveillanceLifelong; Annual Ca/PTH, Pituitary Hormones; q3yr MRI Pituitary/CT Pancreas
GeneticsMEN1 Gene (Menin); AD; Penetrance >95% by 50
Genetic CounsellingOffer Predictive Testing to 1st-Degree Relatives

11. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
MEN1 vs MEN2AMEN1 = 3Ps (Parathyroid, Pituitary, Pancreas); MEN2A = MTC + Phaeo + HPT
MEN1 PHPT vs Sporadic PHPTMEN1 = Multigland Hyperplasia (90%); Sporadic = Single Adenoma (80%)
Gastrinoma LocationDuodenal > Pancreatic in MEN1 (Opposite Sporadic)
Non-Functioning pNET>2cm → Surgery (Malignant Potential); <2cm → Surveillance
Adrenal in MEN1Usually Non-Functioning Incidentaloma; Rarely Functional
MEN1 vs MEN2MEN1 = Parathyroid + Pituitary + Pancreas; MEN2 = MTC + Phaeo ± HPT

12. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((MEN1))
    Genetics
      MEN1 (Menin) - 11q13
      AD, Penetrance >95% by 50
    3 Ps
      Parathyroid: Hyperplasia 90% → Subtotal PTX
      Pituitary: Prolactinoma > Acromegaly > NFPA
      Pancreas: Gastrinoma (Duodenal), Insulinoma, Non-Func
    Associated
      Adrenal (Incidentaloma)
      Thymic Carcinoid (Male)
      Bronchial Carcinoid (Female)
      Lipomas, Angiofibromas
    Screening
      Ca/PTH Annual from 5y
      Pituitary Hormones Annual
      Gut Hormones Annual from 20y
      MRI Pituitary q3yr
      CT Pancreas/Chest q1-3yr
    Surgery
      PTX: Subtotal (3.5 Glands)
      Pituitary: TSS
      Pancreas: Enucleation (Insulinoma), Duodenotomy (Gastrinoma)
    Genetics
      MEN1 Gene (Menin) - Tumour Suppressor
      AD, Penetrance >95%
      Predictive Testing for Relatives

13. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
MEN1 = 3 PsParathyroid, Pituitary, Pancreas
GeneMEN1 (Menin) — Chr 11q13; Tumour Suppressor
InheritanceAD; Penetrance >95% by Age 50
PHPT in MEN14-Gland Hyperplasia (90%) → Subtotal Parathyroidectomy
Pituitary AdenomaProlactinoma > Acromegaly > Non-Functioning
Gastrinoma in MEN1Duodenal (Not Pancreatic); Multiple; ZES
InsulinomaUsually Benign; Enucleation Curative
Non-Functioning pNET>2cm → Surgery (Malignant Potential)
AdrenalIncidentaloma Common (20-40%); Usually Non-Functioning
Thymic CarcinoidMale Predominant; Often Malignant
SurveillanceLifelong; Annual Ca/PTH; q3yr MRI Pituitary; q1-3yr CT Pancreas
GeneticsMEN1 (Menin) AD; Penetrance >95% by 50; Predictive Testing Offered

14. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia|Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[MEN2A]], [[MEN2B]], [[MEN4]], [[Primary Hyperparathyroidism]], [[Pituitary Adenomas: Functioning]], [[Pancreatic NETs]], [[Neuroendocrine Tumours]]

15. MCQs (10)

  1. MEN1 =

A. Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1: 3 Ps (Parathyroid, Pituitary, Pancreas); MEN1 gene (menin)

B. MEN2A

C. MEN2B

D. MEN4

E. Isolated

  1. MEN1 parathyroid:

A. Primary hyperparathyroidism (90-95%); multigland hyperplasia

B. Adenoma only

C. Normal

D. Parathyroid cancer

E. Hypoparathyroidism

  1. MEN1 pituitary:

A. Prolactinoma commonest; GH-secreting; non-functioning; hormonal excess

B. No pituitary involvement

C. Only Cushing

D. Only TSHoma

E. Only gonadotroph

  1. MEN1 pancreas:

A. Gastrinoma (Zollinger-Ellison) commonest; insulinoma; glucagonoma; non-functional

B. No pancreatic involvement

C. Only insulinoma

D. Only glucagonoma

E. Only non-functional

  1. MEN1 genetics:

A. MEN1 gene (chromosome 11); menin tumour suppressor; autosomal dominant

B. RET

C. CDKN1B

D. VHL

E. NF1

  1. MEN1 screening:

A. Annual calcium, PTH, prolactin, IGF-1, fasting gastrin; MRI pituitary q3yr; CT abdomen q3yr

B. No screening needed

C. Only calcium

D. Only PTH

E. One-time only

  1. MEN1 gastrinoma:

A. Zollinger-Ellison syndrome; multiple duodenal tumours; high gastrin; PPI treatment

B. Rare

C. Always malignant

D. No hypergastrinaemia

E. No treatment needed

  1. MEN1 insulinoma:

A. Often multiple; benign; hypoglycaemia; surgery if localised

B. Always malignant

C. Rare

D. No surgery needed

E. Diazoxide only

  1. MEN1 other:

A. Adrenal cortical adenomas, lipomas, thymic carcinoids, facial angiofibromas, collagenomas

B. No other features

C. Only medullary thyroid cancer

D. Only phaeochromocytoma

E. Only parathyroid carcinoma

  1. MEN1 prophylaxis:

A. Total parathyroidectomy with autotransplant if hyperparathyroidism; surveillance for others

B. No prophylaxis

C. Total thyroidectomy

D. Prophylactic pancreatectomy

E. Prophylactic hypophysectomy

16. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 30yo woman: hypercalcaemia, prolactinoma, gastrinoma. Father had similar. Gene?

A. MEN1 (menin)

B. RET

C. CDKN1B

D. VHL

E. NF1

  1. MEN1 patient: hyperparathyroidism, 4-gland hyperplasia. Surgery?

A. Subtotal parathyroidectomy (3.5 glands) + autotransplant

B. Total parathyroidectomy

C. Single gland removal

D. Cinacalcet only

E. Observation

  1. MEN1 patient: gastrinoma, multiple duodenal tumours. Management?

A. High-dose PPI; surgery if localised; somatostatin analogues if metastatic

B. Total gastrectomy

C. Total pancreatectomy

D. PPI only

E. Observation

  1. MEN1 family screening: 20yo asymptomatic relative. Tests?

A. Calcium, PTH, prolactin, IGF-1, gastrin; MRI pituitary; CT abdomen

B. Only calcium

C. Only PTH

D. Only prolactin

E. No screening until symptomatic

  1. MEN1 pancreaticNET: non-functional 2cm. Management?

A. Surveillance (CT/MRI q6-12mo); surgery if >2cm or symptomatic

B. Immediate surgery

C. PRRT

D. Chemo

E. Observation only

17. Flashcards

  • Q: MEN1

A: 3 Ps: Parathyroid, Pituitary, Pancreas; MEN1 gene (menin); AD

  • Q: Parathyroid

A: Hyperparathyroidism 90-95%; multigland hyperplasia

  • Q: Pituitary

A: Prolactinoma commonest; also GH, non-functioning

  • Q: Pancreas

A: Gastrinoma (ZES) commonest; insulinoma; glucagonoma; non-functional

  • Q: Genetics

A: MEN1 gene (chromosome 11); menin; tumour suppressor; AD

  • Q: Screening

A: Annual Ca, PTH, PRL, IGF-1, gastrin; MRI pituitary q3yr; CT abdomen q3yr

  • Q: Gastrinoma

A: ZES; multiple duodenal tumours; high gastrin; PPI + surgery if localised

  • Q: Insulinoma

A: Often multiple; benign; hypoglycaemia; surgery if localised

  • Q: Surveillance

A: Annual Ca, PTH, PRL, IGF-1, gastrin; MRI pituitary q3yr; CT abdomen q3yr

  • Q: Prophylaxis

A: Total parathyroidectomy + autotransplant for HPT; surveillance for others

18. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1: 3 Ps (Parathyroid, Pituitary, Pancreas); MEN1 gene (menin) — MEN1 =
  1. Primary hyperparathyroidism (90-95%); multigland hyperplasia — MEN1 parathyroid:
  1. Prolactinoma commonest; GH-secreting; non-functioning; hormonal excess — MEN1 pituitary:
  1. Gastrinoma (Zollinger-Ellison) commonest; insulinoma; glucagonoma; non-functional — MEN1 pancreas:
  1. MEN1 gene (chromosome 11); menin tumour suppressor; autosomal dominant — MEN1 genetics:
  1. Annual calcium, PTH, prolactin, IGF-1, fasting gastrin; MRI pituitary q3yr; CT abdomen q3yr — MEN1 screening:
  1. Zollinger-Ellison syndrome; multiple duodenal tumours; high gastrin; PPI treatment — MEN1 gastrinoma:
  1. Often multiple; benign; hypoglycaemia; surgery if localised — MEN1 insulinoma:
  1. Adrenal cortical adenomas, lipomas, thymic carcinoids, facial angiofibromas, collagenomas — MEN1 other:
  1. Total parathyroidectomy with autotransplant if hyperparathyroidism; surveillance for others — MEN1 prophylaxis:

SBAs

  1. MEN1 (menin) — 30yo woman: hypercalcaemia, prolactinoma, gastrinoma. Father had similar. Gene?
  1. Subtotal parathyroidectomy (3.5 glands) + autotransplant — MEN1 patient: hyperparathyroidism, 4-gland hyperplasia. Surgery?
  1. High-dose PPI; surgery if localised; somatostatin analogues if metastatic — MEN1 patient: gastrinoma, multiple duodenal tumours. Management?
  1. Calcium, PTH, prolactin, IGF-1, gastrin; MRI pituitary; CT abdomen — MEN1 family screening: 20yo asymptomatic relative. Tests?
  1. Surveillance (CT/MRI q6-12mo); surgery if >2cm or symptomatic — MEN1 pancreaticNET: non-functional 2cm. Management?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.8.02 Men2A
[!info]
MEN2A = Sipple Syndrome. Autosomal Dominant (Penetrance ~95% by Age 50). RET Proto-Oncogene (Chromosome 10q11.2). Triad: MTC + Phaeochromocytoma + Primary Hyperparathyroidism. RET Codon 634 (Cysteine) = 85% of Mutations.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Define MEN2A and its classic triad
  • [ ] Apply RET codon-based risk stratification for prophylactic thyroidectomy
  • [ ] Manage each component (MTC, Phaeochromocytoma, PHPT)
  • [ ] Apply genetic testing and family screening protocols
  • [ ] Counsel on prophylactic thyroidectomy timing based on RET codon

2. Genetics & Pathophysiology

FeatureDetails
GeneRET Proto-Oncogene (Chromosome 10q11.2)
InheritanceAutosomal Dominant (Penetrance ~95% by Age 50)
Mutation TypeGain-of-Function (Activating) — Constitutive RET Activation
Key Codons634 (Cysteine) — 85% of MEN2A; 609, 611, 618, 620, 630 (Cysteine-Rich Domain)
ProteinReceptor Tyrosine Kinase → Constitutive Activation → Cell Proliferation
PenetranceMTC ~100% by Age 70; Phaeo ~50%; PHPT ~20-30%

3. MEN2A Triad — Clinical Features

ComponentFrequencyKey Features
Medullary Thyroid Cancer (MTC)~100% (Penetrance ~100% by Age 70)Earliest Manifestation; C-Cell Hyperplasia → Nodule → MTC
Phaeochromocytoma~50%Often Bilateral; May Be Asymptomatic; Catecholamine Excess
Primary Hyperparathyroidism20-30%Usually Mild; Multigland Hyperplasia (vs Single Adenoma)

4. RET Codon Risk Stratification (ATA Guidelines)

Risk LevelRET CodonsMTC RiskProphylactic Thyroidectomy Timing
Highest (Level A)M918T (Exon 16), A883F (Exon 15)Highest (Aggressive, Early Metastasis)<1 Year (Ideally 6-12 Months)
High (Level B)Codon 634 (Cysteine) — 85% of MEN2AHigh5 Years (or Earlier if Calcitonin ↑)
Moderate (Level C)Codons 609, 611, 618, 620, 630 (Cysteine-Rich Domain)Moderate5-10 Years (Calcitonin-Guided)
Lower (Level D)Codons 768, 804, 891Lower10-20 Years (Calcitonin-Guided)

5. Component Management

Medullary Thyroid Cancer (MTC)

AspectDetails
PresentationThyroid Nodule, Diarrhoea (Calcitonin/Serotonin), Flushing, Bone Pain (Mets)
BiochemicalCalcitonin ↑↑ (Correlates with Tumour Burden); CEA ↑
GeneticsRET Mutation Analysis → Prophylactic Thyroidectomy Timing
Prophylactic ThyroidectomyTotal Thyroidectomy + Central Neck Dissection (Level VI)
Post-OpCalcitonin Monitoring (Basal + Pentagastrin/Calcium Stimulation); Undetectable = Cure
RecurrenceBasal Calcitonin >150 pg/mL → Structural Recurrence (Imaging)

Phaeochromocytoma (MEN2A)

FeatureDetails
Frequency~50% (Often Bilateral)
BiochemistryPlasma Free Metanephrines / Normetanephrines (Gold Standard)
Imaging⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT (Best); CT/MRI Adrenal; ¹²³I-MIBG
Pre-OpAlpha-Blockade First (Phenoxybenzamine 10mg BD → Titrate) → Beta-Blocker After Alpha
SurgeryLaparoscopic Adrenalectomy (Cortical-Sparing if Bilateral)

Primary Hyperparathyroidism (MEN2A)

FeatureDetails
Frequency20-30%
PathologyMultigland Hyperplasia (vs Single Adenoma)
BiochemistryMild Hypercalcaemia; PTH Inappropriately Normal/High
SurgerySubtotal (3.5-Gland) Parathyroidectomy + Autotransplant (Forearm)

6. Prophylactic Thyroidectomy — Timing Guidelines (ATA)

RET Mutation LevelCodonsThyroidectomy AgeProcedure
A (Highest)M918T, A883F<1 Year (6-12 Months)Total Thyroidectomy + Central Neck Dissection
B (High)Codon 634 (85% MEN2A)5 Years (or Earlier if Calcitonin ↑)Total Thyroidectomy + CND
C (Moderate)609, 611, 618, 620, 6305-10 Years (Calcitonin-Guided)Total Thyroidectomy + CND
D (Lower)768, 804, 89110-20 Years (Calcitonin-Guided)Total Thyroidectomy + CND

Key: Calcitonin Monitoring from Infancy (q6-12mo); Rising Calcitonin → Earlier Surgery.

7. Phaeochromocytoma in MEN2A — Specifics

FeatureDetails
Bilaterality50-70% (vs 10% Sporadic)
GeneticsRET Codon 634 (Common); Also SDHB/SDHD in Sporadic
Pre-Op Alpha-BlockadePhenoxybenzamine 10mg BD → Titrate 20-80mg/day Over 2-3 Weeks
Beta-BlockadeONLY After Alpha-Blockade Established (Else Unopposed α → Crisis)
SurgeryCortical-Sparing Adrenalectomy (If Bilateral) → Preserve Adrenal Function

8. Genetic Testing & Family Screening

AspectDetails
GeneRET (Chromosome 10q11.2) — Proto-Oncogene
InheritanceAutosomal Dominant (Penetrance ~95% by Age 50)
Genetic TestingRET Sequencing (All Patients + 1st-Degree Relatives)
Prenatal / PreimplantationAvailable (If Familial Mutation Known)
Cascade Screening50% Risk to Offspring; Offer Predictive Testing from Childhood

9. Surveillance Protocol (MEN2A)

ComponentFrequencyMethod
Calcitonin6-12 Monthly (From Infancy)Basal + Pentagastrin/Calcium Stimulation
Plasma MetanephrinesAnnualPlasma Free Metanephrines
Adrenal ImagingAnnualCT/MRI Adrenal + ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET (If Suspect Phaeo)
Neck UltrasoundAnnual (From Age 5)Thyroid Nodules, Lymph Nodes
Calcium + PTHAnnualFrom Age 10
Genetic CounsellingAt DiagnosisCascade Testing of Relatives

10. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
MEN2A TriadMTC + Phaeochromocytoma + Primary HPT
GeneRET Proto-Oncogene (Chromosome 10q11.2)
InheritanceAutosomal Dominant (Penetrance ~95% by 50)
Most Common MutationCodon 634 (Cysteine) — 85% of MEN2A
Prophylactic ThyroidectomyCodon 634 → Age 5 Years; M918T/A883F → <1 Year
MTCCalcitonin = Tumour Marker; Prophylactic Thyroidectomy by Genotype
Phaeochromocytoma in MEN2A50% Bilateral; Alpha-Blockade First (Phenoxybenzamine)
HPT in MEN2AMultigland Hyperplasia; Subtotal PTX + Autotransplant
RET Codon 63485% of MEN2A; Prophylactic Thyroidectomy at Age 5
M918T / A883FHighest Risk; Thyroidectomy <1 Year
Calcitonin MonitoringBasal + Stimulated (Pentagastrin/Ca); q6-12mo
Phaeochromocytoma Pre-OpAlpha-Blockade FIRST (Phenoxybenzamine) → Then Beta-Blocker
Bilateral Phaeo in MEN2ACortical-Sparing Adrenalectomy Preferred
Genetic TestingRET Sequencing; Cascade Testing of Relatives

11. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
MEN2A vs MEN2BMEN2A = MTC + Phaeo + HPT; MEN2B = MTC + Phaeo + Marfanoid/Neuromas (No HPT)
MEN1 vs MEN2AMEN1 = Parathyroid + Pituitary + Pancreas (3Ps); MEN2A = MTC + Phaeo + Hyperparathyroidism
Codon 634Most Common MEN2A (85%); Prophylactic Thyroidectomy at Age 5
M918T / A883FHighest Risk; Thyroidectomy <1 Year
Prophylactic ThyroidectomyNot Optional — Prevents MTC (Near 100% Penetrance)
Calcitonin Monitoringq6-12mo; Basal + Stimulated (Pentagastrin/Ca²⁺)
Phaeochromocytoma Pre-OpAlpha-Blockade FIRST (Phenoxybenzamine 2+ Weeks) → Then Beta-Blocker
Bilateral PhaeoCortical-Sparing Adrenalectomy (Preserve Adrenal Function)
Calcitonin Post-OpUndetectable = Cure; Rising = Recurrence
Genetic Counselling50% Offspring Risk; Cascade Screening; Prenatal Available

12. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((MEN2A - Sipple Syndrome))
    Genetics
      RET Proto-Oncogene (10q11.2)
      AD, Penetrance ~95% by 50
      Codon 634 (85%) vs M918T/A883F
    Triad
      MTC (100% Penetrance)
      Phaeochromocytoma (50%)
      Primary HPT (20-30%)
    RET Codon Risk
      A: M918T, A883F → Thyroidectomy <1yr
      B: Codon 634 (85%) → Thyroidectomy Age 5
      C: 609,611,618,620,630 → 5-10yr
      D: 768,804,891 → 10-20yr
    MTC
      Calcitonin ↑↑ (Tumour Marker)
      Prophylactic Thyroidectomy by Codon
      Post-Op: Calcitonin Surveillance
    Phaeochromocytoma
      50% Bilateral
      Alpha-Blockade First (Phenoxybenzamine)
      Cortical-Sparing Adrenalectomy
      Genetic: RET, SDHB (if Sporadic)
    HPT
      Multigland Hyperplasia
      Subtotal PTX + Autotransplant
    Genetics
      RET Proto-Oncogene (10q11.2)
      AD, Penetrance ~95%
      Cascade Screening (50% Offspring)
    Surveillance
      Calcitonin q6-12mo (Basal + Stim)
      Metanephrines Annual
      Neck US Annual
      Ca/PTH Annual

13. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
MEN2A TriadMTC + Phaeochromocytoma + Primary HPT
GeneRET Proto-Oncogene (Chromosome 10q11.2)
InheritanceAutosomal Dominant (Penetrance ~95% by Age 50)
Most Common MutationCodon 634 (Cysteine) — 85% of MEN2A
Prophylactic ThyroidectomyCodon 634 → Age 5; M918T/A883F → <1 Year
MTC SurveillanceCalcitonin q6-12mo (Basal + Stimulated)
Phaeochromocytoma in MEN2ABilateral in 50%; Alpha-Blockade First; Cortical-Sparing Adrenalectomy
HPT in MEN2AMultigland Hyperplasia; Subtotal Parathyroidectomy + Autotransplant
RET Codon 63485% of MEN2A; Prophylactic Thyroidectomy at Age 5
M918T / A883FHighest Risk; Thyroidectomy <1 Year
Calcitonin Monitoringq6-12mo (Basal + Stimulated); Undetectable Post-Op = Cure
Phaeo Pre-OpAlpha-Blockade FIRST (Phenoxybenzamine 2+ Weeks) → Beta-Blocker
Bilateral PhaeoCortical-Sparing Adrenalectomy (Preserve Function)
Genetic TestingRET Sequencing; Cascade Screening (50% Offspring Risk)
HPT in MEN2AMultigland Hyperplasia (Not Single Adenoma); Subtotal PTX

15. MCQs (10)

  1. MEN2A =

A. Medullary Thyroid Cancer + Phaeochromocytoma + Primary Hyperparathyroidism; RET gene

B. MEN1

C. MEN2B

D. MEN4

E. Isolated

  1. MEN2A MTC:

A. 100% penetrance; often multifocal/bilateral; C-cell hyperplasia precursor

B. Rare

C. Unilateral only

D. Never spreads

E. No genetic basis

  1. MEN2A phaeochromocytoma:

A. ~50% penetrance; often bilateral; RET codon 634 mutation

B. Never

C. Always unilateral

D. Malignant always

E. Childhood only

  1. MEN2A hyperparathyroidism:

A. Primary HPT (hyperplasia/adenoma); ~20-30% penetrance; usually mild

B. Never

C. Always severe

D. Always carcinoma

E. Hypoparathyroidism

  1. MEN2A RET mutations:

A. Codons 609, 611, 618, 620, 630, 634 (cysteine-rich domain)

B. Random

C. Only codon 634

D. Only codon 918

E. No genetic basis

  1. MEN2A MTC prophylaxis:

A. Prophylactic thyroidectomy guided by RET codon; codon 634 -> age 5yr; codon 609/611/618/620/630 -> age 10-15yr

B. No prophylaxis

C. Total thyroidectomy at birth

D. Surgery only if symptomatic

E. RAI only

  1. MEN2A phaeochromocytoma management:

A. Pre-op alpha-blockade (phenoxybenzamine) -> surgery; bilateral in 50%; genetic testing

B. Surgery without blockade

C. RAI

D. Observation

E. Metyrosine only

  1. MEN2A hyperparathyroidism surgery:

A. Subtotal parathyroidectomy (3.5 glands) + autotransplant if indicated

B. Total parathyroidectomy

C. Single gland removal

D. Cinacalcet only

E. Observation

  1. MEN2A vs MEN2B:

A. MEN2A: MTC + phaeo + HPT; MEN2B: MTC + phaeo + marfanoid + mucosal neuromas; NO HPT

B. Same

C. MEN2A has marfanoid

D. MEN2B has HPT

E. Both same

  1. MEN2A surveillance:

A. Annual calcitonin, CEA, neck US; plasma metanephrines; calcium/PTH

B. No surveillance

C. Only calcitonin

D. Only CEA

E. Only neck US

16. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 25yo woman: MTC, phaeochromocytoma, hyperparathyroidism. RET codon 634. Family screening?

A. RET testing for family; prophylactic thyroidectomy if positive

B. Calcitonin only

C. CEA only

D. No screening

E. Total thyroidectomy for all

  1. Same patient: prophylactic thyroidectomy age?

A. 5 years (codon 634 high-risk)

B. Birth

C. 10 years

D. Adulthood

E. Only if calcitonin elevated

  1. MEN2A phaeochromocytoma: pre-op preparation?

A. Alpha-blockade (phenoxybenzamine) 2-3 weeks -> surgery

B. Beta-blocker first

C. Surgery without blockade

D. RAI

E. Observation

  1. MEN2A family: RET codon 609 mutation. Prophylactic thyroidectomy age?

A. 10-15 years (moderate risk)

B. Birth

C. 5 years

D. 20 years

E. Only if calcitonin elevated

  1. MEN2A vs MEN2B: key difference?

A. MEN2A: HPT present; MEN2B: marfanoid + mucosal neuromas + NO HPT

B. MEN2B has HPT

C. MEN2A has marfanoid

D. Both same

E. MEN2B no phaeo

17. Flashcards

  • Q: MEN2A

A: RET; MTC + Phaeo + HPT (3 Ps of MEN2A)

  • Q: MTC in MEN2A

A: 100% penetrance; multifocal/bilateral; C-cell hyperplasia precursor

  • Q: Phaeo in MEN2A

A: 50% penetrance; bilateral 50%; codon 634 common

  • Q: HPT in MEN2A

A: 20-30% penetrance; mild; hyperplasia/adenoma

  • Q: RET mutations

A: Codon 634 = high risk (early thyroidectomy 5yr); 609/611/618/620/630 = moderate (10-15yr)

  • Q: Prophylactic thyroidectomy

A: Codon 634: age 5yr; Codons 609/611/618/620/630: age 10-15yr

  • Q: Phaeo management

A: Alpha-blockade (phenoxybenzamine) >=2 weeks -> surgery; bilateral 50%

  • Q: HPT surgery

A: Subtotal (3.5 glands) + autotransplant if indicated

  • Q: Genetics

A: RET proto-oncogene; AD; cysteine-rich domain mutations

  • Q: Surveillance

A: Annual calcitonin, CEA, neck US, metanephrines, Ca/PTH

18. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Medullary Thyroid Cancer + Phaeochromocytoma + Primary Hyperparathyroidism; RET gene — MEN2A =
  1. 100% penetrance; often multifocal/bilateral; C-cell hyperplasia precursor — MEN2A MTC:
  1. ~50% penetrance; often bilateral; RET codon 634 mutation — MEN2A phaeochromocytoma:
  1. Primary HPT (hyperplasia/adenoma); ~20-30% penetrance; usually mild — MEN2A hyperparathyroidism:
  1. Codons 609, 611, 618, 620, 630, 634 (cysteine-rich domain) — MEN2A RET mutations:
  1. Prophylactic thyroidectomy guided by RET codon; codon 634 -> age 5yr; codon 609/611/618/620/630 -> age 10-15yr — MEN2A MTC prophylaxis:
  1. Pre-op alpha-blockade (phenoxybenzamine) -> surgery; bilateral in 50%; genetic testing — MEN2A phaeochromocytoma management:
  1. Subtotal parathyroidectomy (3.5 glands) + autotransplant if indicated — MEN2A hyperparathyroidism surgery:
  1. MEN2A: MTC + phaeo + HPT; MEN2B: MTC + phaeo + marfanoid + mucosal neuromas; NO HPT — MEN2A vs MEN2B:
  1. Annual calcitonin, CEA, neck US; plasma metanephrines; calcium/PTH — MEN2A surveillance:

SBAs

  1. RET testing for family; prophylactic thyroidectomy if positive — 25yo woman: MTC, phaeochromocytoma, hyperparathyroidism. RET codon 634. Family screening?
  1. 5 years (codon 634 high-risk) — Same patient: prophylactic thyroidectomy age?
  1. Alpha-blockade (phenoxybenzamine) 2-3 weeks -> surgery — MEN2A phaeochromocytoma: pre-op preparation?
  1. 10-15 years (moderate risk) — MEN2A family: RET codon 609 mutation. Prophylactic thyroidectomy age?
  1. MEN2A: HPT present; MEN2B: marfanoid + mucosal neuromas + NO HPT — MEN2A vs MEN2B: key difference?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.8.03 Men2B
[!info]
MEN2B = Wagenmann-Froehlich Syndrome. RET M918T Mutation (95%). Most Aggressive MEN2 Subtype. Triad: MTC + Phaeochromocytoma + Marfanoid Habitus + Mucosal Neuromas. NO Primary Hyperparathyroidism. Prophylactic Thyroidectomy <1 Year.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise the distinct clinical phenotype of MEN2B (Marfanoid habitus, mucosal neuromas)
  • [ ] Apply RET M918T-based prophylactic thyroidectomy timing (<1 year)
  • [ ] Differentiate MEN2B from MEN2A (No HPT, Marfanoid habitus, mucosal neuromas)
  • [ ] Manage MTC, phaeochromocytoma in MEN2B context
  • [ ] Apply genetic counselling and family screening

2. Genetics & Pathophysiology

FeatureDetails
GeneRET Proto-Oncogene (Chromosome 10q11.2)
MutationM918T (Exon 16) — 95%; Rarely A883F (Exon 15)
MechanismGain-of-Function → Constitutive RET Kinase Activation → MAPK/PI3K Pathways
InheritanceAutosomal Dominant (Penetrance ~100% for MTC by Adulthood)
PenetranceMTC ~100%; Phaeochromocytoma ~50%; No HPT

3. Clinical Features — MEN2B Phenotype

FeatureDetails
Medullary Thyroid Cancer (MTC)100% Penetrance; Earliest Manifestation; Aggressive
Phaeochromocytoma~50%; Often Bilateral; Similar to MEN2A
Primary HyperparathyroidismABSENT (Key Distinction from MEN2A)
Marfanoid HabitusTall, Slender, Long Limbs, Arachnodactyly, High-Arched Palate, Pectus Excavatum
Mucosal NeuromasPathognomonic — Lips, Tongue, Buccal Mucosa, Conjunctiva; Multiple, Nodular, 1-5mm
GastrointestinalGanglioneuromatosis (Intestinal) → Constipation, Megaureter, Megacolon (Hirschsprung-like)
OcularMedullated Corneal Nerves (Slit-Lamp); Thickened Corneal Nerves

4. RET Mutation — MEN2B Specific

FeatureDetails
MutationM918T (Exon 16) — 95% of MEN2B
RareA883F (Exon 15) — ~5%
MechanismConstitutive RET Kinase Activation → MAPK/PI3K Pathways
Phenotype SeverityMost Aggressive MEN2 Subtype (Earlier Onset, More Aggressive MTC)

RET Codon Risk Stratification (ATA Guidelines)

Risk LevelRET CodonMTC RiskProphylactic Thyroidectomy
Highest (Level A)M918T (Exon 16)Highest (Aggressive, Early Metastasis)<1 Year (6-12 Months)
High (Level B)Codon 634 (Cysteine)High5 Years
Moderate (Level C)609, 611, 618, 620, 630, 768, 804, 891Moderate5-10 Years

MEN2B = Level A (M918T) → Prophylactic Thyroidectomy <1 Year (Ideally 6-12 Months).

5. Clinical Features — MEN2B Distinctive Phenotype

Mucosal Neuromas (Pathognomonic)

FeatureDetails
LocationLips, Tongue, Buccal Mucosa, Conjunctiva, Eyelids
AppearanceMultiple, Polypoid, Pedunculated, 1-5mm
OnsetEarly Childhood (Often <10 Years)
SignificancePathognomonic for MEN2B (vs MEN2A)

Marfanoid Habitus

FeatureDetails
SkeletalTall, Slender, Dolichostenomelia, Arachnodactyly, Pectus Excavatum, High-Arched Palate
JointsJoint Laxity, Pes Planus, Scoliosis
FaceHigh Forehead, Malar Flattening, Prognathism

Gastrointestinal

FeatureDetails
GanglioneuromatosisDiffuse Intestinal Ganglioneuromatosis → Constipation, Megacolon, Megaloesophagus
Hirschsprung-LikeAganglionic Segments → Functional Obstruction
MegalocorneaEnlarged Cornea (>13mm)

Ocular

FeatureDetails
Medullated Corneal NervesSlit-Lamp Finding — Thickened, Visible Corneal Nerves
SignificanceHighly Specific for MEN2B

6. MTC in MEN2B — Most Aggressive

FeatureDetails
Penetrance100% (Earliest Onset — Often Childhood)
AggressivenessHighest of All MEN2 Subtypes; Early Metastasis (Nodes, Liver, Lung, Bone)
CalcitoninVery High at Diagnosis; Exponential Rise
Prophylactic Thyroidectomy<1 Year (6-12 Months) — Earliest of All RET Mutations

7. Phaeochromocytoma in MEN2B

FeatureDetails
Frequency~50% (Similar to MEN2A)
BilateralityCommon (50-70%)
BiochemistryPlasma Free Metanephrines / Normetanephrines
Imaging⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT (Best)
Pre-OpAlpha-Blockade First (Phenoxybenzamine) → Beta-Blockade After
SurgeryLaparoscopic Adrenalectomy; Cortical-Sparing if Bilateral

8. Prophylactic Thyroidectomy — MEN2B Specific

AspectDetails
RET MutationM918T (Exon 16) — 95%
Risk LevelLevel A (Highest) — ATA Guidelines
Thyroidectomy Timing<1 Year (Ideally 6-12 Months)
ProcedureTotal Thyroidectomy + Central Neck Dissection (Level VI)
Post-OpCalcitonin Undetectable = Cure; Monitor Basal + Stimulated Calcitonin
LevothyroxineLifelong Replacement (Start Post-Op)

9. MEN2B vs MEN2A — Key Differences

FeatureMEN2BMEN2A (Sipple)
MTC100% (Earliest, Most Aggressive)95% (Later Onset)
Phaeochromocytoma50% (Bilateral Common)50% (Bilateral Common)
Primary HPTABSENT (Key Difference)Present (20-30%)
Marfanoid HabitusPresent (Tall, Arachnodactyly)Absent
Mucosal NeuromasPresent (Pathognomonic)Absent
GastrointestinalGanglioneuromatosis (Megacolon)Absent
RET MutationM918T (95%)Codon 634 (85%)
Prophylactic Thyroidectomy<1 Year (6-12 Months)Age 5 (Codon 634)
GeneticsRET M918T (95%)Codon 634 (85%)

10. Management — MEN2B Specific

Medullary Thyroid Cancer (MTC)

AspectDetails
Prophylactic Thyroidectomy<1 Year (6-12 Months) — Earliest of All RET Mutations
SurgeryTotal Thyroidectomy + Central Neck Dissection (Level VI)
Post-OpLevothyroxine Replacement; Calcitonin Monitoring (Basal + Stimulated)

Phaeochromocytoma

FeatureDetails
BiochemistryFree Metanephrines / Normetanephrines
Imaging⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT (Gold Standard)
Pre-OpAlpha-Blockade First (Phenoxybenzamine) → Beta-Blocker After
SurgeryLaparoscopic Adrenalectomy; Cortical-Sparing if Bilateral

Mucosal Neuromas / GI

FeatureManagement
Mucosal NeuromasSymptomatic (Bleeding, Irritation) → Laser Excision
GanglioneuromatosisConstipation/Megacolon → Laxatives, Prokinetics; Surgery if Obstruction

11. Genetic Testing & Screening

AspectDetails
GeneRET (M918T 95%)
InheritanceAutosomal Dominant
Genetic TestingRET Sequencing (All Index Cases + 1st-Degree Relatives)
PrenatalCVS/Amniocentesis (If Familial Mutation Known)
Cascade Screening1st-Degree Relatives → RET Testing; 50% Risk

12. Surveillance Protocol (MEN2B)

ComponentFrequencyMethod
Calcitoninq6-12mo (Basal + Stimulated)Pentagastrin/Ca²⁺ Stimulation
MetanephrinesAnnualPlasma Free Metanephrines / ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET
Thyroid/Neck USAnnualFrom Infancy
Adrenal CT/MRIAnnual⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET Preferred
GI SurveillanceAnnualGI Symptoms → Endoscopy/Imaging

13. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
MEN2B PhenotypeMarfanoid Habitus + Mucosal Neuromas + MTC + Phaeo (No HPT)
PathognomonicMucosal Neuromas (Lips, Tongue, Buccal, Conjunctiva)
RET MutationM918T (Exon 16) — 95%; A883F (Exon 15) Rare
Prophylactic Thyroidectomy<1 Year (6-12 Months) — Earliest of All RET Mutations
MEN2B vs MEN2AMEN2B = Marfanoid + Neuromas + NO HPT; MEN2A = HPT Present
RET MutationM918T (Exon 16) — 95%; A883F (5%)
Prophylactic Thyroidectomy<1 Year (6-12 Months) — Earliest of All RET Mutations
MEN2B vs MEN2AMEN2B: Marfanoid + Neuromas + NO HPT; MEN2A: HPT Present, No Marfanoid
Mucosal NeuromasPathognomonic (Lips, Tongue, Buccal, Conjunctiva)
GanglioneuromatosisGI Involvement → Constipation, Megacolon
Medullary Thyroid Cancer100% Penetrance; Most Aggressive; Prophylactic Thyroidectomy <1 Year
RET MutationM918T (Exon 16) = 95%; Level A Risk (Highest)
PhaeochromocytomaBilateral 50%; Alpha-Blockade First; Cortical-Sparing Surgery
SurveillanceCalcitonin q6-12mo; Metanephrines Annual; ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET

14. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
MEN2B vs MEN2AMEN2B = Marfanoid + Neuromas + NO HPT; MEN2A = HPT Present, No Marfanoid/Neuromas
M918T vs Codon 634M918T = MEN2B (Highest Risk); Codon 634 = MEN2A (Common)
Prophylactic Thyroidectomy AgeMEN2B: <1 Year; MEN2A Codon 634 = Age 5
Mucosal NeuromasPathognomonic for MEN2B (Lips, Tongue, Buccal, Conjunctiva)
Marfanoid HabitusTall, Slender, Arachnodactyly, Pectus Excavatum, High Palate
GastrointestinalGanglioneuromatosis → Constipation, Megacolon, Megaesophagus
OcularMedullated Corneal Nerves (Slit-Lamp); Thickened Corneal Nerves
PhaeochromocytomaBilateral 50%; Alpha-Blockade First; Cortical-Sparing Adrenalectomy
Genetic TestingRET Sequencing Mandatory; Cascade Screening for Relatives
MEN2B vs MEN2AMEN2B = Marfanoid + Neuromas + NO HPT; MEN2A = HPT + Phaeo + MTC

15. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((MEN2B))
    Genetics
      RET M918T (Exon 16) — 95%
      A883F (Exon 15) — 5%
      AD, Penetrance 100% MTC
    Phenotype
      Marfanoid Habitus (Tall, Arachnodactyly)
      Mucosal Neuromas (Lips, Tongue, Buccal, Conjunctiva)
      Ganglioneuromatosis (GI)
      Medullated Corneal Nerves
      NO Primary HPT
    Tumours
      MTC (100%, Earliest, Most Aggressive)
      Phaeochromocytoma (50%, Bilateral)
      NO Primary HPT
    RET Mutation
      M918T (Exon 16) — 95%
      A883F (Exon 15) — 5%
      Level A Risk → Thyroidectomy <1yr
    Management
      Prophylactic Thyroidectomy <1yr (6-12mo)
      TSS + Central Neck Dissection
      Phaeo: Alpha-Blockade → Surgery
    Surveillance
      Calcitonin q6-12mo
      Metanephrines Annual
      DOTATATE PET Annual
      GI Surveillance (Ganglioneuromatosis)
    MEN2B vs MEN2A
      MEN2B: Marfanoid + Neuromas + NO HPT
      MEN2A: HPT + Phaeo + MTC (No Marfanoid/Neuromas)

TopicKey Point
MEN2B TriadMTC + Phaeochromocytoma + Marfanoid/Neuromas (NO HPT)
PathognomonicMucosal Neuromas (Lips, Tongue, Buccal, Conjunctiva)
RET MutationM918T (Exon 16) — 95%; A883F (Exon 15) 5%
Prophylactic Thyroidectomy<1 Year (6-12 Months) — Earliest of All RET Mutations
MEN2B vs MEN2AMEN2B = Marfanoid + Neuromas + NO HPT; MEN2A = HPT Present
M918T MutationExon 16; Level A (Highest Risk); Thyroidectomy <1 Year
Mucosal NeuromasPathognomonic (Lips, Tongue, Buccal, Conjunctiva)
Marfanoid HabitusTall, Arachnodactyly, Pectus Excavatum, High Palate
GanglioneuromatosisGI Manifestation (Constipation, Megacolon)
PhaeochromocytomaBilateral 50%; Alpha-Blockade First; Cortical-Sparing Adrenalectomy

Prophylactic Thyroidectomy<1 Year (Ideally 6-12 Months)
GanglioneuromatosisGI Tract (Constipation, Megacolon, Megaesophagus)

18. MCQs (10)

  1. MEN2B RET mutation hotspot:

A. M918T (exon 16) — 95% of cases

B. Cys 634 (exon 11)

C. Cys 618/620/630

D. VHL

E. SDHB

  1. MEN2B clinical triad:

A. MTC + Phaeochromocytoma + Marfanoid habitus/Mucosal neuromas

B. MTC + pHPT + Phaeo

C. Parathyroid + Pituitary + Pancreas

D. MTC only

E. Phaeo only

  1. Mucosal neuromas in MEN2B:

A. Lips, tongue, buccal mucosa, conjunctiva; 90-100% penetrance

B. Rare

C. Only in intestine

D. Only in skin

E. Absent

  1. Medullary thyroid cancer in MEN2B:

A. Aggressive, early onset (childhood), 100% penetrance

B. Indolent, late onset

C. 50% penetrance

D. No MTC

E. Only sporadic

  1. Phaeochromocytoma in MEN2B:

A. 50% (often bilateral); similar to MEN2A

B. Absent

C. 100%

D. Extra-adrenal only

E. Malignant

  1. Marfanoid habitus features:

A. Tall, arachnodactyly, joint laxity, high-arched palate, NO lens dislocation

B. Lens dislocation (ectopia lentis)

C. Aortic dissection

D. Only tall stature

E. Only arachnodactyly

  1. MEN2B vs MEN2A: key distinguishing features:

A. MEN2B: Marfanoid habitus + mucosal neuromas + NO pHPT; MEN2A: pHPT + no marfanoid

B. MEN2B has pHPT

C. MEN2A has mucosal neuromas

D. Same phenotype

E. MEN2B no phaeo

  1. Prophylactic thyroidectomy timing in MEN2B:

A. ASAP — infancy/early childhood (within first year of life) due to aggressive MTC

B. Age 5

C. Age 10

D. Adulthood

E. Only if calcitonin elevated

  1. MEN2B genetic testing:

A. RET sequencing (exon 16 M918T); cascade testing of relatives

B. MEN1 gene

C. VHL gene

D. SDHx genes

E. CDKN1B

  1. MEN2B surveillance for MTC:

A. Calcitonin + CEA annually from diagnosis; neck US

B. Only calcitonin

C. Only CEA

D. Only US

E. No surveillance needed

19. SBA Questions (5)

  1. 4yo boy: mucosal neuromas on lips/tongue, marfanoid habitus. Father had MTC at 20. RET testing: M918T. Recommendation?

A. Prophylactic total thyroidectomy + central neck dissection ASAP (within weeks)

B. Monitor calcitonin yearly

C. Wait until age 10

D. Radiotherapy

E. Tyrosine kinase inhibitor

  1. Same child: pre-op evaluation for phaeochromocytoma required?

A. YES — biochemical (plasma metanephrines) + imaging (MRI/CT) before any anaesthesia/surgery

B. No, MEN2B has no phaeo

C. Only if hypertensive

D. Only if symptomatic

E. Only post-op

  1. 25yo woman: MEN2B, post-thyroidectomy, calcitonin 500 pg/mL (normal <10). Neck US: 1.5cm lymph node. Management?

A. Modified radical neck dissection (levels II-VI); consider VKI if metastatic

B. Radiotherapy only

C. Observation

D. Tyrosine kinase inhibitor only

E. Radioactive iodine

  1. MEN2B patient: which feature is ABSENT?

A. Primary hyperparathyroidism

B. MTC

C. Phaeochromocytoma

D. Mucosal neuromas

E. Marfanoid habitus

  1. Family screening for MEN2B:

A. RET M918T cascade testing; if positive → prophylactic thyroidectomy in infancy

B. Only calcitonin screening

C. Only neck US

D. No screening needed

E. Wait for symptoms

20. Flashcards

  • Q: MEN2B gene

A: RET proto-oncogene (10q11); M918T exon 16 (95%)

  • Q: MEN2B triad

A: MTC (100%) + Phaeo (50%) + Marfanoid habitus/Mucosal neuromas

  • Q: Mucosal neuromas

A: Lips, tongue, buccal, conjunctiva; 90-100% penetrance; pathognomonic

  • Q: Marfanoid habitus

A: Tall, arachnodactyly, joint laxity, high-arched palate; NO lens dislocation/heart disease

  • Q: MTC in MEN2B

A: Aggressive, early onset (childhood), 100% penetrance

  • Q: Phaeo in MEN2B

A: 50%, often bilateral; screen biochemically pre-op

  • Q: vs MEN2A

A: MEN2B: marfanoid + neuromas + NO pHPT; MEN2A: pHPT + no marfanoid/neuromas

  • Q: Prophylactic thyroidectomy

A: ASAP — infancy/first year of life (ATA highest risk HST)

  • Q: Post-op surveillance

A: Calcitonin + CEA q6-12mo; neck US; if elevated → imaging (CT/DOTATATE)

  • Q: VKI for metastatic MTC

A: Vandetanib, Cabozantinib, Selpercatinib (RET-specific), Pralsetinib

  • Q: Genetic testing

A: RET sequencing; cascade testing relatives; prenatal/preimplantation available

  • Q: No pHPT in MEN2B

A: Key differentiator from MEN2A (which has 20-30% pHPT)

21. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. M918T (exon 16) — 95% of cases — MEN2B RET mutation hotspot:
  1. MTC + Phaeochromocytoma + Marfanoid habitus/Mucosal neuromas — MEN2B clinical triad:
  1. Lips, tongue, buccal mucosa, conjunctiva; 90-100% penetrance — Mucosal neuromas in MEN2B:
  1. Aggressive, early onset (childhood), 100% penetrance — Medullary thyroid cancer in MEN2B:
  1. 50% (often bilateral); similar to MEN2A — Phaeochromocytoma in MEN2B:
  1. Tall, arachnodactyly, joint laxity, high-arched palate, NO lens dislocation — Marfanoid habitus features:
  1. MEN2B: Marfanoid habitus + mucosal neuromas + NO pHPT; MEN2A: pHPT + no marfanoid — MEN2B vs MEN2A: key distinguishing features:
  1. ASAP — infancy/early childhood (within first year of life) due to aggressive MTC — Prophylactic thyroidectomy timing in MEN2B:
  1. RET sequencing (exon 16 M918T); cascade testing of relatives — MEN2B genetic testing:
  1. Calcitonin + CEA annually from diagnosis; neck US — MEN2B surveillance for MTC:

SBAs

  1. Prophylactic total thyroidectomy + central neck dissection ASAP (within weeks) — 4yo boy: mucosal neuromas on lips/tongue, marfanoid habitus. Father had MTC at 20. RET testing: M918T. Recommendation?
  1. YES — biochemical (plasma metanephrines) + imaging (MRI/CT) before any anaesthesia/surgery — Same child: pre-op evaluation for phaeochromocytoma required?
  1. Modified radical neck dissection (levels II-VI); consider VKI if metastatic — 25yo woman: MEN2B, post-thyroidectomy, calcitonin 500 pg/mL (normal <10). Neck US: 1.5cm lymph node. Management?
  1. Primary hyperparathyroidism — MEN2B patient: which feature is ABSENT?
  1. RET M918T cascade testing; if positive → prophylactic thyroidectomy in infancy — Family screening for MEN2B:
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.8.04 Men4
[!info]
MEN4 = Rare MEN Syndrome phenocopying MEN1 but MEN1-Negative. CDKN1B (p27^Kip1^) Mutation (Cyclin-Dependent Kinase Inhibitor). Autosomal Dominant. Parathyroid + Pituitary + Pancreatic NETs (Like MEN1 but MEN1-Negative).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Define MEN4 and differentiate from MEN1
  • [ ] Recognise the genetic basis (CDKN1B/p27 mutations)
  • [ ] Apply screening and surveillance protocols
  • [ ] Understand the clinical spectrum and management

2. Definition & Genetics

FeatureDetails
DefinitionMEN1-Like Syndrome with MEN1-Negative Genetic Testing
GeneCDKN1B (Cyclin-Dependent Kinase Inhibitor 1B, p27^Kip1^)
LocationChromosome 12p13.1
Protein FunctionCyclin-Dependent Kinase Inhibitor (Cell Cycle Arrest at G1/S)
InheritanceAutosomal Dominant
PenetranceIncomplete (~50-70%) — Lower than MEN1/MEN2
PrevalenceRare (<1% of MEN Syndromes)

3. Pathophysiology

AspectDetails
GeneCDKN1B (Chromosome 12p13.1)
Proteinp27^Kip1^ (Cyclin-Dependent Kinase Inhibitor)
FunctionCell Cycle Arrest (G1/S Checkpoint); Tumour Suppressor
Mutation EffectLoss-of-Function → ↓ Cell Cycle Control → Cell Proliferation
InheritanceAutosomal Dominant
PenetranceIncomplete (~50-70%) — Lower than MEN1/MEN2

4. Clinical Features — MEN1-Like Phenotype

FeatureMEN4 vs MEN1
ParathyroidPrimary Hyperparathyroidism (Commonest; Multigland Hyperplasia)
PituitaryPituitary Adenomas (Prolactinoma > GH > Non-Functioning)
PancreasPancreatic NETs (Gastrinoma, Insulinoma, Non-Functioning)
AdrenalIncidentalomas (Usually Non-Functioning)
Thymus/LungCarcinoids (Less Frequent than MEN1)
SkinLipomas, Angiofibromas (Rare)

Key Difference: MEN1 Mutation Negative; Requires CDKN1B Testing for Confirmation.

5. Clinical Presentation

Organ SystemManifestations
ParathyroidPrimary Hyperparathyroidism (Most Common, 80-90%); Multigland Hyperplasia
PituitaryProlactinoma > Somatotroph (GH) > Non-Functioning; Similar to MEN1
PancreasGastrinoma (ZES), Insulinoma, Non-Functioning NETs
AdrenalIncidentalomas (Non-Functioning); Rarely Functional
Thymus/LungCarcinoids (Less Common than MEN1)
SkinLipomas, Angiofibromas (Rare)

6. Diagnosis — Differentiation from MEN1

StepInvestigation
1. Clinical SuspicionMEN1-Like Phenotype (Parathyroid + Pituitary + Pancreas)
2. MEN1 TestingNegative (No Pathogenic MEN1 Mutation)
3. CDKN1B TestingPathogenic Variant in CDKN1B (p27)
4. Family HistoryAutosomal Dominant; Incomplete Penetrance

Diagnostic Criteria (Proposed)

CriterionRequirement
Clinical≥2 of 3 MEN1-Defining Manifestations (Parathyroid, Pituitary, Pancreas)
GeneticPathogenic CDKN1B Variant (Inactivating)
ExclusionMEN1 Mutation Negative

7. Component Management

Parathyroid (PHPT)

AspectDetails
PathologyMultigland Hyperplasia (Like MEN1)
SurgerySubtotal (3.5-Gland) Parathyroidectomy + Autotransplant
RecurrenceHigher than Sporadic (Due to Hyperplasia)

Pituitary Adenomas

TypeFrequencyManagement
ProlactinomaMost CommonCabergoline 1st Line
Somatotroph (GH)AcromegalyTSS → SST Analogues
Non-FunctioningMass EffectSurgery if Macroadenoma
TSH/ACTHRareAs Per Usual Guidelines

Pancreatic NETs

TumourFeaturesManagement
Gastrinoma (ZES)Duodenal > Pancreatic; MultipleHigh-Dose PPI + Surgery (Enucleation/Duodenotomy)
InsulinomaUsually BenignEnucleation
Non-FunctionalOften Multiple>2cm → Surgery; <2cm → Surveillance

8. Surveillance Protocol

ComponentFrequencyModality
Calcium + PTHAnnual (From Age 10)Blood
Pituitary HormonesAnnualProlactin, IGF-1, ACTH, Cortisol, TSH, fT4, LH/FSH
Gut HormonesAnnual (From Age 20)Gastrin, Insulin, Glucagon, PP, CgA
Imaging
MRI PituitaryEvery 3 Years3mm Gadolinium
CT/MRI PancreasEvery 1-3 YearsMultiphasic
Chest CTEvery 3-5 YearsThymic/Bronchial Carcinoids
Adrenal CTEvery 2-3 YearsIncidentaloma Screening

9. Genetic Testing & Counselling

AspectDetails
GeneCDKN1B (p27^Kip1^) — Chromosome 12p13.1
ProteinCyclin-Dependent Kinase Inhibitor (p27)
FunctionCell Cycle Arrest (G1/S Checkpoint)
MutationLoss-of-Function (Truncating, Missense, Splice)
InheritanceAutosomal Dominant
PenetranceIncomplete (~50-70%) (Lower than MEN1)
Genetic TestingCDKN1B Sequencing (If MEN1-Negative + MEN1-Like Phenotype)
Family ScreeningPredictive Testing for 1st-Degree Relatives (If Mutation Known)
PrenatalCVS/Amniocentesis (If Familial Mutation Known)

10. Differences: MEN4 vs MEN1

FeatureMEN1MEN4
GeneMEN1 (Menin)CDKN1B (p27)
Chromosome11q1312p13.1
ProteinMenin (Tumour Suppressor)p27^Kip1^ (CDK Inhibitor)
PrevalenceCommon (90% of MEN)Rare (<1% of MEN)
Penetrance>95% by Age 50Incomplete (~50-70%)
Phenotype3Ps (Parathyroid, Pituitary, Pancreas)MEN1-Like (Parathyroid, Pituitary, Pancreas)
AdrenalCommon (Incidentalomas)Rare
Thymic CarcinoidCommon (MEN1)Rare
Bronchial CarcinoidCommonRare
Skin LesionsAngiofibromas, CollagenomasRare
GeneticsMEN1 MutationCDKN1B (p27) Mutation

11. Surveillance Protocol

ComponentFrequencyModality
Calcium + PTHAnnual (From Age 10)Blood
Pituitary HormonesAnnualPRL, IGF-1, ACTH, TSH, fT4, LH, FSH
Gut HormonesAnnual (From Age 20)Gastrin, Insulin, Glucagon, PP, CgA
MRI PituitaryEvery 3 Years3mm Gadolinium
CT/MRI PancreasEvery 1-3 YearsMultiphasic
Chest CTEvery 3-5 YearsThymic/Bronchial Carcinoids
Adrenal CTEvery 2-3 YearsIncidentaloma Screening

12. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
MEN4 DefinitionMEN1-Like Phenotype + MEN1-Negative + CDKN1B Mutation
GeneCDKN1B (p27^Kip1^) — Cyclin-Dependent Kinase Inhibitor
Chromosome12p13.1
Proteinp27^Kip1^ (CDK Inhibitor → Cell Cycle Arrest)
InheritanceAutosomal Dominant
PenetranceIncomplete (~50-70%) (Lower than MEN1)
Clinical PhenotypeMEN1-Like (Parathyroid, Pituitary, Pancreas)
Key Difference from MEN1MEN1 Mutation Negative; CDKN1B Mutation Positive
PHPT in MEN4Multigland Hyperplasia → Subtotal Parathyroidectomy
Pituitary AdenomasProlactinoma > Somatotroph > Non-Functioning
Pancreatic NETsGastrinoma, Insulinoma, Non-Functional
AdrenalIncidentalomas (Usually Non-Functional)
Thymic/Bronchial CarcinoidsRare (Unlike MEN1)
Genetic TestingCDKN1B Sequencing (If MEN1 Negative + MEN1 Phenotype)
PenetranceIncomplete (~50-70%)
Genetic CounsellingAD Inheritance; 50% Offspring Risk; Predictive Testing Offered

13. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
MEN4 vs MEN1MEN4 = MEN1 Phenotype + MEN1-Negative + CDKN1B Mutation
CDKN1B vs MEN1 GeneCDKN1B = p27 (CDK Inhibitor); MEN1 = Menin (Tumour Suppressor)
PenetranceMEN4 Incomplete (~50-70%); MEN1 Complete (>95% by 50)
Adrenal InvolvementMEN4: Rare; MEN1: Common (Incidentalomas)
CarcinoidsMEN4: Rare; MEN1: Common (Thymic/Bronchial)
Genetic TestingMEN1 Negative → Test CDKN1B
Family ScreeningPredictive Testing if Familial Mutation Known
CDKN1B Functionp27 → CDK Inhibitor → G1/S Arrest

14. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((MEN4))
    Definition
      MEN1-Like + MEN1-Negative + CDKN1B Mutation
    Gene
      CDKN1B (p27^Kip1^) — 12p13.1
      CDK Inhibitor → G1/S Arrest
      Loss-of-Function → Cell Proliferation
    Inheritance
      AD, Incomplete Penetrance (~50-70%)
    Phenotype (MEN1-Like)
      Parathyroid: PHPT (Multigland Hyperplasia)
      Pituitary: Prolactinoma > GH > NFPA
      Pancreas: Gastrinoma, Insulinoma, Non-Func NET
      Adrenal: Incidentalomas Only
      Carcinoids: Rare
    Genetics
      CDKN1B (p27^Kip1^)
      CDK Inhibitor → G1/S Arrest
      Loss-of-Function Mutations
      AD, Incomplete Penetrance
    Diagnosis
      MEN1-Negative + MEN1-Like Phenotype
      CDKN1B Pathogenic Variant
    Surveillance
      Similar to MEN1 (Annual Ca/PTH, Pituitary, Gut Hormones)
      MRI Pituitary q3yr; CT Pancreas q1-3yr
    Genetics
      AD, Incomplete Penetrance
      Predictive Testing for Relatives
      Prenatal Diagnosis Possible
    Diff from MEN1
      MEN1 : Common, Full Penetrance, Menin Gene
      MEN4 : Rare, Incomplete Penetrance, CDKN1B

15. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
MEN4 DefinitionMEN1-Like Syndrome + MEN1-Negative + CDKN1B Mutation
GeneCDKN1B (p27^Kip1^) — Chromosome 12p13.1
Proteinp27^Kip1^ (Cyclin-Dependent Kinase Inhibitor)
InheritanceAutosomal Dominant
PenetranceIncomplete (~50-70%) (Lower than MEN1)
Key Difference from MEN1MEN1-Negative + CDKN1B Mutation Positive
PHPT in MEN4Multigland Hyperplasia → Subtotal Parathyroidectomy
PituitaryProlactinoma > GH > Non-Functioning
Pancreatic NETsGastrinoma, Insulinoma, Non-Functional
AdrenalIncidentalomas Only (Usually Non-Functional)
CarcinoidsRare (vs MEN1 Common)
Genetic TestingCDKN1B Sequencing (If MEN1-Negative + MEN1 Phenotype)
InheritanceAutosomal Dominant
PenetranceIncomplete (~50-70%)
CDKN1B FunctionCDK Inhibitor → Cell Cycle Arrest (G1/S)
Genetic CounsellingAD Inheritance; 50% Offspring Risk; Predictive Testing
