See also: [[MEN1]], [[MEN2A]], [[MEN2B]], [[Primary Hyperparathyroidism]], [[Pituitary Adenomas]], [[Pancreatic NETs]]

17. MCQs (10)

  1. MEN4 gene:

A. CDKN1B (12p13) encoding p27Kip1 (cyclin-dependent kinase inhibitor)

B. MEN1

C. RET

D. VHL

E. SDHx

  1. MEN4 phenotype:

A. MEN1-like (parathyroid, pituitary, pancreas) but MEN1 gene negative

B. MEN2-like

C. Only hyperparathyroidism

D. Only pituitary

E. Only pancreatic NETs

  1. p27Kip1 function:

A. Cell cycle regulator (G1/S checkpoint); loss → uncontrolled proliferation

B. DNA repair

C. Apoptosis

D. Hormone synthesis

E. Protein folding

  1. MEN4 inheritance:

A. Autosomal dominant

B. Autosomal recessive

C. X-linked

D. Mitochondrial

E. Sporadic

  1. MEN4 parathyroid disease:

A. Primary hyperparathyroidism (most common, like MEN1)

B. Hypoparathyroidism

C. None

D. Secondary HPT

E. Tertiary HPT

  1. MEN4 pituitary tumours:

A. Prolactinoma, somatotroph, corticotroph, gonadotroph, non-functioning

B. Only prolactinoma

C. Only ACTH

D. None

E. Only non-functioning

  1. MEN4 pancreatic NETs:

A. Gastrinoma, insulinoma, non-functioning; duodenal gastrinomas common

B. None

C. Only insulinoma

D. Only gastrinoma

E. Only glucagonoma

  1. MEN4 vs MEN1: distinguishing feature:

A. CDKN1B mutation with MEN1-negative sequencing

B. RET mutation

C. VHL mutation

D. Different phenotype

E. No genetic test

  1. MEN4 prevalence:

A. Very rare (<5% of MEN1-negative MEN1-like families)

B. Common

C. 50%

D. 25%

E. Unknown

  1. MEN4 surveillance:

A. Same as MEN1: Ca/PTH q1-2yr, pituitary MRI q1-3yr, pancreatic imaging q1-2yr

B. No surveillance

C. Only Ca

D. Only pituitary

E. Only pancreas

18. SBA Questions (5)

  1. 40yo woman: recurrent hyperparathyroidism (3 surgeries), pituitary macroadenoma (prolactinoma), duodenal gastrinoma. MEN1 sequencing negative. Next genetic test?

A. CDKN1B (MEN4)

B. RET

C. VHL

D. SDHx

E. CDC73

  1. Same patient: family screening. Brother has hypercalcaemia, normal MEN1. CDKN1B testing positive. Management?

A. Same surveillance as MEN1: Ca/PTH q1-2yr, pituitary MRI q3yr, pancreatic imaging q1-2yr

B. No surveillance

C. Only parathyroid

D. Only pituitary

E. Only pancreas

  1. MEN4 pituitary tumour most common type:

A. Prolactinoma (like MEN1)

B. Somatotroph

C. Corticotroph

D. Gonadotroph

E. TSH-secreting

  1. CDKN1B/p27Kip1 mechanism in MEN4:

A. Loss of cell cycle control (G1/S) → tumorigenesis in endocrine tissues

B. DNA repair defect

C. Hormone overexpression

D. Receptor activation

E. Protein misfolding

  1. MEN4 vs MEN1 genetic testing:

A. Test MEN1 first; if negative + MEN1-like phenotype → test CDKN1B (MEN4)

B. Test CDKN1B first

C. Test RET first

D. Test VHL first

E. No genetic testing

19. Flashcards

  • Q: MEN4 gene

A: CDKN1B (12p13); encodes p27Kip1 (CDK inhibitor, G1/S checkpoint)

  • Q: MEN4 phenotype

A: MEN1-like: pHPT (most common), pituitary adenomas, pancreatic NETs

  • Q: Inheritance

A: Autosomal dominant

  • Q: p27Kip1 function

A: Cell cycle negative regulator; loss → uncontrolled proliferation

  • Q: MEN4 vs MEN1

A: Phenotypically identical; MEN4 = MEN1-negative, CDKN1B-positive

  • Q: Parathyroid

A: Primary HPT (most common manifestation, 80-90%)

  • Q: Pituitary

A: Prolactinoma most common; also somatotroph, corticotroph, NFPA

  • Q: Pancreas

A: Gastrinoma (duodenal), insulinoma, non-functioning NETs

  • Q: Prevalence

A: Very rare (~1-5% of MEN1-negative MEN1-like families)

  • Q: Genetic testing

A: MEN1 sequencing first; if negative → CDKN1B (MEN4)

  • Q: Surveillance

A: Identical to MEN1: Ca/PTH annually, pituitary MRI q1-3yr, pancreatic imaging q1-2yr

  • Q: Other features

A: Adrenal cortical tumours, thyroid nodules, renal angiomyolipomas reported

20. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. CDKN1B (12p13) encoding p27Kip1 (cyclin-dependent kinase inhibitor) — MEN4 gene:
  1. MEN1-like (parathyroid, pituitary, pancreas) but MEN1 gene negative — MEN4 phenotype:
  1. Cell cycle regulator (G1/S checkpoint); loss → uncontrolled proliferation — p27Kip1 function:
  1. Autosomal dominant — MEN4 inheritance:
  1. Primary hyperparathyroidism (most common, like MEN1) — MEN4 parathyroid disease:
  1. Prolactinoma, somatotroph, corticotroph, gonadotroph, non-functioning — MEN4 pituitary tumours:
  1. Gastrinoma, insulinoma, non-functioning; duodenal gastrinomas common — MEN4 pancreatic NETs:
  1. CDKN1B mutation with MEN1-negative sequencing — MEN4 vs MEN1: distinguishing feature:
  1. Very rare (<5% of MEN1-negative MEN1-like families) — MEN4 prevalence:
  1. Same as MEN1: Ca/PTH q1-2yr, pituitary MRI q1-3yr, pancreatic imaging q1-2yr — MEN4 surveillance:

SBAs

  1. CDKN1B (MEN4) — 40yo woman: recurrent hyperparathyroidism (3 surgeries), pituitary macroadenoma (prolactinoma), duodenal gastrinoma. MEN1 sequencing negative. Next genetic test?
  1. Same surveillance as MEN1: Ca/PTH q1-2yr, pituitary MRI q3yr, pancreatic imaging q1-2yr — Same patient: family screening. Brother has hypercalcaemia, normal MEN1. CDKN1B testing positive. Management?
  1. Prolactinoma (like MEN1) — MEN4 pituitary tumour most common type:
  1. Loss of cell cycle control (G1/S) → tumorigenesis in endocrine tissues — CDKN1B/p27Kip1 mechanism in MEN4:
  1. Test MEN1 first; if negative + MEN1-like phenotype → test CDKN1B (MEN4) — MEN4 vs MEN1 genetic testing:
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.9 20.9 5
▸ 20.9.01 Bronchial Nets
[!info]
Bronchial NETs (Carcinoids) = 2nd Most Common Lung NET (After Small Cell). 25-30% of All Lung NETs. Typical (Low-Grade) vs Atypical (Intermediate-Grade). Carcinoid Syndrome Rare (<10%) (Foregut Origin → Systemic Drainage). Surgical Resection = Curative for Localised Disease.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify bronchial NETs (Typical vs Atypical Carcinoid)
  • [ ] Recognise clinical presentation (Cough, Haemoptysis, Obstruction, Carcinoid Syndrome)
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic workup (CT, Bronchoscopy, Biopsy, DOTATATE PET)
  • [ ] Apply surgical and medical management algorithms
  • [ ] Differentiate from Small Cell Lung Cancer and Metastatic NETs

2. Classification (WHO 2019)

CategoryKi-67 IndexMitotic Count (per 2mm²)Former Terminology
Typical Carcinoid (TC)<5%<2Low-Grade (Typical Carcinoid)
Atypical Carcinoid (AC)5-20%2-20Intermediate-Grade (Atypical Carcinoid)
Large Cell Neuroendocrine Carcinoma (LCNEC)>20%>20High-Grade (LCNEC)
Small Cell Lung Cancer (SCLC)>20%>20High-Grade (SCLC)
Key: Typical Carcinoid (TC) = Low-Grade; Atypical Carcinoid (AC) = Intermediate-Grade; Both = Bronchial NETs. LCNEC/SCLC = High-Grade Neuroendocrine Carcinomas.

3. Epidemiology & Risk Factors

FeatureTypical Carcinoid (TC)Atypical Carcinoid (AC)
Incidence~1.5-2/100,000/year~0.5-1/100,000/year
Age40-60 Years50-70 Years
SexF = MSlight Female Predominance
SmokingNo AssociationStrong Association (Current/Former)
LocationCentral (Main Bronchi) > PeripheralPeripheral > Central

4. Clinical Presentation

FeatureTypical Carcinoid (TC)Atypical Carcinoid (AC)
SymptomsCough, Haemoptysis, Wheezing, Recurrent Pneumonia, Chest PainSimilar + Weight Loss, Dyspnoea
Carcinoid SyndromeRare (<5-10%) (Foregut → Systemic Drainage)Rare
Carcinoid SyndromeFlushing, Diarrhoea, Valvular HD (If Liver Mets)Similar
ParaneoplasticRare (Cushing - ACTH, SIADH)Cushing (ACTH), SIADH
PresentationOften Incidental (CT Screening) or Symptomatic (Obstruction)Symptomatic (Invasive)

5. Clinical Presentation by Location

LocationPresentation
Central (Main Bronchi)Cough, Haemoptysis, Wheezing, Stridor, Post-Obstructive Pneumonia
PeripheralOften Incidental (Screening CT); May Present with Pleural Effusion, Chest Pain

Paraneoplastic Syndromes

SyndromeMechanismClinical
Cushing SyndromeACTH Ectopic (More Common in AC)Hypercortisolism, Hypokalaemia, Alkalosis
SIADHADH SecretionHyponatraemia, Confusion
Carcinoid SyndromeRare (<10%); Foregut → Systemic DrainageFlushing, Diarrhoea, Valvular HD (If Liver Mets)

6. Diagnosis — Biopsy & Grading

Bronchoscopy

FeatureTypical Carcinoid (TC)Atypical Carcinoid (AC)
AppearancePolypoid, Endobronchial, Well-CircumscribedInfiltrative, Ulcerated, Bleeding
BiopsyForceps / Transbronchial Needle Aspiration (TBNA)Same
HistologyOrganoid/Nesting/Trabecular; <2 Mitoses/2mm²; Ki-67 <5%Mitotic 2-20/2mm²; Ki-67 5-20%; Necrosis Possible

Immunohistochemistry (IHC)

MarkerTypical CarcinoidAtypical Carcinoid
Chromogranin A++
Synaptophysin++
CD56 (NCAM)++
Ki-67<5%5-20%
TTF-1+ (Variable)+ (Variable)
Ki-67<5%5-20%

7. Staging (TNM 8th Edition)

StageTumour (T)Nodes (N)Metastasis (M)
IT1 (≤3cm)N0M0
IIT2a (>3-4cm) / T2b (>4-5cm)N0M0
IIIAT3 (>5-7cm) OR N1N1M0
IIIBT4 (Invasion) OR N2N2M0
IVAny TAny NM1
N CategoryTypicalAtypical
N1Ipsilateral Hilar/PeribronchialSame
N2Ipsilateral Mediastinal/SubcarinalSame

8. Imaging & Staging

ModalityRole
CT Chest (Contrast)Primary Staging; Tumour Size, Location, Nodes, Liver/Bone Mets
⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET/CTGold Standard Functional Imaging (SSTR2+); Occult Mets, Nodal Staging
PET-CT (FDG)Low Avidity (Low-Grade); High FDG Avidity = Higher Grade/Transformation
BronchoscopyDiagnosis + Local Extent (Endobronchial Extent, Carinal Involvement)
EBUS/EUSNodal Staging (N1/N2)
MRI BrainIf Neurological Symptoms / Staging IIIB/IV

9. Management by Stage

Stage I-II (Resectable)

ProcedureIndicationLymphadenectomy
Sleeve Lobectomy / LobectomyT1-2, N0Systematic N1/N2 Dissection
PneumonectomyT3, Central TumourSystematic
Sublobar (Segmentectomy/Wedge)TC Peripheral ≤2cm, Node-NegativeSampling/Dissection

Lymph Node Dissection: Systematic N1/N2 (Mandatory for Staging/Prognosis).

Adjuvant Therapy

ScenarioRecommendation
TC, R0, N0Observation (No Adjuvant)
AC, R0, N0Consider Adjuvant Chemo (Cisplatin + Etoposide) — Controversial
N1/N2 DiseaseAdjuvant Chemo (Cisplatin + Etoposide ×4 Cycles)
Positive MarginsRe-resection / Adjuvant RT (54Gy) + Chemo

10. Advanced/Metastatic Disease

Medical Therapy

LineRegimenIndication
1st Line (TC/AC)Somatostatin Analogues (Octreotide LAR / Lanreotide)Symptom Control, Tumour Stabilisation
2nd LineEverolimus (mTOR Inhibitor)Progressive, Well-Diff (G1/G2)
2nd LineSunitinib (TKI)Progressive, Well-Diff
Chemotherapy (AC/LCNEC/SCLC)Cisplatin/Etoposide (or Carboplatin)1st Line (AC/LCNEC/SCLC)
PRRT¹⁷⁷Lu-DOTATATESSTR2+, G1/G2, Progressive

Chemotherapy Regimens

HistologyRegimenCycles
Atypical CarcinoidCisplatin + Etoposide4-6
LCNECCisplatin + Etoposide (or Carboplatin)4-6
SCLCCisplatin + Etoposide4-6

PRRT (Peptide Receptor Radionuclide Therapy)

IndicationDetails
SSTR2+⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET Positive
GradeNET G1/G2 (Well-Differentiated)
StatusProgressive Despite SST Analogues
Agent¹⁷⁷Lu-DOTATATE (Lutathera)
Protocol7.4 GBq IV q8wk × 4 Cycles + Amino Acid Infusion

11. Follow-Up & Surveillance

StageSurveillance
Stage I (TC)CT Chest Annual ×5yr, then q2-3yr
Stage I (AC)CT Chest 6-Monthly ×2yr, Then Annually
Stage II-IIICT Chest 6-Monthly ×2yr, Then Annually
Stage IV3-Monthly CT + ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET + Chromogranin A / NSE

Surveillance Biomarkers

BiomarkerFrequency
Chromogranin Aq3-6mo
NSE (Neuron-Specific Enolase)q6mo (AC/LCNEC/SCLC)
ProGRPq6mo (SCLC)

12. Carcinoid Syndrome in Bronchial NETs

FeatureBronchial NET (Foregut)
Frequency<10% (Rare)
MechanismSystemic Venous Drainage (Pulmonary Veins → Left Heart → Systemic)
ClinicalFlushing (Dry/Wet), Diarrhoea, Bronchospasm, Valvular HD (Right > Left)
ManagementOctreotide LAR / Lanreotide (Same as GI Carcinoid)

13. Differential Diagnosis

FeatureTypical CarcinoidAtypical CarcinoidLCNECSCLC
Mitotic Count<2/2mm²2-20/2mm²>20/2mm²>20/2mm²
Ki-67<5%5-20%>20%>20%
NecrosisAbsentFocal/PunctateNecrosis CommonExtensive Necrosis
GrowthSlowModerateRapidVery Rapid
MetastasisLateIntermediateEarlyEarly
Smoking LinkNoYesStrongStrong
FDG-PETLow UptakeVariableHighVery High

14. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Bronchial NET ClassificationTypical (<5% Ki-67, <2 Mitoses) vs Atypical (5-20% Ki-67, 2-20 Mitoses)
Typical vs AtypicalTypical: Low-Grade, No Smoking Link; Atypical: Intermediate, Smoking Linked
Carcinoid SyndromeRare in Bronchial NETs (<10%) → Foregut Drains Systemically
Typical vs Atypical CarcinoidTC: Ki-67 <5%, <2 Mitoses; AC: Ki-67 5-20%, 2-20 Mitoses
Atypical Carcinoid + SmokingStrong Association (Unlike Typical)
ParaneoplasticCushing (ACTH) → Atypical Carcinoid; SIADH
Carcinoid SyndromeRare in Bronchial (<10%) — Foregut Drains Systemically
Imaging⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET = Gold Standard; FDG PET Low Avidity (TC)
SurgerySleeve/Lobectomy + Systematic Lymphadenectomy
Adjuvant ChemoAtypical Carcinoid + N1/N2 → Cisplatin + Etoposide
PRRT¹⁷⁷Lu-DOTATATE (SSTR2+, Progressive G1/G2)
Typical vs AtypicalTC: Ki-67 <5%, ≤2 Mitoses; AC: Ki-67 5-20%, 2-20 Mitoses
Ki-67 CutoffsTC <5%, AC 5-20%, LCNEC/SCLC >20%
FDG-PETLow in TC/AC; High in LCNEC/SCLC
SurgeryLobectomy/Sleeve + Lymphadenectomy (Mandatory)
Atypical Carcinoid AdjuvantConsider Cisplatin + Etoposide (N1/N2)

15. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Bronchial NETs))
    Classification (WHO 2019)
      Typical Carcinoid (TC): Ki-67 <5%, <2 Mitoses
      Atypical Carcinoid (AC): Ki-67 5-20%, 2-20 Mitoses
      LCNEC: Ki-67 >20%, >20 Mitoses
      SCLC: Ki-67 >20%, Necrosis
    Epidemiology
      TC: 40-60y, No Smoking Link
      AC: 50-70y, Smoking Associated
      Location: Central (TC) vs Peripheral (AC)
    Presentation
      Central: Cough, Haemoptysis, Wheeze, Pneumonia
      Peripheral: Incidental
      Paraneoplastic: Cushing (ACTH), SIADH, Carcinoid Syndrome (Rare <10%)
    Diagnosis
      Bronchoscopy + Biopsy
      IHC: Chromogranin A, Synaptophysin, CD56, Ki-67, TTF-1
    Staging (TNM 8th)
      T1-4, N1/N2, M1
      TC: T1-2 N0 M0 = Stage I
      AC: T1-2 N0 M0 = Stage I
    Treatment
      Surgery: Lobectomy/Sleeve + Lymphadenectomy
      Adjuvant: AC N1 → Chemo (Cisplatin+Etoposide)
      Metastatic: SST Analogues, Everolimus, Sunitinib, PRRT, Chemo (Cisplatin+Etoposide)
    PRRT
      68Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT → SSTR2+ → 177Lu-DOTATATE
    Surveillance
      TC Stage I: Annual CT x5y
      AC Stage I: 6mo x2y then Annual
      Advanced: 3-6mo CT + DOTATATE PET

16. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Bronchial NET ClassificationTypical (Ki-67 <5%) vs Atypical (Ki-67 5-20%)
Typical CarcinoidLow-Grade, Non-Smoker, Central, Good Prognosis
Atypical CarcinoidIntermediate-Grade, Smoking Link, Higher Metastatic Potential
Ki-67 CutoffsTC <5%, Atypical 5-20%, LCNEC/SCLC >20%
Carcinoid SyndromeRare in Bronchial NETs (<10%) — Foregut Drains Systemically
ParaneoplasticCushing (ACTH) → Atypical Carcinoid; SIADH
Imaging⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET = Gold Standard (SSTR2+); FDG Low in TC
SurgeryLobectomy/Sleeve + Systematic Lymphadenectomy (Mandatory)
Adjuvant ChemoAtypical + N1/N2 → Cisplatin + Etoposide
PRRT⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET → ¹⁷⁷Lu-DOTATATE (SSTR2+, Progressive G1/G2)
Ki-67 CutoffsTC <5%, AC 5-20%, LCNEC/SCLC >20%
SmokingAC: Yes; TC: No
Carcinoid SyndromeRare in Bronchial (<10%) — Foregut Systemic Drainage
Imaging⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET Gold Standard (SSTR2+); FDG Low in TC

17. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Neuroendocrine Tumours|Neuroendocrine Tumours]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Carcinoid Syndrome]], [[Pancreatic NETs]], [[Merkel Cell Carcinoma]], [[WHO 2019 NET Grading]], [[Small Cell Lung Cancer]]

18. MCQs (10)

  1. Bronchial NETs (carcinoids) represent what percentage of all lung neuroendocrine tumours?

A. 1-2%

B. 10-15%

C. 25-30%

D. 50%

E. 75%

  1. Typical carcinoid (TC) WHO 2019 grading criteria:

A. Ki-67 <5%, mitoses <2/2mm²

B. Ki-67 3-20%, mitoses 2-20/2mm²

C. Ki-67 >20%, mitoses >20/2mm²

D. Ki-67 <3%, mitoses <1/2mm²

E. Ki-67 5-20%, mitoses <2/2mm²

  1. Atypical carcinoid (AC) WHO 2019 grading criteria:

A. Ki-67 <5%, mitoses <2/2mm²

B. Ki-67 5-20%, mitoses 2-20/2mm²

C. Ki-67 >20%, mitoses >20/2mm²

D. Ki-67 3-20%, mitoses <2/2mm²

E. Ki-67 <3%, mitoses 2-20/2mm²

  1. Carcinoid syndrome in bronchial NETs is rare (<10%) because:

A. Foregut NETs drain via pulmonary veins → systemic circulation → first-pass hepatic metabolism bypassed

B. Bronchial NETs don't secrete serotonin

C. Tumours are too small

D. Lung metabolism inactivates mediators

E. Only pancreatic NETs cause carcinoid syndrome

  1. Most common location for typical carcinoid:

A. Central (main bronchi)

B. Peripheral

C. Trachea

D. Carina

E. Pleura

  1. Which paraneoplastic syndrome is MORE common in atypical carcinoid?

A. Cushing syndrome (ACTH)

B. Carcinoid syndrome

C. SIADH

D. Acromegaly

E. Hyperthyroidism

  1. Bronchoscopy appearance of typical carcinoid:

A. Polypoid, endobronchial, well-circumscribed

B. Infiltrative, ulcerated, bleeding

C. Normal mucosa

D. Cavitary lesion

E. Diffuse thickening

  1. Gold standard functional imaging for bronchial NETs:

A. ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT (SSTR2+)

B. FDG-PET/CT

C. CT chest only

D. MRI chest

E. Octreoscan

  1. Surgical management of resectable typical carcinoid ≤2cm peripheral, node-negative:

A. Sublobar resection (segmentectomy/wedge) + lymph node sampling

B. Pneumonectomy

C. Sleeve lobectomy

D. Lobectomy mandatory

E. Radiotherapy alone

  1. Adjuvant chemotherapy recommended for:

A. Atypical carcinoid with N1/N2 disease (cisplatin + etoposide)

B. Typical carcinoid N0

C. Typical carcinoid N1

D. All resected carcinoids

E. Only metastatic disease

19. SBA Questions (5)

  1. 45yo non-smoker: incidental 1.5cm right upper lobe nodule. CT: well-circumscribed, central. Bronchoscopy: polypoid endobronchial lesion. Biopsy: organoid nesting, Ki-67 2%, mitoses 1/2mm². Diagnosis?

A. Typical carcinoid

B. Atypical carcinoid

C. Small cell lung cancer

D. Large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma

E. Metastatic NET

  1. Same patient: surgical approach?

A. Sleeve lobectomy or lobectomy + systematic lymphadenectomy

B. Wedge resection only

C. Pneumonectomy

D. Radiotherapy

E. Observation

  1. 60yo smoker: 4cm left lower lobe mass, hilar nodes. Biopsy: Ki-67 15%, mitoses 10/2mm², focal necrosis. Post-op: R0, N1. Adjuvant therapy?

A. Cisplatin + etoposide ×4 cycles

B. Observation

C. Somatostatin analogues

D. PRRT

E. EGFR TKI

  1. Bronchial NET with liver metastases, SSTR2+ on DOTATATE PET, progressive on octreotide. Next?

A. ¹⁷⁷Lu-DOTATATE PRRT (4 cycles 7.4 GBq q8wk)

B. Cisplatin + etoposide

C. Everolimus

D. Sunitinib

E. Best supportive care

  1. Typical carcinoid: which statement is TRUE?

A. No smoking association; excellent prognosis; 5-year survival >90% for Stage I

B. Strong smoking link

C. 5-year survival <50%

D. Always presents with carcinoid syndrome

E. FDG-PET highly avid

20. Flashcards

  • Q: Bronchial NET classification (WHO 2019)

A: TC: Ki-67<5%, <2 mitoses/2mm²; AC: Ki-67 5-20%, 2-20 mitoses/2mm²

  • Q: TC vs AC epidemiology

A: TC: 40-60y, non-smoker, central; AC: 50-70y, smoker, peripheral

  • Q: Carcinoid syndrome in bronchial NETs

A: Rare <10% (foregut → systemic drainage bypasses liver first-pass)

  • Q: Paraneoplastic syndromes

A: Cushing (ACTH) → AC; SIADH; carcinoid syndrome (rare)

  • Q: Bronchoscopy TC vs AC

A: TC: polypoid, well-circumscribed; AC: infiltrative, ulcerated, bleeding

  • Q: IHC markers

A: Chromogranin A+, Synaptophysin+, CD56+, Ki-67 (grading), TTF-1 variable

  • Q: Imaging gold standard

A: ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT (SSTR2+); FDG-PET low avidity in TC/AC

  • Q: Staging (TNM 8th)

A: T1-4, N1/N2, M1; systematic N1/N2 lymphadenectomy mandatory

  • Q: Surgery

A: Lobectomy/sleeve + lymphadenectomy; sublobar for TC ≤2cm peripheral N0

  • Q: Adjuvant chemo

A: AC N1/N2 → cisplatin + etoposide ×4; TC N0 → observation

  • Q: Metastatic Rx

A: 1st: SST analogues; 2nd: everolimus/sunitinib; PRRT (¹⁷⁷Lu-DOTATATE) for SSTR2+ G1/G2

  • Q: Chemo regimens

A: AC/LCNEC/SCLC: cisplatin + etoposide

  • Q: PRRT

A: ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET+ → ¹⁷⁷Lu-DOTATATE 7.4 GBq q8wk ×4 + amino acid infusion

  • Q: Surveillance

A: TC Stage I: annual CT ×5y; AC Stage I: 6mo ×2y then annual; Stage IV: 3-monthly

  • Q: Biomarkers

A: Chromogranin A q3-6mo; NSE q6mo (AC/LCNEC/SCLC)

21. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. 25-30% — Bronchial NETs (carcinoids) represent what percentage of all lung neuroendocrine tumours?
  1. Ki-67 <5%, mitoses <2/2mm² — Typical carcinoid (TC) WHO 2019 grading criteria:
  1. Ki-67 5-20%, mitoses 2-20/2mm² — Atypical carcinoid (AC) WHO 2019 grading criteria:
  1. Foregut NETs drain via pulmonary veins → systemic circulation → first-pass hepatic metabolism bypassed — Carcinoid syndrome in bronchial NETs is rare (<10%) because:
  1. Central (main bronchi) — Most common location for typical carcinoid:
  1. Cushing syndrome (ACTH) — Which paraneoplastic syndrome is MORE common in atypical carcinoid?
  1. Polypoid, endobronchial, well-circumscribed — Bronchoscopy appearance of typical carcinoid:
  1. ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT (SSTR2+) — Gold standard functional imaging for bronchial NETs:
  1. Sublobar resection (segmentectomy/wedge) + lymph node sampling — Surgical management of resectable typical carcinoid ≤2cm peripheral, node-negative:
  1. Atypical carcinoid with N1/N2 disease (cisplatin + etoposide) — Adjuvant chemotherapy recommended for:

SBAs

  1. Typical carcinoid — 45yo non-smoker: incidental 1.5cm right upper lobe nodule. CT: well-circumscribed, central. Bronchoscopy: polypoid endobronchial lesion. Biopsy: organoid nesting, Ki-67 2%, mitoses 1/2mm². Diagnosis?
  1. Sleeve lobectomy or lobectomy + systematic lymphadenectomy — Same patient: surgical approach?
  1. Cisplatin + etoposide ×4 cycles — 60yo smoker: 4cm left lower lobe mass, hilar nodes. Biopsy: Ki-67 15%, mitoses 10/2mm², focal necrosis. Post-op: R0, N1. Adjuvant therapy?
  1. ¹⁷⁷Lu-DOTATATE PRRT (4 cycles 7.4 GBq q8wk) — Bronchial NET with liver metastases, SSTR2+ on DOTATATE PET, progressive on octreotide. Next?
  1. No smoking association; excellent prognosis; 5-year survival >90% for Stage I — Typical carcinoid: which statement is TRUE?
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.9.02 Carcinoid Syndrome
[!info]
Carcinoid Syndrome = Clinical Syndrome from Bioactive Secretion by Metastatic Neuroendocrine Tumour (NET). Serotonin (5-HT) = Main Mediator. Classic Triad: Flushing, Diarrhoea, Valvular Heart Disease. Midgut NETs (Ileum, Appendix, Right Colon) + Liver Mets → Systemic Release. Diagnosis: 24h Urine 5-HIAA.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise the classic clinical triad of carcinoid syndrome
  • [ ] Explain serotonergic pathophysiology (5-HT, Kallikrein, Prostaglandins)
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic criteria (24h Urine 5-HIAA, Plasma Chromogranin A)
  • [ ] Manage acute flushing/diarrhoea and carcinoid heart disease
  • [ ] Apply somatostatin analogues and emerging therapies

2. Pathophysiology

MechanismMediatorEffect
Serotonin (5-HT)Tumour Cell Secretion → Systemic CirculationFlushing, Diarrhoea, Valvular Fibrosis (Right-Sided), Bronchospasm, Fibrosis (Retroperitoneal, Mesenteric)
Kallikrein → Bradykinin↑ Vascular PermeabilityFlushing (Intense, Prolonged), Angioedema, Hypotension
Prostaglandins (PGE2, PGF2α)Tumour SecretionDiarrhoea, Flushing, Bronchospasm
OtherSubstance P, Neuropeptide K, Motilin, ACTH (Ectopic Cushing)Variable

Why Liver Metastases Required

  • Portal Venous Drainage → Tumour Products → Liver → First-Pass Metabolism (Monoamine Oxidase, Lung) → Systemic Circulation Only If Liver Metastases (or Ovarian Primary → Systemic Venous Drainage)
  • Foregut NETs (Bronchus, Stomach) → Systemic Drainage → Carcinoid Syndrome Without Liver Mets
  • Hindgut NETs (Rectum, Distal Colon) → No Carcinoid Syndrome (No 5-HT Secretion)

3. Clinical Features — Classic Triad

FeatureFrequencyDetails
Flushing90%Dry (Serotonin) vs Wet (Bradykinin); Triggers: Alcohol, Stress, Tyramine, Cheese, Exercise
Diarrhoea80%Watery, Explosive, Nocturnal; Secretory (5-HT → ↑ cAMP → Cl⁻ Secretion)
Valvular Heart Disease50-60%Right-Sided (Tricuspid Regurgitation, Pulmonary Stenosis); Left-Sided Rare (PFO/Shunt)
Bronchospasm10-20%Wheeze, Dyspnoea (5-HT, Bradykinin)
Fibrosis10-20%Retroperitoneal, Mesenteric, Pleural (Trapped Lung), Cardiac (Right-Sided)
PellagraRareNiacin Deficiency (Tryptophan → Serotonin)
Carcinoid CrisisRareLife-Threatening: Severe Flushing, Hypotension, Bronchospasm, DIC; Trigger: Anaesthesia, Embolisation, Chemotherapy

4. Diagnosis — Biochemical & Imaging

Biochemical Markers

MarkerSensitivitySpecificityNotes
24h Urine 5-HIAA75-85%HighGold Standard; Collect in Acid (HCl); Avoid High-Serotonin Foods (Banana, Walnut, Tomato, Pineapple, Kiwi, Avocado) 48h Prior
Plasma Chromogranin A (CgA)90-95%80%General NET Marker; ↑ in Renal Failure, PPI Use, Atrophic Gastritis
Plasma SerotoninVariableVariableLess Standardised
Plasma KynurenineEmerging—Tryptophan Metabolism Ratio (Kynurenine/Tryptophan)

5-HIAA Collection Protocol

StepInstruction
DietLow-Serotonin Diet 48h (Avoid: Banana, Walnut, Tomato, Pineapple, Kiwi, Avocado, Plum, Eggplant, Chocolate, Coffee, Tea)
Collection24h Urine + HCl Preservative (10-20mL Conc. HCl)
Normal<15 mg/24h (8 mg/24h Some Labs)
Positive>25-30 mg/24h (Often >100 in Syndrome)

5. Carcinoid Heart Disease (Right-Sided Valvulopathy)

FeatureDetails
PathologyFibrotic Plaques on Valvular Endocardium (Serotonin → Fibroblast Activation → Collagen Deposition)
Valves AffectedTricuspid Regurgitation (TR) > Pulmonary Stenosis (PS); Left-Sided Rare (PFO/ASD → Systemic Circulation)
Echo FindingsThickened, Retracted, Immobile Leaflets; Fixed, Restricted Opening (PS); Wide-Open (TR)
ClinicalRight Heart Failure (Peripheral Oedema, Ascites, JVD, Hepatomegaly), Exertional Dyspnoea
PrognosisPoor (Median Survival 1-2 Years Untreated); Valve Replacement Improves Survival

6. Diagnosis — Imaging & Localisation

First-Line: ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT

FeatureDetails
Sensitivity90-95% (SSTR2+ Tumours)
Specificity90%+
AdvantageWhole-Body, Functional + Anatomical, Low Radiation

Alternative Imaging

ModalityRole
CT/MRI Abdomen/PelvisAnatomical Detail; Liver Mets; Mesenteric Mass
¹²³I-MIBG ScintigraphyLower Sensitivity; If DOTATATE Unavailable
Octreoscan (¹¹¹In-Octreotide)Older; Lower Sensitivity
EndoscopyGastroscopy/Colonoscopy (Primary Tumour); Capsule Endoscopy (Small Bowel)
EchocardiographyEssential (Valvular Assessment; Baseline + Serial)

7. Management — Acute & Chronic

Acute Carcinoid Crisis (Medical Emergency)

TriggerSurgery, Embolisation, Chemotherapy, Biopsy, Stress
FeaturesSevere Flushing, Hypotension, Bronchospasm, DIC, Cardiovascular Collapse
Immediate RxOctreotide 500-1000µg IV Bolus → 50-100µg/hr Infusion
AdjunctsFluids, Vasopressors (Noradrenaline), Bronchodilators, Antihistamines, Corticosteroids
ProphylaxisOctreotide 200-500µg IV Pre-Procedure (Anaesthesia, Embolisation, Biopsy)

8. Chronic Management

Somatostatin Analogues (1st Line)

AgentDoseRouteFrequency
Octreotide LAR20-30mg IMq4 Weeks
Lanreotide Autogel60-120mg SCq4 Weeks
Pasireotide LAR40-60mg IMq4 Weeks (2nd Line; ↑ Hyperglycaemia)
EffectDetails
Symptom ControlFlushing ↓ 70-90%, Diarrhoea ↓ 50-70%
Tumour ControlStabilisation (↓ Growth Rate); PFS Prolongation (CLARINET)
Side EffectsGI (Nausea, Steatorrhoea), Gallstones, Hyperglycaemia, Injection Site Pain

Telotristat Ethyl (Tryptophan Hydroxylase Inhibitor)

IndicationRefractory Diarrhoea on SST Analogues
Dose250mg TDS (With Meals)
MechanismTryptophan Hydroxylase Inhibitor → ↓ Serotonin Synthesis
Effect↓ Diarrhoea Frequency, ↓ 5-HIAA
AdverseNausea, Abdominal Pain, Peripheral Oedema

Interferon-α (Historical/Refractory)

Dose3-9 MU SC 3x/week
EfficacySymptom Control, Tumour Stabilisation
ToxicityFlu-Like, Depression, Myelosuppression, Thyroiditis

9. Carcinoid Heart Disease — Management

Medical

AgentRole
DiureticsLoop (Furosemide) + Aldosterone Antagonist (Spironolactone)
AnticoagulationWarfarin (Atrial Fibrillation / Thrombus Risk)
OctreotideSymptom Control; May Slow Valve Progression

Surgical

IndicationProcedure
Severe TR/PS + SymptomsValve Replacement (Bioprosthetic Preferred — Avoid Anticoagulation)
TimingBefore Severe RV Dysfunction (TAPSE <16mm, RVSP >60mmHg)
PercutaneousBalloon Valvuloplasty (PS) / Transcatheter Valve Replacement (TR) — Emerging

10. Tumour Control & Surveillance

Tumour Grading (WHO 2019) → Determines Follow-Up

GradeKi-67Mitotic Count (2mm²)Surveillance
NET G1<3%<2Annual CT/MRI + 5-HIAA + CgA
NET G23-20%2-206-Monthly CT/MRI + 5-HIAA + CgA
NEC G3>20%>203-Monthly CT + 5-HIAA + CgA

Somatostatin Analogues (Tumour Control)

AgentDoseIndication
Octreotide LAR20-30mg IM q4wk1st Line
Lanreotide Autogel60-120mg SC q4wk1st Line Alternative
Pasireotide LAR40-60mg IM q4wk2nd Line (Resistant; ↑ Hyperglycaemia)

Peptide Receptor Radionuclide Therapy (PRRT)

Agent¹⁷⁷Lu-DOTATATE (Lutathera)
IndicationSSTR2+ Progressive NET G1/G2 (NETTER-1 Trial)
Protocol7.4 GBq IV q8wk × 4 Cycles + Amino Acid Infusion (Renal Protection)
EfficacyPFS 65% vs 11% (Octreotide LAR) at 20mo (NETTER-1)
ToxicityNausea, Myelosuppression, Renal (Amino Acid Protection)

11. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Carcinoid Syndrome TriadFlushing, Diarrhoea, Valvular Heart Disease (Right-Sided)
5-HIAA Gold Standard24h Urine 5-HIAA; Low-Serotonin Diet 48h Prior
Flushing TypesDry (Serotonin) vs Wet (Bradykinin)
Carcinoid Heart DiseaseRight-Sided (TR > PS); Left-Sided Rare (PFO/Shunt)
Carcinoid CrisisOctreotide 500-1000µg IV Bolus → 50-100µg/hr Infusion; Pre-Op Prophylaxis
5-HIAAGold Standard; Low-Serotonin Diet 48h; >25-30 mg/24h Positive
Carcinoid Heart DiseaseTR > PS (Right-Sided); Fibrotic Plaques; Bioprosthetic Valve Replacement
Octreotide1st Line; LAR 20-30mg IM q4wk / Lanreotide 60-120mg SC q4wk
PRRT (¹⁷⁷Lu-DOTATATE)NETTER-1: PFS 65% vs 11% (Octreotide)
Telotristat EthylTryptophan Hydroxylase Inhibitor → Refractory Diarrhoea
Carcinoid CrisisOctreotide 500-1000µg IV Bolus → 50-100µg/hr Infusion; Pre-Op Prophylaxis
Valvular DiseaseRight-Sided (TR > PS); Bioprosthetic Valve Replacement
Chromogranin AGeneral NET Marker; ↑ Renal Failure, PPI, Atrophic Gastritis

12. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Carcinoid Syndrome vs FlushingSyndrome = Flushing + Diarrhoea + Valvular Disease (+/- Bronchospasm)
Right vs Left HeartRight-Sided (Portal Drainage → Liver → Systemic); Left Only if PFO/Shunt
5-HIAA DietAvoid Banana, Walnut, Tomato, Pineapple, Kiwi, Avocado, Chocolate, Coffee 48h
Carcinoid CrisisPeri-Procedural; Octreotide 500-1000µg IV Bolus → Infusion
Valve ReplacementBioprosthetic Preferred (Avoid Anticoagulation in Cachectic Patients)
PRRT¹⁷⁷Lu-DOTATATE; NETTER-1: PFS 65% vs 11% (Octreotide)
TelotristatTryptophan Hydroxylase Inhibitor → ↓ Serotonin → ↓ Diarrhoea (Refractory)
Carcinoid vs FlushingCarcinoid = Triad (Flush, Diarrhoea, Valve) + 5-HIAA↑
Octreotide vs LanreotideSimilar Efficacy; Lanreotide SC (Self-Admin); Octreotide IM (Pro)
Telotristat EthylTryptophan Hydroxylase Inhibitor; Refractory Diarrhoea Only

13. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Carcinoid Syndrome))
    Pathophysiology
      Serotonin (5-HT) → Flush, Diarrhoea, Fibrosis (Valve)
      Kallikrein → Bradykinin → Flushing, Hypotension
      Prostaglandins → Diarrhoea, Bronchospasm
    Triad
      Flushing (90%)
      Diarrhoea (80%)
      Valvular HD (50-60%, Right-Sided)
    Diagnosis
      24h 5-HIAA (Gold Standard)
      Low Serotonin Diet 48h
      CgA (General NET Marker)
      68Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT (Localisation)
    Carcinoid Heart Disease
      Right-Sided (TR > PS)
      Fibrotic Plaques → TR/PS
      Bioprosthetic Valve Preferred
    Crisis
      Octreotide 500-1000µg IV Bolus + Infusion
      Pre-Op Prophylaxis
    Management
      SST Analogues (1st Line)
      Telotristat Ethyl (Refractory Diarrhoea)
      PRRT (177Lu-DOTATATE)
      Interferon-α (Refractory)
    Carcinoid Heart Disease
      Right-Sided Only (TR > PS)
      Valve Replacement (Bioprosthetic)
      Echo Surveillance
    Grading
      NET G1/G2: PRRT Option
      NEC G3: Chemo (Cisplatin/Etoposide)

14. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Carcinoid Syndrome TriadFlushing + Diarrhoea + Valvular Heart Disease (Right-Sided)
5-HIAAGold Standard; Low-Serotonin Diet 48h; >25-30 mg/24h Positive
Flushing TypesDry = Serotonin; Wet = Bradykinin
Carcinoid Heart DiseaseRight-Sided Only (TR > PS); Left-Sided Only if PFO/Shunt
Carcinoid CrisisOctreotide 500-1000µg IV Bolus + 50-100µg/hr Infusion
5-HIAA DietAvoid: Banana, Walnut, Tomato, Pineapple, Kiwi, Avocado, Coffee, Chocolate
Carcinoid Crisis ProphylaxisOctreotide 200-500µg IV Pre-Procedure
Valve ReplacementBioprosthetic (Avoid Anticoagulation in Cachexia)
PRRT (¹⁷⁷Lu-DOTATATE)NETTER-1: PFS 65% vs 11% (Octreotide)
Telotristat EthylTryptophan Hydroxylase Inhibitor → Refractory Diarrhoea
Carcinoid vs FlushingCarcinoid = Triad + 5-HIAA↑; Simple Flushing = No Diarrhoea/Valve Disease

15. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

16. MCQs (10)

  1. Carcinoid syndrome is caused by systemic release of which mediator?

A. Serotonin (5-HT)

B. Insulin

C. Glucagon

D. VIP

E. Somatostatin

  1. Carcinoid syndrome typically occurs when NET metastases involve:

A. Liver (portal drainage bypassed)

B. Lung

C. Lymph nodes

D. Bone

E. Brain

  1. Classic carcinoid syndrome triad:

A. Flushing, diarrhoea, right-sided valvular heart disease

B. Flushing, hypotension, wheezing

C. Diarrhoea, hyperglycaemia, flushing

D. Bronchospasm, flushing, hypoglycaemia

E. Only flushing and diarrhoea

  1. Carcinoid flushing is typically:

A. Dry (foregut) or wet (midgut); episodic, triggered by stress/alcohol/food

B. Wet only

C. Dry only

D. Persistent

E. Pruritic only

  1. Carcinoid heart disease affects which valves?

A. Tricuspid + Pulmonary (right-sided)

B. Mitral + Aortic (left-sided)

C. All four valves

D. Aortic only

E. Mitral only

  1. 5-HIAA (5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid) is a metabolite of:

A. Serotonin

B. Dopamine

C. Noradrenaline

D. Histamine

E. Bradykinin

  1. 24-hour urine 5-HIAA diagnostic criteria:

A. >25 mg/day (normal <10)

B. >50 mg/day

C. >100 mg/day

D. >5 mg/day

E. >200 mg/day

  1. Foods high in tryptophan to avoid before 5-HIAA collection:

A. Bananas, pineapple, walnuts, tomatoes, avocado, plums, eggplant

B. Red meat only

C. Dairy only

D. Citrus fruits only

E. Grains only

  1. Octreotide (somatostatin analogue) for carcinoid syndrome:

A. Controls flushing/diarrhoea in 70-80%; reduces tumour growth

B. Cures carcinoid syndrome

C. Only for VIPomas

D. Worsens flushing

E. No effect on diarrhoea

  1. Telotristat ethyl (tryptophan hydroxylase inhibitor):

A. Adjunct to SST analogues for refractory diarrhoea; ↓ serotonin synthesis

B. First-line monotherapy

C. Replaces octreotide

D. Only for flushing

E. IV formulation only

17. SBA Questions (5)

  1. 55yo man: metastatic small bowel NET to liver. Episodic facial flushing, watery diarrhoea 8x/day, tricuspid regurgitation on echo. Urine 5-HIAA 120 mg/day. Best initial symptom control?

A. Octreotide LAR 20-30mg IM q4wk

B. Telotristat ethyl alone

C. Loperamide only

D. Cholestyramine

E. Cyproheptadine

  1. Same patient: on octreotide LAR 30mg q4wk, persistent diarrhoea 6x/day. Add?

A. Telotristat ethyl 250mg TDS

B. Increase octreotide to 60mg

C. Loperamide PRN

D. Cholestyramine

E. PRRT immediately

  1. Carcinoid crisis during surgery/anesthesia: prophylaxis?

A. Octreotide 500µg-1mg IV pre-op + infusion intra-op

B. IV fluids only

C. Corticosteroids

D. Antihistamines

E. Beta-blockers

  1. Carcinoid heart disease: echocardiographic findings?

A. Tricuspid regurgitation/stenosis, pulmonary regurgitation/stenosis, RV dilation

B. Mitral stenosis, aortic regurgitation

C. Biventricular failure

D. Normal echo

E. Left-sided lesions only

  1. 5-HIAA false negative causes:

A. Tryptophan-free diet, SST analogue therapy, small tumour burden, renal impairment

B. High tryptophan diet

C. Liver metastases

D. Right heart failure

E. Bronchial NET only

18. Flashcards

  • Q: Carcinoid syndrome mediator

A: Serotonin (5-HT) + kallikrein/bradykinin, prostaglandins

  • Q: When does it occur?

A: Liver metastases (midgut) → systemic drainage; bronchial NETs (foregut) directly systemic

  • Q: Classic triad

A: Flushing (85%), diarrhoea (80%), right-sided valvular HD (50%)

  • Q: Flushing types

A: Dry (foregut) vs wet (midgut); triggers: stress, alcohol, tyramine foods

  • Q: Carcinoid heart disease

A: Right-sided: tricuspid regurg/stenosis, pulmonary regurg/stenosis; fibrotic plaques

  • Q: 5-HIAA

A: Serotonin metabolite; 24h urine >25 mg/day = elevated; avoid tryptophan foods 48h prior

  • Q: Chromogranin A

A: General NET marker; elevated in 80-90%; non-specific

  • Q: Octreotide/Lanreotide

A: SST2/5 agonist; controls symptoms 70-80%; antiproliferative (CLARINET/PROMID)

  • Q: Telotristat ethyl

A: Tryptophan hydroxylase inhibitor; adjunct for refractory diarrhoea on SST

  • Q: Carcinoid crisis

A: Massive mediator release → hypotension, flushing, bronchospasm; prophylaxis: octreotide IV

  • Q: PRRT

A: ¹⁷⁷Lu-DOTATATE for SSTR2+ progressive G1/G2 NETs

  • Q: Surgery

A: Primary tumour resection if resectable; cytoreduction for symptom control

  • Q: Follow-up

A: 5-HIAA, Chromogranin A q3-6mo; DOTATATE PET/CT annually

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. Serotonin (5-HT) — Carcinoid syndrome is caused by systemic release of which mediator?
  1. Liver (portal drainage bypassed) — Carcinoid syndrome typically occurs when NET metastases involve:
  1. Flushing, diarrhoea, right-sided valvular heart disease — Classic carcinoid syndrome triad:
  1. Dry (foregut) or wet (midgut); episodic, triggered by stress/alcohol/food — Carcinoid flushing is typically:
  1. Tricuspid + Pulmonary (right-sided) — Carcinoid heart disease affects which valves?
  1. Serotonin — 5-HIAA (5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid) is a metabolite of:
  1. >25 mg/day (normal <10) — 24-hour urine 5-HIAA diagnostic criteria:
  1. Bananas, pineapple, walnuts, tomatoes, avocado, plums, eggplant — Foods high in tryptophan to avoid before 5-HIAA collection:
  1. Controls flushing/diarrhoea in 70-80%; reduces tumour growth — Octreotide (somatostatin analogue) for carcinoid syndrome:
  1. Adjunct to SST analogues for refractory diarrhoea; ↓ serotonin synthesis — Telotristat ethyl (tryptophan hydroxylase inhibitor):

SBAs

  1. Octreotide LAR 20-30mg IM q4wk — 55yo man: metastatic small bowel NET to liver. Episodic facial flushing, watery diarrhoea 8x/day, tricuspid regurgitation on echo. Urine 5-HIAA 120 mg/day. Best initial symptom control?
  1. Telotristat ethyl 250mg TDS — Same patient: on octreotide LAR 30mg q4wk, persistent diarrhoea 6x/day. Add?
  1. Octreotide 500µg-1mg IV pre-op + infusion intra-op — Carcinoid crisis during surgery/anesthesia: prophylaxis?
  1. Tricuspid regurgitation/stenosis, pulmonary regurgitation/stenosis, RV dilation — Carcinoid heart disease: echocardiographic findings?
  1. Tryptophan-free diet, SST analogue therapy, small tumour burden, renal impairment — 5-HIAA false negative causes:
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.9.03 Merkel Cell Carcinoma
[!info]
Merkel Cell Carcinoma (MCC) = Rare, Aggressive Cutaneous Neuroendocrine Carcinoma. MCPyV (Merkel Cell Polyomavirus) = 80% Causal. CK20+ (Perinuclear Dot), TTF-1 Negative. Highly Aggressive (Early Lymphatic Spread). Immunotherapy (PD-1/PD-L1) = Game-Changer for Advanced Disease.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise clinical presentation (Rapidly Growing Painless Nodule on Sun-Exposed Skin)
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic criteria (AEIOU, Histology, IHC: CK20+, TTF-1-, MCPyV+)
  • [ ] Stage using AJCC 8th Edition (TNM)
  • [ ] Apply multimodal management (Surgery + SLNB → Adjuvant RT → Systemic Therapy)
  • [ ] Understand role of MCPyV and immunotherapy (PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors)

2. Epidemiology & Risk Factors

FeatureDetails
Incidence~0.7/100,000/year (Rare); Increasing
Age>70 Years (Median 75); Rare <50
SexMale > Female (2:1)
Risk FactorsUV Radiation (Major); Immunosuppression (Transplant, HIV, CLL); MCPyV Infection (80%); Fair Skin, Age >50

3. Pathogenesis — MCPyV vs UV-Induced

PathwayMechanismFrequencyFeatures
MCPyV-Positive (80%)Merkel Cell Polyomavirus Integration → Large T Antigen (truncated) → RB/p53 Inactivation~80%Older, Immunosuppressed, Less UV Signature
Virus-Negative (UV-Induced)UV-Induced Mutations (TP53, RB1, PI3K, NOTCH)~20%High UV Signature (C→T Transitions); Older, Immunocompetent, Higher Mutational Burden

4. Clinical Presentation

FeatureDetails
PresentationRapidly Growing, Painless, Firm, Dome-Shaped Nodule (1-2cm)
LocationSun-Exposed Skin (Head/Neck 50%, Extremities 40%, Trunk 10%)
AppearanceRed/Purple, Firm, Non-Tender, Shiny; Often AEIOU Features
GrowthRapid (Doubling Time ~Weeks)
Lymph NodesPalpable Inguinal/Axillary/Cervical (Early Lymphatic Spread)
Mets at Diagnosis~30% (Lymph Nodes, Liver, Lung, Bone, Brain)

AEIOU Clinical Mnemonic

LetterFeature
AAsymptomatic (No Pain/Itch)
EExpanding Rapidly (Doubling Time ~Weeks)
IImmune Suppression (Risk Factor)
OOlder (>70 Years Median)
UUltraviolet Exposure (Sun-Exposed Sites)

5. Histopathology & Immunohistochemistry (IHC)

Histology

FeatureDescription
ArchitectureSmall Round Blue Cells (Nests/Trabeculae); Scant Cytoplasm; High N:C Ratio
Mitotic RateHigh (>10 mitoses/10 HPF typical)
NecrosisCommon (Geographic/Comedo)
ArchitectureNests, Trabeculae, Sheets (Neuroendocrine Pattern)

Immunohistochemistry (IHC) — Diagnostic Panel

MarkerMCCSmall Cell Lung CancerMelanomaLymphoma
CK20+ (Perinuclear Dot)+ (Usually Diffuse)--
CK7-+--
TTF-1-+ (Most)--
Chromogranin A++--
Synaptophysin++--
NSE++--
CD45 (LCA)---+
S100 / HMB-45 / Melan-A--+-
MCPyV Large T Antigen+ (CM2B4 Ab)---

Diagnostic Triad: CK20+ (Perinuclear Dot), TTF-1 Negative, MCPyV Large T Antigen Positive

6. Staging — AJCC 8th Edition (TNM)

Primary Tumour (T)

T CategorySize / Invasion
TisIn Situ
T1≤2 cm
T2>2 - ≤5 cm
T3>5 cm
T4Invades Bone, Muscle, Fascia, Cartilage

Regional Nodes (N)

N CategoryCriteria
N0No Regional Nodal Metastasis
N1aClinically Occult (SLNB Positive)
N1bClinically Detectable (Palpable/Imaging)
N2In-Transit Metastases (Satellite Lesions Between Primary & Nodes)
N3N1 + N2 (Both Nodes + In-Transit)

Distant Metastasis (M)

M CategoryCriteria
M0No Distant Metastasis
M1aSkin/Subcutaneous/Nodes Beyond Regional
M1bLung Metastases
M1bOther Visceral (Liver, Bone, Brain)
M1cMultiple Distant Sites

Stage Grouping (AJCC 8th)

StageTNM
0TisN0M0
IT1N0M0
IIAT2/T3N0M0
IIBT4N0M0
IIIAAny TN1aM0
IIIBAny TN1bM0
IIICAny TN2M0
IVAny TAny NM1

7. Diagnosis — Workup

Initial Assessment

ComponentDetails
History/ExamSkin Exam (Primary + Lymph Nodes), Full Skin Exam
BiopsyCore Needle (Preferred) / Excisional (If Small); NOT Shave
IHCCK20+ (Perinuclear Dot), TTF-1-, Chromogranin+, Synaptophysin+, MCPyV LT+ (CM2B4)
MCPyV TestingCM2B4 Antibody (Large T Antigen) or PCR

Staging Investigations

ModalityIndication
Sentinel Lymph Node Biopsy (SLNB)All Clinically Node-Negative (T1-T2); Mandatory
Imaging (CT/PET-CT)If SLNB+ or Clinically Node-Positive / T3-T4
PET-CT (FDG)High Sensitivity (MCC is FDG-Avid)
Sentinel Lymph Node Biopsy (SLNB)Standard for T1-T2, cN0

8. Surgical Management

Primary Tumour

ProcedureIndication
Wide Local Excision (WLE)Margins: 1-2cm (T1-T2), 2-3cm (T3-T4)
Mohs Micrographic SurgeryOption for Facial/Head-Neck (Tissue Sparing)
MohsMargin Control (99% Clearance); Tissue Conservation

Lymph Node Management

ScenarioManagement
Clinically Node-Negative (cN0)SLNB Mandatory (T1-T2); If Positive → Completion Lymphadenectomy
Clinically Node-Positive (cN+)Therapeutic Lymphadenectomy (Radical)
In-Transit/Satellite MetsExcision + Lymphadenectomy

9. Adjuvant Therapy

Radiotherapy (Adjuvant)

IndicationDose / Fractionation
Primary TumourT2-T4, Positive Margins, Lymphovascular Invasion, Perineural Invasion
Lymph NodesPost-Lymphadenectomy (High-Risk: ≥2 Nodes, ENE+, Large Nodes)
Dose50-56 Gy / 25-28 Fractions (Adjuvant); 60-66 Gy (Positive Margins/ENE)

Systemic Therapy (Adjuvant/Neoadjuvant)

SettingRegimen
Adjuvant (High-Risk Resected)Nivolumab 480mg IV q4wk (CheckMate 238 / ADAM) OR Pembrolizumab 200mg q3w (KEYNOTE-716)
NeoadjuvantNivolumab 240mg q2wk × 3-4 Cycles (Pre-Surgery) + Adjuvant
ChemotherapyNot Standard Adjuvant (Historical: Platinum + Etoposide — Obsolete)

10. Advanced/Metastatic Disease — Systemic Therapy

First-Line Immunotherapy (Standard of Care)

AgentDoseApproval
Pembrolizumab200mg IV q3wkFDA 1st Line (2018)
Avelumab10mg/kg IV q2wkFDA 1st Line (2017)
Nivolumab480mg q4wk2nd Line (Post-Chemo)
Retifulimab500mg IV q4wkFDA 2023

Response & Monitoring

MetricValue
ORR (1st Line)50-60% (Avelumab JAVELIN; Pembrolizumab KEYNOTE-017)
Median PFS~8-12 Months
Median OS~20-25 Months
MonitoringCT q3mo (RECIST 1.1); LDH, CBC, LFTs

Refractory Disease

LineOptions
2nd LineOther PD-1/PD-L1 (If Not Used 1st), Clinical Trial
Chemotherapy (Rarely Used Now)Carboplatin + Etoposide (Like SCLC) — Low Response, High Toxicity
Clinical TrialsBispecifics, CAR-T, ADC, Combination IO

11. Special Situations

Lymph Node Management

ScenarioManagement
cN0 (T1-T2)SLNB Mandatory (Sensitivity 90%+)
SLNB PositiveCompletion Lymphadenectomy (Therapeutic)
cN+ (Palpable/Imaging)Therapeutic Lymphadenectomy (Radical)
In-Transit / Satellite MetsWide Excision + Lymphadenectomy; Consider Isolated Limb Infusion/Perfusion

Special Sites

SiteConsiderations
Head & NeckMohs Surgery (Tissue Sparing); Parotid/Neck Dissection if Nodes
EyelidMohs + Sentinel Node; Lid Reconstruction
GenitalWide Excision + Inguinal/Femoral Lymphadenectomy

12. Surveillance & Follow-Up

TimeframeClinical ExamImagingLabs
0-2 Yearsq3 MonthsCT/PET-CT (q3-6mo)LDH, LFT, CBC q3mo
2-5 Yearsq6 MonthsCT/PET-CT (q6-12mo)LDH, CBC q6mo
>5 YearsAnnualAnnualAs Needed

Lifelong Surveillance (High Recurrence Risk Even After 5 Years)

13. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
MCC ClinicalAEIOU: Asymptomatic, Expanding, Immunosuppressed, Older (>70), UV-Exposed
IHC TriadCK20+ (Perinuclear Dot), TTF-1-, MCPyV LT+ (CM2B4)
MCPyV80% Positive; Large T Antigen (CM2B4 Ab); Viral Integration → LT Ag → RB/p53 Inactivation
Virus-Negative MCCUV Signature (TP53, RB1 Mutations); Older, Immunocompetent
SLNBMandatory for cN0 (T1-T2); Sensitivity >90%
SurgeryWLE 1-2cm Margins (T1-T2); Mohs for Face
Adjuvant RTT2-T4, +Margins, LVI, PNI, N+; 50-56Gy/25-28#
Adjuvant ImmunotherapyNivolumab 480mg q4wk / Pembrolizumab 200mg q3wk / Avelumab 10mg/kg q2w (1 Year)
1st Line MetastaticAnti-PD-1/PD-L1 (Avelumab/Pembrolizumab) — ORR 50-60%
MCPyV80% Positive; Large T Antigen (CM2B4 Ab); Viral Integration → LT Ag → RB/p53 Inactivation
Virus-NegativeUV Signature (TP53, RB1 Mutations); Higher Mutational Burden
StagingAJCC 8th: T1-T4, N1a/b, N2/N3, M1a-c
SLNBMandatory cN0 T1-T2 (>90% Sensitivity)
Adjuvant IONivolumab/Pembrolizumab/Avelumab × 1 Year (High-Risk Resected)
1st Line MetastaticAnti-PD-1/PD-L1 (Avelumab/Pembrolizumab) — ORR ~50-60%
MCPyV80% Positive; LT Ag → RB/p53 Inactivation
Virus-NegativeUV Signature (TP53, RB1); Older, Immunocompetent

14. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Merkel Cell Carcinoma))
    Epidemiology
      Rare (0.7/100k); Male 2:1; Age >70; UV + MCPyV
    AEIOU
      Asymptomatic, Expanding, Immunosuppressed, Older, UV
    Pathogenesis
      MCPyV+ (80%): LT Ag → RB/p53 Inactivation
      Virus-Neg: UV Mutational Signature (TP53, RB1)
    IHC Triad
      CK20+ (Perinuclear Dot), TTF-1-, MCPyV LT+ (CM2B4)
    Staging (AJCC 8th)
      T1-4 (Size/Invasion); N0-3 (SLNB vs Clin Node); M1a-c
    Surgery
      WLE (1-3cm Margins); Mohs (Face); SLNB (cN0 T1-T2)
    Adjuvant
      RT: T2-4, +Margins, LVI, PNI, N+
      IO: Nivolumab/Pembro/Avelumab (1yr)
    Systemic
      1st Line: Anti-PD-1/PD-L1 (Ave/Pembro) — ORR 50-60%
      Refractory: Clinical Trials
    Surveillance
      q3mo x2yr (Exam + CT/PET); q6mo x3yr; Annual >5yr
    MCPyV
      80%+; LT Ag → RB/p53 Inactivation
    Virus-Neg
      UV Signature (TP53, RB1); High TMB

15. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
AEIOU MCCAsymptomatic, Expanding Rapidly, Immunosuppressed, Older (>70), UV Exposure
IHC TriadCK20+ (Perinuclear Dot), TTF-1 Negative, MCPyV Large T Antigen Positive
MCPyV80%; Large T Antigen (CM2B4) → RB/p53 Inactivation
Virus-NegativeUV Signature (TP53, RB1 Mutations); High TMB
SLNBMandatory for cN0 T1-T2; Sensitivity >90%
SurgeryWLE 1-2cm (T1-2), 2-3cm (T3-4); Mohs (Facial)
Adjuvant RTT2-T4, Positive Margins, LVI, PNI, N+; 50-56Gy/25-28#
Adjuvant IONivo/Pembrolizumab/Avelumab × 1 Year (CheckMate 238 / KEYNOTE-716 / JAVELIN)
1st Line MetastaticAnti-PD-1/PD-L1 (Avelumab, Pembrolizumab) — ORR 50-60%
MCPyV80%; LT Antigen → RB/p53 Inactivation
Virus-NegativeUV Signature (TP53, RB1); Higher TMB
StagingAJCC 8th: T1-4, N1a/b/N2/N3, M1a-c
SLNBMandatory cN0 T1-T2; Sensitivity >90%
Adjuvant IO1 Year (Nivo/Pembro/Avelumab) Post-Resection High-Risk
1st Line MetastaticAnti-PD-1/PD-L1 (Ave/Pembro); ORR ~50-60%
MCC vs SCLCTTF-1 Negative (MCC); CK20 Perinuclear Dot (MCC)
MCPyV DetectionCM2B4 Ab (Large T Antigen); PCR for Viral DNA

16. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Neuroendocrine Tumours|Neuroendocrine Tumours]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 20 - Endocrinology Hierarchy|Endocrinology Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Endocrinology Topic Template|Endocrinology Topic Template]]
See also: [[Carcinoid Syndrome]], [[Pancreatic NETs]], [[Bronchial NETs]], [[WHO 2019 NET Grading]], [[Skin Cancers]]

17. MCQs (10)

  1. Merkel cell carcinoma (MCC) is a:

A. High-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma of skin (Merkel cell = touch receptor)

B. Low-grade NET

C. Melanoma

D. Squamous cell carcinoma

E. Basal cell carcinoma

  1. Merkel cell polyomavirus (MCPyV) association:

A. ~80% of MCC (clonal integration); UV-signature mutations in virus-negative

B. 100%

C. 0%

D. 50%

E. Only in immunosuppressed

  1. MCC typical presentation:

A. Elderly, sun-exposed skin (head/neck), painless, rapidly growing, violaceous nodule

B. Young, non-sun-exposed

C. Pigmented

D. Ulcerated only

E. Multiple lesions

  1. Immunohistochemistry for MCC:

A. CK20 (perinuclear dot-like) +, Chromogranin A +, Synaptophysin +, MCPyV large T Ag +

B. CK20 -

C. S100 +

D. HMB45 +

E. Melan A +

  1. MCC staging (AJCC 8th):

A. Stage I: local <2cm; II: local >2cm; III: nodal; IV: distant

B. Same as melanoma

C. Same as SCC

D. No staging

E. Only TNM

  1. MCC treatment (localised):

A. Wide local excision (1-2cm margins) + SLNB + adjuvant radiotherapy to primary + nodes

B. Excision only

C. Radiotherapy only

D. Chemotherapy only

E. Immunotherapy only

  1. SLNB in MCC:

A. Indicated for all (high nodal metastasis rate 30% clinical, 50% occult)

B. Not indicated

C. Only if >2cm

D. Only head/neck

E. Only immunosuppressed

  1. MCC adjuvant radiotherapy:

A. Reduces local/regional recurrence (50% → 10-20%); indicated for most

B. Not beneficial

C. Only for positive margins

D. Only for nodal disease

E. Increases recurrence

  1. Advanced MCC immunotherapy:

A. Anti-PD-1/PD-L1 (avelumab, pembrolizumab, nivolumab) — 1st line; ORR ~50%

B. Chemotherapy 1st line

C. TKI

D. PRRT

E. No effective treatment

  1. MCC surveillance:

A. High recurrence risk (40% at 2yr); skin/nodal exam q3-6mo ×2yr then q6-12mo; imaging as indicated

B. Annual only

C. No surveillance

D. Only if symptomatic

E. Only skin exam

18. SBA Questions (5)

  1. 75yo man: 2cm rapidly growing violaceous nodule on cheek. Biopsy: small blue cells, CK20 perinuclear dot+, TTF-1-, MCPyV+. Staging: no nodes. Treatment?

A. Wide local excision 1-2cm margins + SLNB + adjuvant RT to primary + nodal basin

B. Excision only

C. RT only

D. Chemo only

E. Immunotherapy only

  1. Same patient: SLNB positive (micrometastasis). Adjuvant management?

A. Completion lymph node dissection OR adjuvant RT to nodal basin (RT preferred)

B. Chemo

C. Immunotherapy

D. Observation

E. Completion dissection only

  1. 80yo woman: metastatic MCC (lung, liver). PD-L1 positive. 1st line?

A. Avelumab (anti-PD-L1) or pembrolizumab/nivolumab (anti-PD-1)

B. Chemotherapy (carboplatin/etoposide)

C. Surgery

D. RT

E. PRRT

  1. MCC vs small cell lung cancer IHC:

A. MCC: CK20 dot+; SCLC: CK20-, TTF-1+, CK7+

B. Both CK20+

C. Both TTF-1+

D. Both S100+

E. No difference

  1. Immunosuppressed patient (transplant): MCC risk:

A. 10-30x increased risk; more aggressive; earlier onset; MCPyV+

B. Same as immunocompetent

C. Lower risk

D. Only virus-negative

E. No difference

19. Flashcards

  • Q: MCC definition

A: High-grade cutaneous neuroendocrine carcinoma (Merkel cells = mechanoreceptors)

  • Q: Epidemiology

A: Elderly (70-80y), fair skin, sun-exposed (head/neck 50%), immunosuppression ↑ risk

  • Q: MCPyV

A: Polyomavirus; clonal integration in ~80%; virus-negative = UV signature mutations

  • Q: Clinical

A: Painless, firm, violaceous/red nodule; rapid growth (weeks-months); AEIOU features

  • Q: AEIOU

A: Asymptomatic, Expanding rapidly, Immune suppression, Older >50, Ultraviolet exposure

  • Q: IHC

A: CK20 perinuclear dot+ (pathognomonic), Chromogranin A+, Synaptophysin+, MCPyV LT-Ag+

  • Q: Differential

A: SCLC (TTF-1+, CK20-), melanoma (S100+, HMB45+), lymphoma (CD45+), SCC (p40+)

  • Q: Staging

A: AJCC 8th: I (<2cm), II (>2cm), III (nodal), IV (distant)

  • Q: SLNB

A: Indicated for ALL (occult nodal mets 30-50%); guides adjuvant RT

  • Q: Surgery

A: Wide local excision 1-2cm margins + SLNB

  • Q: Adjuvant RT

A: Reduces local/regional recurrence 50%→10-20%; primary + nodal basin

  • Q: Advanced Rx

A: 1st line: anti-PD-1/PD-L1 (avelumab FDA approved); ORR ~50%

  • Q: Chemotherapy

A: Carboplatin+etoposide (2nd line); response transient (3-6mo)

  • Q: Surveillance

A: q3-6mo ×2yr (skin + nodes + imaging); 40% recurrence at 2yr

  • Q: Immunosuppression

A: Transplant/HIV/CLL: 10-30x risk; earlier onset; more aggressive

20. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. High-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma of skin (Merkel cell = touch receptor) — Merkel cell carcinoma (MCC) is a:
  1. ~80% of MCC (clonal integration); UV-signature mutations in virus-negative — Merkel cell polyomavirus (MCPyV) association:
  1. Elderly, sun-exposed skin (head/neck), painless, rapidly growing, violaceous nodule — MCC typical presentation:
  1. CK20 (perinuclear dot-like) +, Chromogranin A +, Synaptophysin +, MCPyV large T Ag + — Immunohistochemistry for MCC:
  1. Stage I: local <2cm; II: local >2cm; III: nodal; IV: distant — MCC staging (AJCC 8th):
  1. Wide local excision (1-2cm margins) + SLNB + adjuvant radiotherapy to primary + nodes — MCC treatment (localised):
  1. Indicated for all (high nodal metastasis rate 30% clinical, 50% occult) — SLNB in MCC:
  1. Reduces local/regional recurrence (50% → 10-20%); indicated for most — MCC adjuvant radiotherapy:
  1. Anti-PD-1/PD-L1 (avelumab, pembrolizumab, nivolumab) — 1st line; ORR ~50% — Advanced MCC immunotherapy:
  1. High recurrence risk (40% at 2yr); skin/nodal exam q3-6mo ×2yr then q6-12mo; imaging as indicated — MCC surveillance:

SBAs

  1. Wide local excision 1-2cm margins + SLNB + adjuvant RT to primary + nodal basin — 75yo man: 2cm rapidly growing violaceous nodule on cheek. Biopsy: small blue cells, CK20 perinuclear dot+, TTF-1-, MCPyV+. Staging: no nodes. Treatment?
  1. Completion lymph node dissection OR adjuvant RT to nodal basin (RT preferred) — Same patient: SLNB positive (micrometastasis). Adjuvant management?
  1. Avelumab (anti-PD-L1) or pembrolizumab/nivolumab (anti-PD-1) — 80yo woman: metastatic MCC (lung, liver). PD-L1 positive. 1st line?
  1. MCC: CK20 dot+; SCLC: CK20-, TTF-1+, CK7+ — MCC vs small cell lung cancer IHC:
  1. 10-30x increased risk; more aggressive; earlier onset; MCPyV+ — Immunosuppressed patient (transplant): MCC risk:
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 20.9.04 Pancreatic Nets
[!info]
pNETs = Neuroendocrine Tumours of Pancreatic Origin. 2nd Most Common Functional NET (After Carcinoid). Functional (Hormone-Secreting) vs Non-Functional. Insulinoma (Most Common Functional) > Gastrinoma > Glucagonoma > VIPoma > Somatostatinoma. Non-Functional = Majority (60-90%). MEN1 Association (Multiple, Higher Malignant Potential).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify pNETs by functionality and grade
  • [ ] Recognise clinical syndromes (Insulinoma, Gastrinoma/ZES, Glucagonoma, VIPoma, Somatostatinoma)
  • [ ] Apply localisation techniques (EUS, DOTATATE PET, CT/MRI)
  • [ ] Apply management algorithms (Surgery vs Medical vs PRRT)
  • [ ] Understand MEN1-associated pNETs

2. Classification

By Functionality

CategoryPercentageExamples
Functional (Hormone-Secreting)10-40%Insulinoma, Gastrinoma, Glucagonoma, VIPoma, Somatostatinoma, PPoma, ACTHoma
Non-Functional60-90%No Clinical Hormone Syndrome; Often Detected Incidentally or by Mass Effect

By Grade (WHO 2019)

GradeKi-67 IndexMitotic Count (per 2mm²)Behaviour
NET G1<3%<2Well-Differentiated, Indolent
NET G23-20%2-20Well-Differentiated, Intermediate
NEC G3>20%>20Poorly Differentiated, Aggressive

3. Functional pNETs — Clinical Syndromes

1. Insulinoma (Most Common Functional pNET)

FeatureDetails
Frequency~40-50% of Functional pNETs
PathophysiologyAutonomous Insulin Secretion → Hypoglycaemia
Whipple's TriadSymptoms of Hypoglycaemia + Plasma Glucose <2.8 mmol/L + Relief with Glucose
ClinicalFasting Hypoglycaemia (Early Morning), Neuroglycopenic (Confusion, Seizures), Adrenergic (Sweating, Tremor)
Diagnosis72h Supervised Fast: Glucose <2.8 mmol/L + Insulin ≥6 mU/L + C-Peptide ≥200 pmol/L + Proinsulin ≥5 pmol/L
LocalisationEUS (Sensitivity 90%), ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT, MRI/MRCP, Selective Arterial Calcium Stimulation (SACI)
TreatmentSurgical Enucleation (Curative, 90% Benign); Diazoxide (Medical Bridge); Everolimus (Unresectable)

2. Gastrinoma (Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome)

FeatureDetails
Frequency20-30% of Functional pNETs
PathophysiologyGastrin Hypersecretion → ↑ Gastric Acid → Refractory PUD
ClinicalRefractory/Recurrent PUD, Diarrhoea (Acid Inactivation of Lipase), Oesophagitis, Steatorrhoea
DiagnosisFasting Gastrin >1000 pg/mL + Gastric pH <2 (or Secretin Stimulation: Gastrin Rise >120 pg/mL)
LocalisationDuodenal > Pancreatic (MEN1: Duodenal Predominant); ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT, EUS, Secretin Stimulation Test
MEN1 Association20-25% of Gastrinomas; Duodenal Wall (Multiple, Small)
TreatmentHigh-Dose PPI (Omeprazole 40-80mg BD) + Surgical Resection (Duodenotomy + Enucleation)

3. Glucagonoma

FeatureDetails
Frequency5-10% of Functional pNETs
PathophysiologyGlucagon Hypersecretion → ↑ Hepatic Gluconeogenesis/Glycogenolysis
Clinical TriadNME (Necrolytic Migratory Erythema), Diabetes, Weight Loss
OtherGlossitis, Angular Stomatitis, DVT (Hypercoagulable), Anaemia
DiagnosisFasting Glucagon >500 pg/mL (Normal <50-100)
TreatmentSurgical Resection (Curative); Octreotide (Symptom Control); Zinc (Skin); Insulin (Diabetes)

4. VIPoma (Verner-Morrison Syndrome)

FeatureDetails
Frequency<5% (Rare)
PathophysiologyVIP Hypersecretion → ↑ cAMP → ↑ Intestinal Cl⁻/Water Secretion
ClinicalWDHA Syndrome: Watery Diarrhoea (Profuse, >1L/day), Hypokalaemia, Achlorhydria
DiagnosisVIP >75 pg/mL (Normal <75)
TreatmentSurgical Resection; Octreotide (↓ VIP Secretion); Loperamide, Fluid/Electrolyte Replacement

5. Somatostatinoma

FeatureDetails
Frequency<1% (Rarest)
ClinicalDiabetes (↓ Insulin), Steatorrhoea (↓ Pancreatic Enzymes), Gallstones (↓ CCK), Hypochlorhydria, Weight Loss
DiagnosisSomatostatin >100 pg/mL (Fasting)
AssociationNF1 (Associated), MEN1

6. PPoma (Pancreatic Polypeptide)

FeatureDetails
FrequencyRare
ClinicalOften Asymptomatic; Watery Diarrhoea, Weight Loss
DiagnosisPP >300 pmol/L (Fasting)
TreatmentSurgical Resection if Symptomatic

4. Non-Functional pNETs (NF-pNETs)

FeatureDetails
Prevalence60-90% of pNETs
PresentationIncidental (Imaging), Mass Effect (Pain, Obstruction, Jaundice), Metastatic (Liver)
BiochemistryNormal Hormones (May Have ↑ PP, CgA)
GradingG1/G2 (Well-Diff) vs G3 (Poorly Diff)
TreatmentSurgery if >2cm / Symptomatic / Growing; Observation if <1cm, G1, Asymptomatic

5. Localisation — Imaging Modalities

ModalitySensitivitySpecificityRole
EUS (Endoscopic US)90-95%90%+Best for Small Lesions (<1cm); Guided FNA
⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT90-95%90%+Gold Standard for Staging (Whole Body, SSTR2+)
MRI/MRCP (Pancreas Protocol)85-90%90%+Anatomic Detail; Ductal Relationship
CT (Multiphasic)80-90%85%+First-Line (Accessibility)
Selective Arterial Calcium Stimulation (SACI)90%+95%+Insulinoma Localisation (If Non-Localising)

6. Management by Subtype

Insulinoma

AspectDetails
SurgeryEnucleation (Curative; 90% Benign)
MedicalDiazoxide (↓ Insulin Release); Everolimus (Unresectable)
LocalisationEUS, SACI (Selective Arterial Calcium Stimulation)

Gastrinoma (ZES)

AspectDetails
SurgeryEnucleation + Duodenotomy (Duodenal Gastrinomas Often Small/Multiple)
MedicalHigh-Dose PPI (Omeprazole 40-80mg BD); Octreotide (Adjunct)
MEN1Duodenal Gastrinomas (Multiple, Small) → Longitudinal Duodenotomy

Glucagonoma

AspectDetails
SurgeryResection (Curative if Localised)
MedicalOctreotide (↓ Glucagon), Insulin (Diabetes), Zinc (Skin), Anticoagulation (DVT Prophylaxis)

VIPoma

AspectDetails
SurgeryResection (Curative if Localised)
MedicalOctreotide (↓ VIP → ↓ Diarrhoea), Loperamide, Fluid/Electrolyte Replacement

Non-Functional pNETs

Size / GradeManagement
<1cm, G1Observation (EUS/MRI q6-12mo)
1-2cm, G1Consider Surgery (Patient Factors)
>2cm or G2/G3Surgical Resection (Enucleation / Partial Pancreatectomy)
MetastaticSST Analogues (Octreotide/Lanreotide) → PRRT (¹⁷⁷Lu-DOTATATE) → Chemo (CAPTEM) → Everolimus / Sunitinib

7. MEN1-Associated pNETs

FeatureMEN1-AssociatedSporadic
MultiplicityMultiple (Often >3)Usually Solitary
GastrinomaDuodenal (Multiple, Small) > PancreaticPancreatic > Duodenal
MultiplicityCommon (Multiple Synchronou)Rare
GeneticsMEN1 MutationSporadic Mutations (MEN1, DAXX/ATRX, MEN1)
SurgeryEnucleation + Duodenotomy (Gastrinoma)Standard Resection

8. Treatment Algorithms

Localised Disease

ScenarioManagement
InsulinomaEnucleation (Laparoscopic)
GastrinomaDuodenotomy + Enucleation (Multiple in MEN1)
Glucagonoma / VIPoma / SomatostatinomaResection (Distal Pancreatectomy if Body/Tail)
Non-Functional >2cm or G2/G3Resection (Distal Pancreatectomy / Enucleation / Whipple)

Metastatic / Unresectable

LineTherapy
1st LineSomatostatin Analogues (Octreotide LAR / Lanreotide) — Symptom + Tumour Control
2nd LineEverolimus (mTOR Inhibitor) — RADIANT-3: PFS 11 vs 4.6mo
2nd LineSunitinib (TKI) — PFS 11.4 vs 5.5mo
3rd LinePRRT (¹⁷⁷Lu-DOTATATE) — NETTER-1: PFS 65% vs 11% (G1/G2)
ChemotherapyCAPTEM (Capecitabine + Temozolomide) — NEC G3 / High-Grade G2

9. PRRT (Peptide Receptor Radionuclide Therapy)

Agent¹⁷⁷Lu-DOTATATE (Lutathera)
IndicationSSTR2+ Progressive NET G1/G2
Protocol7.4 GBq IV q8wk × 4 Cycles + Amino Acid Infusion (Renal Protection)
Efficacy (NETTER-1)PFS 65% vs 11% (Octreotide LAR) at 20mo
EligibilitySSTR2+ (Krenning ≥2), Progressive, G1/G2
ToxicityNausea, Myelosuppression, Renal (Amino Acid Protection)

10. Surveillance & Follow-Up

GradeImagingBiochemicalFrequency
G1Annual CT/MRI + ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PETCgA, Hormone PanelAnnual
G26-Monthly CT/MRICgA, Hormone Panel6-Monthly
G3 (NEC)3-Monthly CTKi-67, Hormone Panel3-Monthly

Post-Operative Surveillance (Post-Resection)

TimepointAssessments
3 MonthsHormones (Specific + CgA), CT/MRI
1 YearFull Panel + MRI/CT
Annual (Years 1-5)Hormones + MRI/CT
Years 5-10q1-2yr

11. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
pNETs Most Common FunctionalInsulinoma (40-50%) > Gastrinoma > Glucagonoma
InsulinomaWhipple's Triad; Enucleation (Curative, 90% Benign)
GastrinomaZES; Duodenal > Pancreatic in MEN1; Secretin Test (+120 pg/mL)
GlucagonomaNME (Necrolytic Migratory Erythema) + Diabetes + Weight Loss
VIPomaWDHA Syndrome: Watery Diarrhoea, Hypokalaemia, Achlorhydria
SomatostatinomaDiabetes + Steatorrhoea + Gallstones + Hypochlorhydria
Non-Functional pNET60-90%; <1cm G1 → Observe; >2cm or G2/G3 → Resect
LocalisationEUS (90-95%) > ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET (Staging) > MRI/MRCP
Insulinoma LocalisationEUS (90%), SACI (Selective Arterial Calcium Stimulation)
Gastrinoma in MEN1Duodenal > Pancreatic; Multiple; Duodenotomy + Enucleation
GlucagonomaNME (Necrolytic Migratory Erythema) + Diabetes + Weight Loss
VIPomaWDHA Syndrome (Watery Diarrhoea, Hypokalaemia, Achlorhydria)
PRRT (¹⁷⁷Lu-DOTATATE)NETTER-1: PFS 65% vs 11% (Octreotide)
Everolimus/SunitinibRADIANT-3: Everolimus PFS 11 vs 4.6mo; Sunitinib 11.4 vs 5.5mo
CAPTEMCapecitabine + Temozolomide (NEC G3 / High-Grade G2)
SurveillanceG1: Annual; G2: 6-Monthly; G3: 3-Monthly (CT + CgA + Hormones)

12. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Insulinoma vs FactitiousInsulinoma: C-Peptide ↑, Proinsulin ↑; Factitious: C-Peptide Low, Insulin High
Gastrinoma vs ZESZES = Gastrinoma Syndrome (PUD + Diarrhoea + Hypergastrinaemia)
pNET vs CarcinoidpNET = Pancreatic Origin; Carcinoid = GI/Bronchial Origin
Non-Functional pNETNo Hormone Syndrome; Incidental / Mass Effect
Gastrinoma LocationMEN1 = Duodenal (Multiple); Sporadic = Pancreatic
Insulinoma LocalisationEUS (90%) > SACI (Gold Standard Functional)
Glucagonoma TriadNME + Diabetes + Weight Loss
VIPomaWDHA Syndrome (Watery Diarrhoea, Hypokalaemia, Achlorhydria)
SomatostatinomaDiabetes + Steatorrhoea + Gallstones + Hypochlorhydria
PRRT¹⁷⁷Lu-DOTATATE; NETTER-1: PFS 65% vs 11% (Octreotide)
Everolimus/SunitinibRADIANT-3: Everolimus PFS 11 vs 4.6mo; Sunitinib 11.4 vs 5.5mo

13. Mind Map

mindmap
  root((Pancreatic NETs))
    Classification
      Functional (10-40%)
        Insulinoma (40-50%)
        Gastrinoma/ZES (20-30%)
        Glucagonoma (5-10%)
        VIPoma (<5%)
        Somatostatinoma (<1%)
        PPoma
      Non-Functional (60-90%)
        Incidental / Mass Effect / Metastatic
    Grading (WHO 2019)
      NET G1: Ki-67 <3%, Mitosis <2
      NET G2: Ki-67 3-20%, Mitosis 2-20
      NEC G3: Ki-67 >20%, Mitosis >20
    Functional Syndromes
      Insulinoma: Whipple's Triad, Enucleation
      Gastrinoma (ZES): Duodenal > Pancreatic (MEN1), Secretin Test
      Glucagonoma: NME + Diabetes + Weight Loss
      VIPoma: WDHA Syndrome
      Somatostatinoma: Diabetes + Steatorrhoea + Gallstones
    MEN1
      Multiple, Duodenal Gastrinomas, Higher Malignant Potential
    Localisation
      EUS (90-95%), 68Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT, MRI/MRCP, SACI (Insulinoma)
    Management
      Insulinoma: Enucleation (Curative)
      Gastrinoma: Duodenotomy + Enucleation
      Non-Func: <1cm Observe; >2cm/G2/G3 → Resect
      Metastatic: SST Analogues → Everolimus/Sunitinib → PRRT → CAPTEM
    PRRT
      177Lu-DOTATATE (Lutathera)
      NETTER-1: PFS 65% vs 11%
      SSTR2+ G1/G2 Progressive

14. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Most Common Functional pNETInsulinoma (40-50%)
Insulinoma Diagnosis72h Fast: Glucose <2.8 + Insulin ≥6 + C-Pep ≥200 + Proinsulin ≥5
Insulinoma TreatmentEnucleation (Curative, 90% Benign)
Gastrinoma (ZES)Fasting Gastrin >1000 pg/mL + pH <2; Secretin Test >120 pg/mL Rise
Gastrinoma LocationDuodenal > Pancreatic (MEN1: Multiple Duodenal)
Glucagonoma TriadNME + Diabetes + Weight Loss
VIPomaWDHA Syndrome: Watery Diarrhoea, Hypokalaemia, Achlorhydria
SomatostatinomaDiabetes + Steatorrhoea + Gallstones + Hypochlorhydria
Non-Functional pNET60-90%; <1cm G1 → Observe; >2cm/G2/G3 → Resect
LocalisationEUS (90-95%) > ⁶⁸Ga-DOTATATE PET (Staging)
Insulinoma LocalisationEUS (90%) > SACI (Selective Arterial Calcium Stimulation)
PRRT (¹⁷⁷Lu-DOTATATE)NETTER-1: PFS 65% vs 11% (Octreotide)
Everolimus/SunitinibRADIANT-3 / NETTER-1: PFS Benefit
CAPTEMCapecitabine + Temozolomide (NEC G3 / High-Grade G2)
SurveillanceG1: Annual; G2: 6-Monthly; G3: 3-Monthly

15. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)
